⚖️ Chapter 1 — Introductory

⚖️ Chapter 1 — Introductory

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Positive
Problem / Danger / Negative
Condition / If / Factor
Alternative / Discretionary
Neutral / Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
1.1 Expansion of Administrative Law
1.2 Function of Administrative Law
1.3 Definition, Nature & Scope

1.5 E-Governance
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
1.1 — Expansion of Administrative Law
📜 Expansion of Administrative Law
Most "outstanding legal development of the twentieth century"
📝 Administrative Law is not new — it is as old as organised government itself. But in the 20th century it exploded in size, importance and complexity, becoming a recognised branch of public law completely separate from Constitutional Law.
Why did Administrative Law grow so rapidly?
Two Primary Causes
CAUSE 1
Collapse of Laissez Faire — "Law & Order" State Model
📝 Laissez faire = "Let it be." The old 19th century idea that the State should stay out of economic life — no welfare, minimal regulation, free market rules everything.
Results of Laissez Faire:
• Exploitation of labour by management
• Slums, child labour, poverty
• Concentration of wealth in few hands
• Unequal bargaining position
TRANSITION
Rise of "Collectivism" → Social Welfare State
State assumes positive role in socio-economic welfare
📝 Collectivism = The opposite of laissez faire. State actively regulates industry, protects labour, provides welfare — the modern welfare state model adopted by India in 1947.
CAUSE 2
Critical International & Internal Security Situation
Government acquires vast powers for:
• Defence of India
• Internal security (NSA 1980)
• Preventive detention powers
• Maintenance of essential supplies
📝 NSA = National Security Act, 1980. Allows preventive detention — government can detain a person without trial on grounds of national security. A classic example of discretionary administrative power.
💡 GST parallel: CGST officers have vast search, seizure, arrest powers (Sec 67–70 CGST Act) — exactly the kind of administrative power that must be checked by Administrative Law principles.
Five Roles of the Modern Welfare State
(Functions that generate Administrative Law)
1
PROTECTOR
External aggression & internal disorder
2
PROVIDER
Social security, welfare, minimum living standard
3
REGULATOR
Controls economic activities, urban planning, environment
4
ENTREPRENEUR
Runs public enterprises — railways, buses, mines, key industries
5
ARBITER
Balances competing social interests; ensures minimum fairness
MAINE'S CLASSIC AXIOM — REVERSED
Maine said: "Movement of progressive societies is from status to contract."
20th century has REVERSED this — State regulation has reverted society to status-based relations; contract law is giving way to administrative law.
📝 In plain terms: Earlier, your legal rights depended on what contracts you signed. Today, your rights are defined more by your status — as a taxpayer, consumer, employee, citizen — and governed by administrative law rather than contract law. GST is a perfect example: your liability is determined by statute, not by what you agreed.
💡 GST Example: A taxpayer's liability under GST is determined by the CGST Act (administrative law), not by the contract between buyer and seller. Even if a contract says "GST-inclusive price," the law overrides the contract on rate/classification.
INDIA — POST 1947 WELFARE STATE
Constitution Preamble: Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic Republic
Article 38 (DPSP): Social, economic and political justice in all institutions
Article 39: Equal right to adequate livelihood; no concentration of wealth
Article 41: Education & assistance in old age, unemployment
Article 45: Free & compulsory education up to 14 years
1.2 — Function of Administrative Law
⚖️ Function of Administrative Law
Controlling Power — Protecting the Individual
The Core Tension: Power vs. Rights
THE DANGER
Modern administration impinges on every moment of citizen's existence
→ Capacity to infringe rights & liberties enormously
→ Misuse & abuse of power
→ Bureaucratic corruption
📝 "Absolute discretion, like corruption, marks the beginning of the end of liberty" — Justice Douglas, US Supreme Court. Uncontrolled power = abuse. This is the fundamental problem Administrative Law solves.
THE SOLUTION
Administrative Law provides:
• Safeguards on exercise of power
• Control-mechanism (judicial + extra-judicial)
• Effective remedy to citizens
• Checks & balances on bureaucracy
📝 Just as traffic rules exist so fast cars don't kill people, Administrative Law exists so powerful bureaucracies don't crush citizens' rights — even while they do necessary regulatory work.
Objectives of Administrative Law (Per the Chapter)
OBJ 1
Ensure legal control of administrative power
("Administration must have lawful authority to do what it seeks to do")
OBJ 2
Protect individual against abuse of such power
("The powerful engines of authority must be prevented from running amok")
OBJ 3
Draw fine balance: individual claims vs. administration needs
Efficiency is desirable — but NOT the only yardstick
Fairness is equally important
OBJ 4
Redress inequality between citizen and State
"The starting point is the basic inequality of parties" — Schwartz
LOCKE'S MAXIM (17th Century)
"Wherever law ends, tyranny begins."
The age-old conflict between individual liberty and Government — Administrative Law is the bridge.
WHY PARLIAMENTARY CONTROL ALONE IS INSUFFICIENT
(1) Cabinet enjoys majority support in Parliament
(2) Individual grievance cannot be easily raised on floor of House
(3) MPs have no access to departmental files
(4) Ministers do not control every detail of administration
(5) Modern party system gives Government a hold over legislature
→ Therefore: Judicial control + other mechanisms are essential supplements
💡 GST Example: A taxpayer receives a demand notice from an Inspector who has no jurisdiction under Section 74. Parliament cannot help — but High Court writ under Article 226 (Administrative Law remedy) can quash the notice. This is exactly why Administrative Law matters for GST practitioners.
1.3 — Definition, Nature and Scope of Administrative Law
📚 Definitions of Administrative Law
Three Schools — American, English, Indian Approach
📝 No single definition fully captures Administrative Law — all existing definitions are either too broad or too narrow. The chapter presents multiple formulations and identifies the most satisfactory one.
AMERICAN — Kenneth Culp Davis
Administrative Law = "Law concerning the powers and procedures of administrative agencies, including especially the law governing judicial review of administrative action"
Administrative Agency = Governmental authority (not court, not legislature) affecting private rights through adjudication or rulemaking
CRITICISM
Does NOT include purely discretionary administrative functions — which are vast & important
Does not cover enough control mechanisms beyond judicial review
ENGLISH (Old) — Dicey
Administrative Law = "Portion of nation's legal system which determines legal status & liabilities of state officials; defines rights & liabilities of private individuals in their dealings with public officials; specifies procedure by which those rights are enforced"
CRITICISM
Too narrow — excludes public corporations & non-state administrative bodies
Excludes parliamentary control, administrative appeals, ombudsman
Focuses only on judicial control of public officials
Influenced by droit administratif (French system)
📝 Droit administratif = French system of special administrative courts (not ordinary civil courts) handling state disputes. Dicey opposed this concept, hence his narrow definition.
ENGLISH (Modern) — Jennings / Wade & Phillips
Jennings: "Administrative law is the law relating to the Administration. It determines the organisation, powers and duties of administrative authorities."

Wade & Phillips: "Branch of public law concerned with composition, powers, duties, rights and liabilities of various organs of Government engaged in administration."
CRITICISM BY GRIFFITH & STREET
Does not distinguish Constitutional Law from Administrative Law
Too wide — almost every statute could fall within its scope
Does not explicitly mention administrative procedures (implied only)
✅ Most Satisfactory Formulation — Four Limbs
Administrative Law deals with:
LIMB 1
Structure & Powers
Composition and powers of organs of administration
(all kinds of public / administrative authorities)
📝 Who has power — which officer, which body, at what level of government. The architecture of administrative power.
LIMB 2
Limits of Powers
Express limits (statutory norms) + Implied limits (court interpretation)
Most significant & creative aspect — implied limits rarely laid down
📝 Limits can be written in the law (express) or created by courts through interpretation (implied). Courts regularly create implied limits on administrative action — natural justice, reasonableness, proportionality.
💡 GST: Sec 74 demand requires proper "reasons to believe" — courts have implied that SCN cannot be vague; must give reasons. This is an implied limit on administrative power.
LIMB 3
Procedures
Methods & procedures followed in exercising powers
Fair procedures = way to minimise abuse
SC: "Procedural rules infuse life into substantive rights" — Ratlam Municipality v Vardhichand
📝 How power is exercised matters as much as what power exists. Natural justice = fair procedure. Courts now apply "fairness" concept broadly to administrative actions.
💡 GST: Opportunity of personal hearing before adverse order (Sec 75(4)) is a procedural right. Denial = violation of natural justice = ground to quash order in writ.
LIMB 4
Control & Remedies
Judicial + Extra-judicial control
Three basic propositions:
(1) Power conferred by law
(2) No authority can exceed power
(3) No power is absolute & uncontrolled
📝 The most important limb — where do you go when administration acts wrongly? Courts (writ jurisdiction), tribunals (GSTAT), ombudsman, parliamentary control — all covered here.
💡 GST: Appeal to Appellate Authority → GSTAT → High Court → Supreme Court. Each level is an administrative control mechanism. Writ under Art 226 = judicial control of administrative action.
⚠️ KEY EXCLUSION
Matters of purely internal administration (recruitment, leave, promotion, internal management) are NOT Administrative Law — they are "public administration." However, courts in India have increasingly brought service matters within Administrative Law scope (compulsory retirement, dismissal cases).
1.4 — Human Rights Approach in Administration
🌐 Human Rights Approach in Administration
India's International Obligations + Constitutional Framework
India's Constitutional & International Human Rights Framework
CONSTITUTION
Preamble: Justice, Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, Dignity
Part III (Art 12–35): Fundamental Rights
Part IV (Art 36–51): Directive Principles
Art 51A: Fundamental Duties (Part IVA)
INTERNATIONAL
UN Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948)
Covenant on Civil & Political Rights (1966)
Covenant on Economic, Social & Cultural Rights (1966)
India ratified → Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993
WRITS & REMEDIES
Articles 32 & 226 of Constitution
Constitutional Courts: writs for fundamental rights
Human Rights Commissions: quasi-judicial fora
Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993
Rights-Based Approach to Judicial Review
PRINCIPLES EXAMINED
Legality
Procedural propriety
Participation
Openness / Transparency
Rationality
Relevancy
Propriety of purpose
ADDITIONAL PRINCIPLES
Reasonableness
Legitimate expectations
Legal certainty
Proportionality
📝 Proportionality = Punishment/action must be proportionate to the wrong. Government cannot impose a massive penalty for a minor technical lapse. This is now a powerful ground to challenge GST orders.
💡 GST: Section 122 penalty "shall not be less than" 10,000 — Courts apply proportionality to check if penalty is unreasonably harsh for a minor procedural default.
DUTY OF JUDICIARY
Examine if legislative intent was kept in view by administrator
Examine if due regard to individual's fundamental rights
Delineate boundaries of legislative & executive power
Invalidate administrative action infringing human rights
LANDMARK CASE
Puttaswamy v UOI (2017) 10 SCC 1
Supreme Court (9-Judge Bench): Right to Privacy is a fundamental human right.
Any abridgment must pass:
(i) Legitimate State interest test
(ii) Proportionality test
📝 This case arose from the Aadhaar biometric scheme. The SC held that even national security or welfare programmes cannot override privacy without satisfying the proportionality test. A rights-based approach means the burden shifts to the State to justify its action.
💡 GST Application: When a GST officer demands access to all business records or conducts surprise searches, Puttaswamy principles apply — the officer must have lawful authority, the action must be proportionate, and must respect the taxpayer's right to privacy and dignity.
RULE OF LAW FOUNDATION
Rule of Law demands:
• Lawful authority for exercise of power
• Individuals can plan their lives on clear, open, general laws
• State accords pre-eminence to human values
• Accountability of holders of power
• Participation of people in law enforcement
1.4.1 — Impact of Human Rights on Administrative Action
🔍 Impact of Human Rights on Administrative Law
Historical Timeline — Human Rights in India
1948
UN adopts Universal Declaration of Human Rights
1950
Constitution of India — Preamble + Part III + Part IV incorporate human rights
1966
India signs UN Covenants on Civil & Political Rights + Economic, Social & Cultural Rights
1993
Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993 enacted
→ National & State Human Rights Commissions
KEY PRINCIPLE
Human rights are natural rights — possessed by virtue of being born human.
They need protection WHETHER OR NOT recognised by man-made law.
International bodies + national constitutions + laws give them recognition.
📝 Natural rights = rights you have simply because you are a human being — not because a Parliament gave them to you. Even if a law is silent, these rights exist. Administrators must respect them.
New Approach for Courts Under Rights-Based Model
Construe legislation in compliance with national & international human rights law
Decision maker must justify action — not merely explain it
📝 This is a crucial shift. Earlier, explaining a decision was enough. Now the administrator must justify — show the action was reasonable, proportionate, and legally valid. A huge shift in burden.
💡 GST: When an Adjudicating Authority confirms a demand, they must now justify each finding — not merely reproduce the SCN. Courts increasingly quash orders that fail to justify.
Apply reasonableness & proportionality with greater force in light of human rights law
Democracy = individual rights, not merely rule by majority
Judiciary delineates boundaries of legislative & executive power
KEY JUDICIAL MAXIMS (from the chapter)
Krishna Iyer J (Gujarat Steel): "Law is not dogmatics but pragmatics"
Bhagwati J (Maneka Gandhi): "Law is not logic but experience"
Ratlam Municipality v Vardhichand (SC): "It is procedural rules which infuse life into substantive rights, which activate them to make them effective."
1.5 — E-Governance
💻 E-Governance — Opportunities & Dangers
✓ POSITIVE ASPECTS
• Instils transparency in administration
• Promotes accountability of officers
• Helps control corruption
• No alternative for honest administration
• National e-Governance Plan (NeGP): countrywide infrastructure to remotest villages
• Digitization of records — easy, reliable internet access
• Brings public services closer to citizens
💡 GST: The entire GST system — registration, return filing, e-invoicing, e-way bill, GSTR matching — is an e-governance success. Reduces human interface, curbs corruption in revenue collection.
✗ DANGER — The "Wooden/Iron" Administration Risk
• More technology = administration becomes wooden, then iron
• Completely insensitive to human needs & problems
• Administrator becomes fully dependent on technology
• Human sensitivity lost in governance
• Sovereignty rests in people — human problems need human minds
📝 "Wooden → Iron" administration = progressively more rigid, rule-bound, automated — unable to exercise human judgment. An algorithm cannot feel; an administrator must. E-governance must preserve this human element.
💡 GST: System-generated demand notices (ASMT-10, DRC-01A) sometimes throw up wrong mismatches — a human officer must apply mind before converting to a full demand. Purely automated action without DM application is a legal infirmity.
THE ESSENTIAL SAFEGUARD
In promoting e-governance, it is necessary that:
• Human element in administration is not unduly reduced or eliminated
• Administrator retains power & discretion to solve issues with human sensitivity
• Technology serves administration — not replaces human judgment
Civil service must be e-governance friendly but not e-governance enslaved (Hota Committee reference)
📝 The chapter warns: democracy rests on human sovereignty. Even if the entire process is digital, the final decision-making must involve application of human mind — otherwise it is not governance, it is automation. An automated order without a DM applying his mind is legally void.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 1 | Administrative Law Foundations
These ratios and principles are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, First Appellate Authority, GSTAT, and High Courts. State them confidently and cite the authority.
Locke's Maxim — Tyranny & Law
John Locke | 17th Century | Cited in Chapter 1 — Principles of Administrative Law
Key Ratio
"Wherever law ends, tyranny begins." — The age-old conflict between individual liberty and Government demands a proper balance between public interest and private interest. When sweeping powers are conferred on administrative organs, effective control-mechanism must also be evolved, so as to ensure that officers do not use their powers in an undue manner or for an unwarranted purpose.
When to Use — When challenging an SCN or order passed in excess of jurisdiction, or where GST officer uses power for an unintended purpose (e.g., using search power for fishing expedition). Argue: "Where law ends, tyranny begins — the power conferred must be exercised within the four corners of the statute."
Wade — "The Powerful Engines of Authority"
Wade, Administrative Law, 5 (1988) | Cited in Chapter 1, Section 1.2
Key Ratio
"The powerful engines of authority must be prevented from running amok." It is the basic philosophy of Administrative Law that the administration must have lawful authority to do what it seeks to do. It is the task of Administrative Law to ensure that governmental functions are exercised according to law, on proper legal principles and according to rules of reason and justice.
When to Use — When opposing an order passed without jurisdiction or without adequate reason. Cite to argue: the authority must have lawful authority — not just statutory authority exercised arbitrarily. Applicable when challenging SCN issued under wrong section, or demand confirmed without proper reasoning.
Schwartz — Basic Inequality of Parties | Goal of Administrative Law
Schwartz, Administrative Law, 26 (1976) | Cited in Chapter 1, Section 1.2
Key Ratio
"The starting point is the basic inequality of the parties. The goal of administrative law is to redress this inequality — to ensure that, so far as possible, the individual and the State are placed on a plane of equality before the bar of justice."
When to Use — When arguing against any ex-parte order, or when the Department has not shared all materials relied upon in the SCN. The basic inequality between a taxpayer and a Government agency is the starting point — fairness demands full disclosure of material and opportunity to respond.
Justice Douglas (US Supreme Court) — Absolute Discretion
Justice William O. Douglas | New York v United States, 342 US 882, 884 | Cited in Chapter 1, Section 1.2
Key Ratio
"Absolute discretion, like corruption, marks the beginning of the end of liberty." Administrative powers are exercised by thousands of officials and affect millions of people. While the Administration has the capacity to do a lot of good, it also has the capacity to do a lot of damage to the rights and interests of individuals.
When to Use — When challenging orders passed on "satisfaction" of the Commissioner (e.g., provisional attachment u/s 83, provisional assessment, anti-evasion proceedings). Argue: discretion must be guided by law and reason — absolute or unchecked discretion is constitutionally impermissible.
Ratlam Municipality v Vardhichand — Procedural Rules Infuse Life
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1980 SC 1622 | (1980) 4 SCC 162 | Cited in Chapter 1, Section 1.3
Key Ratio
"It is procedural rules which infuse life into substantive rights, which activate them to make them effective."
When to Use — When the Department denies a personal hearing, issues a defective SCN without proper notice period, or passes order without considering written reply. Argue: the procedural right (personal hearing under Sec 75(4) CGST Act) is not merely technical — it infuses life into the substantive right to challenge the demand. Violation = order void.
Puttaswamy v Union of India — Privacy & Proportionality
Supreme Court of India (9-Judge Constitution Bench) | (2017) 10 SCC 1
Key Ratio
Right to privacy is a fundamental human right. Any abridgment of privacy must pass: (i) legitimate State interest test — the purpose must be legitimate; (ii) proportionality test — the action must be proportionate to the purpose. The power of judicial review extends to examining legality, procedural propriety, rationality, reasonableness, legitimate expectations, and proportionality.
When to Use — When GST officers demand access to personal financial records beyond the scope of inquiry, or when search/seizure is disproportionate to the alleged evasion, or when biometric data/Aadhaar-linked compliance is at issue. Argue: right to privacy is fundamental — State must demonstrate legitimate purpose AND proportionality before accessing private business records.
Krishna Iyer J — Law is Pragmatics, Not Dogmatics
Justice V.R. Krishna Iyer | Gujarat Steel Tubes v Its Mazdoor Sabha | AIR 1980 SC 1896 | Cited in Chapter 1
Key Ratio
"Law is not dogmatics but pragmatics." — Bhagwati J in Maneka Gandhi v UOI (AIR 1978 SC 597): "Law is not logic but experience." — The courts take a pragmatic view in reconciling public and private rights, rather than a purely theoretical view.
When to Use — When the Department takes a hyper-technical or purely literal interpretation of a GST provision against the taxpayer. Argue: law must be interpreted pragmatically — not dogmatically. Courts look at substance, purpose, and practical effect, not just the letter of the statute.
Kerr Committee (Australia) — Efficiency + Justice to Individual
Commonwealth Administrative Committee, 3 (1971) | Also: Wade, Towards Administrative Justice, 11 (1963) | Cited in Chapter 1, Section 1.2
Key Ratio
"Although administrative efficiency is a dominant objective of the administrative process, nevertheless the achievement of that objective should be consistent with the attainment of justice to the individual." Efficiency of administration, though desirable, cannot be the only yardstick of good administration. Fairness to the individual concerned is also a value to be achieved along with efficient administration. A fair administration is really good administration.
When to Use — When the Department argues that summary rejection of a reply or denial of personal hearing was done for efficiency. Counter: efficiency cannot override fairness. Good administration requires both. An order passed efficiently but unfairly is not good administration — it is bad administration.
E-Governance & Human Mind Application Principle
Chapter 1, Section 1.5 | Principles of Administrative Law | Administrative Law Principle
Key Principle
In democracy, the sovereignty rests in the people and human problems are required to be resolved by application of human mind. The administration is required to organise its activities so that through e-governance, the scope of human sensitivity is not totally lost. The administrator should not be made completely dependent on technology and must have power and discretion to solve issues with human sensitivity. A safeguard is necessary that human element in administration is not unduly reduced or eliminated.
When to Use — When opposing system-generated demands (ASMT-10, DRC-01A automatically generated), or orders passed based solely on GSTR-2A/2B mismatches without independent application of mind by the officer. Argue: any administrative action requires application of human mind — a purely system-generated action without DM application is legally infirm and violates the fundamental principle that administrative decisions must reflect human judgment.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter II — Some Constitutional Principles & Their Impact on Administrative Law

⚖️ Chapter II — Some Constitutional Principles & Their Impact on Administrative Law

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Positive
Criticism / Defect / Limitation
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative route
Neutral Reference / Concept
Country / Jurisdiction-specific
📝 Plain English
💡 Example / Case
📋 Index of Sections
2.1 Constitutional Law vs Administrative Law
2.2 Rule of Law — Dicey's Formulation
2.2A Criticisms of Dicey's Rule of Law
2.2B Four Values Derivable from Rule of Law
2.2C Rule of Law in India — Article 14 & Constitution
2.3.1 Growth — England
2.3.2 Growth — Australia
2.3.3 Growth — New Zealand

⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
2.1 — Relation between Constitutional Law and Administrative Law
⚖️ Constitutional Law & Administrative Law
Deep, Intimate and Abiding Relationship
📝 Both belong to the same family — Public Law. They govern and regulate the State. But they operate at different levels: Constitutional Law at the apex (3 organs), Administrative Law at ground level (thousands of administrative bodies).
✓ What They Share — Common Ground
Both are branches of Public Law
Both deal with powers and functions of government
Both diffuse powers of State and control them
Supplement each other — neither is complete without the other
≠ Key Distinction — Different Levels of Operation
CONSTITUTIONAL LAW
Deals with structures, organisation, powers & functions of 3 apex organs:
Legislature | Executive | Judiciary
📝 Constitutional Law is the framework — it lays down who has what power at the top level of government.
ADMINISTRATIVE LAW
Refers to operation of administration vis-à-vis the individual
Focus: exercise of statutory powers of administrative authorities
📝 Administrative Law is operational — it governs how thousands of government agencies exercise power over ordinary people in daily life.
💡 GST officer issuing Show Cause Notice, Income Tax officer completing assessment, Municipal authority granting/denying licence — all governed by Administrative Law.
🌍 How Constitution Shapes Administrative Law — Country Comparison
ENGLAND
Sovereignty of Parliament, Rule of Law, ministerial responsibility, prerogatives of Crown, position of courts — shape the character of Admin Law
INDIA
Parliamentary system, judicial review, Fundamental Rights — affect character of Indian Administrative Law
USA
Separation of Powers, Presidential form, Fundamental Rights, judicial review of administrative action — shape US Administrative Law
KEY PRINCIPLE
Administrative Law has now developed an identifiable personality of its own — a branch of law distinct from Constitutional Law. It can no longer be treated as merely an appendage of Constitutional Law. Yet in India, a student of Administrative Law needs a good deal of knowledge of Constitutional Law — the Constitution is the fundamental law of the land and conditions every law.
2.2 — Rule of Law — Dicey's Formulation (1885)
📜 Rule of Law — A.V. Dicey's Classic Formulation (1885)
The Law of the Constitution | Primary meaning + 3 Postulates
📝 Dicey was an English constitutional scholar who, in 1885, described the "Rule of Law" as the foundation of England's constitutional system. His 3 pillars became globally famous — though later heavily criticised.
PRIMARY MEANING
"The absolute supremacy or predominance of regular law as opposed to the influence of arbitrary power and excludes the existence of arbitrariness, of prerogative, or even wide discretionary authority on the part of the government."
📝 Everything the government does must be authorised by law. No official can act beyond what law permits. No person punished except for a breach of established law, before ordinary courts.
Dicey's 3 Postulates of Rule of Law
POSTULATE 1
Absence of Arbitrary Power
No man punishable except for a distinct breach of law established in an ordinary legal manner before ordinary courts
📝 Government cannot punish you by fiat. No jail, no fine, no penalty — unless an ordinary court of law says you broke a specific law. No arbitrary arrests, no detention by executive order alone.
💡 GST context: A GST officer cannot arrest an assessee merely on suspicion — there must be specific grounds under Section 69 CGST Act and prescribed procedure. Arbitrary arrest = violation of Rule of Law.
POSTULATE 2
Equality Before Law
"Equal subjection of all classes to the ordinary law of the land administered by the ordinary law courts"
No man above law; every person subject to ordinary courts regardless of rank or condition
📝 The same law applies to a king, a minister, a tax officer, and a common man. All must answer to the same ordinary courts. No special privileges for government officials.
💡 Tax officer who extorts — faces the same criminal court as anyone else. No special tribunal. This is equality before law in action.
POSTULATE 3
Constitution is Result, Not Source
Constitutional law is not the source but the result of rights of individuals defined and enforced by courts
(Contrast with France — constitution declared rights; in England, courts defined rights through decisions)
📝 In England, rights were not written in a constitution — they came from courts protecting individuals in case after case. Rights grew bottom-up from litigation, not top-down from a constitutional document.
DICEY's OPPOSITION — Droit Administratif (French System)
Dicey vehemently criticised the French system of separate administrative tribunals (where government officials were tried separately, not by ordinary courts). He called this system "despotic" and designed to protect guilty administrative officials. He asserted having separate bodies for government disputes was "utterly unknown to the laws of England, and fundamentally inconsistent with our traditions and customs."
📝 Droit Administratif = French system of separate administrative courts. Dicey thought it protected corrupt officials and was un-English. Ironically, later scholars found Droit Administratif was actually more effective at controlling the administration than England's system.
2.2A — Criticisms of Dicey's Rule of Law
🔴 Why Dicey's Formulation is Criticised — 5 Major Defects
📝 Dicey's 3 postulates were brilliant for 1885 — but they described an idealistic England that never fully existed. Modern scholars have identified 5 main problems with his formulation.
DEFECT 1
Factually Wrong About England
Dicey ignored Crown's privileges & immunities. King could do no wrong — the whole government was protected. He ignored administrative tribunals already existing by 1885.
📝 Even in 1885, England was not the paradise of Rule of Law that Dicey claimed. The Crown and its officials enjoyed extensive immunities — Dicey simply ignored this.
DEFECT 2
Discretionary Powers are Inescapable
Dicey treated discretionary powers as incompatible with Rule of Law. But modern governance makes wide discretionary powers inescapable — welfare state cannot function without them.
📝 Today every government officer exercises discretion — granting licences, assessing tax, granting bail. Without discretion, government would grind to a halt. Dicey's all-or-nothing view is impractical.
💡 GST officer's discretion to extend time, condone delay, grant refund — all discretionary. The solution is not to eliminate discretion but to confine it within proper safeguards (proper reasons, opportunity of hearing).
DEFECT 3
Tribunals are Not Rule of Law Violations
Dicey deprecated creation of tribunals as negating Rule of Law. But tribunals have proliferated in every country — and are accepted as CONSISTENT with Rule of Law if they control admin powers & give quick justice.
💡 GSTAT (GST Appellate Tribunal), CESTAT (Customs), ITAT (Income Tax) — all provide faster, specialised justice. These are not enemies of Rule of Law; they are tools to enforce it.
DEFECT 4
Misjudged Droit Administratif
Dicey's view that French Droit Administratif was inferior and protective of officials proved wrong. Later scholars found it MORE effective in controlling the administration and protecting individual rights than England's system.
DEFECT 5
Government ≠ Citizens in All Respects
Dicey assumed complete equality between government and citizens — fallacious. Government enjoys many powers/privileges citizens don't. Inequalities are inherent in the very nature of political society.
📝 A tax officer can search your premises. A citizen cannot search the officer's office. Perfect equality between state and citizen is a legal fiction — the state has sovereign powers that individuals don't.
MODERN UNDERSTANDING
Rather than saying "Rule of Law prevails in no contemporary society," it is more fruitful to emphasise the basic values inherent in the concept: absence of arbitrary power, equality before law, legal protection of basic human rights. Rule of Law remains a potent instrument — a touchstone and standard to test the Administrative Law of a country and advocate for its reform.
2.2B — Four Values Derivable from Rule of Law
🌟 Four Positive Values Derivable from Rule of Law
Modern Administrative Law — Substantive Content
📝 Even if Dicey's exact formulations are criticised, the concept of Rule of Law gives four practical values that administrative lawyers and courts apply regularly to test and improve government action.
VALUE 1
No Power Outside Law
Administration enjoys no power outside law. Bureaucracy confined within legal parameters. No inherent powers — only what is derived from law. No one deprived of right, liberty or property without authority of law.
📝 A government officer has ONLY the powers that a specific statute gives him. Nothing more. Any action beyond statutory powers = ultra vires = void.
💡 GST officer demands documents not specified in Section 70 CGST Act → beyond authority → assessee can challenge the demand as ultra vires. This is Rule of Law in action.
VALUE 2
Supremacy of Courts & Judicial Review
Judicial power cannot be transferred to another agency — that would negate Rule of Law. Courts must continue to control administrative action. Court-review power should not be diluted. No official should have final, unreviewable decision-making power.
📝 Every decision of every government authority — including tax assessments — must be open to challenge before courts. The right to judicial review cannot be taken away. An authority that is final arbiter of its own powers violates Rule of Law.
💡 A provision that bars appeal against GST officer's order — suspect under Rule of Law. Courts in India have held judicial review is a basic feature of the Constitution that cannot be removed even by constitutional amendment.
VALUE 3
Discretionary vs Arbitrary Power
Distinction must be drawn between discretionary powers (permissible — must be exercised lawfully with proper reasons) and arbitrary powers (impermissible — uncontrolled, unaccountable, unrestrained). Officials can have discretion but NOT arbitrary power.
📝 Discretion means you have a choice — but that choice must be guided by law, reasons, fairness. Arbitrary means you do whatever you want. Discretion guided by law is lawful; discretion that becomes whim is arbitrary and illegal.
💡 GST officer has discretion under Section 54 to grant/refuse refund. If he refuses without reasons, it becomes arbitrary = violates Rule of Law = court will quash. Lord Mansfield: "Discretion means sound discretion guided by law. It must be governed by rule, not humour."
VALUE 4
Implied Limitations on Administrative Power
Administrative Law seeks to explore limitations over and above what statute imposes. Admin Law is not retarding but promoting Rule of Law — discourages arbitrary powers, balances public power vs private interests, ensures exercise of admin powers in a lawful manner, provides redressal when individual is hurt.
📝 Even where a statute is silent on procedural safeguards, courts read in implied obligations — like the duty to give reasons, duty to hear the affected party (audi alteram partem). Administrative Law does not infringe Rule of Law; it promotes it.
💡 Section 74 CGST Act gives officer power to issue SCN for fraud. But even if statute does not say "give personal hearing," courts have read this obligation into the statute as an implied limitation under natural justice / Rule of Law.
2.2C — Rule of Law in India — Article 14 & Constitutional Position
🇮🇳 Rule of Law in India — Not Abstract; Concretised in Constitution
Article 14 | Judicial Review | Basic Structure Doctrine
📝 Unlike England where Rule of Law is a common law concept, in India it is written into the Constitution — primarily through Article 14. Courts have given it teeth by using it to strike down arbitrary government actions.
ARTICLE 14 — THE CORE INSTRUMENT
In India, the Rule of Law is not a mere abstract concept — it is concretised in Article 14.
Article 14 guarantees: (i) equality before law and (ii) equal protection of law to every person.
Courts have derived the concept of Rule of Law from Article 14 and invoked it to invalidate any unreasonable, arbitrary or discriminatory administrative action — on the premise that such action amounts to denial of equality.
If an action is arbitrary → unreasonable → negates equal protection of Article 14 → struck down.
Judicial Review — 'Basic Feature' of the Constitution
Judicial review = an essential part of Rule of Law. In India, so much importance is given to it that it has been characterised as the 'basic feature' of the Constitution which cannot be done away with even by the exercise of constituent power.
All actions of state public authorities and bureaucracy are subject to judicial review — accountable to courts for legality of their actions.
📝 The Supreme Court cannot be stripped of its power to review government actions even if Parliament passes a constitutional amendment to do so. This was established in Kesavananda Bharati (1973) and confirmed in many later cases.
Key Supreme Court Articulations of Rule of Law in India
A.K. Kraipak v. UOI (AIR 1970 SC 150)
Hedge J: "In a welfare State, the organ of the State under our Constitution is regulated and controlled by the rule of law. The concept of rule of law would lose its vitality if the instrumentalities of the State are not charged with the duty of discharging their functions in a fair and just manner."
A.D.M. Jabalpur v. S. Shukla (AIR 1976 SC 1207)
Khanna J (dissenting): "Rule of law is the antithesis of arbitrariness... Rule of Law is now the accepted norm of all civilized societies. Everywhere, it is identified with the liberty of the individual. Such harmonizing can only be attained by the existence of independent courts which can hold the balance between citizen and the State."
Indira Gandhi v. Raj Narain (AIR 1975 SC 2299)
Rule of Law concept discussed as basic constitutional value; government under law, not rule of men; Constitution itself is the rule of law — "No one can rise above the rule of law in the Constitution."
Kantaru Rajeevaru (Sabarimala Temple Review-5J) (2020) 2 SCC 1
SC held Rule of Law is a fundamental constitutional value — State must devise modalities to support court decisions; preservation of Rule of Law is paramount; any organized acts of resistance to court judgments must be met with stiff action.
MODERN CONTENT OF RULE OF LAW IN INDIA
Rule of Law connotes a higher kind of law which is reasonable, just and non-discriminatory. Constitutional values — constitutionalism, absence of arbitrary power, liberty of people, independent judiciary — are imbibed in the concept. Our Constitution envisages a rule of law, not rule of men. In a system governed by Rule of Law, there is nothing like absolute or unbridled power exercisable at the whims and fancies of the repository of power — discretion must be exercised along well-recognised juristic principles promoting fairness, transparency and equity.
2.3.1 — Growth of Administrative Law — England
🏴󠁧󠁢󠁥󠁮󠁧󠁿 Growth of Administrative Law — England
From Dicey's Denial → Developed Branch of Law
📝 England's journey is ironic — Dicey denied Administrative Law existed there, and his thesis actually delayed its growth for decades. Yet England eventually developed a sophisticated system through two channels: judicial creativity and legislation.
Two Channels of Growth of Administrative Law in England
CHANNEL 1
Judicial Creativity
PRE-1963 — DARK PHASE
Courts legitimised delegation of vast powers to Administration without paying heed to safeguards against misuse. Courts "showed no inclination to indulge in fisticuffs with the Government." Admin lawyers were frustrated.
1963 ONWARDS — TRANSFORMATION
Landmark Ridge v. Baldwin [1963] initiated new phase. Judges started playing creative and dynamic role. Courts rendered "yeoman service" in shaping Admin Law — discouraged arbitrariness, developed principles to regulate administrative behaviour.
📝 Ridge v. Baldwin (1963) — a police constable dismissed without hearing. House of Lords held: even if not expressly required, natural justice must be observed. This opened the floodgates of judicial creativity in Admin Law.
Lord Denning in Breen v. A.E.U. (1971): "It may now truly be said that we have a developed system of Administrative Law."
CHANNEL 2
Legislation
1929 — Donoughmore Committee
Lord Hewart's book "The New Despotism" warned bureaucracy had become true rulers. Committee appointed to consider admin powers — delegated legislation + quasi-judicial decisions.
1946 — Statutory Instruments Act
Rules for publication of statutory instruments + procedure before Parliament. Called a "comprehensive procedural code for making of subordinate legislation."
1947 — Crown Proceedings Act
Liberalised law relating to civil proceedings against the Government — made it liable to be sued in courts for contractual and tortious liabilities.
1958 — Tribunals & Inquiries Act
Post-Crichel Down Affair + Franks Committee Report (1957). Procedural improvements in working of tribunals and inquiries. Council on Tribunals appointed to supervise.
1967 — Ombudsman (Parliamentary Commissioner)
Adopted after Whyatt Report (1961) — judicial control inadequate; needed supplementing by institutional arrangements.
1977 onwards — Law Commission & Reforms
Single "application for judicial review" procedure introduced. Rule of standing liberalised. Prerogative orders (mandamus, certiorari, prohibition) + declaration + injunction available under single application.
RESULT
Administrative Law — once characterised as "continental jargon" in England — has now become an identifiable and developed branch of law. The negative influence of Dicey's assertions was overcome through both judicial creativity and legislation.
2.3.2 — Growth of Administrative Law — Australia
🦘 Growth of Administrative Law — Australia
Systematic, Government-sponsored Reform — Most Comprehensive in Common-Law World
📝 Australia has done what England couldn't — the Government itself actively sponsored comprehensive reforms. New institutions were created to oversee the bureaucracy beyond what courts alone could achieve.
Timeline of Australian Administrative Law Reforms
1968
Kerr Committee
Attorney General appointed Administrative Review Committee to make comprehensive study of the prevailing system of Administrative Law.
1971
Kerr Committee report suggested: time has come when a general system of Administrative Law should be introduced in Australia → led to several institutional recommendations.
1975
Administrative Appeals Tribunal Act
Established AAT — a "super-tribunal" hearing appeals from a wide variety of administrative decisions on merits. Radical concept — reviews the actual merits, not just legality.
💡 Comparable to GSTAT in India but with wider jurisdiction — reviews facts and merits, not just legal questions.
1976
Ombudsman Act
Parliamentary Ombudsman established to deal with administrative grievances.
1977
Administrative Decisions (Judicial Review) Act
Federal Court given jurisdiction to review federal administrative action. Single, liberal test of standing — applicant be aggrieved. Grounds of judicial review listed. Obligation to give reasons imposed on decision makers. Freedom of Information Act enacted.
📝 Obligation to give reasons — extremely significant. A decision without reasons can be challenged. This has been adopted in India too through judicial decisions (orally communicated reasons = no reasons).
Ongoing
Administrative Review Council
Standing body to keep constant review over admin procedures — recommends to minister improvements to ensure discretions are exercised justly & equitably.
2.3.3 — Growth of Administrative Law — New Zealand
🥝 Growth of Administrative Law — New Zealand
Pioneer — First Common-Law Country to Adopt Ombudsman (1962)
1962
First common-law country to adopt Scandinavian Ombudsman system — had tremendous impact on other common-law countries in making this institution acceptable.
1967
Public and Administrative Law Reform Committee established — systematic review of Administrative Law; functioned for several years; claimed status comparable to Council on Tribunals in England.
Key Innovation
Administrative Division in the Supreme Court
Created to promote expertise and specialisation — judges deal with Admin Law problems repeatedly, developing skill and consistency of judicial policy. Appeals from many tribunals lie here.
📝 Specialized judges = better, more consistent Admin Law. Similar to how GSTAT will develop GST law expertise over time — judges repeatedly seeing the same issues become more consistent and expert.
"Application for Judicial Review"
Adopted as an additional remedy — applicant can get any relief (writ, injunction, declaration or combination) under single application. Reformed tribunal system and regulation-making procedures.
2.3.4 — Growth of Administrative Law — Canada
🍁 Growth of Administrative Law — Canada
Law Reform Commission of Canada studied problems of Canadian Administrative Law — prepared in-depth studies of several administrative agencies and made recommendations to make administrative practices fairer, more efficient, effective and more accountable to the public.
SIGNIFICANCE
Canada's developments show a common-law world trend: the influence of Dicey has "worn thin." Mere court review does not provide effective control-mechanism over administrative functioning. Some features of Droit Administratif need to be incorporated into the common-law system for more effective accountability.
2.3.5 — Growth of Administrative Law — India
🇮🇳 Growth of Administrative Law — India
Creative Judicial Role + Proliferation of Tribunals + Constitutional Foundation
✓ POSITIVE DEVELOPMENTS
Courts have played a very creative role in developing principles of Administrative Law — taken note of in the following pages of the book.
Proliferation of tribunal system — Parliament has enacted laws to establish a number of tribunals which have taken over some functions from courts (e.g. CESTAT, ITAT, NCLT, GSTAT).
Lokayukta mechanisms (Ombudsman equivalent) established in some states.
Constitutional foundation: all organs of government — elected legislature, appointed executive, selected judiciary — must be conscious that power given to them is to serve and not to rule. Judiciary given constitutional power to uphold constitutional values and control administration.
✗ DEFICIENCIES
Parliament has not enacted any comprehensive Act for Administrative Reforms despite Administrative Reforms Commission proposing this as early as 1967.
Government and legislature have not evinced much interest in improving the law and developing mechanism to control administrative authorities.
India remains as yet untouched by the innovative developments taking place in other common-law countries (ombudsman at national level, systematic review of administrative discretions, freedom of information, etc.).
📝 India has a Central Information Commission under RTI Act (2005) — which is a partial step towards Freedom of Information. But comprehensive administrative reform similar to Australia/UK is still absent.
🏛️ India's Unique Constitutional Democracy Framework
India has a long history of people being in slavery and subjugation — Mughal rulers then British rule for 150+ years. On attaining freedom, people of India resolved to be governed by democratic rule with sovereignty vested in people. In a constitutional democracy like India, all organs of Government — elected legislature, appointed executive, selected judiciary — have to be constantly conscious that power given to them is to serve and not to rule.

The limited decision-making power given to executives is to be exercised in the best possible manner in the interest of the people — not for satisfaction of policy makers or law-making bodies.
💡 Litigation context: When a GST officer issues an SCN that is a copy-paste of previous orders without applying mind — this violates the constitutional duty of a public servant to serve the people. Courts have quashed such SCNs under Article 14 / Rule of Law principles.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter II | Constitutional Principles & Administrative Law
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
A.K. Kraipak v. Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1970 SC 150 | Hedge J
Key Ratio
"In a welfare State like ours it is inevitable that the organ of the State under our Constitution is regulated and controlled by the rule of law. In a welfare State like ours it is inevitable that the jurisdiction of the administrative bodies is increasing at a rapid rate. The concept of rule of law would lose its validity if the instrumentalities of the State are not charged with the duty of discharging their functions in a fair and just manner."
When to Use — When GST/tax officer acts unfairly — summarily dismisses reply, does not give personal hearing, passes non-speaking order. Cite: the instrumentalities of the State are constitutionally required to discharge functions in a fair and just manner. Any deviation violates Rule of Law under Article 14.
A.D.M. Jabalpur v. S. Shukla
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1976 SC 1207 | Khanna J (dissenting) | 2 SCC 521
Key Ratio — Khanna J
"Rule of law is the antithesis of arbitrariness... Rule of Law is now the accepted norm of all civilized societies... Everywhere, it is identified with the liberty of the individual. It seeks to maintain a balance between the opposing notions of individual liberty and public order. In every State, the problem arises of reconciling human rights with the requirements of public interest. Such harmonizing can only be attained by the existence of independent courts which can hold the balance between citizen and the State and compel governments to conform to the law."
When to Use — In appeals before GST Appellate Authority or GSTAT when Department argues that tax demand must prevail over assessee's rights. Cite: Rule of Law requires balancing of public interest with individual liberty. Independent adjudicating authorities must hold the balance, not merely rubber-stamp departmental demands.
Lord Mansfield's Dictum on Discretion
English Common Law | John Wilkes, In Re (1770) 4 Burr 2528 | Lord Mansfield
Key Ratio
"Discretion means sound discretion guided by law. It must be governed by rule, not humour; it must not be arbitrary, vague and fanciful."
When to Use — When GST officer exercises discretion adversely (e.g., rejecting extension application, denying refund, levying maximum penalty) without giving reasons. Cite: discretion must be guided by law and reasons — not by whim, personal preference or departmental pressure. A discretionary order without reasons is arbitrary = violates Rule of Law.
Douglas J Dictum on Law vs Absolute Discretion
United States Supreme Court | United States v. Wunderlich (1951) | Douglas J
Key Ratio
"Law has reached its finest moments when it has freed man from unlimited discretion of some ruler... where discretion is absolute, man has always suffered."
When to Use — When challenging assessments or penalties where officer has exercised absolute discretion without any guidance of law or reason. Cite: absolute discretion = suffering for the citizen. The proper check is: reasons, law, proportionality.
Bachan Singh v. State of Punjab
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1982 SC 1325 | para 10 | 3 SCC 24
Key Ratio
Absence of arbitrary power is the first essential of Rule of Law upon which our whole constitutional system is based. The extent of judicial review of a decision is tested on aspects of arbitrary exercise of power, non-application of minds or malafides.
When to Use — When challenging SCN or assessment order on ground that officer did not apply mind, passed a cyclostyled order, or acted with ulterior motive. Cite: non-application of mind = arbitrary exercise of power = violates Rule of Law = grounds for judicial review.
Kantaru Rajeevaru (Sabarimala Temple Review-5J) v. Indian Young Lawyers Association
Supreme Court of India | (2020) 2 SCC 1
Key Ratio
Rule of Law is a fundamental constitutional value. The State shall devise modalities to support any decision of courts that aids to bring about a major change. Preservation of the Rule of Law, being paramount and human dignity, thwart the judgment. In sensitive cases that are perceived as offending religious sensibilities but which go to advance transformational changes, the State authorities must give wide publicity and administer stiff action against organized acts of resistance to the content of judgment.
When to Use — When Appellate Authority or GST officer defies or ignores a binding judicial precedent on the ground that it is inconvenient or departmentally unacceptable. Cite: Rule of Law requires State to support and implement court decisions — selective implementation or organised resistance to judgments is constitutionally impermissible.
Holdsworth Principle — Courts Must Control Administrative Excess
Academic Principle | 55 LQR 587 | Applied by Indian Courts
Key Principle
The importance of Rule of Law, in so far as it means that the courts can see that administrative officials do not exceed their powers, and that the rights of the citizens are determined in accordance with the law — ousting the court's jurisdiction and giving of a purely administrative discretion abrogates the Rule of Law.
When to Use — When challenging ouster clauses in statutes or when Department argues that an officer's decision is final and not challengeable in court. Cite: Ousting court's jurisdiction = abrogating Rule of Law. Every administrative decision must be judicially reviewable on questions of law — this is a basic feature of the Indian Constitution.
Dicey's Rule of Law — Absence of Arbitrary Power (Administrative Law Principle)
Constitutional Principle | A.V. Dicey, The Law of the Constitution (1885) | Applied Universally in Indian Administrative Law
Key Principle
The first basic value derivable from the Rule of Law is that the administration does not enjoy any power outside the law. Bureaucratic powers are to be confined within legal parameters. Administration does not have any inherent powers of its own — it can enjoy such powers only as emanate or are derived from law. The bureaucracy has to keep itself within the confines of law and it can exercise no power which is not sanctioned by law. This is the cardinal principle operative in a common-law country and leads to the application of the Doctrine of ultra vires and judicial control of administrative action.
When to Use — Foundational argument in any SCN reply or appeal where Department has acted without statutory authority, relied on a circular which goes beyond the Act, or demanded tax not authorised by the CGST/IGST Act. Cite: no power outside law — bureaucracy can only exercise powers explicitly conferred by statute — circular/instruction expanding scope of levy is ultra vires = void.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter II (Part 2) — Separation of Powers & Droit Administratif

⚖️ Chapter II (Part 2) — Separation of Powers & Droit Administratif

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Positive
Criticism / Limitation / Problem
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative
Neutral Reference / Concept
Country / Jurisdiction-specific
📝 Plain English
💡 Example / Case
📋 Index of Sections — Chapter II (Part 2)
2.4 Separation of Powers — Core Doctrine
2.4.1 Separation of Powers — USA
2.4.1A Administrative Procedure Act 1946 (USA)
2.4.2 Separation of Powers — India
2.4.2.1 NJAC — 99th Constitutional Amendment & Collegium
2.4.2.2 Quasi-Judicial Tribunals in India
2.5 Droit Administratif (French System)
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
2.4 — Doctrine of Separation of Powers — Core Concept
⚖️ Doctrine of Separation of Powers
Traceable to Montesquieu | Mutual Exclusiveness of 3 Organs
📝 The idea is simple: if one person or body holds ALL power — to make laws, enforce them AND judge disputes — they will abuse it. Split the power into three separate organs and each acts as a check on the other.
CORE PREMISE — Montesquieu
Each of the three organs should exercise only one type of function:
Legislature → Legislative function only
Executive → Executive function only
Judiciary → Judicial function only
Concentration of all functions in one organ = threat to personal freedom; can enact tyrannical laws and execute them despotically without external control.
📝 Montesquieu was a French philosopher who, in the 18th century, was so alarmed by the arbitrary powers of French kings that he designed the theory of Separation of Powers as a safeguard against tyranny.
PURPOSE OF THE DOCTRINE
(i) Diffuse governmental authority — prevent absolutism
(ii) Guard against tyrannical and arbitrary powers of the State
(iii) Allocate each function to the institution best suited to discharge it
(iv) If all power concentrated in one organ → danger of State absolutism endangering freedoms of the people
TENSION WITH MODERN ADMINISTRATIVE LAW
Administrative Law and Separation Doctrine are somewhat incompatible.
Modern administrative process envisages mingling of various types of functions at administrative level.
New demands on government → solve complex socio-economic problems → new institutions with blended powers created.
Had Separation Doctrine been applied strictly → growth of administrative process would have been extremely difficult → modern government might have become impossible.
📝 Today, a body like SEBI makes regulations (legislative), enforces them (executive) AND adjudicates violations (judicial) — all in one body. This blends all three functions. Strict Separation is impossible in a modern welfare state.
💡 GST context: GST officer issues SCN (executive), conducts adjudication (quasi-judicial), and the same department frames Rules/Circulars (quasi-legislative). All three functions are blended in the same department — a practical necessity, not a constitutional violation.
MODERN ACCEPTANCE — Chief Value
Separation Doctrine, though not applicable in strict form, is not entirely redundant. Its chief value lies in emphasizing that it is essential to develop adequate checks and balances to prevent administrative arbitrariness.
The danger of tyranny lurks in 'unchecked' power — not in 'blended' power. The more important thing is to have checks and balances.
"Its object is the preservation of political safeguards against the capricious exercise of power... the great end of the theory is, by dispersing in some measure the centres of authority, to prevent absolutism."
2.4.1 — Separation of Powers — USA
🇺🇸 Separation of Powers in USA
Constitutional Foundation + Checks & Balances + Impact on Admin Law
US Constitutional Structure — 3-Way Separation (Articles I, II, III)
ARTICLE I
Congress
Legislative power vested in Congress. President is NOT a member of Congress. Strict separation of legislative and executive organs.
ARTICLE II
President
Executive power vested in President. Both Head of State and Chief Executive. Appoints/dismisses executive officers. NOT bound to accept Congress's legislative agenda. Can be removed only by cumbersome impeachment.
ARTICLE III
Supreme Court
Judicial power vested in Supreme Court at apex. Independence constitutionally guaranteed. Appointed by President with Senate consent — a check and balance.
Checks and Balances — Exceptions to Strict Separation
Congress bill can be vetoed by President → President exercises legislative function
Senate must approve high official appointments + treaties → Senate exercises executive function
Congress probes into executive functioning through committees; power to tax/sanction money → checks executive
Supreme Court can declare Acts of Congress unconstitutional → checks legislative power
📝 Checks and balances = deliberate overlapping of functions so each organ can restrain the other. The danger sought to be averted is that an organ having one type of power may exercise it arbitrarily or unwisely if left completely uncontrolled.
Key Case — Buckley v. Valeo, 424 US 1 (1977)
HOLDING
Congressional Act declared unconstitutional as it breached Separation Doctrine — Congress sought to claim administrative power of making appointments to Federal Election Commission.
Court stated: "The Constitution diffuses power the better to secure liberty", though a hermetic sealing off of the three branches from one another would preclude establishment of a Nation capable of governing itself.
📝 Diffuse ≠ hermetically seal. The Constitution wants power spread out — not locked in silos. The point is liberty through distribution, not paralysis through absolute separation.
Key Case — Immigration and Naturalization Service v. Jagdish Rai Chadha, 462 US 919 (1983)
FACTS Section 244(c)(2) of US Immigration Act allowed one House of Congress by resolution to invalidate the decision of the Executive Branch to allow a deportable alien to remain. After AG suspended Chadha's deportation order, House passed a resolution vetoing the suspension. SC held Congressional veto provision unconstitutional.

RATIO — Burger CJ
"The Constitution sought to divide the delegated powers of the new federal government into three defined categories, legislative, executive and judicial, to assure, as nearly as possible, that each Branch of government would confine itself to its assigned responsibility. The hydraulic pressure inherent within each of the separate Branches to exceed the outer limits of its power, even to accomplish desirable objectives, must be resisted."
📝 Each branch has a natural tendency to expand its power — the Constitution's structure is specifically designed to resist that expansion. Even good intentions don't justify crossing constitutional limits.
DOCTRINE OF EXCESSIVE DELEGATION (USA)
A significant breach of Separation Doctrine occurred when courts conceded that legislative power could be conferred on administrative authorities → delegated legislation came in vogue.
To reconcile, courts laid down: Congress cannot confer unlimited legislative power on administrative authority. Congress must not give up its position of primary legislator. Congress should lay down the policy which the delegate follows while making rules.
Delegating statute must contain a framework within which the administrative-delegate has to operate.
If not done → Congress abdicates its function → unconstitutional → emergence of Doctrine of Excessive Delegation.
📝 Parliament/Congress can allow an officer to make detailed rules — but must lay down the policy first. Example: CGST Act allows government to prescribe forms by notification — but the Act itself fixes the framework (registration, assessment procedure, etc.). If the Act gave power to government to impose "any tax in any manner" — that would be excessive delegation = unconstitutional.
💡 In India equivalent: Section 164 CGST Act allows government to make rules. But the CGST Act itself lays down the policy, procedure and framework. The rules must stay within that framework. Rules that go beyond the Act or contradict it = ultra vires the parent Act = void.
2.4.1A — Administrative Procedure Act, 1946 (USA) — Foundation of Federal Admin Law
📜 Administrative Procedure Act, 1946 (USA)
First Legislative Attempt in Common-Law World to Codify Principles of Fair Administrative Procedure
📝 APA 1946 is a landmark — it is the first time any common-law country passed a comprehensive statute laying down minimum procedural standards for all government agencies. Before this, procedure was haphazard. APA brought uniformity and fairness.
What the Administrative Procedure Act Does
✓
Strengthens Hearing Procedures
Provides for a sort of hearing in exercising the function of delegated legislation — ensures agencies hear affected parties before making rules
✓
Strengthens Judicial Review
Provisions for judicial review of administrative action — keeps review available while maintaining flexibility and diversity in administrative process
✓
Separation of Functions within Agency
Provides for some separation of adjudicatory functions from investigation and prosecution within the same agency
📝 The same officer should not investigate AND then adjudicate. The APA separates these roles within one agency.
✓
Lays Down Minimum General Principles
Procedural principles to regulate exercise of powers by ALL agencies. Controls procedures of all administrative tribunals and agencies whose decisions affect person or property of private citizens
HISTORIC SIGNIFICANCE
The APA "represents a moderate adjustment on the side of fairness to the citizens in the never-ending quest, for the proper balance between governmental efficiency and individual freedom."
It represents the first legislative attempt in the common-law world to state essential principles of fair administrative procedure. Its emphasis: "The history of liberty has largely been the history of the observance of procedural safe-guards" (Justice Frankfurter).
💡 Indian parallel: There is no equivalent of APA in India — no single comprehensive statute for administrative procedure. Each department follows its own rules (CGST Rules, Income Tax Rules, etc.). The Kerr Committee (Australia, 1971) recommended Australia should have such a statute — prescribing minimum procedural standards for all tribunals. India still lacks this.
FREEDOM OF INFORMATION ACT, 1967 (USA)
Important step towards open government. Agency must publish orders, interpretations, rules of procedure, policy statements, all documents. General access principle: all agency documents available to public unless covered by specific exceptions.
Burden on agency to sustain withholding — not on citizen to justify access.
Citizens have a legally enforceable right of access to agency files. In case of refusal → District Court jurisdiction.
"No longer is the citizen, seeking information from an administrative agency, a mere suppliant."
📝 India's RTI Act 2005 is inspired by this concept — but the US version is more comprehensive and the burden of proof for withholding is explicitly on the agency. In India, RTI has transformed administrative transparency.
2.4.2 — Separation of Powers — India
🇮🇳 Separation of Powers in India
No Express Provision — But Implicit + Basic Structure of Constitution
POSITION
No express provision for Separation of Powers in Indian Constitution (unlike USA's Articles I, II, III)
BUT functions of different organs have been clearly earmarked so that one organ does not usurp functions of another
Thus some division of powers — Legislative, Executive, Judicial — is implicit in the Constitution
BASIC STRUCTURE
Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
AIR 1973 SC 1461

"Separation of powers between the legislature, executive and the judiciary is a part of the basic structure of the Constitution, this structure cannot be destroyed by any form of amendment."
📝 Basic structure = cannot be removed even by Parliament through constitutional amendment. Separation of powers is so fundamental that even Parliament cannot destroy it.
Checks and Balances in Indian Constitution
Separation of powers creates a system of checks and balances by reason of which, powers are so distributed that none of the three organs can become so pre-dominant as to disable the others from exercising and discharging the powers and functions entrusted to them. The separation of powers between Legislature, Executive and Judiciary constitute one of the basic features of the Indian Constitution.
PARTIAL ENCROACHMENT — PERMISSIBLE IN EXCEPTIONAL CIRCUMSTANCES
These apparently conflicting positions (no strict separation vs. separation as basic structure) indicate: partial encroachment by one organ into field of another is permissible — but ONLY:
(i) in exceptional circumstances, and
(ii) for the limited purpose of protecting the basic fundamental rights of the people.

In practice, Constitution of India does not envisage strict adherence to Separation Doctrine — theory of "checks and balances" is accepted with hope of coordination and cooperation between the three organs of the State to achieve the aim of upholding constitutional values and laws for protection of people's individual and collective rights in democracy.
📝 Think of it this way: each organ stays in its lane normally. But if one organ fails to protect fundamental rights, another can step in temporarily. Courts issue directions to the executive (police reforms, environmental compliance). But they cannot permanently usurp legislative or executive functions.
⚠️ LEGISLATURE CANNOT OVERRULE COURT JUDGMENT BY FIAT
When court declares a legislative or executive action violative of Part III, Legislature/Executive may remove the defect even retrospectively.
BUT: where there is a mere validation with retrospective effect, without the defect being legislatively removed — such legislative action amounts to overruling the judgment of the Courts by way of legislative fiat and will be invalid as being contrary to the Doctrine of Separation of Powers.
📝 Court strikes down a tax. Government cannot simply pass a law saying "the tax was always valid" — that would be overruling the court by legislation, not removing the defect. Government must actually remove the unconstitutional defect in the law, not just declare the court's decision inapplicable.
💡 GST context: If a GST provision is struck down by High Court as unconstitutional, Parliament can amend the provision prospectively (or retrospectively with a valid curative amendment). But Parliament cannot simply pass a validation Act saying "the notice/demand already issued shall be deemed valid" — that would be violating Separation of Powers.
Mutual Culture of Cooperation — Expected Role of Each Organ
LEGISLATURE
Has to be guided from the decisions of the Judiciary. Laws must reflect constitutional values — not contradict them. Cannot delegate essential legislative function.
EXECUTIVE
Has to implement laws — not only in letter but in spirit. Must seek guidance from both laws made by Legislature and decisions of Judiciary. Must act within its allotted sphere.
JUDICIARY
Has to give due consideration to realities and practical difficulties of the Executive implementing laws. Should not lay down policies or make laws. Should act within its allotted sphere — only in exceptional circumstances and to limited extent may trench upon field of other organs.
💡 Administrative circular laying down conditions for stay of orders of Principal Commissioner of Income Tax will NOT regulate or fetter the discretion of the quasi-judicial authority to vary the condition laid down — the circular cannot override the quasi-judicial function. [CIT v LG Electronics India (P) Ltd (2018) 18 SCC 447]
2.4.2.1 — NJAC — 99th Constitutional Amendment, 2014 & Collegium System
🏛️ National Judicial Appointment Commission (NJAC)
99th Constitutional Amendment Act 2014 | Struck Down | Collegium System Restored
📝 This is a classic Separation of Powers battle — between the Executive (wanting a role in appointing judges) and the Judiciary (insisting on primacy in its own appointments). The Supreme Court struck down the NJAC as violating judicial independence.
Timeline — Evolution of Judges Appointment Cases
1st Judges Case
S.P. Gupta v. UOI
AIR 1982 SC 149
7-Judge Bench held: "consultation" by President with CJI does not mean "concurrence" — President's appointment prevails
2nd Judges Case
Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record Assn. v. UOI
AIR 1994 SC 268
9-Judge Bench — OVERRULED 1st case. Held: opinion of Judiciary will have primacy. "Consultation" = Collegium of 5 senior judges (SC) / 2 senior judges (HC)
3rd Judges Case
In re: Appointment and Transfer of Judges
AIR 1999 SC 1
9-Judge Bench — Reaffirmed Collegium system
99th Amendment 2014
Parliament amended Articles 124 & 217 to create NJAC under new Article 124A — replacing Collegium with a Commission having executive representation
NJAC Struck Down
Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record Assn. v. UOI
(2016) 5 SCC 1
5-Judge Bench — 4:1 majority. Entire 99th Constitutional Amendment and NJAC Act 2014 struck down as ultra vires the Constitution. Articles 124 and 217 in original form restored.
NJAC Composition (as per struck-down Amendment)
#MemberCategory
1Chief Justice of IndiaChairperson, Ex-officio
2Two other senior Judges of Supreme Court next to CJIMembers, Ex-officio
3Union Minister In-charge of Law and JusticeMember, Ex-officio
4Two eminent persons nominated by PM + CJI + Leader of OppositionOne must be from SC/ST/OBC/Minorities/Women | 3-year term | No re-nomination
WHY NJAC WAS STRUCK DOWN — Administrative Law Perspective
Main conclusions of SC:
(i) Primacy of judiciary in appointment of judges is an integral part of independence of judiciary — a constituent part of the basic structure of the Constitution.
(ii) "Consultation with CJI" means primacy of the opinion of the CJI expressing through the Collegium of Judges.
(iii) Role of executive in selection can only be participatory — not decisive. Decisive role must repose in the judiciary to insulate it from pressures of the executive.
(iv) Sections 5 & 6 of NJAC Act conferred a veto power on any two Commission members — including non-judicial members — this was incompatible with judicial independence.
📝 The core issue: Can the executive (Law Minister + eminent persons nominated partly by PM) have an effective veto over judicial appointments? SC said NO — because if executive can block appointment of judges who might rule against the government, judicial independence is destroyed.
BALANCED VIEW EXPRESSED IN CHAPTER
The chapter argues the NJAC could have been given a chance to operate for a reasonable period rather than being struck down at the threshold. The responsive model requires combining:
(i) Judicial independence from political branches
(ii) Reasonable degree of political and societal responsibility
(iii) Civil, disciplinary and penal responsibility
A closed Collegium procedure is not preferable to an open, transparent selection in which representatives of people from different walks of life have a say — even to a limited extent. Power without responsibility is incompatible with a democratic system. People are the ultimate stakeholders and consumers of law and justice.
2.4.2.2 — Experiments of Quasi-Judicial Tribunals in India
⚖️ Quasi-Judicial Tribunals in India
Experiment to Reduce Congestion in Courts + Specialized Adjudication
📝 India has created a network of quasi-judicial tribunals to handle specialized disputes (tax, telecom, labour, environment) — so ordinary courts are not overwhelmed. These are not substitutes for judiciary — they supplement it.
WHAT ARE QUASI-JUDICIAL TRIBUNALS
Bodies comprising judicial and expert (non-judicial) members from concerned fields. Set up to hear grievances against administration — taxes, excise, railways, telecom, motor accidents, foreign exchange etc. Their decisions subject to appeal/writ to Constitutional Courts.
💡 Examples: GSTAT (GST), CESTAT (Customs & Excise), ITAT (Income Tax), NCLT (Company Law), TDSAT (Telecom), NGT (Environment), Armed Forces Tribunal, Debt Recovery Tribunal
PROBLEMS
Decisions subject to appeal/writ → congestion merely transferred to regular courts — problem of over-burdening not solved
Various tribunals posts vacant for want of suitable willing former judges and public officers
Experience for government, litigants and lawyers has been disappointing
Rojer Mathew v. South Indian Bank Ltd (2020) 6 SCC 1 — Majority held delegation of legislative power to executive to make rules specifying qualifications, method of selection, terms of office etc. of tribunal members is permissible. But Chandrachud J and Deepak Gupta J dissented — qualification of members and essential attributes of persons to occupy such posts as Chairpersons and Members of tribunals are essential part of legislative function which could not be delegated.
PROPOSED SOLUTION
Instead of separate infrastructure for each tribunal — create a specialized quasi-judicial forum for each department with simplified procedures: flexible, speedy, informal, socially and technically conscious
For each department in government and public undertakings — independent quasi-judicial forum comprising judges and experts from outside the department should be set up to hear grievances
Such quasi-judicial bodies would NOT be substitutes of judiciary — appeal/review remains with District Court, High Court, Supreme Court
📝 Like having a GST-specific quasi-judicial forum at the district level — comprising a retired judge + GST expert — to hear taxpayer grievances before they escalate to High Courts. Quick, specialized, accessible.
KEY JUDICIAL LIMITS ON TRIBUNAL/LEGISLATIVE INTERFERENCE
UOI v. All India Trade Union Congress (2019) 5 SCC 773 — SC limited its own power; set aside HC order interfering with a scheme for regularisation of causal labourers

State of HP v. Satpal Saini (2017) 11 SCC 42 — Direction to amend/enact a legislative enactment to HP Tenancy and Land Reform Rules was impermissible — violates Separation of Powers

Administrative circular laying down conditions for stay of orders of Principal Commissioner of Income Tax will NOT regulate or fetter the discretion of the quasi-judicial authority — circular cannot override quasi-judicial function [CIT v LG Electronics India (P) Ltd (2018) 18 SCC 447]
2.5 — Droit Administratif (French System of Administrative Law)
🇫🇷 Droit Administratif — French System
Product of Separation of Powers | Administration Independent of Ordinary Courts
📝 Droit Administratif = Administrative Law in France. The key difference from common-law countries: in France, ordinary courts have NO control over government actions.
