There are completely separate administrative courts/tribunals. Dicey hated this system — but later scholars found it was actually better at protecting citizens.
CORE FEATURE — AUTONOMY OF ADMINISTRATION FROM ORDINARY COURTS
Droit Administratif is a product of the Doctrine of Separation of Powers — applied more strictly in France than in USA.
Separation theory in France has resulted in non-interference by ordinary courts in working of administrative authorities.
Ordinary courts exercise NO control over administrative functioning.
A person has no avenue for redressal against administration through the ordinary courts.
The most original aspect of French Administrative Law = independence of Administration from judicial control.
📝 In England/India: if government acts illegally, you go to High Court and challenge it. In France: if government acts illegally, you cannot go to the ordinary civil courts — you must go to a completely separate system of administrative courts/tribunals.
The Conseil d'État — France's Supreme Administrative Court
Composition
Mostly civil servants (can be dismissed by political authorities in theory). But in practice, because of emergence of certain practices and conventions, very independent. Composed of cream of French Civil Service.
Powers
Court of appeal for all other admin tribunals. ALL decisions of administrative tribunals subject to its review on points of law. Court of first instance for recours pour excès de pouvoir (excess of power) against decrees of Administration.
📝 Recours pour excès de pouvoir = Action to quash administrative decision for excess of power. Similar to writ of certiorari in common-law countries.
Characteristics
Achieved high degree of objectivity. Characterised as "bulwark of civil liberties" and "guardian of administrative morality." Can supervise form, content and grounds of administrative decisions. Can grant damages when administration causes injury.
Tribunal des Conflits
Separate body established to decide whether a matter should go before ordinary courts or administrative tribunals. Has judges and civil servants in equal numbers with Minister of Justice as President.
TWO PRINCIPAL LIMITATIONS ON ADMINISTRATIVE BODIES
The Administrative Tribunals in France have spelled out two principal limitations:
(i) These bodies must not act against the law
(ii) They must pay damages when they cause injuries
If administrative action is ultra vires → it can be nullified by the tribunal by recours pour excès de pouvoir on action brought by affected private citizen. Scope of this action is very broad — it constitutes the best means to protect citizens against abuse of power. Conseil can supervise the form and content of administrative decisions, and also the grounds on which action is taken.
Droit Administratif vs Common Law System — Final Assessment
ParameterDroit Administratif (France)Common Law System (England/India)
Forum for Admin disputes Separate administrative courts (Conseil d'État) Ordinary courts (High Court, Supreme Court)
Control over administration More effective — deep and broad range of control Limited — courts can only review on narrow grounds
Expertise of judges Administrative experts — cream of Civil Service General judges — may lack admin expertise
Individual protection Considered better at protecting individuals against despotism of public administration Adequate but less comprehensive
Dicey's view Called it "despotic" — designed to protect guilty officials Called it ideal — equality before same courts
Modern scholarly view Found to stand on higher footing in some respects — more satisfactory than common-law system Dicey was factually wrong — Droit Administratif is more effective
Transparency / Coherence High coherence — single apex Conseil unifies all admin law Piecemeal — scattered decisions in many courts
CONCLUSION  Gradually, some elements of Droit Administratif are filtering into the common-law system. Some British scholars have suggested importing Conseil d'État into England. The crucial test is not who decides — but how effectively the administration is controlled and whether the citizen has an adequate redressal mechanism when hurt by administrative action. From both these tests, Droit Administratif is found more satisfactory. Dicey had an utter lack of perception into the system.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter II (Part 2) | Separation of Powers & Droit Administratif
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Kesavananda Bharati Sripadagavaru v. State of Kerala
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1973 SC 1461 | (1973) 4 SCC 225 | 13-Judge Constitution Bench
Key Ratio — Separation of Powers as Basic Structure
"Separation of powers between the legislature, executive and the judiciary is a part of the basic structure of the Constitution, this structure cannot be destroyed by any form of amendment."
When to Use — When challenging legislative validation of orders already quashed by courts, or when Department argues that a circular/rule overrides a court judgment. Cite: Separation of Powers is a basic structure of the Constitution. Legislature cannot simply overrule a court judgment by fiat — it must remove the defect in the law, not declare the court wrong.
Buckley v. Valeo — Separation of Powers (US Supreme Court)
US Supreme Court | 424 US 1 (1977)
Key Ratio
"The Constitution diffuses power the better to secure liberty", though "a hermetic sealing off of the three branches of Government from one another would preclude the establishment of a Nation capable of governing itself."
When to Use — In arguments about blended functions (same authority investigating + adjudicating). Cite: Separation is not about hermetic sealing — it is about diffusing power for liberty. The key safeguard is checks and balances, not absolute silos. However, the investigative and adjudicative functions should be separated within the same agency wherever possible.
Immigration and Naturalization Service v. Jagdish Rai Chadha — Burger CJ
US Supreme Court | 462 US 919 (1983)
Key Ratio
"The Constitution sought to divide the delegated powers of the new federal government into three defined categories, legislative, executive and judicial, to assure, as nearly as possible, that each Branch of government would confine itself to its assigned responsibility. The hydraulic pressure inherent within each of the separate Branches to exceed the outer limits of its power, even to accomplish desirable objectives, must be resisted."
When to Use — When executive authority (tax department) exceeds its statutory mandate and performs quasi-legislative acts (issuing circulars/instructions that go beyond the parent Act). Cite: each organ must confine itself to its assigned responsibility. A departmental circular that adds to or modifies the tax liability under the Act is ultra vires — the executive exceeds its constitutional limits.
Indira Sawhney v. UOI — Legislative Overruling of Court by Fiat
Supreme Court of India | AIR 2000 SC 498 | (2000) 1 SCC 168
Key Ratio
Where there is a mere validation with retrospective effect, without the defect being legislatively removed with retrospective effect, the legislative action will amount to overruling the judgment of the Courts, by way of legislative fiat and will be invalid as being contrary to the Doctrine of Separation of Powers.
When to Use — When Government/Department relies on a retrospective validation amendment to revive demands already struck down by courts, or when it passes a curative provision that merely declares the struck-down action valid without removing the defect. Cite: legislature cannot override a court judgment by legislative fiat — it must remove the actual constitutional defect.
Doctrine of Excessive Delegation — No Unlimited Legislative Power on Executive
Constitutional Principle | USA + India | Accepted in Indian Constitutional Law
Key Principle
Congress/Parliament cannot confer an unlimited legislative power on an administrative authority. Congress/Parliament must not give up its position of primary legislator. The delegating statute must contain a framework within which the administrative-delegate has to operate. If this is not done, then the primary legislator abdicates its function to the delegate — and this is unconstitutional. This has led to the emergence of the Doctrine of Excessive Delegation.
When to Use — When challenging a rule/notification that goes beyond the framework of the parent Act, or when Department relies on a Rule or Circular that exceeds the scope of delegation in the main statute. Cite: every delegation must have a framework — if a rule travels beyond the framework of the parent Act, it is an exercise in excessive delegation = ultra vires = void.
CIT v. LG Electronics India (P) Ltd — Circular Cannot Fetter Quasi-Judicial Discretion
Supreme Court of India | (2018) 18 SCC 447
Key Ratio
Administrative circular laying down conditions for stay of orders of Principal Commissioner of Income Tax will not regulate or fetter the discretion of the quasi-judicial authority to vary the condition laid down by the circular.
When to Use — When Department argues that an assessee cannot get stay because of conditions prescribed in a departmental circular — and the circular is being used to restrain the quasi-judicial authority's discretion. Cite: a circular cannot fetter the discretion of a quasi-judicial authority. The quasi-judicial function operates independently of executive instructions. Adjudicating officer must apply his own mind — he cannot be bound by a circular to refuse stay.
Droit Administratif Principle — Effective Control Over Administration
French Constitutional Principle | Discussed in Principles of Administrative Law | Jain
Key Principle
The crucial test for any system of Administrative Law is not whether ordinary courts decide all controversies, but whether it effectively controls administrative powers and whether it provides an effective redressal mechanism to the individual, in case he feels aggrieved by administrative action. From this point of view, Droit Administratif has been found in some respects to stand on a higher footing than the common-law system of Administrative Law. The real test is how effectively the administrative powers are controlled and how effective is the control mechanism.
When to Use — In writ petitions or before appellate authorities when arguing that the adjudicating/appellate mechanism is ineffective in controlling the administration. Cite: the constitutional standard for any quasi-judicial body is not merely formal compliance — but whether it provides an effective control mechanism and effective redressal for the aggrieved citizen. A rubber-stamp adjudicating authority that never decides against the department fails this constitutional standard.
Rojer Mathew v. South Indian Bank Ltd — Tribunal Member Qualifications — Dissent of Chandrachud J
Supreme Court of India | (2020) 6 SCC 1 | Chandrachud J & Deepak Gupta J (dissenting)
Key Ratio — Dissent (stronger on constitutional principle)
Qualification of members of tribunal could not be delegated. That what should be the essential qualification and attributes of persons to occupy such posts as Chairpersons and Members of tribunals are an essential part of legislative function which could not be delegated to the executive.
When to Use — When challenging the constitution or composition of a tribunal, or when arguing that essential criteria for tribunal appointment cannot be set by executive notification. Cite: the essential qualifications and attributes of tribunal members go to the heart of the institution's independence — Parliament cannot delegate this to the executive. Any rules on tribunal qualification issued by executive that go beyond the framework are constitutionally suspect.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
Chapter III — Classification of Functions

Chapter III — Classification of Functions

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
🗺️ LEGEND
Root Node
Level-1 Branch
Positive / Permitted
Negative / Prohibited
Condition / Unless / If
Optional / Pass-on
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
📋 INDEX — Click to navigate
3.1 Threefold Classification 3.2 Identification of Legislative Order Tests: USA & England Price-Fixing: Legislative or Admin? Natural Justice & Legislative Function Key Cases on Hearing 3.3 Doctrine of Separation of Powers ⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
3.1 Threefold Classification of Functions ⬆ Index
Classification of Administrative Functions
⚖️ Legislative
General in nature
Applies to all / class
✓Must be published
in Official Gazette
📝 Think of a price-control notification — it applies to everyone buying that commodity. The whole country must know, so it's published in the Gazette.
🏛️ Administrative
Applies to specific
individual(s)
✓Served on affected
person only; no Gazette
📝 Think of an eviction notice to one person — only that person needs to know. No need to broadcast it to the nation.
🔨 Quasi-Judicial
(Adjudicative)
Adjudicates rights
between parties
✓Natural Justice
MUST be followed
📝 Like a judge deciding between two sides. Even if the law is silent, you must be given a fair hearing before a decision hurts you.
⚠️ WHY Classification Matters — Practical Significance
IFLegislative
No Natural Justice required
Narrower judicial review
Mala fides ground — doubtful
IFAdmin / QJ
Natural Justice applies
Wider judicial review
Mala fides can be pleaded
ALSOSub-delegation
Distinction governs
sub-delegation rules
📌 Terminological Inexactitude — "Administrative" has TWO senses
BROADSense
Everything done by the
Administration — incl.
legislative & quasi-judicial
NARROWSense
Only limited functions
in juxtaposition to
legislative & quasi-judicial
KEY: Identify sense from context in which word is used
💡 Illustration — Essential Commodities Act, 1955 (S.3 & S.3(5))
General nature / class order → Gazette notification
Specific individual order → Service on that person only
💡 Under S.3, Govt can fix prices for all sugar factories (legislative — must gazette) OR direct one particular trader to restrict movement (administrative — served on him alone).
3.2 Identification of a Legislative Order — Core Difficulty ⬆ Index
Identifying a Legislative Order
⚠️ No Bright Line — No articulate definition possible
Any test either over-includes or under-includes
📝 The law hasn't drawn a clear wall between "legislative" and the rest. Whatever test courts use, some situations will fall on the wrong side.
Causes: Admin bodies exercise MIXED functions
(e.g. Election Commission — all three types)
Statutes can confer power for BOTH types of orders
(e.g. Imports & Exports Control Act, 1947)
Tests to Identify Legislative Function — USA, England & India ⬆ Index
Tests for Legislative Function
🇺🇸 USA — Test 1
GENERALITY / APPLICABILITY
Legislative → General application
to broadly identifiable class
Administrative → Specific individuals
or particular situations
📝 A rule fixing price of rice affects all of India — general. An order to one trader is specific — administrative.
💡 GST notification fixing tax rate on a commodity = legislative (applies to all suppliers). Show-cause notice to one taxpayer = administrative.
🇺🇸 USA — Test 2
FUTURITY (APA emphasis)
Legislative → Prescribes future
patterns / prospective effect
Administrative → Determines liabilities
on present/past facts
📝 A law saying "from next month, all manufacturers must do X" is legislative — it shapes the future. A decision saying "you owe tax for last year" is administrative/adjudicative.
💡 GST demand for short-paid ITC in FY 2022-23 = adjudicative (past facts). Notification prospectively restricting ITC on construction = legislative.
🇬🇧 England — Committee
on Ministers' Powers
Legislation = General rule of conduct,
no reference to particular cases,
usually operating in futuro
Administration = Performing particular
acts / issuing particular orders /
applying general rules to particular cases
NOT LEGISLATIVE
(i) Issue particular command
(ii) Issue a licence
(iii) Remit a penalty
(iv) Inspect premises
(v) Inquire
📝 England says: if you're making a general rule for the future — that's legislation. If you're dealing with one person or one situation — that's administration.
📚 de Smith's
Applicability Test
Legislative = Creation & promulgation
of GENERAL rule of conduct
without reference to particular cases
Administrative = Adoption of policy /
specific direction / applying
general rule to particular case
📝 General = legislative. Particular = administrative. The line between them is itself a matter of degree — not always clean-cut.
CAUTION: Test of generality has limits — "general" vs "particular" is itself a matter of degree
Example: Order fixing price of RICE (thousands of farmers) = general; but pig iron (few producers) = particular → same legal power, different character
Price-Fixing — Legislative or Administrative? (Key Case Law) ⬆ Index
Price-Fixing: Evolution of Judicial View
OLDER VIEW (pre-S.I.Syndicate)
Price-fixing = Administrative
Dwarka Prasad v UP (1954)
Diwan Sugar Mills v UOI (1959)
S.I. Syndicate v UOI (1975)
Price-fixing = "More in nature
of legislative measure"
Natural Justice excluded
BUT criterion must be reasonable
NOTE: Court still applied admin-type tests — nexus, non-arbitrariness
📝 Sugar price fixed by Govt. Court said: legislative = no hearing needed. But still checked: was the price arbitrary? Was there a rational basis?
💡 GST rate notification fixing 18% on construction services — legislative. Taxpayer cannot demand personal hearing before rate is fixed. But rate must be non-arbitrary.
UOI v Cynamide India Ltd (1987)
LANDMARK — Price-fixation =
LEGISLATIVE ACTIVITY
TEST: A legislative act = general rule
of conduct, without reference to
particular cases, usually in futuro
Generality + Prospectivity
= Legislative character
Adjudication = past/present facts
Legislation = future course
No Natural Justice in legislative activity
unless statute expressly provides
📝 Drugs price control: the Govt fixed max prices for all bulk drug manufacturers. Court: this is a law for the future, applies to all — not about any one company. No hearing needed.
💡 Central Govt notifies GST rate change effective next quarter — legislative. No individual taxpayer has a right to be heard before such notification is issued.
EXCEPTIONPrice-fixing becomes
Admin/QJ character
When it relates to acquisition
or requisition from a SPECIFIC
individual or property
Hearing required in such case
📝 If Govt compels one factory-owner to sell goods at a fixed price — that's about him specifically. He must be heard before the price is imposed on him.
State of UP v Renusagar Power Co (1988) — Exemption Power: Quasi-legislative OR administrative?
Power exercised for a CLASS → Subordinate legislation (legislative)
Power exercised for an INDIVIDUAL → Administrative; hearing required
💡 Exemption from electricity duty sought by one company (Renusagar) = quasi-judicial. Govt must give that company a hearing.
Exemption order of general nature (Bakul) → Legislative
Natural Justice & Legislative Function — Key Principles ⬆ Index
Natural Justice vis-à-vis Legislative Activity
GENERAL RULE:
No Natural Justice in
Legislative Function
Legislature makes law without
hearing anyone — analogy used
to exclude NJ from admin
legislative orders
📝 Parliament doesn't call every affected person before passing a law. This analogy is used to say: if the Administration makes a law-like order, no hearing is needed either.
BUT: This analogy is IMPERFECT — Legislature is representative body with inbuilt safeguards; Administration has no such safeguard
IFStatute provides for notice
& hearing in legislative process
Failure = Procedural Ultra Vires
→ Subordinate legislation VOID
📝 Ultra vires means "beyond powers." If a statute says the Govt must consult before making a rule, and it doesn't — the rule is struck down as ultra vires.
💡 If GST rules say CBIC must consult GST Council before notification, skipping that step = rule is ultra vires and can be challenged.
IMPORTANT:Even if end-product
is legislative — pre-order
stage may need hearing
Courts can apply adjudicative
process to facts that need
to be determined pre-order
Example: Cost of production &
profit margin in price-fixing
= adjudicative facts → producer
may have a right to be heard
📝 Even if the final price notification is "legislative," figuring out the producer's costs before fixing the price may require giving that producer a hearing.
💡 GST rate determination for a new sector — if Govt needs to ascertain actual production costs from manufacturers, some form of consultation with industry may be required at the pre-notification stage.
LIBERAL APPROACH:Courts may insist on
hearing even in legislative
orders when established rights
/ interests are disturbed
Bharat Kala Bhandar — consultation
required before wages notification
affecting settled employer-employee
relations
Mysore v J.V. Bhat — hearing before
declaring 'slum area' notification
(affects property rights)
SC & Weaker Section — hearing before
rescinding slum clearance notification
📝 Courts sometimes say: yes, this order looks "legislative" (general in scope) — but it directly and seriously harms specific people's established rights. Give them a chance to be heard.
💡 GST notification retrospectively withdrawing ITC for a class of goods already purchased: though legislative in form, affected assessee might argue for consultation / hearing on the ground of legitimate expectation.
📌 Doctrine of Legitimate Expectation — Basis for claiming hearing even in legislative orders
If administration adversely affects a person through a legislative order, a person may claim hearing on the basis of legitimate expectation — founded on reasonableness and fairness
📝 "Legitimate expectation" means: if you had a reasonable belief (based on past practice or promises) that Govt would act a certain way, the Govt must at least hear you before taking away that benefit — even through a general order.
💡 Exporter who reasonably expected duty exemption based on past notifications → Govt's prospective withdrawal through a new notification may give rise to a hearing right.
⚠️ Courts must be WARY of unduly expanding "legislative function" — Administration often invokes this concept to deny hearing. Expanding it dilutes Natural Justice gains.
Key Cases — Nature of Functions (Judicial Characterisation) ⬆ Index
Judicial Characterisation of Administrative Functions
Express Newspapers v UOI (1958)
Wage Board
HELD: Court declined to label — functions do not fall in watertight compartments. Look at PREDOMINANT function.
📝 The Wage Board fixed wages (legislative?) but also adjudicated between employers and employees (quasi-judicial?). Court refused a final label.
K.J. Shephard v UOI (1987)
Bank Amalgamation Scheme
HELD: Scheme affecting 125 employees = NOT legislative despite being placed before Parliament
Employees entitled to hearing
Test of generality = only a broad test, not always decisive
📝 Even though the amalgamation scheme was placed before Parliament (suggesting legislation), the court said: 125 people losing jobs need a hearing. Generality test doesn't trump fairness.
💡 GST Council's decision on merger of departments affecting specific officers — if it affects their service rights, adjudicative process applies.
Common Cause v UOI (2017)
Lokpal Act — Amendment
HELD: Framing of Amendment Bill, reference to Standing Committee, its report — all essential legislative functions exclusively within Parliament's domain
Gulf Goans Hotels v UOI (2015)
Environmental Guidelines
HELD: Ungazetted guidelines = no force of law
Law must: (i) have a set form; (ii) contain clear mandate; (iii) achieve identifiable purpose; (iv) be notified / published
📝 You can't enforce ungazetted government policy against citizens. Policy acquires "force of law" only if it follows proper form AND is notified in the Gazette.
💡 CBIC circulars/instructions not issued as notifications under GST Act — cannot be enforced against taxpayers as statutory law. Only proper notifications in Official Gazette bind.
State of Uttaranchal v Sunil Kumar Vaish
File Notings
HELD: File noting of an officer = mere expression of opinion; NOT an order
Executive order valid only if expressed in name of President/Governor and authenticated per Art. 77(2) / 166(2)
📝 A minister's file note saying "approve this" is just his personal opinion on paper. Unless it becomes a proper authenticated government order, it has no legal effect.
💡 GST officer's noting on file recommending refund is not a "refund order." Only a proper order in prescribed format, signed and authenticated, constitutes an order.
New Bank Employees Union v UOI (1996)
Bank Amalgamation (S.9)
HELD: Scheme under S.9 of Banking Companies Act, 1980 = LEGISLATIVE — because Parliament can modify/reject it (30-day placement with power to amend/reject)
Contrast: S.45 Banking Regulation Act — Parliament only places scheme, no power to modify → NOT legislative
📝 If Parliament actively approves / rejects a scheme, that scheme is legislative. If Parliament just "receives" it without power to change it — not legislative. The power to modify is the key.
3.3 Doctrine of Separation of Powers ⬆ Index
Doctrine of Separation of Powers
📖 Origins & Philosophical Basis
Division into 3 branches dates to Aristotle
Constitutional theory developed by Locke & Montesquieu
— functions must be substantially independent
Indian Constitution accepts the doctrine
esp. judicial independence
🇮🇳 Indian Position — Not Rigid Separation; Checks & Balances
Kesavananda Bharati v State of Kerala (1973): Separation of powers between 3 organs = BASIC STRUCTURE of Constitution — but not cast-iron rigid separation. Checks and balances preferred.
📝 India doesn't have a strict wall between Legislature, Executive and Judiciary. But no organ can swallow another's core functions. Courts can review executive excess. That's the safety valve.
Undesirable: Same body making laws AND adjudicating disputes about their application
Solution: Abuse guarded by giving ANOTHER branch power to scrutinise & invalidate acts done in excess/abuse of power
🔒 Limits on Each Branch — Key Principles from Cases
Legislature CANNOT
declare court decision void
Legislature CAN pass
amending Act to remedy defect
pointed out by court
Medical Council of India v State of Kerala (2019)
📝 If a court strikes down a law, Parliament can't just pass a resolution saying "ignore that judgment." It must fix the underlying legal defect through legislation.
Courts CANNOT direct Parliament
to enact a specific law
EXCEPT when: legal vacuum exists
& judiciary steps in temporarily
till Parliament acts
Ashwani Kumar v UOI
Election Commission cannot act
beyond law made by Parliament —
cannot add new grounds for
candidate disqualification
Courts cannot direct EC on
deregistration of political parties
Courts cannot add safeguards
against arrest that statute
does not provide
Direction to get SSP approval
before SC/ST arrest = encroaching
on legislature's field
Tribunalisation — Rojer Mathew v South India Bank (2020)
Non-discriminatory & uniform conditions
for tribunal members directed
National Tribunals Commission directed
to be constituted as independent body
— Madras Bar Association v UOI (2020)
S.144 CrPC orders — open to
judicial review. Power must be
used responsibly, not casually
Order must be reasoned & notified;
otherwise judicial review stands crippled
📈 Overall Trend of Supreme Court
Delegated legislation / executive rule-making UPHELD unless it goes against the spirit of the parent statute
Increasing deference to executive on matters of national security and public order — overpowering fundamental freedoms in some cases
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Classification of Functions
⬆ Index
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
UOI v Cynamide India Ltd
Supreme Court | AIR 1987 SC 1802 | (1987) 2 SCC 720
"A legislative act is the creation and promulgation of a general rule of conduct without reference to particular cases; legislation is process of formulating a general rule of conduct without reference to particular cases and usually operating in future. Price fixation is a legislative activity and the question of observing principles of natural justice does not arise."
🎯 When to Use: Dept issues GST rate notification / ITC restriction without giving industry a hearing. Argue: legislative act — no NJ required; but criterion must be non-arbitrary.
S.I. Syndicate v UOI
Supreme Court | AIR 1975 SC 460 | (1974) 2 SCC 630
"Price fixation is more in the nature of a legislative measure even though it may be based upon objective criteria found in a report or other material. It could not therefore give rise to a complaint that a rule of natural justice has not been followed in fixing the price. Nevertheless, the criterion adopted must be reasonable."
🎯 When to Use: Challenge GST rate / valuation rule as arbitrary — even if legislative, the criterion must be reasonable and nexus must exist between the objective and the measure.
K.J. Shephard v UOI
Supreme Court | AIR 1988 SC 686 | (1987) 4 SCC 431
"The test of 'generality' to distinguish between a legislative or an adjudicatory act is only a broad test which may not necessarily be true or decisive always, and other considerations may have to be taken into account by the courts to decide the nature of a particular act of the administration."
🎯 When to Use: Dept argues its circular/notification is "legislative" to deny hearing. Argue: generality alone is not decisive — look at the effect on affected parties' rights; hearing still required.
Gulf Goans Hotels Co Ltd v UOI
Supreme Court | AIR 2015 SC 2032 | (2014) 10 SCC 673
"Law must possess a set certain form; contain a clear mandate/explicit command which may be prescriptive, permissive or penal; and must seek to achieve a clearly identifiable purpose. What is claimed to be a law must be notified or made public in order to bind the citizens. Legislation will take effect only when it is published through the customarily recognized official channel namely the Official Gazette."
🎯 When to Use: Dept enforces ungazetted CBIC instruction/circular/press release against taxpayer. Argue: not law — not published in Official Gazette; cannot bind the taxpayer.
State of Uttaranchal v Sunil Kumar Vaish
Supreme Court
"A noting recorded in the file is merely a noting simpliciter and nothing more. It merely represents expression of opinion by the particular individual. By no stretch of imagination can such noting be treated as the decision of the government. A noting in the file or even a decision gets culminated into an order effecting right of the parties, only when it is expressed in the name of President or the Governor as the case may be and authenticated in the manner provided."
🎯 When to Use: Dept relies on internal noting / DIN-less communication / unauthenticated letter as basis of demand or rejection. Argue: file noting is not a valid order; no legal sanctity.
State of Assam v Bharat Kala Bhandar
Supreme Court | AIR 1967 SC 1766
"Such 'wide' and 'far-reaching' powers ought not to be exercised without consulting the interests concerned. Some kind of collection of data with the help of the interests concerned seems to us to be the barest minimum necessary to enable Government to exercise the power."
🎯 When to Use: Wide-ranging GST notification/circular disturbs settled trade practices without any industry consultation. Argue: barest minimum consultation required before disturbing established rights.
Govt of Mysore v J.V. Bhat
Supreme Court | AIR 1975 SC 596 | (1975) 1 SCC 110
Even a notification of general applicability (which could be regarded as legislative in nature) was quashed on the ground of non-hearing, where it had "deep and abiding impact" on the property rights of the people.
🎯 When to Use: GST notification having retrospective effect on existing contracts / ITC entitlements — argue: deep impact on established rights = hearing required even if notification is general in character.
Kesavananda Bharati v State of Kerala
Supreme Court | AIR 1973 SC 1461 | (1973) 4 SCC 225
"The system of checks and balances is a surer method to avoid excessive concentration of power in the hands of a single official or agency because it encourages or at least facilitates abuse of power. The abuse of power is guarded against by giving some other body, usually belonging to one of the other branches of the government, the power to scrutinize the activities of the authority and to invalidate acts done in abuse or excess of power."
🎯 When to Use: Challenge excessive/arbitrary exercise of delegated legislative power by GST Council / CBIC — argue: even legislative functions are subject to constitutional review under checks & balances doctrine.
Medical Council of India v State of Kerala
Supreme Court | (2019) 12 SCC 185
"The legislature cannot declare any decision of a court of law to be void or of no effect. It can, however, pass an amending Act to remedy the defects pointed out by a court of law. A court's decision must always bind, unless the conditions on which it is based are so fundamentally altered that the decision could not have been given in the altered circumstances."
🎯 When to Use: Legislature or executive attempts to override a High Court or Tribunal judgment by circular/notification rather than amending the Act. Argue: court's decision binds unless fundamental conditions change.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | CA Pawankumar Soni
⚖️ Chapter IV — Delegated Legislation

⚖️ Chapter IV — Delegated Legislation

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative Route
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
4.1 Introductory — Nature & Definition
4.1.1 Statutory Character of Rules
4.1.2 Objects & Purpose
4.1.3 Views of Expert Committee
4.1.4 Deeming / Legal Fiction
4.2 Constituents of Delegated Legislation
4.3 Hierarchy of Norms
4.4 Factors Leading to Growth
4.5 Mode of Enactment
4.6 Need for Safeguards
4.7 Restraints on Delegation
4.7.1 England
4.7.2 USA
4.7.3 India — Delhi Laws Act
4.7.4 Delegator Not to Review Delegate's Order
4.8.1 Skeleton Legislation
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
4.1 — Introductory: Nature & Definition of Delegated Legislation
📜 Delegated Legislation — Nature & Foundation
Principles of Administrative Law | Chapter IV
DEFINITION — SALMOND
"That which proceeds from any authority other than the sovereign power and is therefore dependent for its continued existence and validity on some superior or supreme authority."
📝 Simply put: Parliament makes the main law; then it hands over power to the Government or a body to fill in the finer details. That filled-in detail = Delegated Legislation. It lives and breathes only because Parliament allowed it.
Term "Delegated Legislation" Used in TWO Senses
SENSE (i)
The Technique / Exercise — the act of a subordinate agency exercising legislative power delegated by the legislature
📝 The process of making the rule — who makes it and under what authority.
SENSE (ii)
The Output — the subsidiary rules/regulations themselves that come out of the exercise
📝 The actual rules, notifications, orders that are issued — what you read in the Gazette.
Forms / Names of Delegated Legislation in India
Rules
(most common in India — used for Central Govt legislation)
Regulations
(used by statutory corporations — e.g. SEBI Regulations)
Bye-laws
(municipal bodies & lower authorities)
Orders / Notifications / Schemes
(e.g. Essential Commodities Act uses all three)
Key Principles on Delegation — Judicially Settled
PRINCIPLE 1
"To delegate is NOT to denude yourself" — Wills J in Huth v Clarke
Delegation = conferring authority, not giving it up
📝 Parliament doesn't lose its power when it delegates. It still retains ultimate control — like a boss who assigns work but doesn't resign.
PRINCIPLE 2
Delegatee must act within the four corners of the statute
Acts beyond → no legal sanction unless ratified by delegator
PRINCIPLE 3
Rules made to supplement the Act — NOT to supplant it
Power fills in details, cannot override enabling Act
📝 Supplement = add detail. Supplant = replace. Rules can only do the first — they can never override the parent Act.
PRINCIPLE 4
Subordinate legislation validly framed = part of the principal Act itself
LIMIT
Government CANNOT by subordinate legislation nullify directions of the Supreme Court
GST / TAX LINK
CGST Rules, IGST Rules, GST Notifications & Circulars are all delegated legislation under CGST Act 2017. They must supplement the Act — any Rule/Notification that widens or contradicts the Act is ultra vires and can be challenged. This is the bedrock of many GST writ petitions.
💡 GST Example: Rule 89(5) restricting ITC refund on inverted duty structure was challenged as exceeding Section 54 of CGST Act — Courts held Rule cannot restrict what the Act permits. Classic case of Rule attempting to supplant the Act.
4.1.1 — Test to Determine Statutory Character of Rules
🔍 When is a Rule "Statutory"? — The Test
✓ STATUTORY RULE — IF:
(i) Issued under a statutory provision
(ii) AND notified in Official Gazette
📝 Both conditions needed — issued under a law AND published in the Gazette. Miss either and the rule is just an executive instruction, not law.
✗ NOT STATUTORY — IF:
Not issued under any statutory provision, OR
Not notified in Official Gazette
(e.g. Orissa Police Manual, 1940)
⚠️ DATE OF PUBLICATION — KEY
Unlike statutory provisions (which may have retrospective effect), Rules / Notifications apply from date of publication in Official Gazette — NOT earlier.
UOI v M/S G S Chatha Rice Mills (SC 2020): Notification increasing tariff for imports could NOT apply to goods already imported before its publication date.
📝 If a Gazette Notification was issued on 5 March, goods cleared on 3 March cannot be subjected to new duties under it — even if the goods were awaiting customs clearance at the port.
💡 GST Practice: Always check effective date of notification. Many GST circulars/notifications have been challenged where Department applied them retrospectively. Cite Chatha Rice Mills — notification date is the determinative date.
4.1.2 — Objects and Purpose of Delegated Legislation
📋 The "Objects & Purpose" Problem in Delegated Legislation
THE GAP
Unlike an Act of Parliament (which has a "Statement of Objects and Reasons"), Regulations and their amendments have NO "object and purpose" clause. This leaves courts working "in complete darkness without a glimpse into the mind of the maker."
📝 When Parliament passes a law, it tells you WHY — the Statement of Objects and Reasons. But when a Ministry issues a regulation or amendment, it gives no explanation. Courts then struggle to interpret what was intended.
JUDICIAL RECOMMENDATION
High time to change the old practice — "object and purpose" clause should be added at the beginning of delegated legislation, especially in complex regulatory regimes (SEBI, RBI, GST, etc.), just as in primary legislation.
Daiichi Sankyo Co. Ltd. v Jayaram Chigurupati (2010) 7 SCC 449
💡 GST Litigation Use: When a GST Circular or Rule is ambiguous, argue that since no "object and purpose" is stated, it must be interpreted consistent with the parent Act's scheme and the Statement of Objects of CGST Act 2017 — not in isolation.
4.1.3 — Views of the Expert Committee & Judicial Review Limits
🏛️ Expert Committee Reports & Scope of Judicial Review
SEBI Takeover Regulations
Courts CAN refer to Committee Reports (e.g. Justice Bhagwati Committee on Takeover Code) to understand the raison d'être (reason for existence) of amendments.
These reports provide the interpretive backdrop for new provisions.
📝 Raison d'être = French for "reason for being." When Parliament or SEBI amends a law, the Committee Report that led to the amendment tells you WHY — courts use this to understand what the provision is trying to achieve.
Limits of Judicial Review of Delegated Legislation — MERC Case
✓ COURT CAN INTERVENE — IF:
Manifest unreasonableness or arbitrariness shown, OR
Delegate failed to follow statutory procedures, OR
Determination based on extraneous considerations
✗ COURT CANNOT:
Substitute its own determination for that of an expert body that considered material circumstances
"Tariff fixation is a complex scientific exercise" — SC in Reliance Infrastructure v State of Maharashtra (2019)
💡 GST Illustration: GST Council recommendations, while persuasive, when implemented through Notifications are expert-body decisions. Courts are reluctant to second-guess rate fixation. But procedural irregularities (e.g. ultra vires exemption withdrawal) are reviewable.
4.1.4 — Deeming / Legal Fiction in Delegated Legislation
⚖️ Deeming Fictions — Scope & Limits
KEY RULE
A deeming fiction in delegated legislation (e.g. SEBI Takeover Regulation 2(1)(e)(2) — "deemed to be acting in concert") operates PROSPECTIVELY only — NOT retrospectively.
📝 A "deeming fiction" makes you treat something as if it were something else — e.g. treat Person A as if they were acting together with Person B. Such artificial constructs can only work going forward, not to undo past transactions.
LIMIT ON DEEMING FICTION
Legal fiction must remain within its stated limits. The Punjab Electricity Board fiction ("deemed to have been appointed by direct recruitment") applied only to those who fulfilled prerequisite qualifications for direct recruitment — cannot be stretched beyond its scope.
💡 GST Relevance: Section 7(1A) and Schedule II of CGST Act create deeming fictions (e.g. sale of land deemed as supply of service under Para 5(b)). These fictions must be applied strictly within their limits — cannot be extended by inference or analogy.
4.2 — Constituents of Delegated Legislation
📑 What Forms Part of a Statute? — Constituents
SETTLED RULE — Biman Basu v Kallol Guha Thakurta (2010)
Schedules, Forms and Appendices form part of the statutes and/or the rules as the case may be.
They are not mere annexures — they are integral parts of the legislation.
📝 The Schedule to the GST Act (Schedule I, II, III) is not just an appendix — it is part of the Act. Any interpretation must read the Schedule as part of the whole statute, not in isolation.
💡 GST Illustration: Schedule III para 5 (sale of land) and Schedule II para 5(b) (construction service) are integral parts of CGST Act — not mere guidelines. They have the same statutory force as the sections of the Act.
4.3 — Hierarchy of Norms
🏛️ Kelson's Hierarchy of Legal Norms — Grundnorm Theory
📝 Hans Kelson (Austrian legal philosopher) said every legal system has a pyramid of laws. The "Grundnorm" (basic norm) sits at the top. Any lower norm that conflicts with a higher norm is automatically invalid.
Constitution
(Grundnorm)
Acts of Parliament
(Primary Legislation)
Rules / Regulations / Notifications
(Delegated Legislation)
Bye-laws / Orders / Circulars
(Sub-delegated / Sub-ordinate)
GOLDEN RULE
If a legal norm in a lower layer conflicts with a legal norm in a higher layer → the higher layer PREVAILS.
The lower norm is invalid to the extent of inconsistency.
💡 GST Application: If a GST Rule conflicts with the CGST Act, the Act prevails. If an Act provision conflicts with the Constitution, the Constitution prevails. Hierarchy of norms is the doctrinal basis for challenging ultra vires GST Rules/Notifications.
4.4 — Factors Leading to the Growth of Delegated Legislation
📈 Why Has Delegated Legislation Grown? — Key Factors
FACTOR 1
Legislative Pressure
Legislature cannot cope with all legislative work — delegated legislation saves time for broad policies
📝 CMP Report (1932): "If Parliament were not willing to delegate law-making power, it would be unable to pass the kind and quantity of legislation modern opinion requires."
FACTOR 2
Technical Complexity
Modern legislation is technical and complex — specialists in administration are better equipped than generalist legislators
FACTOR 3
Flexibility & Experimentation
Easier to change rules than to amend a statute — allows adaptation to experience; "constant adjustment in light of experience"
FACTOR 4
Emergency Situations
Legislature cannot meet at short notice — executive needs standby powers to act immediately
📝 In war, pandemic, economic crisis — waiting for Parliament to pass a law is impractical. Executive must be pre-armed with delegated powers.
FACTOR 5
Socio-Economic Reforms
India's planned economy & welfare state model required massive delegation to control trade, commerce & social development
SC OBSERVATION — Agricultural Market Committee v Shalimar Chemical Works (1997)
"The power of delegation has by now as per necessity become a constituent element of legislative power as a whole." Delegated legislation is not merely convenient — it is indispensable.
4.5 — Mode of Enactment
📰 Publication in Official Gazette — Mandatory Requirement
RULE — Rajendra Agricultural University v Ashok Kumar Prasad (2010)
Once the law requires publication in the Official Gazette as part of the process of making a statute/rule, the requirement of publication becomes mandatory and binding.
Purpose behind the provision becomes irrelevant — compliance is non-negotiable.
📝 You cannot skip Gazette notification on the ground that the public might come to know by other means. Publication IS the statute coming into force. No publication = No statute.
💡 GST Notifications: Every GST rate change, exemption or amendment must be notified in the Official Gazette. A circular or instruction cannot substitute for a Gazette notification — they have different legal standing.
4.6 — Need for Safeguards Against Delegated Legislation
⚠️ Dangers of Delegated Legislation — Why Safeguards Are Essential
DANGER 1
Legislature passes "Skeleton" Acts — only barest principles, leaves executive to fill in BOTH details AND policies
📝 Skeleton Act = a law so bare it is like a skeleton without flesh. The executive then has total freedom to put any flesh it likes on the bones — without democratic debate.
DANGER 2
Delegated legislation drafted in anonymous government chambers — no publicity, no public debate, no press criticism
DANGER 3
Power to affect life, liberty and property of individuals without democratic restraints available in normal legislation
Controls Over Delegated Legislation — Two Levels
LEVEL 1
At Point of Delegation
How much power should legislature be permitted to delegate? Standards / policy must be laid down by the legislature itself.
LEVEL 2
At Point of Exercise
Subject to what restraints should the delegate function? Courts can void delegated legislation that exceeds the parent Act.
CMP RECOMMENDATION
"The precise limits of the law-making powers which Parliament intends to confer on a Minister should always be defined in clear language by the statute which confers it."
Committee on Ministers' Powers Report, 1932
4.7 — Restraints on Delegation of Legislative Power
🌍 Comparative Framework — Restraints on Legislative Delegation
🇬🇧 ENGLAND — §4.7.1
Parliamentary Sovereignty doctrine
Parliament can delegate ANY amount of power
No constitutional limitation whatsoever
📝 In England, Parliament is supreme — what Parliament says is law. There's no Constitution to question it. So Parliament can hand over any amount of rule-making power to anyone it pleases.
RISK
CMP: "Legislative powers are freely delegated without members of the two Houses fully realising what is being done."
Remedy lies in Parliament's own hands only.
🇺🇸 USA — §4.7.2
Written Constitution + Judicial Review
Two doctrines restrict delegation:
(i) Separation of Powers
(ii) Delegatus non potest delegare
📝 Delegatus non potest delegare = A delegate cannot further delegate. Congress is a delegate of the people — so it cannot further pass its law-making power to the Executive.
Cardozo J Test: "To uphold delegation, need to discover in the terms of the Act a standard reasonably clear whereby the discretion must be governed."
EXTREME
Panama Refining Co. v Ryan: Held unconstitutional — Congress declared no policy, established no standards, laid down no rule. President given unlimited authority.
VALID
Yakes v US: Broad delegation upheld — Congress stated legislative objective AND method AND standards sufficiently definite for courts to test conformity.
🇮🇳 INDIA — §4.7.3
American model adopted in In re Delhi Laws Act (1951)
Written Constitution → cannot enjoy same freedom as British Parliament
Legislature CAN delegate — but must lay down
legislative policy + standards + guidelines
CANNOT
Legislature CANNOT delegate "essential legislative function" — the determination of policy and its formulation as a binding rule of conduct
FORBIDDEN
Delegate CANNOT become a parallel legislature — agency must remain subordinate authority only
4.7.3 — India: Delhi Laws Act Case & Doctrine of Excessive Delegation
⚖️ In re Delhi Laws Act, 1912 — AIR 1951 SC 332
7-Judge Constitution Bench | The Foundational Case
📝 Seven judges, seven different opinions — but two points of unity: (1) Delegation of legislative power is necessary in modern India, and (2) Unlimited delegation is not permitted — the Constitution constrains the legislature.
Two Schools of Thought in Delhi Laws Act Case
VIEW 1 (Liberal)
Legislature can delegate to any extent subject ONLY to not effacing itself — must retain ultimate control & power to withdraw delegation whenever delegate acts wrongly
VIEW 2 (Restrictive) — MAJORITY
Legislature must NOT delegate "essential legislative function" = formulation of policy + enactment as binding rule of conduct
Legislature must lay down standards/policy; delegate can only execute
Fazl Ali J — 4 Conclusions on Legislative Delegation (Delhi Laws Act)
(1)
Legislature must normally discharge its primary legislative function ITSELF — not through others
(2)
Legislature may utilise any outside agency to any extent ancillary and necessary for full exercise of its legislative power
(3)
It CANNOT abdicate legislative functions — outside agency must remain subordinate authority, NOT a parallel legislature
(4)
Indian courts are NOT committed to doctrine of separation of powers as in USA — only two checks: good sense of delegate + principle of non-abdication
Development After Delhi Laws Act — Gwalior Rayon & Beyond
GWALIOR RAYON — AIR 1974 SC 1660
Mathew J proposed: So long as Parliament retains power to repeal the delegating provision, there is no abdication — delegation can be as broad as needed.
"The hunt by court for legislative policy in the crevices of a statute is not an edifying spectacle."
MAJORITY REJECTED
Khanna J (majority): "The rule against excessive delegation flows from and is a necessary postulate of the sovereignty of the people. It is not permissible to substitute in the matter of legislative policy the view of individual officers for that of the popular will."
Legislature must lay down policy, principle or standard when delegating power.
PRESENT POSITION
In practice, courts show deference to legislative will. Doctrine of excessive delegation has "more of symbolic value." Courts adopt strategies to UPHOLD broad delegation:
(i) Find policy in preamble or legislative history
(ii) Accept procedural safeguards as substitute for standards
(iii) "Faint glimmerings of policy" sufficient — Ramesh Birch v UOI
💡 GST Example: Section 9(1) CGST Act delegates power to fix GST rates to Govt by Notification. This is extremely broad. Yet courts have upheld it finding legislative policy in the Act's preamble and objectives. Challenge requires showing patent irrationality, not mere disagreement with the rate.
4.7.4 — Delegator Not to Revise/Review His Delegate's Order
🔁 The Delegation-Review Problem: Who Can Review Whose Order?
📝 Once a principal (the boss) delegates power to a delegate (the agent), and the agent passes an order, can the principal revisit that order? Indian law says generally NO — the order of the delegate is treated as the order of the principal itself.
FOUNDATIONAL RULE — State of Orissa v Commissioner of Land Records (1998)
Order of the delegate is to be treated, for ALL intents and purposes, as an order of the principal itself. Therefore, the Board of Revenue CANNOT revise an order passed by its delegate, the Commissioner of Land Records & Settlement.
Key Rules on Delegator-Delegate Relationship
PRINCIPAL RETAINS:
Power to revoke the grant
Power to act concurrently on matters within delegated area (unless bound by delegate's act)
PRINCIPAL CANNOT:
Once delegatee has exercised revisional power on an order — principal cannot exercise revisional jurisdiction on the SAME order again
(Power exhausted by delegatee)
REVIEW RULE:
Review application lies only to the SAME authority (or successor if unavailable) — applies to quasi-judicial authorities too
Board CANNOT review Commissioner's order — only Commissioner or successor can
CONCURRENT POWERS:
Principal does NOT lose powers merely because they were delegated — both principal & delegate may exercise revisional jurisdiction (but in respect of DIFFERENT matters)
💡 GST Application: Once an Additional Commissioner (delegatee of Principal Commissioner) passes an order under section 73/74, the Principal Commissioner cannot issue a fresh SCN on the same transaction — the delegate's power having been exercised, it is exhausted. Further action requires appeal/revision under proper provisions.
4.8.1 — Skeleton Legislation
💀 Skeleton Legislation — Definition & Doctrine
DEFINITION — Gwalior Rayon
"Skeleton Legislation" = A statute which delegates legislative power without laying down ANY principle or policy for the guidance of the delegate.
Executive is given power to legislate not just on details — but even on matters of principle and policy.
📝 A skeleton has bones but no flesh. A skeleton Act gives the executive a blank canvas — it can paint any picture it likes. Theoretically this is bad. In practice, courts have often upheld skeleton statutes by finding "faint glimmerings of policy."
Judicial Treatment of Skeleton Legislation — Key Cases
CaseProvisionOutcomeReasoning
Harishankar Bagla v State of MP
AIR 1954 SC 465		 S.3, Essential Supplies (Temporary Powers) Act, 1946 — power to regulate/prohibit supply of essential commodities ✓ UPHELD Policy found in the section itself: "maintaining or increasing supplies." Area of essential commodities requires broad delegation. "Ambit of Act such that details can only be worked out by delegation."
Bhatnagars & Co. v UOI
AIR 1957 SC 478		 S.3(1)(a), Imports & Exports (Control) Act, 1947 — power to prohibit/restrict import/export of any goods ✓ UPHELD Policy found in preceding Defence of India Act, 1939 — subject matter of import/export is so variable that articulate norms cannot be fixed for all time.
Harakchand v UOI
AIR 1970 SC 1453		 S.5(2)(b), Gold Control Act, 1968 — Gold Administrator empowered "as appears necessary or expedient" to regulate gold manufacture/distribution ✗ STRUCK DOWN Power was legislative in character, very wide, no guidelines, no procedural safeguard (unlike Govt's power under S.114 which required Parliamentary laying). Delegation of essential legislative power without guidelines = abdication.
KEY DISTINCTION — Valid vs Invalid Skeleton Legislation
Valid: Where broad delegation is inevitable given the subject matter (essential commodities, import/export) AND there is at least a general policy statement.
Invalid: Where legislature has delegated essential policy-making WITHOUT any standard AND without any procedural safeguard (e.g. laying before Parliament).
💡 GST Parallel: Section 11(1) CGST Act empowers Central Govt to grant exemption by Notification. This is skeleton-level delegation. The doctrine says it must be consistent with public interest — the policy constraint the Act provides. Challenge to arbitrary withdrawal of exemptions must show the withdrawal lacks rational nexus with public interest.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter IV | Delegated Legislation
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, Appellate Authorities and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
In re Delhi Laws Act, 1912
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1951 SC 332 | 7-Judge Constitution Bench
Key Ratio — Fazl Ali J (Majority)
"The legislature must normally discharge its primary legislative function itself and not through others. It cannot abdicate its legislative functions, and therefore while entrusting power to an outside agency, it must see that such agency acts as a subordinate authority and does not become a parallel legislature."
Further Ratio on Policy
Delegated legislation must be exercised within the parameters of essential legislative policy. Delegation of essential legislative function is impermissible. The legislature cannot delegate its power to repeal the law or modify its essential features.
When to Use — When challenging a GST Rule or Notification that modifies, restricts or effectively nullifies a right or benefit conferred by the CGST Act itself. Argue: the Rule/Notification has crossed the line from supplementing to supplanting — it has become a parallel legislature and is therefore ultra vires.
Gwalior Rayon Co. v Asstt. Commissioner of Sales Tax
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1974 SC 1660 | (1974) 4 SCC 98 | Khanna J (Majority)
Key Ratio
"The rule against excessive delegation of the legislative authority flows from and is a necessary postulate of the sovereignty of the people. The rule contemplates that it is not permissible to substitute in the matter of legislative policy the view of individual officers or other authorities, however competent they may be, for that of the popular will as expressed by the representatives of the people."
Further Principle
The power to legislate carries with it the power to delegate, but "excessive delegation may amount to abdication" and "delegation unlimited may invite despotism uninhibited."
When to Use — When a GST Notification or Rule effectively takes away what the Act gives, or when a subordinate authority (e.g. GST Officer/Circular) attempts to impose conditions/restrictions not contemplated by Parliament. The delegate cannot substitute its own legislative policy for Parliament's will.
Supplement vs Supplant — Supreme Court's Golden Rule
Multiple SC Decisions | Key Principle of Delegated Legislation | Always Applicable
Key Ratio
"The power to make subordinate legislation is derived from the enabling Act and it is fundamental that the delegate on whom such a power is conferred has to act within the limits of authority conferred by the Act. Rules cannot be made to supplant the provisions of the enabling Act but to supplement it. A delegate cannot override the Act either by exceeding the authority or making provisions inconsistent with the Act."
When to Use — The single most powerful ratio in any GST writ petition challenging a Rule or Notification. Use when: Rule 89(5) restricts ITC refund beyond what Section 54 allows; Rule 36(4) restricts ITC not contemplated by Section 16; any Rule/Notification creates a liability or restricts a right beyond what the Act provides. Cite and argue: this Rule supplants the Act — it is therefore ultra vires and void.
UOI v M/S G S Chatha Rice Mills — Notification Date Rule
Supreme Court of India | CA No. 3249 of 2020 | SC dated 23 September 2020
Key Ratio
Characterising a notification as an instance of delegated legislation, the Supreme Court affirmed that if the author of the legislative effort was the executive, the question of publication necessarily assumes significance. The time at which the notification was published had relevance. Consequently, goods already imported before publication were held not to be subjected to the additional levy.
When to Use — When Department seeks to apply a GST Notification/Circular retrospectively to cover transactions completed before its publication date. Cite to establish that delegated legislation (Rules/Notifications) takes effect from date of Gazette publication — NOT earlier. Also use when a favourable exemption notification is withdrawn and Department attempts retrospective application.
Harakchand v UOI — Excessive Delegation Invalidation
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1970 SC 1453
Key Ratio
"The delegation of essential legislative power of the principal to the delegatee would amount to abdication of its legislative power and, if it is bereft of any guidelines, then it is unsustainable in the eye of the law." The power conferred was characterised as 'legislative' in character and declared invalid because it was very wide and suffered from the vice of 'excessive delegation'.
When to Use — When challenging the constitutional validity of a broadly worded Section/Rule under GST that gives unbridled power to an officer (e.g. power to reject refund claims "as may be prescribed" without standards; or power to call for "any documents" without limitation). Argue: power bereft of guidelines = abdication of legislative function = ultra vires.
Delegator Cannot Review Delegate's Order — State of Orissa v Commissioner of Land Records
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1998 SC 3067 | (1998) 7 SCC 162
Key Ratio
The order of the delegate is to be treated, for all intents and purposes, as an order of the principal itself. Once the delegatee has exercised its revisional power over an order, the delegator (principal) cannot exercise revisional jurisdiction over the same order again — the power of the principal has been exhausted by the delegatee.
When to Use — In GST proceedings where a higher officer seeks to reopen or re-examine an order already decided by a lower-level delegated officer on the same facts/same period. Particularly useful in SCN situations where Principal Commissioner seeks to review what an Additional Commissioner already settled. Cite to shut the second bite at the cherry.
Agricultural Market Committee v Shalimar Chemical Works — Delegation as Constituent Element
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1997 SC 2502 | (1997) 5 SCC 516
Key Ratio
"The power of delegation is a constituent element of the legislative power as a whole under Article 245 of the Constitution. The legislature has to lay down the legislative policy and principle to afford guidance for carrying out the said policy before it delegates its subsidiary powers. The essential legislative function consists of the determination of the legislative policy and the legislature cannot abdicate essential legislative function in favour of another."
Further Principle
"The delegate which has been authorised to make subsidiary rules and regulations has to work within the scope of its authority and cannot widen or constrict the scope of the Act or the policy laid down thereunder. It cannot, in the garb of making rules, legislate on the field covered by the Act and has to restrict itself to the mode of implementation of the policy and purpose of the Act."
When to Use — Universal ratio for any challenge to GST Rules/Notifications. The delegate (Central Government making Rules under Section 164 CGST Act) cannot widen or constrict what Parliament has laid down. Any Rule that adds a condition not in the Act, or removes a right the Act grants, is beyond this mandate and void.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter IV — Delegated Legislation

⚖️ Chapter IV — Delegated Legislation

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Ultra Vires
Condition / Subject to
Optional / Alternative
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
4.8.2 Power to Include
4.8.3 Power to Exempt
4.8.4 Power to Modify Statute
4.8.5 Power to Amend Schedule
4.8.6 Territorial Extension
4.8.7 Applying Provisions from One Act to Another
4.8.8 Henry VIII Clause
4.8.9 Power of Taxation
4.8.10 Delegation to Municipal Bodies
4.9 Binding Effect
4.10 Conditional Legislation
4.11 Need for Doctrine of Excessive Delegation
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
4.8.2 — Power to Include
📋 Power to Include — Expanding Scope of an Act
Executive power to bring new persons/commodities within ambit of statute
📝 Instead of formally amending the law each time, Parliament can allow the Government to add new items/persons to a schedule — so the law's reach expands without going back to Parliament.
Technique: Statute lists specific items in schedule → Government empowered to declare any other item as falling within scope
✓ VALID — Mohmedalli v UOI (1964)
Employees' Provident Funds Act: Central Govt empowered to bring additional establishments within purview → upheld as policy of Act gave sufficient indication
📝 Court found the Act's scheme itself showed clear policy — so adding more factories was fine, not an abuse of power.
💡 GST parallel: Govt's power to add new goods/services to taxable list by notification is similar — valid if policy of CGST Act gives guidance.
✗ INVALID — Hamdard Dawakhana v UOI (1960)
Drugs & Magic Remedies Act: Power to add "any other disease" to rules — held uncanalised & uncontrolled — no criteria, standards or principles prescribed in Act
📝 The law gave no clue whatsoever about which diseases could be added. That blank cheque to Government was struck down.
✗ INVALID — V. Sudeer v Bar Council of India (1999)
Bar Council Training Rules 1995: Power under Advocates Act §24(3)(d) extends only to making ineligible persons eligible — cannot be used to make eligible persons ineligible by adding pre-enrolment training requirement
📝 The rule-making power was a one-way street — it could only open doors, not shut them. Adding new hurdles for eligible advocates exceeded the statutory power.
⚠️ KEY PRINCIPLE Power to include is valid subject to: statute laying down a policy; delegate acts within that policy; power is not uncanalised or uncontrolled
4.8.3 — Power to Exempt
🔓 Power to Exempt — Executive Power to Restrict Scope of Act
Exemption clauses: flexibility tool to handle hardship cases
📝 Parliament makes a general law. But some people/situations deserve to be left out. Instead of amending the law each time, Parliament authorises the Government to grant exemptions as situations arise.
Rationale: Legislature cannot foresee every hardship; exemption clause gives operational flexibility
NATURE OF ORDER
Single entity exempted → Administrative Order
Class of entities exempted → Legislative Order
📝 If you exempt one specific company — that's an admin decision. If you exempt all companies in a sector — that's a legislative act, challengeable on wider grounds.
💡 GST Exemption Notifications (e.g., Notification 12/2017) = Legislative orders, subject to equality challenge under Article 14.
✓ VALID — Jalan Trading v Mill Mazdoor Union (1967)
Payment of Bonus Act §36: Govt power to exempt any establishment from Act having regard to financial position → upheld — Parliament laid down adequate guidance
✓ VALID — Registrar, Co-Op Societies v Kunjabmu (1980)
Madras Co-op Societies Act §60: Broad exemption + modification power → upheld — policy in preamble (facilitate formation & working of societies) was sufficient
HINDUSTAN PAPER CORP v GOVT OF KERALA (SC)
Key observations: (i) Exemption provisions inevitable in fiscal/economic statutes; (ii) Legislature cannot examine every hardship; (iii) Exemption must be notified in Gazette — not granted secretly; (iv) Subject to legislative scrutiny; (v) Power exercisable only in public interest
📝 An exemption cannot be given in a back room. It must be publicly notified, Parliament can review it, and it must serve public interest — not private benefit.
⚠️ CHALLENGE GROUND Exemption orders susceptible to challenge under Article 14 (equality) on grounds of discrimination
💡 GST context: Exemption notification for specific sector (e.g., healthcare, education) can be challenged if it discriminates against similarly placed entities without rational basis.
4.8.4 — Power to Modify the Statute
⚡ Power to Modify the Parent Statute — Most Drastic Form of Delegation
Executive given power to change the very law enacted by legislature
📝 This is the most extreme form — the Government can actually change what Parliament wrote. Courts are cautious about this but allow it when genuinely necessary for administration.
CMP CHARACTERISATION "Exceptional form of delegation" — Prima facie drastic because it makes executive supreme over legislature
Two Factors to Balance
(i) Danger of executive misusing its power
(ii) Need for delegating the power to meet changing/unforeseen situations
JUDICIAL APPROACH — INDIA Courts in India are generally favourable to conferring modification power on executive — subject to ONE RIDER: executive cannot use it to change the basic policy of the Act or effect any essential changes
📝 Government can fine-tune the law. Government CANNOT rewrite the law. The core policy set by Parliament is untouchable.
✗ STRUCK DOWN — International Spirits & Wines v State of Haryana (2019)
Punjab Excise Act rule-making: Haryana Liquor Licence Rules amendment empowered Financial Commissioner to grant single licence for entire State → exceeded rule-making power (statute only allowed local area licences) → struck down
📝 The rules vested wider powers in the Commissioner than even the State Government had. Procedural rules cannot enlarge substantive provisions of an Act.
💡 GST parallel: CBIC circular cannot expand or restrict the scope of an exemption notification. If it does, it's ultra vires — can be challenged before Appellate Authority.
SETTLED RULE Procedural rules can neither enlarge nor curtail the substantive provision of an Act — State of AP v Vatsavji Kumara Venkata Verma (1999)
4.8.5 — Power to Amend Schedule
📋 Power to Amend Schedule — Common Legislative Practice
Executive alters scheduled list by addition/deletion without formal amendment
📝 Parliament attaches a schedule (list) to the Act. The Government can add or remove items from that list — so the law's reach expands or contracts without going back to Parliament for a formal amendment.
Prima facie involves modification of parent Act — but consistently upheld if exercised in light of policy of the Act
✓ Edwards Mills v State of Ajmer (SC 1955)
Minimum Wages Act: Govt power to add any employment to schedule → upheld — policy apparent from face of Act (to fix minimum wages to avoid labour exploitation)
📝 Court read the policy from the preamble and spirit of the Act — even without explicit standards, the purpose was clear enough.
✓ Babu Ram v State of Punjab (SC 1979)
Punjab General Sales Tax Act: Govt power to add/remove goods from Schedule C (tax exemption list) after 3 months notice → upheld on two grounds: (i) common practice; (ii) prior notice = check on arbitrary exercise
💡 GST analogy: Rate schedule changes (e.g., adding goods to 5% or 18% category) by notification follow same principle — valid if policy of GST Act guides it.
Bihar Agrl. Markets Act
§39: Power to add item to agricultural produce schedule → upheld in same manner — power has been delegated by legislature to State Govt in the same manner as power to Central Govt under Essential Commodities Act
⚠️ LIMIT Schedule amendment power cannot be used to change the policy underlying the Act — it must be exercised within the policy framework — Rajnarain v Chairman, Patna Administration Committee
4.8.6 — Territorial Extension of Statute
🗺️ Territorial Extension — Executive Power to Extend a Law to New Area
With "such modifications as it thinks fit" — subject to limits
📝 Parliament makes a law for Area A. It also empowers the Government to extend that same law to Area B (say a Union Territory or cantonment) — with minor changes to suit local conditions. This power is valid but limited.
Lachmi Narain v UOI (SC) — 5 Limitations on Extension Power
(i) Power = Extension of enactment already in force in a State to Union Territory
(ii) Modification power is integral to extension — not a separate independent power
(iii) Power exhausts itself once extended — cannot be exercised again after extension
(iv) Only modifications necessary to adjust to local conditions of Union Territory are permissible
(v) "Restrictions & modifications" cannot alter any essential feature or legislative policy — only peripheral/insubstantial changes allowed
📝 Government can change punctuation, local references, dates. It CANNOT change the core rights or obligations created by the law. Essential structure must remain intact.
Cantonments — Brij Sunder Kapoor v First Additional District Judge (SC 1989)
Act 46 of 1957 §3: Central Govt may extend to any cantonment any State rent control law with "such restrictions and modifications as it thinks fit" → SC upheld — modification power = "matter of detail of drafting, of not much significance or importance" — Central Govt "cannot change basic essential structure or material provisions"
RAMESH BIRCH v UOI — Additional Rule
Extension to Union Territory valid when: (1) No existing law = vacuum; OR (2) Law being extended supplements existing law without actual conflict. Giving power to repeal existing law and substitute another = NOT permissible
✗ — Shama Rao v Union Territory of Pondicherry (SC 1967)
Pondicherry Act adopted Madras Sales Tax Act + all future amendments by Madras Legislature → struck down as "total surrender" — Pondicherry Legislature could not anticipate future laws of Madras Legislature
📝 You can hand over a ready-made law. You cannot hand over a blank cheque to adopt whatever another legislature may write in future — that is abdication of legislative power.
💡 GST context: If a State's GST rules automatically adopt any future CBIC circular — that could be challenged as excessive delegation if no standards are prescribed.
4.8.7 — Applying Provisions from One Act to Another with Modifications
🔀 Cross-Application of Statutory Provisions
Statute A empowers Govt to apply provisions of Statute B with modifications
Examples of such provisions
LIC Act §43: Central Govt may apply Insurance Act 1938 provisions with modifications to LIC
Central Excises Act §12: Central Govt may apply Customs Act 1962 provisions to excise matters with modifications
SETTLED RULE — NCJ Mills v Asstt. Collector, Central Excises (SC 1971)
"Power to restrict and modify does NOT import the power to make essential changes" — confined to alterations of a minor character — no change in principle involved — "No delegation of essential legislative functions or change of legislative policy"
📝 Minor tweaks allowed. Essential changes — forbidden. This is the line drawn by the Supreme Court repeatedly.
💡 GST: CBIC's power to apply TDS/TCS provisions of one section to another context — valid only if it doesn't change the essential structure of liability.
RBI & IBC — Dharani Sugars v UOI (SC 2019)
RBI's §35A powers (Reserve Bank of India Act) invoked for IBC 2016 directions — SC upheld: statutes are "always speaking" — modern legislature making laws for fast-moving society must be presumed aware of enlarged meanings with march of time
📝 A law made in 1949 can validly apply to a situation created by a 2016 law — courts don't freeze legislative intent to the year of enactment.
4.8.8 — Henry VIII Clause (Removal of Difficulty Clause)
👑 Henry VIII Clause — "Removal of Difficulty" Provision
Named after King Henry VIII — regarded as impersonation of executive autocracy
📝 This is a clause in the law that says "if any difficulty arises, the Government may do whatever is necessary to remove it." At its extreme, it lets the Government modify even the parent Act — which is why it's named after an autocratic king.
TYPE 1 — NARROW (VALID)
"Power to remove difficulties must be exercised consistently with provisions of parent Act" — Govt cannot modify parent statute itself
📝 Government can smooth edges of implementation. Cannot rewrite the Act. Example: States Reorganisation Act §128.
3 Requirements (Banking Regulation Act §45(10))
(i) Difficulty actually arose in giving effect to provisions
(ii) Order is necessary/expedient to remove that difficulty
(iii) Order is not inconsistent with any provision of scheme
TYPE 2 — BROADER (CONDITIONALLY VALID)
Govt may modify parent Act or any other Act in name of removal of difficulties — usually time-limited (2-3 years from commencement)
📝 More drastic version — Government can actually amend the law. Allowed when new complicated socio-economic scheme is introduced, but subject to strict conditions.
JALAN TRADING — STRUCK DOWN
Payment of Bonus Act §37(1): Government made sole judge of whether difficulty arose + finality of order under §37(2) → excessive delegation — Government had unreviewable power
POST-JALAN — CONDITIONS FOR VALIDITY
(a) Existence of "difficulty" = sine qua non — objective fact, not subjective satisfaction
(b) Difficulty must be in giving effect to parent Act — not extraneous
(c) Government may only "slightly tinker" — cannot change basic structure or primary features
COURT'S OWN WORDS Government "may slightly tinker with the Act to round off angularities and smoothen the joints or remove minor obscurities to make it workable, but it cannot change, disfigure or do violence to the basic structure and primary features of the Act."
✗ Ultra Vires — Bengal Iron Corp v CTO (SC 1993)
AP GST Act §42: Govt issued order including "cast iron castings" in tax-exempt "cast iron" → quashed — power was to give effect to provisions, not to alter them. What was taxable under Act could not be made exempt by govt order.
📝 The Government cannot use a "removal of difficulty" clause to grant a tax exemption that Parliament did not grant. That would be rewriting the fiscal policy.
💡 GST application: CBIC circular cannot exempt a supply that is taxable under CGST Act by claiming "removal of difficulty." If it does, it's ultra vires and challengeable.
CMP COMMITTEE RECOMMENDATION Henry VIII clause should be used only: (i) when "demonstrably" essential; (ii) for sole purpose of bringing Act into operation; (iii) for maximum 1 year from passing of Act
💡 GST: Many "removal of difficulty" orders under CGST Act §172 have been issued. Each must satisfy: real difficulty existed, order doesn't change basic GST scheme, time-limited.
4.8.9 — Power of Taxation — Delegation in Fiscal Area
💰 Delegation of Taxing Powers
Legislature retains core; executive gets operational flexibility
📝 Tax is the most important power of Parliament. But practically Parliament cannot fix every rate for every item at all times. So it sets the policy and maximum limit, then lets the Government fill in the details.
Three Main Forms of Taxing Power Delegation
FORM 1 — Exempt from tax
Orient Weaving Mills v UOI: Central Govt power to exempt excisable goods from duty → valid — legislature can delegate power to select goods/persons on whom tax is to be levied
📝 Parliament cannot list every item. Govt can select which goods get exempted — valid if policy guides the selection.
💡 GST Exemption Notifications = exactly this Form 1 delegation.
FORM 2 — Bring additional items within tax
Babu Ram v State of Punjab: Power to bring goods within purview of sales tax law → valid
💡 GST: Power to add new goods/services to taxable schedule by notification.
FORM 3 — Fix rate of tax
Generally valid if maximum limit is fixed by legislature — executive fixes within that range
✓ Valid Devi Das v State of Punjab: Rate not exceeding 2% → valid (reasonable area of discretion)
Nagappa v Iron Ore Cess Commr: Up to 50 paise per tonne → valid (area of discretion clearly demarcated)
✗ Invalid Rate at "such rates as Govt may deem fit" without any maximum → uncanalised power — "delegation running riot" — Papiah (not regarded as good law)
📝 "Whatever rate the Govt wants" is a blank cheque for taxation — courts have struck it down consistently.
