💡 GST: If a notification said "GST at such rate as CBIC may notify" without any ceiling — that would be unconstitutional.
✓ Gwalior Rayon v Asstt. Commr. of Sales Tax — CST Act §8(2)(b)
Central Sales Tax pegged to State sales tax rate (whichever is higher) → valid — clear policy: prevent evasion of Central sales tax by keeping Central rate ≥ local rate. Parliament had no control over local rates, so pegging was necessary.
§4.8.9.1 — Delegation to Non-Elected Body (BDA)
Bangalore Development Authority Act 1976: Taxing power delegated to BDA (non-elected) → upheld — BDA undertakes developmental activities for the area, procedure governed by Bangalore Corporation Act 1949, delegation not arbitrary
📝 Even a non-elected body can be given taxing power if: it is not a stranger to the subject matter, procedure for levy is prescribed by law, and delegation is not arbitrary.
§4.8.9.2 — Power to Determine Compensation
Railways Act §124-A: Amount of compensation for untoward incidents left to Central Govt rules → valid — compensation is a "varying phenomenon", Govt better placed to determine just and reasonable amounts
4.8.10 — Delegation to Municipal Bodies
🏛️ Delegation to Municipal Bodies — Liberal Judicial Approach
Representative nature of municipal bodies justifies broad delegation
Why Courts are More Liberal with Municipal Bodies
Elected representatives — accountable to local people
Possibility of being voted out = built-in check on excess
Needs are limited (local area) vs. Govt needs which are unlimited
Statute may prescribe maximum rates; Govt approval may be needed
CORPORATION OF CALCUTTA v LIBERTY CINEMA (SC)
Corporation given power to fix licence fee on cinemas at rates it may resolve — upheld — policy implied from Act: "imposing taxes at such rates as necessary to defray cost of discharging its duties"
MODI — Municipal Corporation levying tax on land & buildings without max limit → upheld
Mere absence of maximum rate ≠ per se excessive delegation — corporation's taxing power limited to extent of its statutory needs — that furnishes sufficient control
📝 A municipality cannot tax beyond what it needs to run itself. That practical limit substitutes for a legislated maximum.
CONTRAST State Legislature conferring power on municipalities to levy ANY tax it can levy itself = extremely broad but upheld — only such taxes levied as necessary to implement statutory purposes — prior State Govt sanction required
JRG MFG ASSOCIATION v UOI — Rubber Board
Rubber Board (high powered body with representation of all rubber interests) given power to levy excise duty → upheld — (i) sufficient policy clarity; (ii) Govt has power to modify/cancel board's acts; (iii) rules subject to Parliament
4.9 — Binding Effect of Delegated Legislation
⚡ Binding Effect of Statutory Rules & Delegated Legislation
Statutory Rules — Mandatory
RK Trivedi v UOI (1998): When statutory rules prescribe particular mode of selection — Tribunal cannot direct that mode be given go-by. Statutory rules come into force → procedure laid down therein must be followed.
📝 Once Parliament or the executive makes valid rules under a statute, those rules are as binding as the Act itself — courts and tribunals cannot substitute their own procedure.
Notification — Continues until Repealed
A duly issued notification continues to govern unless same is repealed. When Act is repealed → delegated legislation made thereunder also falls to the ground unless expressly preserved.
Override — Subordinate Legislation
Regulation u/s 178, Electricity Act 2003 = subordinate legislation → can override existing contracts including power purchase agreements — cannot be done by CERC order under §79(1)(j)
📝 A properly made regulation has higher force than a contractual arrangement — even PPAs must align with the regulation.
4.10 — Conditional Legislation vs Delegated Legislation
🔀 Conditional Legislation — A Colonial-Era Concept
Law is complete; only its operation depends on external determination
📝 In conditional legislation, Parliament makes the law fully. It just says "this law will apply to Area X when Government decides the time is right." Government doesn't make law — it just pulls the trigger.
Aspect Conditional Legislation Delegated Legislation
Law itself Complete — made by legislature Incomplete — details to be supplied by delegate
What is delegated Power to determine if/when conditions are fulfilled — time, area, manner Power to make rules — fill in details — wider discretion
Delegation of legislative power None — no element of delegation of legislative power Yes — some portion of legislative power is delegated
Challenge ground NOT open to attack for excessive delegation Open to attack on ground of excessive delegation
Classic example Queen v Burah (1878): Lt. Governor given power to decide when to bring law into force in Garo Hills Hamdard Dawakhana: Rules to be made specifying diseases — delegate completing the law
Three Categories of Conditional Legislation (ITC Bhadrachalam)
Category I
Law complete — future applicability to given area left to subjective satisfaction of delegate (when is right time to apply Act)
Category II
Act complete — delegate applies it uniformly to given area at appropriate time — pure conditional legislation based on subjective satisfaction
Category III
Delegate's satisfaction must be based on objective consideration of rival classes' data — one class benefits, another loses — natural justice / consultation cannot be excluded — quasi-judicial in character
📝 When your exemption harms someone else, the process must be fair — not just the Government's private decision.
⚠️ CURRENT STATUS OF DOCTRINE Conditional legislation = now largely redundant — the greater (delegated legislation) includes the lesser (conditional legislation). Courts increasingly use term "delegated legislation." Still pops up occasionally. Supreme Court in Lachmi Narain: "no useful purpose served by calling a power conditional legislation instead of delegated legislation."
📝 The concept was a colonial compromise. Now that full delegation is accepted, calling something "conditional legislation" vs "delegated legislation" makes little practical difference — except the former cannot be attacked for excessive delegation.
Vasu Dev Singh v UOI (SC 2006) — Clean Summary
"In conditional legislation the delegatee has to apply the law to an area or to determine the time and manner of carrying it into effect... The legislature in such a case makes the law, which is complete in all respects but the same is not brought into operation immediately."
Retrospective effect — A. Thangal Kunju Musaliar (SC 1956)
Conditional legislation can be exercised with retrospective effective — notification fixing commencement of Act from an earlier date (after its passing but before notification) = not retrospective — no vested rights affected — valid
4.11 — Need for Doctrine of Excessive Delegation
🛡️ Why the Doctrine of Excessive Delegation Matters
Not mere formalism — a vital tool for democratic governance
📝 This doctrine says: Parliament cannot hand over all law-making to bureaucrats. It must set the policy itself and only delegate the details. The doctrine keeps bureaucracy in check and democracy alive.
Four Practical Purposes of the Doctrine
Purpose 1
Preserves role of elected representatives — important social policy choices must be made by legislature (most responsive to people), not by bureaucracy
Purpose 2
Forces legislature to provide "intelligible principle" guiding delegate's discretion — controls exercise of delegated power
Purpose 3
Safety valve — provides courts a judicial tool to control delegation beyond legitimate degree — obligates legislature to formulate broad policies itself
Purpose 4
Enables courts to apply doctrine of ultra vires effectively — if statute sets precise standards, court has a workable benchmark to test validity of delegated legislation
GWALIOR RAYON (SC) — STRONG WARNING
Khanna J: "The rule against excessive delegation of the legislative authority flows from and is a necessary postulate of... sovereignty of the people... delegation unlimited may invite despotism." — Delegation of unlimited power "may on critical occasions be subversive of responsible government and erosive of democratic order."
📝 An all-powerful executive that can make any law it wants — without Parliamentary limits — is despotism. The doctrine prevents that.
⚠️ INDIA-SPECIFIC CONCERN In India: (i) Consultation in rule-making is not normal; (ii) Parliament exercises little effective control over delegated legislation; (iii) Party system makes parliamentary control nominal; (iv) Judicial control is minimal — courts broadly interpret delegating provisions. Hence doctrine of excessive delegation has great significance in India.
💡 GST context: CBIC's rule-making under CGST Act §164 is extensive. The doctrine of excessive delegation is the key tool to challenge rules/circulars that go beyond the policy framework set by Parliament in the CGST Act.
AJOY KUMAR BANERJEE v UOI (SC 1984) — Synthesis
"Legislature must retain in its own hand the essential legislative function which consists in declaring the legislative policy and laying down the standard... what can be delegated is the task of subordinate legislation which by very nature is ancillary to the statute... delegation is valid only when the legislative policy and guidelines to implement it are adequately laid down."
ROJER MATHEW v SOUTH INDIA BANK (SC 2019)
Finance Act 2017 §184 (Tribunal appointments): No guidelines on qualification, eligibility, experience of Chairpersons/Members delegated entirely to Government → suffered from vice of excessive delegation → struck down
💡 GSTAT: Appointment provisions for GSTAT members must prescribe adequate qualifications — if entirely left to Government notification, challengeable on Rojer Mathew principle.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter IV | Delegated Legislation
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, First Appellate Authority, and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Gwalior Rayon v Asstt. Commissioner of Sales Tax
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1974 SC 1660 | (1974) 4 SCC 98
Key Ratio
"The rule against excessive delegation of the legislative authority flows from and is a necessary postulate of the sovereignty of the people. Delegation unlimited may invite despotism. Constitutional legitimation of unlimited power of delegation to the Executive by the Legislature may on critical occasions be subversive of responsible government and erosive of democratic order."
When to Use — When challenging CBIC Circular or Rule as exceeding power delegated under CGST Act. Argue: Parliament set policy; delegate exceeded that policy; uncanalised power is unconstitutional.
Ajoy Kumar Banerjee v UOI
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1984 SC 1130 | (1984) 3 SCC 127
Key Ratio
"The Legislature must retain in its own hand the essential legislative function which consists in declaring the legislative policy and laying down the standard which is to be enacted into a rule of law with sufficient clearness, and what can be delegated is the task of subordinate legislation which by very nature is ancillary to the statute which delegates the power to make it effective. Delegation is valid only when the legislative policy and guidelines to implement it are adequately laid down and the delegate is only empowered to carry out the policy within the guidelines laid down by the legislature."
When to Use — When a CBIC rule or circular creates a new obligation not found in CGST Act. Argue: the essential legislative function (declaring policy) was Parliament's; CBIC can only fill details — it cannot create substantive obligations beyond the Act.
Hindustan Paper Corp. Ltd. v Government of Kerala
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1986 SC 1541 | (1986) 3 SCC 398
Key Ratio — On Exemption Clauses
"In almost all the statutes by which the fiscal or economic interests of the State are regulated, provision for granting exemption in appropriate cases would have necessarily to be there... Such exemptions cannot ordinarily be granted secretly. A notification would have to be issued and published in the Gazette and in the ordinary course it would be subject to the scrutiny by the Legislature. The power can be exercised only in the public interest as provided by the section itself."
When to Use — When arguing that a GST exemption was granted or withdrawn arbitrarily. Cite to establish: exemption power must be exercised in public interest, must be notified publicly, and is subject to legislative scrutiny.
Bengal Iron Corp. v CTO
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1993 SC 2414 | 1994 Supp (1) SCC 310
Key Ratio — On Henry VIII / Removal of Difficulty Clause
The power under a "removal of difficulty" clause is to be used not for "altering" the provisions of the Act but only "for giving effect" to those provisions. What was taxable under the Act could not be exempt from taxation by the government order. "The said power cannot be utilised for dispensing with the levy created by the Act, over a class of goods or a class of persons, as the case may be."
When to Use — When a CBIC "removal of difficulty" order under CGST Act §172 purports to exempt a taxable supply or alter tax liability. Cite to challenge: the order goes beyond implementation — it changes the fiscal policy set by Parliament.
NCJ Mills Co. v Asstt. Collector, Central Excises
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1971 SC 454 | (1970) 2 SCC 820
Key Ratio — On Cross-Statutory Application
"The power to restrict and modify does not import the power to make essential changes" and "it is confined to alterations of a minor character and no change in principle is involved." "No question is thus involved of delegation either of any essential legislative functions or any change of legislative policy."
When to Use — When CBIC applies provisions of one section/Act to another context through a notification or circular making essential changes. Cite to argue: modification power only covers minor adjustments, not changes in principle.
Rojer Mathew v South India Bank Ltd.
Supreme Court of India | (2019) 8 SCC 368
Key Ratio — On Excessive Delegation in Tribunal Appointments
Finance Act 2017 §184 providing for appointment of Tribunal members without laying down any guidelines on qualification, eligibility criteria, experience — entirely delegated to Government — suffered from the vice of excessive delegation and accordingly struck down.
When to Use — When challenging GSTAT member appointment provisions or any delegated legislation that sets no qualification/eligibility criteria and leaves everything to Government discretion. This is the latest authority on excessive delegation.
International Spirits & Wines Association of India v State of Haryana
Supreme Court of India | (2019) 20 SCC 294
Key Ratio — Procedural Rules Cannot Enlarge Substantive Provisions
Procedural rules can neither enlarge the substantive provision of an Act nor curtail the same. Rules that vested wider powers on the Excise Commissioner than the State Government itself amounted to impermissible delegation — the amendment to the Rules was consequently struck down.
When to Use — When CGST Rules or CBIC circular purports to create an obligation or right not found in the CGST Act itself. Cite: rules are procedural — they cannot enlarge or curtail the substantive provision of the parent Act.
Petroleum and Natural Gas Regulatory Board v Indraprastha Gas Ltd.
Supreme Court of India | AIR 2015 SC 2978 | (2015) 9 SCC 209
Key Ratio — Power Must Be Conferred Expressly
"The Board has not been conferred such a power as per section 11 of the Act. That is the legislative intent. Section 61 enables the Board to frame Regulations to carry out the purposes of the Act and certain specific aspects have been mentioned therein... On a scanning of the entire Act and applying various principles, we find that the Act does not confer any such power on the Board... As the entire Regulation centres around the said subject, the said Regulation deserves to be declared ultra vires, and we do so."
When to Use — When challenging a CBIC/GST Council circular or rule that addresses a subject not covered by any specific provision of CGST Act. Argue: power to regulate a specific subject must be expressly conferred — cannot be derived by implication where Act specifically enumerates what the delegate can do.
Hamdard Dawakhana v UOI
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1960 SC 554 | (1960) 2 SCR 671
Key Ratio — Distinction: Conditional Legislation vs Delegated Legislation
"The distinction between conditional legislation and delegated legislation is this that in the former the delegate's power is that of determining when a legislative declared rule of conduct shall become effective... In other words by delegated legislation the delegate completes the legislation by supplying details within the limits prescribed by the statute and in the case of conditional legislation the legislation is exercised by the legislature conditionally leaving to the discretion of an external authority the time and manner of carrying its legislation into effect."
When to Use — When distinguishing between a rule that is merely implementing an Act (conditional/valid) vs a rule that is substantively creating new obligations (delegated/subject to excessive delegation challenge).
Vasu Dev Singh v UOI
Supreme Court of India | (2006) 12 SCC 753
Key Ratio — Delegated Legislation Definition
"Delegated legislation involves delegation of rule making power of legislation and authorises an executive authority to bring into such an area by reason thereof. The direction conferred on the executive by way of delegated legislation is much wider. Such power to make rules or regulations, however must be exercised within the four corners of the Act. Delegated legislation, thus, is a device which has been fashioned by the legislature to be exercised in the manner laid down in the legislation itself."
When to Use — Foundational statement when making any argument about the limits of rule-making power. Emphasize the phrase "within the four corners of the Act" — any rule going beyond is ultra vires.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter 5 — Judicial Control Over Delegated Legislation

⚖️ Chapter 5 — Judicial Control Over Delegated Legislation

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Intra Vires
Prohibited / Ultra Vires / Invalid
Condition / Ground / Unless
Optional / Alternative Route
Neutral Reference / Statute
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
5.1 Need for Controls
5.2 Competent Court/Tribunal
5.3 Judicial Control — Grounds
5.3.1 Constitutionality of Parent Act
5.3.2 Constitutionality of Delegated Legislation
5.3.3 Doctrine of Ultra Vires
5.3.4 Non-compliance with Court Orders
5.3.5 Non-application of Mind
5.4 Substantive Ultra Vires
5.4.1 Control of Rule-maker's Discretion
5.4.2 Implied Restrictions
5.4.2.1 Unreasonableness
5.4.2.2 Rules Excluding Court's Jurisdiction
5.4.2.3 Financial Levy
5.4.2.4 Retrospectivity
5.5 Procedural Ultra Vires
5.6 Grounds for Plea of Ultra Vires
5.7 Effect of Ultra Vires
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
5.1 — Need for Controls over Delegated Legislation
⚖️ Judicial Control Over Delegated Legislation
Chapter V | Principles of Administrative Law
📝 Delegated legislation = Laws made by the government/bureaucracy (not Parliament directly). Because bureaucrats — not elected representatives — make these rules, normal democratic safeguards are absent. Hence strong controls are essential.
WHY CONTROLS ARE NECESSARY
NATURE
Delegated legislation is bureaucratic legislation — transfer of legislative power from Parliament to the Administration
📝 Parliament cannot manage every detail — so it delegates rule-making to government departments. But those departments are not elected — hence undemocratic.
DANGER
Normal democratic safeguards of a representative legislature are ABSENT in delegated legislation
OBJECTIVE
Controls must ensure: (a) benefits of delegated legislation are utilised; AND (b) its disadvantages are minimised
TYPES OF CONTROLS
1. Judicial Control (primary)
2. Legislative Control
3. Procedural Controls (publication, consultation)
FIRST PLACE
In the entire control-mechanism, Judicial Control occupies the first place. Courts review delegated legislation on grounds including: violation of Constitution; violation of parent Act; violation of mandatory procedure; bad faith; arbitrariness; non-conformity with other statutes.
5.2 — Competent Court/Tribunal
🏛️ Which Court/Tribunal Can Rule on Validity of Delegated Legislation?
PRINCIPLE
In every case, examine the statutory context to determine whether the court or tribunal hearing the case has jurisdiction to rule on a defence based on invalidity of subordinate legislation or administrative act
📝 Not every court can strike down every rule. The court hearing the case must first check whether it has the power to question the delegated legislation. Some tribunals may be barred from doing so by statute.
EXCEPTION
Parliament may legislate to preclude such challenges — in the interest of promoting certainty about the legitimacy of administrative acts on which the public may have to rely
📝 Sometimes Parliament deliberately says: "This rule cannot be questioned in court." This is called an ouster clause. It limits judicial review to protect administrative finality.
5.3 — Judicial Control: Grounds for Questioning Subordinate Legislation
📋 Grounds on Which Subordinate Legislation Can Be Questioned
Indian Express Newspapers (Bom) Pvt Ltd v UOI | AIR 1986 SC 515
📝 Unlike an Act of Parliament which enjoys high immunity, subordinate legislation (rules, regulations, notifications) can be questioned on MORE grounds — both those applicable to plenary legislation AND additional ones specific to delegated law-making.
VENKATARAMAIAH J — Grounds of Challenge (Indian Express Newspapers)
(a)
Lack of legislative competence to make the subordinate legislation
(b)
Violation of Fundamental Rights guaranteed under the Constitution
(c)
Violation of any provision of the Constitution of India
(d)
Failure to conform to the statute under which it is made / exceeding the limits of authority of the enabling Act
(e)
Repugnancy to the laws of the land — any other enactment
(f)
Manifest arbitrariness/unreasonableness — to the extent Court can say "Parliament never intended to give authority to make such rules"
⚠️ KEY DISTINCTION: Arbitrary Action vs. Delegated Legislation
EXECUTIVE ACTION
Test of arbitrary action fully applicable — can be struck down on arbitrariness alone
DELEGATED LEGISLATION
Test of arbitrary action does NOT necessarily apply — must establish manifest arbitrariness to strike down
📝 A rule that merely seems unwise or unfair cannot be struck down. It must be so patently arbitrary that no reasonable legislature would have made it.
STATE OF MP v BHOLA | AIR 2003 SC 1191
Delegated legislation can be declared invalid by Court on two grounds:
(i) It violates constitutional provisions
(ii) It violates the enabling Act — authority exceeded its authority and makes provisions inconsistent with parent Act

Where enabling Act itself permits ancillary/subsidiary functions, delegated legislation cannot be held to be in violation of enabling Act.
PRESUMPTION — ALWAYS APPLIES
There is a presumption in favour of constitutionality/validity of subordinate legislation. Burden is on the challenger to show it is invalid. Unless challenge is based on all applicable parameters, it will not be sustainable.
📝 Courts don't start by assuming a rule is bad. They start by assuming it is good and valid. The person challenging the rule must prove it is illegal — not the government which made it.
💡 GST Application: When an assessee challenges a Notification/Rule (e.g., Rule 86B restricting ITC, or Rule 89(4) formula for export refund) — the assessee must demonstrate manifest arbitrariness or violation of CGST Act, not merely inconvenience. The burden is heavy.
5.3.1 — Constitutionality of the Parent Act
🏗️ What if the Parent Act (Enabling Act) Itself is Unconstitutional?
📝 If the law that gave power to make the rules is itself bad, then everything made under it is also bad — automatically. It is like a tree with a rotten root; the fruit is also poisonous.
IF PARENT ACT IS UNCONSTITUTIONAL
Delegated legislation emanating thereunder will ipso facto be invalid
📝 Ipso facto = automatically, by that very fact itself. No further challenge needed — the rules fall the moment the parent Act falls.
GROUNDS FOR PARENT ACT'S UNCONSTITUTIONALITY
(a) Excessive delegation
(b) Breach of Fundamental Right
(c) Distribution of powers — Centre vs States
ROJER MATHEW v SOUTH INDIAN BANK LTD | (2019) 8 SCC 368
Even Speaker's certificate under Art. 110(3) not conclusive — Judicial Review can determine whether conditions for valid Money Bill were fulfilled
📝 Finance Act 2017 brought in as a Money Bill. SC held that the Speaker's decision is final but not immune from judicial review if the issue is justiciable illegality vs. exempted irregularity.
GOLDEN RULE
Always a presumption in favour of constitutionality. Law will not be declared unconstitutional unless the case is so clear as to be free from doubt.
"To doubt the constitutionality of a law is to resolve it in favour of its validity."

Where two interpretations possible — one valid, one void — prefer the one that makes the law valid. Courts use doctrine of 'reading down' or 'reading into' the provision to make it effective, workable and ensure the object of the Act is attained.
5.3.2 — Constitutionality of Delegated Legislation
⚖️ Parent Act Constitutional — But Delegated Legislation Unconstitutional?
📝 Possible situation: The parent Act is perfectly valid, but the rules/notifications made under it may violate the Constitution. In that case, only the rules are struck down — the parent Act survives.
PRESUMPTION
Presumption in favour of constitutionality of both statutes AND delegated legislation. Court should declare it unconstitutional only when there is clear violation beyond reasonable doubt.
LEADING CASES — Delegated Legislation Struck Down as Unconstitutional
Dwarka Prasad Laxmi Narain v State of UP | AIR 1954 SC 224
UP Coal Control Order, 1953 — certain provisions declared ultra vires as infringing Art. 19(1)(g)
Rashid Ahmad v Municipal Board | AIR 1950 SC 163
Municipal bye-laws held bad under Art. 19(1)(g)
Narendra Kumar v UOI | AIR 1960 SC 430
Non-Ferrous Metals Order, 1958 — even though parent Act valid, order challenged under Art. 19(1)(g). Court: law may not be unconstitutional, but order made thereunder may yet be challenged
KEY PRINCIPLE — Narendra Kumar
A law may not be unconstitutional, but an order made thereunder may yet be challenged under the Constitution — because the law cannot be presumed to authorise anything unconstitutional.
💡 GST Example: CGST Act is constitutional. But if a specific Rule or Notification (e.g., restricting refund of ITC on certain exports) violates Art. 14 or Art. 19(1)(g) — it can be struck down even though the Act itself is valid. Writ petition to High Court is the remedy.
5.3.3 — Doctrine of Ultra Vires
🔍 Doctrine of Ultra Vires — The Core Doctrine of Administrative Law
📝 Ultra vires literally means "beyond the powers." If a rule-maker makes a rule that goes beyond the power given to it by the parent Act — the rule is void. The parent Act both gives power AND limits it.
BASIC PRINCIPLE
An authority is the creature of the law — it has only such powers as are granted to it by law.
Parent statute = (i) Source of authority; AND (ii) Limits on authority.
Action within statutory limits = VALID. Action outside limits = ULTRA VIRES = INVALID.
TWO ASPECTS OF ULTRA VIRES DOCTRINE
SUBSTANTIVE ULTRA VIRES
Delegated legislation goes beyond the scope of authority conferred by parent statute OR is in conflict with it
→ Invalid
📝 The substance/content of the rule is outside the power given. Like a shopkeeper selling goods outside the category of licence granted to him.
PROCEDURAL ULTRA VIRES
Rule-making authority deviates from the procedure prescribed by parent statute for making rules
→ Invalid
📝 Even if the rule is substantively OK, if the procedure was not followed (e.g., mandatory consultation not done, laying before Parliament skipped) — the rule is still invalid.
WHAT ULTRA VIRES IS ABOUT
The doctrine concerns: Is the rule within the scope of authority conferred by parent statute?
Court does NOT concern itself with the wisdom or efficaciousness of the rules.
It is for the rule-maker to decide as a matter of policy how to implement the statute.
Ultra vires is concerned with LEGALITY — not the POLICY underlying the rules.
LIMITATION ON ULTRA VIRES CHALLENGES
Subordinate legislation CANNOT be questioned on the ground of violation of principles of natural justice on which administrative action can be questioned.

DISTINCTION: Legislative function → reasonableness cannot be gone into. Discretionary power given by statute → all grounds of administrative challenge available (non-application of mind, irrelevant considerations etc.)
📝 If Parliament delegates pure legislative function, the delegate's exercise of it is like a mini-legislature. Courts cannot question it on natural justice grounds. But if Parliament delegates a discretion (e.g., to grant exemption in individual cases), that is more like administrative action and can be questioned more broadly.
5.3.4 — Non-compliance with Court Orders
🚫 Government Attempting to Evade Court Directions
RULE
Where the government attempts to evade the directions of the Supreme Court through delegated legislation, the Court will strike it down.
📝 Government cannot use its rule-making power as a backdoor to undo what a court has directed. If rules are framed to circumvent court orders, those rules are invalid.
💡 GST Example: If Supreme Court orders that a particular refund shall be granted, and the government amends the rule to deny that refund — such an amendment would be liable to be struck down as non-compliance with court orders.
5.3.5 — Non-application of Mind
🧠 Rule Made Without Applying Mind to Relevant Facts
GROUND
Delegated legislation may be struck down on the ground of non-application of mind on the part of the delegatee to the relevant facts in taking decisions
📝 The rule-maker must apply its mind to the actual facts and relevant factors before making the rule. A mechanical, rubber-stamp exercise of rule-making power is vulnerable.
💡 GST Example: If the government fixes the valuation rule for a particular service without considering the actual market structure or commercial realities of that sector — the rule may be challenged on non-application of mind.
5.4 — Substantive Ultra Vires: Key Principles
📜 Substantive Ultra Vires — The Rule Goes Beyond the Parent Act
📝 If a rule-maker makes a rule outside the scope of what the parent Act authorises — the rule is substantively ultra vires. It doesn't matter if the rule seems reasonable or beneficial — if it exceeds the power given, it is void.
STATE OF KARNATAKA v H. GANESH KAMATH | AIR 1983 SC 550
Rule-making power "cannot include within its scope the power to make a rule contrary to the provisions of the Act conferring the rule-making power."

Conferment of rule-making power does NOT enable the rule-making authority to make a rule which:
(a) Travels beyond the scope of the enabling Act; OR
(b) Is inconsistent therewith; OR
(c) Is repugnant thereto.
RENUSAGAR POWER CO | AIR 1988 SC 1737
"If the exercise of power is in the nature of subordinate legislation, the exercise must conform to the provisions of the statute. All the conditions of the statute must be fulfilled."
THREE-FOLD TEST BY COURT IN APPLYING ULTRA VIRES DOCTRINE
STEP 1
Determine the meaning of the words used in the Act itself to describe the delegated legislation which the delegate is authorised to make
STEP 2
Determine the meaning of the subordinate legislation itself
STEP 3
Decide whether the subordinate legislation complies with the description in the enabling Act
GENERAL RULE-MAKING POWER + SPECIFIC ILLUSTRATIVE HEADS
PRINCIPLE
When a general rule-making power is given AND specific heads are listed — the specific heads are ILLUSTRATIVE, NOT EXHAUSTIVE. The general clause is the real source of power. Specific heads do not restrict general power. A rule justifiable under general power is valid even if not relatable to any enumerated specific head.
📝 Example: If an Act says "Government may make rules for carrying out purposes of the Act" and then lists specific examples like "registration, fees, procedure" — even if a new rule doesn't fit any of these examples, it can still be valid under the general power — as long as it promotes the purposes of the Act.
DOES "REGULATE" INCLUDE "PROHIBIT"?
FLEXIBLE MEANING
Courts have interpreted "regulation" both ways — restrictively (excluding prohibition) and broadly (including prohibition). No fixed rule.

State of TN v M/s Hind Stone | AIR 1981 SC 711: Word "regulating" in section 15 of Mines & Minerals Act interpreted broadly to include "prohibiting" — rule banning private granite quarrying upheld.

K. Ramanathan v State of TN | AIR 1985 SC 660: Word "regulating" in Essential Commodities Act — includes prohibition. "Regulate" is word of wide import, broad meaning, comprehensive scope.
💡 GST Application: Rule restricting ITC (e.g., Rule 86B) — Government can argue "regulation" of ITC includes restriction. Assessee must show restriction is beyond any reasonable interpretation of "regulating ITC" under Section 49/50 CGST Act.
⚠️ WHY ULTRA VIRES CHALLENGES OFTEN FAIL
(a) Rule-making power delegated in broad and general terms → difficult to hold any rule falls outside the purview
(b) Skeletal legislation → legislature only lays down barest outlines, gives no guidance → ultra vires doctrine becomes extremely difficult to apply
(c) Courts adopt benevolent, indulgent attitude toward delegated legislation — lean toward validity
(d) Courts interpret delegating provision broadly, making it easier to uphold rules as intra vires

In modern legislation, rule-making powers are usually delegated in very broad terms → declaration of rules ultra vires becomes a rarity.
5.4.1 — Control of Rule-maker's Discretion (Subjective Rule-making Power)
🎯 What if Rule-making Power is Conferred in Subjective Terms?
📝 Some statutes say: "The authority may make rules as appear to it to be necessary or expedient for giving effect to the provisions of the Act." This is subjective — the authority decides what is "necessary." But this does NOT mean unlimited power.
WRONG ASSUMPTION — MUST AVOID
Subjective formula does NOT give authority a carte blanche to make any rule it sees fit. The doctrine of ultra vires is NOT excluded. Discretion given is NEVER completely unfettered.
📝 Carte blanche = unlimited power. Courts say: even if you have wide discretion, whatever you do must be capable of being related to one of the prescribed purposes of the Act.
PRIVY COUNCIL — A-G FOR CANADA v MALLET & CAREY LTD | (1952) AC 427
"Parliament has chosen to say explicitly that he (the delegate) shall do whatever things he may deem necessary and advisable. That does not allow him to do whatever he may feel inclined, for what he does must be capable of being related to one of the prescribed purposes."
CUSTOMS & EXCISE v CURE AND DEELEY | (1961) 3 ALL ER 641 — Landmark English Case
Commissioners authorised to make regulations for "any matters for which provision appears to them to be necessary" for giving effect to the Act.
A regulation barring access to courts was challenged.

Court held: Even subjective formula does NOT make commissioners sole judge of extent of their powers. The regulation-making power is given to promote the policy and objects of the Act — a regulation to be valid should be capable of being related to the specified or underlying purposes of the legislation.

Court's test: Is there any nexus or connection between the subsidiary legislation and the purposes underlying the parent Act? If no nexus → regulation is invalid.
💡 GST Example: If CBIC issues a circular or notification under the wide power of Section 168 CGST Act "as may be necessary for the implementation of this Act" — it still cannot do something that has no nexus with the purposes of the CGST Act. A notification that amounts to taxation not authorised by the Act = ultra vires even under subjective formula.
5.4.2 — Implied Restrictions on Rule-making Power
🔒 Even Wide Rule-making Power Has Implied Restrictions
📝 Even when Parliament gives the government very broad rule-making power, there are certain implied (unstated) limits. Courts have recognised several such implied restrictions over time.
KEY IMPLIED RESTRICTIONS — Overview
5.4.2.1
Unreasonableness
(Manifest Arbitrariness)
5.4.2.2
Rules Excluding Court's Jurisdiction
5.4.2.3
Financial Levy (Tax)
— No implied power
5.4.2.4
Retrospectivity
— Generally not implied
5.4.2.1 — Unreasonableness / Manifest Arbitrariness
⚡ Can Delegated Legislation Be Struck Down as Unreasonable?
IN ENGLAND
Judges say: "Parliament never intended authority to make such rules. They are unreasonable and ultra vires."
Unreasonableness = separate express ground
IN INDIA
Arbitrariness is NOT a separate ground — it comes within the embargo of Article 14.
Subordinate legislation must be so arbitrary that it cannot be said to be in conformity with the statute or it offends Article 14.
📝 In India, if a rule is so unreasonable it amounts to manifest arbitrariness, it fails under Article 14 (equality). You don't need a separate "unreasonableness" ground.
KEY STANDARD — Indian Express Newspapers
Delegated legislation to be struck down as unreasonable must be manifestly arbitrary — a law which could not be reasonably expected to emanate from an authority delegated with law-making power.

Tests of arbitrary action applicable to executive actions do NOT necessarily apply to delegated legislation. Standard is higher.
ARTICLE 14 — MOST POTENT TOOL
Article 14 has emerged as the most potent constitutional provision to control rule-making. Delegated legislation has been declared invalid on grounds of being: arbitrary; discriminatory; or vesting unguided and uncontrolled discretion in the government.

Secretary, Ministry of Chemicals v Cipla Ltd | AIR 2003 SC 3078: Government must make real and earnest attempt to apply the criteria laid down by itself. Delegated legislation that follows policy formulation must be broadly and substantially in conformity with that policy — otherwise vulnerable on ground of arbitrariness violating Article 14.
💡 GST Example: If Rule 89(4) for export refund uses a formula that gives a result so disproportionate to actual tax paid that it amounts to manifest arbitrariness — it can be challenged under Art. 14 as violating the substantive equality guarantee.
5.4.2.2 — Rules Excluding Court's Jurisdiction
🚪 Can Delegated Legislation Bar Access to Courts?
GENERAL RULE
General rule-making power does NOT give implied power to make rules that exclude access to courts or exclude jurisdiction of courts.
Barring court access is a matter of "substantive" right — requires express specific statutory authority.
📝 Courts jealously guard the right of access to justice. If a rule tries to bar a person from going to court, it needs very specific and clear statutory permission — the general rule-making power is not enough.
💡 GST Example: Rule purporting to make a particular authority's decision final and unchallengeable (ouster clause in delegated legislation) — would be ultra vires general rule-making power of Section 164 CGST Act, and challengeable by writ petition.
5.4.2.3 — Financial Levy / Tax by Delegated Legislation
💰 Can a Tax/Levy Be Imposed by Delegated Legislation?
STRICT RULE — No Implied Power to Tax
General rule-making power does NOT carry with it an implied power to impose financial levies or taxes. Tax statutes must be strictly construed.
Authority to tax must be expressly conferred by the parent Act — it cannot be implied from a general enabling provision.
📝 Taxation is a sovereign function that requires explicit legislative backing. Courts will not "read in" a power to tax from general rule-making authority. No tax without express legislative authorisation.
💡 GST Example: CGST Act specifically authorises certain levies. If CBIC tries to impose a new kind of charge or levy through a Rule or Notification without specific enabling provision in the Act — it would be ultra vires financial levy power.
5.4.2.4 — Retrospectivity of Delegated Legislation
⏪ Can Delegated Legislation Operate Retrospectively?
GENERAL RULE — No Implied Retrospectivity
Delegated legislation cannot operate retrospectively unless there is express or necessary implication of such power in the parent Act.
Retrospectivity must be expressly conferred — it cannot be implied from general rule-making power.
📝 Retrospective = giving effect to a rule from a date in the past. This is especially harmful when it imposes new obligations or liabilities on past transactions. Courts will not allow this unless Parliament explicitly said so.
💡 GST Example: If a Notification retrospectively amends the classification of a product (e.g., changing HSN of solar panels from exempt to 12% from a past date) — it would be ultra vires unless CGST Act specifically empowers retrospective amendment through notifications. Can be challenged by writ.
5.5 — Procedural Ultra Vires
📋 Procedural Ultra Vires — Ignoring Mandatory Procedure
📝 Even if the substance of the rule is fine, if the procedure prescribed for making it was not followed — the rule can be struck down as procedurally ultra vires. The procedure protects the public from arbitrary rule-making.
TYPES OF PROCEDURAL REQUIREMENTS IN PARENT ACT
PRIOR CONSULTATION
Parent Act may require consultation with affected parties / advisory bodies before making rules
If skipped → Procedural Ultra Vires → Rule invalid
PRIOR PUBLICATION / NOTICE
Some Acts require prior publication of draft rules for public comment
If skipped → Rule invalid (subject to whether requirement is mandatory or directory)
LAYING BEFORE PARLIAMENT
Many Acts require rules to be laid before Parliament after making. Parliament may have power to modify or annul.
Compliance with laying does NOT confer immunity — rules can still be challenged as ultra vires. But non-laying may be a procedural defect.
APPROVAL / SANCTION
State Govt. rules may require prior concurrence/sanction of Central Govt.
Without Central Govt. concurrence → State rules invalid — substantive restriction on competence
MANDATORY vs. DIRECTORY
Not all procedural requirements are mandatory. Courts distinguish between mandatory (compliance compulsory, non-compliance = invalidation) and directory (substantial compliance sufficient) requirements.
General principle: If procedural requirement is for the benefit of the public or affected persons → likely mandatory. If merely a formality → likely directory.
💡 GST Example: Section 164(5) CGST Act requires rules to be laid before Parliament within 15 days of making. Non-compliance with this may be a procedural defect — but courts may still consider whether it is mandatory or directory based on the purpose of the requirement.
5.6 — Grounds for the Plea of Ultra Vires (Consolidated)
📌 Complete Checklist — All Grounds on Which to Challenge Delegated Legislation
Ground Legal Basis / Principle Standard Required GST Relevance
1. Lack of legislative competence Rule-maker had no power to make this rule under the parent Act Show the rule falls outside the scope of enabling power CGST Rules made outside Section 164 CGST Act; or Rules under IGST Act that are outside Section 22 IGST Act
2. Violation of Fundamental Rights Arts. 14, 19, 21 — Rules cannot infringe fundamental rights Demonstrate clear violation of guaranteed rights Rule 86B restricting ITC affecting right to carry on business — Art. 19(1)(g) challenge
3. Violation of Constitution Any constitutional provision, not just fundamental rights Show clear constitutional violation beyond reasonable doubt E.g., inter-state supply classification violating Art. 286 principles
4. Failure to conform to parent Act Rule inconsistent with/contrary to enabling Act provisions Demonstrate direct inconsistency Rule 89(4) export refund formula producing results contrary to Section 54 CGST Act
5. Repugnancy to other laws Rule conflicts with another statute on the same subject Show conflict with plenary legislation GST Rule conflicting with Customs Act provisions on import valuation
6. Manifest arbitrariness / Art. 14 Rule so arbitrary that Parliament never intended such power Must be MANIFESTLY arbitrary — high threshold Valuation rules producing absurd results; anti-profiteering methodology challenged as arbitrary
7. Non-application of mind Delegatee did not apply mind to relevant facts before making rule Show irrelevant factors considered or relevant factors ignored Notification fixing rate without considering industry cost structure
8. Non-compliance with Court orders Rule designed to circumvent court directions Show nexus between rule and court order being evaded Retrospective amendment to undo court-ordered refund
9. Procedural ultra vires Mandatory procedure for making rules not followed Identify mandatory procedural requirement; show non-compliance Rules made without required GST Council recommendation
10. Mala fides (Bad faith) Rule-maker's real object is improper — to confer benefit on individual or harm a class Difficult to establish; show improper motive Rare — requires strong evidence of ulterior motive behind notification
5.7 — Effect of Ultra Vires + Miscellaneous Principles
⚡ What Happens When a Rule is Found Ultra Vires?
GENERAL EFFECT
Ultra vires rule is void ab initio — treated as never having existed. All actions taken under it are without legal authority.
📝 Void ab initio = void from the very beginning. If a rule is struck down as ultra vires, it is as if it was never made. Demands, assessments, penalties made under it — all collapse.
SEVERABILITY
If only part of rule is ultra vires, courts may sever that part and uphold the rest — provided the valid and invalid parts are separable (not inextricably mixed)
📝 Like removing the rotten part of a fruit and eating the rest. If the invalid part can be cut out cleanly, the rest of the rule may survive.
INTRA VIRES PRESUMPTION
Courts start with presumption that rule is intra vires. If open to two constructions — one valid, one invalid — must adopt the construction that makes it valid.
Courts may "read down" the rule.
WRONG LABEL / WRONG SECTION CITED
If rule-maker wrongly cites wrong provision but has power under another provision — rule is NOT invalid merely because of wrong label. Crucial question: Does the authority have power? Not which section cited.
📝 Mere clerical error in citing the enabling section does not invalidate the rule if the power actually exists elsewhere in the Act.
COURTS DO NOT JUDGE POLICY — ONLY LEGALITY
"So long as the body entrusted with the task of framing the rules or regulations acts within the scope of authority conferred on it... the court should not concern itself with the wisdom or efficaciousness of such rules or regulations. It is exclusively within the province of the legislature and its delegate to determine, as a matter of policy, how the provisions of the statute can best be implemented... It is not for the courts to examine the merits or demerits of such a policy."
— Supreme Court
5.7.1 REPEAL OR MODIFICATION OF RULES
Ultra vires rules can also be addressed through: (a) Repeal by rule-maker; (b) Modification by court order requiring rule-maker to amend; (c) Judicial invalidation.

However: Court will NOT direct a particular rule to be enacted — that would be a policy decision reserved for the executive. Courts can declare a rule invalid; they cannot legislate in its place.
💡 GST Example: Court can strike down Rule 89(4) export refund formula — but cannot prescribe the correct formula itself. It can direct the government to formulate a proper, non-arbitrary rule consistent with Section 54 CGST Act.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 5 | Judicial Control Over Delegated Legislation
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, First Appellate Authority, GSTAT, and High Courts. State them confidently and cite the case name and court.
Indian Express Newspapers (Bom) Pvt Ltd v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1986 SC 515 | Venkataramaiah J | (1985) 1 SCC 641
Key Ratio — Grounds for Challenging Subordinate Legislation
"A piece of subordinate legislation does not carry the same degree of immunity which is enjoyed by a statute passed by a competent legislature. Subordinate legislation may be questioned on any of the grounds on which plenary legislation is questioned. In addition it may also be questioned on the ground that it does not conform to the statute under which it is made... It may also be questioned on the ground that it is unreasonable, unreasonable not in the sense of not being reasonable, but in the sense that it is manifestly arbitrary. In England, the Judges would say 'Parliament never intended authority to make such rules. They are unreasonable and ultra vires.'"
When to Use — Foundation argument whenever challenging any GST Rule or Notification. Cite to establish that Rules and Notifications under CGST Act do NOT enjoy the same immunity as the Act itself and can be challenged on multiple grounds including non-conformity with parent Act and manifest arbitrariness.
State of Karnataka v H. Ganesh Kamath
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1983 SC 550 | (1983) 2 SCC 402
Key Ratio — Scope of Rule-making Power
"Conferment of rule-making power by an Act does not enable the rule-making authority to make a rule which travels beyond the scope of the enabling Act or which is inconsistent therewith or repugnant thereto."
When to Use — When a GST Rule or Notification exceeds the power under CGST Act / IGST Act. E.g., Rule prescribing valuation method inconsistent with Section 15; Notification expanding taxable event beyond Section 7; Rule restricting ITC beyond Section 17 — cite to argue the rule travels beyond the scope of the enabling Act.
State of UP v Renusagar Power Co (Renusagar Case)
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1988 SC 1737 | (1988) 4 SCC 59
Key Ratio — All Conditions of Statute Must Be Fulfilled
"If the exercise of power is in the nature of subordinate legislation, the exercise must conform to the provisions of the statute. All the conditions of the statute must be fulfilled."
When to Use — When challenging a GST Notification or Rule on procedural grounds (e.g., Rule made without GST Council recommendation, Notification not following prescribed conditions). Also useful when arguing that a Rule does not satisfy all conditions imposed by parent section (e.g., Section 54 conditions for refund — Rule cannot add conditions not in Section 54).
Customs & Excise Commrs v Cure and Deeley
Queen's Bench Division, England | (1961) 3 All ER 641 | Landmark case on subjective rule-making
Key Ratio — Even Subjective Power Not Unlimited
"A court is bound, before reaching a decision on the question whether a regulation is intra vires, to examine the nature, objects and scheme of the piece of legislation as a whole, and in the light of their examination to consider exactly what is the area over which powers are given by the section under which the competent authority is purporting to act." The question therefore for the courts to consider is whether there is any nexus or connection between the subsidiary legislation in question and the purposes underlying the parent Act. If no such nexus can be found then the regulation is invalid.
When to Use — When Department argues CBIC's wide power under Section 168 CGST Act ("as may be necessary for implementation of this Act") justifies a particular circular or notification. Counter: Even subjective rule-making power requires nexus with the purposes of the Act — if the circular/notification has no such nexus, it is ultra vires.
Secretary, Ministry of Chemicals & Fertilizers v Cipla Ltd
Supreme Court of India | AIR 2003 SC 3078 | (2003) 7 SCC 1
Key Ratio — Government Must Follow Its Own Policy Criteria
The Government exercising its delegated legislative power should make a real and earnest attempt to apply the criteria laid down by itself. The delegated legislation that follows the policy formulation should be broadly and substantially in conformity with that policy, otherwise it would be vulnerable to attack on the ground of arbitrariness resulting in violation of Article 14.
When to Use — When a GST Notification departs from the criteria/policy announced by the government itself (e.g., Circular says one thing but Notification implements differently; or GST Council recommendation is implemented in a manner inconsistent with the Council's stated rationale). Argue manifest arbitrariness and Article 14 violation.
Narendra Kumar v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1960 SC 430 | (1960) 2 SCR 375
Key Ratio — Order Under Valid Act May Still Be Unconstitutional
Though a law may not be unconstitutional, an order made thereunder may yet be challenged under the Constitution, because the law could not be presumed to authorise anything unconstitutional.
When to Use — When Department argues CGST Act is constitutional (true) therefore Rules and Notifications under it cannot be challenged (false). Counter with Narendra Kumar: Even under a valid Act, individual Rules and Notifications can be challenged under the Constitution on grounds of Articles 14, 19, or 21 — the Act cannot be presumed to authorise unconstitutional rules.
Bombay Dyeing & Mfg Co Ltd v Bombay Environmental Action Group
Supreme Court of India | (2006) 3 SCC 434 (para 104)
Key Ratio — Subordinate Legislation Must Be Reasonable and In Good Faith
"A subordinate legislation apart from being intra vires the Constitution, should not also be ultra vires the parent Act under which it has been made. A subordinate legislation, it is trite, must be reasonable and in consonance with the legislative policy as also give effect to the purport and object of the Act and in good faith."
When to Use — Comprehensive citation covering all requirements of valid delegated legislation. Use in writs challenging GST Rules to establish the three-pronged test: (a) intra vires Constitution; (b) intra vires parent Act; AND (c) reasonable and in consonance with legislative policy and in good faith.
GP Singh's Principles of Statutory Interpretation (10th Edn, p. 916)
Authoritative Treatise | Cited and affirmed by Supreme Court in Kerala Sansthana Chethu Thozhilali Union v State of Kerala (2006) 4 SCC 327
Key Ratio — Grounds of Judicial Review of Delegated Legislation
"Delegated legislation is open to the scrutiny of Courts and may be declared invalid particularly on two grounds: (a) Violation of the Constitution; and (b) Violation of the enabling Act. The second ground includes within itself not only cases of violation of the substantive provisions of the enabling Act, but also cases of violation of the mandatory procedure prescribed. It may also be challenged on the ground that it cannot be said to be in conformity with the statute or Article 14 of the Constitution or that it has been exercised in bad faith."
When to Use — Cite before GSTAT or High Court as a comprehensive summary of grounds. Particularly useful in written submissions where you need to set out the legal framework before applying it to the specific Rule/Notification being challenged. GST Notification ultra vires CGST Act = falls squarely under ground (b).
Ajay Kumar Bannerjee v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1984 SC 1130 | (1984) 3 SCC 127
Key Ratio — Purpose of Delegation Must Be Borne in Mind; Restrictive Interpretation
"The purpose or object of the conferment of the power must be borne in mind." The Court pointed out that "the authority and scope for subordinate legislation can be read in either of the two ways; namely one which creates wider delegation and one which restricts that delegation." In the peculiar facts of this case, "the one which restricts the delegation must be preferred to the other."
When to Use — Rare but powerful argument where two interpretations of rule-making power are possible. When the GST Rule can be read broadly or narrowly — argue for the narrow reading that restricts the delegation to its true purpose. This is one of the few cases where SC consciously adopted the restrictive interpretation of delegation. Relevant when challenging overly broad exercise of CBIC's notification powers.
Hotel Balaji v State of Andhra Pradesh
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1993 SC 1048 | 1993 Supp (4) SCC 536 (para 31)
Key Ratio — Rules Cannot Travel Beyond the Purview of the Act
"The necessity and significance of the delegated legislation is well accepted and needs no elaboration at our hands. Even so, it is well to remind ourselves that rules represent subordinate legislation. They cannot travel beyond the purview of the Act. Where the Act does not say so, the rules do not become part of the Act."
When to Use — When Department argues that a GST Rule has the same force as the Act and therefore cannot be questioned. Counter: Rules are subordinate legislation — they cannot travel beyond the purview of the CGST Act. Where a Rule imposes liability not authorised by the Act — the Rule, not the Act, must yield. Use in ITC denial cases where Rule 86B or Rule 36(4) goes beyond Section 17 CGST Act.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter V — Judicial Control Over Delegated Legislation

⚖️ Chapter V — Judicial Control Over Delegated Legislation

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Upheld
Prohibited / Invalid / Ultra Vires
Condition / Unless / If
Exception / Alternative / Discretionary
Neutral Statutory Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
5.4.2 Implied Restrictions — Overview
5.4.2.1 Unreasonableness
5.4.2.2 Excluding Court's Jurisdiction
5.4.2.3 Financial Levy
5.4.2.4 Retrospectivity
5.5 Procedural Ultra Vires
5.6 Grounds for Ultra Vires Plea
5.7 Effect of Ultra Vires
5.8 Exemption from Operation of Rules
5.9 Statutory Exclusion of Judicial Review
5.10 Conclusive Evidence Clause
5.11 Miscellaneous Principles
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
5.4.2 — Implied Restrictions (Substantive Ultra Vires)
⚖️ Implied Restrictions — Doctrine of Substantive Ultra Vires
Courts go beyond express words of enabling provision to imply restrictions
📝 Courts don't just read what the law says — they also read in what Parliament could NOT have meant to allow. If a rule crosses those unspoken limits, it is void even if the enabling Act does not expressly prohibit it.
Purpose of Implied Restrictions
Preservation of individual liberty
Strengthening judicial control over delegated legislation
Broader dimension to doctrine of ultra vires
Four Key Implied Restrictions recognised by Courts:
① UNREASONABLENESS
Unreasonable rules = ultra vires
② COURT JURISDICTION
Cannot exclude courts by delegated legislation
③ FINANCIAL LEVY
No tax/charge through general rule-making power
④ RETROSPECTIVITY
No retrospective rules unless parent Act authorises
5.4.2.1 — Unreasonableness as Implied Restriction
🚫 Unreasonableness — An Implied Restriction on Delegated Legislation
Kruse v Johnson (1898) 2 QB 91 | Lord Russell's Doctrine
📝 Unreasonable rules made by a subordinate legislature are void — because Parliament never intended to give power to make irrational or oppressive rules. But "unreasonable" has a strict legal meaning — it does not mean "judges disagree with it."
🇬🇧 Position in England — Kruse v Johnson Test
UNREASONABLE = VOID
Bye-law is unreasonable if:
(a) Partial/unequal operation between different classes
(b) Manifestly unjust
(c) Disclosed bad faith
(d) Oppressive / gratuitous interference with rights that no reasonable man could justify
📝 These are the ONLY situations where a court calls a rule "unreasonable." This is a high bar — mere inconvenience or disagreement is NOT enough.
NOT UNREASONABLE
Bye-law NOT unreasonable merely because:
• Judges think it goes further than necessary
• Not accompanied by qualifications judges would prefer
• Judges personally disagree with it
DIPLOCK LJ — Mixnam Properties (1964)
All grounds on which subordinate legislation can be void are particular applications of the general rule that subordinate legislation must be shown to be within powers conferred by statute — manifest arbitrariness, injustice or partiality that Parliament never intended to authorise.
Departmental Rules vs Bye-Laws — Different Standards?
EARLIER VIEW
Unreasonableness test applied only to bye-laws of local authorities, NOT to departmental rules (minister accountable to Parliament)
SETTLED NOW — McEldowney v Forde (1969)
Ministerial regulations can be held void on grounds of vagueness, ambiguity, arbitrariness, uncertainty, unreasonableness, bad faith
🇮🇳 Position in India — Broader Foundation
📝 In India, unreasonableness has a much wider base — not just English common law, but also Article 14 (equality) and Article 19 of the Constitution. Any arbitrary rule violates Article 14 and is void. Indian courts have more flexibility than English courts in striking down unreasonable rules.
Air India v Nergesh Meerza (SC 1981)
Rule retiring air hostess on first pregnancy after marriage — "most unreasonable and arbitrary, shocks conscience, extremely detestable, open insult to Indian womanhood" — violates Art. 14
Railway Board Rule
Rule taking away vested right of railway employee retrospectively with no objective found — arbitrary and void
Civil Judge — Age 48 Rule
Rule requiring civil judge to be below 48 for promotion — quashed — no nexus between age restriction and promotion criteria — irrational
Re 1/- Subsistence Allowance
Rule prescribing Re.1/- per month to government servant under suspension — unreasonable, illusory, meaningless — void
⚠️ COURT STANDARD
Court must need a strong case made against a bye-law before striking it down. Court will not determine whether it would have been wiser to make it differently, nor hold it unreasonable because framers overlooked considerations the court would have regarded. "The responsible representative body must ordinarily be presumed to know what is necessary, reasonable, just and fair."
💡 GST Application: If the GST Council frames a notification creating a differential rate between similar goods/services and the assessee challenges it as arbitrary — the Kruse v Johnson standard applies. Court will strike it down ONLY if the distinction has NO nexus to any intelligible differentia — not merely because the rate seems unfair.
5.4.2.2 — Rules Excluding Court's Jurisdiction
🏛️ Implied Restriction — Cannot Exclude Courts by Delegated Legislation
Cure & Deeley | Raymond v Honey | Harbhajan Singh v State of Punjab
📝 Only Parliament (through a clear statute) can take away your right to go to court. A subordinate legislator — like a ministry or board — CANNOT do this by making rules, even if the enabling Act gives a general rule-making power. Citizens' access to courts is a fundamental safeguard.
✗ INVALID — Cure & Deeley (1961)
Customs Commissioners made regulation: if proper return not filed, they themselves determine tax due — no appeal to court.
Court: Excluding assessee from courts is REPUGNANT to the general nature and scheme of the Act → Regulation invalid.
"Normal scheme of Act is to define goods/transactions and leave disputes to court — not bypass it."
📝 Even if the Act says "make regulations for any matters as necessary" — this general power cannot be used to take away the right to approach a court. That right needs an explicit statute to remove.
💡 GST: If GST Rules provide that the GST Officer's determination of ITC disallowance is "final and not challengeable" — such a rule would be ultra vires for excluding judicial review.
PRINCIPLE — Raymond v Honey (HL 1983)
Citizen's right to unimpeded access to courts can ONLY be taken away by express enactment (Act of Parliament)
→ A rule fettering prisoner's right of access to court = ultra vires
INDIA — Harbhajan Singh v State of Punjab (2020)
Even if civil court's jurisdiction excluded by principal enactment, writ jurisdiction under High Court is ALWAYS available — power of judicial review cannot be ousted by delegated legislation
PARLIAMENTARY COMMITTEE
Parliamentary Committee on Subordinate Legislation has obligation to draw attention of House if court's jurisdiction is excluded through a rule
5.4.2.3 — Financial Levy — Implied Restriction
💰 No Tax / Financial Levy through General Rule-Making Power
AG v Wilts United Dairies | Hymal Chandra v AUDA | Mines & Minerals Act Cases
📝 A government body cannot impose a tax, fee, or charge on citizens just because it has a general power to "make rules." To levy any money from people, the parent statute must SPECIFICALLY authorise that levy. This is a safeguard against taxation without parliamentary authority.
CORE RULE
No tax/charge/financial levy can be imposed by any bye-law, rule or regulation made under general rule-making power
Parent statute must specifically authorise such imposition
AG v Wilts United Dairies (1922)
Food Controller imposed levy per gallon of milk without express power to do so in regulation.
Court: Executive can justify charge on subject ONLY if Legislature authorised it in clear terms. Levy invalid.
SC India — Fees for Services
For levy of fee to be valid, power to charge fees must be expressly conferred by law on executive. Cannot be implied from general rule-making power.
EXCEPTION — Mines & Minerals Act
SC held: Power to make rules for regulating grant of mining leases includes power to fix consideration (royalty) payable by lessee. Act amended in 1972 to expressly confer this power.
📝 SC took a broader view here — but this is an exception to the general rule. Parliament later expressly confirmed the power by amending the Act.
GST APPLICATION
CGST Rules can prescribe fees only where CGST Act expressly authorises levy. Any Rule levying fee/late fee/penalty beyond what the parent Act authorises is ultra vires and void.
Example: Late fee under Rule 68 is valid only because Section 47 CGST Act expressly authorises it.
💡 GST: Rule levying "processing fee" for GST registration without any enabling provision in CGST Act → Ultra vires. Cannot be sustained. Assessee can refuse to pay and challenge the Rule.
5.4.2.4 — Retrospectivity of Delegated Legislation
📅 Retrospectivity — Implied Restriction Against Retrospective Rules
Hukam Chand v UOI | Bakul Cashew | State of Bihar v Krishna Kumar Kabra
📝 Parliament can make laws with retrospective effect (affect past transactions). But a subordinate legislator CANNOT — unless the parent Act expressly or by necessary implication gives that power. People act on the faith of existing laws; changing past rights by subordinate rules is unfair and void.
CORE RULE — SC in Hukam Chand v UOI
"An authority which has the power to make subordinate legislation cannot make it with retrospective effect unless it is so authorised by the Legislature which has conferred that power on it."
REASON
Retrospective rules may prejudicially affect vested interests and rights. People act on faith of existing law. Rules ought not to change the character of past transactions.
EXCEPTIONS — When Retrospective Effect is Permissible
Beneficent Notifications
Notification conferring benefit on tax payers — can be given retrospective effect (Mathra Pd. & Sons v State of Punjab)
Clarificatory Notifications
A clarificatory notification (not amending substantive rights) can be given retrospective effect
Article 309 Service Rules
Service rules under Art. 309 may be retrospective — rule-making power co-extensive with legislative power (B.S. Vadera v UOI)
Quasi-judicial purpose (HC Suman)
Retrospective notification to effectuate order of quasi-judicial body — upheld — not unreasonable, not mala fide, within rule-making power
NEW PRINCIPLE — BS Yadav v State of Haryana
Retrospective operation of a rule will be struck down if there exists no reasonable nexus between the concerned rule and its retrospective application.
Nexus may be shown from face of the rule or by extrinsic evidence.
📝 Even when Art. 309 allows retrospective rules, the court will strike down the retrospective effect if no good reason exists for going back in time. The government cannot use retrospective service rules as "a plaything" to benefit specific individuals.
CaseRule / NotificationResult
Hukam Chand v UOI Central Govt. amended rules with retrospective effect — no such power in parent statute INVALID — no retrospective power in statute
Bakul Cashew Co. v STO Retrospective notification exempting cashew industry from sales tax INEFFECTIVE — delegated legislation cannot impose tax retrospectively
UOI v M/s GS Chataha Mill (2021) 200% customs duty on Pakistan goods — no date of exclusion specified Effective from date immediately when passed — not retrospective — upheld
AV Nachane v UOI Retrospective amendment of rules to nullify earlier court writ INVALID — cannot nullify writ by retrospective amendment; rules operate prospectively only
State of Rajasthan v Basant Agrotech (2014) Notification enhancing mineral tax rate with retrospective effect from 1 Apr 2008 INVALID — words "from time to time" are prospective; parent Act does not confer retrospective power
GST APPLICATION
CBIC notifications/circulars issued retrospectively to demand tax or deny ITC for past periods — challengeable on this ground unless CGST Act expressly authorises. Clarificatory circulars can be retrospective; amendatory ones cannot without statutory backing.
💡 GST: A notification dated 1 Oct 2023 imposing GST on a service from 1 Jul 2017 — without an enabling provision in CGST Act for retrospective taxation — is ultra vires. Assessee can challenge demands for the period prior to the notification.
5.5 — Procedural Ultra Vires
📋 Procedural Ultra Vires — Failure to Follow Prescribed Procedure
Raza Buland Sugar Co. v Rampur Municipality | AIR 1965 SC 895
📝 Delegated legislation is also invalid if the rule-maker did not follow the correct procedure laid down in the enabling statute — for example, failing to publish draft rules for objections, or not consulting required bodies. Whether this matters depends on whether the procedure was "mandatory" or merely "directory."
Key Question: Is the Procedural Norm Mandatory or Directory?
MANDATORY
Must be strictly / meticulously observed
Non-observance = rules ultra vires and void
Usually indicated by word 'shall'
📝 Mandatory = No shortcut. If the statute says "shall consult the mining board" — that MUST be done. Skipping it voids the rules.
DIRECTORY
Substantial compliance is sufficient
Non-compliance does not necessarily void rules
Usually indicated by word 'may'
📝 Directory = Close enough counts. Minor procedural lapses in directory steps can be overlooked if no prejudice caused.
CAUTION — 'Shall' / 'May' not conclusive
Whether 'shall' is mandatory or 'may' is directory depends on CONTEXT and PURPOSE of the provision — not just the word used. The object of the statute making the provision is the determining factor. (Raza Buland Sugar Co. v Rampur Municipality)
📝 Example: 'May' in Section 12(1) Mines Act = Directory (Central Govt. has discretion to constitute mining board). But 'shall' in Section 59(3) Mines Act = Mandatory (draft regulations MUST be sent to every mining board). Same Act — different results based on context.
Procedural NormCategoryEffect if Violated
Consultation with affected interests / mining board before rule-making MANDATORY Rules void if not consulted (Kalipada v UOI)
Opportunity to file objections against proposed rules MANDATORY Rules invalid if opportunity denied (Rajnarain Singh v Chairman)
Pre-publication of draft rules MANDATORY Failure to pre-publish = rules invalid (Raza Buland)
Three months' notice required by statute for rule effectiveness MANDATORY Rules ineffective without notice (Lachmi Narain v UOI)
Publication of delegated legislation in gazette MANDATORY Rule unenforceable if not published (Govindlal v APMC)
Simple 'laying before legislature' procedure DIRECTORY Non-compliance does not void rules
Clarification to be issued by "public notice" under Export-Import Policy (Para 16) MANDATORY Clarification not issued by public notice — cannot be sustained (UOI v Shree Ganesh Steel)
AUTHOR'S COMMENT
It is NOT proper for courts to mitigate the importance of procedural norms. Legislature does not lay down procedural norms for merely ornamental purposes. Saying a procedure is "directory" and need not be followed amounts to negativing legislative intent.
💡 GST: CGST Rules provide for opportunity of being heard before adverse orders. If GST Officer passes assessment order without any hearing — this is a mandatory procedural requirement — order is void. Challenge on procedural ultra vires grounds.
5.6 — Grounds for Plea of Ultra Vires
⚔️ Grounds on Which Delegated Legislation Can Be Challenged as Ultra Vires
①
Absence of power to issue directions / make the rule at all
②
Power exists but exercised to provide relief NOT contemplated in parent Act
③
Conflict with parent statute or Constitutional provision
④
Violation of Fundamental / Constitutional Rights (Arts. 14, 19)
⑤
Unreasonableness / Arbitrariness / Vagueness
⑥
Encroachment on legislative field not within rule-maker's province (federal structure)
Key Case Illustrations
Power Machines India v State of MP (2017)
MSME Rules — treating Facilitation Council award as arrears of revenue (Act only provided for decree of court) — UPHELD. Plural remedies further Act's mandate of quick, inexpensive relief.
VC Banaras Hindu University v Shrikant
VC terminated teacher service — only Executive Council had power. VC had no jurisdiction. Even subsequent approval by competent authority made no difference. Ultra vires plea succeeded.
Tata Sky v State of MP (2013)
Notification levying entertainment duty on DTH subscriptions. Parent Act levied duty only on "admission to entertainment." Court: Notification cannot enlarge charging section. Demand invalid.
📝 A notification/rule cannot expand what the parent Act says can be taxed. The parent Act is the boundary.
SETTLED PRINCIPLE — State of UP v Mahindra & Mahindra (2011)
"It is inappropriate for the courts to issue a mandate to legislate an Act and also to make a subordinate legislation in a particular manner. Each organ must function within its domain — Judiciary, Executive, Legislature." High Court CANNOT direct how a notification should be worded.
💡 GST: GST Notification defining "restaurant services" cannot go beyond what Section 9 CGST Act empowers. If notification taxes something that is exempt under the Act, the notification is ultra vires. Challenge it directly.
5.7 — Effect of Ultra Vires Declaration
💥 Effect When Delegated Legislation Declared Ultra Vires
VOID
Delegated legislation declared ultra vires = void and incapable of having any effect on rights and duties of concerned parties
BINDING EFFECT
Decision directly binding only as between parties to proceedings — BUT doctrine of precedent extends benefit to all persons whose legal rights were interfered with by the declared legislation
PRESUMPTION
Until challenged and invalidated — presumption of validity of delegated legislation and legality of acts done in pursuance thereof
Severability — Valid and Invalid Parts of a Rule
IF SEVERABLE
Only invalid portion quashed — valid portion continues to operate
IF INEXTRICABLY MIXED
Entire rule set aside — cannot separate valid from invalid
Other Important Principles
NO REVIVAL
Invalidation of superseding order does NOT revive earlier order (Indian Express case)
VOID RULE — NO OFFENCE
No offence committed by infringing an invalid (void) rule
VOID RULE — NO ADMIN ACTION
Void rule cannot be the basis of any administrative action
WAYS TO CHALLENGE
Directly through writ petition OR collaterally by way of defence to civil claim OR as defence in prosecution
5.11.2 — VOID RULE
A rule which is void remains void — regardless of how long it has been in force without challenge. A void rule does NOT become valid by lapse of time. A void rule remains void whether acquiesced in or not.
📝 Silence = not acceptance. Just because no one challenged a bad rule for 10 years does not make it good law. It remains void and can be challenged at any time.
💡 GST: If a GST Rule has been operating for years but is ultra vires the CGST Act — it can still be challenged in a writ petition today. The long period of operation creates no estoppel.
5.8 — Exemption from Operation of Rules
🔓 Exemption from Operation of Rules — Limits
✗ NO RELAXATION
Rules mandatory in nature CANNOT be relaxed — cannot be dispensed with, especially for high-rise buildings
POSSIBLE EXCEPTION
One or two-storeyed buildings — minor deviations from Rules that do not affect public safety and convenience — different position may apply
✗ GOVT CANNOT DEVIATE
Government cannot deviate from prescribed procedure when granting exemption from operation of Rules
GST LINK
CBIC cannot grant exemptions from GST provisions by mere circular or instruction — exemptions must comply with Section 11 CGST Act procedure. Administrative relaxations beyond what Section 11 permits are without authority.
5.9 — Statutory Exclusion of Judicial Review | "As If Enacted" Clause
🔒 Attempts to Exclude Judicial Review — Can They Succeed?
Hoffman-La Roche | Hukam Chand | State of Kerala v KM Charia Abdulla
📝 Sometimes, Parliament places rules before both Houses (the "laying" procedure) or says rules will have effect "as if enacted in the Act itself." People argue this makes rules unchallengeable. Both arguments fail — courts retain full power to declare ultra vires rules invalid.
5.9.1 — Parliamentary Laying Procedure — Does It Oust Courts?
✗ NO OUSTER
Doctrine of ultra vires is NOT excluded by laying procedure. Rules laid before Parliament remain subject to ultra vires challenge. 'Laying' requirement has NO impact on legal validity of rules.
(Hukam Chand v UOI | Indian Express)
LORD DIPLOCK — Hoffman-La Roche
"Courts have jurisdiction to declare rule invalid even if contained in order approved by both Houses of Parliament — whether ultra vires by reason of contents (patent defects) OR defects in procedure (latent defects)"
INDIA — SC ruling
"Parliament is concerned with merits or policy underlying rules; the Court with the legality. Parliament does not consider whether Minister's action was lawful — this is a matter for the Court."
5.9.2 — 'As If Enacted' Clause — Effect
📝 Some statutes say rules "shall have effect as if enacted in the Act itself." The idea is — since Acts of Parliament are unchallengeable, if rules are "part of the Act," they should also be unchallengeable. This argument was initially accepted in England but later overruled.
OVERRULED — Lockwood Doctrine
House of Lords initially in Institute of Patent Agents v Lockwood (1894) — rules given full statutory force, unchallengeable.
OVERRULED in Minister of Health v The King ex parte Yaffe (1931) — delegated legislation inconsistent with parent Act cannot be "part of the Act" — ultra vires doctrine applies regardless.
INDIA — Settled Position
State of Kerala v KM Charia Abdulla & Co. (SC 1965): "If rule-making authority transcends limits of power conferred, rules are invalid and 'as if enacted' clause can attach no additional sanctity."

Two conditions for rule to have effect of statutory provision:
(1) Must conform to provisions of statute
(2) Must come within scope of rule-making power
If either not fulfilled — rule is VOID.
CONCLUSION
