No formula — 'as if enacted,' 'laying before Parliament,' or any other — has succeeded in completely barring courts from reviewing delegated legislation on ground of ultra vires. Courts jealously preserve their jurisdiction as safeguard against improper use of statutory powers.
💡 GST: Even if CGST Rules say they have effect "as if part of the CGST Act" — a rule that goes beyond what the Act empowers remains challengeable. The formula does not cure ultra vires defects.
5.10 — Conclusive Evidence Clause
🔏 'Conclusive Evidence' Clause — Effect on Judicial Review
Raza Buland Sugar Co. | Sitapur Municipality v Prayag Narain | Berar Swadeshi Vanaspati
📝 Sometimes a statute says: "When rules are notified, the notification shall be conclusive evidence that the rules have been duly made in accordance with the Act." This is an attempt to make rules unchallengeable on procedural grounds. Courts have partially accepted this — but with clear limits.
✗ CANNOT CURE
• Complete lack of jurisdiction in authority to frame rules (substantive ultra vires)
• Infringement of mandatory procedural norms (of a fundamental character)
• Deviation causing substantial prejudice to affected persons
📝 Even a "conclusive evidence" clause cannot paper over a situation where the rule-maker had NO power at all. Jurisdictional error is never condoned by courts.
✓ CAN CURE
• Deviation from directory (not mandatory) procedural norms
• Minor procedural irregularities not causing substantial prejudice
• Technical defects in procedure
(Raza Buland | Sitapur Municipality v Prayag Narain)
📝 Example: Municipality published draft tax proposal in newspaper along with draft rules but not as a separate notice — cured by conclusive evidence clause as no substantial prejudice caused.
HIDAYATULLAH J — Raza Buland
"It lays down emphatically a rule of evidence which precludes courts from making inquiries into the minutiae of procedure with a view to declaring the imposition invalid." But only as to directory, not mandatory, defects. Substantial compliance with directory norms → cured.
CMP CHARACTERISATION
Committee on Ministers' Powers characterised "conclusive evidence" clause as "objectionable" — doubted whether it was ever "justified." If interpreted narrowly → serves no purpose (directory lapses already immune). If interpreted broadly → negates mandatory safeguards and frees executive from all legal restraints.
5.10.1 — Bagla Case
A formula protecting rules from being invalidated for inconsistency with any other statute was upheld in Harishankar Bagla v State of MP — as power to override existing laws was itself enacted by Parliament in the Essential Supplies Act (Essential Commodities Act, 1955). Formula again upheld in AV Nachane v UOI. BUT: No retrospective rules could nullify effect of earlier Supreme Court writ.
5.11 — Miscellaneous Principles in Delegated Legislation
📚 Miscellaneous Principles — Sections 5.11.1 to 5.11.12
5.11.1 Repugnance Between Rules
Later subordinate legislation under one Act impliedly repeals earlier subordinate legislation under another Act if both cover same subject-matter and cannot operate simultaneously.
Status of delegate irrelevant — both are delegates of legislature.
📝 Later rule wins over earlier rule when both cover the same ground — even if made by different government bodies.
5.11.3 Explanatory Notes = Part of Rules
Explanatory notes appended to rules are treated as PART of the rules — having same legal effect. (Tara Singh v State of Rajasthan)
Notes provide procedure, control discretion, fill gaps where rules are silent.
5.11.4 Non-exercise of Rule-Making Power
Court CANNOT compel government to make rules if it chooses not to — rule-making is permissive. (AK Roy v UOI | Indian Express)
BUT: mandamus can issue to compel government to CONSIDER whether time to bring provision into force has arrived. (Aeitemesh Rein v India)
5.11.5 Repeal of Parent Act
If parent Act is repealed or invalidated — delegated legislation automatically falls to the ground. Exception: Repealing Act may continue rules till new rules framed.
5.11.6 Interpretative Rules
Administrative interpretation of a statutory provision has persuasive but NOT binding effect on courts. Courts are free to put their own meaning. Long-standing administrative interpretation may have some evidentiary weight but is never conclusive.
📝 Circulars and notifications giving government's own interpretation of rules are helpful but courts are NOT bound by them. Court rejected even a long-standing administrative interpretation as "untenable" (Oudh Sugar Mills).
5.11.7 Power to Relax Rules
Relaxation cannot be read into a provision where the rule itself mandates maximum period. Municipal authorities cannot relax/condone delay in auction payment outside what the rules permit. Rules prescribing qualifying examination for promotion — relaxation by note = contrary to settled service jurisprudence.
5.11.9 Court Cannot Direct Amendment
Court has NO power to direct amendment of Act or Rules. Act can be amended only by Legislature; Rules only by the empowered authority. Court's role = declare invalid; not substitute its own version.
📝 Court can strike down a bad rule — but cannot write a new rule in its place. That power belongs to Parliament/Government.
5.11.10 Challenge to Validity
Challenge to validity of rules does NOT require impleading all persons likely to be affected. Validity decided on merits — consequences flow automatically to all.
5.11.11 — Interpretation of Delegated Legislation (Purposive Approach)
State of West Bengal v RKBK Ltd.
Limitation period in Control Order — "30 days from date of order" — interpreted purposively. An order adverse to dealer becomes effective only on COMMUNICATION (not mere passing). Period of limitation runs from date of communication.
📝 Delegated legislation is interpreted to give meaningful opportunity to affected parties — not to defeat their rights through technical readings. Courts apply purposive interpretation to subordinate legislation just as to parent statutes.
💡 GST: Limitation period for appeal starts from date of communication of order to assessee — not from date of internal passing of order by officer. This principle from West Bengal v RKBK supports arguing that limitation runs from date of receipt, not date of order.
5.11.12 — Non-Interference in Expert Body's Recommendation
HANDS-OFF APPROACH
Recommendation made by statutory expert body (like TAFRC — Fee Regulatory Committee) — accepted by State Government — courts should be LOATH to interfere unless: (i) arbitrariness, (ii) irrationality, (iii) perversity, or (iv) violation of any provisions of law under which constituted.
Court cannot sit as appellate authority entering arena of disputed facts to opine how TAFRC ought to have proceeded.
Brij Mohan Lal v UOI — Tests for Judicial Interference in Policy Decisions
TestStandard
I. Reasonableness Policy fails test of reasonableness → unconstitutional
II. Fairness in change Change in policy must be made fairly — not arbitrarily or with ulterior motive
III. Malafides / Arbitrariness Policy faulted on grounds of mala fide, unreasonableness, arbitrariness, unfairness
IV. Conflict with statute/Constitution Policy against statute or Constitution or its philosophy → struck down
V. Dehors legislation Policy dehors (goes beyond) provisions of Act → struck down
VI. Excess delegation Delegate acted beyond power of delegation → struck down
Norma Within these, court will NOT substitute its wisdom for government's policy choices
💡 GST: When challenging a GST Council recommendation or CBIC circular on policy — frame the challenge under Tests I–VI above. Don't argue "this policy is unwise" — argue it is arbitrary/ultra vires/violates CGST Act provisions. That is the only way courts will intervene.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter V | Judicial Control Over Delegated Legislation
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Kruse v Johnson (1898) 2 QB 91 | Lord Russell
Queen's Bench Division, England | Landmark on Unreasonableness of Delegated Legislation
Key Ratio
"The courts should jealously watch the exercise of these powers, and guard against their unnecessary or unreasonable exercise to the public disadvantage." A bye-law would be unreasonable if it is partial and unequal in its operation as between different classes, if it is manifestly unjust, if it disclosed bad faith, or if it involved such oppressive or gratuitous interference with the rights of those subject to them as could find no justification in the minds of reasonable men.
When to Use — When challenging a GST notification or rule as arbitrary, discriminatory or manifestly unjust. Establish that the rule goes beyond what any reasonable Parliament could have intended to authorise.
Customs & Excise Commissioner v Cure & Deeley (1961) 3 All ER 641
England | Exclusion of Court's Jurisdiction by Delegated Legislation
Key Ratio
Excluding the subject from having access to the court to have the issues determined was held to be repugnant to the general nature, objects and scheme of the Act. The normal scheme of the Act was to define the goods and transactions which attracted the tax and leave it to the courts to decide disputes between the executive and the subject in the normal way. The regulation constituted an attempt to assume arbitrary power to determine a tax liability and amounted to an attempt to oust the court's jurisdiction.
When to Use — When a GST Rule or circular purports to make the officer's determination "final" or bars the assessee from approaching courts. Cite to argue that such provision is ultra vires — only Parliament (by express enactment) can oust court's jurisdiction.
Hukam Chand v UOI | AIR 1972 SC 2427 : (1972) 2 SCC 601
Supreme Court of India | Retrospective Effect of Delegated Legislation
Key Ratio
"An authority which has the power to make subordinate legislation cannot make it with retrospective effect unless it is so authorised by the Legislature which has conferred that power on it." A mere general power to make rules "to carry out the purposes of the Act" does not entitle the rule-making authority to make retrospective rules. The 'laying' requirement also would not confer validity on such retrospective rules.
When to Use — When a GST notification, circular or amendment to GST Rules is applied retrospectively to demand tax or deny ITC for past periods. Cite to argue that without express retrospective power in CGST Act, the retrospective application of delegated legislation is void.
State of Rajasthan v Basant Agrotech (India) Limited | AIR 2014 SC 487 : (2013) 15 SCC 1
Supreme Court of India | Retrospective Effect — Tax Notifications
Key Ratio
As the parent legislation does not specifically empower fixation and imposition of tax retrospectively, the notification which is a delegated legislation cannot have retrospective operation. The notification so far as it covers the period prior to the date of publication of the notification in the official gazette is really a transgression of the statutory postulate. The words "from time to time" have a prospective tenor and do not have the etymological potentiality to operate from a previous day.
When to Use — When Department seeks to apply a new GST notification/amendment to periods before its publication date. Cite to argue notification cannot have retrospective effect without explicit power in CGST Act.
State of Kerala v KM Charia Abdulla & Co. | AIR 1965 SC 1585
Supreme Court of India | 'As If Enacted' Clause — Ultra Vires Still Applies
Key Ratio
"If in making a rule, the State transcends its authority, the rule will be invalid, for statutory rules made in exercise of delegated authority are valid and binding only if made within the limits of the authority delegated. Validity of a rule whether it is declared to have effect as if enacted in the Act or otherwise is always open to challenge on the ground that it is unauthorised." Two conditions for a rule to have effect of a statutory provision: (1) it must conform to the provisions of the statute; (2) it must come within the scope and purview of the rule-making power. If either not fulfilled — rule is void.
When to Use — When Department argues a GST Rule has "statutory force equivalent to the Act" and cannot be challenged. Cite to rebut: Even rules with "as if enacted" formula are void if ultra vires the CGST Act.
Raza Buland Sugar Co. v Rampur Municipality | AIR 1965 SC 895 : (1965) 1 SCR 970
Supreme Court of India | Mandatory vs Directory Procedural Norms
Key Ratio
"The purpose for which the provision has been made and its nature, the intention of the legislature in making the provision, the serious general inconvenience or injustice to persons resulting from whether the provision is read one way or the other, the relation of the particular provision to other provisions dealing with the same subject and other considerations which may arise on the facts of a particular case including the language of the provision, have all to be taken into account in arriving at the conclusion whether a particular provision is mandatory or directory." Whether 'shall' is mandatory depends on context — not the word alone.
When to Use — When challenging GST notices/orders passed without following a prescribed procedure (e.g., no opportunity of hearing, no notice before attachment). Cite to argue the procedural requirement was mandatory — non-observance renders the order ultra vires.
Hoffman-La Roche v Secretary of State for Trade and Industry | (1974) 2 All ER 1128
House of Lords, England | Parliamentary Approval Cannot Cure Ultra Vires
Key Ratio (Lord Diplock)
"Despite this indication that the majority of members of both Houses of the contemporary Parliament regard the order as being for the common weal, the courts have jurisdiction to declare it to be invalid if they are satisfied that in making it the Minister who did so acted out with the legislative powers conferred on him by the previous Act of Parliament — and this is so whether the order is ultra vires by reason of its contents (patent defects) or by reason of defects in the procedure followed prior to its being made (latent defects)."
When to Use — When Department argues that a GST notification placed before Parliament/approved by Committee cannot be challenged. Cite Lord Diplock: Parliamentary approval does not cure ultra vires. Courts assess legality; Parliament assesses policy.
Air India v Nergesh Meerza | AIR 1981 SC 1829
Supreme Court of India | Broader Unreasonableness under Article 14
Key Ratio
The Supreme Court characterised the rule as the "most unreasonable and arbitrary provision which shocks the conscience of the court", which is "extremely detestable and abhorrent to the notions of a civilized society," and "contains the quality of unfairness and exhibits naked despotism." In India, unreasonableness doctrine is based on a more solid foundation — not just common law but Article 14 of the Constitution. Courts in India enjoy more flexibility for expanding the concept of unreasonableness.
When to Use — When challenging a GST rule/notification that creates arbitrary, oppressive or shocking discrimination between taxpayers. Invoke Article 14 along with ultra vires doctrine — India's standard is broader than England's Kruse v Johnson test.
Tata Sky Ltd. v State of Madhya Pradesh | (2013) 4 SCC 656
Supreme Court of India | Notification Cannot Enlarge Charging Section
Key Ratio
"It is elementary that a notification issued in exercise of power under the Act cannot amend the Act. The notification cannot enlarge either the charging section or amend the provisions of collection under the Act read with the Rules." Where the parent Act does not allow levy of duty on a particular transaction, a notification cannot create that liability.
When to Use — When a GST notification seeks to levy tax on a transaction/supply that the CGST Act does not cover in its charging sections. The notification is ultra vires — it cannot be a source of new tax liability beyond what the parent Act authorises.
BS Yadav v State of Haryana | AIR 1981 SC 561
Supreme Court of India | Nexus Test for Retrospective Rules
Key Ratio
The retrospective operation of a rule will be struck down if there exists no reasonable nexus between the concerned rule and its retrospective application. Such a nexus may be shown either from the face of the rule or by extrinsic evidence. The Court advised the Government not to make retrospective service rules as it causes frustration, discontent and demoralisation by falsifying just expectations.
When to Use — When challenging any GST amendment or notification given retrospective effect — argue there is no reasonable nexus between the rule and the period to which it is being applied retrospectively. The nexus test applies equally to taxation rules.
Vitarelli v Seaton | 3L Ed 1012 : 359 US 535 (1958) — Cited in UOI v Rajpal Singh (2009) 1 SCC 216
US Supreme Court | Justice Frankfurter | Standards of Conduct for Executive Authority
Key Ratio (as applied in India)
"It is well settled rule of administrative law that an executive authority must be rigorously held to the standards by which it professes its actions to be judged and it must scrupulously observe those standards on pain of invalidation of an act in violation of them."
When to Use — When GST Department deviates from its own circulars, guidelines or stated procedures while passing orders or conducting enquiries. Cite to argue: Department must follow its own standards — violation invalidates the action.
State of West Bengal v RKBK Ltd. | AIR 2015 SC 3411 : (2015) 10 SCC 369
Supreme Court of India | Purposive Interpretation of Delegated Legislation — Limitation Periods
Key Ratio
An order passed by authority which is adverse to the interest of the dealer or agent would not be an effective order unless communicated. The period of limitation must be given a purposive meaning — the order comes into effect from the date of receipt by the dealer/agent. Limitation of 30 days runs from date of communication and becomes effective, not from date of internal passing of the order.
When to Use — When Department argues an appeal or objection is time-barred based on the date the order was passed internally. Cite to argue limitation runs from date of communication/receipt by assessee — not date of internal order. Purposive interpretation of limitation provisions in GST Rules.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter VI — Legislative & Other Controls Over Delegated Legislation

⚖️ Chapter VI — Legislative & Other Controls Over Delegated Legislation

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | Tree Diagram Notes | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Defective
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative Route
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
6.1 Legislative Control — Overview
6.1.1 Memorandum on Delegation
6.1.2 Laying Procedure
6.1.3 Laying Procedure in UK
6.1.4 Laying Requirement: Legal Effect
6.1.5 Laying & Judicial Review
6.1.6 Ordinance Before Parliament
6.1.7 Parliamentary Scrutiny Committees
6.2 Publication of Delegated Legislation
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
6.1 — Legislative Control Over Delegated Legislation — Overview
🏛️ Legislative Control Over Delegated Legislation
Chapter VI | Principles of Administrative Law
Why Legislative Control is Necessary
📝 Parliament gives rule-making power to the Executive. But giving power also means watching over how it is used. Parliament is the watchdog — the Executive is the dog on a leash.
REASON 1
Legislature is the principal — Executive is its agent
Principal must supervise its agent's exercise of power
REASON 2
Administration may ignore individual rights in zeal for administrative convenience
REASON 3
Judicial control is limited — broad language in enabling acts dilutes courts' supervisory power
REASON 4
Courts reluctant to closely supervise delegated legislation — legislative check fills this gap
Two Significant Limbs of Legislative Supervision in India
(i) Laying of delegated legislation before the Legislature
📝 The government must place its rules before Parliament — Parliament then has the opportunity to scrutinise them.
(ii) Scrutiny by a Parliamentary Scrutiny Committee (Subordinate Legislation Committee)
📝 A specialist committee in each House examines rules in detail on behalf of Parliament — like an audit team for delegated laws.
6.1.1 — Memorandum on Delegation
📋 Memorandum on Delegation — First Stage of Control
📝 Before Parliament even passes a law that delegates rule-making power, it must be told clearly what power is being handed over. The Memorandum is the "notice" given to Parliament at the very beginning.
RULE OF PROCEDURE
Rule 70 (Lok Sabha) / Rule 65 (Rajya Sabha): Every bill containing delegation proposals must be accompanied by a Memorandum on Delegation
What the Memorandum Must Cover
(a) Full purport and effect of delegation of power to subordinate authorities
(b) Points which may be covered in the rules
(c) Particulars of subordinate authorities who will exercise the power
(d) Whether delegation is of exceptional or normal character
(e) Manner in which the power is to be exercised
GROUND REALITY
The rule is mandatory (per Lok Sabha Subordinate Legislation Committee) — but in practice, memoranda are usually scrappy, routine, and not very informative. They do not amount to much as a control mechanism.
📝 Parliament is supposed to be warned before giving away rule-making power. But usually the warning is vague and bureaucratic — Parliament passes the bill anyway.
SPEAKER'S POWER
Speaker may refer bills containing delegation provisions to a Committee to examine the extent of powers sought to be delegated.
6.1.2 — Laying Procedure
📜 Laying Procedure — Second Link in Parliamentary Control
📝 After rules are made by the Executive, they must be "laid" — i.e., placed — before Parliament. This is how Parliament gets to know what rules have been made in its name. It is primarily an informational mechanism.
PURPOSE — To bring delegated legislation to the "potential notice" of Members of Parliament (Garner)
No general obligation unless the statute has a laying clause — if no clause, it is optional for government to lay or not to lay.
Three Variants of Laying Procedure (from Statutory Instruments Act, England 1946)
TYPE 1
Simple Laying
Rules must be laid before Parliament — no further action required
Laid before it comes into operation
📝 Parliament just gets a copy — it cannot do anything automatically. Like being sent a FYI memo.
💡 GST analogy: Notification issued under Section 9(3) CGST Act — laid before Parliament for information but valid from date of issuance.
TYPE 2
Laying with Annulment (Negative Laying)
Rules come into force but can be annulled by a negative resolution of a House within specified period
Legitimacy precedes, disallowance follows
📝 Rules are live from day one — but Parliament has a window to say "cancel this." If Parliament does nothing, rules survive.
💡 Most common in India. Standard Indian formula (30 session days): if both Houses agree to modify/annul — rule stands modified/annulled for future, past action protected.
TYPE 3 — MOST EFFECTIVE
Laying Subject to Affirmation (Positive/Affirmative Laying)
Rules laid in draft — come into operation ONLY when Houses pass resolution affirming them
Parliamentary approval is condition precedent
📝 Parliament must actively say "yes" before the rules take effect. The most democratic form — but rarely used in India.
💡 Examples in India: Salaries and Allowances of Ministers Act, 1952; Essential Services Maintenance Act, 1968.
Standard Laying Formula Used in India (Incorporated in Every Central Statute)
"Every rule made under this Act shall be laid, as soon as may be, after it is made, before each House of Parliament, while it is in session, for a total period of thirty days which may be comprised in one session or in two or more successive sessions, and if, before the expiry of the session immediately following the session or the successive session aforesaid, both Houses agree in making any modification in the rule or both Houses agree that the rule should not be made, the rule shall thereafter have effect only in such modified form or be of no effect, as the case may be; so, however, that any such modification or annulment shall be without prejudice to the validity of anything previously done under that rule."
✓ Rules come into force as soon as made — laying is post-facto
✓ 30 session days of laying required (in one or multiple sessions)
CONDITION Both Houses must agree to modify/annul — initiative lies with individual members, not government
✓ PROSPECTIVE ONLY Any modification/annulment is prospective — past action under the rules is protected
EXCEPTION Affirmative Procedure — rules laid in draft before Houses become enforceable only after Houses pass affirmative resolution
CRITICAL OBSERVATION (Author)
In practice, legislative orders are hardly discussed in the House — laying procedure triggers no automatic control. The Court has attributed exaggerated importance to the laying provision — used as a pretence to uphold broad delegations of legislative power.
Problem: Rules Under Central Acts Made by State Governments
CONCURRENT LIST
Three alternatives:
(a) State Legislature rules of procedure
(b) Speaker's direction to State Subordinate Legislation Committee
(c) Central Act amended to require laying before State Legislature
Preferred: Option (c)
UNION LIST
Legal difficulty: Power delegated to State Government not State Legislature
Even if rules laid before State Legislature — only State Government (not legislature) has power to modify
EXTENDED SCOPE (Committee View)
Laying should cover not just rules but all forms of delegated legislation: notifications, regulations, bye-laws — whether by Central Government or any other authority under a Central Act.
6.1.3 — Laying Procedure in UK
🇬🇧 Laying Procedure in UK — Comparative Reference
📝 UK realised early that delegated legislation was growing fast and needed a systematic publication and reference system. India's system largely follows the UK model.
HISTORY
Rules Publication Act, 1893 → replaced by
Statutory Instruments Act, 1946
📝 Statutory Instruments (SIs) are the principal form of delegated/secondary legislation in UK. They replaced the old "Statutory Rules and Orders" in 1948.
TWO CONTROLS
Most SIs subject to Negative Resolution Procedure (more common)
Some subject to Affirmative Resolution (less common but more stringent)
REGULATORY REFORM ACT 2001
Enables government to amend Acts of Parliament to remove burdens — but requires special extra-rigorous affirmative procedure
💡 Example: Regulatory Reform (Sunday Trading) Order 2004 legalised selling methylated spirits on Sunday by amending Revenue Act 1889.
6.1.4 — Laying Requirement: Legal Effect (Mandatory vs Directory)
⚖️ Legal Effect of Laying Requirement — Mandatory or Directory?
📝 "Mandatory" means: if you don't comply, the rule is void/invalid. "Directory" means: if you don't comply, there may be consequences but the rule itself stays valid. Courts have had to decide which category laying falls into.
SIMPLE LAYING
Not coupled with approval requirement
Effect: DIRECTORY
Failure to lay does NOT affect legal validity of rules
Atlas Cycle Industries v State of Haryana (SC)
📝 Even if the Executive forgets to lay rules before Parliament — the rules are still valid and enforceable. Parliament can complain but the law stands.
💡 SC held in Atlas Cycle: Section 3(6) Essential Commodities Act — non-laying of order before Parliament does NOT make order void. Use of word 'shall' not conclusive.
AFFIRMATIVE PROCEDURE
Rules laid in draft + approval required before coming into force
Effect: MANDATORY
Rules CANNOT come into force without being laid and approved
Parliamentary resolution is condition precedent for validity
📝 Here Parliament must say "yes" first. So if the government skips this — rules are void. The procedure is a precondition, not an afterthought.
NEGATIVE RESOLUTION
Rules effective from making — laying is post-facto
Effect: DIRECTORY
Rules come into force immediately. Laying is informational — failure to lay does not void the rule
📝 Like simple laying — the rule works from day one. Parliament's power is reactive (it can annul) but the rule isn't waiting for Parliament's permission.
Two Judicial Considerations for Treating a Provision as Directory (from Atlas Cycle)
(i) Absence of any provision for meeting the contingency of non-compliance
(ii) Serious general inconvenience and prejudice that would result to the public if government act is declared invalid for non-compliance
AUSTRALIA CONTRAST
In Australia, even simple laying procedure is treated as mandatory — laying is part of publication requirements and is an exercise of the legislature's right to check its delegate. Australia's approach is considered more principled.
ORDINANCES — MANDATORY EXCEPTION
Krishnakumar v State of Bihar (2017) 3 SCC 1 (7-Judge Bench)
Laying of Ordinance (issued by President/Governor) before Parliament/Legislature is MANDATORY. Failure to place ordinance before legislature relates back to its creation — ordinance that has not been placed before legislature is of no consequence whatsoever.
📝 Ordinances are special emergency laws — Parliament MUST see them within 6 weeks of next session. If not placed: the ordinance dies. This is different from ordinary rules where non-laying only invites criticism, not invalidity.
💡 GST relevance: If CGST Ordinance (e.g., Section 168A time extension ordinance) is not placed before Parliament in next session — it would be of no consequence. All extensions of limitation granted under it could be challenged.
6.1.5 — Laying Procedure and Judicial Review
🏛️ Laying and Judicial Review — No Immunity Conferred
📝 Does the fact that Parliament has seen and approved rules protect those rules from being challenged in court? The answer is: NO. Parliamentary laying gives no immunity from judicial review. The court's job and Parliament's job are different.
PRINCIPLE
Laying confers NO sanctity or immunity on rules
Rules laid before Parliament still subject to doctrine of ultra vires
📝 Ultra vires = Beyond powers. If rules exceed the authority given by the parent Act, courts will strike them down — even if Parliament saw them and did nothing.
LEGISLATURE ROLE
Legislature looks at merits and policy of rules
Does not probe into question of legality
COURT ROLE
Court probes legality — whether rules fall within parameters of enabling provision
Not concerned with policy underlying rules
Key Cases on Laying and Judicial Review
CaseCourtRatio / Principle
Hukam Chand v UOI
AIR 1972 SC 2427		 Supreme Court Rules cannot be made with retrospective effect — laying requirement could NOT confer validity on such rules. Laying is no cure for substantive illegality.
S.R. Bommai v UOI
1994 (3) SCC 1		 SC — 9-Judge Bench Presidential proclamation (Art. 356) approved by both Houses does NOT stand validated if the proclamation is inherently invalid. "Judicial Review is in no way inhibited by the fact that rules or regulations have been laid before Parliament and approved."
Rojer Mathew v South Indian Bank
(2020) 6 SCC 1		 Supreme Court If parent Act did not spell out necessary qualifications for tribunal heads and left it wholly to executive — placing Rules before Parliament detailing qualifications will not cure the defect. Excess delegation — impermissible. Laying is no cure for excess delegation.
Bharatidasan University v AICTE Supreme Court "The fact that the regulations when made have to be laid down before the Legislature does not confer any more sanctity or immunity as though they are statutory provisions themselves... courts are bound to ignore them when the question of their enforcement arises..."
Quarry Owners' Association v State of Bihar
AIR 2000 SC 2870		 Supreme Court Laying procedure done in three ways: (i) subject to negative resolution, (ii) subject to confirmation, (iii) mere laying. Merely laying notification not sufficient compliance with section 16(2) of Water Pollution Cess Act requiring positive act of approval.
EXCEPTION — UPHOLDING VALIDITY
In some cases, SC has taken into account laying procedure + Parliament's power to suggest modifications (in the enabling statute) to uphold validity of the statute against a challenge of excessive delegation — treating it as a safeguard that Parliament has not abdicated its powers.
6.1.6 — Requirement to Place Ordinance Before Parliament
📋 Ordinance — Mandatory Laying Before Legislature
📝 An Ordinance is emergency legislation promulgated by the President/Governor when Parliament is not in session. It has the same force as a law — but it MUST be placed before Parliament. If not placed, it is as if the Ordinance never existed.
LEADING CASE
Krishnakumar v State of Bihar (2017) 3 SCC 1
7-Judge Constitutional Bench — resolved conflict between earlier decisions
SC RULING
Ordinance laying is MANDATORY — not directory
Failure relates back to its very creation
CONSEQUENCE OF NON-LAYING
Deeming fiction (that ordinance = law) does NOT come into existence
Ordinance not placed before legislature = of no consequence whatsoever
JUDICIAL REVIEW OF ORDINANCE
President/Governor satisfaction (Art. 123/213) is NOT immune from judicial review
Court may interfere if: fraud on power / abuse of power
DEMOCRATIC SAFEGUARD
Ordinance-making power appears undemocratic but is not — Executive is answerable to Legislature; Legislature can pass no-confidence vote
GST CONTEXT — CRITICAL
Section 168A CGST Act time-extension orders were promulgated as Ordinances/notifications during COVID-19. If any such Ordinance was not placed before Parliament in the next session, the extension of limitation it granted would be of no legal consequence. All demands raised under such extended limitation would be time-barred.
💡 Practical argument: In any SCN where Department relies on COVID-19 time extension — verify whether the enabling ordinance/notification was duly laid before Parliament. If not — raise mandatory laying argument based on Krishnakumar.
6.1.7 — Parliamentary Scrutiny Committees (Subordinate Legislation Committees)
🔍 Parliamentary Scrutiny Committees — Organised Watchdog Over Delegated Legislation
📝 Since laying alone is toothless, Parliament created specialist Committees in each House to actually read, scrutinise and report on delegated legislation. These committees are the real "teeth" of parliamentary oversight.
Structure of the Two Committees
Lok Sabha Committee on Subordinate Legislation
Established: 1953 (older)
15 members — appointed by Speaker for a year
Represents all political parties proportionally
Chairman: usually from opposition
Ministers DEBARRED from membership
Decisions: UNANIMOUS — no party politics
Rajya Sabha Committee on Subordinate Legislation
Established: 1964 (newer)
15 members — nominated by Chairman RS
Chairman appointed by Chairman RS
No prohibition on Ministers joining
Holds office until new Committee nominated
9 Criteria for Scrutiny (Rule 320, Lok Sabha Rules)
#Scrutiny CriterionType of Defect Targeted
1Whether order is in accord with general object of Constitution or parent ActConstitutional conformity
2Whether it contains matter that should more properly be in an Act of ParliamentExcess delegation — substantive law in rules
3Whether it contains imposition of any taxFinancial imposition only by statute, not rules
4Whether it directly or indirectly bars jurisdiction of courtsOuster of judicial review — not permissible by rules
5Whether it gives retrospective effect without express power in parent ActRetrospectivity disfavoured — prejudices vested rights
6Whether it involves expenditure from Consolidated Fund / public revenuesFinancial scrutiny
7Whether it appears to make unusual or unexpected use of powersNearest to policy review — broadest discretion given
8Whether there appears to be unjustifiable delay in publication or layingTimely laying insisted — "as soon as may be" means promptly
9Whether form or purport calls for elucidation (complex or ambiguous language)Language clarity — rules must be simple and understandable
Key Positions Taken by the Committees Over the Years
TAX BY RULES
Financial levy (tax, fee, charge) must be by statute — NOT by rules. Financial imposition is privilege of Parliament.
OUSTER OF COURTS
No subordinate legislation should directly/indirectly oust jurisdiction of courts — even if not binding on courts, it creates wrong impression and should not be in rules.
RETROSPECTIVITY
Retrospective operation disfavoured — affects vested rights. If given: explanatory note required stating reasons + confirmation no one adversely affected. Note must be published in gazette to prove no mala fide.
PROCEDURAL SAFEGUARDS
Committees insisted on: natural justice where individual adversely affected, reasons for adverse orders, right of appeal, prior notice, written reasons for decisions.
PUBLICATION
Rules must be published in Gazette of India even when parent Act silent. Committees insisted rules not remain buried in government files.
KALPANA MEHTA v UOI (2018) 7 SCC 1 — SC on Parliamentary Committee Reports
Four important propositions on admissibility of Parliamentary Standing Committee Reports:
(i) Can be used for interpretation of statutory provisions — taken as historical fact
(ii) Judicial notice under Section 57(4) Evidence Act — admissible as public document under Section 74
(iii) Petitioner can collect facts from these Reports as evidence
(iv) Being in public domain — report can invite fair comments/criticism from citizens without privilege violation
💡 GST Application: Parliamentary Standing Committee reports on CGST Bill, Finance Bill etc. can be cited before GST Officers/AAR/GSTAT to interpret legislative intent. Use them in hearings to establish what Parliament intended when granting rule-making power.
LIMITATION OF COMMITTEES
Committees do NOT go into merits or policy of delegated legislation — administrative policy-making is largely immune from their scrutiny. They have no effective sanction — can only report to Parliament. Where Government disagrees and discussions fail — no further recourse for the Committee.
6.2 — Publication of Delegated Legislation
📰 Publication of Delegated Legislation — Essential Validity Requirement
📝 Ignorance of law is no excuse — but this maxim only works if the law is published and accessible. For delegated legislation, publication is even more important than for Acts — because rules are drafted in secrecy by departments, unlike Acts which are debated publicly.
Comparative Framework: UK, USA, India
🇬🇧 ENGLAND
Statutory Instruments Act 1946
SI sent to Queen's Printer — numbered, printed, sold
Non-publication = good defence in prosecution (unless reasonable steps taken to bring SI to notice)
Non-publication does NOT affect civil liability/validity
Publication = constructive notice only
📝 In UK — non-publication of a rule only excuses a person from criminal liability for breach of it. The rule itself stays valid. You can still be sued under it.
🇺🇸 USA
Before 1935: No publication requirement — exposed by Panama Refining Co. v Ryan (1935)
Federal Register Act 1935: All federal rules published in Federal Register (Monday-Friday)
Code of Federal Register: Codified compilation by subject
Failure to publish = regulation unenforceable (but only against person without actual knowledge)
Publication = directory approach (constructive notice)
📝 In USA — if a rule is not published in the Federal Register, it cannot be used against someone who didn't know about it. But if you actually knew, you're bound even without publication.
🇮🇳 INDIA — STRICTER POSITION
No general statutory provision on publication
But publication in Official Gazette = essential requirement for validity
Non-publication affects legal validity itself
Actual notice/constructive notice irrelevant — publication is mandatory
Govindlal case: SC held non-publication of rule in newspaper (as required by statute) = unenforceable even if published in Gazette
📝 India goes furthest: even if you personally know about a rule — if it was not published in the Gazette as required, it is invalid. Publication is not just notice — it is a condition of legal existence.
Key Indian Supreme Court Cases on Publication
CaseIssueRuling / Principle
Harla v State of Rajasthan
AIR 1951 SC 467		 Law remained buried in government files — never published. No law requiring publication at that time. SC held law INVALID. Promulgation/publication of some reasonable sort is essential to make law legally effective. Against natural justice to punish people under law of which they could not have had knowledge.
State of Maharashtra v George
AIR 1965 SC 722		 No statutory requirement for publication — what mode is sufficient? Publication in Gazette of India held sufficient — it is the usual method. Publication creates constructive notice — ignorance by accused irrelevant once law published in India.
Govindlal v Agricultural Produce Marketing Committee
AIR 1976 SC 263		 Statute required double publication: (i) Official Gazette + (ii) local newspaper in Gujarati. Only Gazette publication done. SC declared notification invalid — BOTH modes of publication mandatory. Failure to publish in newspaper = unenforceable. Cannot be cured by constructive notice or 'curing of defect' clause.
B.K. Srinivasan v State of Karnataka
AIR 1987 SC 1059		 Required: publish approved plan in Gazette. Done: published notice in Gazette saying plan available at office. SC held: substantial compliance sufficient. Notice in Gazette that plan can be inspected = enough. (Controversial — appears inconsistent with Govindlal)
UOI v G.S. Chatha Rice Mills
2020 (374) ELT 289		 Notification increasing import duty on Pakistan goods — date of effect questioned Time at which notification under Section 8A Tariff Act published = relevant date. Rules come into effect on date of gazette publication — not date of making.
When Does Delegated Legislation Come Into Force?
IF DATE SPECIFIED (subsequent)
Rules come into effect from date mentioned — no issue
IF DATE = ANTERIOR TO PUBLICATION
Rules would be ultra vires on ground of retrospectivity — invalid
IF DATE NOT MENTIONED
Three possibilities:
(1) Date of making
(2) Date of publication in gazette ← (most likely applicable for rules imposing liability)
(3) Some other specified date
PUBLICATION IN INDIA — KEY PRINCIPLES SUMMARY
(1) Publication in Official Gazette = mandatory (unless statute specifies different mode)
(2) Non-publication affects validity — unlike UK/USA where it only affects notice
(3) Lack of publication cannot be cured by constructive notice, actual knowledge, or 'curing of defect' clauses where gazette publication is mandatory
(4) Rules imposing liability on individual become effective only on date of gazette publication
(5) Publication in wrong gazette (e.g., district instead of state gazette) = invalid
💡 GST Application: If a CGST Circular, Notification or Rule was published in gazette on date X — demand for period prior to X based on such notification is illegal. Also: notification prescribing rate change effective from an anterior date is ultra vires on grounds of retrospectivity.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter VI | Legislative Controls Over Delegated Legislation
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, First Appellate Authority, and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Atlas Cycle Industries Ltd. v State of Haryana
Supreme Court of India | (1979) 2 SCC 196 | Section 3(6) Essential Commodities Act, 1955
Key Ratio — Simple Laying is Directory
Non-laying of an order before Parliament does not make it void. Section 3(6) is only directory and not mandatory. The use of the word 'shall' is not conclusive and decisive of the matter — it is for the court to determine the true intention of the Legislature. The two considerations for regarding a provision as directory are: (i) absence of any provision for meeting the contingency of non-compliance; and (ii) serious general inconvenience and prejudice that would result to the public if the act of government is declared invalid for non-compliance.
When to Use — When Department argues that a notification/circular is invalid because it was not laid before Parliament / not placed before the House within prescribed time. Counter: simple laying is directory — non-laying does not void the notification. Validity of underlying notification is unaffected.
S.R. Bommai v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | 1994 (3) SCC 1 | 9-Judge Constitutional Bench
Key Ratio — Laying Does Not Immunise from Judicial Review
"If the proclamation is invalid, it does not stand validated because it is approved by the Parliament." "Judicial Review is in no way inhibited by the fact that rules or regulations have been laid before Parliament and approved."
When to Use — When Department argues that a Rule/Notification has been laid before Parliament and therefore cannot be challenged. Counter with this ratio: Parliamentary approval does not cure substantive illegality. Courts retain full power of judicial review regardless of laying.
Rojer Mathew v South Indian Bank
Supreme Court of India | (2020) 6 SCC 1 | Constitution Bench
Key Ratio — Laying Cannot Cure Excess Delegation
If the principal Act did not spell out the necessary qualifications for the persons who would head the tribunals and left it wholly to the executive, the requirement of placing the Rules before the Parliament detailing the qualifications will not cure the defect. It would be a case of excess delegation which will be impermissible.
When to Use — When challenging the vires of CGST Rules/GST Regulations where the parent CGST Act does not spell out the criteria or limits (e.g., valuation rules, anti-profiteering regulations, GSTAT procedure rules). Argue: laying before Parliament cannot cure excess delegation — the defect goes to the root of the enabling power.
Krishnakumar v State of Bihar
Supreme Court of India | (2017) 3 SCC 1 | 7-Judge Constitutional Bench
Key Ratio — Ordinance Laying is Mandatory
Laying of Ordinance before the legislature is mandatory and the failure to do so will relate back to its creation and deeming the deeming fiction attributing to the ordinance the same force and effect as a law enacted by the legislature would not come into existence. An ordinance which has not been placed before the legislature would be of no consequence whatsoever.
When to Use — In disputes involving COVID-19 time extension ordinances under Section 168A CGST Act, or any ordinance-based amendment to GST law. If such ordinance was not placed before Parliament in next session — the entire extension of limitation it granted is of no legal consequence. SCNs issued on such extended time are time-barred.
Govindlal v Agricultural Produce Marketing Committee
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1976 SC 263 | (1975) 2 SCC 482
Key Ratio — Publication in Prescribed Mode is Mandatory (India's Position)
Failure to publish rules/notifications in the mode required by the parent statute affects the enforceability of the rules. This lapse cannot be cured by invoking the doctrine of constructive notice. Where statute requires double publication (gazette + newspaper), both are mandatory. Publication in gazette alone is insufficient. Rules held to be not enforceable because of non-observance of the additional publicity procedure prescribed.
When to Use — When Department issues SCN based on a circular/notification that was not published in the required mode (e.g., required gazette notification made only as web-upload or trade notice, or published in wrong gazette). Argue: non-observance of mandatory publication mode = notification unenforceable — demand falls.
Harla v State of Rajasthan
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1951 SC 467
Key Ratio — Publication is Essential for Legal Existence of Delegated Legislation
Promulgation or publication of some reasonable sort is essential to bring a law into force, to make it legally effective. It would be against natural justice to punish people under a law of which they had no knowledge, and of which they could not, even with the exercise of reasonable diligence, have acquired any knowledge.
When to Use — When Department seeks to apply an unpublished circular/instruction/guideline against an assessee. Argue: delegated legislation that was never published in a mode giving reasonable opportunity to know it has no legal existence. Natural justice forbids penalty/demand under a provision the assessee could not have known.
Kalpana Mehta v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | (2018) 7 SCC 1
Key Ratio — Parliamentary Standing Committee Reports Are Admissible
Parliamentary Standing Committee report can be taken aid of for the purpose of interpretation of a statutory provision wherever it is so necessary and also it can be taken note of as a historical fact. Judicial notice can be taken of the Parliamentary Standing Committee Report under section 57(4) of the Evidence Act and it is also admissible as a public document under section 74.
When to Use — When arguing legislative intent of CGST Act / IGST Act provisions in hearings. Cite Parliamentary Standing Committee Reports on the GST Bills, Finance Bills etc. to show what Parliament intended when granting rule-making power. These reports are admissible evidence of legislative intent and can be cited before any GST authority.
Quarry Owners' Association v State of Bihar
Supreme Court of India | AIR 2000 SC 2870 | (2000) 8 SCC 655
Key Ratio — Merely Laying Not Always Sufficient; Positive Approval May Be Required
The laying of any rule may be subject to any negative resolution within a specified period or may be subject to its confirmation. This is spoken of as negative or positive resolution respectively. Merely laying down the notification before each House of Parliament is not sufficient compliance with the provisions of Section 16(2) [which required positive act of approval].
When to Use — When Section 9(3) / 9(4) CGST Act notifications (RCM entries) are challenged on the ground that mere laying before Parliament was prescribed but the parent Act required positive approval. Distinguish between simple laying (directory) and affirmative procedure (mandatory). Also useful when distinguishing the strength of parliamentary oversight at hearings on rules ultra vires the enabling Act.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📋 Chapter VI — Legislative & Other Controls Over Delegated Legislation

📋 Chapter VI — Legislative & Other Controls Over Delegated Legislation

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Valid / Mandatory / Upheld
Invalid / Void / Not Permitted
Condition / Requirement / If
Exception / Discretionary / Alternative
Neutral Statutory Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
Publication of Rules — When Operative
Gazette Publication — Legal Effect
6.3 Consultation of Interests
6.3.1 Statutory Consultative Formulae
6.3.2 Previous Publication
6.3.3 Pre-publication Procedure & Courts
6.3.4 Legitimate Expectation
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
Publication of Rules — When Do Rules Come Into Force?
📰 Publication of Delegated Legislation — When Does It Become Operative?
G. Narayan Reddy v State of AP | GTC Industries v UOI | BK Srinivasan v State of Karnataka
📝 A rule or notification is not effective the moment it is printed or signed. It becomes operative only when it is actually made available to the public. Mere printing in the Gazette is not enough — the Gazette must be released/available for purchase. This is critical for determining from which date GST demands can be raised.
Three Possible Dates in the Life of a Rule — Which Matters?
① DATE OF MAKING
Date when rule-making authority signs/passes the rule
IRRELEVANT for effectuation
📝 The day the minister signs the rule means nothing to the public — they can't comply with rules they don't know exist.
② DATE OF GAZETTE PRINTING
Date printed on the face of the Gazette
May differ from actual release date
Not determinative if Gazette not yet released to public
📝 A Gazette dated "1 December" but actually released to subscribers on 12 December — effective from 12 December, not 1 December. (G. Narayan Reddy v State of AP)
③ DATE GAZETTE AVAILABLE TO PUBLIC ✓
Date from which Gazette copies are actually made available / on sale to the public
THIS IS THE OPERATIVE DATE
📝 Rule becomes effective when anyone who is interested can actually get a copy and read it. SC suggests adopting this as the standard date through legislation on lines of Statutory Instruments Act.
💡 GTC Industries v UOI: Excise exemption withdrawn by order dated 1 Mar 1979. Gazette with the order was on sale from 8 Dec 1982. Liability to pay excise duty arose from 8 Dec 1982, not 1 Mar 1979.
BK Srinivasan v State of Karnataka (AIR 1987 SC 1059) — Chinnappa Reddy J — Key Principles
Unlike Parliamentary legislation (publicly made), subordinate legislation is often made unobtrusively in chambers of ministers/officers → MUST be published or promulgated
If parent statute prescribes mode of publication → that mode MUST be followed (mandatory)
If parent statute silent but subordinate legislation itself prescribes mode → may be sufficient if reasonable
If no mode prescribed or mode prescribed is unreasonable → takes effect when published through customarily recognised official channel = Official Gazette or other reasonable mode
SituationRuleAuthority
Gazette bears date X but copies made available on date Y (later) Effective from date Y (when available to public) G. Narayan Reddy v State of AP (1975)
Administration takes steps to notify public before Gazette copies available May become operative from that earlier date General principle
Office Memorandum never made public, existence not made known Treated as never acted upon — cannot be relied on by Govt. Sub-Inspector Rooplal v Lt. Governor (2000)
Statute requires Gazette + other mode of publication Effective from date ALL requirements completed Govindlal v APMC (1976)
Levy created by statute — sought to be lifted/withdrawn Can be lifted ONLY by statute or in the manner prescribed by the statute creating the levy. Strict compliance required. Wide publicity + no dispute as to date/contents required. General principle — ITC Bhadrachalam (1996)
GST APPLICATION
CGST Notifications levying or withdrawing GST exemptions become effective from the date the Gazette is made available to public — NOT the date printed on the Gazette. For limitation of ITC claims or demand periods, the operative date of notification must be carefully verified from the date of actual public availability of the Gazette.
💡 GST: A CGST Notification dated "28 June 2017" but whose Gazette copies were placed on sale on "1 July 2017" — the exemption/levy became effective from 1 July 2017, not 28 June 2017. Demands for 28–30 June based on this notification are without jurisdiction.
Official Gazette — Legal Character and Evidentiary Value
🗞️ Official Gazette — Its Legal Character in Administrative Law
OFFICIAL CHARACTER
Official Gazette = official document published under authority of Government
Publication in Gazette = official confirmation of making of the order/rule
Version in Gazette is FINAL
EVIDENTIARY STATUS
Section 83, Evidence Act 1872: Court SHALL PRESUME genuineness of Gazette
Court takes JUDICIAL NOTICE of what is published
Newspaper publication must be PROVED as a fact — Gazette need not be
DATE PRIMACY
If dispute about when order/rule was made — Gazette publication date is relevant, NOT date of newspaper publication or media broadcast
Courts routinely refer to Gazette for language/punctuation disputes
MANDATORY PUBLICATION
Requirement to publish in Gazette = MANDATORY, not directory (Sammbhu Nath Jha v Kedar Prasad Sinha)
Cannot be dispensed with. "Gazette publication is an imperative."
Scheme of Publication — India's Classification System (from 1 March 1958)
CategoryPrefixPublished In
General statutory rules/orders of general character — Central Ministries (except Defence) GSR Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i) of Gazette of India
Non-general character orders — Central Ministries S.O. Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (ii)
Rules/orders by Ministry of Defence S.R.O. Part II, Section 4 of Gazette of India
Rules/orders under Constitution C.O.1, C.O.2 etc. Separately numbered
Other offices/authorities Various Other parts of Gazette — each is annual series
📝 GST practitioners must know: CGST notifications are typically GSR-prefixed (general) or S.O.-prefixed. The correct prefix/number identifies the notification uniquely and establishes the statutory authority. Always cite by GSR/S.O. number when arguing before GST officers.
DEFECTS IN INDIA'S PUBLICATION SYSTEM
Not all rules published at one place; no uniformity of numbering; no yearly consolidated index; amended rules not reprinted; no explanatory notes to rules; no reference to original rules when amended; corrigenda not always published within 15 days. Lok Sabha Committee on Subordinate Legislation has repeatedly flagged these deficiencies.
💡 GST: When CBIC amends CGST Rules multiple times, finding the current operative version of a Rule requires checking all amending notifications. This defect in publication system means assessees must independently compile the current version — a burden that ideally should not exist.
6.3 — Consultation of Interests — Rationale & Indian Position
🤝 Consultation of Interests — Democratisation of Rule-Making
Laxmi Khandsari v State of UP | UOI v Cynamide India Ltd. | SC in Indian Express
📝 "Consultation of interests" means giving affected parties a chance to share their views before rules affecting them are finalised. It makes the rule-making process less bureaucratic and more democratic. In India, unlike the USA, there is no general law requiring this — each statute must specify it.
Why Consultation Matters — The Rationale
Enables affected interests to communicate their views to rule-making authority → safeguard against improper exercise of rule-making power
Administration gets necessary information about the issues; apprised of all facets; less likely to make rules on insufficient information
Secures co-operative spirit between rule-maker and affected interests — essential for successful implementation of government policies
Dilutes stigma of bureaucratic, non-representative delegated legislation. People have sense of participation in administrative process.
🇮🇳 India — No General Statutory Requirement for Consultation
✗ NO GENERAL RIGHT
In India, no hearing or consultation can be claimed as matter of right or natural justice when Administration is exercising a legislative (rule-making) function.
"Rules of natural justice are NOT applicable to legislative action — plenary or subordinate." (SC — Indian Express)
📝 Just as Parliament doesn't hold hearings before passing laws, the rule-making body is not legally required to consult anyone — unless the parent Act says so.
✓ WHEN CONSULTATION IS REQUIRED
Consultation can be claimed only if:
(a) Parent statute expressly provides for it, OR
(b) Based on legitimate expectation (promise/established practice)
If statute provides consultative technique → courts regard it as MANDATORY — breach may result in invalidity of rules.
JUDICIAL ADVICE (not legal requirement)
Laxmi Khandsari v State of UP: SC suggested — even if not legally required, Administration "may consider desirability of giving a bare minimum hearing" to representative of affected association before imposing bans. "Not legal requirement but will generate greater confidence."
📝 Courts cannot compel consultation but encourage it as good governance practice.
🇺🇸 USA Model (APA Section 553) vs 🇮🇳 India — Comparison
AspectUSA — Administrative Procedure ActIndia
General requirement for consultation YES — APA Section 553 mandates minimum obligatory procedure in ALL rule-making NO — No general statute. Each parent Act must provide specifically.
Advance notice of rule-making Federal Register notice required — specifying time, place, nature, authority, subject matter No general requirement
Opportunity to file written views Mandatory — "notice and comment rule-making" Only if parent Act specifically requires ("previous publication" procedure)
Oral hearings Available for "formal procedure" when parent statute requires rule-making "on the record after opportunity for agency hearing" No minimum formalized consultative procedure imposed on rule-making authority in India
Effect of non-compliance Rules invalid Only if parent Act mandates consultation and it is not followed
💡 GST: When CBIC amends CGST Rules adversely affecting taxpayers without any prior consultation or notice — currently, taxpayers have NO legal right to object to that process unless CGST Act specifically requires prior publication. This is the weakness of India's system vs USA's APA model.
6.3.1 — Statutory Consultative Formulae in Indian Statutes
⚙️ Types of Statutory Consultative Formulae Used in Indian Legislation
📝 Where Parliament wants consultation to happen before rule-making, it says so in the parent Act using different formulae. Courts treat these formulae as mandatory — breach can void the rules made.
FORMULA 1 — Invite Objections
Rule-making authority must publish draft rules → invite objections → consider them before finalising
Example: Section 43 Motor Vehicles Act 1939; Section 5(b) Minimum Wages Act 1948
📝 Gives affected persons chance to file written objections. Failure to consider objections = ultra vires.
💡 GST: CGST Rules 2017 were initially notified without this procedure. Courts may treat such Rules as valid if CGST Act does not prescribe prior consultation — which it largely does not.
FORMULA 2 — Consult Advisory Committee
Rule-maker must consult a statutory committee/board of experts, employers, employees, nominated representatives before making rules
Example: Section 8 & 9 Minimum Wages Act 1948 (Central Advisory Board); Drugs & Cosmetics Act 1940 (Drug Technical Advisory Board)
📝 An independent expert body screens the rule. Court gave considerable weight to Drug Technical Advisory Board's expert opinion — upheld notification banning drug made on their advice.
FORMULA 3 — "As it thinks fit" Consultation
Government may consult such bodies or associations as it may think fit
Discretionary — does not specify which interests must be consulted
📝 Very weak formula — leaves matter entirely to government's discretion. Cannot be enforced as mandatory. Only directional.
FORMULA 4 — Interests Frame Own Rules
Statute confers power on concerned interests themselves to initiate and frame rules (subject to Govt. approval)
Example: Section 9-A, Forward Contracts (Regulation) Act 1952 — "recognised association" itself frames rules, effective on Central Govt. approval
📝 Maximum consultation — the affected party itself drafts the rules, not the Government. Government only approves/modifies.
Section 59 Mines Act 1952 — Model Consultative Procedure
Draft regulations → first referred to every mining board → board's opinion on expediency and suitability sought → then subject to "previous publication" procedure → minimum 3-month lag between draft publication and final consideration.
Banwari Lal Agarwalla v Bihar (SC 1961): Section 59 consultation with mining boards = MANDATORY. Regulations in contravention = void. Constitution of mining boards itself = only directory.
📝 This is one of India's best consultative procedures — two layers: (1) expert board opinion, then (2) public pre-publication with 3 months for objections. Section 59 became a model for procedural ultra vires doctrine.
UOI v Cynamide India Ltd. (AIR 1987 SC 1802) — Price Fixation is Legislative
Drug price fixation = legislative function → "inquiry as it thinks fit" before fixing prices = enabling provision, not quasi-judicial → drug manufacturers cannot claim notice or hearing as of right → inquiry and review are not quasi-judicial proceedings → Court characterised review as "legislative review of subordinate legislation at instance of aggrieved person." Manufacturers directed to seek review from Government — not court.
📝 Price fixation by Government is legislative — not administrative or quasi-judicial. So manufacturers cannot demand natural justice (notice, hearing, reasons) before the Government fixes prices. This applies equally to GST rate fixation by GST Council.
💡 GST: GST Council's recommendations on tax rates are legislative in character. Taxpayers cannot demand a hearing before the GST Council before rates are fixed or changed. They can challenge the notification in court on grounds of ultra vires or violation of Article 14 — but not for lack of natural justice in the rate-fixing process.
6.3.2 — Previous Publication — Section 23 General Clauses Act 1897
📋 "Previous Publication" — Formal Consultative Procedure
Section 23 General Clauses Act 1897 | Tulsipur Sugar Co. v Notified Area Committee
📝 "Previous publication" means: before the final rules are made, a DRAFT is published for public comment. After receiving objections, the authority considers them and then makes the final rules. Section 23 of the General Clauses Act defines how this must work.
4 Steps of "Previous Publication" Procedure — Section 23 GCA 1897
STEP 1
Rule-making authority SHALL publish DRAFT of proposed rules for information of affected persons
STEP 2
Publication shall be in such manner as the authority deems sufficient (mode is discretionary unless prescribed)
STEP 3
Published with draft rules: a NOTICE specifying date on/after which draft will be taken into consideration
STEP 4
Authority SHALL TAKE INTO CONSIDERATION any objections/suggestions received
Objections also considered by authority giving sanction/approval/concurrence
SECTION 23(5)
Publication in Official Gazette of a rule purporting to have been made after previous publication = "conclusive proof that the rule has been duly made." This is the conclusive evidence clause applicable to previous publication. Courts hold: only immunizes minor/directory deviations — not fundamental procedural breaches.
Weaknesses of India's "Previous Publication" Procedure
NO MINIMUM TIME-LAG
No minimum period prescribed between draft publication and final making. Rule-maker can give 4 days — sham consultation.
DISCRETIONARY MODE
Mode of publication left to authority's discretion — may choose obscure publication that affected parties never see.
NOT MANY STATUTES USE IT
Condition of previous publication not found in many Indian statutes. No rational basis for inclusion or exclusion — sometimes used for trivial matters, absent for important ones.
LONG DELAYS
Lok Sabha Committee noted delays of 2-4 years between draft and final publication — defeats purpose of consultation. Committee recommends: final publication within 1 year of draft.
REFORM SUGGESTION
Karnataka HC: Persons likely to be affected must have reasonable opportunity to go through draft rules and file objections. Where only 4 days given → denial of reasonable opportunity. Minimum of 30 days should be prescribed by law for filing comments. Conclusive evidence clause should be dropped from Section 23 so courts can declare rules ultra vires if any prescribed element ignored.
💡 GST: CGST Rule amendments via Gazette Extraordinary notifications sometimes take effect immediately with no prior publication of draft. Taxpayers have no chance to file objections. This is a systemic weakness acknowledged even by Lok Sabha Committees.
6.3.3 — Pre-publication Procedure & Courts — Key Cases
⚖️ Pre-publication Procedure — How Courts Treat Compliance Failures
Govindlal | Raza Buland | Sitapur Municipality | State of Orissa v Sridhar Kumar | Syed Hasan Rasul Numa v UOI
📝 "Pre-publication" (in a broader sense) = publishing draft rules/notifications to invite public objections, before finalising them. Courts generally treat this as mandatory because it is a prelude to democratic consultation. But courts also apply the substantial compliance rule for minor deviations.
General Rule — Courts Treat Pre-Publication as MANDATORY
RAZA BULAND SUGAR CO. v RAMPUR MUNICIPALITY (SC 1965)
"The purpose of such publication obviously is to further the democratic process and to provide a reasonable opportunity of being heard to those who are likely to be affected by the tax before imposing it on them." → Statutory pre-publication provision = MANDATORY.
Exception: Substantial compliance with mode of publication is sufficient even if not strict — as long as purpose served.
📝 In Raza Buland: Hindi resolution published in Urdu newspaper (wider circulation). Court held this was substantial compliance with the mode of publication — purpose of informing public was achieved. Conclusive evidence clause also helped.
CasePre-Publication RequirementWhat HappenedCourt's Ruling
Govindlal v APMC (SC 1976) Draft notification in Gazette AND in Gujarati in local newspaper (statute said "shall") Published in Gazette only — not in Gujarati newspaper Publication in Gujarati newspaper = MANDATORY (not directory). Gazette alone insufficient to reach affected persons. Rule invalid. (No conclusive evidence clause in that statute)
Rajnarain Singh v Chairman, PA Committee (SC 1954) Provision authorising affected persons to file objections against municipal taxation Objections procedure Right to file objections = matter of legislative policy — cannot be tampered with by Administration. Mandatory.
Lachmi Narain v UOI (SC 1976) 3 months' notice to effect any modification in schedule to sales tax law 3-month notice required before changing tax schedule 3 months' notice = MANDATORY and matter of legislative policy. Dealers need sufficient time/opportunity to object to proposed amendment.
Sitapur Municipality v Prayag Narain (SC 1970) Pre-publication of draft proposal and rules before levying municipal tax Several procedural irregularities in pre-publication stage Defects not of fundamental character, no substantial prejudice. Object of pre-publication (informing public so they can object) achieved. Levy upheld. Conclusive evidence clause also invoked.
State of Orissa v Sridhar Kumar (SC 1985) Statute: publish proclamation in Gazette + "at least one newspaper circulating in the area" Proclamation published in English in local Oriya newspaper (language not local) Must be published in LOCAL LANGUAGE to reach affected people. English in Oriya area does not serve purpose. Notification quashed.
Also: Proclamation vague — did not specify "all or some" purposes of Act → residents could not properly frame objections. Void for vagueness.
Syed Hasan Rasul Numa v UOI (SC 1991) Section 44 Delhi Development Act: notice "shall be widely made known" by any TWO of three methods: affixing copies, beat of drum, newspaper advertisement Notice published only in newspapers — only ONE mode used Using ANY TWO modes = MANDATORY. No discretion. Publishing in only newspapers (one mode) = mandatory requirement not complied with. Notice invalid. Action pursuant = invalid.
Punjab Towns Improvement Trust case (High Court) Inviting objections and considering them before notifying improvement scheme Objections received but NOT considered at all Non-consideration of objections = "colourable exercise of power", even mala fides. Cannot be cured by conclusive evidence clause (which only covers minor deviations).
CORE PRINCIPLE — Syed Hasan Rasul Numa
"In order to effectuate these rights, the prescribed means of publication must be faithfully followed giving persons clear notice as specified in the statute. The provision providing such notice to persons whose rights or interests are likely to be impaired must always be considered as mandatory. As otherwise, it would defeat the very purpose of giving public notice inviting objections and suggestions against the proposed action."
💡 GST: When CBIC issues notices/communications about proposed rule changes in the GST portal only and not in the Gazette in the prescribed form — and the parent provision requires Gazette notification — the rule may be challenged as not properly published. The medium of communication matters.
6.3.4 — Legitimate Expectation — Duty to Consult
🔮 Legitimate Expectation — A Novel Ground for Duty to Consult
Regina v Lord Chancellor ex parte Alexander | Re Liverpool Taxi Owners' Association
📝 Even without a statutory requirement for consultation, courts have developed a doctrine: if the rule-maker PROMISED to consult, or if there has been a consistent PRACTICE of consultation, affected parties can claim a right to be consulted before rules are changed. This is the doctrine of legitimate expectation in the rule-making sphere.
BASIS 1 — PROMISE
A specific representation or promise by the rule-maker to consult the affected persons before making rules → creates duty to consult
Example: Lord Chancellor promised Bar Council consultation before fixing Legal Aid fees. Court granted permission to challenge regulations made without consultation.
BASIS 2 — ESTABLISHED PRACTICE
Where rule-maker has consistently followed a practice of consulting certain interests in the past → those interests have legitimate expectation to be consulted in future rule-making on the same subject
CURRENT STATUS
The doctrine of 'legitimate expectation' seems established now in the rule-making sphere — requiring rule-maker to consult affected interests in certain circumstances even without statutory mandate. Courts have granted permission to challenge rules made without consultation where legitimate expectation existed.
📝 This doctrine bridges the gap between "no legal obligation to consult" and actual democratic practice. If CBIC has consistently consulted trade associations before amending certain GST Rules, and then changes rules without any consultation — affected parties may have a legitimate expectation argument.
💡 GST: If CBIC had over several years always issued draft amendments to CGST Rules for trade comment before final notification, and then abruptly amends Rules without any prior publication — taxpayers may invoke legitimate expectation doctrine to challenge the amendment as procedurally ultra vires.
📌 Chapter VI — Summary: Controls Over Delegated Legislation
Control MechanismLegal BasisEffect of Non-compliance
Publication in Official Gazette MANDATORY — Section 83 Evidence Act, BK Srinivasan ruling Rule not operative until published and available to public
Consultation under parent statute MANDATORY when prescribed — courts regard it as mandatory Rules made in breach = invalid / ultra vires
Pre-publication / Previous publication MANDATORY when prescribed — Raza Buland, Govindlal Rules invalid unless substantially complied with
Invitation and consideration of objections MANDATORY when prescribed — Rajnarain Singh, Syed Hasan Rasul Numa Non-consideration = colourable exercise, may be mala fides
Legitimate expectation of consultation Based on promise / established practice — Regina v Lord Chancellor May give right to challenge rules made without consultation
Consultation without statutory mandate Not legally required but judicially encouraged (Laxmi Khandsari) No legal remedy but seen as good governance
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter VI | Legislative & Other Controls Over Delegated Legislation
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
GTC Industries Ltd. v UOI | (1988) 33 ELT 83 | Universal Cans Containers Ltd. v UOI (1993) 64 ELT 23
Supreme Court of India | Operative Date of Gazette Notification
Key Ratio / Principle
An order becomes operative from the date from which the Gazette copies are made available to the public. Where an exemption notification was published in the Gazette which was on sale from 8 December 1982, the liability to pay excise duties on cigarettes arose from 8 December 1982 — not from the internal order date of 1 March 1979, nor from the date the Department informed petitioners about the withdrawal.
When to Use — When Department computes GST liability or denies exemption from a date that the Gazette was merely printed (but not yet publicly available). Cite to argue: the notification became operative only from the date the Gazette copies were made available to the public — not the printed date on the Gazette.
BK Srinivasan v State of Karnataka | AIR 1987 SC 1059 : (1987) 1 SCC 658 | Chinnappa Reddy J
Supreme Court of India | Publication of Subordinate Legislation — Mandatory Character
Key Ratio
"But unlike Parliamentary legislation which is publicly made, delegated or subordinate legislation is often made unobtrusively in the chambers of a Minister, a Secretary to the Government or other official dignitary. It is, therefore, necessary that subordinate legislation, in order to take effect, must be published or promulgated in some suitable manner, whether such publication or promulgation is prescribed by the parent statute or not. Where the parent statute prescribes the mode of publication or promulgation that mode must be followed. Where the parent statute is silent but the subordinate legislation itself prescribes the manner of publication, such a mode of publication may be sufficient, if reasonable. If the subordinate legislation prescribes a plainly unreasonable mode of publication, it will take effect only when it is published through the customarily recognised official channel, namely, the Official Gazette."
When to Use — When arguing that a GST Circular or Rule published through an obscure or non-standard channel (not through the Official Gazette when required) cannot be treated as having come into effect. Also use when arguing that an Office Memorandum not published in the Gazette cannot be relied upon by the Department.
Sammbhu Nath Jha v Kedar Prasad Sinha | AIR 1972 SC 1515 : (1972) 1 SCC 573 | Khanna J
Supreme Court of India | Gazette Publication — Mandatory / Imperative Requirement
Key Ratio
The requirement of publication in the Gazette is an imperative and cannot be dispensed with. The object of publication in an Official Gazette is twofold: (i) to give publicity to the notification, and (ii) to provide authenticity to the contents of that notification in case some dispute arises with regard to the contents. A notification has no legal effect until it is published in the manner required — Gazette publication is not a formality but an essential legal requirement.
When to Use — When a GST order, exemption, or notification is claimed to be effective without proper Gazette publication. Cite to establish: Gazette publication is MANDATORY — not directory. Order without proper publication has no legal existence.
Govindlal v Agricultural Produce Marketing Committee | AIR 1976 SC 263 : (1975) 2 SCC 482
Supreme Court of India | Pre-Publication in Newspaper — Mandatory
Key Ratio
When a statute requires publication of draft notification not only in the Gazette but also in a local language newspaper in the area, both requirements are mandatory. Mere publication in the Gazette is not adequate to communicate the authority's intention to those who would be vitally affected by the proposed notification. "It is a matter of common knowledge that publication in a newspaper attracts greater public attention than mere publication in the gazette and that is why the legislature has directed publication of the draft notification in Gujarati in a newspaper so that those who would be vitally affected by the proposed notification could offer their objections and suggestions." Violation of pre-publication provision "is likely to affect valuable rights of traders."
When to Use — When a GST notification or rule is challenged on the ground that the prescribed mode of pre-publication was not followed.
