Cite to argue: each prescribed mode of publication is mandatory — not directory — especially when it is designed to reach affected persons in their own language/territory.
Syed Hasan Rasul Numa v UOI | AIR 1991 SC 711 : (1991) 1 SCC 401
Supreme Court of India | Multiple Modes of Publication — All Mandatory
Key Ratio
"In order to effectuate these rights, the prescribed means of publication must be faithfully followed giving the persons clear notice as specified in the statute. The provision providing such notice to persons whose rights or interests are likely to be impaired must always be considered as mandatory. As otherwise, it would defeat the very purpose of giving public notice inviting objections and suggestions against the proposed action." Where statute requires any TWO of three modes — using only one mode is non-compliance with mandatory requirement. Notice and all action taken pursuant = invalid.
When to Use — When challenging a GST notice or consultation procedure on the ground that not all prescribed publication modes were used. Cite to argue: mandatory publication requirements must be strictly followed — substantial compliance in publication of rules inviting objections is non-negotiable.
Raza Buland Sugar Co. v Rampur Municipality | AIR 1965 SC 895 : (1965) 1 SCR 970
Supreme Court of India | Pre-Publication = Mandatory — Furthers Democratic Process
Key Ratio
The statutory provision requiring pre-publication of a draft rule imposing a tax is mandatory "for the purpose of such publication obviously is to further the democratic process and to provide a reasonable opportunity of being heard to those who are likely to be affected by the tax before imposing it on them." However, substantial compliance with the mode of publication is sufficient — strict letter-perfect compliance is not required if the purpose of informing affected persons has been achieved.
When to Use — Dual use: (a) To argue that pre-publication requirement is MANDATORY and non-compliance voids the rule; (b) To counter an argument that a minor deviation from the prescribed mode should void the rule — cite the substantial compliance doctrine to save a rule.
State of Orissa v Sridhar Kumar | AIR 1985 SC 1411 : (1985) 3 SCC 697
Supreme Court of India | Pre-Publication in Local Language — Mandatory; Notification Must Be Precise and Clear
Key Ratio
Publication of proclamation in English in a local Oriya newspaper did not serve the statutory requirement: "having regard to the object with which a proclamation is required to be published, it must be published in the local language of the area in which the newspaper circulates. To reach out to the people, it must be published in a language with which they are familiar." Further: a notification calling for public objections must be "precise and clear" — vague or ambiguous notifications that do not enable residents to properly understand what they are objecting to are void.
When to Use — When a GST notification or public notice is issued in English where the affected persons are primarily non-English speakers, OR when a CBIC circular/notification is so vague or ambiguous that taxpayers cannot properly respond to it. Cite to argue: notices must be in language accessible to affected parties AND must be precise enough to enable meaningful objections.
Laxmi Khandsari v State of UP | AIR 1981 SC 873 : 1981 2 SCC 600
Supreme Court of India | Natural Justice Not Applicable to Legislative Action; But Consultation Desirable
Key Ratio
"The rules of natural justice are not applicable to legislative action plenary of subordinate. The procedural requirement of hearing is not implied in the exercise of legislative powers unless hearing was expressly prescribed." Even so, while imposing a ban, the Government "may consider the desirability of giving a bare minimum hearing" — at least to one representative of the association affected. "Not that such action is a legal requirement but it will generate greater confidence of the persons who may be affected by an order to be passed against them."
When to Use — When challenging whether GST Rule changes/notifications require prior hearing of affected parties. The rule: No legal right to hearing before legislative action. But cite the Court's suggestion to argue that voluntary minimum consultation (though not legally required) should be followed by the Administration — and its absence reflects arbitrariness.
UOI v Cynamide India Ltd. | AIR 1987 SC 1802 : (1987) 2 SCC 720
Supreme Court of India | Price Fixation = Legislative Function — No Natural Justice Rights
Key Ratio
Price fixation is a legislative function — neither the 'inquiry' before price fixation nor the 'review' of a price-fixation order can be regarded as a quasi-judicial proceeding. The formula "such inquiry as it thinks fit" is an enabling provision — the nature and extent of the enquiry is within the discretion of the concerned authority. The provision does not vest any right in drug manufacturers to notice or hearing by the Government. Review of drug prices is "in the nature of a legislative review of legislation, or more precisely a review of subordinate legislation by a subordinate legislative body at the instance of an aggrieved person."
When to Use — When challenging CBIC circulars or GST Council recommendations fixing tax rates or withdrawing exemptions. Assessee cannot demand natural justice (notice, hearing, reasons) before rate fixation/change. However, they can challenge the notification on grounds of ultra vires, arbitrariness under Article 14, or lack of power in parent statute.
Lachmi Narain v UOI | AIR 1976 SC 714 : (1976) 2 SCC 953
Supreme Court of India | 3 Months' Notice = Mandatory | Pre-Publication = Legislative Policy
Key Ratio
The requirement of three months' notice to effect any modification in the schedule annexed to the sales tax law was held to be mandatory, as well as a matter of legislative policy. Dealers and others likely to be affected by the proposed amendment should have sufficient time and opportunity for making representations, objections or suggestions in respect of the intended amendment. A levy created by a statute can be lifted, suspended or withdrawn only by a statute or in the manner prescribed by the statute creating the levy. Such power, when conferred by legislature, must be exercised in strict compliance with the requirements of the provision conferring that power.
When to Use — When CBIC amends GST exemption schedules or GST rates without giving the statutorily prescribed notice period for objections. Cite to argue: prescribed notice periods before amending tax schedules are MANDATORY — not directory — protecting dealers' right to make representations. Amendments without notice = procedurally ultra vires.
Banwari Lal Agarwalla v Bihar | AIR 1961 SC 849 : (1962) 1 SCR 33
Supreme Court of India | Consultation with Mining Boards = Mandatory | Two-Stage Consultation
Key Ratio
The procedure laid down in Section 59 Mines Act 1952 for consultation with mining boards before making regulations has been held to be mandatory. Regulations made in contravention of this stipulation would not be valid. However, the constitution of the mining boards itself has been held to be directory — only the consultation requirement is mandatory.
When to Use — When CGST Rules are amended without following any statutorily prescribed consultation process for sectors specifically required to be consulted. Cite Banwari Lal for the proposition: statutory consultation requirements are MANDATORY — breach voids the rules. To be combined with Kalipada v UOI (Chapter V) for comprehensive procedural ultra vires argument.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
Chapter VII — Sub-Delegation of Legislative Power

Chapter VII — Sub-Delegation of Legislative Power

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
Legend
Root / Chapter
Level 1 Branch
Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Invalid
Condition / Unless / If
Optional / Route
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
📋 Index — Chapter VII
7.1 Sub-Delegation 7.1.1 Unguided Power 7.1.2 Ultra Vires 7.2 Publication 7.2.1 Laying 7.2.2 Consultation 7.3 Excessive Delegation 7.4 Statutory Rules vs Administrative Action ⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
7.1 — Sub-Delegation: Concept & Principles
Sub-Delegation of Legislative Power
[Chapter VII Root]
📌 Definition of Sub-Delegation
📝 Parliament gives law-making power to Agency A. Agency A then passes that same power further down to Agency B (or its own officer). That second handover is sub-delegation.
Statute confers legislative power on an agency → Agency further delegates rule-making power to itself / its officers / another person or agency
'Sub-delegation' = further delegation of the same power originally delegated by the Legislature
General Presumption
Legislative power must be exercised by those to whom it is given — not by further delegates
📝 Unless Parliament says otherwise, the person who got the power must use it themselves. They cannot hand it off again on their own.
UNLESSParliament expressly or impliedly permits sub-delegation
UNLESSEmergency — presumption greatly weakened
📝 In wartime or national emergency, courts have allowed sub-delegation even without express authority, because the urgency demands speed and flexibility.
💡 WWI example: Power to make regulations freely sub-delegated under Defence of the Realm Act even without express authority. WWII (Canada): Governor-General could sub-delegate emergency powers without express authorisation. Britain's Emergency Powers (Defence) Act 1939 expressly permitted an elaborate pyramid of sub-sub-delegated regulations.
Governing Doctrine
Delegatus non potest delegare
📝 Latin for "a delegate cannot further delegate." If you were given a power, you must use it yourself — you cannot hand it to someone else unless the law specifically allows you to.
A delegate cannot sub-delegate unless parent law permits — expressly or by necessary implication
✓ VALIDExpress authorisation in parent statute
✓ VALIDImplied authorisation — spelt out from statutory provisions by courts
📝 Even if the statute doesn't say sub-delegation is allowed in plain words, courts can read it as permitted if the law's overall scheme clearly demands it.
💡 A.S. Rao & Co. v Agricultural Market Committee (AIR 1977 AP 325): Statute silent on sub-delegation of licence-fee fixation power. Court upheld it by reading implied authorisation from the statute's scheme. Relevant in GST disputes where a GST Commissioner delegates adjudication to a subordinate officer without explicit rule — the delegation may still be valid if implied by the scheme.
Modern Use — Pyramid of Delegation
Sub-delegation used on elaborate scale as an administrative technique in modern times
Example: Essential Commodities Act, 1955
Stage 1: Parliament → Central Govt (Sec. 3: rule-making power)
Stage 2: Central Govt → State Govts / Officers (Sec. 5)
Stage 3: State Govt → Further sub-delegation (e.g. Cotton Control Order → Textile Commissioner → authorised officer)
📝 Think of it as a relay race: Parliament passes the baton to Central Govt, which passes it to the State, which passes it to a Commissioner. Each handover must be authorised.
💡 GST parallel: Parliament → CGST Act → Central Govt → Notification-based delegation to Principal Commissioner / Commissioner → further to Additional Commissioner. If any link in the chain lacks authority, orders at that level are void.
⚠ RISKSub-delegation at several stages: dilutes accountability, weakens safeguards, transfers power to lower authorities → necessary to limit degrees of sub-delegation
Key Issue in All Cases: Essential Function vs Ministerial/Ancillary Task
📝 The big question is: did the sub-delegate just help gather facts (which is fine), or did they actually make the decision (which is not allowed unless authorised)?
✗ NOT ALLOWEDSub-delegation of essential function — considering all factors & arriving at informed decision
✓ ALLOWEDSub-delegation of ministerial & ancillary tasks — gathering relevant information, inspection, reporting
Inspector General of Registration v K. Baskaran (AIR 2020 SC 3194) — 4 Principles laid down by SC:
(i) Deputing a competent official to enquire & report = ordinary mode of exercise of power. NOT delegation.
(ii) If delegate gives preparatory / initial decision but superior retains power to approve/disapprove AND maintains close control → decision is validly that of the superior
(iii) Even in sub-delegation: primary delegate must perform essential decision-making. Ancillary/clerical tasks can be shouldered by others.
(iv) Practical necessities & exigencies of administration require decision-making authority to be able to delegate tasks when situation demands
💡 GST Relevance: A GST officer (Additional Commissioner) directed a subordinate to inspect books, prepare a report, and submit findings. The Additional Commissioner then passed the final adjudication order. VALID — essential function (decision) stayed with the Additional Commissioner; subordinate only did ministerial groundwork.
7.1.1 — Unguided Legislative Power Ought Not to Be Sub-Delegated
Unguided Sub-Delegation
Broad Proposition: Sub-delegate ought NOT to be given uncanalised and unguided legislative power
📝 Even after lawfully sub-delegating power, the parent body must give the sub-delegate a roadmap — rules, limits, policies to follow. Blank-cheque power is not permitted.
What Counts as Sufficient Guidance?
✓Fixing a maximum limit (e.g. max licence fee cap) → sub-delegate fixes within cap → VALID
📝 If the law says "the committee may fix the fee but not more than ₹100," that ceiling itself is the policy guidance. The committee has a boundary — it's not free-floating.
💡 A.S. Rao: Govt fixed max licence fee; market committee to fix specific fee through bye-laws. Upheld because (a) maximum cap = guidance, and (b) representative body (traders + growers + govt nominees) itself a check. GST parallel: If a notification fixes maximum late fee but allows the officer to reduce — that ceiling is sufficient guidance.
✓Parent Act's own policy + scheme provides sufficient guidelines to sub-delegate — court reads them together
📝 Courts don't look at the sub-delegating clause in isolation. They read the entire Act — its preamble, purpose, and scheme — to find implied guidance. If the Act has a clear policy, that policy governs the sub-delegate too.
✓High status of the delegate (e.g. Central Government) — presumed to act justly and reasonably (Laxmi Khandsari)
✓Representative nature of the sub-delegated body is itself a safeguard (A.S. Rao)
Key Cases on Guidance
UOI v Bhanamal Gulzarimal (AIR 1960 SC 475)
Iron & Steel Controller empowered to fix maximum prices under Essential Supplies Act. Held: control order as a whole gave necessary guidance → power not "uncanalised or unbridled or unguided" → VALID
Laxmi Khandsari v State of UP (AIR 1981 SC 873)
Sugar Controller's power to issue impugned order derived from Cl.8 of Sugarcane Order, itself made under Sec.3 ECA. Held: notifications must be read in light of main Act — sufficient guidelines, checks and balances → VALID
K.T. Plantation Pvt. Ltd. v State of Karnataka (AIR 2011 SC 3430)
Courts shall NOT invalidate legislation for conferring vague/unguided powers WITHOUT first considering the preamble and other provisions of the statute which may clarify meaning → VALID
Lok Sabha Committee on Subordinate Legislation — Recommendations
✗ OBJECTEDSub-delegation in very wide language
✗ OBJECTEDSub-delegation where parent statute is silent
EXAMPLECoal Mines (Conservation & Safety) Act, 1952: Govt left 'principles' for land acquisition to Coal Board → Committee objected → Govt amended the rule
EXAMPLECentral Silk Board Rules, 1955: rule-making power sub-delegated to Silk Board despite parent Act authorising only Central Govt → Committee objected → amended
EXAMPLERule 13, Carbide Calcium Rules, 1937: delegated rule-making power to subordinate without parent Act authorising → Committee objected → deleted
SAFEGUARDCommittee: some safeguards must be imposed before delegate is allowed to sub-delegate; wide language in statutes permitting sub-delegation is objectionable
7.1.2 — Sub-Delegate Must Act Within the Power Delegated (Ultra Vires)
Ultra Vires Principle in Sub-Delegation
Well-established principle: sub-delegate cannot act beyond the scope of power delegated to it
📝 Once power is sub-delegated, it comes with a boundary. The sub-delegate cannot do more than what was handed to them. If they exceed that boundary, their order is illegal and void — as if it never existed.
SC: "A delegate is not entitled to exercise powers in excess or in contravention of the delegated powers. If any order is issued or framed in excess of the powers delegated to the authorities, such order would be illegal and void."
Courts' Strategy: Liberal Interpretation to Protect Validity
Courts often liberally interpret scope of delegation to protect validity of sub-delegated legislation
📝 When there's doubt about whether a sub-delegate had authority, courts lean in favour of validity by reading the delegation broadly — this saves orders from being quashed unnecessarily.
K. Ramanathan v State of Tamil Nadu (AIR 1985 SC 660)
Central Govt sub-delegated power to State Govt under Sec. 3(2)(d) ECA. SC broadly interpreted: "general power to issue the impugned order flows from Sec. 3(1) which stands delegated to the State Government" → VALID
💡 GST: When a Commissioner's circular or order is challenged as beyond the power delegated by CBIC, courts may read CGST Act's scheme broadly to uphold it — especially if it falls within the policy framework of the parent Act.
Condition: Prior Concurrence of Delegating Authority
IFCentral Govt delegates power to State Govt under Sec. 5, ECA with condition of prior concurrence
✗ INVALIDRules made by State Govt WITHOUT Central Govt's prior concurrence
📝 If the handover of power came with a condition ("only make rules after we agree"), breaking that condition voids the rules. You cannot cure the violation by getting approval later.
Central Govt's concurrence must be in writing (though need not be authenticated under Art. 77(2))
Foremost Dairies v State (AIR 1986 Raj 116)
State issued Milk Export Control Order without prior Central consent; obtained ex post facto consent → SC: order was void ab initio — cannot be validated retrospectively by ex post facto consent
📝 "Void ab initio" means it was never valid from the very beginning. Even if approval comes later, it cannot breathe life into something that was dead at birth.
💡 GST: If a State GST officer issues an anti-evasion order purportedly under a delegated power, but that delegation required prior approval of GSTN / Board, and that approval was missing — order is void ab initio. Ex post facto ratification by Board cannot cure it.
Distinction: 'Prior Consent' ≠ 'Consent simpliciter'
If order of sub-delegation says 'consent' (not 'prior consent') → may include ex post facto consent
ESI Corporation — Sub-Delegation Scenarios
Director General, ESI v T. Abdul Razak (AIR 1996 SC 2292)
Rule 16(2) — Part 1: Delegation of independent statutory powers under Rules/Regulations → NOT violative of sub-delegation principle → VALID
Same case — Part 2: Delegation of powers/duties under resolution of Corporation/Standing Committee → amounts to sub-delegation → IMPERMISSIBLE
📝 Two types of powers in the same rule. One type (statutory) can be re-delegated. The other type (resolution-based) cannot. The court split them apart and treated each differently.
Sourin Knit Works v ESI Corp. (AIR 1997 SC 1771)
Sec. 94-A ESI Act: Corporation may delegate to its officers. Regional Director = an officer → exercise of power does NOT suffer from vice of further delegation → VALID
7.2 — Publication of Sub-Delegated Legislation
Publication Requirement
Sub-delegated legislation must receive publicity — those affected must be able to know the rules that bind them (Harla principle)
📝 You cannot be bound by a law you were never told about. Even if the rule-maker didn't publish in the gazette, the obligation to publish exists because affected persons need access to know their rights and obligations.
When Parent Statute Requires Gazette Publication
QUESTIONDoes the gazette-publication requirement for rules also extend to sub-rules / sub-delegated legislation?
Narendra Kumar v UOI (AIR 1960 SC 430)
Principles for issuing copper permits (sub-delegated legislation) communicated to Controller but NOT notified in gazette → SC: principles not legally effective → Cl. 4 of Non-Ferrous Metals Control Order could not be activated → NO permits could be issued
💡 GST: If CBIC issues permit/approval criteria to a field officer through an internal communication (not a gazette notification), and those criteria affect taxpayers' rights (e.g. ITC eligibility conditions), they may be unenforceable — not legally effective as sub-delegated legislation.
State of Maharashtra v George (AIR 1965 SC 722)
Parent Act required gazette publication of rules. Sub-delegated notification by RBI published in gazette → SC: publication in gazette (usual & recognisable way) adequate for validity → VALID
Even if parent Act does NOT specifically require publication of sub-delegated legislation → publishing it is still imperative for its effectiveness and validity
📝 Silence in the parent law about publication does not mean "no need to publish." The obligation exists independently. If a rule is not published, the affected person cannot be expected to follow it.
Flexible Publication Clause — What It Means
Some provisions say authority shall publish "in such manner as may, in the opinion of such authority be best adapted for informing persons whom the order concerns"
This signifies: (i) Authority must apply its mind to best means of publication AND (ii) sub-delegated legislation must have been published accordingly
📝 The authority cannot just pick any random mode. It must think about it, choose the best way, and then actually publish that way. Mere making of the rule is not enough — publication is mandatory.
Mere making of sub-delegated legislation is NOT enough — its publication is essential. Publication in any other mode than that prescribed is not sufficient.
India vs England:
In England — no obligation (statutory or common law) to publish sub-delegated legislation (SI Act 1946 covers only delegated, not sub-delegated legislation) → this is a "big lacuna" in England.
In India — obligation established by courts.
Lok Sabha Committee + Judicial Criticism
Rules often confer powers on officers to issue directives — non-compliance is penal — but NO publication procedure laid down → Committee suggested rules must specifically lay down publication procedure
Committee: All statutory orders, regulations or principles issued under rules should be published in same manner as rules under parent Act. Pre-publication requirement must also be followed.
Scott L.J. in Blackpool Corp. v Locker: "The affected person should be able at any time to ascertain what legislation affecting his rights has been passed under sub-delegated powers."
Scott L.J. in Jackson Stansfield & Sons v Butterworth: Administration mixed with sub-delegated legislation, none of mixture made public → "really unfair and indeed unjust to the public."
7.2.1 — Laying Before Parliament
Laying of Sub-Delegated Legislation Before Parliament
QUESTIONDoes a parent statute's "laying" requirement cover only rules (primary delegated legislation) OR also sub-rules (sub-delegated legislation)?
📝 Parliament's rules say all orders made "under the Act" must be placed before Parliament for review. The question is: does "under the Act" include orders made three or four levels deep? Legally yes — practically, it creates chaos.
View 1 — Only Rules Need to be Laid
Sub-rules escape parliamentary scrutiny → gap in control
Government's position: Cotton Control Order sub-order issued under Control Order (not directly under Act) → need not be laid under Sec. 3(6) ECA
Practical reason accepted: sub-orders run into thousands; many ad hoc & temporary; authentication of each would be stupendous task → Committee fell in line with Govt
📝 The Government's argument was essentially: there are too many sub-orders to put before Parliament, most are routine and temporary. Parliament agreed to let it go for practical reasons.
View 2 (Legally Correct) — Sub-Rules Should Also be Laid
Lok Sabha Committee: ultimate authority of sub-order traceable to Act → should be laid
Legally: statutory provision requiring laying of orders "made under the Act" should cover even sub-orders → Committee's logic was impeccable
📝 If Parliament made the Act, and the sub-order ultimately flows from that Act (even via several intermediate steps), it should still face parliamentary scrutiny. The legal logic is sound — but practical convenience overruled.
Conclusion: "Practical considerations of administrative convenience prevailed over theoretical considerations."
Mandatory vs Directory — Laying Requirement
Accountant General, MP v S.K. Dubey (2012) 4 SCC 578
Lodha J.: Whether laying is mandatory or directory depends on circumstances & wording of each statute — no universal rule
Atlas Cycle Industries v State of Haryana (AIR 1979 SC 1149)
Non-laying of iron & steel price notification before Parliament → did NOT nullify notification → laying provision under Sec. 3(6) ECA = DIRECTORY
Association of Management of Private Colleges v AICTE (AIR 2013 SC 2310)
Amended AICTE regulations NOT placed before Parliament under Sec. 24 → Court: non-laying vitiates the amended regulations → INEFFECTIVE → laying provision = MANDATORY here
📝 Two opposite outcomes — both from the Supreme Court. This shows that the answer is fact and statute specific. Each case's wording of the laying clause determines whether it is mandatory (violation = void) or directory (violation = irregularity only).
Justice G.P. Singh, Principles of Statutory Interpretation (12th Edn.): "If complete non-compliance of a laying requirement has no repercussion, rule-making authority can do away with vigilance and control which the legislature wants to keep in its hand."
SC (ECA context): Rules made by State Govt under delegated power from Centre NOT required to be placed before Parliament under Sec. 3(6) — State Govt not responsible to Parliament
Result: Parliamentary supervision over sub-delegated legislation is on the whole weaker than on delegated legislation
7.2.2 — Consultation in Sub-Delegated Legislation
Consultation Requirement
Position same as delegated legislation: sub-delegating authority NOT obligated to consult affected interests UNLESS a legal provision requires it
📝 There's no automatic duty to consult. Consultation is required only when the parent Act or the delegated legislation that created the sub-delegation specifically says so.
Sources of Consultation Obligation
Legal provision in parent Act → consultation required
Legal provision in delegated legislation under which sub-delegated legislation arises → consultation required
Legitimate expectation → consultation can also be claimed on this ground in sub-delegated legislation
📝 Legitimate expectation: if a party was always consulted before, they can expect to be consulted again even if there's no explicit legal requirement. This doctrine applies in sub-delegation too.
Selective Consultation — Sec. 26A, Drugs & Cosmetics Act
Statute may require consultation for some issues but not others
Sec. 26A: Central Govt must be satisfied about risk to human beings / therapeutic value / ingredients — must apply mind to one or all three factors; must find it necessary/expedient in public interest
Notification of 10 March 2016: Govt banned manufacture & sale of 239 FDCs (fixed dose combinations) after Kokate Committee Report
No requirement of consultation with Drugs Technical Advisory Board (DTAB) in Sec. 26A (added 1982) — though DTAB is only advisory body
SC (UOI v Pfizer Ltd (2018) 2 SCC 39): Non-consultation with DTAB does NOT make power under Sec. 26A non-est — its advice is desirable but not mandatory
📝 "Non-est" means the power simply does not exist. Court rejected that argument. The government's power to ban drugs under Sec. 26A is real and valid — DTAB's advice is optional, not a condition precedent.
Need for Greater Consultation in Sub-Delegation
Lacuna: parliamentary supervision over sub-delegated legislation is weak (as established in 7.2.1)
REMEDYDeficiency can be compensated by promoting more consultation between interested groups and Administration
New India Industrial Corp. v UOI (AIR 1980 Del 277): HC observed problem is more acute in sub-delegated legislation — sub-delegated legislation not required to be laid before legislature; no scrutiny possible at any stage → more consultation must be promoted
Need for MORE effective control over sub-delegated legislation compared to delegated legislation because: (i) its bulk is immense and (ii) it involves transfer of legislative power from higher to lower authority
7.3 — Sub-Delegation and Excessive Delegation
Excessive Delegation Challenge
Bangalore Development Authority v Aircraft Employees Cooperative Society Ltd. (2012) 3 SCC 442
BDA levied charges for sanctioning new extensions/layouts → Cooperative Society challenged: BDA exceeded delegated legislative power → BDA: sufficient guidelines in statutes
Test for Excessive Delegation Challenge
Court must look into policy underlying particular legislation — done by reference to: (i) Preamble, (ii) Object sought to be achieved, (iii) Scheme of the legislation
Court would NOT sit over wisdom of legislature; NOT nullify provisions under which power to implement is conferred on executive authority
📝 Courts give maximum benefit of doubt to Parliament. They look at the whole picture — the law's purpose, its preamble, its design — before deciding if power given was "too much." If the overall scheme shows sufficient control, the sub-delegation stands.
💡 GST: When a taxpayer argues that power given to Commissioner to prescribe conditions for ITC is excessive/uncanalised — court will look at CGST Act's preamble (tax compliance, revenue protection) and scheme (input tax chain, anti-avoidance provisions) before striking it down.
BDA Ruling — Outcome
Sec. 32(5-A) expression "such portion of the expenditure as the authority may determine towards execution of any scheme or works for augmenting water supply, electricity, road transportation and such other amenities" = sufficient guidance for BDA → NOT unbridled or uncanalised → VALID
BDA's exercise of power always subject to State Govt directions under Sec. 65 → additional safeguard
Legislature could not have made provision for effective implementation of development Acts without delegating tasks to some agency → delegation was necessary
📝 Delegation was upheld because (a) the statute gave a framework — specific categories of expenditure — and (b) the State Govt had override power. No blank cheque was given; there were limits and checks.
7.4 — Statutory Rules vis-à-vis Administrative Action
Statutory Rules vs Executive Orders
Established Principle: Statutory Rules prevail over executive orders / administrative instructions
📝 Rules made under a statute (delegated legislation) are law. Administrative circulars, clarifications, or executive orders are not law in the same sense. If they contradict the statutory rules, the rules win.
Case 1 — Executive Order Cannot Amend Statutory Rules
PSC Uttaranchal v Jagdishichandra Bora (2014) 8 SCC 644
Selection procedure for Junior Engineers commenced under 2001 Rules; new 2003 Rules framed; executive order directed 10 marks weightage to trained apprentices
SC: (i) 2001 selection procedure cannot be changed midway on basis of 2003 Rules; (ii) executive orders cannot supplant rules framed under Art. 309 proviso
Executive orders/instructions can only supplement (fill gaps in) statutory rules — NOT supplant (override/replace) them
📝 Supplement = add to; Supplant = replace. An administrative circular can explain or fill in details not covered by the rules. It cannot change what the rules say.
💡 GST: A CBIC circular clarifying the procedure for filing an appeal can supplement the CGST Rules (fine). But a circular that says "ITC shall be disallowed in all cases of vendor mismatch" cannot override Sec. 16 of CGST Act or the rules made thereunder — it would be supplanting statutory provisions.
Case 2 — Statutory Rules over Commission Recommendations
Sasidhar Reddy Sura v State of AP (AIR 2014 SC 444)
Rule 5: max age 45 years for District Judge recruitment. Shetty Commission recommended min age 35. Argued both should apply together.
SC: Commission recommendation not supported by rules CANNOT be implemented. Rules are statutory under Art. 309. If recommendation and statutory rule are at variance → statutory rule prevails.
📝 A Commission's report is a suggestion, not law. Once Parliament or the Government frames rules on the subject, those rules are binding — the report becomes merely historical background.
💡 GST: GST Council recommendations (e.g. on HSN classification, rate) are not automatically law. Only when they are implemented by a notification under the GST Act do they become binding. If a taxpayer is taxed based on a GST Council recommendation not yet notified, they can challenge it as lacking statutory force.
Case 3 — COVID Emergency Exemptions / Proportionality
Gujarat Mazdoor Sabha v State of Gujarat (2020) 10 SCC 459
State of Gujarat under Sec. 5, Factories Act 1948 exempted factories from hours of work, weekly rest, etc. — during COVID-19 — to help employers facing financial stringency
SC applied proportionality principle. Blanket exemption to ALL factories regardless of their products: NOT justified. Only factories producing essential COVID-related goods (drugs, PPE, sanitisers) could validly be exempted under Sec. 65(2).
📝 Even in emergencies, executive power is not unlimited. The action must be proportionate to the problem. A sledgehammer approach — exempting everyone because some need relief — fails the proportionality test.
Proportionality principle: exemption must be related to the purpose & limited to those who genuinely need it — blanket application fails this test
💡 GST: CBIC granting across-the-board waiver of interest or penalty to all taxpayers (when only those genuinely affected by a disaster deserve it) could be challenged on proportionality grounds — especially where the parent statute requires a nexus between relief and genuine hardship.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter VII
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Inspector General of Registration v K. Baskaran
Supreme Court | AIR 2020 SC 3194 | (2020) 14 SCC 345
"A statutory functionary exercising a power cannot be said to have delegated his functions merely by deputing a responsible and competent official to enquire and report, as that is the ordinary mode of exercise of any administrative power. So long as the essential function, that is to say of considering all the necessary factors and inputs and thereafter arriving at an informed decision is done by the appellate authority, the burden of performing ancillary tasks need not be shouldered by the appellate authority."
When to Use: When Revenue argues that adjudicating officer improperly delegated inspection or fact-gathering to a subordinate. Counter: gathering facts ≠ delegation of essential function. The essential function (decision) stayed with the officer.
UOI v Bhanamal Gulzarimal
Supreme Court | AIR 1960 SC 475
The control order, taken as a whole, gave the delegate the necessary guidance to exercise his power; it constituted an integrated scheme to enable the Controller to effectuate the policy of the Act; and the authority conferred on the Controller was not "uncanalised or unbridled or unguided." The parent Act governed the discretion not only of the Government but of the Controller as well.
When to Use: When challenging that a GST notification/circular is an excessive or uncanalised sub-delegation. Counter (for department): read the CGST Act as a whole — sufficient policy guidance exists. Counter (for assessee): if guidance is genuinely absent, this ratio shows the test and invites invalidation.
Foremost Dairies v State
Rajasthan High Court | AIR 1986 Raj 116 (critical analysis)
A sub-delegate cannot exceed the power given to him; his power is subject to the limitation imposed on him by the delegator. Non-observance of this limitation will make his order ultra vires and void ab initio. Therefore, in the instant case, the order made without the Centre's prior consent was void ab initio — it could not be validated retrospectively by the ex post facto consent.
When to Use: Where a GST demand order was issued by an officer whose delegation was conditional on prior approval from a higher authority, and that approval was missing. The order is void ab initio — ex post facto approval cannot save it.
A.S. Rao & Co. v Agricultural Market Committee
Andhra Pradesh High Court | AIR 1977 AP 325
The maxim delegatus non potest delegare is not a rule of law but only a rule of construction. Even when a statute does not contain any express provision authorising delegation of legislative power, such a power can be spelt out by necessary implication from the provisions of the statute. In sub-delegation also, like delegation, sufficient guidance must be given to the sub-delegate concerned.
When to Use: When Revenue relies on implied authority to sub-delegate. This ratio cuts both ways — use it to argue (a) sub-delegation needs guidance even if impliedly permitted, or (b) implied permission exists if the statute's scheme supports it.
Narendra Kumar v UOI
Supreme Court | AIR 1960 SC 430
The principles for issue of permits, which may be regarded as sub-delegated legislation, were specified by the Central Government in a communication to the Controller but had not been notified in the gazette. The Supreme Court held that the principles were not legally effective as they were not validly specified due to their non-publication in the gazette.
When to Use: Where CBIC / department issues permit/approval conditions or restrictive conditions through internal office memoranda or communications (not gazette notifications). Argue: such sub-delegated conditions are not legally effective — cannot be enforced against the taxpayer.
K. Ramanathan v State of Tamil Nadu
Supreme Court | AIR 1985 SC 660 | (1985) 2 SCC 116
Although Cl.(d) of sub-section (2) of section 3 of the Act deals only with a specific power, the general power to issue the impugned order flows from the provisions of sub-section (1) of section 3 which stands delegated to the State Government. — Courts interpret sub-delegation rather broadly.
When to Use: To uphold validity of a GST notification issued by State GST authority under delegated CGST/SGST power. If the specific sub-section doesn't cover the topic, argue the general delegation under the parent section validates it.
Public Service Commission Uttaranchal v Jagdishichandra Bora
Supreme Court | (2014) 8 SCC 644
It is settled preposition of law that the executive orders cannot supplant the rules framed under the proviso to Art. 309 of the Constitution. Such executive orders/instructions can only supplement the rules framed under the proviso to article 309 of the Constitution of India.
When to Use: Where a CBIC circular, CGST instruction, or trade notice purports to override a rule made under CGST Act (e.g. Rule 86A, Rule 36(4)). Argue: circular can supplement rules (clarify gaps) but cannot supplant (override) them — statutory rules prevail.
Gujarat Mazdoor Sabha v State of Gujarat
Supreme Court | (2020) 10 SCC 459
A blanket notification of exemption to all factories, irrespective of the manufactured product, while denying overtime to the workers, is indicative of the intention to capitalize on the pandemic. The proportionality principle demands that the relief be targeted and limited to those who genuinely need it.
When to Use: To challenge a blanket GST exemption or restriction notification that is not proportionate to the stated object. If CBIC issues a sweeping restriction (e.g. suspension of all ITC claims in a sector) without justification targeting those who genuinely evade, invoke proportionality.
K.T. Plantation Pvt. Ltd. v State of Karnataka
Supreme Court | AIR 2011 SC 3430 | (2011) 9 SCC 1
The courts shall not invalidate a legislation on the ground of conferring unguided, uncontrolled and vague powers upon the delegate without taking into account the preamble of the Act as also other provisions of the statute in the event they provide good means of finding out the meaning of the offending statute.
When to Use: When Revenue defends a GST notification against an attack that it is vague or uncanalised. Point to CGST Act's preamble and scheme as providing the guiding framework — prevents invalidation on grounds of excessive delegation.
Laxmi Khandsari v State of Uttar Pradesh
Supreme Court | AIR 1981 SC 873 | (1981) 2 SCC 600
The power cannot be deemed to be arbitrary or unguided because the impugned notification derives its source from section 3 which clearly lays down sufficient guidelines. The notifications under the Control Order have to be read in the light of the main Act. Also, the Central Government being a very high authority, it must be presumed to act in a just and reasonable manner.
When to Use: To uphold validity of a GST notification challenged on grounds of arbitrariness or lack of guidelines. Argue: (i) read it with the parent Act; (ii) the issuing authority (CBIC / Central Govt) is a high authority presumed to act reasonably.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | CA Pawankumar Soni
📜 Chapter VIII — Directions

📜 Chapter VIII — Directions

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Valid / Enforceable / Permitted
Invalid / Not Enforceable / Prohibited
Condition / Exception / Unless
Discretionary / Alternative Route
Neutral Statutory Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
8.1 Directions — Nature & Forms
Directions vs Delegated Legislation
8.1.1 Need for Directions
8.2 Identification of Directions
8.2.5 Tests for Identification
8.3 Enforceability of Directions
Directions vs Statutory Rules — Hierarchy
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
8.1 — Directions — Nature, Forms and General Principles
📜 Directions — A Modern Phenomenon in Administrative Law
"Administrative Quasi-Legislation" | Megarry, 60 LQR 125 (1944)
📝 Directions are informal rules made by the Administration — without the formality of delegated legislation. They are the government's way of communicating how it will act, what officers should do, and what benefits/duties apply — without going through the full rule-making process. They are faster, more flexible, but less legally certain.
Forms in Which Directions Appear
Letters & Circulars
Instructions & Orders
Memoranda & Directives
Bulletins & Guidelines
Manuals & Pamphlets
Public Notices, Press Notes, Clarifications, Trade Notices
Two Types of Directions
SPECIFIC DIRECTION
Applicable to a specific person, matter or case (e.g., a notice to one taxpayer)
GENERAL DIRECTION
Lays down some general norm, principle, policy, practice or procedure for ALL similar cases
This chapter focuses on general directions
8.1.1 — Administrative Practice — Can It Acquire Legal Status?
PRINCIPLE — UOI v Alok Kumar (2010) 5 SCC 349
A practice can adopt the status of an instruction provided it is in consonance with law and has been followed for a considerable time. A practice adopted for a considerable time, not violative of the Constitution or bad in law or against public policy, can be termed good law. Practice adopted and followed in the past and within knowledge of the public at large can legitimately be treated as good practice acceptable in law. A good practice of the past can even provide good guidance for the future — provided it is not in conflict with statutory provisions or principal document.
📝 Long-standing administrative practices — if lawful — can be treated as acceptable norms. To bar such a practice, there must be specific prohibition in statutory provisions.
Directions vs Delegated Legislation — Key Distinctions
⚖️ Directions vs Delegated Legislation — Fundamental Differences
AspectDelegated Legislation (Rules)Directions
Source of power Issued ONLY when authority has STATUTORY power to do so Issued under GENERAL ADMINISTRATIVE power — statutory power NOT sine qua non
Hierarchy A rule CAN OVERRIDE a direction A direction CANNOT OVERRIDE a rule — direction occupies secondary place
Binding force Binding on both individual AND Administration — legally enforceable Generally NOT enforceable in court — more of administrative discipline
Formalities Requires formalities: publication in Gazette, laying before Parliament, pre-publication, consultation, etc. Less formal — can be issued by letters, circulars, etc. without following statutory formalities
Amendment A rule can only be amended by another rule A direction can be amended by issuing another direction — more flexible
Guiding label Called: rules, regulations, bye-laws, orders, notifications Called: guidelines, circulars, instructions, manuals, codes, press notes — BUT label is NOT conclusive
Effect on private rights Rules affect private rights directly and can impose legal liability Directions generally cannot impose legal liability — can confer benefits but NOT impose burdens enforceable in court
⚠️ KEY PRINCIPLE — Jayantilal Amratlal v FN Rana (SC 1964)
"This is not to say that every order issued by the executive authority has the force of law. If the order is purely administrative or is not issued in exercise of any statutory authority it may not have force of law. But where a general order is issued even by an executive authority which confers power exercisable under a statute, and which thereby in substance modifies or adds to the statute, such conferment of powers must be regarded as having the force of law."
💡 GST: A CBIC Circular interpreting a provision of CGST Act = direction / administrative instruction. A CGST Rule notified under Section 164 CGST Act = delegated legislation / rule. The Circular cannot override the Rule; if both conflict, the Rule prevails.
8.1.1.1 — Need for Directions / Why Directions Instead of Rules?
🔍 Why Does the Administration Use Directions Instead of Rules?
📝 Directions are the government's "quick and flexible alternative" to formal rule-making. When a situation doesn't fit neatly into formal rules, or when the government wants flexibility, it reaches for directions. Understanding why directions are used is essential to challenging their misuse.
① INFORMALITY
Directions can be issued without formal procedures — no Gazette publication, no laying before Parliament, no pre-publication required
② FLEXIBILITY
Can be changed or modified more easily than rules — no procedural formalities in amending them
③ FILL GAPS
Used to fill gaps in the rules — where rules are silent, directions can supplement. Government can function pending rule-making.
④ BENEFIT WITHOUT LEGAL RIGHT
A department may like to confer a benefit on the public but not make it a legal right — directions achieve this without creating enforceable entitlements
⑤ DISCRETION STRUCTURING
Used to structure wide discretionary powers given to officials — announce in advance how discretion will be exercised (e.g., section 5B(2) Cinematograph Act — principles guiding Film Censor Board)
⑥ FLUID SITUATIONS
When factors are rapidly changing (e.g., import licence conditions, foreign exchange situation), fixed rules are impractical — directions provide flexibility to respond quickly
⚠️ NEGATIVE EFFECT
Courts' view that directions can substitute for rules pending rule-making LESSENS THE URGENCY to make rules. Administration may indefinitely avoid formal rule-making because directions serve its purpose with less accountability and scrutiny. This undermines the rule of law.
💡 GST: CBIC issues hundreds of Circulars every year interpreting CGST Act provisions instead of amending the Rules formally. This allows CBIC to "legislate" without going through the proper rule-making process — at the cost of taxpayer certainty and Parliamentary oversight.
8.2 — Identification of Directions — What is a Direction and What is a Rule?
🔍 How to Identify Whether a Government Pronouncement is a Direction or a Rule?
Area is "open to judicial legislation" — no stable principle has emerged
📝 The label doesn't matter. A document called "Rules" may actually be a direction. A document called "Guidelines" may actually have the force of a rule. The courts look at substance — particularly the SOURCE OF POWER under which it was issued — to decide its true character.
General Propositions for Identification
ISSUED UNDER STATUTORY POWER + FORMALITIES MET
= RULE
What government issues under rule-making power in a statutory provision, with all prescribed formalities (Gazette publication, laying, etc.) = rule
ISSUED UNDER GENERAL ADMINISTRATIVE POWER
= DIRECTION
What is issued under Articles 73/162 general executive power (not under any specific statutory provision) = direction, regardless of label or Gazette publication
NON-PUBLISHED "RULES"
= DIRECTIONS
Rules made under statutory power but NOT published in Official Gazette as required → lose legal force → treated as directions / guiding norms only (State of UP v Kishori Lal)
Document / CodeHeld to beReason
Mysore PWD Code (Fernandez v State of Mysore) DIRECTION Issued under general administrative power, not statutory power
Punjab Jail Manual — Para 516-B (commutation of sentences) DIRECTION Based on Government of India resolution — no statutory force
Exchange Control Manual DIRECTION Compendium of administrative instructions, advisory opinions, comments, notes — not statutory
UP Police Regulations — Chapter XX (personal liberty interference) DIRECTION No statutory foundation — guidance for police officers only
UP Police Regulations — Chapter XXIII (disciplinary proceedings) RULE Had statutory foundation
MP Police Regulations (similar to UP Chapter XX) RULE SC distinguished earlier ruling as based on concession — same type of provision held rule
Madhya Pradesh Pre-Medical Examination Rules 1972 DIRECTION Could not be related to any purpose/provision of concerned statute; not pre-published as required; ultra vires statute — treated as direction under general administrative power
UP Excise Rules (published only in Excise Manual, not Official Gazette) DIRECTION Non-publication in Gazette = no legal force; only guidance to officials
CBDT Circulars under Section 119(1) Income Tax Act DIRECTION WITH QUASI-STATUTORY STATUS Issued under express statutory power (Section 119) — binding on tax authorities
Election Symbols (Reservation & Allotment) Order 1966 — Election Commission DIRECTION with legal effect Article 324 confers only executive (not legislative) power; direction of general nature given legal effect. EC can issue directions only when no statutory provision exists in that respect.
Import Policy (announced periodically by Central Government) DIRECTION (Administrative — not statutory) Substance = executive instructions to departmental officers + advice to importers. No pattern or logical sequence — "jumble of executive instructions". Can be changed by mere administrative instructions.
Reserve Bank Circulars on staff seniority (VT Khanzode) DIRECTION (under Section 7(2) — general administrative power) Not made with Govt. sanction as required by Section 58(1); source of power not mentioned; issued under general superintendence power of Central Board under Section 7(2) RBI Act
NDMC Guidelines under Section 235 New Delhi Municipal Council Act 1994 RULE (force of law) Statutory power specifically conferred → cannot be termed merely administrative instructions
8.2.2 — Import Policy — Key Principle (UOI v Anglo Afghan Agencies)
"It is not the form of the order, the method of its publication or source of its authority, but its substance, which determines its true character." A scheme merely because it is of general nature, published in the gazette, and issued under a statutory provision does NOT automatically become statutory in character. Both legislative and executive orders can be issued under the same provision — substance determines nature.
📝 So even a Gazette-published document may be a direction — what matters is whether it was issued under STATUTORY rule-making power or general administrative power, and what its SUBSTANCE is.
💡 GST: CBIC issues both GST Notifications (under Section 11/164 CGST Act = statutory) and CBIC Circulars (under general administrative power = directions). The source of power determines character — not the label or Gazette publication.
8.2.3 — Directions in lieu of Rules — Government Can Function Without Rules
SETTLED PRINCIPLE — Surinder Singh v Central Government (SC 1986)
"Where a statute confers powers on an authority to do certain acts or exercise power in respect of certain matters, subject to rules, the exercise of power conferred by the statute does not depend on the existence of Rules unless the statute expressly provides for the same. In other words, framing of the rules is not condition precedent to the exercise of the power expressly and unconditionally conferred by the Statute."

Meaning of "subject to the Rules": "The expression 'subject to the Rules' only means, in accordance with the rules, if any. If rules are framed, the powers so conferred on authority could be exercised in accordance with these rules. But if no rules are framed there is no void and the authority is not precluded from exercising the power conferred by the statute."
📝 Government does NOT stand still while waiting to frame rules. It can issue administrative directions to fill the gap. This is judicially approved — but it has the negative effect of reducing the urgency to make proper rules.
💡 GST: When CGST Rules are absent for a specific situation (e.g., before e-invoice Rules were notified), CBIC issued circulars/instructions. These circulars are not Rules — they are directions in lieu of rules. They guide departmental officers but cannot impose legal liability on taxpayers.
8.2.5 — Tests for Identification of Directions
🧪 Tests to Identify Whether a Government Instrument is a Direction or a Rule
📝 No clear single test has emerged. Courts use a combination of factors. The source of power is the most critical — but form, publication, and substance also matter.
TEST 1 — STATUTORY SOURCE MENTIONED
If instrument mentions the specific statutory provision under which it was issued:
• Specific provision authorises RULES → = RULE
• Specific provision authorises DIRECTIONS → = DIRECTION
Source of power under which issued is the CRUX of the matter
TEST 2 — STATUTORY POWER + ALL FORMALITIES
If issued under statutory rule-making power AND all formalities (Gazette publication, laying, pre-publication, consultation) have been complied with → = RULE invariably
TEST 3 — SOURCE SILENT / AMBIGUOUS
If source of power not indicated and instrument can relate to either statutory or general administrative power → apply SUBSIDIARY FACTORS:
Subsidiary Factors When Source of Power is Ambiguous
FactorPointing to RULEPointing to DIRECTION
(i) Form of instrument Formal, structured, numbered — typical rule format Informal — letter, circular, memorandum format
(ii) Procedural formalities for delegated legislation All prescribed formalities complied with (Gazette, consultation, etc.) Formalities ignored / not followed
(iii) Gazette publication Published in Gazette AND statute requires Gazette publication for rules → strong indication of rule Not published in Official Gazette even when required → points to direction
(iv-a) Substance — Imposes obligations on INDIVIDUALS May be safer to treat as direction to protect individual (East India case) Informational / procedural content only → DIRECTION
(iv-b) Substance — Only informational / administrative machinery Clearly DIRECTION — no regulatory effect on private rights DIRECTION
(iv-c) Substance — Imposes obligations on ADMINISTRATION If all statutory formalities met → safer to treat as RULE so individuals can enforce it If formalities not met → direction — only administrative remedy available
(iv-d) Substance — Confers RIGHTS on individuals If all statutory formalities met → safer to treat as RULE so rights are enforceable If issued under administrative power only → direction — courts may still enforce benefit-conferring directions
KEY INSIGHT — Raman and Raman v State of Madras (SC 1959)
The distinction between rules and directions lies partly in purpose: "Whenever the Act intended to affect the rights of persons, it used the term 'Rules'. Directions were appropriate to control administrative functions." Rules affect private rights; directions control how the administration functions internally.
💡 GST: CBIC Circular interpreting Section 17(5) CGST Act (ITC blocked items) is a direction. It can guide officers but cannot add to or restrict the statutory provisions. A CGST Rule framed under Section 164 CGST Act can. If a Circular contradicts the Rule or the Act, the Rule and Act prevail.
8.3 — Enforceability of Directions — When Are They Binding?
⚖️ Enforceability of Directions — The Law is in Flux
UOI v KP Joseph | State of Assam v Ajit Kumar Sarma | Raman and Raman v State of Madras
📝 The basic rule is: directions are NOT enforceable in court. But this rule has exceptions — courts have started enforcing directions in certain situations, particularly when they confer benefits, or when the government has been following them for a long time. The law is NOT settled — it is ad hoc and case-by-case.
GENERAL RULE — NOT ENFORCEABLE
Since directions are not "law" — basic rule is that a direction is not enforceable in a court.

• Direction does NOT confer right on a person
• Does NOT impose obligation on Administration/any person
• Even if Administration violates/misapplies/ignores direction → affected person CANNOT claim remedy in court
• No writ can be issued even for patent breach of direction
• Non-compliance leads to ADMINISTRATIVE discipline — not court action
• Mandamus usually not issued to enforce direction lacking statutory force
📝 The only remedy when a direction is breached = internal administrative remedy: go to higher authority in the hierarchy and plead enforcement.
EXCEPTIONS — DIRECTIONS MAY BE ENFORCED

① Directions conferring BENEFITS on persons — may be enforced (UOI v KP Joseph — office memorandum on pay fixation of re-employed military personnel — enforced by mandamus)

② Service matter directions followed for nearly two decades — held binding (Baleshwar case — office memorandum on seniority followed for ~20 years)

③ When government itself is bound by its own instructions — must follow them when causing civil consequences to employees

④ Directions under statutory power (like CBDT circulars under Section 119 Income Tax Act) — binding on all tax authorities

⑤ Directions accepted by party voluntarily (grant-in-aid conditions)
📝 "Generally speaking, an administrative order confers no justiciable right, but this rule, like all other general rules, is subject to exceptions... To say that an administrative order can never confer any right would be too wide a proposition." (UOI v KP Joseph)
What Directions CANNOT Do — Absolute Limits
CANNOT OVERRIDE STATUTE
Any departmental letter or executive instruction cannot prevail over statutory rules and constitutional provisions
CANNOT OVERRIDE RULES
Administrative instructions / circulars / orders cannot change the principle contained in the statutory rules
CANNOT CREATE RETROSPECTIVE LIABILITY
What cannot be done by way of regulation cannot be done by executive order, that too retrospectively
CANNOT IMPOSE LIABILITY ON INDIVIDUALS
"It seems to be clear that directions cannot impose liability on anyone, only rules can do so. But directions may confer benefits."
GOVERNMENT RESOLUTION ≠ LEGISLATIVE ACT
Government resolution cannot override a legislative Act. A circular letter is not a statutory instrument.
CBDT / CBIC CIRCULARS — Special Position
Section 119(1) Income Tax Act expressly confers power on CBDT to issue circulars. Such circulars — being issued under a specific statutory provision — are BINDING ON ALL TAX AUTHORITIES. They cannot be ignored by Assessing Officers. This is one situation where directions have quasi-statutory force.
By analogy: CBIC Circulars issued under Section 168 CGST Act (which expressly empowers CBIC to issue orders, instructions, directions to ensure uniformity) — are binding on GST officers.
💡 GST: A CBIC Circular says "X supply is exempt." The GST Officer cannot demand GST on X supply by ignoring the Circular — even if he personally disagrees with its interpretation. If the Circular is wrong, the Department must challenge it — not unilaterally ignore it while making a demand.
Directions vs Statutory Rules — Priority and Hierarchy
📊 When Do Directions Prevail and When Do Statutory Rules Win?
SituationRule / PrincipleAuthority
Statutory rules SILENT on a point — direction fills gap Direction can fill gap and supplement rules — PROVIDED direction NOT inconsistent with existing rules Sant Ram Sharma v State of Rajasthan (SC 1967)
Later statutory rules vs earlier administrative instructions Statutory rules PREVAIL over executive instructions — once statutory rules come into force, administrative instructions are not to be adhered to Shish Ram v State of HP (SC 2000)
Non-statutory order vs statutory notification Non-statutory order CANNOT replace statutory notification even if it purports to do so specifically. But statutory notification CAN substitute non-statutory notification/order. UOI v Diljeet Singh (SC 2001)
Administrative instructions vs Constitutional provisions Administrative instructions CANNOT override constitutional provisions Municipal Corporation Amritsar v Senior Supdt. Post Offices (SC 2004)
Specific instruction vs General instruction (two competing directions) SPECIFIC instruction PREVAILS over GENERAL instruction (later specific circular prevails over earlier general circular) Secretary to Govt. Haryana v Vidya Sagar (SC 2009)
Circular contradicts provisions of parent Act In case of inconsistency or conflict — statutory provisions PREVAIL over executive instructions (despite prior existence of instructions) DDA v Joginder S. Monga (SC 2004)
Office Memorandum that has substance of a rule but not formally a rule Courts may treat it as rule if its purport was later incorporated into a rule — to avoid confusion (EV Naidu v UOI — SC treated memorandum on retirement age as issued under Article 309) EV Naidu v UOI (SC 1973)
Noting in departmental file NOT a direction or decision. Noting = opinion of individual officer for internal use. Does NOT acquire status of a government decision until sanctioned and communicated in proper form under Articles 77(1)/(2) or 166(1)/(2). Shanti Sports Club v UOI (SC 2009)
Guidelines with no statutory flavour Advisory only — cannot have force of statute. Not enforceable. Per se do not confer any legal right. (State of Haryana v Mahendra Singh) State of Haryana v Mahendra Singh (SC 2007)
Directions issued under Section 35-A RBI Act — interest of banking policy BINDING on regulated entities — issued under statutory power expressly conferred. Court upheld RBI circular directing banks to exit cryptocurrency relationships as clearly in public interest. (Internet & Mobile Association v RBI) Internet & Mobile Association of India v RBI (SC 2020)
STATE IS BOUND BY ITS OWN INSTRUCTIONS
When the State lays down a rule for taking action against an employee causing civil or evil consequences, it is imperative on its part to scrupulously follow the same. The State is bound by its own instructions. Non-compliance would amount to MALICE IN LAW. When an order suffers from malice in law, no strict proof is insisted upon. Such an order being illegal = wholly unsustainable. (Swaran Singh Chand v Punjab State Electricity Board)
💡 GST: If CBIC has issued instructions that summons should not be issued for lower-level enquiries and should be issued by officers of certain rank only — and a lower-ranked GST officer issues a summons ignoring these instructions — the summons suffers from malice in law and is unsustainable. The government is bound by its own circular.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter VIII | Directions
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Punjab Water Supply & Sewerage Board v Ranjodh Singh | (2007) 2 SCC 491
Supreme Court of India | Departmental Letters / Circulars Cannot Override Statutory Rules
Key Ratio
Any departmental letter or executive instruction cannot prevail over statutory rules and constitutional provisions. Administrative instructions cannot override the provisions of the Act. An administrative act must yield to a statute — it is no longer res integra.
When to Use — When a CBIC Circular or CBDT Circular is being used to override or restrict the benefit clearly available under the CGST Act / Income Tax Act. Cite to argue: circular is a direction — it cannot prevail over the statute or statutory rules. The statute governs; the circular is subordinate.
Swaran Singh Chand v Punjab State Electricity Board | (2009) 13 SCC 758
Supreme Court of India | State Bound by Its Own Instructions — Violation = Malice in Law
Key Ratio
When the State lays down the rule for taking any action against an employee which would cause civil or evil consequences, it is imperative on its part to scrupulously follow the same. The State is bound by its own instructions. Non-compliance wherewith would amount to malice in law. When an order suffers from malice in law, neither any averment as such is required to be made nor strict proof thereof is insisted upon. Such an order being illegal would be wholly unsustainable.
When to Use — When a GST Officer violates CBIC's own circulars/instructions while passing an order (e.g., summons issued by wrong officer; opportunity of hearing not provided as required by departmental instructions; ITC denied contrary to CBIC clarification). Cite to argue: the Department is bound by its own circulars — violation = malice in law — order is unsustainable.
UOI v KP Joseph | AIR 1973 SC 303 : (1973) 1 SCC 194
Supreme Court of India | Administrative Orders Can Confer Enforceable Rights
Key Ratio
"Generally speaking, an administrative order confers no justiciable right, but this rule, like all other general rules, is subject to exceptions. To say that an administrative order can never confer any right would be too wide a proposition. There are administrative orders which confer rights and impose duties." A memorandum conferring benefit on the respondent formed part of service conditions and Court saw no reason why it should not enforce that right by issuing mandamus.
When to Use — When the Department argues that a CBIC Circular granting exemption or benefit to the assessee has no legal force and cannot be enforced. Cite to argue: administrative orders CAN confer enforceable rights — especially when they confer benefits. A direction conferring benefit may be enforced by mandamus.
Raman and Raman v State of Madras | AIR 1959 SC 694 : 1959 Supp (2) SCR 227
Supreme Court of India | Directions vs Rules — Key Distinction on Private Rights
Key Ratio
Whenever the Act intended to affect the rights of persons, it used the term 'Rules'. Directions were appropriate to control administrative functions, but rules affected private rights. Directions need not be published — these could be issued in the form of secret communication to the concerned authorities. Rules and directions cannot thus be equated. The declaration that directions would be binding on concerned authorities indicates that these are not law — for if the directions were law, no such declaration would have been necessary.
When to Use — When a CBIC Circular is being used to impose tax liability on an assessee, or to override a benefit available under the statute. Cite to argue: Circulars/directions can only control administrative functions — they CANNOT impose liability on taxpayers. Only rules/statutory provisions can do that.
Sant Ram Sharma v State of Rajasthan | AIR 1967 SC 1910 : 1968 (1) SCR 111
Supreme Court of India | Government Can Issue Directions Pending Rules / Fill Gaps
Key Ratio
In the absence of service rules made under Article 309, there is no bar in the way of the Administration issuing directions as long as such directions are not inconsistent with any rule on the subject. While the Government cannot amend or supersede statutory rules by directions, if the rules are silent on any particular point, the Government can fill up the gaps and supplement the rules and issue directions not inconsistent with the rules already framed.
When to Use — When the Department argues that a CBIC Circular fills a gap in the CGST Rules and should be followed. Counter: Circulars can fill gaps but CANNOT be inconsistent with the Rules. If the Circular contradicts any Rule, the Rule prevails. Also use to explain why circulars exist even when Rules are silent.
UOI v Anglo Afghan Agencies | AIR 1968 SC 718 : 1968 (2) SCR 366
Supreme Court of India | Nature of Import Policy / Substance Determines Character
Key Ratio
It is not the form of the order, the method of its publication or source of its authority, but its substance, which determines its true character. Merely because a policy statement is of a general nature, published in the gazette, and issued under a statutory provision, it cannot be assumed to be of a statutory character. Both "legislative" and "executive" (or administrative) orders could be issued under the same statutory provision. The import trade control policy constituted "executive or administrative instructions" in character and was not legislative in character.
When to Use — When determining the nature of any government document — whether a CBIC Circular, Public Notice, or Press Release has the force of a statutory notification. Cite the substance test: look at what the document DOES — does it impose obligations, confer rights, or merely give procedural guidance? The label and Gazette publication are not conclusive.
Sukhdev Singh v Bhagatram | AIR 1975 SC 1331 : (1975) 1 SCC 421
Supreme Court of India | Regulations Made Under Statutory Power = Delegated Legislation
Key Ratio
"Rules, regulations, schemes, by laws, orders made under statutory powers are all comprised in delegated legislation." There is no substantial difference between 'rules' and 'regulations' inasmuch as both are made under powers conferred by the statute and therefore regulations are imperative and have the force of law.
When to Use — When Department argues that CGST Regulations or Rules framed under Section 164 CGST Act are merely administrative instructions that can be ignored or are non-binding. Cite to establish: rules, regulations, orders made under statutory power = delegated legislation = imperative = have force of law = MUST be followed.
State of Assam v Radha Kanoo | (1996) 8 SCC 692
Supreme Court of India | Administrative Instructions Cannot Override Law Laid Down by Supreme Court
Key Ratio
The administrative instructions cannot override the statutory operation of the Regulations and Rules nor do they give legitimacy to illegal acts done in compliance of them; they cannot override the law of the land as laid down by the Supreme Court either. Rules and regulations or administrative instructions can neither be supplemented nor substituted under any provision of the Act or the rules and regulations framed thereunder.
When to Use — When CBIC issues a circular that contradicts a Supreme Court judgment on GST law (e.g., a CBIC Circular that contradicts the SC's ruling on ITC eligibility or place of supply). Cite to argue: the Circular is void — it cannot override the law as laid down by the Supreme Court.
VT Khanzode v Reserve Bank of India | AIR 1982 SC 917 : (1982) 2 SCC 7
Supreme Court of India | Source of Power is Critical / Regulations vs Administrative Circulars
Key Ratio
"The power to frame rules or regulations does not necessarily imply that no action can be taken administratively in regard to a subject-matter on which a rule or regulation can be framed, until it is so framed. The only precaution to observe in the cases of statutory corporations is that they must act within the framework of their charter. Its express provisions and necessary implications must at all events be observed scrupulously." The source of power under which regulations are issued is the crucial and determining factor. Failure to mention the source of power in recitals — when practice has been to mention it — is a relevant factor in determining whether regulations have statutory force.
When to Use — When a government body (like GSTN, CBIC) issues "regulations" or "circulars" without clearly indicating the statutory source. Cite Khanzode principle: if source of power not mentioned when it was the practice to mention it — inference is that it was NOT issued under statutory power = direction only = less enforceable.
Internet & Mobile Association of India v Reserve Bank of India | (2020) 10 SCC 274
Supreme Court of India | Directions Under Express Statutory Power (Section 35-A RBI Act) = Binding
Key Ratio
The power under Section 35-A of the RBI Act to issue directions is to be exercised under four contingencies: (i) public interest, (ii) interest of banking policy, (iii) interest of depositors, and (iv) interest of the banking company. The RBI Circular directing banks to exit relationships with virtual currency entities was clearly in public interest and that public interest permeates all these three areas. Directions issued under an express statutory provision conferring specific power are binding on regulated entities — such directions have quasi-legislative force.
When to Use — Dual use: (a) When CBIC issues a direction under Section 168 CGST Act expressly empowering it — that direction is BINDING on GST officers. (b) When defending a CBIC Circular from attack — cite that directions under express statutory power are binding. (c) When CBIC circular imposes restrictions BEYOND what the statute permits — challenge by showing the contingencies prescribed in the statutory power were not satisfied.
Shanti Sports Club v UOI | (2009) 15 SCC 705
Supreme Court of India | File Notings Are NOT Directions or Decisions
Key Ratio
A noting recorded in the file is merely a noting simpliciter and nothing more. It represents expression of opinion of the particular individual. By no stretch of imagination, such noting can be treated as a decision of the Government. Even if the competent authority records its opinion in the file on the merits of the matter under consideration, the same cannot be termed as a decision of the Government unless it is sanctified and acted upon by issuing an order in accordance with articles 77(1) and (2) or articles 166(1) and (2) of the Constitution. An order would be deemed to be a government order as and when it is issued and publicised.
When to Use — When Revenue Department tries to use internal file notings / noting sheets / internal DRI/IB reports as "evidence" of mala fide or as the basis for a demand order. Cite to argue: file notings have NO legal status — they are opinions, not decisions. A legitimate government order must be in proper form, sanctified, and communicated. Internal communications while processing a matter are NOT orders.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
📋 Chapter VIII — Directions

📋 Chapter VIII — Directions

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | Tree Diagram Notes | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Enforceable
Prohibited / Invalid / Not Binding
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative Route
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
8.1 Nature of Directions — Overview
8.2 Legal Hierarchy
8.3.1 Directions Inconsistent with Statute/Rules
8.3.2 Directions by Lieutenant Governor
8.3.3 Directions Overruling Court Judgments
8.3.4 Interpretive Directions
8.3.5 Directions vs Judicial Orders
8.3.6 Judicial Direction to Fill Vacuum
8.3.7 Discretion and Direction
8.3.8 Directions to Quasi-Judicial Bodies
8.3.9 Directions & Private/Fundamental Rights
8.3.10 Article 14 and Directions
8.3.11 Other Grounds — Non-Enforcement
8.3.12 Other Grounds — Enforcement
8.4 Publication of Directions
8.5 Defects of System of Directions
8.6 Rules by Private Bodies
8.7 Amendment in Directions
CBDT Circular Enforceability Matrix
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
8.1 — Nature and Concept of Directions (Administrative Instructions/Circulars)
📋 Directions — The Third Tier of Governance
Circulars | Memoranda | Policy Notes | Guidelines | Instructions | Press Notes
📝 After Parliament makes a law, the Executive frames Rules. Below that, the Administration issues Directions. Directions are the day-to-day operational instructions government officers use. They are NOT law — but they are extremely important in practice.
What are Directions? — Different Names, Same Animal
Circulars
Memoranda / GOs
Policy Notes / Press Notes
Guidelines / Instructions
Office Memoranda (OMs)
Administrative Orders
Why Directions Exist — The Administrative Need
(1) Fill gaps and lacunae in rules where rules are silent
(2) Supplement rules by providing procedure and criteria
(3) Regulate exercise of discretionary powers of officers
(4) Provide uniform standards and norms for administration
(5) Act as substitute for delegated legislation in modern practice
FUNDAMENTAL NATURE
Directions are administrative in nature — they do NOT have the force of law unless backed by statute.
A direction is NOT delegated legislation — it is NOT published as required by statute — it is NOT subject to parliamentary scrutiny.
There is always an element of doubt about enforceability of a direction.
📝 Unlike a Rule (which is like a mini-law), a Direction is like an internal memo — it tells officers what to do, but it does not automatically create rights or obligations for citizens. Whether a direction is binding or not depends on the facts — courts decide case by case.
8.2 — Legal Hierarchy: Constitution → Act → Rules → Directions
⚖️ Legal Hierarchy of Norms in India
📝 Think of this as a pyramid. Higher tiers always prevail over lower tiers. A direction can never override what is higher up in the pyramid — if it tries to, it is void to that extent.
🏛️ TIER 1: Constitution of India
(Supreme — overrides everything)
📜 TIER 2: Act of Parliament / State Legislature
(Must not violate Constitution)
📋 TIER 3: Rules / Regulations made under the Act
(Must not violate Act or Constitution)
📄 TIER 4: Directions / Circulars / Instructions
(Lowest — must not violate Rules, Act, or Constitution)
CRITICAL CONSEQUENCES
(1) Directions cannot prevail over, impinge upon, or supersede statutory provisions
(2) Provisions of law can NEVER be circumvented or overridden by directions
(3) A rule can be amended ONLY by another rule — NOT by a direction
(4) While a rule can override an earlier rule OR a direction, a direction can only change a direction, never a rule
(5) Directions can only supplement, never supplant the rules
📝 A circular cannot do what the law itself cannot do. If the Income Tax Act says X, a CBDT circular cannot say not-X. If CGST Act Section 16(2) says ITC requires invoice — no circular can say invoice is not needed.
