💡 GST Example: CBIC issues a circular saying ITC can be claimed without filing GSTR-3B. But Section 16(2) CGST Act requires GSTR-3B. The circular is invalid to the extent it overrides the Act. Directions cannot override statute — Kerala Financial Corp. principle applies to CBIC circulars too.
8.3.1 — Directions Inconsistent with Statute or Rules are Bad
🚫 Directions Inconsistent with Parent Statute — Invalid
✓ VALID — SUPPLEMENT
Direction fills gap in rules where rules are silent
Directions add relevant considerations without subtracting from statute
Direction supplements, never supplants
📝 A valid supplement is like a footnote to a contract — it adds detail but doesn't contradict the main text.
💡 Police probation rules prescribe 2-year minimum but no maximum. Government direction prescribing maximum 3 years = valid supplement — read together with rules.
✗ INVALID — CONFLICT
Direction conflicts with, or runs counter to, statutory provisions
Direction amends/supplants/supersedes rules
Even if followed for long, convenient, working fairly in practice — MUST be ignored
📝 Even if a bad direction has been followed for 30 years and everyone likes it — it must be ignored. Convenience and practice cannot override law.
💡 Service rule: promotions based on merit alone. Direction: seniority-cum-merit. Direction is BAD — inconsistent with rule (State of Haryana v Shamsher Jang Shukla SC).
GOLDEN RULE — S.L. Sachdev v UOI (SC)
"No one can issue a direction which, in substance and effect, amounts to amendment of the Rules made by the President under article 309. This is elementary."

A direction superimposing a new criterion on recruitment rules was held to be "lacking in jurisdiction."
CBDT Circulars — Special Position Under Section 119 Income Tax Act
✓ BINDING ON OFFICERS
CBDT circulars u/s 119 are binding on ALL Income Tax officers and persons employed in execution of IT Act
Navnit Lal C. Jhaveri v K.K. Sen (SC)
✓ BENEFICIAL DEVIATIONS ALLOWED
Circulars that "tone down the rigour of the law" in favour of assessee are binding even if they deviate from correct interpretation
UCO Bank v CIT (SC)
✗ CANNOT OVERRIDE ACT
Circulars cannot override provisions of the Act or impose higher burden on assessee than Act itself
Kerala Financial Corp. v CIT (SC)
Section 119(2)(a): circulars cannot be ADVERSE to assessee
✗ NOT BINDING ON ITAT/COURTS
CBDT circulars are NOT binding on:
• Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT)
• High Courts
• Supreme Court
• The assessee himself
💡 GST Application: CBIC circulars u/s 168 CGST Act = same position as CBDT circulars. Binding on GST officers, not on AAR/AAAR/GSTAT/Courts. A circular beneficial to assessee (e.g., relaxing time limits, clarifying exemptions) should be enforced even if it deviates from strict reading of the Act. A circular adverse to assessee (e.g., denying ITC beyond Act) = invalid.
8.3.4 — Interpretive Directions — Administration's Interpretation of Law
🔍 Interpretive Directions — Circulars Clarifying How Law Should Be Applied
📝 Sometimes government issues circulars saying "this is how we interpret Section X." These are called interpretive directions. The key question: are they binding? The answer depends on WHO is asking — an officer or a court.
✗ NOT BINDING ON COURTS
Interpretive direction cannot bind courts — courts are free to interpret the law themselves
Chief Settlement Commissioner v Om Prakash (SC)
📝 Just because the Tax Department says a provision means X does not mean the court has to agree. The court independently reads and interprets the law.
✗ NO ESTOPPEL AGAINST STATUTE
Clarifications/circulars represent merely government's understanding — State can still recover tax if truly leviable
No estoppel against statute
Bengal Iron Corp. v CTO (SC)
✗ NOT BINDING ON QUASI-JUDICIAL FUNCTION
Even within the Department — when acting in quasi-judicial capacity, officers bound by LAW not by circulars
Bengal Iron Corp. v CTO (SC)
📝 An assessing officer acting quasi-judicially (e.g., during scrutiny assessment) must follow the law, not just the circular. If law and circular conflict — law wins.
MAY BE CONSIDERED
Court may take interpretive direction into consideration as "contemporanea expositio" — how law was understood at time of making
Gives way where language of statute is plain and unambiguous
K.P. Varghese (SC)
FUNDAMENTAL PRINCIPLE — UOI v Tulsiram Patel (SC)
Executive instructions on interpretation of statute "stand on a lower footing than a statutory rule for they do not have the force of a statutory rule. If an Act or a rule cannot alter or liberalize the exclusionary effect of the second proviso, executive instructions can do so even much less."
💡 GST Application: A CBIC circular saying "Section 17(5) does not apply to X" — this is an interpretive direction. When challenged before GSTAT/High Court — the court independently interprets Section 17(5). The circular is only the government's view — not the law. If Section 17(5) clearly covers X, the circular cannot save the taxpayer's ITC claim.
8.3.5 & 8.3.6 — Directions vs Judicial Orders | Judicial Direction to Fill Vacuum
⚖️ When Administrative Directions Meet Judicial Orders
ADMIN vs JUDICIAL — JUDICIAL WINS
Administrative instructions/circulars/orders (ipse dixit) cannot infiltrate into arena covered by judicial orders
Where High Court direction and administrative direction conflict — judicial order prevails
Municipal Corp. v Veer Singh Rajput (SC)
📝 Ipse dixit = "he said it himself." An administrative direction exists only because the government said so. A court order has constitutional backing. Court always wins.
CANNOT OVERRULE COURT JUDGMENT
Executive/Legislature cannot simply declare earlier court decisions as invalid
To render judicial decision ineffective — must remove/alter the LEGAL BASIS of the judgment
A government resolution merely reiterating earlier circulars rejected by court = invalid
Vijay v State of Maharashtra (SC)
JUDICIAL DIRECTION TO FILL VACUUM
When Act/Rules are silent on a subject AND authority has constitutional/statutory power to implement — Court CAN issue directions to fill vacuum until suitable law enacted
Court CANNOT direct Government to issue a notification (would be policy decision)
📝 Courts fill gaps — but courts don't make policy. If Parliament has not granted exemption under Section 11C Excise Act — court cannot direct government to grant it. That's Parliament's job.
8.3.7 — Discretion and Direction — Can Directions Bind Discretionary Authorities?
🎯 Directions Regulating Discretion — When Binding, When Not
📝 Statutes often give officers broad discretion ("may" grant a licence, "may" compulsorily retire). Directions then try to structure how officers use that discretion. The question is: can a superior authority bind a discretionary authority through directions?
BASIC PRINCIPLE
Directions cannot interfere with or dictate the exercise of discretion conferred by statute on a designated officer. The legislature, by vesting power in a specific officer, has expressed trust in that officer's judgment. Directions may only lay down relevant considerations — they cannot dictate the outcome.
✓ DIRECTIONS BINDING — GAP FILLING
Rules silent on criteria/procedure → Government issues directions filling the gap → Directions are binding as part of statutory scheme
State of UP v Chandra Mohan (SC)
"directions fill up the yawning gap in the provisions"
💡 All India Services rule: compulsory retirement at 50 — but no criteria. Government issues directions with procedure and criteria. SC held: directions binding — they fill the gap and prevent arbitrariness.
✓ SUPERIOR → SUB-DELEGATEE
Central Government sub-delegates power to CCI/Commissioner with guidelines → Guidelines binding on sub-delegatee
Because power was given SUBJECT TO those guidelines
Narendra Kumar v UOI (SC)
📝 If you receive power with conditions attached — the conditions are binding on you. You cannot ignore the conditions that came with your delegated power.
✓ CONSTITUTIONALLY SAVING A RULE
Rule gives broad discretion (potentially unconstitutional) → Directions lay down procedural guidelines → Rule read with directions = constitutional
Directions treated as integral part of statutory scheme
✗ CANNOT FETTER DISCRETION
Direction dictating outcome to discretionary authority = invalid
Authority must apply its own mind — cannot be reduced to rubber stamp
No direction can deprive officer of independent judgment
💡 State Government directing licensing authority to grant licence ONLY to one co-operative society (Mannalal — SC): invalid — government concentrated entire licensing power in itself, destroying the scheme.
IMPORTANT — Dharani Sugars v UOI (2019) 5 SCC 480
After Section 35-AA Banking Regulation Act: RBI can direct banking companies to initiate insolvency process ONLY under two conditions — (i) Central Government authorisation + (ii) specific defaults. Any RBI direction without CG authorisation under 35-AA = ultra vires. Section 35-AA, by necessary implication, prohibits this power from being exercised in any other manner.
💡 GST Application: Where CGST Act requires specific prerequisites before a power is exercised (e.g., Section 67 search requires authorisation from officer of at least Joint Commissioner rank), a circular relaxing this condition = ultra vires. Directions cannot override the statutory conditions precedent.
8.3.8 — Directions to Quasi-Judicial Bodies — The Independence Principle
🏛️ Directions Cannot Bind Quasi-Judicial Bodies in Their Adjudicatory Function
📝 A quasi-judicial body is like a mini-court — it hears disputes and gives decisions. The government cannot tell a quasi-judicial body how to decide a case, any more than the government can tell a High Court what verdict to give. This protects fairness and independence.
CORE PRINCIPLE — B. Rajgopala Naidu v State Transport Appellate Tribunal (SC)
It is of the essence of fair and objective administration of law that quasi-judicial bodies should be left unfettered by any extraneous guidance by the executive. The exercise of judicial powers should be regulated by law and rules — NOT by administrative directions which are neither required to be published nor communicated to parties whose claims are affected.
✗ CANNOT BIND QUASI-JUDICIAL FUNCTION
Directions cannot control exercise of quasi-judicial discretion
Government cannot impose restrictions on exercise of quasi-judicial power through directions
Section 119 IT Act: CBDT cannot give instructions to Appellate Commissioner in exercise of his appellate functions
✓ CAN BIND ADMINISTRATIVE FUNCTION
Directions can control exercise of administrative/executive powers
Transport Commission's power to issue directions is "referable only to directions of executive or administrative nature"
EXCEPTION — RELEVANT FACT
If direction lays down a relevant fact, not addressed to the QJ body directly, and the tribunal TAKES IT INTO CONSIDERATION voluntarily → decision not questionable
📝 There's a difference between a direction that commands ("you must consider X") and a direction that simply informs ("X is relevant"). The first is invalid; the second is permissible if the tribunal accepts it voluntarily.
✗ ITAT EXEMPT
Income Tax Appellate Tribunal is exempt from instructions of CBDT
Section 119 IT Act specifically says: NO instructions to Appellate Asst. Commissioner in his appellate functions
ITAT is independent
GST — CRITICAL APPLICATION
GST Officers exercising powers under Sections 73/74/75 (adjudication), Section 107 appeals (Appellate Authority), GSTAT (Tribunal) are all quasi-judicial. CBIC circulars binding on departmental officers are NOT binding on these bodies in their quasi-judicial function. GSTAT can independently interpret the law and override CBIC circulars.
💡 A CBIC circular says "interest u/s 50 runs from due date." Taxpayer challenges before GSTAT that interest runs only from date of demand. GSTAT must independently interpret Section 50 — the circular is not binding on GSTAT. (Confirmed by SC in Union of India v Bharti Airtel Ltd. approach.)
8.3.9 — Directions Cannot Affect Private Rights or Fundamental Rights
🚫 Directions Cannot Curtail Rights — Only Law Can
📝 If the government wants to restrict your freedom of speech, your right to trade, or any other right — it must do so through a law passed by Parliament. The government cannot bypass Parliament by simply issuing a circular or direction restricting rights.
✗ CANNOT IMPOSE LIABILITY
Directions cannot impose any liability on anyone — ONLY rules can impose liability
Government cannot interfere with rights of people without a specific rule of law authorising the act
✗ CANNOT RESTRICT FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS
Directions cannot adversely affect fundamental rights of a person — Art. 21 (personal liberty) restrictions only through "procedure established by law" not through directions
Kharak Singh v State of UP (SC)
✗ CANNOT RESTRICT FREEDOM OF SPEECH
Newsprint policy restricting freedom of press = invalid under Art. 19(1)(a) — cannot be imposed through direction
Bennett Coleman & Co. v UOI (SC)
✗ CANNOT CREATE PENAL PROVISIONS
"Any power to impose penalty must be statutorily warranted — executive government cannot create penal provisions by issuing circulars when there is no authority to impose such penalty from any provision of law."
GST — CRITICAL APPLICATION
CBIC cannot by circular create new tax liability, new penalty, new disqualification for ITC, or new compliance burden beyond what CGST Act mandates. Any circular purporting to impose a condition or disqualification not found in the Act or Rules = invalid.

Example: A circular saying "ITC on construction services disallowed even if building not covered by Section 17(5)" — if the statute doesn't say so, the circular cannot say so.
8.3.10 — Article 14 and Directions — Equality Challenge to Directions
⚖️ Article 14 — Directions Must Satisfy Equality Test
📝 Even if a direction is otherwise valid, it can be struck down if it treats similarly-situated persons differently without a rational basis. Article 14 (equality before law) applies to directions just as it applies to laws.
DISCRIMINATORY → INVALID
Direction that makes irrational/arbitrary classification violates Article 14 and can be quashed
A. Periakaruppan v State of TN (SC)
Selection committee awarding marks in lump instead of under 5 separate heads as directed = arbitrary = Art. 14 violation
UNIFORM APPLICATION → BINDING
Direction must be "uniformly and invariably" applied by concerned authority to ALL cases to avoid charge of discrimination under Article 14
Sadhu Singh v State of Punjab (SC)
DIRECTION ITSELF CHALLENGEABLE
A person can challenge the administrative direction itself if it is discriminatory OR based on an illogical/irrational principle (no nexus with object sought to be achieved)
State of AP v U.S.V. Balaram (SC)
STANDARD FOR DIRECTIONS
Service rules (including directions) must be "reasonable, fair and not grossly unjust" to survive test of Articles 14 and 16
Baleshwar Dass v State of UP (SC)
GST APPLICATION
A CBIC circular that applies different GST treatment to similarly-placed taxpayers without rational basis can be challenged under Article 14. Example: A circular exempting one category of service provider but not another similarly-placed category — if no rational distinguishing basis exists — challengeable as arbitrary and discriminatory.
8.3.11 & 8.3.12 — Other Grounds for Non-Enforcement / Enforcement of Directions
📋 When Can Directions Be Enforced / Not Enforced — Summary Matrix
Grounds for NON-ENFORCEMENT
• Inconsistent with statute or rules
• Curtails fundamental rights or private rights
• Violates Article 14 (discriminatory)
• Issued to quasi-judicial body restricting adjudicatory function
• Vague or ambiguous (same as rule — can be struck down)
• Retrospective effect (directions cannot be retrospective — same as rules)
• Creates penalty/liability without statutory authority
• Non-application of mind in formulating direction
• Never communicated to person concerned (no legal force)
• State direction inconsistent with Central Government direction u/s 36 ULC Act
• Government company (separate juristic entity) not bound by government direction in absence of statutory provision
Grounds for ENFORCEMENT
• Fills gap in rules (no inconsistency with rules)
• Beneficial to the individual (usually enforced by courts)
• Promissory estoppel — Administration having given reason to believe it will follow direction
• Legitimate expectation — person given reason to believe state will abide by its directions
• Compliance made condition of licence renewal or government contract award
• Direction in public interest — member of public can enforce if deviated from to prejudice public
• Non-statutory code given statutory recognition (binding on all concerned)
• Direction clarifies/implements conditions for exercise of rights conferred in favour of persons
• Army instructions laying down procedure for release of officers — held to have statutory force
Virendra Kumar v UOI (SC)
• Policy followed for over three decades and providing complete procedure
Bant Singh v Man Singh (P&H HC)
CBDT / CBIC Circular Enforceability Matrix — GST Practitioner's Quick Reference
Type of Circular / Direction Binding on GST Officers? Binding on AAR / AAAR? Binding on GSTAT / HC / SC? Key Principle / Case
CBIC Circular u/s 168 CGST Act — Beneficial to Assessee (relaxing liability) YES — Must follow Should follow unless plainly wrong NO — Courts independently interpret law UCO Bank v CIT; Keshavji Ravji & Co. v CIT
CBIC Circular — Adverse to Assessee (imposing new burden not in Act) NO — Invalid NO — Invalid NO — Invalid Kerala Financial Corp. v CIT; Section 119(2)(a) IT Act — circulars cannot be adverse to assessee
CBIC Circular — Interpretive (how to apply a provision) YES — Must follow (it's the government's official view) May be considered; not binding NO — contemporanea expositio only; court independently interprets; no estoppel against statute Bengal Iron Corp. v CTO; K.P. Varghese v ITO
CBIC Trade Notice / Press Note (not issued u/s 168) NO — Not binding; mere information NO NO CIT v Anjum M.H. Ghaswala — press releases not binding
CBIC Circular contrary to CGST Act (e.g., granting exemption not in Act) NO — Circular cannot override statute NO NO Chief Settlement Commissioner v Om Prakash; Kerala Financial Corp. v CIT
GST Council Recommendations (not converted into notification) NO — Recommendations only; must be implemented by notification u/s 9/11 NO NO Mohit Minerals v UOI (SC) — GST Council not binding legislature
CBIC Circular — Classifying item for GST rate YES — Binding on officers as administrative circular May consider as relevant; not conclusively binding NO — CCE v Dhiren Chemical Industries: even here if circular places different interpretation, binding on Revenue (but not on court) British Machinery Supplies v UOI; CCE v Dhiren Chemical Industries
8.4 & 8.5 — Publication of Directions | Defects of System of Directions
📰 Publication of Directions — Current State & Defects
CURRENT POSITION — NO OBLIGATION
No legal provision requiring publication of directions
Directions may be issued secretly/confidentially to officers
Individuals may be affected without their knowledge
Unlike rules — no mandatory gazette publication for directions
📝 The Harla doctrine (law must be published to be valid) does NOT apply to directions. A direction can be validly secret — this is a major weakness of the system.
DESIRABLE (NOT MANDATORY)
Important policy decisions affecting public should be published in official gazette
But publication in official gazette is NOT sine qua non of validity
State largesse (contracts, allotments, permits) — policy MUST be published in Gazette
Akhil Bhartiya Upbhokta Congress v State of MP (SC)
DEFECTS OF SYSTEM — SUMMARY
(1) Cannot curtail constitutional/fundamental/legal rights
(2) Cannot impose liability — only rules can
(3) No parliamentary scrutiny
(4) Not required to be published
(5) May be issued secretly
(6) Inferior in status to rules
(7) Always uncertain whether enforceable
(8) Cannot control discretion of quasi-judicial bodies
(9) No clear principle when individual can enforce
RECOMMENDED SAFEGUARDS
(1) Administration should indicate whether a notice is a direction or a rule
(2) System of publication of directions in convenient/accessible form
(3) Directions that have stood the test of time should be converted to rules (formalisation)
Lok Sabha Committee on Subordinate Legislation has emphasised this repeatedly
ANURADHA BHASIN v UOI (2020) 3 SCC 637
Section 144 CrPC orders (directions restricting internet/movement) must state material facts to enable judicial review. Power should be exercised in bona fide, reasonable manner. Repetitive Section 144 orders = abuse of power. Principles of proportionality apply — least intrusive measure must be applied. Authorities must publish nature of orders.
8.6 & 8.7 — Rules Made by Private Bodies | Amendment in Directions
🏢 Rules by Private Bodies — Do They Have Force of Law?
GENERAL RULE — NO FORCE OF LAW
Bye-laws of co-operative society / private association registered under statute = NOT "law"
Binding between parties (contractual) but do not have force of law
Industrial tribunal can vary society's bye-laws in industrial disputes
📝 Articles of Association of a company don't bind the world — they bind shareholders. Similarly, a co-op society's bye-laws bind members but are not law like the CGST Act.
EXCEPTION — STATUTORY RECOGNITION
When parent Act declares contravention of bye-laws = void → bye-laws have statutory force
Megna Mills v Ashoka Marketing (SC) — East India Jute Exchange bye-laws enforceable because Act said contravention = void
Non-statutory code given statutory recognition = binding on all
FAIRNESS GROUND
Bye-laws of Indian Statistical Institute — upheld on ground of fair play even without deciding whether rules or directions
Should be followed to avoid arbitrariness
B.S. Minhas v Statistical Institute (SC)
AMENDMENT IN DIRECTIONS — 8.7
Administrative rules (directions) can be modified, amended or consolidated by authorities WITHOUT following any particular procedure. No legal restrictions, so long as they do not offend Constitution or statutes or statutory rules.
A circular can cancel another circular. Directions can be altered any time by concerned authority issuing fresh directions.
📝 Because directions are not law — you don't need to follow the same process as amending a statute or rule. Government can change its mind about a direction by simply issuing a new direction. This is both a strength (flexibility) and a weakness (instability).
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter VIII | Directions
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, First Appellate Authority, and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Kerala Financial Corporation v CIT
Supreme Court of India | (1994) 4 SCC 375 | Section 119 Income Tax Act
Key Ratio — Circulars Cannot Override the Act
"What section 119 has empowered is to issue orders, instructions or directions for the 'proper administration' of the Act or for such other purposes specified in sub-section (2) of the section. Such an order, instruction or direction cannot override the provisions of the Act; that would be destructive of all the known principles of law as the same would really amount to giving power to a delegated authority to even amend the provision of law enacted by Parliament."
When to Use — When CBIC circular purports to deny ITC, impose additional conditions, or restrict exemptions beyond what CGST Act mandates. Cite: just as CBDT cannot override IT Act via circular, CBIC cannot override CGST Act via circular. The circular is void to the extent it overrides the Act.
UCO Bank v Commissioner of Income Tax
Supreme Court of India | (1999) 4 SCC 599
Key Ratio — Beneficial Circulars Binding Even if Deviating from Act
"The Board thus has power inter alia to tone down the rigour of the law and ensure a fair enforcement of its provisions, by issuing circulars in exercise of its statutory powers under section 119 of the Income Tax Act which are binding on the authorities in the administration of the Act. Under section 119(2)(a), however, the circulars as contemplated therein cannot be adverse to the assessee."
When to Use — When officer refuses to follow a CBIC circular that is beneficial to the assessee (e.g., circular relaxing time limits, clarifying non-taxability of a transaction). Officer must follow beneficial CBIC circulars — they cannot cherry-pick which circulars to follow.
S.L. Sachdev v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1981 SC 411 | (1980) 4 SCC 562
Key Ratio — Directions Cannot Amend Rules
"No one can issue a direction which, in substance and effect, amounts to amendment of the Rules made by the President under article 309. This is elementary."
When to Use — When Department relies on a CBIC circular/instruction that in substance amends CGST Rules. Example: CGST Rule 89 specifies refund formula — a circular changing the formula is ultra vires. A rule can only be amended by another rule notified by the Government, not by a circular. Cite this principle directly.
Bengal Iron Corporation v Commercial Tax Officer
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1993 SC 2414 | (1994) Supp 1 SCC 310
Key Ratio — Interpretive Circulars Are Government's Opinion, Not Law
"So far as clarifications/circulars issued by the Central Government and/or State Government are concerned, they represent merely their understanding of the statutory provisions. They are not binding upon the Courts. It is doubtful whether such clarifications and circulars bind the quasi-judicial functioning of the authorities under the Act. While acting in quasi-judicial capacity, they are bound by law and not by any administrative instructions, opinions, clarifications or circulars. Law is what is declared by this Court and the High Court."
When to Use — Before any GST adjudicating authority, appellate authority, or tribunal acting in quasi-judicial capacity. Cite to establish: (i) you are not bound by CBIC circular in your quasi-judicial capacity; (ii) the law is what courts say it is; (iii) there is no estoppel against the statute — tax is leviable if legally leviable regardless of circular.
B. Rajgopala Naidu v State Transport Appellate Tribunal
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1964 SC 1573 | (1964) 7 SCR 1
Key Ratio — Directions Cannot Bind Quasi-Judicial Bodies in Adjudicatory Function
It is of the essence of fair and objective administration of law that quasi-judicial bodies should be left unfettered by any extraneous guidance by the executive. The exercise of judicial powers should be regulated by law and rules and not by administrative directions which were neither required to be published nor were they required to be communicated to the parties whose claims were affected by them.
When to Use — Before GSTAT or Appellate Authority when officer/respondent argues they are bound by a CBIC circular in deciding your appeal/adjudication. Counter: as a quasi-judicial body, you are bound by the law — not by CBIC circulars. Your obligation is to decide in accordance with CGST Act and Rules, not as CBIC has told you to decide.
Dharani Sugars and Chemicals Ltd. v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | (2019) 5 SCC 480
Key Ratio — When Statute Specifies Conditions Precedent, Directions Without Those Conditions Are Ultra Vires
After section 35-AA was enacted, RBI can only direct banking institutions to move under the Insolvency Code if two conditions precedent are specified: (i) Central Government authorisation; and (ii) specific defaults. The Section, therefore, by necessary implication, prohibits this power from being exercised in any manner other than the manner set out in section 35-AA. Any direction issued without the concurrence of the Central government will be ultra vires.
When to Use — In GST challenges where CBIC circular or departmental instruction is issued without satisfying conditions precedent specified in the Act for exercise of that power. Example: Section 67 search authorisation requires a specific-rank officer's written opinion — a circular relaxing this = ultra vires. The statute specifies the conditions; directions cannot bypass them.
CCE v Dhiren Chemical Industries (1)
Supreme Court of India | AIR 2002 SC 453 | (2002) 2 SCC 127
Key Ratio — Departmental Circulars Placing Interpretation on a Phrase Are Binding on Revenue
"We need to make it clear that, regardless of the interpretation that we have placed on the said phrase, if there are circulars which have been issued by the Central Board of Excise and Customs which place a different interpretation upon the said phrase, that interpretation will be binding upon the Revenue."
When to Use — Two-way sword: (1) When Revenue is taking a position contrary to its own earlier circular — cite this to say Revenue is bound by its own circular's interpretation. Revenue cannot depart from its own interpretive circular to the prejudice of the assessee. (2) When CBIC circular favours your interpretation — officers are bound to follow it. This principle applies equally to CGST Act provisions.
State of UP v Chandra Mohan Nigam
Supreme Court of India | (1977) 4 SCC 345
Key Ratio — Directions Filling Gap in Rules Are Binding and Enforceable
"Since rule 16(3) itself does not contain any guidelines, directions or criteria, the instructions issued by the Government furnish an essential and salutary procedure for the purpose of securing uniformity in application of the rule. These instructions really fill up the yawning gap in the provisions and are embedded in the conditions of service. These are binding on the Government and cannot be violated to the prejudice of the Government servant."
When to Use — When assessee seeks to enforce a CBIC circular/instruction that fills a procedural gap in CGST Rules. Example: CGST Rules silent on how to process certain categories of refund — CBIC circular provides procedure — this circular is binding on officers and cannot be ignored to the prejudice of the assessee.
Akhil Bhartiya Upbhokta Congress v State of MP
Supreme Court of India | (2011) 5 SCC 29
Key Ratio — State Largesse Must Be Distributed Based on Published, Transparent, Non-Discriminatory Policy
The State and/or its agencies/instrumentalities cannot give largesse to any person according to the sweet will and whims of the political entities and/or officers of the State. Every action/decision of the State and/or its agencies/instrumentalities to give largesse or confer benefit must be founded on a sound, transparent, discernible and well-defined policy, which shall be made known to the public by publication in the Official Gazette and other recognised modes of publicity and such policy must be implemented/executed by adopting a non-discriminatory and non-arbitrary method.
When to Use — When Department selectively grants or denies GST refunds, exemptions, or benefits based on undisclosed internal policies/instructions not published in the Official Gazette. Argue: any policy governing distribution of such State largesse must be published and applied non-discriminatorily. Unpublished, selective instructions are invalid under Article 14.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter VIII-A — Interpretation of Delegated / Subordinate Legislation

⚖️ Chapter VIII-A — Interpretation of Delegated / Subordinate Legislation

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Caution
Condition / Unless / If
Optional / Pass-on route
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
Intro: Core Principles
8A.1 Rules
8A.2 Draft Rules
8A.3 Manual
8A.4 Amendment in Regulation
8A.5 Bye-laws
8A.6 Scheme
8A.7 Government Order
8A.8 Notification
8A.8.1 Clarificatory Notification
8A.8.2 Date of Operation
8A.9 Exemption Notification
8A.10 Note
8A.11 Marginal Notes
8A.12 Circulars
8A.13 Circular — SC Decision
8A.14 Government Instructions
8A.15–17 Admin Orders / Govt Letters
8A.18–23 Legal Fiction / Words
8A.24 Retrospectivity
8A.25–27 Policy / Quasi-Legislation
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
Introduction — Core Principles of Interpreting Delegated/Subordinate Legislation
📜 Delegated / Subordinate Legislation — Interpretation Framework
Chapter VIII-A | Principles of Administrative Law
📝 Delegated legislation = Rules, Regulations, Notifications, Circulars, Orders made by the government/authority under powers given by the parent Act (e.g., CGST Rules made under CGST Act). When courts interpret these, they apply specific rules different from interpreting the parent statute.
🔑 Golden Rule — Meaningful Reading in Consonance with Parent Statute
✓ RULE 1
Must be read meaningfully to give effect to provisions of the parent statute
📝 Even if the rule's language is awkward — give it an interpretation that makes it work and aligns with the Act's purpose.
✓ RULE 2
If two constructions possible → choose one that makes provision workable and in consonance with statutory scheme
⚠️ CONFLICT
Delegated legislation read in context of primary statute
In case of conflict → Primary legislation prevails
📝 A CGST Rule cannot override the CGST Act. If there is inconsistency, the Act wins. The Rule is struck down to that extent.
💡 Rule 89(5) restricting refund of ITC on inverted duty — challenged as ultra vires CGST Act Section 54. Parent Act provision prevails: VKC Footsteps (SC 2021).
⚖️ Rules of Interpretation — When Two Sets of Rules Govern Same Subject
(i)
Later rule on same subject → no presumption of repeal of earlier rule; rule-making authority deemed to know existing law
(ii)
General law + Special law on same matter → harmonious construction; if intention clear → that law prevails
📝 Try to read both rules together. Only if that fails, choose the one the legislature intended to prevail.
(iii)
Repugnancy remains despite harmonious reading → prior special law NOT repealed by later general law
(unless clear universal intent to supersede)
(iv)
Later special law repugnant to earlier general law → later special law prevails
📝 Special beats general; later beats earlier — but the interaction is nuanced and must be examined in each case.
📌 Key Canons of Construction — Always Applied
Res magis valeat quam pereat
Better for a thing to have effect than to be made void
📝 Courts prefer an interpretation that keeps the rule alive and operative, not one that kills it.
Construction leading to absurdity, futility, palpable injustice or anomaly must be avoided
Contextual interpretation — read every word in context; keep preamble, other provisions, pari materia statutes in mind
📝 Pari materia = statutes dealing with the same subject. E.g., CGST Act and IGST Act are pari materia — read together for consistent interpretation.
If language clear and unambiguous → plain meaning given effect; court cannot add words or evolve legislative intent not found in statute
ADMIN INSTRUCTIONS / CIRCULARS
In case of lack of clarity → interpreted by taking into consideration their object and intention of the Government

⚠️ BUT Public orders made in exercise of statutory authority cannot be construed in light of explanations subsequently given by the decision-making authority — must be construed objectively with reference to the order itself
📝 The government cannot later say "that circular meant something else" to escape its effect. The circular speaks for itself as it was written and published.
💡 A CBIC Circular issued on GST classification — if it is clear and unambiguous, the Department cannot depart from it by issuing a later clarification contradicting it without formal withdrawal. Reliance Industries Ltd v UOI principle.
8A.1 — Rules
📋 Rules — Interpretation Principles
PRINCIPLE 1
Rule is subservient to the provisions of the Act — must be given a meaning in accordance with the object of the relevant provision
📝 A rule cannot go beyond what the Act authorises. If it does, it is ultra vires (beyond power) and void.
💡 CGST Rule restricting ITC eligibility — if it goes beyond the scope of Section 17 CGST Act, the Rule itself is challengeable as ultra vires: VKC Footsteps (SC 2021).
PRINCIPLE 2
Where language is clear and admits no doubt → plain meaning of provision accepted (applied in Copywriters Rules 1982)
PRINCIPLE 3
Court can iron out creases of manifestly erroneously printed rule entries in subordinate legislation — cannot presume legislature has made a mistake does NOT apply to subordinate legislation
📝 Typographical errors in Rules can be corrected by courts. Courts have more flexibility with delegated legislation than with Acts of Parliament.
LEGAL FICTION
Rules not only create legal fiction but also provide limitations in operation
All consequences ordinarily flowing from rule → given effect unless rule itself limits
📝 A deemed supply under GST Rules carries all legal consequences of a real supply — UNLESS the rule itself limits those consequences.
📌 Special Situations — Two Set of Rules / Conflicting Rules
Two Sets of Rules
General + Specific, no conflict, same object → read conjunctively
Conflicting Rules
Generalibus specialia derogant → Special things derogate from general
Later special overrides earlier general
📝 The specific rule wins over the general rule when they conflict.
GST APPLICATION
Reasonable and practical interpretation of rule preferred over striking it down. If two interpretations possible — one favouring long-standing practice preferred unless clearly violating rules.
Heydon's Rule (mischief rule) applies to Rules also — court entitled to look at legislative history to find mischief sought to be remedied.
💡 GST Rule 96 (refund of IGST on exports) — when two interpretations of sub-rule are possible, court will prefer the one that aligns with the legislative intent of facilitating export refunds (zero-rating of exports).
8A.2 Draft Rules | 8A.3 Manual
📄 Draft Rules & Manuals — Status in Law
8A.2 Draft Rules
Draft rules may be followed where no rules in accordance with statutory provisions have been framed
📝 In a legal vacuum — where the Act gives a power but no rules have been made yet — draft rules (proposed rules not yet notified) can guide conduct.
8A.3 Manual
Manual (e.g., CAG Manual of Standing Orders) supplements statutory rules — shift from manual to electronic forms of governance must also be recognised
📝 Government manuals are not law — they supplement rules. They cannot override the statute or the rules. Electronic governance forms are equally valid.
💡 GST context: GSTN technical manual / FAQs — these supplement CGST Rules but cannot create rights/obligations not found in the Act or Rules. E.g., system-generated GSTR forms are not conclusive — Rule-based procedures govern.
IMPORTANT Statutory rule cannot be supplemented by an executive order. If no rules framed in accordance with law, earlier rules validly framed shall prevail. Tax administration must ensure certainty for citizens — interpretation aiding efficiency of administration is preferred.
8A.4 — Amendment in Regulation
🔄 Amendment in Regulation — Key Principles
PROCEDURE
Once regulation framed → must be amended in accordance with procedures laid down in principal enactment (General Clauses Act, 1897)
📝 You cannot change a rule by a backdoor circular or executive order — the amendment must go through the same formal process as the original rule-making.
PROSPECTIVE
Amendment through notification enhancing rate of levy → legislative act operating prospectively (UOI v G.S. Chatha Rice Mills)
💡 GST rate notification issued on 16 Feb 2019 at 20:46 hrs — not applicable to bills of entry already filed that day. Prospective from time of publication in e-gazette: G.S. Chatha Rice Mills (SC 2021).
NO RETROSPECTIVE
Rule-making power under Mines and Minerals Development Regulation Act does NOT permit retrospective operation → Contribution Rules cannot have retrospective operation by necessary implication
REPEAL & RE-ENACT
Repeal and re-enactment has same effect as amendment on subordinate legislation. Earlier notification issued under repealed enactment → continues valid unless new rule/notification inconsistent or express repeal of earlier regulation
📝 If the parent Act is replaced but the new Act has no repeal clause for the old Rules — the old Rules continue to operate.
KEY PRINCIPLE
Amendments in regulation must be construed in furtherance of legislative policy and not in derogation thereof — but past experience of the state that paved necessities for modifying earlier regulation must not be forgotten.
8A.5 Bye-laws | 8A.6 Scheme
📋 Bye-laws and Schemes
8A.5 Bye-laws
Read as a whole; intent of rule-making authority must be taken into consideration
Rules, Regulations and Bye-laws are of same genre of delegated legislation — none of them can negate what the principal Act permits
CANNOT
Power to recall executive/legislative orders cannot be applied to quasi-judicial orders (e.g., Section 12A ITC registration — cannot be recalled like a bye-law)
Bye-laws which are administrative in character cannot benefit from statutory appeal (e.g., SEBI bye-laws on dematerialisation charges — no appeal to SAT)
📝 Administrative bye-laws ≠ quasi-judicial orders. The rights of appeal and challenge differ.
8A.6 Scheme
Must be read and understood in its entirety in the given background — cannot be read as a statute
Test: Administrative vs Legislative — an order of amalgamation of two specific companies under Companies Act is administrative (not delegated legislation) → must conform to fundamental rights Art 14 & 19(1)(g)
📝 A government scheme that affects a large class (legislative) is different from a specific order affecting particular parties (administrative). The distinction matters for judicial review.
8A.7 — Government Order
🏛️ Government Order (G.O.) — Interpretation
PURPOSE-BASED
G.O. construed keeping in view purpose and substance as well as the object underlying the same — more to promote the same rather than stifle it
CANNOT
G.O. cannot be interpreted in light of a rule subsequently framed
BUT court may determine intention and object of G.O. with help of a subsequent Act
CONJOINT READING
Parent Act + various orders made thereunder → read conjointly with a view to give harmonious and purposive construction
NOTIFICATION SCOPE
Specific notification for specific collector — cannot act as such for any other proceeding unless specifically so notified
(EA. Aboobacker v State of Kerala)
📝 When the State acquires private property (a constitutional right of the citizen — Art 300-A), it must strictly follow the procedure prescribed by law. No liberal view for "public interest" can bypass procedure.
⚠️ RULE — REGULATION — NOTIFICATION
Cannot transgress the provisions of an Act. Two conditions for a rule to have statutory form:
(1) It must conform to the provisions of the statute under which it is framed
(2) It must also come within the scope and purview of the rule-making power of the authority framing the rule
If either condition not fulfilled → rule so framed is VOID (General Officer Commanding-in-Chief v Subhash Chandra Yadav)
💡 A CGST Rule that creates an obligation not traceable to any provision of the CGST Act fails condition (1) and is void. E.g., Rule 86B (restriction on use of ITC) — constitutional validity challenged on ground of exceeding rule-making power.
8A.8 — Notification
📢 Notification — Interpretation Principles
BACKGROUND
Notification interpreted taking into account relevance of background in which it was issued
PURPOSE/OBJECT
Construed having regard to purpose and object it seeks to achieve + statutory scheme under which issued
AVOID
If literal meaning leads to anomaly and absurdity → should be avoided. Where wording is clear → plain language must be given effect
LEGISLATIVE ACT
Executive act of issuing a notification is a legislative action → authorities supposed to know the meaning of the word used therein
📝 Once a notification is issued — the government is bound by the plain meaning of the words used. It cannot later say "we used the wrong word."
CASE — Papad and Badi / Mangori
Rajasthan Sales Tax: Notification exempted Papad and Badi (Mangori). Manufacturing concern applied for exemption for Gole Papad. SC: since notification mentions Papad and Badi together — no intention to differentiate between gole papad and flat papad — Tribunal's liberal view upheld.
💡 GST exemption notifications — when notification exempts "papad" (HSN 1905) the exemption applies to all varieties of papad — flat, gole, masala etc. Departmental restrictive interpretation of "papad" rejected.
ULTRA VIRES NOTIFICATION
A notification cannot override the provision of the Act. A Rule, Regulation or Notification cannot transgress the provisions of an Act.
State Government had no statutory authority to issue notification empowering MTCL to allot contracts for Bus Shelters on roads — powers vest in Municipal Corporation, not MTCL. (Nova Ads v Metropolitan Transport Corporation)
8A.8.1 — Clarificatory Notification
🔍 Clarificatory Notification — Retrospective Effect
Core Rule
An amendment which is clarificatory in nature is deemed to be retrospective in its application
📝 If a notification merely clarifies what the law always meant — it applies from day one of the original provision. Not a new law — just making clear what was already there.
💡 Amending notification (1 Dec 2009) clarified that "highways" always included "expressways" in the 2006 Notification — construed as clarificatory and therefore retrospective. GST parallel: CBIC circular clarifying that a particular service was always exempt — entitled to retrospective benefit.
POSITION
Retrospective application of clarificatory amendments to notifications is analogous to position under statutory enactments — same principles apply
⚠️ CAUTION
Merely requiring additional information or fresh samples does NOT mean that the original exercise became non-est or has to be redone — merely because clarification was sought
8A.8.2 — Date of Operation of Notification
📅 Date of Operation — When Does a Notification Take Effect?
GENERAL RULE
A notification becomes operative and effective from the date of its publication in the Official Gazette
📝 Announcement on radio or in press on the same day of issuance confirms enforceability — but the Gazette publication date is the operative date.
🏛️ Key Case — UOI v G.S. Chatha Rice Mills Ltd (SC 2021)
Notification increasing import duty uploaded on e-gazette at 20:46:58 hrs on 16 Feb 2019
Goods already arrived at Attari border — Bills of Entry already self-assessed at old rate — Customs office closed (Saturday)
Revenue argued: notification applies from midnight of 15/16 Feb 2019
SC HELD
Notification under Section 8-A (Customs Tariff Act) = delegated legislation → effective from time of publication in e-gazette — NOT from midnight
Rate applicable = rate "in force" on date of Bill of Entry presentation
GST PRINCIPLE
Publication of delegated legislation is based on a rationale — need to make it known to the public before it becomes effective. Section 5 (General Clauses Act) applicable to Parliamentary law ≠ applicable to notification under Section 8-A. Each notification under GST Act takes effect from the date/time of Gazette publication — not from midnight of that date.
💡 A GST rate change notification issued on a Sunday evening at 8 PM — applicable from that time, not from 12 AM of that day. Bills of Entry / invoices issued before that time are governed by old rate. Chatha Rice Mills principle applies directly to GST rate notifications.
8A.9 — Exemption Notification
🔖 Exemption Notification — Strict vs Liberal Interpretation Framework
THE SYNTHESIS — No Real Contradiction
Majority of judgments say exemptions to be strictly interpreted while some say liberally interpreted — this is only apparent contradiction. A close scrutiny reveals:

Strict rule = fiscal exemptions (general tax exemptions)
Liberal rule = beneficial / promotional exemptions (encourage specific economic activity)
📝 GST exemptions for healthcare, education (essential goods/services) — if granted to promote social welfare — liberal construction. But general exemptions (claiming tax exclusion on a commercial transaction) — strict construction, burden on assessee to prove eligibility.
STRICT INTERPRETATION
Burden on assessee to prove that item falls within four corners of the exemption notification
Read strictly — literal meaning given
Recourse to other principles of interpretation → only if plain meaning gives rise to anomaly or absurdity
📝 Cannot claim exemption by analogy or extension. Must be squarely within the four corners of the notification.
💡 GST exemption for "goods transport agency services" — if your service doesn't fit the exact definition in the exemption notification, strict interpretation bars the claim.
LIBERAL INTERPRETATION
Once entrepreneur fulfils conditions laid down — liberal construction made
Beneficial exemptions for encouragement/promotion of activities → liberally interpreted
Government sought to increase industrial development — such benevolent act, unless statutory interdict, given full effect
📝 If an exemption is designed to encourage investment (like area-based exemptions) — once you meet the conditions, the exemption is applied generously.
BOTH
Strict or liberal question arises only when plain meaning is absurd or anomalous
If plain meaning is clear — apply it regardless of strict/liberal debate
Gujarat Mazdoor Sabha: Factories Act Section 5 — blanket exemption of all factories from humane working conditions during COVID pandemic — STRUCK DOWN — financial losses cannot be offset on workers' shoulders
📝 Even in a crisis, an exemption notification must stay within the four corners of the parent Act's power. Section 5 Factories Act required "public emergency" — pandemic alone ≠ public emergency in that sense.
WITHDRAWAL OF EXEMPTION
An exemption notification and a notification withdrawing the benefit granted — stand on different footings. Industrial policy must be kept in mind.
An exemption cannot be withdrawn by a subsequent notification with retrospective effect.
💡 If GST exemption was available on a service from 2017 to 2020 and then withdrawn — the withdrawal operates prospectively only. Demands for past period when exemption was valid cannot be raised. GST Notification 12/2017 exemptions — withdrawal notifications operate prospectively.
8A.10 Note | 8A.11 Marginal Notes
📝 Notes and Marginal Notes in Delegated Legislation
8A.10 Note
The note of a rule, being a part thereof, has to be construed accordingly — treated as integral part of the rule
📝 Notes in Rules are not mere footnotes — they are part of the rule and must be read along with it to understand the complete obligation.
💡 CGST Rules — Explanation/Note below a rule (e.g., Note below Rule 36 on ITC) forms part of the rule — must be read with main provision to understand ITC eligibility conditions.
8A.11 Marginal Notes
Marginal notes in delegated/subordinate legislation — may NOT be determinative of contents of provision — but may act as intrinsic aid to construction
📝 The heading/title of a rule (marginal note) can help understand what the rule is about — but it cannot restrict or extend the operative text of the rule itself.
8A.12 — Circulars
📋 Circulars — Legal Status and Interpretation
KELSEN'S HIERARCHY (8A.14)
Constitution (Grund Norm) → Statutory Law → Delegated Legislation (Rules, Regulations) → Administrative Instructions (GOs, Circulars)
📝 Circulars are at the bottom of the legal hierarchy. They cannot override statutes or rules — they only guide implementation within the framework of law.
CANNOT
Administrative circulars and government resolutions are subservient to legislative mandate — cannot be contrary to constitutional norms or statutory principles
(FCI v Jagdish Balaram Bahira)
📝 No circular can protect an illegal appointment or override the law. Even well-intentioned administrative circulars cannot protect constitutional violations.
CANNOT
Circular is not a substitute for determination of tax liability — Sales Tax Commissioner's circular cannot determine liability
💡 CBIC Circulars on GST classification cannot determine final tax liability — they guide, they do not bind courts. A court can always take a different view from a CBIC Circular.
CAN
Whether circular binding or not depends on nature of statute. In some cases — held binding

Ambiguity in circular → relevant factor = how the department itself understood it (especially when two interpretations possible)
📝 If the department has consistently applied a circular one way — that consistent understanding is a relevant factor in interpretation of ambiguous circulars.
BENEFICIAL CIRCULAR
Beneficial circular → can be applied retrospectively
E.g., CBDT circular restraining appeals where tax impact < Rs.10 lakh — applies to all pending cases on date circular came into effect
📝 A circular that benefits taxpayers can be applied to pending matters too — even if issued after the dispute arose. This is a well-settled principle in tax litigation.
💡 CBIC circular raising monetary limits for GST litigation — beneficial to taxpayers — applies to all appeals pending on date of circular. Taxpayer can rely on this circular even in old disputes.
ULTRA VIRES CIRCULAR — Dharani Sugars Case
RBI circular issued under Section 45-L(3) of BR Act for restructuring of borrower entities — SC struck down the entire circular as ultra vires because RBI had not satisfied mandatory conditions of Section 45-L(3). Since circular applied to both banking and non-banking institutions jointly, the entire circular declared of no effect. All actions taken under said circular — including triggering of Insolvency Code — fall along with circular.
📝 Even a circular issued by a powerful authority like RBI must strictly comply with the enabling provision. If it doesn't — it is void in its entirety, and all consequential actions fall like dominoes.
💡 GST parallel: A CBIC instruction that goes beyond the CGST Act or Rules (e.g., directing field officers to deny ITC without following Section 73/74 procedure) — ultra vires and cannot be relied upon. Taxpayer can challenge such instructions in writ jurisdiction.
8A.13 — Circular in Pursuance of Supreme Court Decision
🏛️ Circular Issued in Pursuance of SC Decision
RULE
The circular issued in pursuance of the Supreme Court decision cannot be construed regardless of that decision
📝 If the government issues a circular to implement an SC judgment — that circular must be read and interpreted in light of that SC judgment. The circular draws its validity and scope from the SC decision, not independently.
💡 If CBIC issues a circular implementing an SC judgment on ITC (e.g., Filco Trade Centre on TRAN-1) — the circular cannot go beyond or restrict what the SC judgment directed. The SC judgment is the master; the circular is the servant.
8A.14 — Government Instructions / Kelsen's Hierarchy
🗂️ Kelsen's Hierarchy of Legal Norms — Government Instructions
📊 Descending Order of Legal Norms (Kelsen)
🔼 Constitution of India (Grund Norm)
↓
Statutory Law (Acts of Parliament)
↓
Delegated Legislation (Rules, Regulations etc.)
↓
Administrative Instructions (GOs, Circulars, etc.)
📝 Grund norm = the foundational norm. Everything below it must be consistent with it. A circular inconsistent with a Rule is invalid. A Rule inconsistent with the Act is invalid. An Act inconsistent with the Constitution is invalid.
Subordinate legislation through rules have primacy over administrative orders/decisions/executive instructions/orders/circulars
CANNOT
Executive instructions cannot displace statutory rules
(UOI v Ashok Kumar Aggarwal)
💡 GST: GSTIN-level instructions/system limitations cannot override the statutory time limit for ITC claim (Section 16(4) CGST Act). Technical glitch in system ≠ valid excuse for disallowing ITC that is otherwise eligible.
Gaps in rules can be filled by instructions — but instructions must not be inconsistent with statutory rules framed
Government instructions must be read in conformity with provisions of parent Act
If departmental instructions don't state deviation = nullity → they are directory in nature
8A.15 Admin Orders/Govt Resolutions | 8A.16 Govt Letters | 8A.17 Confidential Letters
📁 Administrative Orders, Government Letters & Confidential Letters
8A.15 Admin Orders/Govt Resolutions
Interpreted on basis of contents alone — no external aid (like affidavit) permissible for interpretation
Administrative orders cannot simply be ignored by a person aggrieved — no court will grant injunction against operation unless declaratory relief sought about illegality or voidness
📝 You cannot say "I'll just ignore this Government Order because I disagree with it." You must challenge it in court and get a declaration that it is illegal.
8A.16 Government Letters
Once Government by its letter clarified that land to be transferred to "actual occupant" → no question of interpreting "occupant" as de jure occupant
De facto = person in actual possession
📝 De jure = legally entitled. De facto = actually holding. A government letter giving benefit to actual (de facto) occupant — courts will apply that interpretation consistently.
8A.17 Confidential Letters
Confidential letters of Railway Board — cannot be construed as if they laid down a rigid statutory period
They were "by way of guidelines" to ensure power of reversion exercised within reasonable period
Order of reversion in substantial compliance of directions = valid
📝 Confidential administrative letters are not law — they are guidelines. Even if issued late by one month — if there is substantial compliance with the spirit of the letter, the order is not vitiated.
8A.18 Legal Fiction | 8A.19 Interpretation Clause | 8A.20–23 External Aids / Words
🔍 Legal Fiction, Interpretation Clause, External Aids & Key Words
8A.18 Legal Fiction
Legal fiction must be given full effect and be taken to its logical conclusion
📝 Once law creates a fiction (e.g., "deemed supply" in GST), you must carry the fiction to its full conclusion — all rights, liabilities and consequences that flow from a real transaction also flow from the deemed transaction.
💡 GST Section 7(1)(c) + Schedule I Para 2 — deemed supply between related persons even without consideration. This legal fiction must be given full effect — all obligations of a taxable supply (registration, invoice, return) apply to deemed supply too.
8A.19 Interpretation Clause
Should be given contextual meaning — not exhaustive
When statutory enactment defines its terms — that definition should govern what is proved, authorised or done under or by reference to that enactment
All statutory definitions to be read subject to qualifications in interpretation clause
📝 The definition section (Section 2 of CGST Act) governs how words are used throughout the Act. But definitions are subject to qualifiers — "unless the context otherwise requires."
8A.20 External Aids
Explanatory memorandum of Regulations taken into consideration for ascertaining true meaning of provision introduced by amendment
8A.21 Conditions for Licensing
Licensing conditions construed with regard to purpose and object — conditions must not be vague, unreasonable, uncertain and arbitrary — must be proportional — purposive construction given
8A.22 Exception Clause
Normally part of enacting section — must be strictly interpreted and cannot be assumed — must be proved
In case of doubt — must befriend general provision and disfavour the exception
Who claims exception must establish coming within it
📝 Exceptions in law are narrowly construed. The party claiming an exception must prove it — the burden never shifts to the other side to prove the exception doesn't apply.
💡 GST Schedule III (negative list — no supply) — these are exceptions to the charging section. Party claiming Schedule III benefit must strictly prove the transaction falls within it. Cannot be assumed or inferred.
📌 8A.23 Words/Phrases — Key Interpretive Principles
Word/PhraseInterpretation PrincipleGST Relevance
"such as" Merely illustrative, not exhaustive — other interpretation causing violence to language rejected GST notifications using "such as" — list is illustrative, not exhaustive
"regulate" Can include power to grant/refuse licence, to tax or exempt from taxation, to prescribe reasonable rules — power of regulation conferred with obligations not spent with grant of permission GST officer's power to "regulate" does not mean power to prohibit — but may include conditional permission
"control" Wide and ambiguous — taken to mean something weaker than "restraint", something equivalent to "regulation" Holding company "controls" subsidiary — for deemed supply / related person analysis in GST
"shall" Prima facie imperative — prohibitive/negative words following "shall" indicate mandatory provision. But context may show directory nature. Section 16(4) CGST — "shall not be entitled to" — mandatory bar on ITC beyond time limit
"ordinarily" Does not promote cast-iron rule — flexible. Excludes something extraordinary or special. Means "normally" and "generally" subject to special provision. Place of effective management "ordinarily" — not absolute test
"rectification" vs "amendment" Conceptually different — rectification corrects a mistake; amendment changes the law GST — Section 39(9) rectification of returns is different from amendment under Section 37/38
Disjunctive conditions Three contingencies using "or" — disjunctive in nature, not conjunctive — each is an independent path to eligibility GST exemption conditions connected by "or" — each condition independently qualifies for exemption
"Executive estoppel" Meaning given by executive to Parliament at time of introduction of a Bill — may be used against the executive if it takes contrary position before court after Bill becomes law GST — Government's representations before Parliament on ITC scope may be used against Department
8A.24 — Retrospectivity / Retroactivity
⏪ Retrospectivity / Retroactivity of Subordinate Legislation
GOLDEN RULE
Ordinarily, subordinate legislation should NOT be construed to be retrospective in operation
In constitutional scheme — statute and any direction thereunder must be presumed prospective unless retrospectivity indicated expressly or by necessary implication
📝 This is the starting point. Unless the rule/notification expressly says it applies to past periods, or by necessary implication must apply to past — it is prospective. The burden is on the party claiming retrospective operation.
CANNOT
Right or liability created for first time → cannot be given retrospective effect
Benefit already given cannot be withdrawn retrospectively
📝 If a GST notification creates a new obligation (like reversing ITC) — it cannot be applied to past transactions. If it gives a benefit (lower rate) — that benefit once given cannot be retrospectively taken away.
💡 GST rate reduction on affordable housing — prospective from date of notification. Developers who had already collected GST at higher rate cannot retrospectively claim lower rate benefit under a later notification.
EXPRESS SPECIFICATION
If rule/notification/circular claims to be retrospective → must expressly specify
If language clearly shows same was intended to be prospective → cannot be given retrospective effect
📝 Silence on retrospectivity = prospective operation only. The notification/rule must say in clear terms that it applies from an earlier date.
CAN BE RETROSPECTIVE
(i) Clarificatory notification → deemed retrospective
(ii) Clarificatory executive order → retrospective under Contemporanea expositio
(iii) Relaxation of rules for scheduled caste promotion — with retrospective effect — upheld
(iv) State may amend rule retrospectively if no existing right of any person taken away
📝 Contemporanea expositio = interpreting an ambiguous document by reference to how it was understood at the time of making. A contemporaneous clarification can retroactively resolve ambiguity.
💡 CBIC clarification on a GST classification question — if purely clarificatory of what the law always was — applies retrospectively. Benefits taxpayers whose assessments are still open. Available as defence even in pending proceedings.
OFFICE MEMORANDUM
Office memorandum cannot have retrospective effect unless intention of authorities to make it so is revealed expressly or by necessary implication
EXEMPTION
An exemption cannot be withdrawn by subsequent notification with retrospective effect
Where word "now" used in circular → operates henceforth = prospectively only
POLICY DECISION vs STATUTE
A policy decision — prima facie no retroactive operation. A statute may have retroactive operation. That a policy decision affects a past transaction does not, by itself, come in way of legislature enacting an enactment or executive exercising power of subordinate legislation.

Where notification was given retrospective effect — must be given purposive construction and not a construction defeating its object.
8A.25 Intention of Policy-maker | 8A.26 Incorporation by Reference | 8A.27 Quasi-Legislation
🎯 Policy-maker Intention | Incorporation by Reference | Quasi-Legislation
8A.25 Intention of Policy-maker
When subordinate legislation framed by same authority which exercises power under the policy → intention of such policy-maker must be found out from the words used therein
Richa Mishra v State of Chhattisgarh
Omission in 2000 Rules to include age relaxation for women — held casus omissus (accidental omission) NOT intentional exclusion → 1997 Rules giving benefit of age relaxation applied
"After all, we are called upon to interpret subordinate legislation, salutary aim whereof is to achieve social purpose"
📝 Casus omissus = a case omitted. Courts can fill an accidental gap in delegated legislation to give effect to the obvious legislative intent — cannot add what Parliament deliberately left out.
💡 If a GST Rule accidentally omits a procedure step that the Act clearly contemplates — court can fill the gap by reading the Act's intent into the Rule. Cannot use casus omissus if legislature deliberately excluded the provision.
8A.26 Incorporation by Reference
Rule of incorporation by reference in construction or application of subordinate legislation → should not be applied unless clear case is made out therefor
📝 Incorporation by reference = when one law says "the provisions of X Act shall apply." Courts will apply this only when it is clear and explicit — not inferred.
8A.27 Quasi-Legislation
Rules made by governmental bodies but NOT in exercise of statutory or common law power — called:
"Quasi-Legislation", "Administrative Rules", "Administrative Guidelines", "Circulars", "Informal Rules", "Codes of Practice"
Courts have recognised value of informal rules in variety of contexts — subject to judicial review on recognised grounds
Can fill gaps where statutory rules are silent — but must not be inconsistent with statutory rules
CANNOT
Quasi-legislation cannot amend or supersede statutory rules
Executive instructions cannot be issued in contravention of statutory rules — they supplement, they cannot supplant
(UOI v Ashok Kumar Aggarwal)
📝 Supplement = add to, help implement. Supplant = replace. Quasi-legislation can supplement statutory rules — fill gaps — but cannot replace or contradict them.
💡 GSTN-issued FAQs, CBICs Twitter clarifications, CBIC Instruction letters — these are quasi-legislation. They can guide implementation but cannot override CGST Act, CGST Rules or binding judicial precedents. An instruction letter denying a statutory right of personal hearing is void.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter VIII-A | Delegated Legislation Interpretation
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, First Appellate Authority and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Conflict — Primary Law vs Delegated Legislation
Settled Legal Principle | Ramesh Mehta v Sanwal Chand Singhvi (2004) 5 SCC 409 | NOVVA ADS v Secretary (2008) 8 SCC 42
Key Ratio
A delegated legislation would have to be read in the context of the primary statute under which it is made and, in case of any conflict, it is the primary legislation that will prevail. A Rule, Regulation or Notification cannot transgress the provisions of the Act. Before a rule can have the effect of a statutory provision, two conditions must be fulfilled: (1) it must conform to the provisions of the statute under which it is framed; and (2) it must also come within the scope and purview of the rule-making power of the Authority framing the rule. If either of these two conditions is not fulfilled, the rule so framed would be void.
When to Use — When challenging a CGST Rule, Notification or Circular that exceeds the scope of the CGST Act. Argue: the Rule/Notification fails one or both conditions and is ultra vires — void to that extent. The Act prevails over the Rule.
Exemption Notification — Strict Interpretation and Burden of Proof
Settled Legal Principle | Commissioner of Central Excise and Customs, Indore v Parenteral Drugs India Ltd (2000) | Multiple SC decisions
Key Ratio
Exemption notifications have to be read strictly and the burden is on the assessee to prove that the item falls within the four corners of the exemption notification. Once it is found that the entrepreneur fulfils the conditions laid down therein, liberal construction would be made. The general rule is strict interpretation while the special rule in the case of beneficial or promotional exemption is liberal interpretation — because they relate to different sets of circumstances. A construction which permits one to take advantage of one's own wrong or to impair one's own objections under a statute should be disregarded.
When to Use — (i) Assessee claiming exemption: argue once conditions of exemption notification are met — liberal construction applies — benefit cannot be denied on narrow/technical grounds. (ii) Department challenging exemption claim: argue burden is on assessee to prove strict eligibility within four corners — any ambiguity resolved against exemption claim.
Date of Operation — Notification Effective from Time of Publication, Not Midnight
UOI v G.S. Chatha Rice Mills Ltd | (2021) 2 SCC 209 | Supreme Court
Key Ratio
A notification becomes operative and effective from the date of its publication in the Official Gazette. Publication in the case of delegated legislation is based on a rationale — to make it known to the public before it becomes effective. A notification issued by the executive under Section 8-A (Customs Tariff Act / analogously GST Act) takes effect from the time of publication in the e-gazette — not from midnight of that date. The principle embedded in Section 5 of the General Clauses Act (applicable to Parliamentary law) cannot be applied to a notification issued under a delegated power. The rate applicable is the rate "in force" "on the date on which" a bill of entry / invoice is presented.
When to Use — When Department argues GST rate change notification issued in the evening applies from midnight of that day — covering transactions done earlier in the day. Counter: Notification under CGST Act is delegated legislation — effective only from time of Gazette publication. Transactions done before that time — governed by old rate.
Clarificatory Notification — Retrospective Effect
Settled Legal Principle | Multiple SC decisions including BDA cases
Key Ratio
An amendment which is clarificatory in nature is deemed to be retrospective in its application. The position of retrospective application of clarificatory amendments to notifications is analogous to the position under statutory enactments. When a notification merely clarifies what was always the legal position — it must be read as having always had that meaning from the date of the original notification.
When to Use — When CBIC issues a circular/notification clarifying GST treatment of a particular service/goods — and the clarification is beneficial — argue it is clarificatory and retrospective. All past assessments, appeals pending, and even concluded assessments within time can benefit. Also use to rebut Department's argument that past demand is valid when later clarification shows no tax was due.
Administrative Circulars Cannot Override Statutory Provisions
FCI v Jagdish Balaram Bahira | UOI v Ashok Kumar Aggarwal (2013) 16 SCC 147 | Multiple SC decisions
Key Ratio
Administrative circulars and government resolutions are subservient to legislative mandate and cannot be contrary either to constitutional norms or statutory principles. Executive instructions cannot displace statutory rules. Statutory rules can be supplemented by executive instructions, directions or guidelines but they cannot supplant the statutory rules. Orders, office memorandum, executive instructions cannot be issued in contravention of statutory rules. Quasi-legislation cannot amend or supersede statutory rules.
When to Use — When GST Officer relies on an instruction/circular to deny a statutory right. Argue: the instruction/circular cannot override the statutory provision. The CGST Act and CGST Rules prevail. Particularly useful when: (i) circular denies ITC contrary to Section 16, (ii) instruction restricts refund contrary to Section 54, (iii) SOP denies personal hearing contrary to Section 75(4).
Retrospectivity of Subordinate Legislation — Prospectivity is the Default
Multiple SC decisions | State of Jharkhand v Shiv Karampal Sahu (2009) 11 SCC 453 | UOI v Kartick Chandra Mondal
Key Ratio
Ordinarily, a subordinate legislation should not be construed to be retrospective in operation. A delegated legislation is ordinarily prospective in nature. A right or liability which was created for the first time, cannot be given a retrospective effect. In the constitutional scheme of India, the statute and/or any direction issued thereunder must be presumed to be prospective unless retrospectivity is indicated either expressly or by necessary implication. Benefit already given cannot be withdrawn retrospectively. An exemption cannot be withdrawn by a subsequent notification with retrospective effect.
When to Use — (i) When Department raises demand on past period under a new Rule/Notification — counter: delegated legislation is prospective — past transactions governed by law as it stood then. (ii) When benefit/exemption was validly claimed and Department seeks to retrospectively withdraw — counter: benefit once given cannot be withdrawn retrospectively. (iii) When seeking to apply a beneficial notification to past transactions — argue: clarificatory notifications are retrospective; beneficial notifications should be read to include pending cases.
Res Magis Valeat Quam Pereat — Workable Interpretation Preferred
Settled Maxim | Applied in Multiple SC Decisions | H.S. Vankani v State of Gujarat (2010) 4 SCC 301
Key Latin Maxim
Res magis valeat quam pereat — it is better for a thing to have effect than to be made void. A construction should not be put on a statutory provision which would lead to absurdity, futility, palpable injustice and absurd inconvenience or anomaly. The rule-making authority also expects the rule framed by it to be made workable and never visualises absurd results. If the choice is between two interpretations, the narrower of which would fail to achieve the manifest purpose of the legislation should be avoided. A statute is designed to be workable and the interpretation thereof by a court should be to secure that object unless crucial omission or clear direction makes that end unattainable.
When to Use — When Department's interpretation of a GST provision leads to absurdity, double taxation, or result clearly not intended by Parliament. Argue: res magis valeat quam pereat demands the interpretation that gives effect to the GST legislation's purpose (ease of doing business, seamless credit flow, preventing cascading). A literal interpretation that cascades taxes or blocks legitimate ITC is absurd and must be rejected.
Legal Fiction — Full Effect to Logical Conclusion
Corporation Bank v Saraswati Abharansala (2009) 1 SCC 540 | UOI v Arjun Singh (1998) 8 SCC 390
Key Ratio
Legal fiction must be given full effect and be taken to its logical conclusion. The rules not only create a legal fiction but also provide the limitations in operation thereof. In other words, all the consequences ordinarily flowing from a rule would be given effect to if the rule otherwise does not limit the operation thereof. If the rule itself provides a limitation on its operation, the consequences flowing from the legal fiction have to be understood in the light of the limitations prescribed.
When to Use — GST disputes involving deemed supply (Schedule I), legal fiction of supply between related persons or principal-agent. Argue: the legal fiction of deemed supply must be given all consequences of a real supply — but only up to the limitations prescribed in the Schedule/Rule. If the rule creating the fiction limits its consequences, those limitations must be respected. Cannot extend fiction beyond what the statute prescribes.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter 9 — Right to Hearing: When Can It Be Claimed

⚖️ Chapter 9 — Right to Hearing: When Can It Be Claimed

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Applies
Prohibited / Excluded / Void
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative Route
Neutral Reference / Statute
📝 Plain English
💡 Example / GST Link
📋 Index of Sections
9.1 Need for Natural Justice
9.2 Concept & Purpose
9.4–9.5 Application & Basis
9.6 Quasi-Judicial: Natural Justice
9.6.1 Lis Inter Partes
9.6.2 Authority vs A Person
9.7 From Quasi-Judicial to Fairness
9.8 Natural Justice in India
9.9 Functional Approach — Specific Situations
9.9.1 Students
9.9.3–9.9.4 Sanction / Dismissal
9.9.5–9.9.7 Removal / Transfer / Appointments
9.9.8–9.9.10 Trade / Licensing
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
9.1 — Need for Natural Justice
⚖️ Right to Hearing — Why it Exists
Natural Justice | Audi Alteram Partem | Fair Administrative Procedure
Natural Justice = Procedural safeguard against undue exercise of administrative power
📝 When the government or any authority makes a decision that affects you adversely, you have a right to be heard before that decision is made. This is called natural justice — it is not a special privilege; it is a basic rule of fair governance.
TWO COMPONENTS of Natural Justice
① Audi Alteram Partem
"Listen to the other side"
= Right to Hearing
📝 Before any adverse order is passed against you, you must be given a fair chance to present your version of facts.
② Nemo Judex in Causa Sua
"No one should be judge in his own cause"
= Doctrine of Bias
📝 The person deciding against you should not have a personal interest in the outcome. An interested judge is no judge at all.
FOUNDATIONAL ADAGE
"No one should be condemned unheard" — Fundamental principle of civilised jurisprudence.
SC: Natural Justice rules are "foundational and fundamental concepts — part of the legal and judicial procedures."
Why Hearing is important — multiple perspectives:
GOOD DECISIONS
Hearing brings all relevant facts before decision-maker → reduces arbitrary errors
DEMOCRATIC VALUE
Gives participation to concerned persons → decisions become more acceptable
ACCOUNTABILITY
Courts can review administrative action more effectively when hearing was given
GOVERNMENT IMAGE
Prevents elementary blunders in decision-making; bad decisions upset later injures reputation
GST APPLICATION
In GST adjudication (SCN → Order), the right to hearing under Section 75(4) CGST Act is a statutory right grounded in natural justice. Any order passed without meaningful hearing opportunity is vulnerable to challenge — the SCN itself must give adequate notice of the case to be met.
