💡 Where GST officer passes assessment order without giving proper opportunity to reply to SCN, or considers material not disclosed to taxpayer — the order is liable to be quashed for violation of natural justice / audi alteram partem.
9.2 — Concept of Natural Justice & 9.3 Purpose
🔍 What is Natural Justice? — Concept & Purpose
NOT A straitjacket formula
NOT A general rule of universal application
NOT Embodied / codified rules
NOT Capable of precise definition
📝 Natural justice cannot be reduced to one fixed formula. It changes from case to case — what is fair in one situation may not apply identically in another.
IS Flexible — adapts to facts
IS Part of "a judicially-developed, unwritten constitution"
IS Pragmatic requirement of fair play in action
IS Applicable to administrative law & public law
📌 PURPOSE of Natural Justice Principles
Prevention of miscarriage of justice
Check on arbitrary exercise of power by State
Duty to act fairly — "fair play in action"
KEY FLEXIBILITY RULE
Rules cannot be put in a legal straitjacket. They yield to and change with the exigencies of different situations. Can be adapted and modified by statutes, statutory rules, and the constitution of the Tribunal — UOI v Tulsiram Patel
📝 Natural justice is flexible — it bends to fit the situation. A disciplinary inquiry needs full hearing; a time-sensitive regulatory action may need less. The core — that you must have a chance to be heard — remains constant.
9.4–9.5 — Application of Natural Justice & Basis to Claim Right of Hearing
🏛️ When Does Natural Justice Apply? — Four Bases
TRIGGER RULE
If there is a power to decide and decide detrimentally to the prejudice of a person → duty to act judicially is implicit.
Natural justice operates in areas not covered by any law validly made.
📝 Wherever any authority has power to make a decision that will hurt you — even if no law specifically says "give a hearing" — the right to be heard is implied from the very nature of that power.
📌 Four Sources / Bases to Claim Right of Hearing
BASIS 1
Fundamental Rights
Article 19 — restrictions must be procedurally reasonable; courts insist on hearing
BASIS 2
Article 14
"Audi alteram partem is part of Article 14." Violation of natural justice = arbitrariness = violation of Article 14
📝 Syllogism: NJ violation → arbitrariness → discrimination → Article 14 violation. SC in Tulsiram: Article 14 is not the sole repository — it is the "constitutional guardian" of NJ.
BASIS 3
Express Statutory Provision
Parent statute itself imposes hearing requirement (e.g., Article 311, Section 57 Motor Vehicles Act)
💡 Section 75(4) CGST Act expressly requires opportunity of hearing before adverse order — invoking this is mandatory in SCN reply.
BASIS 4
Residuary / Common Law
Even when statute is silent — hearing implied from nature of power
Byles J in Cooper: "justice of common law will supply the omission of the legislature"
📝 Silence of a statute does NOT exclude the right to be heard — unless exclusion flows from necessary implication.
CIVIL CONSEQUENCES RULE
Whenever an action of public body results in civil consequences for the affected person → duty to act fairly can be presumed → hearing must be given.
"Civil consequences" = infraction of property / personal rights / civil liberties / material deprivations / non-pecuniary damages — everything that affects a citizen in his civil life.
💡 GST demand + penalty affecting taxpayer's business = "civil consequences" → duty to give effective hearing before confirming demand is non-negotiable. Order passed without hearing is void.
9.6 — Quasi-Judicial: Natural Justice
🔬 What is Quasi-Judicial? — The Three-Requisite Test
📝 "Quasi" means "not exactly." A quasi-judicial function is between a pure judicial function (like a court) and a pure administrative function. If an authority is quasi-judicial, it MUST follow natural justice before deciding.
Three Requisites for Quasi-Judicial Act — Radheshyam Khare v State of MP
① Body of persons must have legal authority
② Must determine questions affecting the rights of subjects
③ Must have duty to act judicially
KEY PRINCIPLE — Province of Bombay v Khushaldas Advani
Where (a) statutory authority empowered under a statute to do any act (b) which would prejudicially affect the subject (c) although there is no lis or two contending parties and contest is between authority and subject AND (d) statutory authority is required to act judicially under the statute → decision is quasi-judicial.
With Lis vs Without Lis — Both can be Quasi-Judicial
WITH LIS
Two+ parties making rival claims before authority → prima facie quasi-judicial
📝 Lis = lawsuit / dispute between two parties. Like two companies fighting over ITC — the GST authority adjudicating is quasi-judicial.
WITHOUT LIS
No rival parties but authority required to act judicially → still quasi-judicial if duty to enquire into facts and impose consequences — R v Dublin Corporation
GST CONTEXT
A GST Adjudicating Authority / First Appellate Authority acts in a quasi-judicial capacity. It must: (a) hear both sides, (b) base decision on material on record, (c) not be influenced by Revenue bias, (d) give reasoned order. Instructions from superiors cannot fetter its quasi-judicial discretion.
9.6.1 — Lis Inter Partes
⚖️ Lis Inter Partes — Dispute Between Two Parties
📝 "Lis inter partes" means a dispute between two contesting parties — like a triangle: Party A, Party B, and an Authority deciding between them. In such cases, the authority is almost always quasi-judicial and must observe natural justice.
Classic Lis Situations — held quasi-judicial:
Industrial Tribunals under Industrial Disputes Act
Licensing Tribunals under Motor Vehicles Act (where competing operators object)
State Transport scheme — undertaking vs objecting operators (Gullapalli Nageswara Rao — majority view)
Central Govt. power u/s 111 Companies Act — shareholder vs company (Harinagar Sugar Mills)
IMPORTANT NOTE
Today, concept of lis is rarely invoked alone to claim/deny hearing — the vista of right of hearing has expanded greatly. Hearing is now available even without a lis, based on "civil consequences" and "fairness."
9.6.2 — Authority versus A Person (Bilateral Situation)
🔍 Most Common Situation: Authority vs ONE Person
📝 This is the most frequent situation in administrative law today — not two private parties fighting, but the Government/Authority on one side and a private citizen/taxpayer on the other. GST adjudication is exactly this — Department vs Taxpayer.
THE QUESTION
Must the authority give a hearing or not in this bilateral (not triangular) situation?
ANSWER
If authority has to base its decision on facts determined by it AND decision adversely affects the person → function is quasi-judicial → hearing must be given.
LIBERAL TREND
Judicial trend insists on opportunity of being heard irrespective of whether function is characterised as quasi-judicial or administrative — the key is "adverse impact on rights/interests."
💡 GST SCN leading to demand = bilateral — Authority vs Taxpayer. Regardless of classification as "administrative" — meaningful hearing is mandatory.
9.7 — From Quasi-Judicial to Fairness: The Evolution
📈 Evolution of the Right to Hearing — From Quasi-Judicial to Fairness
PRE-1963: RESTRICTIVE PHASE
Natural justice only applied to quasi-judicial functions. Courts refused to apply it to disciplinary proceedings, licence cancellations etc. characterising them as "administrative."
Nakkuda Ali v Jayaratne (1951): Licence is a privilege, not a right → no hearing required. Super-added duty needed.
📝 Old rule: If authority had no "super-added" duty to act judicially (explicitly imposed by statute), they could cancel your licence without giving you a chance to speak. Courts refused to imply hearing in administrative functions.
1963: RIDGE v BALDWIN — MAGNA CARTA OF NATURAL JUSTICE
House of Lords landmark case. Rejected "super-added duty" requirement. Held: the duty to act judicially flows from the nature of the rights affected and the power conferred — not from express statutory language.
Chief constable dismissed without hearing → dismissal declared null and void.
📝 Ridge v Baldwin was a revolution. Courts no longer needed to label a function "quasi-judicial" to give natural justice. The question became: does this decision affect someone's rights? If yes — hear them.
POST-RIDGE: FAIRNESS DOCTRINE — Re H.K. (An Infant)
Court of Appeal abandoned the quasi-judicial vs administrative classification entirely. Propounded: whether function is quasi-judicial or administrative — it must be discharged with fairness.
Emphasis shifted from "acting judicially" → "duty to act fairly".
LEGITIMATE EXPECTATION — Schmidt (Lord Denning)
A person can claim hearing even when he has no right but a "legitimate expectation" — where (a) there is some express statement/undertaking by authority, or (b) there is a regular practice which claimant can reasonably expect to continue.
📝 Legitimate expectation = you were given a reasonable hope (by conduct or promise of the authority) that things would continue as before. When that expectation is defeated adversely, you have a right to be heard — even without a formal legal right.
💡 GST context: If Department regularly accepted a tax position of taxpayer for years and then suddenly changes it — the taxpayer has a "legitimate expectation" of hearing before the reversal. Selvi Travels v UOI (passport context) — similar principle.
DANGER OF FAIRNESS DOCTRINE
If "fairness" completely replaces natural justice — there is risk of dilution. Natural justice embodies minimum content: (a) absence of bias, (b) right to know other party's case, (c) duty to give reasons, (d) findings on record. "Fairness" may not always comprehend all these safeguards.
Prof. Northey: "To equate the two may result in tribunals with judicial functions being required to meet only standards of fairness — diminishing protection of natural justice."
CORRECT APPROACH
Both concepts co-exist: (1) Quasi-judicial bodies → full natural justice; (2) Administrative bodies → fairness at minimum. Courts should: (a) broaden scope of right to be heard; AND (b) insist on maximum procedural safeguards consistent with nature of function.
9.8 — Natural Justice in India: From Conceptualism to Functionalism
🇮🇳 Natural Justice in India — Key Milestones
1962 — Board of High School v Ghanshyam Das
SC embarked on expansionist course — recognised nature of right affected and power conferred as controlling question of hearing. Anticipated Ridge v Baldwin (decided 1963).
1970 — A.K. Kraipak v UOI — EPOCH-MAKING DECISION
SC propounded: distinction between quasi-judicial and administrative ought to be discarded for purpose of giving hearing. Hegde J.: "The dividing line between administrative power and quasi-judicial power is being gradually obliterated. The requirement of acting judicially is nothing but a requirement to act justly and fairly and not arbitrarily."
Kraipak moved Indian Admin Law from Conceptualism → Functionalism.
📝 Before Kraipak — if the function was labeled "administrative," no hearing was required. After Kraipak — the label doesn't matter. What matters is: does this decision affect someone's rights? If yes, give a hearing.
1978 — Maneka Gandhi v UOI — UNIVERSALISATION OF HEARING
Bhagwati J.: "The soul of natural justice is fair play in action which is important in BOTH quasi-judicial and administrative proceedings... Application of natural justice is to be regarded a RULE, its non-application an EXCEPTION in administrative proceedings."
Natural justice called a great "humanising principle."
Mohinder Singh Gill v Chief Election Commissioner
"The dichotomy between administrative and quasi-judicial functions vis-à-vis doctrine of natural justice is presumably obsolescent after Kraipak in India."
Natural justice is "so integral to good government" that "the onus is on him who urges exclusion to make out why."
PRESENT POSITION IN INDIA
"Fairness in action" is the established test. Terms "fairness," "fair play," and "natural justice" are used interchangeably. Right of hearing is not denied even when the act is administrative. The question raised now is not whether the function is quasi-judicial or administrative — but whether in the specific proceeding, an opportunity of hearing ought to be afforded.
💡 GST adjudication: Whether SCN response is full or partial, whether personal hearing was given or refused, whether officer considered all submissions — all are judged on "fairness in action" standard. Mohinder Singh Gill ratio directly applicable to challenge one-sided GST orders.
EFFECTIVE HEARING RULE (§9.8(a))
When a decision is required to be taken after giving opportunity of hearing to a person who may suffer civil or evil consequence → it must mean an EFFECTIVE HEARING — not a mere ritualistic or nominal hearing.
📝 Giving a "hearing" is not enough — the hearing must be real and effective. If the decision is pre-determined or the reply is not considered, it is not an effective hearing and the order can be challenged.
💡 GST Orders passed with standard template reasoning without addressing specific replies submitted by taxpayer = violation of effective hearing principle = order liable to be set aside.
9.9 — Functional Approach: General Framework
⚙️ Functional Approach — How Courts Decide if Hearing is Required
📝 Courts no longer ask "Is this quasi-judicial or administrative?" They ask: What is the NATURE of this function? What is its IMPACT on the person's rights? The answer to that determines if hearing is needed.
THE FUNCTIONAL TEST (Automotive Tyre Manufacturers)
The question of application of natural justice must be considered bearing in mind:
(a) Express language and basic scheme of provision conferring power;
(b) Nature of the power conferred;
(c) Purpose for which the power is conferred;
(d) Final effect of the exercise of that power.
Minimum Requirements of Natural Justice (Keshav Mills — Mukherjea J.):
Person must have reasonable opportunity of presenting his case
Administrative authority must act fairly, impartially and reasonably
Requirements can be moulded to suit peculiar situations of a given case
FLEXIBILITY FACTORS (Russell v Duke of Norfolk / Lloyd v McMohan)
Requirements of natural justice depend on: (1) Circumstances of the case; (2) Nature of inquiry; (3) Rules under which tribunal acts; (4) Subject-matter; (5) Character of decision-making body.
BUT — basic principles remain the same; only the moulding changes.
FAIRNESS AS PRIME TEST (M.S. Nally Bharat Engg.)
"Fairness is a fundamental principle of good administration. It is a rule to ensure: (a) vast power in modern State is not abused; (b) authority is not misguided by extraneous or irrelevant considerations; (c) statutory authority arrives at a just decision. Justice should not only be done but be seen to be done — this is the essence of fairness."
💡 GST: Even if SCN was issued, if the hearing was token (5-minute call, no written record, reply not addressed in order) — it fails the "fairness" test and the order should be challenged.
9.9.1 — Disciplinary Action against Students
🎓 Students: When is Natural Justice Required?
✓ HEARING REQUIRED
Expulsion / rustication for misconduct (Hira Nath Misra)
Cancellation of examination result as penalty (Ghanshyam Das — quasi-judicial function)
Board of H.S. & I.E. UP v Chitra — cancelled result without hearing → penalty → set aside
Cancellation of examination result on mere suspicion without inquiry — not valid
Assessment made on BOTH academic and non-academic grounds
✗ NO HEARING NEEDED
Mass copying at entire exam centre — Bihar S.E. Board v Subhas Chandra: No individual charged; entire centre vitiated
Roll number written inside answer book — no misconduct imputed, no cancellation; no punishment
Admission by fraud cancelled — natural justice not attracted
Removal on purely academic grounds (unsatisfactory performance) — JNU v B.S. Narwal: "Courts least qualified to judge academic performance"
KEY DISTINCTION
Removal on academic grounds → NO hearing needed.
Expulsion in the name of discipline → Hearing mandatory.
Assessment on both academic + non-academic grounds → Hearing needed.
9.9.3 Sanction to Prosecute | 9.9.4 Dismissal from Service
⚖️ Sanction to Prosecute & Dismissal from Service
SANCTION TO PROSECUTE
✗ NO HEARING TO ACCUSED
Grant of sanction = purely administrative act. No opportunity of hearing to accused before sanction — State of Bihar v P.P. Sharma
📝 Sanction to prosecute is just the government giving permission to file a case. The accused gets to defend himself fully during the trial — not at the sanction stage.
But: Basic facts constituting offence must be apparent and on record. Sanctioning authority must apply mind.
PRIMA FACIE STAGE
Where administration is merely initiating procedure / establishing prima facie case → courts will not extend natural justice (Wiseman v Borneman — Lord Reid)
DISMISSAL FROM SERVICE
✓ HEARING MANDATORY
Article 311(2) Constitution — before dismissal/removal/reduction in rank of civil servant → inquiry + reasonable opportunity to be heard
Employment in statutory corporation = "public employment" — natural justice mandatory even without statutory regulations (UP Warehousing Corporation)
COMPULSORY RETIREMENT
Generally no hearing needed — not dismissal, no stigma. BUT if order is camouflage for dismissal → court looks behind the order → natural justice applies
PENSION
Cannot be reduced / withheld without hearing. "Right to livelihood" under Art. 21 — termination of service only in accordance with just, fair & reasonable procedure conformable with natural justice.
9.9.5 Removal from Office | 9.9.6 Transfer | 9.9.7 Appointments & Promotions
🏛️ Removal / Transfer / Appointments — Natural Justice Framework
REMOVAL FROM OFFICE
Elected member of local authority, elected president panchayat samiti, member of statutory body → removal as punishment → hearing mandatory to rebut charges
EXCEPTION
Dismissal as matter of policy (not individual misconduct) → no hearing. D.C. Saxena v Haryana — all board chairmen removed by policy decision → no hearing required
SUSPENSION FROM OFFICE
Does NOT require natural justice by authority concerned (as opposed to suspension as punishment)
TRANSFER OF SERVICE
Simple transfer within organization simpliciter → ordinarily no hearing.
BUT if transfer: (a) results in civil consequences, (b) substantially affects status, (c) deprives existing right → NJ must be complied with
Transfer must be to equivalent post. Cannot be for unauthorized purpose (= malice in law). Must be bona fide and within existing rules.
APPOINTMENTS / PROMOTIONS
Selection committee = administrative function. But must act fairly (India v M.L. Capoor — fairness required even if not quasi-judicial)
APPOINTMENT MADE → Cannot cancel without hearing. Order confers vested right — cannot be taken away without opportunity to be heard
SELECT LIST ONLY (No Appointment) → Cancellation of select list for bona fide reasons → NO hearing needed even to selected candidates (Union Territory of Chandigarh v Dilbagh Singh)
9.9.8 Trade & Commerce | 9.9.9 Power Theft | 9.9.10 Licensing
🏭 Trade, Commerce & Licensing — Natural Justice Framework
9.9.8 — Regulation of Trade & Commerce
Kesava Mills — Sec.18-A(b)
Take-over of industrial unit after investigation u/s 15 → Natural justice must be observed before take-over order
Swadeshi Cotton Mills — Sec.18-AA
Emergency take-over (no investigation) → STILL natural justice must be observed. Drastic power, far-reaching consequences → hearing cannot be dispensed with
Price Fixing
Regarded as legislative / non-quasi-judicial function → Natural justice NOT attracted
9.9.9 — Power Theft
M.P. Electricity Board v Harsh Wood Products
Detection of electrical theft → Notice to pay compensation → In view of conditions of supply + prima facie conclusion of theft → NOT necessary to give further hearing before payment of compensation.
📝 Once theft of electricity is prima facie established by the authority on objective criteria (meters tampered, devices found), no further hearing is needed before demanding the compensation amount. The party can contest in appeal.
9.9.10 — Licensing — Hearing at Various Stages
GRANT of Licence
Increasing judicial feeling: if grounds exist for rejection, applicant should be given opportunity to present his side (Chingleput Bottlers — "fundamental rule of law — no decision affecting rights without hearing")
SUSPENSION of Licence
Held: Administrative/interim measure pending further inquiry → NOT quasi-judicial → generally no hearing needed (but courts have sometimes insisted even here)
REVOCATION of Licence
Connected to livelihood/property ("new property") → strong argument for hearing. Liverpool Taxi Fleet — legitimate expectation of operators must be honoured before increasing cab numbers
LIMITING NUMBER
Limiting stage carriage permits u/s 47(3) Motor Vehicles Act → administrative policy → NOT quasi-judicial
SHOW CAUSE NOTICE — NATURAL JUSTICE REQUIREMENTS
Party to whom show cause notice is issued must be made aware that the allegation against him is of collusion / wilful misstatement / suppression of fact. If notice does not disclose this — it is a requirement of natural justice and notice may be time-barred or void.
Raj Bahadur Narain Singh Sugar M. Ltd. v UOI
💡 GST SCN: If the Department wants to invoke the extended period of 5 years u/s 73/74 CGST Act on grounds of fraud/suppression — the SCN must specifically allege fraud/suppression. A vague SCN that does not specify the allegation violates natural justice and the extended period cannot be sustained.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 9 | Right to Hearing
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, First Appellate Authority & GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Mohinder Singh Gill v Chief Election Commissioner
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1978 SC 851 | Krishna Iyer, J.
Key Ratio / Principle
"Once we understand the soul of the rule as 'fair play in action'—and it is so—we must hold that it extends to both the fields. After all, administrative power in a democratic set-up is not allergic to fairness in action and discretionary executive justice cannot degenerate into unilateral injustice."
Further Ratio on Dichotomy
"The dichotomy between administrative and quasi-judicial functions vis-à-vis the doctrine of natural justice is presumably obsolescent after Kraipak in India."
On Exclusion
Natural justice "is so integral to good government" that "the onus is on him who urges exclusion to make out why."
When to Use — When Department argues GST adjudication order need not follow natural justice being an "administrative" function. Cite to establish: administrative vs quasi-judicial distinction is obsolete; natural justice applies to both; burden is on Department to show why hearing was not needed.
A.K. Kraipak v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1970 SC 150 | Hegde, J.
Key Ratio / Principle
"The dividing line between an administrative power and a quasi-judicial power is quite thin and is being gradually obliterated.... The concept of rule of law would lose its vitality if the instrumentalities of the State are not charged with the duty of discharging their functions in a fair and just manner. The requirement of acting judicially in essence is nothing but a requirement to act justly and fairly and not arbitrarily and capriciously..."
When to Use — To challenge any GST order passed without meaningful hearing on the ground that the distinction between administrative and quasi-judicial function has been obliterated. Cite Hegde J.'s ratio that "acting judicially = acting justly and fairly." Any adverse GST order without fair hearing violates this principle.
Maneka Gandhi v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1978 SC 597 | Bhagwati, J.
Key Ratio / Principle
"The law must, therefore, now be taken to be well settled that even in an administrative proceeding, which involves civil consequences, the doctrine of natural justice must be held to be applicable."
On Universalisation
"Application of natural justice is to be regarded a RULE, its non-application an EXCEPTION, in administrative proceedings."
When to Use — In GST hearings to establish that natural justice is the rule, not the exception. When officer denies personal hearing or passes order without considering reply — cite Bhagwati J.'s ratio to argue that non-application of natural justice must be justified, not its application.
Ridge v Baldwin
House of Lords, England | (1964) AC 40 | Lord Reid | "Magna Carta of Natural Justice"
Key Ratio / Principle
Whether in a particular case the audi alteram partem rule is to be applied or not depends upon the nature of the duty or the power conferred. The duty to act judicially flows merely from the nature of the rights of the individual — not from a "super-added" statutory duty to act judicially. Such inference could be drawn "when the power relates solely to the treatment of a particular individual."
When to Use — When arguing that the GST adjudicating authority was required to hear the taxpayer even without explicit statutory language mandating it. The duty to hear flows from the nature of the power (which is to impose a demand/penalty affecting the taxpayer's financial rights) — not from express words in the statute.
Union of India v Tulsiram Patel
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1985 SC 1416 | (1985) 3 SCC 398
Key Ratio / Principle
"Though the two rules of natural justice, namely, nemo judex in causa sua and audi alteram partem, have now a definite meaning and connotation in law... they are nonetheless not statutory rules. Each of these rules yields to and changes with the exigencies of different situations. They do not apply in the same manner to situations which are not alike. These rules are not cast in a rigid mould nor can they be put in a legal straitjacket. They are not immutable but flexible."
On Article 14
"The principles of natural justice are not the creation of Article 14. Article 14 is not their begetter but their constitutional guardian... article 14... is not the sole repository of the principles of natural justice."
When to Use — In appeals where Department argues natural justice is fully satisfied by a minimal SCN. Cite to argue: (a) natural justice has definite content and must be substantially observed; (b) Article 14 is the guardian of these principles — any violation is also an Article 14 violation enabling Writ petition.
Swadeshi Cotton Mills v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1981 SC 818 | (1981) 1 SCC 664
Key Ratio / Principle
Even where the power under Section 18-AA (emergency take-over without investigation) is invoked — natural justice cannot be dispensed with. The power under Section 18-AA is drastic, and the consequences of take-over are far reaching and its effect on the rights and interests of the owner grave and deprecatory. Investigation is dispensed with, not natural justice to the concerned owner.
When to Use — In GST cases involving provisional attachment u/s 83 CGST Act or search and seizure — argue by analogy: even emergency/protective powers cannot completely dispense with principles of natural justice. The more drastic the power, the greater the need for fair procedure.
Cooper v Wandsworth District Board of Works
(1863) 14 CBNS 180 | Byles, J. | Common Law Principle
Key Ratio / Principle — The Classic Statement
"Although there are no positive words in a statute requiring that the party shall be heard, yet the justice of the common law will supply the omission of the legislature."
Indian Counterpart — Supreme Court
"The silence of a statute has no exclusionary effect except where it flows from necessary implication."
When to Use — When Department argues that Section 73/74 CGST Act does not specifically require personal hearing at every stage. Cite Byles J. to establish: statute's silence does not exclude hearing; justice of common law fills in the omission. Also cite the Indian SC counterpart for domestic authority.
M.S. Nally Bharat Engineering Co. Ltd. v State of Bihar
Supreme Court of India | (1990) 2 SCC 48 | "Fairness" Doctrine
Key Ratio / Principle
"Fairness, in our opinion, is fundamental principle of good administration. It is a rule to ensure the vast power in the modern State is not abused but properly exercised. The State power is used for proper and not for improper purposes. The authority is not misguided by extraneous or irrelevant considerations... To use the time-hallowed phrase that 'justice should not only be done but be seen to be done' is the essence of fairness equally applicable to administrative authorities. Fairness is thus a prime test for proper and good administration. It has no set form or procedure. It depends upon the facts of each case."
When to Use — When challenging a GST order where hearing was given in name only (perfunctory hearing, reply not considered, pre-drafted order). Cite that "fairness" — the prime test — was not met. Justice must be "seen to be done" — an order that ignores all submissions and is stereotyped fails this test.
Raj Bahadur Narain Singh Sugar M. Ltd. v UOI
Supreme Court of India | (1997) 6 SCC 81
Key Ratio / Principle — SCN Specificity
The party to whom a show-cause notice is issued must be made aware that the allegation against him is of collusion or wilful misstatement or suppression of fact. This is a requirement of natural justice. A notice that does not disclose the specific allegation fails the test of natural justice.
When to Use — In GST cases where SCN invokes 5-year extended period u/s 74 CGST Act (fraud/suppression) but the SCN body does not specifically allege fraud, collusion, or suppression of material facts. Cite to argue the SCN is bad in law for not disclosing the specific allegation — the extended period invocation collapses.
Lloyd v McMohan
House of Lords | 1987 AC 625 | Lord Bridge
Key Ratio / Principle — On What Fairness Demands
"The so-called rules of natural justice are not engraved on tablets of stone. To use the phrase which better expresses the underlying concept, what the requirements of fairness demand when any body, domestic, administrative or judicial, has to make a decision which will affect the rights of individuals depends on the character of the decision-making body, the kind of decision it has to make and statutory or other framework in which it operates. In particular, it is well established that when a statute has conferred on any body the power to make decisions affecting individuals, the Courts will not only require the procedure prescribed by the statute to be followed, but will readily imply so much and no more to be introduced by way of additional procedural safeguards as will ensure the attainment of fairness."
When to Use — To argue for procedural safeguards beyond what is expressly mentioned in CGST Act. GST officers must not only follow Sections 73/74 procedure but also imply additional fairness — e.g., providing copies of relied-upon documents, disclosing investigation report, giving adequate time to respond.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter 9 — Right to Hearing: When Can It Be Claimed

⚖️ Chapter 9 — Right to Hearing: When Can It Be Claimed

Sections 9.9.11–9.10.16 | Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Hearing Required
Prohibited / Excluded / Void
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Discretionary
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections

9.9.13 Demolition
9.9.14 Mining Lease
9.9.15 Allotment of Plot

9.9.21 Power to Exempt

9.9.24 Miscellaneous
9.9.25 Admin Appeals / Revisions
