9.10 Exclusion of Natural Justice
9.10.1 Express Exclusion
9.10.2 Implied Exclusion
9.10.3 Statutory Procedure
9.10.4 No Prejudice
9.10.5 Hearing in One Situation
9.10.6 Doctrine of Pleasure
9.10.7 Prompt Action
9.10.9 Legislative Function
9.10.10 Post-Decisional Hearing
9.10.11–9.10.16 Other Exclusions
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
9.9.11 — Blacklisting
🚫 Blacklisting — Natural Justice is Mandatory
Disqualification from Government Contracts / Business Activity
📝 Blacklisting means putting a person/firm on a list that bars them from doing business with the government. Wade called it "an instrument of oppression combining constitutional and legal impropriety." Once blacklisted, the disability is general — not limited to one contract.
Key Distinction: General Blacklisting vs Rejection of Specific Tender
GENERAL BLACKLISTING
Brings about disability generally in relation to occupation
"Practical metamorphosis of ineligibility"
→ Hearing MANDATORY before blacklisting
📝 Blacklisting disables you from all government work — it's not just one contract rejection. Because of this sweeping civil consequence, you must be heard before the axe falls.
💡 GST: Before cancelling GST registration with blacklisting effect on future tender eligibility, hearing u/s 73/74 procedure must be followed. No SCN = order void.
REJECTION OF SPECIFIC TENDER
Not blacklisting — person remains eligible for other work
No forfeiture of pre-existing rights
→ Hearing NOT required on relative unsuitability ground
📝 If govt rejects your tender as relatively unsuitable for that particular job but you can still bid elsewhere — that's not blacklisting and natural justice is not compulsory.
Leading Cases on Blacklisting
Eurasian Equipment & Co. v State of West Bengal AIR 1975 SC 266
SC: Natural justice must be observed before blacklisting a firm from approved government list. Fundamentals of fair play require opportunity to represent before blacklist.
Raghunath Thakur v State of Bihar AIR 1989 SC 620
"Any order having civil consequences should be passed only after following principles of natural justice... blacklisting has civil consequences for future business in any event."
Gorakha Security Services v Govt. of Delhi AIR 2014 SC 3371
Blacklisting NOT proposed in show cause notice → contractor prejudiced → SC quashed. Failure to mention blacklisting in SCN is fatal to the order.
⚠️ CRITICAL GST PRINCIPLE
Show cause notice must specifically propose blacklisting/debarment if that is the intended consequence. Non-mention of proposed extreme penalty = prejudice to assessee = order liable to be set aside.
"The extreme nature of such a harsh penalty like blacklisting with severe consequences, would itself amount to causing prejudice."
9.9.12 — Property Rights
🏠 Property Rights — Natural Justice Always Required
Demolition / Acquisition / Slum Declaration / Land Lease
📝 Any administrative action that adversely affects a person's property — demolishing a house, acquiring land, restricting building rights — attracts natural justice. Property deprivation without hearing = void order.
Slum Declaration
Govt. of Mysore v JV Bhat AIR 1975 SC 596
SC: Powers u/s 3 & 9 of Mysore Slum Act cannot be exercised without hearing affected persons. Natural justice saved statute from constitutional challenge.
Land Acquisition
Bai Malimabu v State of Gujarat AIR 1978 SC 515
Collector under S.5-A cannot violate natural justice whether inquiry is administrative or judicial. "Before depriving a person of property — prior notice and reasonable opportunity must be given."
Cancellation of Slum Notification
SC Castes & Weaker Sections v Karnataka AIR 1991 SC 1117
Earlier notification cannot be cancelled without hearing slum dwellers — they have legitimate expectation of the benefit. No one can be denied legitimate expectation without being heard.
SETTLED LAW
"It is settled law that before depriving a person of his property or imposing any further liability, the principles of natural justice require prior notice and reasonable opportunity to him to put forth his claim or objections."
💡 GST Application: Before attachment of property u/s 83 CGST Act, notice must be issued to assessee. Provisional attachment without any prior hearing or post-attachment opportunity is challengeable as violating natural justice (read into the statute by courts).
9.9.13 — Demolition of a Building
🏗️ Demolition Orders — Hearing is Mandatory Even if Statute Silent
PRINCIPLE
Before a demolition order is passed → owner and occupant must be given opportunity to show cause.
Even if statute is silent → hearing procedure is read in from elementary principles of natural justice.
Order passed without hearing → null and void
LEADING CASE
Cooper v Wandsworth Board of Works 143 ER 414 (1863) — Classical English Case
Statute empowered Board to demolish house built without 7-day notice. Board demolished without hearing. Court: Power of demolition carries enormous consequences → power open to misuse → hearing essential as Board was acting 'judicially'.
📝 Even in 1863, English courts held: when an authority has power to cause drastic consequences (demolition), it must hear the affected person first. This principle applies universally — including in Indian GST/tax law — wherever a drastic action destroys someone's existing right or asset.
State of UP v Maharaja Dharmander Prasad Singh AIR 1989 SC 997
Cancellation of building permission is "akin to and partakes of quasi-judicial complexion" → must follow natural justice.
Government must also observe natural justice while cancelling a land lease.
💡 GST Application: Cancellation of GST registration u/s 29 CGST Act without proper SCN and hearing = void order. Show cause notice must specify reasons; assessee must be given opportunity to reply before final cancellation order.
9.9.14 — Mining Lease
⛏️ Mining Lease — Premature Termination Requires Hearing
RULE
State of Haryana v Ram Kishan AIR 1988 SC 1301
When mining lease is sought to be terminated prematurely → lessee is entitled to be heard before such order can be made.
Lessee had acquired a "valuable right" to exploit the mines → cannot be deprived without opportunity of being heard.
📝 A mining lease gives you a valuable right to extract minerals over the lease period. If the government tries to cut this short, you must be heard — the right you hold is real property in law, not a mere privilege.
💡 GST Application: Royalty/mining rights are immovable property (profit-à-prendre). Before cancellation of mining lease, hearing mandatory. GST on mining royalty subject to separate analysis — but procedural rights of lessee to be heard before adverse action cannot be bypassed.
9.9.15–9.9.16 — Allotment of Plot & Government Contracts
📋 Government Contracts & Plot Allotment — Natural Justice Applicability
WHEN NATURAL JUSTICE APPLIES
When action has a statutory basis — government acting under statutory power.
DFO South Kheri v Ram Sanehi: Contract executed under statutory authority → must act consonant with natural justice where it affects respondent's right to property.
WHEN NOT APPLICABLE
Radhakrishna Agarwal v State of Bihar: Termination under terms of contract (not statutory power) → natural justice NOT applicable.
"Natural justice attaches to statutory functions, NOT contractual functions."
ALLOTMENT OF PLOT
A. Jithendernath v Jubilee Hills Coop. Society (2006) 10 SCC 96
Plot allotted to another in proceeding where original allottee was not made party → violation of natural justice → nullity.
KEY DISTINCTION
Pre-entry discrimination (at threshold of contract) → Eurasian principle applies → hearing required.
Post-contract termination under contract terms → no statutory power exercised → natural justice not required.
📝 If the government rejects you at the door (before contract) → it must act fairly. But if you already have a contract and government terminates it per contract terms (not a special statutory power) → it's just contract law, not administrative law.
💡 GST Application: Cancellation of government-awarded contract for excess toll collection (A.S. Motors v UOI) — show cause notice + disclosure of material sufficient. No right to cross-examine complainants in contractual enquiry — court held procedure fair.
9.9.17 — Tax Assessment & 9.9.20 — Imposition of Penalty
🏛️ Tax Assessment & Penalty — Quasi-Judicial Functions
Natural Justice is Mandatory in All Tax Proceedings
SETTLED RULE
Tax authorities exercising power to assess discharge a quasi-judicial function → must apply natural justice in assessment proceedings.
Applies to: Income tax, Sales tax, Excise duties, Property tax, Customs duty — and by parity, GST assessments.
Dwarka Nath v Income Tax Officer AIR 1966 SC 81
Commissioner of IT must follow principles of natural justice in revision of assessment order even though he cannot revise to prejudice of assessee.
Kantilal Babulal & Bros. v HC Patel AIR 1968 SC 445
"Imposition of a penalty on a person is at least of a quasi-judicial character." Penalty requires higher procedural standards.
ICDS Ltd v CIT (2020) 10 SCC 529
Where Revenue relied on third-party statements as evidence, right of cross-examination must be made available. Remand to first appellate authority ordered.
9.9.20 — Imposition of Penalty: Natural Justice Mandatory
RULE
Natural justice must be afforded before imposing pecuniary punishment (damages/penalties).
Quantification of damages is NOT a mechanical process — authority must apply mind to facts and circumstances of each case → hearing is mandatory before quantification.
"Any exercise of power prejudicially affecting another must be in conformity with rules of natural justice."
💡 GST Application: Before passing demand-cum-penalty order u/s 73/74 CGST Act — SCN mandatory, opportunity to reply mandatory, personal hearing if requested mandatory (s.75(4)). Order passed without SCN or without considering reply = void. Penalty under s.122 also requires quasi-judicial approach.
9.9.21 — Power to Exempt & 9.9.23 — Impounding of Passport
📋 Exemption Power & Passport — Legitimate Expectation & Civil Consequence
9.9.21 — Power to Exempt
Indian Sugar & Refineries v Amarvathi Service Coop. AIR 1976 SC 775
Sugarcane growers had statutorily accrued right to additional price. Exemption order adversely affected them → they must be given a hearing before granting exemption to mill.
Legitimate expectation of additional price being paid → deprivation requires hearing.
📝 The growers had a right to expect additional payment. Exempting the mill removed that right. They could challenge the mill's claim for exemption — so they must be heard.
💡 GST: Before exempting any class from GST compliance or withdrawing notification adversely affecting legitimate expectations of taxpayers — procedural fairness required.
9.9.23 — Impounding of Passport
Suresh Nanda v CBI (2008) 3 SCC 674
Impounding of passport has civil consequences → passport authority must give opportunity of hearing before impounding.
"Any order which has civil consequences must be passed after giving opportunity of hearing to the party."
📝 Your passport controls your freedom to travel — a purely civil right. Taking it away without hearing you is a serious civil consequence. Courts insist on prior notice.
OVERARCHING PRINCIPLE
The Court observed in Indian Sugar: "The situations in which a duty will arise to act judicially according to natural justice cannot be exhaustively enumerated. The factors which point to an exercise of powers judicially are the nature of the interest to be affected, the circumstances in which the power falls to be exercised and the nature of the sanctions, if any, involved."
9.9.24 — Miscellaneous Situations Requiring Hearing
📌 Miscellaneous — List Not Exhaustive
Courts Have Consistently Recognised Right of Hearing
PRINCIPLE
It is NOT possible to enumerate exhaustively where Administration must follow natural justice. The courts continue to concede rights of hearing to persons affected in new situations.
SituationRequirementAuthority / Case
Termination of citizenship Hearing required Md. Ayub Khan v Commr. of Police AIR 1965 SC 1623
Winding up of co-operative society Hearing required President, Commonwealth Co-op Society v Joint Registrar AIR 1971 Ker 34
Cancellation of agency by LIC Hearing required S. Prasanna v Sr. Divisional Manager, LIC AIR 1993 Mad 150
Cancellation of lease of fishing rights Hearing required PF Co-op Society v Collector, Thanjavur AIR 1975 Mad 81
Suppression of governing body of college Hearing required Khagendra Nath v Calcutta University AIR 1974 Cal 187
Withdrawal of ex gratia benefit / pension Hearing required (modern view) G. Rama Subbu Pillai v Govt of India AIR 1980 Mad 23
Application for winding up of company Workers entitled to be heard Nally Bharat Engineering Co. Ltd. v State of Bihar (1990) 2 SCC 48
Disciplinary action against professional Hearing mandatory before disciplinary authority In re: An Advocate AIR 1989 SC 245; ICAI v LK Ratna AIR 1987 SC 71
⚠️ GST RELEVANCE The list above confirms that wherever there is a civil consequence from administrative action, courts will read in natural justice. SCN u/s 73/74 CGST, cancellation of registration u/s 29, blocking of ITC, provisional attachment u/s 83 — all attract natural justice principles.
9.9.25 — Administrative Appeals / Revisions & 9.9.26 — Discretionary Powers
🔄 Appeals, Revisions & Discretionary Powers — Natural Justice Applies
9.9.25 — Admin Appeals
Nagendra Nath Bora v Commissioner (AIR 1958 SC 398): "Where there is a right vested in an authority created by statute to hear appeals and revisions, it becomes its duty to hear judicially."
Appellate authority → quasi-judicial even if original authority acted administratively.
📝 Once a statute gives someone the power to hear appeals, that power must be exercised fairly — like a judge. You cannot run an appeal without giving both sides a chance to speak.
Suo Motu Revision
Jaswant Singh Saluja v Chief Settlement Commr. AIR 1986 SC 2166
Suo motu revisional power cannot be exercised in respect of finalised claim without giving notice + reasonable opportunity of being heard.
Central Govt. must intimate grounds + give time to show cause before exercising suo motu power.
9.9.26 — Discretionary Powers
Wide discretion does NOT exclude natural justice.
"Greater the discretion, greater is the opportunity for adversely affecting the person — greater the need of hearing."
Rampur Distillery v Company Law Board: "Investment of power carries with it a duty to act judicially."
📝 Just because an officer has wide discretion doesn't mean he can act without hearing. The more power, the more need for fair process — not less.
GST APPLICATION
Additional Commissioner exercising revisional jurisdiction u/s 108 CGST: must follow natural justice.
Commissioner's suo motu revision u/s 108: Must give notice to taxpayer before exercising power adversely. No suo motu revision without hearing = procedurally void.
First Appellate Authority under s.107: Appellate function → quasi-judicial → must hear both sides properly.
9.10 — Exclusion of Natural Justice — Framework
🚧 When Can Natural Justice Be Excluded?
Framework for Determining Applicability / Exclusion
📝 Natural justice is not absolute. There are limited situations where it can be excluded — but the courts have made clear the bar is very high. The Supreme Court has emphasised that natural justice "is so integral to good government that the onus is on him who urges exclusion to make out why."
Test for Applicability — ALL Factors to Be Considered:
(1) Express language of the provision
(2) Basic scheme of the provision
(3) Nature of power conferred
(4) Purpose for which power conferred
(5) Final effect of exercise of power
CARDINAL RULE — Swadeshi Cotton Mills v UOI AIR 1981 SC 818
"This rule of fair play must not be jettisoned save in very exceptional circumstances where compulsive necessity so demands. The court must make every effort to salvage this cardinal rule to the maximum extent possible with situational modifications."
COURT'S STRONG WARNING
"It is not permissible to interpret any statutory instrument so as to exclude natural justice, unless the language of the instrument leaves no option to the court."
(Liberty Oil Mills v UOI AIR 1984 SC 1271)
9.10.1 — Express Exclusion & 9.10.2 — Implied Exclusion
📜 Express & Implied Exclusion of Natural Justice
9.10.1 — Express Exclusion (Rare)
Article 311(2) Second Proviso — Constitution
Disciplinary inquiry may be dispensed with if "not reasonably practicable" → reasons must be recorded in writing.
NOT absolute practicability — "reasonable man taking a reasonable view of prevailing situation." (Tulsiram Patel AIR 1985 SC 1416)
LIMITS
Disciplinary authority cannot dispense with inquiry: lightly, arbitrarily, out of ulterior motives, or merely to avoid inquiry where department's case is weak.
Court can examine reasons ex facie — if not germane, power exercise is bad/without jurisdiction.
📝 Even the rare constitutional express exclusion has judicial teeth — courts will check whether the reason given for bypassing inquiry is genuine.
9.10.2 — Implied Exclusion (Problematic)
UOI v ME Reddy AIR 1980 SC 563: Compulsory retirement power worded as "require" → held "absolute/unqualified" → natural justice excluded impliedly.
However, this is criticised as "odd" — sails against strong present-day currents.
CORRECT POSITION
(1) Natural justice must not be lightly excluded (Maneka Gandhi, Kapoor, Swadeshi).
(2) No discretion is absolute.
(3) Vesting of power in high authority does not ipso facto exclude natural justice (Mohinder Singh).
(4) Natural justice is procedural only — no inconsistency with discretion.
UOI v WN Chadha AIR 1993 SC 1082 — Key Propositions on Exclusion:
1. Audi alteram partem not attracted unless impugned order deprives person of liberty or property.
2. Rule excluded where its application would itself lead to injustice.
3. When investigating officer is merely collecting material (not deciding) — no obligation to issue prior notice.
4. Existence of prima facie case alone is not determinative — must consider whether full inquiry follows.
9.10.3 — Statutory Procedure & 9.10.4 — No Prejudice
⚖️ When Statute Has Procedure / When No Prejudice Shown
9.10.3 — Statutory Procedure
When statute prescribes a procedure → that procedure must be followed.
IF procedure is deficient in natural justice norms → natural justice must SUPPLEMENT it.
IF statutory procedure is detailed, fair and complete → may imply natural justice excluded beyond that procedure (Furnell — use with caution).
📝 A statute's prescribed procedure is the floor, not the ceiling. Courts read natural justice into gaps ("unoccupied interstices" — ICAI v LK Ratna). But if the procedure is comprehensive and fair, nothing more may be required.
9.10.4 — No Prejudice Defence
Department often argues: "Even with hearing, same result — so no prejudice, no violation."
SC in S.L. Kapoor: This argument is "pernicious" — must be rejected.
"Non-observance of natural justice is itself prejudice to any man and proof of prejudice independently of proof of denial of natural justice is unnecessary." (Swadeshi)
Exception: If on admitted/indisputable facts only ONE conclusion possible and ONE penalty permissible → court may not compel observance of natural justice (not because it approves, but courts do not issue futile writs).
But if conclusions controversial even slightly → natural justice is a MUST.
MEGARRY J IN JOHN v REES — Powerful Ratio on No Prejudice
"As everybody who has anything to do with the law well knows, the path of the law is strewn with examples of open and shut cases which, somehow, were not; of unanswerable charges which, in the event, were completely answered; of inexplicable conduct which was fully explained; of fixed and unalterable determinations that, by discussion, suffered a change."
💡 GST Application: Department cannot say "we have documents proving evasion, so no prejudice in not giving hearing." The assessee might have explanations for every document. "No prejudice" defence almost never succeeds in the face of proper SCN requirements under ss. 73/74 CGST Act.
9.10.5 — Hearing in One Situation But Not Another
📋 Statutory Silence ≠ Automatic Exclusion of Natural Justice
OLD (Weak) View — Radeshyam
If statute expressly provides hearing in Situation A but silent for Situation B → legislative silence = exclusion of hearing in B.
This view is largely overruled.
CORRECT VIEW — Kapoor, Lalla, Swadeshi
"The silence of a statute has no exclusionary effect except where it follows from necessary implication."
"It is not always a necessary inference that if opportunity is expressly provided in one provision and not so provided in another, opportunity is to be considered as excluded from that other provision."
Decisive question: Does the administrative action entail civil consequences? If yes → hearing must be afforded.
📝 Legislative silence about hearing does not mean the legislature said "no hearing." It may just be an oversight. Courts will still read in hearing if civil consequences exist.
Coal Mines PF Commissioner v JP Lalla AIR 1976 SC 676
S.7-B expressly provided hearing for contribution determination. S.10-F (damages) was silent. SC: Natural justice must be observed in determining damages under S.10-F despite legislative silence.
9.10.6 — Doctrine of Pleasure
👑 Doctrine of Pleasure — Employment Terminable at Will
Natural Justice Does Not Apply — But With Limits
📝 "Pleasure doctrine" = certain appointments (like nominated members, Crown servants) can be terminated any time without notice or hearing. This originates from English Crown service. BUT in a democracy governed by rule of law, it is not a licence for arbitrary, whimsical or capricious action.
WHEN DOCTRINE APPLIES
Appointment held at pleasure of Government → no need to give opportunity before removal.
Examples: Nominated members (UP Municipalities Act, Mara Autonomous District Council).
BP Singhal v UOI (2010): Withdrawal must be for valid reasons — not sweet will/whim/fancy — but need for cause may vary.
LIMITS ON DOCTRINE
1. Withdrawal of pleasure is NOT a licence for unfettered discretion or arbitrary action.
2. "Need for a cause for withdrawal exists — degree of scrutiny during judicial review may vary."
3. When stigma is cast on performance/character → statute may specifically provide for hearing before removal even in pleasure doctrine context.
📝 Even pleasure doctrine has limits in a democracy. The government cannot act purely on whim. There must be some reason, even if not required to be stated formally.
9.10.7 — Prompt Action & 9.10.9 — Legislative Function
⚡ Urgency / Prompt Action & Legislative Function — Hearing Excluded or Modified
9.10.7 — Prompt Action
Hearing may be excluded if prompt action needed in interest of public safety/health/morality/national security.
Examples: Preventive detention; pulling down property to prevent fire spread; destruction of contagious animals; demolition of imminently dangerous structure.
MODERN POSITION — Natural Justice Flexible, Not Excluded
SC's consistent position (Swadeshi, Kapoor, Mohinder Singh Gill):
"Natural justice being flexible can always be tailored to situation. The question is whether 'barest notice and littlest opportunity' cannot be provided."
Court must make every effort to salvage the principle with situational modifications.
📝 Even in urgent cases, courts now insist: can a short/minimal hearing be given? If yes, it must be. Complete exclusion only when urgency is truly extreme and even minimal hearing would defeat the purpose.
9.10.9 — Legislative Function
When administrative action is legislative in character (general order not targeting specific persons) → no hearing required.
Examples: Price fixation; royalty/dead rent revision; notification prohibiting power crushers; declaration of area as dry area; enhancement of electricity rates for all consumers; formation of municipal corporation.
EXCEPTION
Even in legislative action — hearing can be claimed on basis of deprivation of legitimate expectation.
Some SC cases have insisted on hearing even where function was legislative.
📝 General rules affecting everyone (like price fixation) don't need individual hearings — it would be impractical. But personal rights/expectations can still be protected.
9.10.10 — Post-Decisional Hearing
🔄 Pre-Decisional vs Post-Decisional Hearing
Maneka Gandhi v UOI AIR 1978 SC 597 — Foundation Case
Pre-Decisional Hearing
Hearing BEFORE decision is taken.
→ PREFERRED and BETTER safeguard for affected person.
Must be used whenever feasible.
Post-Decisional Hearing
Hearing AFTER tentative decision.
→ LESS EFFECTIVE: "once decision taken, there is tendency to uphold it; representation may not yield fruitful purpose" (K.I. Shephard).
NOT adequate in dismissal cases with serious consequences.
Resort only when pre-decisional hearing NOT FEASIBLE.
📝 A post-decisional hearing is like asking someone to change their mind after they've already made it up. Courts allow it reluctantly and only when pre-decisional hearing would defeat the purpose of the action.
When Post-Decisional Hearing Is Acceptable (Maneka Gandhi):
(i) Authority has large number of cases — pre-decisional not feasible
(ii) Prompt decision needed (e.g., impounding passport — person may flee)
(iii) National emergency / imminent danger
(iv) Interim/provisional order pending investigation
⚠️ HL TREHAN PRINCIPLE
"The post-decisional opportunity of hearing does not subserve the rules of natural justice. The authority who embarks upon a post-decisional hearing will naturally proceed with a closed mind and there is hardly any chance of getting proper consideration."
→ Post-decisional hearing as substitute for pre-decisional hearing = INVALID.
💡 GST Application: Provisional attachment u/s 83 CGST Act without any SCN/hearing is often challenged. Courts have held it must not be used mechanically. Post-attachment representation must be bona fide considered. Section 83 attachment should be of limited duration and challenged before Commissioner. Pre-decisional safeguards under s.73/74 cannot be substituted by post-order representations.
9.10.11–9.10.16 — Other Specific Exclusions of Natural Justice
📋 Specific Situations Where Natural Justice Excluded / Not Required
SectionSituationPrincipleGST Relevance
9.10.11
Enforcement of Final Award		 Tribunal made final binding award Application of NJ limited to disputed factual/legal situations. Where only one conclusion possible, writ won't issue merely for NJ violation. Final assessment orders upheld by tribunal: post-tribunal hearing formality not required before enforcement.
9.10.12
Useless Formality		 Appellant not qualified on cut-off date (selection illegal); fake appointment letters; nullity ab initio Court will not insist on formality where result would remain same. Hearing would be futile exercise. Where GST registration obtained by fraud/fake documents — cancellation may not require elaborate hearing.
9.10.13
Vague/Belated Representation		 Large scale misuse of medical equipment exemption — vague and belated representation Non-consideration of vague and belated representation: no violation of NJ. Assessee's representation against GST demand must be clear, specific, timely — vague objections may be rejected without detailed hearing.
9.10.14
Allottee Non-Compliance		 DDA plot allottee failed to comply with allotment conditions Cancellation of allotment + forfeiture of earnest money without hearing — valid when allottee failed own obligations. GST registration cancelled for non-filing of returns: assessee who has not complied cannot always insist on elaborate pre-cancellation process.
9.10.15
No Discretion/Flexibility		 Decision is automatic consequence of facts with no room for discretion When no discretion or flexibility exists — no need to extend opportunity of hearing. Automatic late fee u/s 47 CGST — no discretion in levy — no hearing required before levy.
9.10.16
Fraud Categorically Proved		 Commission of fraud categorically proved Where fraud is categorically proven — principles of natural justice not required to be complied with. Section 74 CGST (fraud) — while procedural steps still apply, courts may be less sympathetic to NJ objections where fraud is clearly established on record.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 9 | Right to Hearing
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Eurasian Equipment & Co. Ltd. v State of West Bengal
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1975 SC 266 : 1975 (1) SCC 70
Key Ratio
"Natural justice is to be observed whenever the government proposes to blacklist a firm on the approved government list. As this has the effect of debarring the firm from entering into contracts with the government, fundamentals of fair play require that the person concerned should be given an opportunity to represent his case before he is put on the blacklist."
When to Use — When Department or authority passes adverse order (cancellation of registration, debarment, blacklisting) without giving SCN / opportunity. Argue: Civil consequence of debarment/cancellation attracts full natural justice. Order without SCN = void.
Gorakha Security Services v Government (NCT) of Delhi
Supreme Court of India | AIR 2014 SC 3371 : (2014) 9 SCC 105
Key Ratio
"When we apply the ratio of the aforesaid judgment to the facts of the present case — had the action of blacklisting been specifically proposed in the show-cause notice, the appellant could have mentioned as to why such extreme penalty is not justified... The extreme nature of such a harsh penalty like blacklisting with severe consequences, would itself amount to causing prejudice to the appellant."
When to Use — When SCN proposes a penalty/demand but does NOT specifically mention the drastic consequence (e.g., blacklisting, cancellation of registration, recovery + interest + penalty together). Argue: Non-mentioning of proposed consequence in SCN = prejudice to assessee = order liable to be set aside.
Swadeshi Cotton Mills v UOI
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1981 SC 818 : (1981) 1 SCC 73 | Constitution Bench
Key Ratio — The Cardinal Rule
"This rule of fair play must not be jettisoned save in very exceptional circumstances where compulsive necessity so demands. The court must make every effort to salvage this cardinal rule to the maximum extent possible with situational modifications."
Further Ratio — Silence ≠ Exclusion
"Dispensing with the requirement of prior investigation does not necessarily indicate an intention to exclude the application of fundamentals of natural justice or the duty to act fairly."
When to Use — When Department argues that the statute doesn't expressly provide for hearing at a particular stage (e.g., during search u/s 67, during survey u/s 70, before provisional attachment u/s 83). Argue: Statutory silence ≠ exclusion of natural justice. Courts must salvage the principle with situational modifications.
S.L. Kapoor v Jagmohan
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1981 SC 136 : (1980) 4 SCC 379
Key Ratio — On No Prejudice Argument
"The argument that 'hearing would have made no difference' is pernicious and must be rejected. Where conclusions are controversial, howsoever slightly, and penalties are discretionary, natural justice is a must. The principle that justice should not only be done but should also be seen to be done applies precisely when the Court is concerned not with a case of actual injustice but with the appearance of possible injustice."
Key Ratio — On Statutory Silence
"The silence of a statute has no exclusionary effect except where it flows from necessary implication. The decisive consideration is: does the administrative action entail civil consequences? If so, hearing must be afforded."
When to Use — When Department argues "no prejudice caused" to defeat the assessee's plea of violation of natural justice. Also when Department claims hearing not required as statute is silent. Both arguments can be countered with this single case.
Megarry J in John v Rees (1969) | Adopted by Indian Courts
English Case | Adopted consistently by Supreme Court of India
Key Ratio — Why Hearing Always Matters
"As everybody who has anything to do with the law well knows, the path of the law is strewn with examples of open and shut cases which, somehow, were not; of unanswerable charges which, in the event, were completely answered; of inexplicable conduct which was fully explained; of fixed and unalterable determinations that, by discussion, suffered a change."
When to Use — When officer in personal hearing says "the documents speak for themselves, you have no defence." Counter with this ratio: Every "open and shut" case has been answered. Every "inexplicable" conduct has been explained. The hearing is not a formality — it is the means of justice. Department cannot prejudge the outcome.
Liberty Oil Mills Ltd. v UOI
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1984 SC 1271 : (1984) 3 SCC 465
Key Ratio — Natural Justice Cannot Be Excluded by Interpretation
"We do not think that it is permissible to interpret any statutory instrument so as to exclude natural justice, unless the language of the instrument leaves no option to the court. Procedural fairness embodying natural justice is to be implied whenever action is taken affecting the rights of the parties."
On Communication of Order
"Ours is a constitutional government, an open democracy founded upon the rule of law and not a cloak and dagger regimen. It is inconceivable that under our constitutional scheme a decision which may have the effect of bringing to standstill the entire business activity of the person affected... should be made and implemented without being communicated to that person."
When to Use — When GST order is passed without intimation / when ex-parte order is implemented without communication. Argue: Constitutional government requires communication. Any statutory instrument must be interpreted to INCLUDE natural justice unless there is absolute no option. Applies to u/s 73/74 ex-parte orders, u/s 83 attachment, u/s 29 cancellation orders.
Mohinder Singh Gill v Chief Election Commissioner
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1978 SC 851 : (1978) 1 SCC 405 | Constitution Bench
Key Ratio — On Urgency and Natural Justice
"The cardinal principle of natural justice as a condition precedent for decision making cannot be martyred for the cause of administrative immediacy unless the clearest case of public injury flowing from the least delay is self-evident. The standards of natural justice vary with situations — contracting into a brief, even post-decisional opportunity, or expanding into trial-type trappings. The full panoply may not be there, but a manageable minimum may make do."
On Silence of Statute
"The silence of a statute has no exclusionary effect except where it flows from necessary implication. Where some direct consequence on candidates/parties emanates from exercise of a wide power, we must read this functional obligation."
When to Use — When adjudicating authority says "we had to act quickly / no time for hearing." Argue: Even in urgency, minimum natural justice must be given. At least a brief post-decisional opportunity must be offered. Urgency is not a blanket excuse to eliminate all procedural fairness.
Raghunath Thakur v State of Bihar
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1989 SC 620 : (1989) 1 SCC 229
Key Ratio — Blacklisting and Civil Consequences
"But it is an implied principle of the rule of law that any order having civil consequences should be passed only after following the principles of natural justice. It has to be realised that blacklisting any person in respect of business ventures has civil consequences for the future business of the person concerned in any event. Even if the rules do not express so, it is an elementary principle of natural justice that parties affected by any order should have right of being heard."
When to Use — In GST hearing before adjudicating authority or first appellate authority: when order proposed has serious civil consequence (large demand, ITC reversal, penalty, cancellation of registration). Even if the relevant GST rule/notification does not expressly mandate hearing — the implied rule of law requires it. Use to compel personal hearing even where officer argues it is optional.
HL Trehan v UOI
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1989 SC 568 : (1989) 1 SCC 764
Key Ratio — Post-Decisional Hearing Not Adequate
"It is now well-established principle of law that there can be no deprivation or curtailment of any existing right, advantage or benefit enjoyed by a government servant without complying with the rules of natural justice by giving the government servant concerned an opportunity of being heard. The post-decisional opportunity of hearing does not subserve the rules of natural justice. The authority who embarks upon a post-decisional hearing will naturally proceed with a closed mind and there is hardly any chance of getting a proper consideration."
When to Use — When Department says "we gave you an opportunity after passing the order, so natural justice is satisfied." Counter: Post-decisional hearing with closed mind is no hearing at all. Pre-decisional hearing is the only proper compliance with natural justice. Order must be quashed and fresh proceedings with proper pre-decisional hearing must be directed.
ICDS Ltd. v CIT
Supreme Court of India | (2020) 10 SCC 529
Key Ratio — Right of Cross-Examination in Tax Proceedings
Where the Revenue relied on evidence through statements of some persons, the Supreme Court said that right of cross-examination ought to be made available, while remanding the matter to the first appellate authority. The right of cross-examination is an integral element of natural justice in adversarial tax proceedings where third-party statements are relied upon.
When to Use — When GST adjudicating authority relies on statements of third parties (suppliers, customers, transporters) recorded during investigation/inspection to raise demand against assessee. Argue: Assessee has right to cross-examine persons whose statements are used against them. Non-supply of such statements and non-opportunity of cross-examination = violation of natural justice. Matter must be remanded for fresh adjudication with cross-examination right.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter 10 — Principles of Natural Justice or Fairness

⚖️ Chapter 10 — Principles of Natural Justice or Fairness

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Followed
Violated / Void / Quashed
Condition / If / Unless
Exception / Optional Route
Neutral Statutory / Principle
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
10.1 Audi Alteram Partem
10.1.1 Three Basic Essentials
10.1.2 Three Categories of Compliance
10.2 Notice
10.2.1 Service of Notice
10.2.2 Sufficient Time to Reply
10.2.3 Notice Must be Adequate
10.2.4 Form of Notice
10.2.5 Second Show-Cause Notice
10.3 Disclosure of Materials
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
10.1 — Audi Alteram Partem (Right to a Fair Hearing)
⚖️ Audi Alteram Partem
Natural Justice / Fairness — Chapter X Overview
📝 Latin for "hear the other side." The cardinal rule that no person shall be condemned without being given a chance to be heard. It is the first pillar of natural justice — the other being the rule against bias (nemo judex in causa sua).
Two Twin Pillars of Natural Justice
PILLAR 1
Audi Alteram Partem
Right to Fair Hearing
"No one to be condemned unheard"
📝 You must be told what charges are against you AND given a real opportunity to respond before any adverse action.
PILLAR 2
Nemo Judex in Causa Sua
Rule Against Bias
"No one may be judge in his own cause"
📝 The adjudicating authority must be independent and impartial — not personally interested in the outcome.
Nature of the Doctrine — Key Characteristics
NOT CODIFIED
Rules of Natural Justice are NOT codified and NOT unvarying — they are flexible
📝 Unlike CPC or CrPC, there is no written code. Courts decide case by case what fairness requires.
ONE WORD
Summarised in one word:
FAIRNESS
TEST
Question to ask:
"Have the authorities acted fairly?"
SUPPLEMENT
Rules supplement law — do NOT supplant it. Operate only in areas NOT covered by validly made law
FLEXIBLE
Standards vary — can contract into brief post-decisional opportunity OR expand into trial-type proceedings
Expansion from Judicial to Administrative Actions
EARLIER VIEW
Natural Justice applied only to judicial or quasi-judicial proceedings
BINAPANI — AIR 1967
Distinction between quasi-judicial and administrative decisions mitigated
Even administrative orders involving civil consequences must comply
📝 State of Orissa v Dr. Binapani Dei — landmark shift: if an administrative action affects your civil rights (property, livelihood, service), natural justice applies even without a formal tribunal.
CONDITION
Application depends on nature of duty to be performed by authority under statute
(paras 84 & 85 — Competition Commission case)
⚠️ EXCLUSION POSSIBLE
Legislature has competence to enact laws that specifically exclude application of natural justice in larger public interest and for valid reasons. However, courts will read natural justice into unoccupied interstices of statute unless there is a clear mandate to the contrary — per Ratna (AIR 1987 SC 71).
📝 If a law is completely silent on procedure, courts will imply natural justice norms. But if a law explicitly and clearly excludes natural justice, courts cannot override that legislative mandate.
💡 GST Application: Section 75(4) CGST Act mandates opportunity of hearing if requested or if adverse decision proposed. Even where an officer proceeds without request, fairness demands a hearing where tax implications are substantial. A SCN must state specific grounds — a bald repetition of statutory language is inadequate notice.
Flexible Concept — Advantages vs. Dangers
✓ Advantage of Flexible Concept⚠️ Danger of Flexible Concept
Court can modulate hearing to practical needs of each body. Procedure doesn't become a strait-jacket. Absence of minimum procedural norms — no one can predict what is required before any specific body.
Administrative process not unduly hampered. Courts may take indulgent view, natural justice gets denuded of substance — becomes empty formality.
Adapts to wide variety of adjudicatory bodies (from courts to administrative bodies). Law becomes completely unpredictable — no proposition of law can be laid down with certainty.
10.1.1 — Three Basic Essentials of Audi Alteram Partem
🔺 Three Basic Essentials of Audi Alteram Partem
Shukla and Brothers case | (2010) 4 SCC 785
ESSENTIAL 1
Person against whom order is proposed OR whose rights are likely to be adversely affected must be granted an opportunity of being heard
📝 Before any adverse action — you must get a chance to speak. This is not optional; it is mandatory.
💡 GST: Before confirming demand in adjudication order, officer must give personal hearing if requested or if order is adverse — Section 75(4) CGST.
ESSENTIAL 2
Authority concerned should provide a fair and transparent procedure
📝 The process itself must be fair — not just the outcome. Secrecy, hidden materials, pre-decided orders all violate this.
ESSENTIAL 3
Authority must apply its mind and dispose of the matter by a reasoned or speaking order
📝 The order must say WHY the decision was taken. A bare "demand confirmed" with no reasoning is bad in law.
💡 GST: Adjudication order must contain reasons — mechanical confirmation of SCN demand without addressing reply is a non-speaking order liable to be set aside.
10.1.2 — Three Categories of Compliance / Non-Compliance
📊 Three Categories of Compliance with Natural Justice
Competition Commission of India v Steel Authority of India | (2010) 10 SCC 744
📝 Not every breach of natural justice automatically voids an order. The consequence depends on which category the breach falls into. Courts examine facts of each case — no straitjacket formula.
CATEGORY 1 — EXCLUDED
Application of natural justice is excluded by specific legislation
Natural Justice does NOT apply. Legislature has made it inapplicable.
📝 If the statute itself clearly excludes natural justice, courts will give effect to the legislative intent.
CATEGORY 2 — MANDATORY
Law contemplates strict compliance with natural justice; default can result in vitiating not only orders but entire proceedings
Non-compliance = proceedings void ab initio. Even the delinquent cannot be re-proceeded without fresh compliance.
💡 GST: Where Section 75 mandates hearing and no hearing at all was given — entire adjudication is void, not merely the order.
CATEGORY 3 — DIRECTORY
Law requires compliance but an irresistible conclusion of no prejudice is drawn AND non-compliance relates to directory action
Non-compliance may be condoned if no prejudice caused
📝 Even if the procedure was not perfectly followed, if you were not actually prejudiced in defending yourself — the order may stand. But prejudice must be absent — not merely assumed absent.
💡 Minor defect in notice format (e.g., pro forma words not deleted) — not fatal if party was not prejudiced and could still present case.
⚠️ KEY PRINCIPLE — Cases may fall in any of these categories. Court must examine facts of each case in light of the Act/rules. It is not only difficult but also not advisable to spell out any straitjacket formula applicable universally to all cases without variation.
10.2 — Notice — Foundation of Fair Hearing
📢 Notice — The Gateway to Natural Justice
From Latin "notifia" — making something known
📝 Notice is not just a formality — it is the FIRST and most critical step in any adjudicatory process. Without valid notice, no hearing is possible. No hearing = no adjudication. The right to be heard becomes illusory without knowledge of the charges.
FUNDAMENTAL RULE
The starting point of adjudicatory process is notice to the party concerned detailing the case against him and informing him of the action proposed to be taken against him.
Notice is the sine qua non of a fair hearing.
📝 Sine qua non = An indispensable condition without which nothing can exist. Without notice, there is no fair hearing. Period.
IF NO NOTICE
Proceeding against a person without notice = violates natural justice
Condemning without telling = flagrant violation
Action taken without notice = INVALID
WHEN STATUTE PRESCRIBES NOTICE
Requirement is invariably mandatory
Action without such notice = INVALID
💡 Section 11A, Central Excise and Salt Act — mandatory notice before raising demand. Same principle for GST Section 73/74 SCN requirement.
IF NO REPLY TO SCN
Adjudicatory proceedings may proceed ex parte
No violation of natural justice if opportunity was given
📝 Ex parte = Proceeding decided on one side's material only. If you don't reply to SCN, the officer can proceed without you. But if you appear at second hearing, officer MUST hear you.
SUO MOTU APPEARANCE RULE
Notice cannot be dispensed with merely because party appeared suo motu or had imputed knowledge. Requirement of notice (when statutory) goes to jurisdiction of authority. Cannot be circumvented by invoking principles of estoppel or participation.
📝 Suo motu = on one's own initiative. Even if you walk into the officer's room and present your case voluntarily, that does NOT excuse the officer's failure to give you proper statutory notice. The officer's jurisdiction itself depends on valid notice.
💡 GST: Assessee attends summons or inquiry and gives statement. This does NOT replace the mandatory SCN under Section 73/74. Demand cannot be raised without proper SCN just because assessee "participated" in inquiry.
10.2.1 — Service of Notice
📬 Service of Notice — How Notice Must Reach the Party
📝 It is not enough to just issue a notice — it must actually reach the right person through the right method. A notice not properly served is no notice in law.
PRESCRIBED MODE
When statute prescribes mode of service, that prescribed procedure must be followed
Non-compliance with prescribed mode = Notice not served = No proceedings can be initiated
💡 FERA rules prescribed: (i) personal delivery, (ii) registered post to last known address, (iii) affixing on outer door — if registered post returned undelivered, MUST proceed to mode (iii). Failure to do so = notice not served.
NOTICE ON WRONG PERSON
Notice served on wrong person does NOT vest jurisdiction in authority
Even if assessee appeared before authority on date of hearing — estoppel does NOT apply; notice giving jurisdiction must be properly served
📝 If officer sends notice to wrong address but you happen to come, the officer cannot claim jurisdiction was cured by your appearance. The defect of notice goes to the root of jurisdiction.
SOCIETY / COMPANY RULE
Notice to a co-operative society = deemed notice to ALL its members
Member loses individual identity qua the society
📝 When a society is given notice, each member is deemed to have received it — they need not be individually served. But each member has a right to be heard if they so desire.
NOTICE NOT SERVED BY PARTY'S OWN FAULT
If party evades or absconds making service impossible — authority MAY proceed in absence after notice on notice board
📝 Absconding students case — if party itself makes service impossible, authority's proceedings upheld.
VAGUE / INADEQUATE NOTICE = NO NOTICE
A vague and unspecific notice does not provide reasonable opportunity to noticee to file objections. Show-cause notice must mention specific grounds — a notice mentioning only date of hearing and nothing more has been quashed as "not one of inadequacy of notice but a case where no notice at all was given."
10.2.2 — Sufficient Time to Reply
⏰ Sufficient Time to Reply — Opportunity Must Be Real, Not Illusory
📝 Giving notice but not giving enough time to prepare a reply is as bad as giving no notice. The opportunity must be meaningful — not a sham.
✗ INADEQUATE — QUASHED
Return of post to show cause → natural justice violated (Liverpool Taxi Owners)
One day's notice to person out of station → inadequate
3 days notice for demolition of house → inadequate
5 days notice (2 of which Sat-Sun) while authority had 2 months 20 days → inadequate (SC — Income Tax case)
Order passed before expiry of 15-day period given in notice → quashed
✓ VALID — UPHELD
Urgency warrants short notice — valid if enquiry must be held urgently
Explanation received despite insufficient notice + order passed after considering explanation → no violation (order cannot be automatically quashed)
2-day deficiency in 60-day statutory notice for electricity tariff enhancement → not vitiative as no prejudice caused
GENERAL RULE
Authority must NOT take decision BEFORE expiry of the period allowed to the noticee to show cause. Length of notice period depends on nature of interest and issues involved.
💡 GST SCN: Where SCN gives 30 days for reply but officer schedules personal hearing on day 15 and passes order the same day — violation of natural justice. Reply period must be respected.
10.2.3 — A Notice to be Effective Must be Adequate
📋 Adequacy of Notice — Two Mandatory Attributes
📝 A notice that gives you a date but not the charges is meaningless. A notice that repeats only statutory language without specifics is no notice at all. The two non-negotiable attributes: (1) Adequate; (2) Must fully mention ALL grounds on which action is proposed.
ATTRIBUTE 1 — ADEQUATE
Must indicate time, place of hearing + specific case/allegations noticee must defend against
Must NOT be vague
Must give enough information to prepare case
📝 Test: Has the concerned person been prejudiced in presenting his case? If yes — inadequate notice. Court's conscience must be satisfied that party had fair chance to know the case against him.
ATTRIBUTE 2 — ALL GROUNDS
Must fully mention ALL grounds on which action is proposed
Action CANNOT be taken on grounds NOT mentioned in notice
There must be relationship between grounds in notice and grounds for action
📝 You cannot receive a notice for one reason and get punished for another reason. The grounds in the notice bind the authority — additional grounds cannot be added later.
💡 GST: SCN for wrong availment of ITC on goods — order cannot additionally demand GST on services not mentioned in SCN. Such demand = without jurisdiction.
Characteristics of a BAD Notice — Will Be Quashed
Defect in NoticeLegal EffectCase
Bare-bones statutory language — repeats provision without facts/particulars Insufficient and inadequate — no notice in law Nasir Ahmed v Asst. Custodian-General (AIR 1980 SC 1157)
Mentions only one ground but action taken on additional grounds not in notice Action on additional ground is without jurisdiction and void Annamunthodo v Oilfields Workers (1967 AC 945)
Vague allegations of fraud without particulars Party not given opportunity to meet charge — invalid Board of Technical Education UP v Dhanwantari Kumar (AIR 1991 SC 271)
Does not mention date, time, location of incident Suffers from vagueness — invalid State of UP v Mohd. Sharif (AIR 1982 SC 937)
Does not mention source of power under which action proposed Notice invalid per Ambika Devi — must state act complained, action proposed, AND source of power Ambika Devi v State of Bihar (AIR 1988 Pat 258)
Trap charges framed so that any explanation = guilty Not proper — if alternative charges, prefer them in alternative in recognised form Sloan v General Medical Council (1970) 2 All ER 686
Mentions skeletal allegations even where authority need not state formal reasons (import licence abeyance) Valid — minimum skeletal allegations still required to enable representation Liberty Oil Mills v UOI (AIR 1984 SC 1271)
⚠️ 3-PART TEST FOR VALID NOTICE (Ambika Devi)  A valid notice must satisfy: (i) state the act complained of attracting adverse action; (ii) state the action proposed to be taken; (iii) state the source of power under which action is proposed to be taken. Besides: time, place of hearing, and period for show cause.
10.2.4 — Form of Notice
📄 Form of Notice — Prescribed Form vs. Substantial Compliance
GENERAL RULE
When statute prescribes form of notice — prescribed form must be followed
Notice not in accordance with prescribed form = invalid
💡 Section 4(2)(a) Public Premises Act — notice must specify grounds for eviction. Notice without grounds = bad notice — vitiates entire proceedings.
STANDARD PROFORMA
Notice issued in standard proforma where inappropriate words/paragraphs not deleted = implies non-application of mind
📝 If officer uses a template but doesn't delete inapplicable portions, it shows the officer didn't actually think about this specific case. That itself vitiates the notice.
EXCEPTION — MINOR IRREGULARITY
Minor irregularity / technical irregularity may be condoned by Court if:
(i) No substantial irregularity
(ii) No prejudice caused to party
(iii) Purpose of notice (furnishing opportunity) was served
📝 Not every typo or missed deletion kills a notice. Only substantial defects that actually prevented you from defending yourself are fatal.
10.2.5 — Second Show-Cause Notice
📩 Second Show-Cause Notice — Quantum of Punishment
RULE
Issuance of second show-cause notice for the purpose of obtaining the views of the delinquent officer in regard to quantum of punishment is NOT a part of common law principles of natural justice.
📝 Under natural justice alone, you only get one notice and one hearing. A second SCN on quantum is NOT a natural justice entitlement — it must be specifically provided by statute or rules.
EXCEPTION
Such a provision CAN be laid down by reason of a statute. If the statute/rules provide for second show-cause notice before determining quantum, then it becomes mandatory.
💡 GST: Section 75 CGST Act does not expressly provide for a second SCN on quantum after determination of liability. However, where adjudication order is for a drastically enhanced amount beyond SCN demand, principles of fairness may require an opportunity. Check specific facts and nature of enhancement.
10.3 — Disclosure of Materials to the Party
📂 Right to Know Materials Relied Upon — Disclosure Principle
Fundamental requirement of Natural Justice
📝 You cannot fight a case if you don't know what evidence is being used against you. The right to be heard means little unless you also know WHAT the authority is relying on. Materials used against you MUST be disclosed before reliance.
CORE RULE — Supreme Court
"No materials should be relied on against him without his being given opportunity of explaining them."
Adjudicatory body CANNOT base its decision on any material unless the person against whom it is sought to be utilised has been apprised of it and given opportunity to rebut, comment, criticize or explain.
Specific Situations — What Must / Need Not Be Disclosed
SituationRuleEffect if Violated
Expert / Inquiry Report relied upon MUST be disclosed to affected party before reliance (Kesar Enterprises principle) Order vitiated — Kashinath Dikshita v UOI
Departmental representative's information to ITAT MUST be disclosed — Dhakeswari Cotton Mills v CIT (AIR 1955 SC 65) Violation of natural justice if considered without disclosure
State Government's report in revision proceedings (mining lease) MUST be disclosed — party entitled to know what State said and rebut it (Brajlal Manilal v UOI) Order quashed
Confidential enquiries conducted behind back of party Natural justice infringed — Khagendra Nath v Calcutta University Decision vitiated
Senior officer's opinion (income tax assessee) MUST be disclosed — opportunity given to state view against senior's opinion (Mahadayal case) Assessment quashed
Documents not relied upon, not going to be used as evidence Need NOT be disclosed — Chandrama Tiwari v UOI (AIR 1988 SC 117) No violation
Copy of letter with no bearing on charges Need NOT be supplied — no prejudice = no violation of natural justice No violation
Documents relevant but inquiry officer sees them without showing to party May vitiate proceedings — Surinder Singh Kanda v Govt. of Malaya (1962 MLJ 169) Inquiry vitiated — Kanda principle
Gist of documents brought to party's notice even if full copies not given Sufficient if party was not misled — City Corner v PA to Collector (AIR 1976 SC 143) No violation if gist disclosed and summary not misleading
⚠️ SCOPE OF DISCLOSURE  Disclosure is confined to relevant and material documents only (Chandrama Tiwari). No copy need be supplied of: (i) documents with no bearing on charges; (ii) documents not relied upon by enquiry officer; (iii) documents not necessary for cross-examination of witnesses. Decision depends on facts and circumstances of each case.
GST APPLICATION — DISCLOSURE PRINCIPLE
Where adjudicating authority relies on intelligence reports, third-party audit reports, DGGI investigation reports, statements of other persons, or comparison with other assessees' data — all such materials MUST be specifically communicated to the noticee with opportunity to rebut. An order based on undisclosed materials is liable to be set aside as violating principles of natural justice.
💡 DGGI seizes documents from supplier. Uses those documents to raise demand on buyer without disclosing them to buyer. Buyer asks for copies — denied. This is a violation of natural justice. The buyer had no opportunity to controvert or explain the supplier's records used against him.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 10 | Natural Justice & Fairness
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, First Appellate Authority, and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Ridge v Baldwin
House of Lords | 1964 AC 40 : (1963) 2 All ER 66 (HL)
Key Ratio / Principle
The doctrine of natural justice was incapable of exact definition but what a reasonable man would regard as a fair procedure in particular circumstances. Natural justice is not only to secure justice but to prevent miscarriage of justice.
When to Use — When officer claims there is no fixed standard of natural justice and therefore the procedure adopted was acceptable. Counter: the very flexibility of the doctrine demands that the procedure actually followed in the specific case must be what a reasonable man would regard as fair — and the procedure in this case falls short.
Maneka Gandhi v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1978 SC 597 : (1978) 1 SCC 248
Key Ratio / Principle
"The rules of natural justice are not embodied rules. What particular rules of natural justice should apply to a given case must depend to a great extent on the facts and circumstances of that case, the framework of the law under which the enquiry is held and the constitution of the tribunal or body of persons appointed for that purpose. Whenever a complaint is made before a Court that some principle of natural justice has been contravened the Court has to decide whether the observance of that rule was necessary for a just decision on the facts of the case."
When to Use — In all GST hearings where procedural fairness is in question. This ratio establishes that the applicable norms of natural justice must be assessed on the specific facts of THIS case — not by reference to a fixed general standard. Use to argue that the specific infraction in this matter (inadequate notice, no disclosure of materials, no personal hearing) was necessary for a just decision in these facts.
Competition Commission of India v Steel Authority of India Limited
Supreme Court | (2010) 10 SCC 744 | paras 84, 85, 86
Key Ratio — Three Essentials
The doctrine of audi alteram partem has three basic essentials: Firstly, a person against whom an order is required to be passed or whose rights are likely to be affected adversely must be granted an opportunity of being heard. Secondly, the authority concerned should provide a fair and transparent procedure. Lastly, the authority concerned must apply its mind and dispose of the matter by a reasoned or speaking order.
Key Ratio — Flexible but Non-Negotiable
The aim of rules of natural justice is to secure justice or to prevent miscarriage of justice; there is no invariable standard of reasonableness in the matter of hearing and whether in a particular case natural justice has been contravened or not is ultimately for the Courts to decide.
When to Use — To challenge any adjudication order where: (a) no personal hearing was given; (b) order is non-speaking / without reasons; (c) procedure was opaque. Cite all three essentials and demonstrate which one was violated. Also use the "reasoned order" ratio when the impugned order merely reproduces the SCN demand without addressing specific contentions raised in the reply.
Kesar Enterprises Ltd. v State of Uttar Pradesh
Supreme Court | (2011) 13 SCC 733 : AIR 2011 SC 2709 | para 17
Key Ratio / Principle
The principles of natural justice demanded that the show cause notice ought to have been issued and an opportunity of hearing should have been afforded to the person concerned before an order was made, notwithstanding the fact that the said rule does not contain any express provision for the affected party being given an opportunity of being heard. Consequently, the appeal was allowed and the demand of excise duty and/or penalty and interest was quashed.
When to Use — When GST officer argues that the specific provision of the Act/Rules does not expressly provide for a hearing before the order in question. Counter with this ratio: silence of the statute does not exclude natural justice — courts will read it in. Where rule is silent, natural justice mandates notice + hearing before any adverse order.
Ambika Devi v State of Bihar
Patna High Court | AIR 1988 Pat 258
3-Part Test for Valid Notice
A notice to be valid must satisfy at least three requirements: (i) it must state the act complained of attracting adverse action; (ii) it must state the action proposed to be taken; and (iii) it must state the source of power under which the action is proposed to be taken. Besides, it must also prescribe the time and place of hearing and the period within which the show cause may be filed. A notice that mentions only the date of hearing and nothing more is not inadequate notice — it is NO notice at all.
When to Use — To challenge defective SCN in GST proceedings. If the SCN: (a) does not specify the provision under which demand is raised; (b) does not specify the exact act/omission alleged; or (c) does not specify the action proposed — use this 3-part test to demonstrate the SCN fails the basic adequacy standard. A defective SCN is non est — all subsequent proceedings including the adjudication order fall.
Dhakeswari Cotton Mills v Commissioner of Income Tax
Supreme Court | AIR 1955 SC 65 : 1955 (1) SCR 941
Key Ratio — Non-Disclosure of Materials
It is a violation of natural justice when an adjudicatory body takes into consideration, but does not disclose to the assessee, information supplied against him. No materials should be relied on against a person without his being given opportunity of explaining them. If without disclosing any evidence to the party, the authority decides the matter against the party, the decision is vitiated for it amounts to denial of a real and effective opportunity to meet the case against him.
When to Use — When GST adjudicating authority uses intelligence inputs, DGGI reports, third-party statements, or comparable assessees' data without disclosing the same to the noticee. Use to demand disclosure and, if refused, to challenge the final order for violating this fundamental principle. Any order based on undisclosed adverse material is liable to be quashed.
Gokak Patel Volkart Ltd. v Collector, Central Excise
Supreme Court | AIR 1987 SC 1161 : (1987) 2 SCC 93
Key Ratio — Notice Must Precede Assessment
A demand for excise duty was quashed because the same had been assessed without giving a notice to the assessee. Proceedings against a person without notice to him violates natural justice. Notice is the sine qua non of a fair hearing.
When to Use — In GST adjudication proceedings where the demand amount in the order significantly differs from (or exceeds) the demand in the SCN without any separate notice for the enhanced amount. The excess demand beyond SCN = demand without notice = violates natural justice = liable to be quashed to that extent.
Kashinath Dikshita v Union of India
Supreme Court | AIR 1986 SC 2118 : 1986 (2) LLJ 468
Key Ratio — Right to Copies of Documents
A government servant facing disciplinary proceedings is entitled to a reasonable opportunity of being heard to meet the charges against him in an effective manner and unless copies of the relevant statements and documents to be used against him were made available to him, he could not prepare his defence, cross-examine witnesses and point out the inconsistencies in the allegations. Refusal to furnish copies of documents relied upon is fatal to the validity of the hearing proceedings.
When to Use — When assessee requests copies of: (a) statements of third parties relied upon in SCN; (b) intelligence reports forming basis of demand; (c) audit reports / reconciliation statements used by department. Use this ratio to argue that denial of copies of relied-upon documents effectively denies meaningful opportunity of defence.
Ratna — Institute of Chartered Accountants of India v L.K. Ratna
Supreme Court | AIR 1987 SC 71 | (1987) 164 ITR 1
Key Ratio — Natural Justice to be Read Into Statute
The principles of natural justice must be read into the unoccupied interstices of the statute unless there is a clear mandate to the contrary. No statute can possibly lay down each and every procedural detail involved in a hearing.
When to Use — To counter the officer's argument that "the Act does not provide for this procedural right." Response: Natural justice fills the gaps in the statute. Where the GST Act is silent on whether material relied upon must be disclosed, or whether a personal hearing must be given before enhancing demand beyond SCN — natural justice mandates compliance. No clear contrary mandate in GST law excludes these rights.
Olga Tellis v Bombay Municipal Corporation
Supreme Court | AIR 1986 SC 180 : (1985) 3 SCC 545
Key Ratio — Natural Justice Implied Even Without Express Provision
Natural justice is implied in the law even if the concerned persons have no effective answer to give. Justice should not only be done, it must manifestly be seen to have been done. A departure from the fundamental rules of natural justice could be presumed to be intended by the legislature only in circumstances which warranted the same and such circumstances must be shown to exist by those who assert their existence.
When to Use — When officer argues that hearing/notice was not given because "assessee has no answer anyway" or "the case is clear." Counter: Natural justice is implied even where person may have no effective answer. The burden is on the authority to show that a departure from natural justice was warranted — mere clarity of the case is not enough. The procedure must be seen to be fair.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter 10 — Duty to Give Reasons (Natural Justice / Fairness)

⚖️ Chapter 10 — Duty to Give Reasons (Natural Justice / Fairness)

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Right
Prohibited / Void / Bad in Law
Condition / Exception / If
Discretionary / Alternative
Neutral Reference / Principle
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
10.10 General Rule — No Common-Law Duty & Its Erosion

10.10 — General Rule: No Common-Law Duty & Its Erosion (England)
⚖️ Duty to Give Reasons — Natural Justice / Fairness
Principles of Administrative Law | Chapter X (§§ 10.10–10.12)
England — Starting Position: No General Common-Law Duty to Give Reasons
📝 In English law, adjudicating bodies were not automatically required to explain their decisions. This was criticised as a big gap in legal protection — if you don't know why you lost, how do you appeal?
EROSION Courts progressively engrafted exceptions on the general rule — expanded duty case by case on 3 grounds:
GROUND 1
Right of Appeal exists → reasons must be given so that the right is not frustrated
📝 If you have a right to appeal, you need the reasons to know how to challenge the decision. Without reasons, your appeal right is hollow.
GROUND 2
Legitimate Expectation that reasons would be given → obligation arises
📝 If past practice or representation created an expectation that reasons would follow, the body is bound to give them.
GROUND 3
Fairness / Natural Justice demands reasons — Lord Donaldson in Cunningham: fairness requires tribunal to give sufficient reasons so parties know what issues were addressed and that it acted lawfully
TREND Present-day judicial strategy: decide case-by-case whether specific body in specific circumstances must give reasons. Hope expressed that exceptions would eventually eat away the general rule — exceptions would become the rule and the rule an exception.
10.10 — India: Constitutional Basis for Duty to Give Reasons
🇮🇳 India — Stronger Duty to Give Reasons
Constitutional provisions + Natural Justice + Supreme Court creativity
KEY ACHIEVEMENT Indian Courts developed the idea that natural justice itself demands that adjudicatory bodies give reasons for their decisions
📝 Unlike England where it was left to Parliament, Indian courts used the Constitution (judicial review provisions) and natural justice to impose a general duty to give reasons on all adjudicating bodies.
BASIS 1
Natural Justice itself demands reasoned decisions
BASIS 2
Constitutional provisions guaranteeing judicial control → body must render reasoned decisions so judicial review is effective & meaningful
📝 If adjudicating bodies don't give reasons, the courts cannot properly check if they acted correctly. Judicial review becomes an empty exercise.
BASIS 3
Failure by lower authority → higher authority cannot effectively review → person loses right to seek review even with statutory right of appeal (SC in S.N. Mukherjee)
FIRMLY ESTABLISHED Proposition now firmly established: An adjudicatory body must give reasons for its decisions. Reiterated by Supreme Court and High Courts in numerous cases.
💡 In GST adjudication: A GST officer who passes an order confirming demand without reasoning why the assessee's submissions were rejected acts in violation of this fundamental principle — such order is vulnerable to challenge on non-speaking order ground.
10.10 — Statutory Duty vs Judge-Made Duty to Give Reasons
📜 Statutory Duty vs Judge-Made Duty — Two Sources
STATUTORY DUTY — Statute expressly requires reasons
Provision treated as mandatory
Failure to give reasons → fatal to the action taken
Statutory duty enforceable by writ of mandamus
📝 Mandamus = a court order commanding a public body to do something it is legally required to do. If the law says "record reasons" and they don't, court can force them to do it.
Recording reasons ensures authority applies its mind and that reasons are germane to scope of power — If reasons totally irrelevant → exercise of power becomes void
KEY RULE
Reasons stated in show-cause notice do NOT absolve authority from giving reasons in the final order
📝 Even if the SCN had reasons, the final adjudication order must also contain its own reasons. SCN reasons cannot be borrowed to justify the final order.
💡 GST: If SCN lists grounds but adjudication order says "confirmed as per SCN" — this is a non-speaking order. Challenge maintainable — officer must give reasons for rejecting assessee's reply in the order itself.
JUDGE-MADE DUTY — No express statutory provision
Supreme Court spelled out general obligation for adjudicatory bodies to give reasons — partly from Natural Justice + partly from Constitutional provisions for judicial review
Uma Charan v State of MP: Selection Committee omitted to record reasons for supersession as required by regulation → decision quashed
Maneka Gandhi v UOI: Authority impounded passport, refused to give reasons — SC held: giving of reasons is a healthy check against abuse/misuse of power
📝 Maneka Gandhi is the high watermark — the SC insisted that adjudicatory authorities must give reasons. Even if reasons are sensitive, authority must satisfy the court that disclosure would be "clearly and indubitably" against public interest.
Siemens Engg v UOI (SC): Rule requiring reasons in support of an order is a basic principle of natural justice which must inform every quasi-judicial process
Case Court / Year Principle Laid Down Outcome
Uma Charan v State of MP Supreme Court | AIR 1981 SC 1915 Regulation required reasons for supersession — omission = infringement of mandatory duty Selection Committee decision quashed
Maneka Gandhi v UOI Supreme Court | AIR 1978 SC 597 Giving reasons = healthy check against abuse of power; court not bound to accept claim of public interest without material High watermark of judicial insistence on reasons
Siemens Engg v UOI Supreme Court | AIR 1976 SC 1785 Rule requiring reasons = basic principle of natural justice informing every quasi-judicial process Customs duty adjudication without reasons — quashed at all 3 levels
Anil Kumar v Presiding Officer Supreme Court | AIR 1985 SC 1121 In disciplinary enquiry affecting livelihood — minimum expectation is reasoned report; non-application of mind = non-speaking = unsustainable Order of termination quashed
ICAI Council (Ratna case) Supreme Court | AIR 1987 SC 72 ICAI obligated to give reasons for finding member guilty; member entitled to know basis to exercise right of appeal to High Court Applies to all professional regulatory bodies
10.10.1 — Validity of a Non-Speaking Order
📄 Non-Speaking Order — Validity & Judicial Response
📝 A "non-speaking order" is an order that gives no reasons — it just announces the conclusion. Like a judgment that says "dismissed" without saying why. Courts usually quash such orders.
ORDINARY RULE
Court should declare non-speaking order null and void — error of law apparent on face of record → Madhusudan (non-speaking order cancelling examination of candidate quashed)
DILUTION (Courts sometimes apply)
Courts exercise discretion — do not always quash; especially in civil servant discipline cases judges make compromises to avoid declaring non-speaking orders invalid
Tripathi: Reasons implicit in order — "apparent from fair reading of order" → dismissal sustained even without express reasons
Woolcombers: SC admitted absence of reasons is serious flaw but upheld industrial tribunal award because "evidence on record supported conclusion" — went through entire evidence itself
📝 This dilution approach is criticised — it undermines the salutary principle that adjudicatory bodies must give reasons.
CORRECT PRINCIPLE (SC — Consumer Action Group)
Even if statute does not provide for recording of reasons, it is obligatory on the authority to do so — obligation is implicit
When wide power given to statutory authority including delegatee → obligatory to clearly record reasons in order itself → reveals application of mind
💡 GST: Adjudicating officer acting as delegate of Commissioner → must record reasons in order itself even if CGST Act only requires "reasons to believe" at SCN stage.
10.10.2 — Communication of Reasons
📬 Communication of Reasons — Must Reach the Affected Party
📝 It is not enough to write the reasons in the file. The person affected must actually receive the reasons — otherwise they cannot challenge the order. Reasons locked in a file are useless to the person against whom the order was passed.
GENERAL RULE
Reasons must be communicated to the affected person — Ajantha Industries v CBDT (SC): order bad on account of non-communication; not saved by showing reasons existed in file
EXCEPTIONS (Courts did not quash)
Some cases: orders upheld on ground that reasons existed in file even though not communicated — this approach is criticised as not good law and contrary to Ajantha
📝 These cases force the affected party to file a writ petition just to find out why the order was passed — which defeats the purpose of the duty to give reasons.
Art 311(2)(b)
Dismissal without enquiry → reasons must be recorded by disciplinary authority — communication to civil servant: not obligatory but desirable (avoids allegation of concoction)
BISHWANATH BHATTACHARYA EXCEPTION (SC 2014)
Where reasons recorded (even without express statutory provision) but not communicated initially, yet subsequently made available to the party to meet the same AND there is further provision of appeal → non-communication of recorded reasons cannot be held breach of natural justice — provided the party could effectively meet the case
📝 This is a narrow exception: the party must have actually received the reasons at some stage before the final order and had opportunity to rebut them. It does not permit permanently hiding reasons in a file.
IDEAL RULE (as per text)
Supreme Court should lay down unequivocally: giving of reasons AND communication to concerned person is the norm; a non-speaking order is invalid irrespective of the fact that reasons exist in the file.
10.10.3 — Exceptions to the Duty to Give Reasons
🚫 Exceptions — When Duty to Give Reasons Does NOT Apply
📝 Even the strong duty to give reasons has some narrow exceptions. These arise mainly where the statute itself excludes it, or the subject-matter makes it impractical or dangerous.
EX 1 — Excluded by Statute
Requirement to give reasons may be excluded directly by law or by necessary implication arising from nature of subject-matter, scheme and provisions of enactment
EX 2 — Externment Order
State of Maharashtra v Salem Hasan Khan: State Govt need not give reasons for rejecting appeal from externment order — if evidence discussed, easy to fix identity of witnesses who could then be harassed
EX 3 — Court Martial
