Court-martial not required to record reasons at stage of recording findings & sentence; reasons only required for recommendation to mercy
EX 4 — Selection Committees
R.S. Dass v UOI (debatable): Selection committee need not give reasons for including/excluding officer from select list — function is "administrative" not "quasi-judicial"
UPSC v Hiranyalal Dev: reiterated
📝 This ruling is criticised — supersession demoralises senior officers and the committee should arguably give reasons. The Court was swayed by change in law deleting the reasons requirement from the rules.
EX 5 — Preventive Detention
Order confirming preventive detention for maximum permissible period need not be a reasoned one
EX 6 — Administrative Decision on Urgency
Decision on urgency under a statute = administrative order on basis of subjective satisfaction → no need to pass reasoned order to dispense with enquiry (Central Govt extending Delhi Police jurisdiction)
DISCORDANT NOTE — Som Datt Datta v UOI (Constitution Bench)
"Apart from any requirement imposed by statute, there is no legal obligation that the statutory tribunal should give reasons for its decision. There is also no general principle or any rule of natural justice that a statutory tribunal should always and in every case give reasons in support of its decision."
Note: This sweeping statement cannot prevail — obligation of adjudicatory bodies to give reasons is now well established (S.N. Mukherjee v UOI)
📝 Som Datt Datta was in the context of the Army Act and tried to import English common law into India. It has been effectively overruled by subsequent cases and should not be relied upon in GST or general administrative proceedings.
10.10.4 — Adequacy of Reasons
📏 Adequacy of Reasons — What is Good Enough?
📝 Reasons don't have to be as elaborate as a court judgment, but they must be real, relevant and clear enough to show that the decision-maker actually thought about the problem. Rubber-stamp language and copied words from the statute are not "reasons".
STANDARD — S.N. Mukherjee v UOI (SC)
"It is not required that the reasons should be as elaborate as in a Court of law. The extent and nature of the reasons would depend on particular facts and circumstances. What is necessary is that the reasons are clear and explicit so as to indicate that the authority has given due consideration to the points in controversy."
Tests for Adequacy — Reasons Must:
Be adequate, proper & intelligible
Reasonably deal with substantial points raised in the matter
Enable parties and courts to see what matters were taken into consideration & what view was taken on points of fact and law
Disclose rational nexus between facts considered and conclusions reached
Reveal how mind of deciding authority was applied to the disputed subject-matter — speaking order speaks of the mind of the authority
What is NOT Adequate — Reasons That FAIL the Test:
✗ Mechanical & stereotype reasons
✗ Mere repetition of statutory language — e.g. just quoting the section number without saying why it applies
💡 GST: Order saying "supply taxable under Section 9 of CGST Act" without addressing why assessee's objections are wrong = not a reasoned order.
✗ "Reply found unsatisfactory" without stating why — not adequate (Cycle Equipments v Delhi Municipality)
✗ Bald conclusion without reasoning — "the entire examination is cancelled" doesn't say WHY (UOI v M.L. Kapoor)
✗ Refusing trademark on ground of "interests of general public and development of indigenous industry" without explaining HOW — merely recited statutory words (Oramco Chemicals)
✗ Disproportionate punishment with mere expression "shockingly disproportionate" — must record specific reasons for coming to that conclusion
💡 GST: Penalty order under Sec 122/74 must not merely say "penalty imposed for wilful suppression" — must record why reply was rejected and how penalty quantum was determined.
AFFIRMATION ORDER Order of affirmation need not be as elaborate as order of reversal, but must contain some reasons — at least in brief — so one can know whether the appellate authority applied its mind. "Whether there was application of mind or not can only be disclosed by some reasons, at least in brief, mentioned in the order."
10.10.5 — Record of Hearing
📋 Record of Hearing — Why It Must Be Preserved
📝 A reasoned order is only as good as the record of what happened during the hearing. If the adjudicating body doesn't maintain a record of evidence, the appeal to a higher body becomes a hollow formality — nobody can check what actually happened.
PRINCIPLE
Nibaran Chandra v Mahendra Nath (SC): Record of evidence is as necessary as a reasoned order — without materials placed before appellate authority, appeal is an "empty formality"
Authority not bound to follow civil court procedure for recording evidence — but must maintain some record from which appellate authority can gather what materials were before the officer when making decision
CALCUTTA HC
East Jamuria Co. v Collector of Customs: Administrative adjudicatory authorities must record evidence produced by parties in examination and cross-examination
CAVEAT In East Jamuria, court did not quash the order even though evidence not recorded — found no miscarriage of justice occurred in that particular case.
💡 GST: During personal hearing before Adjudicating Officer, assessee should ensure a panchnama / minutes of personal hearing are drawn up and signed. If key submissions are not recorded, they may be treated as not made at all — and the appellate authority has no record to examine.
10.10.6 — Reasons by Appellate Authorities
🔼 Appellate Authorities — Each Level Must Give Reasons
📝 When a case moves from lower to higher authority (like from GST Officer → Commissioner (Appeals) → GSTAT), each body must give its own reasons. You cannot assume that silence means agreement with the lower order.
SCENARIO 1: Lower authority gives NO reasons + Appellate merely affirms
Order of appellate authority is bad — Bhagat Raja v UOI: "Court has to grope in the dark for finding reasons for upholding or rejecting the decision of the reviewing authority"
SCENARIO 2: Lower gives reasons + Appellate affirms without own reasons
Travancore Rayons v UOI: Appellate body must give its own reasons even when affirming reasoned decision of lower body — lower order may contain several reasons, some good some bad; appellate body must at least indicate it accepts the lower reasons
Siemens: Reiterated Travancore Rayons — each authority in hierarchical adjudicatory order (customs duty: Asst. Collector → Collector → Central Govt) must make a speaking order
📝 A higher body may agree with the conclusions of the lower body but for different reasons. So it must give its own reasons even when affirming — otherwise the SC's appellate function becomes futile.
💡 GST: Commissioner (Appeals) cannot simply say "I agree with the adjudicating authority" without addressing the specific grounds of appeal. Such order is non-speaking and liable to be set aside by GSTAT/HC.
SCENARIO 3: Appellate DISAGREES / REVERSES
State of WB v Krishna Shaw: When appellate authority disagrees with reasons and findings of primary authority — it must assign its own reasons for why it disagrees. "Unless adequate reasons are given, merely because it is an appellate authority, it cannot brush aside the reasoning or findings recorded by the primary authority."
SCENARIO 4: Appellate REVERSES order of lower authority
CIT v Walchand & Co.: Appellate authority must always record reasons when reversing the order of lower authority — whether or not lower authority gave reasons
BEST RULE (recommended by text) Simplest and most effective rule: Every body — appellate or original — must give its own reasons for its decision, irrespective of any consideration, unless there is some specific reason (security / public interest) for not doing so. Giving reasons is not just a formal requirement — it ensures the adjudicatory body applies its own mind.
10.10.7 — Disciplinary Proceedings Against Public Employees — Special Rules
👔 Disciplinary Proceedings — Exception to General Rule on Appellate Reasons
📝 For disciplinary actions against government servants, the Supreme Court carved out a relaxed rule for appellate bodies. But the disciplinary authority itself must still give proper reasons — and this exception is increasingly criticised.
TARA CHAND RULE (Tara Chand v Delhi Municipality | AIR 1977 SC)
Disciplinary authority agrees with findings of enquiry officer → need NOT record own reasons
Appellate authority agrees with decision of lower authority which gave reasoned decision → need NOT give own reasons
BUT: If disciplinary authority DIFFERS from enquiry officer's findings → must record own reasons for disagreement
If appellate body does NOT accept findings of lower body in favour of delinquent and proposes to impose penalty → must give reasons for differing (though need not be detailed/elaborate)
STATUTORY RULE OVERRIDES — Ram Chander v UOI (AIR 1986 SC)
Railway employee's appeal to Railway Board → Board issued non-speaking order merely reproducing statutory language → SC quashed — statutory rule required Board to consider specified matters and record findings thereon
Appellate body like Railway Board must: (1) not only hear appellant but also (2) pass reasoned order dealing with contentions raised in appeal — promotes public confidence in administrative process
📝 The Tara Chand relaxation applies only where no mandatory statutory rule imposes the reasons requirement on the appellate body. Where statute requires it, the body must comply.
CRITICISM OF TARA CHAND
No justification in distinguishing disciplinary proceedings from other adjudicatory proceedings for purpose of giving reasons by appellate bodies
Whole career of person may be blasted by disciplinary action — requiring the disciplinary authority to make a reasoned order is the only way to ensure it actually applied its mind and did not mechanically agree with enquiry officer
Where no statutory provision, a disciplinary appellate body "may well get away by giving a non-reasoned decision just by agreeing" — this is unjustifiable
ANIL KUMAR RATIO (SC | AIR 1985 SC 1121)
"Where a disciplinary enquiry affects the livelihood and is likely to cast a stigma and it has to be held in accordance with the principles of natural justice, the minimum expectation is that the report must be a reasoned one... where the evidence is annexed to an order-sheet and no correlation is established between the two showing application of mind, it is not an enquiry report at all."
💡 GST: In penalty proceedings under Section 122 CGST Act, the adjudicating officer acts in a quasi-disciplinary role — the order must correlate the evidence with the conclusions. An order that merely lists the alleged suppression and imposes maximum penalty without addressing the reply is non-speaking and unsustainable.
10.10.8 — Purpose of Giving Reasons
🎯 Why Reasons Matter — Dual Purpose (Nandlal Tejmal Kothari v Inspecting Asst. CIT)
PURPOSE 1
Party aggrieved acquires knowledge of reasons → can demonstrate before HC/SC that reasons which persuaded the authority were erroneous, irrational or irrelevant
📝 Without knowing the reasons, you don't know what to challenge in appeal. Reasons are your roadmap to the appellate forum.
PURPOSE 2
Obligation to record & convey reasons operates as a deterrent against possible arbitrary action by quasi-judicial or executive authority invested with judicial powers
📝 When officials know they have to explain their decisions in writing, they are less likely to act arbitrarily or for extraneous reasons. Reasons discipline the decision-maker.
SC IN S.N. MUKHERJEE — Key Ratios on Purpose
"A right to reason is an indispensable part of sound system of judicial review. Reasoned decision is not only for showing that the citizen is receiving justice, but also a valid discipline for the Tribunal itself. Therefore, statement of reasons is one of the essentials of justice."

"Reasons are live links between the mind of the decision-taker to the controversy in question and the decision or conclusion arrived at. Reasons substitute subjectivity by objectivity."

"If the decision reveals the 'inscrutable face of the sphinx', it can, by its silence, render it virtually impossible for the Courts to perform their appellate function or exercise the power of judicial review."
📝 "Inscrutable face of the sphinx" = a silent, unexplained decision that gives no clue as to what was decided or why. Such decisions are fundamentally incompatible with rule of law and judicial review.
LORD DENNING in Breen v Amalgamated Engineering Union
"The giving of reasons is one of the fundamentals of good administration."

Alexander Machinery (Dudley) Ltd. v Crabtree
"Failure to give reasons amounts to denial of justice."
CONSUMER PROTECTION Obligation to give reasons not only introduces clarity but also excludes, or at any rate minimises, chances of arbitrariness — and the higher forum can test correctness of those reasons.
💡 GST: Every SCN reply filed by assessee must be carefully recorded in the adjudication order. If the order is silent on any ground raised in reply, it is a non-speaking order on that ground — each ground of reply must be met with a reason for acceptance or rejection.
10.11 — Fairness & Transparency in Public Administration
🌐 Fairness & Transparency — Expanding Horizon of Natural Justice
📝 Natural justice is not a closed list of 2 rules. Courts can develop new principles as situations demand. Fairness and transparency are now recognized as components of natural justice under Article 14 of the Constitution.
Dev Dutta v UOI (SC 2008) — ACR Entries Must be Communicated
All ACR entries (whether poor, fair, average, good or very good) in annual confidential report of public servant must be communicated within reasonable period — so employee can make representation for upgradation
Principle of non-arbitrariness under Article 14 requires communication — overrides any rule/G.O. prohibiting it
Representation must be decided by authority higher than the one who gave the entry — otherwise becomes "appeal from Caesar to Caesar"
📝 This shows how Article 14 (equality and non-arbitrariness) works together with natural justice to expand procedural rights of affected persons.
T.S.R. Subramanian v UOI (SC) — Civil Servants Must Record Oral Instructions
Civil servants cannot function on basis of verbal/oral instructions — must also be protected against wrongful & arbitrary pressure from administrative superiors, political executive, business & other vested interests
Oral directions if not recorded → rights under RTI Act defeated; creates room for favouritism & corruption
📝 Recording instructions is necessary for fixing responsibility and accountability in functioning of civil servants. RTI Act (2005) recognises right of citizens to such information.
City Industrial v Platinum Entertainment (SC 2015) — Government Land Allotment
State and its agencies cannot give largesse at sweet will of political entities or officers — decisions must be founded on sound, transparent and well-defined policy made known to the public
Discriminatory and arbitrary method of disposal of government land → always be avoided; allotment based on favouritism/nepotism influences exercise of discretion
Pick and choose method while allotting government land = unconstitutional; authorities cannot adopt this approach
DELAYED REVISION — Joint Collector Ranga Reddy District v D. Narsingharao (SC 2015)
Unexplained and inordinate delay (50 years) in invocation of power of revision = tantamount to fraud upon statute, arbitrary and opposed to rule of law. "Even when there is no period of limitation, power must be exercised within a reasonable period." Intervening delay may have created third-party rights that cannot be trampled by belated exercise of discretionary power.
📝 This principle is directly applicable in GST: If department seeks to reopen proceedings after an unreasonable delay (even if within the technical limitation period of 5/3 years), the delay and its effect on the assessee can be raised as a ground of natural justice violation.
10.12 — One Who Decides Must Hear
👂 One Who Decides Must Hear — Natural Justice Maxim
📝 This principle says the same person who hears the case must decide it. If one officer hears the arguments and another (who was not present) passes the order — the person effectively got no real hearing. Personal hearing becomes an empty ceremony.
Gullapalli Nageswara Rao v APSRTC (SC | AIR 1959 SC 308) — Landmark Case
FACTS
Bus nationalisation scheme — hearing conducted by Secretary (Transport Dept) but decision made by Chief Minister under Rules of Business
HELD
"This divided responsibility is destructive of the concept of judicial hearing" — order quashed
📝 "Personal hearing enables the authority to watch the demeanour of witnesses and clear up doubts during the course of the arguments." If one hears and another decides — personal hearing becomes an empty formality.
CRITICISM
Unrealistic to expect Chief Minister to personally hear all objectors — may force government into dilemma of either delegating power to subordinate or seriously interfering with CM's other duties
Settled Principles After Gullapalli
SETTLED
Decision-making function cannot be delegated to another official unless adjudicatory body is authorised to do so — expressly or impliedly — by relevant statute
PERMITTED
Function of enquiry CAN be assigned to another official (Pradyat Kumar)
REAL REQUIREMENT
What is required: administrative agency making findings must address itself to the evidence; must conscientiously reach own conclusion — decision is bad if decision-making functionary applied no mind to evidence (Kerala HC in Raghava Menon)
Precautions When Hearing and Deciding Are Bifurcated
(1) Hearing officer must make a report giving own impressions of witnesses & assessment of evidence + recommend a decision
(2) Decision-maker must take this report into consideration before deciding
(3) Decision-maker must base decision on entire record of hearing — apply mind to materials collected at inquiry and reach own conclusions — NOT merely mechanically accept report of hearing officer
💡 GST: Personal hearing conducted by Inspector/Superintendent but order passed by Asstt. Commissioner (without applying mind) = violation of this principle. Order passed by authority who did not conduct hearing must show application of mind to submissions made.
AUTOMOTIVE TYRE MANUFACTURERS ASSOCIATION v DESIGNATED AUTHORITY (SC 2011)
Final order of Designated Authority passed by successor who had no occasion to hear the appellants — offends basic principle of natural justice — "divided responsibility is destructive of the concept of judicial hearing."
📝 This is particularly relevant in GST adjudication: if the officer who conducted the personal hearing is transferred and a new officer passes the order without reviewing the hearing record and without independently applying mind — the order is vulnerable to challenge.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 10 | Duty to Give Reasons & Natural Justice
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, First Appellate Authority (FAA), and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Siemens Engineering & Mfg. Co. v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1976 SC 1785 | (1976) 2 SCC 981
Key Ratio
"The rule requiring reasons to be given in support of an order is a basic principle of natural justice which must inform every quasi-judicial process. Adjudicatory authorities should accord fair and proper hearing to persons sought to be affected by their orders and give sufficiently clear and explicit reasons in support of their orders. That way, adjudicatory bodies will carry credibility with the people by inspiring confidence in the adjudicatory process."
When to Use — When challenging a GST order that gives no reasons or merely reproduces the show-cause notice. Cite to establish that even in customs/excise/GST adjudication, each tier of hierarchy must pass a speaking order with clear, explicit reasons.
S.N. Mukherjee v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1990 SC 1984 | (1990) 4 SCC 594
Key Ratio on Right to Reasons
"A right to reason is, therefore, an indispensable part of sound system of judicial review. Reasoned decision is not only for the purpose of showing that the citizen is receiving justice, but also a valid discipline for the Tribunal itself. Therefore, statement of reasons is one of the essentials of justice. The requirement that reasons be recorded should govern the decisions of an administrative authority exercising quasi-judicial functions irrespective of the fact whether the decision is subject to appeal, revision or judicial review."
Key Ratio on Adequacy
"It is not required that the reasons should be as elaborate as in a Court of law. The extent and nature of the reasons would depend on particular facts and circumstances. What is necessary is that the reasons are clear and explicit so as to indicate that the authority has given due consideration to the points in controversy."
When to Use — When GST adjudicating authority or appellate authority argues that since no statutory rule expressly requires detailed reasons, they are not obligated to give them. Cite to demolish this argument — quasi-judicial function itself triggers the obligation regardless of whether decision is appealable.
Travancore Rayons v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1971 SC 862 | (1969) 3 SCC 868
Key Ratio — Appellate Authorities Must Give Own Reasons
Where a non-judicial authority exercises judicial functions, it is obviously necessary to give sufficient reasons which disclose proper appreciation of the problem to be solved, and the mental process by which the conclusion is reached. The Court would require to be satisfied that the decision had been reached after due consideration of the merits of the dispute, uninfluenced by extraneous considerations of policy or expediency. A higher body may agree with the conclusions of the lower body but may not necessarily agree with all its reasons. The reasons for the decision for the appellate body may be different from those of the lower body. The appellate body should at least indicate clearly that it was accepting the reasons given by the lower authority.
When to Use — When Commissioner (Appeals) order merely says "I confirm the order of adjudicating authority" without addressing your specific grounds of appeal. Cite to argue the appellate order is non-speaking and must be set aside for fresh disposal with proper reasons.
Maneka Gandhi v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1978 SC 597 | (1978) 1 SCC 248
Key Ratio — Reasons as Check Against Misuse of Power
Giving of reasons is a healthy check against abuse or misuse of power. The Court would be very slow in accepting the claim of the authority that it would not be in public interest to disclose the reasons. The authority would have to satisfy the Court by placing proper material before it that the giving of reasons would be "clearly and indubitably" against the interest of the general public. If the Court is not so satisfied it would require the authority to disclose the reasons.
When to Use — When GST officer refuses to explain reasons for adverse findings, claiming the order speaks for itself or that reasons need not be communicated. Cite to insist that reasons must be stated and communicated — only in rare public interest cases can this be withheld, and the authority must justify non-disclosure.
Anil Kumar v Presiding Officer
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1985 SC 1121 | (1985) 3 SCC 378
Key Ratio — Non-Application of Mind = Non-Speaking = Unsustainable
"Where a disciplinary enquiry affects the livelihood and is likely to cast a stigma and it has to be held in accordance with the principles of natural justice, the minimum expectation is that the report must be a reasoned one. But where the evidence is annexed to an order-sheet and no correlation is established between the two showing application of mind, it is not an enquiry report at all. Therefore, there was no enquiry in this case worth the name and the order of termination based on such proceedings disclosing non-application of mind would be unsustainable."
When to Use — When GST order lists out alleged discrepancies and evidence but makes no attempt to correlate specific evidence with specific conclusions. Cite to argue that no correlation between evidence and findings = non-application of mind = non-speaking order = unsustainable.
Alexander Machinery (Dudley) Ltd. v Crabtree
NIRC (UK) | 1974 ICR 120 | Cited with approval by Supreme Court of India
Key Ratio — Failure to Reason = Denial of Justice
"Failure to give reasons amounts to denial of justice. Reasons are live links between the mind of the decision-taker to the controversy in question and the decision or conclusion arrived at. Reasons substitute subjectivity by objectivity. If the decision reveals the 'inscrutable face of the sphinx', it can, by its silence, render it virtually impossible for the Courts to perform their appellate function or exercise the power of judicial review in adjudging the validity of the decision."
When to Use — When arguing before GSTAT or High Court that the adjudicating authority or appellate authority failed to give reasons. Cite "inscrutable face of the sphinx" to vivid effect — an order that gives no explanation is incompatible with rule of law and renders judicial review impossible.
Nandlal Tejmal Kothari v Inspecting Assistant Commissioner of Income Tax
Supreme Court of India | JT 1997 (4) SC 621
Key Ratio — Two-fold Purpose of Reasons
Recording of reasons which lead to the passing of an order serves a two-fold purpose: (1) The "party aggrieved" in the proceeding acquires knowledge of the reasons and has an opportunity to demonstrate that the reasons which persuaded the authority to pass an order adverse to his interest were erroneous, irrational or irrelevant; and (2) The obligation to record reasons and convey the same to the party concerned operates as a deterrent against possible arbitrary action by the quasi-judicial or the executive authority invested with judicial powers.
When to Use — Before GST Officer or First Appellate Authority at the stage of arguing why the impugned order must be set aside — the two-fold purpose directly explains why a non-reasoned GST order prejudices both the taxpayer (cannot know why he lost) and the public interest (arbitrary orders cannot be checked).
Gullapalli Nageswara Rao v APSRTC
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1959 SC 308 | 1959 Supp (1) SCR 319
Key Ratio — One Who Decides Must Hear
"This divided responsibility is destructive of the concept of judicial hearing. Personal hearing enables the authority concerned to watch the demeanour of the witnesses and clear up his doubts during the course of the arguments, and the party appearing to persuade the authority by reasoned argument to accept his point of view. If one person hears and another decides, then personal hearing becomes an empty formality."
When to Use — When the GST officer who conducted personal hearing is different from the officer who signed the adjudication order, and the signing officer has not indicated any independent application of mind to the submissions made in personal hearing. Cite to challenge the order as violating the basic requirement that the deciding authority must itself hear the case.
State of West Bengal v Krishna Shaw
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1990 SC 2205 | (1991) Supp (1) SCC 414
Key Ratio — Appellate Authority Cannot Brush Aside Lower Authority's Findings Without Reasons
"When the appellate authority disagrees with the reasons and findings of the primary authority, it must assign its own reasons as to why it disagrees. Unless adequate reasons are given, merely because it is an appellate authority, it cannot brush aside the reasoning or findings recorded by the primary authority."
When to Use — When Commissioner (Appeals) reverses a finding in assessee's favour (made by adjudicating authority) without assigning any reason for disagreement, or when GSTAT reverses lower order without addressing specific grounds. Cite to argue the reversal without reasons is an invalid exercise of appellate jurisdiction.
Oramco Chemicals Pvt. Ltd. v Gwalior Rayon Silk Mfg. Co. Ltd.
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1987 SC 1564 | (1987) 2 SCC 620
Key Ratio — Bald Conclusion Not Sufficient; Government Must State Specific Reasons
The government's order must contain good reasons in its support and not mere 'bald conclusion'. The government should state why it was against public interest and how it would hinder the development of the industry. The government should have given "facts, circumstances and reasons in their order so that the petitioners are able to know the official viewpoint and counter them by placing before the government the industries' case."
When to Use — When GST adjudicating authority's order merely quotes the SCN allegations as conclusions without explaining: (a) why assessee's reply was not accepted; (b) what specific facts led to the demand; (c) why penalty is being imposed. Cite to argue that bald conclusions = non-speaking order = order must be quashed and remanded for fresh decision with specific reasons.
Lord Donaldson M.R. in R v Civil Service Appeal Board, ex p. Cunningham
Court of Appeal, UK | (1991) 4 All ER 310 | Cited in Principles of Administrative Law Chapter X
Key Ratio — Fairness Requires Reasons Even Without Legitimate Expectation
"I would therefore dismiss the appeal not only on the ground of legitimate expectation..., but also on the broader ground that fairness requires a tribunal such as the board to give sufficient reasons for its decision to enable the parties to know the issues to which it addressed its mind and that it acted lawfully."
When to Use — When arguing before GST authorities that regardless of whether any specific provision requires reasons, the principles of fairness embedded in natural justice independently require the authority to give sufficient reasons to show it addressed the issues and acted within law.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter X — Principles of Natural Justice: Decision-Maker, Institutional Decisions & Prejudice

⚖️ Chapter X — Principles of Natural Justice: Decision-Maker, Institutional Decisions & Prejudice

Sections 10.12–10.16 | Principles of Administrative Law | Tree Diagram Notes | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Violation / Invalid
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Exception
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
10.12 — One Who Decides Must Hear
10.13 — Consultation by Deciding Authority
10.14 — Institutional Decisions
10.15 — Disclosure of Hearing/Inquiry Officer's Report
10.16 — Prejudice
Karunakar Principle — Inquiry Report Rights Matrix
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
10.12 — "One Who Decides Must Hear" — The Core Principle
👂 "One Who Decides Must Hear" — Audi Alteram Partem
The person who makes the decision must be the person who heard the evidence
📝 This is one of the most important sub-rules of natural justice. If Officer A hears your case but Officer B signs the order — that is fundamentally unfair. You argued to A but B decides. B never saw your face, never heard your arguments, never assessed your credibility.
The Golden Rule — Jeffs v New Zealand Dairy Production & Marketing Board (Privy Council 1967)
The decision-maker must apply his mind to the evidence on record before deciding the matter and should not accept mechanically the views of the hearing officer. In the latter case, the decision-making power effectively flows to the hearing officer, and the decision-maker discharges only an empty formality.
✓ VALID HEARING
Decision-maker fully informed of evidence given and submissions made
Decision-maker considers them and applies own mind
Decision-maker may accept accurate summary of evidence if it adequately discloses all evidence and submissions
📝 Even if someone else conducted the hearing, the decision-maker can still satisfy the rule — IF he was fully briefed with accurate summary of EVERYTHING that happened at the hearing.
✗ EMPTY FORMALITY — VIOLATION
One person hears → another person decides mechanically
Decision-maker accepts hearing officer's report without knowing what evidence was
Quasi-judicial functions cannot be delegated
📝 "Quasi-judicial functions cannot be delegated" — the POWER to hear and decide belongs to the designated authority. You can appoint someone to gather evidence for you — but you cannot hand over the decision itself.
💡 GST: SCN issued by Officer A. Personal hearing conducted by Officer A. Order signed by Officer B (successor/transfer). If B did not have access to full record of hearing before A — the order is vitiated. (Automotive Tyre Manufacturers principle).
Key Cases on This Principle
CaseCourtRuling / PrincipleGST Relevance
Automotive Tyre Manufacturers Assn. v Designated Authority
(2011) 2 SCC 258		 Supreme Court Final order by SUCCESSOR Designated Authority who never heard parties = violation of natural justice. "If one person hears and another decides, then personal hearing becomes an empty formality." Even written arguments are no substitute for oral hearing. Adjudication order passed by officer who did not conduct hearing = void. Anti-dumping principle directly applicable to GST adjudication orders.
UOI v Shivraj
AIR 2014 SC 2242		 Supreme Court Person/officer who accords the hearing to the objector must also submit the report/take the decision. If successor decides without giving fresh hearing — order stands vitiated having been passed in violation of principles of natural justice. GST Officer hears reply to SCN → transferred → successor passes order without fresh hearing = violates NJ. Object for violation before Appellate Authority.
Laxmi Devi v State of Bihar
AIR 2015 SC 2710		 Supreme Court "The person who heard and considered the objections can alone decide them; and not even his successor is competent to do so even on the basis of materials collected by his predecessor." Decision should be self-contained, speaking and reasoned. Reasons cannot be added later. Orders-in-Original: the officer who heard the personal hearing must pass the OIO. If transferred — fresh hearing must be given. Post-facto reasons cannot be added to OIO.
ICA v L.K. Ratna
AIR 1987 SC 71		 Supreme Court Though CA heard by disciplinary committee of Institute, he should also be heard by the Council which was the actual decision-making body. Different levels of authority: if final order is passed by Commissioner but hearing was only before Deputy Commissioner — hearing must be before decision-making authority.
Calinga Mining Corporation v UOI
(2013) 5 SCC 252		 Supreme Court Exception: Institutional hearing — hearing to be given by Central Government as institution, not by a particular individual. Note of officer (47 paragraphs) approved by Secretary and Minister = valid institutional decision. Where statute confers decision on "Central Government" / "Commissioner" as institution, any authorised officer may hear and any other authorised officer may decide under business allocation rules.
⚠️ KEY EXCEPTION — GROUP HEARINGS
When a group of persons is to decide after hearing, ALL members of the group should hear the WHOLE of the evidence and submissions. If some members are present only part of the time and did not hear entire evidence — difficult for them to appreciate and appraise same in judicial way. Justice should not only be done but should manifestly and undoubtedly be seen to be done.
10.13 — Consultation by Deciding Authority — The CVC/Vigilance Commissioner Problem
🔍 Consultation by Decision-Maker — When Allowed & When Violates Natural Justice
📝 After hearing is completed, the decision-maker sometimes consults a third party (like Vigilance Commissioner, CVC, or legal advisor) before deciding. The question is: can he do this without telling the party being heard? The answer depends on whether the party gets a chance to respond to what the consultant says.
BASIC PROPOSITION
After hearing has been completed, no additional material unknown to the concerned party ought to be taken into consideration by the decision-maker.
Decision-maker cannot seek advice from outside agency and not disclose it to the concerned party before taking it into consideration.
ARGUABLE — Sunil Kumar v State of WB (SC)
Disciplinary authority consulted Vigilance Commissioner (no statutory status). VC's views and DA's conclusions were "at variance." SC held: no violation because DA arrived independently. But this decision is criticised — VC's views did influence the DA.
📝 The author criticises this case as adopting "a rather perfunctory attitude" toward natural justice. Just because the DA's final decision differed from VC's opinion doesn't mean the VC's input had no influence.
✗ VIOLATION — SBI v D.C. Aggarwal (SC)
CVC disagreed with inquiry officer, held all charges proved, suggested major punishment. Bank's disciplinary committee agreed with CVC on charges but disagreed on quantum. SC quashed — CVC's report was prepared behind the back of employee without his participation. Not supplying copy of CVC report = violative of procedural safeguard and contrary to fair inquiry.
💡 GST: If Adjudication officer consults CBIC legal cell/CVC/superior officer after hearing and before passing order — the party must be given copy of that consultation note and chance to respond. Otherwise order is vitiated.
THREE OPTIONS FOR GOVERNMENT (Suggested)
(i) Do NOT consult VC/outside agency for drawing conclusions from record
(ii) IF consulted — give copy of his report to delinquent officer for comments BEFORE final decision
(iii) Give legal status to VC and provide statutory procedure for consultation
📝 The right approach: if you consult someone, tell the party. Let the party respond. Then decide. Secrecy in consultation violates the basic fairness principle.
10.14 — Institutional Decisions — When Government or Department Decides
🏛️ Institutional Decisions — "One Who Decides Must Hear" — Modified
📝 An institutional decision is one made by a government department or organisation as a whole — not by one named individual. The Minister, the Commissioner, the Board — they decide "institutionally." This modifies (but does not abolish) the "one who decides must hear" principle.
What Makes a Decision "Institutional"?
Decision-making power conferred on an INSTITUTION (government, department, Minister) not on a designated official specifically
Decision stands in name of the organisation/institution — not a personal decision of one identified officer
One official may hear, another may submit the record, a third (superior) may take the decision — all in name of "Government"
Government is an impersonal entity — functions only through officers. Minister cannot take each and every decision personally.
Local Government Board v Arlidge (1915 AC 120 — House of Lords) — Foundational Case
In entrusting the power to a government department (not a Court), Parliament must have intended that the department acts in the normal manner following its own departmental procedure, and takes its decision without having to conduct itself like a Court. The Minister is entrusted with a large volume of work — he is compelled to rely on the assistance of the staff. When a Minister is entrusted with the task of taking a decision, he is expected to obtain his materials vicariously through his officials.
Five Effects of the Arlidge Principle
(1) Not necessary to identify or individualise the exact official who makes the decision in a given case
(2) Department can deal with matter in usual departmental routine with any other administrative matter
(3) Not necessary to disclose the enquiry report to the party concerned
(4) One person can hold enquiry/hearing and another can decide the matter
(5) BUT — must fairly listen to both sides; must not use material unknown to party without opportunity to rebut
Indian Position — Where Institutional Decision Does NOT Save the Order
SPECIFIC OFFICIAL
When adjudicatory power conferred on a SPECIFIC OFFICIAL (not an institution) — that official alone must decide
Lt. Governor of UT — not same as Governor of State
Devi Datt v UOI (Delhi HC)
📝 Lt. Governor is a specific officer — he must apply his own mind. He cannot delegate the decision to a lower officer because the statute named HIM specifically.
REASONED ORDER ESSENTIAL
Travancore Rayons v UOI (SC) — Order merely saying "Government has carefully considered" without naming who, what was considered = insufficient
Party in statutory adjudication is entitled to know official designation of person who considered the matter
💡 GST Application: An Order-in-Appeal stating "the Appellate Authority has carefully considered the submissions and finds no merit" without engaging with specific arguments = inadequate. Does not satisfy NJ even under institutional decision framework.
NON-APPLICATION OF MIND
Rajendra Shankar Shukla v State of Chhattisgarh (SC 2015) — Officer acted in dual capacity as Special Secretary to Government AND CEO of Development Authority. Conflict of interest. Total lack of application of mind. SC quashed scheme addition.
REASONS MANDATORY
Mahabir Prasad Santosh Kumar v State of UP (SC) — Appeal dismissed by State Government without giving reasons = "negation of the rule of law." Right of appeal becomes empty formality without reasons. Licence could only be cancelled for good and adequate reasons.
LABH SINGH v UOI — Delhi HC — Institutional Decision Framework
Government's business can be done by various officers on its behalf under Business Rules. Unless a specific item is required to be done by the Minister or Secretary personally — any officer of usual hierarchy can do it. Deputy Secretary's decision = Government's institutional decision. Officer acting for Government under Business Rules acts not for himself but for the Government.
📝 This is why a Deputy Commissioner of CGST can pass an Order-in-Original even though the statute says "Commissioner." The business rules allocate this power. The decision is institutional — it is the Commissioner (as institution) deciding through an authorised officer.
💡 GST Practical Point: Under CGST Act, SCN can be issued and order passed by any officer authorised under Rule 99 + Section 2(91). The decision is institutional. But — if a SPECIFIC SECTION names a specific rank (e.g., Commissioner in Section 107 or 108 appeals) — that specific officer must apply mind. Cannot be delegated to junior officer without specific authorisation.
10.15 — Disclosure of Hearing/Inquiry Officer's Report — The Karunakar Landmark
📋 Must the Inquiry/Hearing Officer's Report be Disclosed to the Affected Party?
📝 In disciplinary proceedings, one officer holds the inquiry (enquiry officer) and another officer decides the punishment (disciplinary authority). The enquiry officer writes a report. Must the accused employee see this report before the disciplinary authority passes the final order? After much debate — the Supreme Court finally said YES in the landmark Karunakar case.
Journey of the Law — From Non-Disclosure to Mandatory Disclosure
EARLY VIEW — NON-DISCLOSURE OK
Suresh Koshy George v University of Kerala (AIR 1969 SC 198): report not demanded by student nor given — SC rejected challenge. Inquiry report need not be furnished.

Hira Nath Mishra v Rajendra Medical College: Public interest justified not showing report (girls' evidence to boys).

Kesava Mills v UOI (SC): Not ready to lay down inflexible rule — depends on individual case — non-disclosure = NJ violation only if it causes prejudice.
📝 These early cases followed the Arlidge principle too liberally — they essentially said: "you were heard during enquiry, that's enough." But this misses the point — the report is a new material that conditions the decision-maker's mind.
TRANSITIONAL — UOI v E. Bashyam (AIR 1988 SC 1000)
Thakkar, J. argued powerfully for disclosure:
"It appears to us to be a startling proposition to advance that the only authority which really and actually holds him guilty need not afford any opportunity to the person against whom such finding of guilt is recorded..."
Referred to larger bench as question of "mega importance."
📝 Justice Thakkar's dissent was the turning point. He pointed out that serving a copy of the report is NOT a "second show cause notice" — it's enabling the party to point out errors in the report before the disciplinary authority relies on it.
✓ LANDMARK — Managing Director, ECIL v B. Karunakar (SC 1993) 4 SCC 727
Constitution Bench — Settled the law finally
When inquiry officer is NOT the disciplinary authority — delinquent employee has RIGHT to receive copy of inquiry officer's report before disciplinary authority arrives at its conclusions.
Right exists WHETHER OR NOT employee asks for it.
Applies to ALL employees — Government and non-Government, public and private.
Right to make representation against report = integral part of opportunity of defence.
Denial = breach of natural justice.
💡 GST Application: The Karunakar principle applies wherever the show cause notice officer and the adjudicating officer are different (like a field officer issuing SCN and Commissioner passing OIO based on field officer's note). The party is entitled to see the file note/recommendation before OIO is passed.
Special Rule — When Disciplinary Authority Disagrees with Enquiry Officer
IF EO finds GUILTY → DA agrees → No further hearing needed
IF EO finds NOT GUILTY → DA disagrees → DA MUST give hearing before recording finding of guilty
Punjab National Bank v Kunj Behari Misra (SC 1998)
Formation of opinion to disagree must be tentative first; then opportunity to employee to show that disagreement is unwarranted
📝 The cruellest injustice: inquiry officer says "not guilty" — and then without hearing the person, the disciplinary authority reverses it and says "guilty." This is condemned unheard. Courts have emphatically said this is unconstitutional.
IF EO finds GUILTY → DA proposes HARSHER punishment → DA MUST give hearing before enhancing
WHAT CANNOT HAPPEN
DA mechanically adopting EO's report without applying own mind = bad order
DA rubber-stamping = "surrender of judgment" = violates NJ
But if DA independently reaches same conclusion as EO = valid
Karunakar Principle — Inquiry Report Rights Matrix (GST Application)
Situation in GST Adjudication Right to Report/Note? Hearing Required? Basis
SCN issued by Field Officer → OIO passed by same officer after personal hearing Not separately required — same officer heard and decided Hearing at time of SCN reply/personal hearing is sufficient Standard adjudication — no split function
SCN issued and hearing given by Officer A → Officer A transferred → Officer B (successor) passes OIO YES — Officer B must have full record; party must be given fresh hearing opportunity YES — Fresh personal hearing before new officer is required; otherwise OIO violates NJ (Automotive Tyre / Shivraj) Automotive Tyre Manufacturers; UOI v Shivraj (2014)
Departmental Audit para → Officer issues SCN based on audit observation; passes OIO incorporating audit finding entirely without independent analysis Party should be given audit para / departmental note before OIO is passed Mechanical adoption of audit report without independent reasoning = "surrender of judgment" = violation Karunakar principle; Board of Mining Examination v Ramjee (SC)
OIO by Commissioner (as designated authority under specific section) vs Commissioner (as institutional Central Government) Depends — if statute names specific rank/official, that specific person must apply mind If same level officer hears and decides institutionally = valid. If decision effectively taken by subordinate on file without Commissioner applying mind = may be challenged Labh Singh v UOI; Travancore Rayons (SC)
Order-in-Appeal by Appellate Authority (AA) who was also reviewing officer at first stage Conflict of interest — AA should not review own previous recommendation; recusal required BIAS issue under Rule against Bias — violates audi alteram partem and nemo judex in causa sua Bias principle — Chapter X generally
Internal consultation by Officer with CBIC/DGGSTI after hearing but before passing OIO — without telling party YES — copy of such consultation note must be given to party and party must be given chance to respond Consultation with outside authority after hearing = additional material unknown to party = must be disclosed (SBI v D.C. Aggarwal) SBI v D.C. Aggarwal (SC 1993); Post-hearing material principle
10.16 — Prejudice — Does Every NJ Violation Void the Order?
⚖️ Prejudice — The Modern Approach to Natural Justice Violations
📝 Earlier: any violation of natural justice automatically = void order. Modern trend: courts ask "was the party actually PREJUDICED by this violation?" If the violation made no real difference — the order may be upheld. But if it caused real harm to ability to defend — order is quashed.
OLDER ENGLISH APPROACH
Non-compliance with NJ = order null and void
No further inquiry into prejudice necessary
(Automatic nullity)
MODERN APPROACH (India & England)
Non-compliance with NJ does NOT make order ipso facto illegal and void
UNLESS it is shown that non-observance had prejudicially affected the applicant
Haryana Financial Corporation v Kailash Chandra Ahuja (2008) 9 SCC 31
📝 Prejudice must be shown — but actual prejudice, not imagined prejudice. The burden is on the party complaining. However, in some cases prejudice is so obvious it is presumed.
Key Propositions — Haryana Financial Corporation v Kailash Chandra Ahuja (SC)
#Proposition (Para No.)GST Relevance
25 Natural justice is not a fixed concept. It has many colours, shades, shapes and forms. Rules of NJ are not embodied rules and cannot be imprisoned within straitjacket formula. NJ violations in GST adjudication must be evaluated contextually — not with mechanical rigidity.
31 Under older English law, non-compliance with NJ = order null and void and no further inquiry necessary. This trend has changed. Even if GST officer failed to give adequate hearing, the order may survive if no actual prejudice can be shown.
36 Even where procedural requirements not complied with, action not held ipso facto illegal unless it is shown that non-observance had prejudicially affected the applicant. Department cannot always defeat NJ argument by saying "you got the SCN." The question is: was the violation prejudicial?
83 Where majority of documents are furnished and party gets opportunity to defend, but SOME copies of formal documents not furnished — onus is on employee to show non-furnishing resulted in de facto prejudice. In GST adjudication: if all key documents attached to SCN, but one annexure missing — assessee must show how that specific missing document prejudiced his defence.
85 Doctrine of de facto prejudice applied in English and Indian law. Courts do not favour frustrating departmental enquiries on hyper-technical approach. Minor procedural omissions in SCN/adjudication (e.g., wrong DIN, wrong section number) without actual prejudice = may not vitiate order.
89 De facto prejudice should not be based on mere apprehension or reasonable suspicion. Element of prejudice should exist as matter of fact OR there should be definite inference of likelihood of prejudice from default. Assessee arguing "I was prejudiced" must be specific — show what he could have argued/produced but was unable to because of the violation.
92 De facto prejudice is one essential ingredient to be shown by delinquent officer before punishment order can be set aside. Judicia posteriora sunt in lege fortioria. Prejudice = matter of fact — must be pleaded and shown by cogent documentation. GST: NJ violation argument must specifically plead and document what prejudice resulted. Bald assertion insufficient. Show: what argument was prevented, what document was denied, how that affected the outcome.
WHEN PREJUDICE IS PRESUMED — NO PROOF NEEDED
Violation of MANDATORY statutory provision (not just NJ) = prejudice presumed
Being condemned unheard entirely on a charge = prejudice per se
When disciplinary authority reverses EO's "not guilty" finding without hearing = prejudice is obvious and no further proof needed
WHEN PREJUDICE MUST BE SHOWN
Violation of DIRECTORY procedural rule
Minor procedural omission (late supply of notice, wrong section cited)
Non-supply of one ancillary document when rest of record was given
Where party had adequate other opportunities to defend and heard at length
SPECIAL RULE — REVENUE PREJUDICE
Non-supply of revised settlement application annexures to Revenue before order under Section 245-D(1) IT Act = prejudicial to Revenue
Prejudice can run BOTH ways — both assessee AND revenue can be prejudiced by NJ violations
GST PRACTITIONERS' FRAMEWORK — ARGUING PREJUDICE IN HEARINGS
When challenging an OIO/OIA for NJ violation, specifically plead:
(1) WHAT was violated — mandatory or directory provision?
(2) WHAT was the specific violation — no hearing / no SCN / no reasons / report not disclosed?
(3) HOW were you prejudiced — what could you have argued/produced that you could not because of violation?
(4) WHAT would the outcome have been — show that an adequate opportunity would likely have resulted in a different or at least different-reasoned order.
(5) IS IT DE FACTO PREJUDICE — actual, documented, not merely apprehended?
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter X | NJ: Decision-Maker, Institutional Decisions & Prejudice
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, First Appellate Authority, and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Automotive Tyre Manufacturers Associations v Designated Authority
Supreme Court of India | (2011) 2 SCC 258
Key Ratio — One Who Hears Must Decide; Successor Cannot Pass Order Without Fresh Hearing
If one person hears and another decides, then personal hearing becomes an empty formality. The final order passed by the successor Designated Authority offends the basic principle of natural justice. Unless a statutory provision, either specifically or by necessary implication, excludes the application of principle of natural justice, the requirement of giving reasonable opportunity of being heard before an order is made, is generally read into the provision of the statute — particularly when the order has civil consequences which obviously covers deprivation of property, personal rights and material deprivations of the party affected.
When to Use — When GST adjudication order is passed by an officer who did not conduct the personal hearing — the previous officer who heard was transferred. Cite this to argue that the order-in-original is vitiated as the officer who passed it never heard your submissions. Demand fresh hearing before the order-passing officer.
UOI v Shivraj
Supreme Court of India | AIR 2014 SC 2242 | (2014) 6 SCC 564
Key Ratio — Person Who Heard Must Submit Report/Decide; Successor's Order Without Fresh Hearing = Vitiated
The person/officer, who accords the hearing to the objector must also submit the report/take the decision on the objection and in case his successor decides the case without giving a fresh hearing, the order would stand vitiated having been passed in violation of the principles of natural justice. The hearing given to a person must be an effective one and not a mere formality.
When to Use — In all cases where the officer who issued/heard the SCN and the officer who passed the OIO are different due to transfer/succession. The OIO without a fresh hearing is not merely procedurally defective — it is vitiated. Raise this before the First Appellate Authority citing this ratio directly.
Laxmi Devi v State of Bihar
Supreme Court of India | AIR 2015 SC 2710 | (2015) 10 SCC 241
Key Ratio — Decision Must be Self-Contained, Speaking and Reasoned; Reasons Cannot Be Added Later
Every person likely to be adversely affected by a decision must be granted a meaningful opportunity of being heard. This right cannot be taken away by a side wind. So stringent is this right that it mandates that the person who heard and considered the objections can alone decide them; and not even his successor is competent to do so even on the basis of materials collected by his predecessor. Furthermore, the decision on the objections should be available in a self-contained, speaking and reasoned order; reasons cannot be added to it later as that would be akin to putting old wine in new bottles.
When to Use — Two important uses: (1) Successor officer challenge (same as Shivraj/Automotive Tyre); (2) When Department tries to supplement an unreasoned OIO/OIA by filing affidavit giving reasons before Appellate Authority/GSTAT — cite that post-hoc reasons are like "old wine in new bottles" — inadmissible.
Managing Director, ECIL v B. Karunakar
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1994 SC 1074 | (1993) 4 SCC 727 | Constitution Bench
Key Ratio — Right to Receive Copy of Inquiry/Report Officer's Report is Integral to Natural Justice
Whenever, therefore, the service rules contemplate an inquiry before a punishment is awarded, and when the inquiry officer is not the disciplinary authority, the delinquent employee will have the right to receive the inquiry officer's report notwithstanding the nature of punishment. When the inquiry officer is not the disciplinary authority, the delinquent employee has right to receive a copy of the Inquiry Officer's report before the disciplinary authority arrives at its conclusions with regard to the guilt or innocence of the employee with regard to the charges levelled against him. A denial of the Inquiry Officer's report before the disciplinary authority takes its decision on the charges is a denial of reasonable opportunity to the employee to prove his innocence and is a breach of the principles of natural justice.
When to Use — In GST adjudication: where the SCN/file note was prepared by one officer and OIO is being passed by another (designated authority) who relies on the field investigation report — argue that the assessee is entitled to see the field investigation report/internal recommendation before the OIO is passed. Non-supply = breach of NJ and vitiates OIO.
Punjab National Bank v Kunj Behari Misra
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1998 SC 2713 | (1998) 7 SCC 84
Key Ratio — If DA Proposes to DISAGREE with Inquiry Officer's Finding, Must Give Hearing Before Doing So
When the disciplinary authority differs with the view of the enquiry officer and proposes to come to a different conclusion, there is no reason as to why a reasonable opportunity of hearing should not be granted. It will be most unfair and iniquitous that where the charged officers succeed before the enquiry officer, they are deprived of representing to the disciplinary authority before that authority differs with the enquiry officer's report and, while recording a finding of guilt, imposes punishment on the officer. In our opinion, in any such situation, the charged officer must have an opportunity to represent before the disciplinary authority before final findings on the charges are recorded and punishment imposed.
When to Use — In GST context: where an internal review note/DGGSTI recommendation contradicts an earlier favourable finding by a field officer on the same evidence — and the Commissioner proposes to follow the adverse recommendation without hearing the assessee specifically on the contradiction. Cite: the assessee must be heard on the proposed disagreement before the adverse order is passed.
State Bank of India v D.C. Aggarwal
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1993 SC 1197 | (1993) 1 SCC 13
Key Ratio — Post-Hearing Consultation With Outside Agency Without Disclosure = Violation of Natural Justice
Action against an employee on a "confidential document" obtained behind his back and without his knowledge or supplying him a copy thereof was violative of natural justice. The report of the CVC was prepared behind the back of the employee without his participation and this was violative of "procedural safeguard and contrary to fair and just inquiry."
When to Use — In GST adjudication: where DGGSTI report, internal legal opinion, or advice from CBIC Head Office was obtained by the adjudicating officer after the personal hearing and before passing OIO, without giving assessee a copy. Cite: any material obtained behind assessee's back after hearing = violates NJ. OIO based on such undisclosed post-hearing material is vitiated.
Travancore Rayons v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1971 SC 862 | (1969) 3 SCC 868
Key Ratio — Institutional Order Must Disclose Who Decided and What Was Considered; Party Entitled to Know
The order does not disclose the name or designation of the authority of the Government of India who considered "the points made by the applicants," and it is impossible to say whether the officer was familiar with the subject-matter so that he could decide the dispute without elucidation and merely on a perusal of papers... A party who approaches the Government in exercise of a statutory right, for adjudication of a dispute is entitled to know at least the official designation of the person who considered the matter, what was considered by him, and the reasons for recording a decision against him.
When to Use — When an OIO or Order-in-Appeal is in printed format with a standard one-liner "all submissions have been considered and no merit found" — without identifying which officer considered what, and without engaging with specific arguments. Cite this to show such "institutional" orders that are no more than empty formalities are a violation of the rule of law. Orders must disclose designation of person deciding and reasoning — even in institutional decisions.
Haryana Financial Corporation v Kailash Chandra Ahuja
Supreme Court of India | (2008) 9 SCC 31
Key Ratio — De Facto Prejudice Required; NJ Violations Do Not Automatically Void Orders
The principles of natural justice cannot be put into a straitjacket formula. Their application depends upon the facts and circumstances of each case. To sustain a complaint of non-compliance with the principles of natural justice, one must establish that one has been prejudiced thereby for non-compliance with the principles of natural justice. Prejudice de facto should not be based on a mere apprehension or even on a reasonable suspicion. Element of prejudice should exist as a matter of fact or there should be such definite inference of likelihood of prejudice flowing from such default which relates to statutory violations.
When to Use — TWO uses: (1) As ASSESSEE: when establishing NJ violation, show specific de facto prejudice (e.g., "we were denied hearing on valuation — we would have filed expert valuation report which would have reduced demand by X"); (2) As DEPARTMENT (rare situation): when challenging assessee's NJ argument where violation was minor and assessee suffered no real prejudice — cite that frustrating proceedings on hyper-technical NJ grounds is deprecated by courts in recent times.
Mahabir Prasad Santosh Kumar v State of UP
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1970 SC 1302 | (1970) 1 SCC 764
Key Ratio — Dismissing Statutory Appeal Without Reasons = Negation of Rule of Law; Right of Appeal Becomes Empty Formality
The practice of the executive authority dismissing statutory appeals against orders which prima facie seriously prejudice the rights of the aggrieved party without reasons is a negation of the rule of law. A licence could only be cancelled for good and adequate reasons, the licensing authority should have given reasons for cancelling the licence. Only then could the right of appeal to the State Government be exercised effectively. If the aggrieved party is not supplied the reasons, the right to appeal becomes an empty formality.
When to Use — When the Order-in-Appeal dismisses the appeal with a brief non-speaking order. Cite: dismissal of appeal without reasoning on specific grounds raised = negation of right of appeal = violates Rule of Law. GSTAT/High Court can remand for fresh speaking order. Particularly powerful when the OIA is on a pre-printed form with a single sentence confirming OIO.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter 10 — Principles of Natural Justice: Disclosure & Hearing

⚖️ Chapter 10 — Principles of Natural Justice: Disclosure & Hearing

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Discretionary
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
10.3.1 Preliminary Inquiry Report — Disclosure
10.3.2 Expert Report — Disclosure
10.3.3 Evidence to the Contrary — Disclosure
10.4 Hearing — Core Principle
10.4.1 Hearing — Considered Necessary
10.4.2 One More Opportunity
10.4.3 Hearing — NOT Necessary
10.4.4 Sufficient Compliance
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
10.3.1 — Disclosure of the Preliminary Inquiry Report
📋 Preliminary Inquiry Report
Disclosure to Affected Party — When Required?
📝 Before formal proceedings start, an officer may run a background check / preliminary inquiry. The question is: must that report be shared with the person being proceeded against? The answer is YES — if the decision-maker relies on it.
General Rule — Preliminary Inquiry Report OUGHT to be Disclosed
if the decision-making authority takes it into consideration
✗ VIOLATION OF NATURAL JUSTICE IF NOT DISCLOSED
Report is given to deciding authority AND relied upon
Report contains not only conclusions of fact but also evidence supporting those conclusions → Party must be told of the evidence against him
Risk of prejudice is enough — no need to prove actual prejudice
(Privy Council — B. Surinder Singh Kanda)
📝 Fairness demands you know what is being used against you. Even if the report is shown only to the judge/officer, if you don't get a copy, the entire proceeding is vitiated.
💡 In GST adjudication: If a Superintendent's confidential report about suppression/evasion is placed before the Adjudicating Authority — that report must be shared with the assessee before order is passed. Failure = violation of natural justice.
✓ NOT REQUIRED IF
Deciding authority makes a DE NOVO (fresh) inquiry and arrives at own conclusions
without any reference to the preliminary report
Report merely checks/corroborates representations made by the party — adds nothing new to the charge sheet
(Shadi Lal v State of Punjab — AIR 1973 SC 1124)
📝 De novo = Fresh start. If the officer ignores the preliminary report and independently examines the evidence afresh — the old report need not be shared.
💡 If the GST officer does not rely on the internal investigation report in the SCN but independently re-examines returns, invoices etc. — and the SCN reflects only that fresh material — disclosure of the earlier investigation report is not mandatory.
CaseCourt / YearHeldKey Principle
B. Venkata Rao v Principal, Andhra Medical College Andhra Pradesh HC | AIR 1989 AP 159 Evidence collected by DTW shared with student before relying on it → Valid Competent authority can collect evidence; must supply it before relying on it
Shadi Lal v State of Punjab Supreme Court | AIR 1973 SC 1124 Preliminary report not shown — No prejudice; report merely corroborated charge-sheet No disclosure required if report adds nothing new to allegations
T.V.R. Radhakrishnan v State of Tamil Nadu Supreme Court | AIR 1974 SC 1862 Panchayat dissolved on basis of two reports NOT disclosed — Natural justice violated When summary of documents is mentioned in SCN, duty to disclose full document if party asks
Krishna Chandra v UOI Supreme Court | AIR 1974 SC 1589 Preliminary report not disclosed — Not relied upon → No violation If report not relied upon, no entitlement to copy; purpose was only prima facie screening
B. Surinder Singh Kanda v Government of Malaya Privy Council | (1962) MLJ 169 Report given to adjudicating officer but NOT to Kanda → Order of dismissal VOID Risk of prejudice is enough — proof of actual prejudice not needed. Lord Denning's famous right-to-be-heard ratio
State of UP v Mohd. Sharif Supreme Court | AIR 1982 SC 937 Statements of witnesses not furnished to delinquent employee → Proceedings quashed Witness statements forming part of preliminary report must be disclosed
Chingleput Bottlers v Majestic Bottling Co. Supreme Court | AIR 1984 SC 1030 Secret inquiry by collector not disclosed → SC upheld non-disclosure in liquor licensing context No fundamental right to liquor trade; but author notes this appears contrary to Kanda principles — only justifiable if disclosure endangers informants
⚖️ LORD DENNING — KANDA PRINCIPLE (PRIVY COUNCIL)
"If the right to be heard is to be a real right which is worth anything, it must carry with it a right in the accused man to know the case which is made against him... the judge who has to adjudicate must not hear evidence or receive representations from one side behind the back of the other. The risk of it is enough."
📝 In India, the Supreme Court additionally asks whether actual prejudice was caused. The Privy Council's standard is broader — even the risk of prejudice is enough to void the proceedings.
10.3.2 — Disclosure of the Report of the Expert
🔬 Expert Report — Disclosure Obligation
FACTS
Railway candidate stood first in written test → Accused of using unfair means → Substantiated by opinion of Forensic Department → Copy of Forensic Report NOT supplied to her → Name dropped from selected panel
HELD — APEX COURT
Order dropping her name set aside → Matter remanded → Department directed to provide ALL relevant documents to her (including expert report) BEFORE proceeding further
📝 An expert opinion used against you is just like any other evidence — you have the right to see it and respond to it. Without the report, you cannot defend yourself against a technical finding.
💡 GST Application: If a Chemical Examiner's / Valuation Expert's report is used by the Department to reclassify goods or reject declared value — that expert report must be shared with the assessee before passing order. Silent reliance = natural justice violation.
PRINCIPLE
Same rule as preliminary inquiry report — Any document/opinion relied upon by the adjudicating authority must be disclosed to the concerned party before the final decision
10.3.3 — Disclosure of Evidence to the Contrary
📄 Evidence to the Contrary — Must be Disclosed Before Use
📝 If the authority has "evidence to the contrary" — something that contradicts what you have stated — it must tell you what that evidence is before using it against you. You cannot be condemned by hidden evidence.
CONTEXT
Election Commission of India v Dr. Manmohan Singh (AIR 2000 SC 231)
Statement of holder of declared office for electoral roll purposes → Can be questioned ONLY when Electoral Registration Officer has "evidence to the contrary" → Substance of that evidence must be disclosed to enable rebuttal
PRINCIPLE
Where authority has contrary evidence, it must inform the holder/party of the substance of that evidence so that he can rebut it
No inquiry can be initiated in absence of such evidence being disclosed
📝 You have the right to know what specific evidence contradicts your claim — and you must be given a real opportunity to explain or disprove it before any adverse order.
💡 GST Example: If the GST officer has data from GSTN mismatches, third-party information, or intelligence reports that contradict the assessee's returns — that specific material must be disclosed in the SCN or before the personal hearing. Using undisclosed contrary evidence in the final order is a ground to challenge it.
10.4 — Hearing — Core Principle of Audi Alteram Partem
👂 Audi Alteram Partem — Hear the Other Side
Core Natural Justice Requirement
📝 Audi alteram partem = Hear both sides. Before any adverse order is made against a person, that person must be given a fair opportunity to rebut the allegations and present his case. This is a minimum requirement of natural justice.
Three Pillars of Fair Hearing
PILLAR 1
Know the allegations
Person must know what case is being made against him
📝 You can't defend what you don't know. The charges / grounds of action must be clearly communicated.
PILLAR 2
Real opportunity to rebut
Not ceremonial — must be meaningful opportunity to contradict or correct the case
📝 Opportunity given must be real — not a sham. If you are given notice but never actually allowed to speak, that is not a hearing.
PILLAR 3
Person must know action is contemplated AGAINST HIM
Information given "casually" or "for other purposes" does not satisfy requirement
📝 Even if you provided information during fact-finding, that doesn't mean you were "heard" — you must specifically know that adverse action is proposed against you.
⚖️ LORD MORRIS — RIDGE v BALDWIN (1964) AC 40
"The essential requirements of natural justice at least include that before someone is condemned he is to have an opportunity of defending himself."
⚠️ WHAT IS NOT A "HEARING" — S.L. Kapoor v Jagmohan | AIR 1981 SC 136
Prior correspondence between municipality and Government ≠ hearing, because:
(i) Correspondence was with Government of India, action taken by Delhi Administration
(ii) Delhi Administration never gave any opportunity to the committee
(iii) None of the grounds of action emerged in that correspondence
(iv) No hint that supersession was contemplated

"Information furnished by the committee in the course of an exploratory or fact-finding expedition could not be regarded as an answer to an action-inspired notice."
📝 Pre-action correspondence / information furnished before the threat is known does NOT substitute for a proper opportunity of hearing. You must be heard in the context of the specific proposed adverse action.
💡 GST Example: If an assessee filed replies during scrutiny assessment before issuance of SCN — that does not substitute for a fresh hearing after the SCN is issued. The assessee must be given opportunity specifically in response to the SCN and proposed demand.
KEY PRINCIPLE
There is NO fixed form of hearing procedure — it varies from situation to situation.
Ultimately, the Court decides whether the procedure in a specific case accords with natural justice.
10.4.1 — Opportunity of Hearing Considered NECESSARY — Illustrative Cases
✅ When Hearing is MANDATORY — Civil Consequences Doctrine
📝 Civil consequences = Any action that affects a person's property rights, civil liberties, livelihood, or non-pecuniary interests. If an authority's order will cause civil consequences, the rule of audi alteram partem is mandatory — even if the statute doesn't expressly say so.
SCOPE OF "CIVIL CONSEQUENCES"
Civil consequences = Infraction of not merely property/personal rights but of civil liberties, material deprivations and non-pecuniary damages.
Anything which affects a citizen in his civil life comes under its wide umbrella.
Even income tax proceedings u/s 142(2-A) entail civil consequences → Rule audi alteram partem applies.
⚠️ THIN LINE OBLITERATED
When civil consequences ensue, there is HARDLY ANY DISTINCTION between an administrative order and a quasi-judicial order — the principle of hearing applies to BOTH
📝 Even if the authority claims "we are just administrative officers, not a court" — if their order causes civil consequences (tax demand, penalty, cancellation of registration, licence etc.) they must follow natural justice.
💡 GST: Cancellation of GST registration, attachment of bank account, provisional attachment of property — all carry civil consequences → Opportunity of hearing is mandatory before such orders even though these are "administrative" actions.
SituationAuthority / CasePrinciple
Adverse remarks in service / compulsory retirement Maharashtra Civil Services (Pension) Rules matter — SC Prior written opportunity to employee before recording adverse entry; cannot alter conditions of service without pre-decisional hearing — violates Art. 14
Revocation of allotment without notice Sir Shadilal Distillery v State of UP — SC Allotment revoked; given to another without giving former allottee opportunity → Natural justice violated; proper speaking order required
Seniority re-fixation affecting existing employees Vinod Kumar Sharma v State of UP — SC Prior opportunity must be given to affected persons before seniority is re-fixed
Erroneous promotion sought to be corrected R.K. Mobisana Singh v Kh. Temba Singh — SC Principles of natural justice require opportunity before rectifying erroneous promotion
Probationer terminated for misconduct (not unsatisfactory performance) UOI v Narendra Singh — SC When termination is for overt misconduct, opportunity of hearing is imperative
Statutory body like a municipal council / panchayat dissolved Alfred Thangarajah Jurayappah v W.J. Fernando — Privy Council | (1967) 2 AC 337 Three-factor test: (1) nature of property/status at stake, (2) circumstances of person claiming control, (3) sanction that can be imposed. Municipal Council dissolution → Hearing mandatory as a matter of elementary justice
Rights accrued by passing some order Well-settled law — SC Accrued rights cannot be withdrawn, recalled or modified without following natural justice and giving notice before passing such order
Enhancement of punishment (Bar Council of India) SC — Advocate disciplinary matter Power to vary includes power to enhance — but must give advocate specific notice and reasonable opportunity before enhancing punishment
Minor penalty — even then opportunity required O.K. Bhardwaj v UOI — (2001) 9 SCC 180 Even for minor penalty, opportunity must be given to delinquent employee. If charges are factual and denied — inquiry is called for. Stigma in service records demands pre-order opportunity.
10.4.2 — One More Opportunity — Justice Must Not Only Be Done but Appear to Be Done
⚖️ Justice Must Appear to Be Done — At Least One Chance to Be Heard
📝 Even if a party earlier refused or missed an opportunity due to circumstances, courts can insist on giving one more chance — especially when the consequence is severe (like winding up a company) and justice must visibly be done.
FACTS — Cement Workers Karamchari Sangh v Jaipur Udyog Ltd. | (2008) 4 SCC 701
Company became sick → Board for Industrial and Financial Reconstruction (BIFR) directed winding up → Company appealed to Appellate Authority for Industrial and Financial Reconstruction (AAIFR) → AAIFR gave opportunity → Company refused as High Court had stayed proceedings → AAIFR confirmed BIFR order → SC approached
HELD — APEX COURT
Company was in circumstances practically denied remedy of appeal against winding-up order.
Under the maxim: Justice should not only be done but should also appear to have been done — at least ONE CHANCE should be afforded to place case before the Appellate Authority.
📝 The hackneyed but very useful maxim: justice must appear to have been done. Even where a party once refused to avail the opportunity (due to a stay order), if the circumstance was practically forced — one more chance must be given.
💡 GST: If an appeal before Appellate Authority was not heard due to some procedural impediment (e.g., system error, stay by HC) and ex-parte order was passed — appeal for one more hearing opportunity before confirmation of demand is a well-founded ground citing this principle.
MAXIM
"Justice should not only be done but should also appear to have been done"
10.4.3 — Opportunity of Hearing Considered NOT Necessary — Illustrative Cases
❌ When Hearing is NOT Required — Exceptions to Audi Alteram Partem
