📝 The right to hearing is strong but not absolute. In certain situations — legislative action, purely administrative decisions, preliminary screening, urgent public interest, admission of guilt — the requirement of prior hearing is either inapplicable or modified.
1
LEGISLATIVE ACTION
No question of natural justice when pure legislative action or delegated legislative action is brought in
Imposition of tax, fee or charges via legislation
📝 Parliament or Legislature making a law doesn't need to hear you first. Natural justice applies to adjudicative decisions, not law-making.
2
PURELY ADMINISTRATIVE ORDER (NO LIS)
Industrial dispute reference by State Government under Industrial Disputes Act
Being administrative order — no need to give notice to employer before making/refusing reference
📝 Lis = dispute between two parties before a decision-maker. If there is no lis and the action is purely administrative (like referring a dispute), no prior hearing needed.
3
PRELIMINARY / SCREENING STAGE
Order of preliminary verification/inquiry by Lokayukta → Conducted in private → No obligation to hear public servant at preliminary stage
📝 At the prima facie screening stage (to decide whether to investigate at all), hearing the accused might destroy the investigation — so it's not required yet.
4
URGENCY / PUBLIC INTEREST
Port Board determining quantum of damages before vessel leaves country → Urgency demands prior hearing be sacrificed; post-decisional remedy available via civil suit
📝 When the situation is so urgent that giving a hearing first would defeat the very purpose (vessel leaving country, destroying evidence) — post-decisional hearing is an acceptable substitute.
5
ADMISSION OF GUILT / CONSENT
If employee admits misconduct, or pleads guilty, or consents to alteration of service conditions → No inquiry or hearing required
📝 If you admit the charge — you cannot later claim the right to hearing was denied. Admission waives the right.
SituationCaseWhy Hearing Not Required
Disinvestment in PSUs — employees' consultation right BALCO Employees' Union v UOI | (2002) 2 SCC 350 Policy decision on disinvestment not subject to natural justice rights of employees as long as process doesn't contravene any law
Retrenchment under Section 6-N, Industrial Disputes Act SC — settled law Question of complying with natural justice would not arise in valid retrenchment under statutory provision; applies only when stigma attached
Recent encroachment on public property Ahmedabad Municipal Corporation v Nawab Khan Gulab Khan — AIR 1997 SC 152 No one has a right to encroach; tedious hearing procedure would create premium for unauthorised acts; recent encroachments may be removed without elaborate hearing
Suspension of Sarpanch (not removal) Orissa Gram Panchayats Act case — SC Suspension stage — hearing not required; only at stage of removal is reasonable opportunity to show cause required
Termination of temporary employee (no stigma) State of Orissa v Md. Illiyas | (2006) 1 SCC 275 Termination order simplicitor without stigma — not entitled to show-cause opportunity
Mass malpractice in examination Kumari Rani Swati Monalisa — AIR 2006 Ori Individual show-cause notices impractical in mass malpractice — question of affording opportunity is inapplicable
Policy decision to withdraw benefit SC — settled principle Taking a policy decision, Government not required to hear persons who have been granted benefit which is sought to be withdrawn — unless specific stand is taken justifying withdrawal (then natural justice applies)
No vested right — Grace marks withdrawal State of UP v Girish Bihari — AIR 1997 SC 1354 Power to relax includes power to withdraw on valid grounds — no vested right to grace marks; natural justice not violated
10.4.4 — Otherwise Sufficient Compliance of Opportunity of Hearing
✅ When Opportunity Given is Sufficient — What Counts as Adequate Compliance?
📝 The opportunity given does not have to be perfect — it has to be reasonable and sufficient in the circumstances. Courts ask: was the party actually informed, did they respond, was the response considered? If yes — the natural justice requirement is met.
Tests for Sufficient Compliance
✓ SUFFICIENT IF
Adverse entry in ACR communicated → Objections filed → Objections considered and rejected
(Haryana Warehousing Corporation v Ram Avtar)
✓ SUFFICIENT IF
SCN issued → Reply filed → Reply considered → Detailed speaking order passed
Even if party contends "material not supplied" — if SCN was replied to and considered, compliance is met
✓ SUFFICIENT IF
Notice sent by registered post → Received by family member → Employee in custody → Family/representative could have participated
Service of notice on family member is valid service
✓ SUFFICIENT IF
Opportunity given but party failed to avail it after repeated reminders → Services dispensed with
Sufficient compliance of natural justice
⚠️ REASONABLENESS OF OPPORTUNITY — KEY TEST
The opportunity to be given should be reasonable, but the reasonableness must be judged keeping in view the time frame available.
A realistic view has to be taken — not a leisurely standard.
Liquor licence default for 2 months → Notice proposing cancellation issued → Cannot expect lengthy hearing window
📝 What is reasonable depends on context. In a tax matter with months of assessment, a short notice period may not be reasonable. In a case involving forfeiture of perishable deposits or urgent cancellation, a short window is reasonable. Time-frame of the subject matter governs.
BLACKLISTING — Sufficient Compliance Test
State of Orissa v Krishan Stores | AIR 1997 SC 871
Show-cause notice issued → Replied → Considered → Blacklisting order passed
Held: Principle of audi alteram partem satisfied. It was NOT required to supply material on which charges were based — sufficient that opportunity of show-cause was given and availed.
Requirement: opportunity of show-cause. NOT: supply all underlying material.
💡 GST Relevance: In GST adjudication, once SCN is issued and assessee is heard (even if the full investigation file is not supplied), the requirement of natural justice is satisfied — provided the SCN adequately stated the grounds. The assessee cannot later claim that he was denied hearing merely because internal investigation file was not handed over.
STATUTORY SILENCE — RULE STILL APPLIES
Even if a statute is silent on the need for a hearing, where the statute uses the word "determine" or "sum due" or "satisfied" — the requirement of hearing is implied.
Haryana Public Moneys (Recovery of Dues) Act — "sum due as determined" implies hearing the alleged defaulter first.
📝 The word "determine" in a statute implies there may be a dispute. Disputes must be resolved by hearing both sides. A statute that says "determine the sum due" means the Managing Director must hear the defaulter before concluding what is due.
💡 GST: Section 74 / 73 CGST Act uses "proper officer is satisfied" — this implies that the proper officer must consider the assessee's reply before passing order. An order passed without genuinely applying mind to the assessee's reply violates the implied requirement of hearing.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 10 | Principles of Natural Justice: Disclosure & Hearing
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
B. Surinder Singh Kanda v Government of Malaya
Privy Council | (1962) MLJ 169 | Lord Denning
Key Ratio — The Real Right to Be Heard
"If the right to be heard is to be a real right which is worth anything, it must carry with it a right in the accused man to know the case which is made against him. He must know what evidence has been given and what statements have been made affecting him; and then he must be given a fair opportunity to correct or contradict them... The risk of prejudice is enough. No one who has lost a case will believe he has been fairly treated if the other side has had access to the judge without his knowing."
When to Use — When the GST adjudicating authority has relied on an internal investigation report, surveillance report or intelligence note that was not disclosed to the assessee before passing the order. Cite to establish that the order is vitiated — risk of prejudice is enough; proof of actual prejudice is not required.
Ridge v Baldwin
House of Lords | (1964) AC 40 | Lord Morris
Key Ratio — Minimum of Natural Justice
"The essential requirements of natural justice at least include that before someone is condemned he is to have an opportunity of defending himself."
When to Use — Universal citation for the core principle of audi alteram partem. Use whenever challenging a GST order passed without adequate opportunity of hearing — adjudication orders, penalty orders, order confirming demand without hearing the assessee's reply.
S.L. Kapoor v Jagmohan
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1981 SC 136
Key Ratio — What Constitutes a Real Hearing
The demands of natural justice are not met even if the very person proceeded against has furnished the information on which the action is based, "if it is furnished in a casual way or for some other purpose." What is important is that the person proceeded against must know that he is being required to meet the allegations which might lead to a certain action being taken against him.
When to Use — When the Department argues "assessee already submitted documents during scrutiny / investigation / audit — no fresh hearing needed." Counter: information given during scrutiny for other purposes does not substitute for a hearing specifically in context of the proposed adverse action (SCN + demand).
Alfred Thangarajah Jurayappah v W.J. Fernando
Privy Council | (1967) 2 AC 337
Key Ratio — Three Factors for Applying Audi Alteram Partem
"First, what is the nature of the property, the office held, status enjoyed or services to be performed by the complainant of injustice. Secondly, in what circumstances or upon what occasion is the person claiming to be entitled to exercise the measure of control entitled to intervene. Thirdly, when a right to intervene is proved, what sanctions in fact is the latter entitled to impose upon the other. It is only upon a consideration of all these matters that the question of the application of the principle can properly be determined."
When to Use — When the authority claims natural justice does not apply in a particular GST proceeding (e.g., suo motu revision, cancellation of registration). Cite this three-factor test to establish that the assessee's significant commercial interests, the Government's power of intervention in GST enforcement, and the severe sanction (demand, penalty, registration cancellation) — all three factors together mandate hearing before action.
Disclosure of Preliminary Inquiry Report — General Principle
Krishna Chandra v UOI | AIR 1974 SC 1589 | Supreme Court — Read with Kanda
Key Ratio — When Report Must Be Disclosed
Any document relied upon by the enquiry officer must be disclosed to the concerned party. If the hearing is being held de novo, without any reference to any preliminary enquiry report, then the report need not be disclosed to the party affected. But if the preliminary inquiry report is being depended upon to arrive at findings against the person concerned, then he should be given its copy.
When to Use — In GST appeals where the adjudicating authority or appellate authority relies on internal investigation report / DGGI report / intelligence note / third-party information that was not disclosed in the SCN. Establish: (a) the authority relied on the report; (b) the report was not supplied; (c) therefore the order is vitiated.
Civil Consequences — Hearing Mandatory Even for Administrative Orders
Rajesh Kumar v Deputy CIT | (2007) 2 SCC 181 | Supreme Court
Key Ratio — Obliteration of Administrative / Quasi-Judicial Distinction
When civil consequences ensue, there is hardly any distinction between an administrative order and a quasi-judicial order. The principle of audi alteram partem will hold good irrespective of whether the power conferred on a statutory body or tribunal is administrative or quasi-judicial. If upon giving an opportunity of hearing to an affected party, it is possible to arrive at a different finding, the principles of natural justice must be complied with.
When to Use — When the GST Department argues "this is an administrative action, not a quasi-judicial one, so hearing is not required" — e.g., in cases of attachment, seizure, provisional attachment, or administrative classification. Cite to establish that the civil consequences test overrides the administrative/quasi-judicial distinction.
O.K. Bhardwaj v UOI
Supreme Court | (2001) 9 SCC 180
Key Ratio — Even for Minor Penalty; Factual Charges Must Have Inquiry
Even in the case of a minor penalty, an opportunity has to be given to the delinquent employee to have his say or to file his explanation with respect to the charges against him. Moreover, if the charges are factual and if they are denied by the delinquent employee, an inquiry should also be called for. This is the minimum requirement of the principle of natural justice and the said requirement cannot be dispensed with.
When to Use — In GST penalty proceedings under Section 122/125 CGST Act, especially where penalty is imposed summarily or without adequate inquiry. Argue: even for minor penalty, if the assessee denies the factual allegations, an inquiry is mandated — penalty order without such inquiry is bad in law.
Justice Must Appear to Be Done — One More Opportunity
Cement Workers Karamchari Sangh v Jaipur Udyog Ltd. | (2008) 4 SCC 701 | Supreme Court
Key Ratio — Hackneyed but Very Useful Maxim
It cannot be overlooked that the company was, in the circumstances of the case, practically denied remedy of appeal against the winding-up order. Hence under the hackneyed but very useful maxim: justice should not only be done but should also appear to have been done, at least one chance should be afforded to the company to place its case before the Appellate Authority.
When to Use — In GST appeals where the appellate authority passed ex-parte order (no one appeared) due to practical difficulties — COVID, system errors, postal delays, wrong address, etc. Cite this to urge: at least one more hearing opportunity must be given; justice must appear to be done, not merely technically dispensed.
Statutory Word "Determine" Implies Hearing
S.K. Bhargava v Collector, Chandigarh | AIR 1998 SC 2885 | Supreme Court
Key Ratio — Natural Justice Implied by Statutory Words
The requirement of determination of the sum due by the Managing Director of the Financial Corporation must be regarded as providing for the Managing Director hearing the alleged defaulter before coming to the conclusion as to what is the sum due. The very use of the word "determine" and "sum due" in the section implies that there may be a lis between the parties and they have to be heard before a final conclusion is arrived at. This determination cannot be done without notice to the alleged defaulter.
When to Use — In GST matters where Section 73/74 uses "proper officer is satisfied" or "determine the tax due" — argue that the statutory language of determination necessarily implies a hearing of the assessee's contentions. An order confirming demand without genuinely applying mind to the assessee's reply is not a valid "determination."
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter 10 — Principles of Natural Justice or Fairness

⚖️ Chapter 10 — Principles of Natural Justice or Fairness

§§ 10.4.5–10.10 | Hearing, Evidence, Cross-Examination, Counsel, Reasoned Orders | Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Violation
Condition / Unless / If
Discretionary / Optional
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
10.4.5 Non-Compliance of Opportunity of Hearing
10.4.6 Oral / Personal Hearing — Rule & Exceptions
10.5 Receiving Evidence in Presence of Concerned Party
10.6 Receiving Evidence Produced by Concerned Person
10.6.1 Summoning of Witnesses
10.7 Cross-Examination
10.8 Right to Counsel / Legal Representation

10.10 Reasoned Decision (Speaking Order)
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
10.4.5 — Non-Compliance of Opportunity of Hearing | Illustrative Cases
🔔 Non-Compliance of Opportunity of Hearing
When State / Authority Acts Without Hearing — Consequences
📝 The State or any authority cannot take adverse action against a person without giving that person a fair chance to explain their position first. If it does so, the action is set aside by courts. The cases below illustrate how courts have consistently enforced this rule.
VIOLATION Acting without opportunity of hearing renders order non est (void) or liable to be quashed
📝 Non est = Does not exist in law. Just like an unregistered compulsory document, an order passed without hearing is treated as if it was never made.
📋 Illustrative Supreme Court Situations
CIVIL SERVANTS
Dismissed / discharged without reasonable opportunity of representation → Departmental inquiry must be conducted & opportunity given → Order without this vitiated
📝 Even if a govt employee is clearly in the wrong, the procedure must be followed — no short-cuts to punishment.
SALARY/ARREARS
State of MP v Sadashiv Zamindar — Show-cause notice vitiated; matter remitted for fresh decision after giving State opportunity to be heard
📝 Even the State (as a party) must be given hearing before an adverse order is made against it.
EX PARTE INQUIRY
Where delinquent refuses to appear or respond → Authority may proceed ex parte after due notice → But must give show-cause before final punishment order
📝 Ex parte = Proceeding in absence of one party. You cannot ignore someone indefinitely — proper notice must be given first.
💡 GST: SCN issued, assessee does not respond. Department can pass ex parte order — but must first issue proper SCN with adequate time u/s 73/74 CGST Act.
CONFISCATION
Opportunity prior to confiscation of seized timber, vehicle, sawmill — mandatory. Without hearing → order unsustainable
💡 GST: Seizure of goods u/s 67 CGST Act → Notice in Form GST INS-01 → Opportunity of hearing before confiscation u/s 130 — mandatory.
🏗️ Patel Engineering Ltd v UOI — AIR 2012 SC 2342 | Blacklisting + Proportionality
FACTS
Contractor accepted National Highway bid but backed out as commercially non-viable → Blacklisted for 1 year by NHAI
DOCTRINE
Proportionality Test (from Teri Oat Estates): Action must use least restrictive means to achieve legislative/administrative purpose; proper balance between adverse effects and purpose
📝 Proportionality = Don't use a sledgehammer to crack a nut. The punishment must fit the breach — no overkill.
💡 GST: Heavy penalty for minor technical default may be disproportionate — proportionality doctrine applies to GST penalty proceedings.
HELD
Blacklisting upheld — contractor unreliable, NHAI suffered financial loss. Oral hearing not inviolable — reasonable written opportunity sufficient. No violation of natural justice.
⚠️ KEY PRINCIPLE There is no inviolable rule that personal oral hearing must precede every State decision. A reasonable opportunity to explain one's case (even in writing) satisfies natural justice — unless context demands oral hearing.
10.4.6 — Oral or Personal Hearing — Rule & Exceptions
🎤 Oral / Personal Hearing — Is it Always Mandatory?
GENERAL RULE
Oral/personal hearing is NOT an inevitable or indispensable ingredient of natural justice in all cases.
Written explanation / representation may be an adequate substitute.
Natural justice does not necessarily predicate a personal hearing unless context requires.
📝 Natural justice requires that the affected person get a fair chance to put their case — but this does not always mean face-to-face hearing. A written show-cause notice + written reply can satisfy natural justice in many situations.
MINIMUM REQUIREMENT (Courts insist)
The person affected must have an opportunity of adequately meeting the case against him and presenting his own case — whether through written memorandum or oral hearing. If this minimum is absent → natural justice violated.
✓ ORAL HEARING MAY BE NECESSARY WHEN:
Complex and technical questions of law or fact involved → Travancore Rayons v UOI
Oral evidence to be taken from witnesses
Cancellation of liquor licence with heavy financial loss where licensee raises controversial issues of fact
Disciplinary action by professional body against chartered accountant — personally heard by Disciplinary Committee AND Council of ICAI (Ratna case)
💡 GST: Complex technical classification dispute (e.g. whether a product is medicament or cosmetic) warrants personal hearing before adjudication u/s 75(4) CGST Act.
✗ PERSONAL HEARING HELD NOT NECESSARY (inter alia):
(i) President of India determining dispute about age of High Court Judge
(ii) Government of India in appeal against confiscation of illegally imported goods
(iii) Disciplinary authority at final stage after inquiry officer has given hearing
(iv) Central Government in revision against mining lease refusal (Mineral Concession Rules 1960)
(v) Motor Vehicles Act Sec 60(1) — opportunity for explanation ≠ personal hearing for permit cancellation (Madras HC)
(vi) Cinema licensing — no personal hearing to each of large number of objectors (MP Cinemas Act)
(vii) Court-martial confirmation & post-confirmation review (stages b & c)
(viii) Presidential pardon under Art. 72 — "manner of consideration lies within discretion of President"
ANTI-DUMPING SPECIAL RULE
Customs Tariff (Anti-Dumping Duty) Rules 1995 — Designated Authority MUST afford all parties who filed objections a personal hearing before final decision. Written arguments are no substitute for oral hearing here — personal hearing enables authority to watch demeanour of witnesses and clear doubts.
💡 GST parallel: Adjudicating Officer has mandatory duty to provide personal hearing where requested u/s 75(4) CGST Act — this is a statutory right, not merely a procedural courtesy.
GST SPECIFIC — Section 75(4) CGST Act
Opportunity of hearing shall be granted where a request is received from taxable person or where adverse decision is contemplated. Non-grant of opportunity under s.75(4) = violation of natural justice = order liable to be quashed.
10.5 — Receiving Evidence in the Presence of the Concerned Party
👁️ Evidence Must Be Received in Presence of Affected Party
Audi Alteram Partem — Cardinal Rule of Natural Justice
📝 Audi alteram partem = Hear the other side. This principle means: any evidence used against a person must be disclosed to them, and they must get a chance to rebut it. Evidence taken behind someone's back violates natural justice.
IDEAL PROCEDURE
In oral hearing — evidence against party should be taken in his presence. Taking evidence behind his back without disclosure = prima facie violation of natural justice (Errington v Minister of Health)
✓ IDEAL — FULL PRESENCE
Evidence taken in presence of party
→ Party can cross-examine
→ Party can rebut
💡 GST: Statements of third parties u/s 70 CGST relied upon in SCN — assessee entitled to cross-examine those parties (ICDS Ltd v CIT principle applies).
ACCEPTABLE — GIST DISCLOSURE
Evidence recorded in absence but gist communicated to party + opportunity to rebut = No breach of natural justice
📝 You don't always need to be present when evidence is recorded — but you MUST be told what was said against you, so you can respond.
💡 Board of Education v Rice: "They can obtain information in any way they think best, always giving a fair opportunity to those who are parties for correcting or contradicting any relevant statement prejudicial to their views."
EXCEPTION — CONFIDENTIAL WITNESS
Where witness safety at risk / identity confidential → Evidence in absence permissible IF gist disclosed without revealing identity
📝 The courts balance safety of witnesses against fairness to the accused — the solution is: tell the person what was said (gist), but not who said it.
Hira Nath Misra v Rajendra Medical College: Inquiry against male students for entering girls' hostel — girls testified in absence of boys — no violation as gist communicated, safety of girls protected
Goonda Acts: Evidence collected behind goonda's back — witnesses not disclosed — upheld as no witness would come forward otherwise
R v Gaming Board ex p. Benaim: Board need not reveal sources of police information to club applicant "if that would put informant in peril or contrary to public interest" — but must give sufficient indication of objections raised
⚠️ ABSOLUTE MINIMUM (Cannot be compromised)
Even where witness identity is kept confidential — party must either be (a) apprised of statements made by witnesses, OR (b) supplied a copy, OR (c) at minimum given gist of evidence against him + opportunity to rebut.
Without this minimum → natural justice violated → order quashed.
RAW / INTELLIGENCE EXCEPTION
Where confidential RAW reports form basis of compulsory retirement → officer cannot demand text of report be shown to him. Compulsory retirement itself = no stigma. Courts will not interfere on ground of denial of natural justice. (Nisha Priya Bhatia v UOI, 2020)
💡 GST parallel: Intelligence inputs forming basis of search u/s 67 CGST need not be disclosed to assessee at time of search — but if used in adjudication, gist must be given and opportunity to rebut must be provided.
10.6 — Receiving Evidence Produced by the Concerned Person
📂 Right to Produce Evidence in Defence
Basic Component of Natural Justice — Audi Alteram Partem
📝 Natural justice requires not just that you be heard — but that you be given a real chance to put forward all your evidence. Refusing to look at evidence the party wants to show is itself a violation of natural justice.
BASIC PRINCIPLE
Adjudicating authority MUST give the person concerned an opportunity to produce all relevant evidence on which he relies and to rebut evidence against him.
Refusal to receive evidence on behalf of affected person may amount to breach of natural justice, vitiating the decision.
📝 In Dhakeswari Cotton Mills v CIT (SC) — ITAT violated natural justice by refusing to look at account books the taxpayer wanted to produce, which he could not produce earlier due to reasons beyond his control.
💡 GST: If assessee offers to produce books of account or evidence during adjudication and adjudicating officer refuses to receive it — natural justice violated — order liable to be set aside.
EX PARTE PROCEEDINGS
Authority may proceed ex parte if person does not appear after notice
BUT must be liberal — violation if authority refuses to hear person who appears subsequently during hearing (second notice)
If adequate reasons for absence at first hearing → authority MUST restore and hear even after ex parte decision
📝 Missing one hearing date does not forfeit your right to be heard permanently — if you have good reason, courts will give you another chance.
💡 GST: Assessee unable to attend personal hearing due to illness — adjudicating officer must grant fresh hearing on reasonable application — not pass ex parte order without second opportunity.
ADJOURNMENTS
"Sometimes, if justice is to be done, adjournments are essential"
But if party had full knowledge of case + ample time to prepare → refusal of adjournment not violative of natural justice
📝 Adjournments are a tool of justice, not a weapon of delay. Courts balance the need for fairness with the need to avoid procrastination.
💡 GST: ITAT/Commissioner (A) refusing adjournment after multiple previous adjournments — valid if party has had adequate opportunity. But first or second genuine request should ordinarily be granted.
LIMITING EVIDENCE
Authority can limit irrelevant / immaterial evidence — does not violate natural justice
But discretion to limit evidence must be exercised reasonably, in good faith and on proper grounds
📝 An authority is not a passive receiver of all evidence a party wants to pile up — but it cannot arbitrarily exclude relevant evidence on flimsy grounds.
SC — CARDINAL PRINCIPLE OF NATURAL JUSTICE
"Our laws of procedure are grounded on a principle of natural justice which requires that men should not be condemned unheard, that decisions should not be reached behind their backs, that proceedings that affect their lives and property should not continue in their absence and that they should not be precluded from participating in them."
10.6.1 — Summoning of Witnesses
📋 Summoning of Witnesses — Powers of Adjudicatory Bodies
INHERENT POWER
Power to call witnesses and request production of documents = inherent in adjudicatory body (no statutory sanction needed)
📝 Inherent power = a power that exists without being written down in a statute, because it is necessary for the body to function.
NO INHERENT COERCIVE POWER
Authority has NO inherent power to compel attendance of witnesses or production of documents — coercive power requires statutory provision
📝 Issuing a summons is one thing — forcing someone to appear against their will requires legal authority specifically conferred by Parliament.
DISCRETIONARY — LETTERS OF REQUEST
Without coercive power → issuing letters of request to witnesses = matter of discretion. If authority chooses not to issue = no breach of natural justice (provided party can produce own witnesses)
FAILURE OF NATURAL JUSTICE
If authority HAS statutory power to summon but declines party's request to exercise it → amounts to failure of natural justice
💡 GST: If adjudicating officer has power under CGST Act to examine persons u/s 70 but refuses assessee's request to summon key witness — natural justice issue arises.
STATUTE — Departmental Inquiries (Enforcement of Attendance of Witnesses & Production of Documents) Act, 1972
Clothes Inquiry Officers in departmental inquiries with coercive powers same as Civil Court under CPC to summon witnesses. Such authority deemed to be Civil Court for purposes of Sections 345 & 346 CrPC 1973.
📝 Before 1972, departmental inquiry officers had no coercive power — persons not in government employment could simply ignore summons. The 1972 Act solved this gap.
10.7 — Cross-Examination
🔀 Cross-Examination — Is it an Integral Part of Natural Justice?
📝 Cross-examination = questioning a witness called by the other side, to test their credibility and expose falsehoods. It is one of the most powerful tools in a hearing — but courts do not require it in every situation.
GENERAL RULE
Cross-examination is NOT an obligatory or essential part of natural justice in all situations.
Whether to give opportunity for cross-examination depends on facts and circumstances of each case and the statute under which hearing is held.
✓ CROSS-EXAMINATION NECESSARY WHEN:
Credibility of witness is in doubt, OR version of witness is disputed (K.L. Tripathi v State Bank of India guideline)
Domestic enquiry by employer for disciplinary action — labour-management relations → cross-examination essential component
Disciplinary proceedings by government / statutory corporation against employees
Income Tax assessment based on third-party statements — assessee entitled to cross-examine those parties (ICDS Ltd v CIT)
Where statute specifically confers right of cross-examination (e.g., FERA rule 3 — personal hearing implies cross-examination right)
💡 GST: SCN based on statements of third parties under s.70 → assessee demands cross-examination → adjudicating officer must provide it — ICDS principle applies directly.
✗ CROSS-EXAMINATION NOT NECESSARY WHEN:
No dispute as to facts — only explanation of circumstances → absence of cross-examination does not create prejudice (K.L. Tripathi guidelines)
Party does NOT question veracity of facts or credibility — and accepts factual basis
Party NEVER ASKED for cross-examination opportunity
📝 If you don't ask, you cannot later complain. Silence at the hearing stage = waiver of cross-examination right.
Only affidavits filed (not oral testimony) before fact-finding body — Bakshi Ghulam Mohammad case
Preventive detention before Advisory Board — detention based on subjective satisfaction, not proof beyond doubt
Sensitive cases — gender/safety concerns (girls' hostel case — Hira Nath Misra)
Where party has made confession and is bound by it (FERA case)
K.L. TRIPATHI v STATE BANK OF INDIA — GUIDELINES ON CROSS-EXAMINATION

"If the credibility of a person who has testified or given some information is in doubt, or if the version of the statement of the person who has testified, is, in dispute, right of cross-examination must inevitably form part of fair play in action but where there is no lis regarding the facts but certain explanation of the circumstances there is no requirement of cross-examination to be fulfilled to justify fair play in action."
📝 Lis = dispute/controversy. So if there is NO real dispute about what happened (just about why), cross-examination is not needed. If there IS a dispute about what happened or whether the witness is truthful — cross-examination is a must.
💡 GST: If assessee disputes the statements of the person summoned by department (says the person is lying / mistaken) → cross-examination must be given. If assessee admits the facts but disputes legal interpretation → cross-examination need not be given.
Kishanchand Chellaram v CIT — Letter Not Shown to Assessee
Income tax authorities relied on a letter from bank manager — extract reproduced in order — but original letter NOT shown to assessee. SC held: Authorities bound to produce letter before assessee so he could cross-examine the manager with reference to his statement. Not doing so = infraction of assessee's rights.
💡 GST Direct Parallel: If adjudicating officer relies on private communication or third-party statement in order — assessee must be given opportunity to see it and cross-examine — failure to do so vitiates the order.
10.8 — Right to Counsel / Legal Representation
⚖️ Right to Legal Representation — Is it a Guaranteed Right?
📝 The question is: can a person facing inquiry / adjudication insist on being represented by a lawyer as a matter of right? The answer in India is — generally no, but with important exceptions based on fairness.
POSITION IN INDIA
Legal representation is NOT claimable as a matter of right before administrative adjudicatory bodies (unlike courts). The hearing officer has discretion to permit it. Courts can review whether discretion was rightly exercised.
DENIAL = VIOLATION OF NATURAL JUSTICE WHEN:
Complex questions of law and fact arise WHERE evidence is elaborate AND party cannot handle case himself
Other side is represented by a lawyer / trained prosecutor — party pitted against trained mind without assistance = unequal contest (Board of Trustees, Port of Bombay v Dilipkumar)
📝 The Port of Bombay case: If the employer's side has lawyers, the employee must also be allowed a lawyer — otherwise scales are unfairly tilted.
Disciplinary action against professional person by professional body — Chartered Accountant before ICAI Council (Ratna case)
Retired HC Judge appointed as Inquiry Officer → denial of lawyer to employee = unfair (Ramesh Chandra v Delhi University)
💡 GST: If Revenue is represented by an experienced Tax Superintendent in personal hearing — assessee should be allowed CA/advocate. Denial may be challenged as violation of natural justice.
DENIAL DOES NOT VIOLATE WHEN:
No legal complexity in case
No oral evidence to be recorded (no need for cross-examination)
Party itself is qualified to handle case
Request made at very belated stage of inquiry (Sunil Kumar case)
Disciplinary proceedings against students — legal representation invariably refused
PREVENTIVE DETENTION SPECIAL RULES
Detenu under preventive detention has NO right to demand legal representation before Advisory Board (Art. 22(3)(b))
BUT: Board has discretion to permit. Request must be considered on merits (Kavita case)
EQUALITY RULE — A.K. Roy v UOI
If government/detaining authority is represented by lawyer/legal adviser before Board → detenu MUST also be given similar right (Art. 14, 21, 39A)
Right to 'Friend': Even without right to lawyer, detenu entitled to assistance of a 'friend' (not legal practitioner) before Advisory Board — expansive meaning of 'friend' = ally / cause supporter (D'Cuoto case)
⚔️ KEY RATIO — Board of Trustees, Port of Bombay v Dilipkumar

"In our view we have reached a stage in our onward march to fair play in action that where in an enquiry before a domestic tribunal the delinquent officer is pitted against a legally trained mind, if he seeks permission to appear through a legal practitioner the refusal to grant this request would amount to denial of a reasonable request to defend himself and the essential principles of natural justice would be violated."
💡 GST Application: In personal hearing before Adjudicating Officer — if the Revenue side has trained technical officer presenting detailed forensic case — assessee (especially small trader) should be allowed CA/advocate. Refusal may vitiate the adjudication order.
DUTY OF INQUIRY OFFICER (Bhagat Ram v State of HP)
In certain circumstances, inquiry officer may have an obligation to himself enquire from the party whether he wants legal assistance — especially when (a) party is poor/illiterate, (b) other side has brought a lawyer. Fairness demands no one be condemned without a proper hearing.
10.9 — Absence of Presenting Officer
👨‍⚖️ Enquiry Officer — Independent Adjudicator, NOT Prosecutor
📝 The Enquiry Officer in a disciplinary proceeding must be neutral — like a judge. If he starts acting like a prosecutor (trying to elicit evidence to punish the employee), his independence is compromised and the inquiry is vitiated.
ACCEPTABLE
Where statute is silent on Presenting Officer → Enquiry Officer may lead examination-in-chief of prosecution witness → ordinarily no exception (UOI v Ram Lakhan Sharma)
📝 This is the normal course when no one else is designated to present the management's case.
VITIATION
If Enquiry Officer acts in any other capacity + proceeds as if interested in eliciting evidence to PUNISH employee → bias becomes manifest → capacity as independent adjudicator lost → order set aside
📝 The moment the "judge" starts behaving like the "prosecutor" — the game is over. The proceedings are fatally flawed.
💡 GST: Adjudicating Officer drafting detailed SCN AND also adjudicating is acceptable. But if during personal hearing the officer appears to be gathering more evidence against assessee rather than hearing the defence — bias issue arises.
10.10 — Reasoned Decision (Speaking Order)
📝 Reasoned Decision — Principle of Natural Justice & Good Administration
📝 A speaking order = an order that speaks for itself — tells you WHY the decision was made. Courts require that adjudicatory orders must be supported by recorded reasons. An unreasoned order may be just — but it does not appear to be just.
RULE
Recording of reasons is a principle of natural justice. Every judicial / quasi-judicial order must be supported by reasons recorded in writing. Ensures transparency and fairness in decision-making. Affected person comes to know WHY his case was rejected.
📝 Absence of reasons in an appealable order deprives the appellate court of the advantage of examining the reasons — casts onerous responsibility on the appellate court to re-examine the entire matter on its own.
🎯 WHY Reasons Matter — Seven-Point Framework
1st
Safeguard against arbitrariness — compulsion to articulate reasoning curbs arbitrary exercise of power
2nd
Improves quality of decision-making — forces adjudicator to consider matter carefully; introduces clarity, objectivity, impartiality
3rd
Increases public confidence in administration — assures adversely affected parties that decision was not arbitrary and relevant points were considered
4th
Linked to right of equality (Art. 14) — fairness founded on reason is the essence of the right of equality (Government Branch Press v Belliappa)
5th
Enables effective appeal — without reasons, statutory right of appeal becomes nugatory. Failure to give reasons = depriving party of right of appeal (Summary orders = silence appeal provisions)
💡 GST: Non-speaking SCN / adjudication order — assessee cannot effectively appeal. Courts regularly quash unreasoned GST orders.
6th
Part of open government / right to know — giving of reasons is an aspect of right to knowing
7th
Facilitates judicial review — reviewing court can assess whether decision was on legally valid grounds. Absence of reasons may restrict court's capacity to review.
SUPREME COURT
"Reasoned decision is not only for the purpose of showing that the citizen is receiving justice, but also a valid discipline for the tribunal itself. Therefore, statement of reasons is one of the essentials of justice."
📝 Lord Denning in Breen: "The giving of reasons for a decision is one of the fundamentals of good administration. It constitutes a safeguard against arbitrariness."
⚠️ GST CRITICAL APPLICATION
SCN must be a speaking document — state reasons for invoking extended period (if applicable), basis of calculation, specific legal provision violated. Similarly, Adjudication Order must be a speaking order. Unreasoned orders / orders without DIN (Digital Invoice Number) → liable to be quashed by High Court in writ jurisdiction. Courts have consistently quashed non-speaking GST adjudication orders.
💡 Where Department issues SCN merely reproducing extracted statements without applying mind to specific transactions → non-speaking SCN → assessee can challenge in writ: "Failure to give reasons amounts, in substance, to depriving the affected party of the right of appeal or revision." (Supreme Court)
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 10 | Natural Justice: Hearing, Evidence, Counsel & Reasoned Orders
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, First Appellate Authority, and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Patel Engineering Ltd v UOI — Proportionality Doctrine
Supreme Court of India | AIR 2012 SC 2342 : (2012) 11 SCC 257 | Relying on Teri Oat Estates (P) Ltd v UT of Chandigarh
Key Ratio — Proportionality
"By proportionality, it is meant that the question whether, while regulating exercise of fundamental rights, the appropriate or least restrictive choice of measures has been made by the legislature or the administrator so as to achieve the object of the legislation or the administrative order, as the case may be. Under the principle, the Court will see that the legislature and the administrative authority maintain a proper balance between the adverse effects which the legislation or the administrative order may have on the rights, liberties or interests of persons keeping in mind the purpose which they were intended to serve."
Further Ratio — No Inviolable Rule of Oral Hearing
There is no inviolable rule that a personal hearing of the affected party must precede every decision of the State. A reasonable opportunity to explain its case is sufficient compliance with natural justice.
When to Use — (a) When Department imposes disproportionate penalty for minor GST default — argue proportionality; (b) When order passed without oral hearing — cite that written opportunity may suffice, but if complexity warrants oral hearing, Department must grant it.
Dhakeswari Cotton Mills Ltd v CIT — Refusal to Receive Evidence
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1955 SC 65 : 1955 (1) SCR 941
Key Ratio
Principles of natural justice were violated by the refusal of the Income Tax Appellate Tribunal to look into the account books produced by the taxpayer which he did not have the opportunity to produce earlier owing to reasons beyond his control.
When to Use — When Adjudicating Officer / Commissioner (Appeals) refuses to receive additional evidence (books, invoices, contracts) offered by assessee. Cite to establish that refusal to receive relevant evidence on behalf of affected person may amount to breach of natural justice, vitiating the decision.
ICDS Ltd v CIT — Right to Cross-Examine Witnesses in Tax Proceedings
Supreme Court of India | (2020) 10 SCC 29
Key Ratio
Denial of opportunity to cross-examine witnesses will offend the principle of natural justice. Cross-examination by the assessee of the witnesses on whose evidence the tax assessing officer was depending to hold the assessee's return to be inaccurate was compulsory. Denial by assessment officer to the tax payer an opportunity to cross-examine witnesses testifying against him, amounted to an infraction of the tax-payer's rights.
When to Use — When SCN / adjudication order is based on statements of third parties recorded under Section 70 CGST Act and Department refuses assessee's request to cross-examine those witnesses. This is the direct GST parallel — cite to demand cross-examination as a matter of right in such circumstances.
Kishanchand Chellaram v CIT — Letter / Document Used Without Disclosure
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1980 SC 2117 : 1980 Supp SCC 660
Key Ratio
Before the income tax authorities could rely upon the letter (from bank manager), they were bound to produce it before the assessee so that he could controvert the statements made therein by asking for an opportunity to cross-examine the manager with reference to his statement in the said letter.
When to Use — When Adjudicating Officer relies on third-party communication, statement, or intelligence report in the SCN/order but does not share it with assessee. Cite to demand: (a) copy of document relied upon; (b) opportunity to cross-examine author. Non-supply = violation of natural justice = order liable to be quashed.
K.L. Tripathi v State Bank of India — Cross-Examination Guidelines
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1984 SC 273 : (1984) 1 SCC 43 : 1984 (1) LLJ 2
Key Ratio / Guidelines
"If the credibility of a person who has testified or given some information is in doubt, or if the version of the statement of the person who has testified, is, in dispute, right of cross-examination must inevitably form part of fair play in action but where there is no lis regarding the facts but certain explanation of the circumstances there is no requirement of cross-examination to be fulfilled to justify fair play in action."
Further Ratio
"The party who does not want to controvert the veracity of the evidence from or testimony gathered behind his back cannot expect to succeed in any subsequent demand that there was no opportunity of cross-examination specifically when it was not asked for and there was no dispute about the veracity of the statements."
When to Use — Dual use: (a) FOR assessee — when you dispute the credibility/version of the person whose statement is relied upon by Revenue, cite first ratio to demand cross-examination as mandatory; (b) AGAINST Revenue — when Revenue relies on disputed third-party statements without providing cross-examination opportunity.
Board of Trustees of Port of Bombay v Dilipkumar — Legal Representation When Other Side Has Lawyer
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1983 SC 109 : (1983) 1 SCC 124 : 1983 (1) LLJ 1
Key Ratio
"In our view we have reached a stage in our onward march to fair play in action that where in an enquiry before a domestic tribunal the delinquent officer is pitted against a legally trained mind, if he seeks permission to appear through a legal practitioner the refusal to grant this request would amount to denial of a reasonable request to defend himself and the essential principles of natural justice would be violated."
Further Ratio
"The enquiry officer combines the judge and prosecutor rolled into one. Witnesses are generally employees of the employer who directs an enquiry into the misconduct. This is sufficient to raise serious apprehensions. Add to this uneven scales, the weight of legally trained minds on behalf of employer simultaneously denying that opportunity to delinquent employee. Justice must not only be done but must seem to be done."
When to Use — When Department's representative at personal hearing is a trained technical officer making complex legal arguments and assessee's request to appear through CA/advocate is denied. Cite to establish: (a) unequal contest; (b) denial of meaningful hearing; (c) request for adjournment to arrange representation.
Travancore Rayons v UOI — Complex Technical Questions Require Oral Hearing
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1971 SC 862 : (1969) 3 SCC 868
Key Ratio
Even though relevant rules do not require personal hearing in a matter, yet in appropriate cases "where complex and difficult questions requiring familiarity with technical problems are raised, if personal hearing is given, it would conduce to better administration and more satisfactory disposal of the grievances of the citizen." Government decided the matter without giving personal hearing — Supreme Court criticised this approach.
When to Use — When Adjudicating Officer disposes of matter without personal hearing in a complex GST classification / valuation / ITC dispute involving technical questions. Cite to demand personal hearing as a matter of fairness — and if denied, argue in appeal/writ that natural justice was violated.
Reasoned Decision Principle — Supreme Court & Lord Denning
State of WB v Krishna Shaw, AIR 1990 SC 2205 | Breen v AEU (1971) 2 QB 175 (Lord Denning)
Key Ratio — Supreme Court
"Reasoned decision is not only for the purpose of showing that the citizen is receiving justice, but also a valid discipline for the tribunal itself. Therefore, statement of reasons is one of the essentials of justice."
Further Ratio — Absence of Reasons Kills Right of Appeal
Failure to give reasons amounts, in substance, to depriving the affected party of the right of appeal or revision. Summary orders by lower authorities reduce the appeal provisions to silence.
Lord Denning in Breen
"The giving of reasons for a decision is one of the fundamentals of good administration. It constitutes a safeguard against arbitrariness on the part of the decision-maker."
When to Use — (a) When SCN does not give specific reasons for invoking extended period — non-speaking SCN, liable to be quashed; (b) When Adjudication Order / Appeal Order does not give reasons for rejecting submissions — non-speaking order, sets at naught the right of further appeal; (c) In writ petitions challenging cryptic/non-speaking GST orders.
Errington v Minister of Health — Evidence from One Side Without Notice to Other Side
Court of Appeal, England | (1935) 1 KB 249
Key Ratio
A quasi-judicial officer must not hear one side in the absence of the other. The confirmation order must be quashed because the Minister took into consideration evidence which was given ex parte without the owners having any opportunity to deal with the same. The Court emphasized that a quasi-judicial officer must not hear evidence from one side in the absence of the other side, and view the property in question without notice to the owners.
When to Use — When Department conducts inspection, survey, or collects information from buyers/suppliers/banks and uses it in SCN/adjudication without sharing it with assessee beforehand. Cite to establish: "information collected behind assessee's back and used against him = violation of natural justice = order non est."
Supreme Court — Condemned Unheard — Cardinal Principle
Supreme Court of India | Sangram Singh v Election Tribunal, AIR 1955 SC 425 : (1955) 2 SCR 1
Key Ratio
"Our laws of procedure are grounded on a principle of natural justice which requires that men should not be condemned unheard, that decisions should not be reached behind their backs, that proceedings that affect their lives and property should not continue in their absence and that they should not be precluded from participating in them ... our laws should be construed, wherever that is reasonably possible, in the light of that principle."
When to Use — The universal opening argument in any GST hearing where natural justice is in issue. Cite at the commencement of reply to SCN, and in appeal grounds. This ratio covers: (a) ex parte orders; (b) orders without notice; (c) orders based on undisclosed material; (d) denial of opportunity to present defence. Use as the foundational principle underpinning all other arguments.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter 11 — Rule Against Bias

⚖️ Chapter 11 — Rule Against Bias

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / No Bias
Prohibited / Vitiated / Bias Found
Condition / Test / Unless
Optional / Exception / Pass-on route
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
11.1 Introductory — Bias & Malice
11.1.1 Malice in Law

11.10 Waiver of Bias
11.11 Necessity Excludes Bias
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
11.1 — Introductory — Rule Against Bias & Malice
⚖️ Rule Against Bias — Second Limb of Natural Justice
Common Law Origin | Integral Part of Administrative Jurisprudence
📝 Natural Justice has two pillars: (1) Right to be Heard (audi alteram partem) and (2) Rule Against Bias (nemo judex in causa sua). This chapter covers the second pillar — no one shall be a judge in their own cause.
Fundamental Requirement: Doctrine of Natural Justice must be complied with
— an integral part of administrative jurisprudence in India
DEFINITION — BIAS
Bias = Pre-conceived opinion / pre-disposition / pre-determination to decide a case in a particular manner — such that the mind is not open to conviction.
In popular parlance, bias is included within "malice" (spite or ill-will).
📝 Bias is a condition of mind — a mental state that makes the judge/adjudicator unable to exercise impartiality. It sways judgment and pre-disposes the outcome even before evidence is considered.
What Needs to Be Proved for Bias to Vitiate Proceedings
NOT REQUIRED
Actual proof of bias
(bias is mental — extremely difficult to prove)
📝 You don't need to show the judge was actually biased inside his mind. That's impossible to prove.
REQUIRED
Cogent evidence that bias EXISTED which resulted in miscarriage of justice
SUFFICIENT
Real likelihood of bias OR Reasonable suspicion of bias
(from factual circumstances)
📝 Courts look at the APPEARANCE — not the reality. If a reasonable man would think bias exists, that is enough to quash the proceedings.
GST / ADMINISTRATIVE RELEVANCE
Adjudicating officers, Appellate Authorities, and GSTAT proceedings are all "adjudicatory" in nature — Rule Against Bias fully applies. Any officer who is a complainant, informant, investigator, or has financial interest cannot adjudicate the same matter. Order passed in such circumstances = coram non judice (void).
📝 Coram non judice = Before a person who is not a judge. The order is a nullity — as if it was never passed.
11.1.1 — Malice in Law
🔴 Malice in Law — Concept & Limits
MALICE IN FACT
Actual personal ill-will / spite / personal enmity
— Rare; requires direct evidence of hostility
📝 When the decision-maker personally hates you and acts out of that hatred. Very hard to prove — you need clear facts of personal animosity.
💡 Minister had filed criminal case against the petitioner — Revenue Minister then cancelled petitioner's mining licence. SC held: personal bias disqualified him (Mineral Development Ltd v State of Bihar).
MALICE IN LAW
Act done without lawful excuse, wrongfully and wilfully, without reasonable or probable cause — even without personal ill-will
= Exercise of statutory power for purposes foreign to those for which it was intended
📝 Even if the authority had no personal grudge against you — if it used its power to hit you, not to serve public interest — that is legal malice. The State must always act fairly.
💡 Municipal Council President removed for minor charge + explanation acceptable → SC found politically motivated removal = legal malice (Ravi Yashwant Bhoir v District Collector, Raigad).
KEY PRINCIPLES — LEGAL MALICE
(i) Act must be in bad faith — power exercised not to protect regulated entities/public BUT to hit those who form the target
(ii) Done wrongfully and wilfully without reasonable and probable cause
(iii) Mala fide exercise of power does NOT require moral turpitude
(iv) "Conscious violation of law to the prejudice of another" = malice in law
LIMITS — WHAT IS NOT MALICE IN LAW
(i) Mere procedural irregularities / mistakes — action can remain bona fide
(ii) Order found untenable in law ≠ malafide
(iii) Suspicion (however strong) ≠ proof of mala fide
(iv) Multi-layered decision-making with discordant notes among officers = normal, not malafide
(v) Advisory opinions by technical experts → recommendatory only; final decision by owner = not malafide
📝 Just because the government made a mistake or took a harsh decision does not mean it was acting in bad faith. Clear proof of a high degree is required to strike down on malafide grounds.
GST APPLICATION
SCN issued with pre-determined conclusion / officer who investigated also adjudicates / order passed ignoring explanation entirely → Grounds for challenge on Malice in Law / Legal Malice in SCN reply and Writ Petition.
11.2 — Pecuniary Bias
💰 Pecuniary Bias — Any Financial Interest = Automatic Disqualification
📝 If the decision-maker has any financial stake in the outcome — however small or indirect — he is automatically disqualified. No need to prove actual bias. The rule is absolute.
ABSOLUTE RULE
A direct pecuniary interest, howsoever small or insignificant, disqualifies a person from acting as a decision-maker in a dispute.
"Any direct pecuniary interest, however small, in the subject-matter of inquiry will disqualify an adjudicator." — Supreme Court
DIMES CASE (UK)
Lord Chancellor = shareholder in company that was party before him
→ Decision QUASHED even though no actual bias suspected
📝 The Lord Chancellor himself may have had no bias — but the maxim "no man judge in his own cause" is sacrosanct. Even Lord Chancellors must step aside.
J. MOHAPATRA (SC)
Committee members who were authors of books selected those books for school libraries
→ Author = financial interest (royalty) → Disqualified
Even if only one of many members — subtle influence on others
💡 A GST officer who has a business interest in the taxpayer's competitor segment — however remote — should recuse from adjudication. Financial interest = automatic disqualification.
TRANSPORT PERMIT
Regional Transport Authority member granted permit to one of its own members
→ High Court cancelled permit — authority was biased
IPSO FACTO RULE
A pecuniary or proprietary interest in the subject matter of the dispute ipso facto disqualifies the adjudicator. It is NOT necessary to prove that there was a real likelihood of bias in the circumstances of the case.
Ipso facto = by that very fact itself — automatically.
📝 Unlike other forms of bias where you have to show likelihood — with pecuniary bias, the interest itself IS the disqualification. Period. No further analysis needed.
11.3 — Personal Bias — Types, Situations & Cases
👤 Personal Bias — Relationship, Friendship, Animosity, Prior Involvement
📝 Personal bias arises when the decision-maker has a personal connection with a party — as a friend, enemy, relative, accuser, or prior participant in the same dispute. All such connections create a real likelihood that the adjudicator cannot be impartial.
Circumstances That Create Personal Bias
(i)
Decision-maker is a FRIEND or RELATIVE of a party
(ii)
Decision-maker has personal ANIMOSITY or hostility against a party
(iii)
Decision-maker is the ACCUSER — presides over meeting to discipline person against whom he made accusations
(iv)
Decision-maker is a RELATION of a party to the dispute
(v)
Decision-maker was INVOLVED earlier in same dispute in different capacity
Landmark Cases on Personal Bias
Mineral Development Ltd v State of Bihar (SC 1960)
Revenue Minister cancelled petitioner's mining licence → Minister had filed criminal case against petitioner (political rivalry) → SC: personal bias disqualified Minister from cancelling licence
A.K. Kraipak v UOI (SC 1970) — LANDMARK
Acting Conservator of Forests was himself a candidate + member of Selection Board → Board selected him → SC QUASHED: "At every stage of his participation, there would be a conflict between his interest and duty" → Bias of one member INFECTS the whole group
📝 Even if the biased member did not vote on his own case — his presence in the group influences others subtly. The group decision is tainted as a whole.
ICAI v L.K. Ratna (SC 1987)
Disciplinary Committee (President + VP + 3 members) investigated member → Same President & VP participated in Council Meeting when report was considered
→ SC: Members of Disciplinary Committee DISQUALIFIED from Council meeting considering their own report
→ Justice must not only be done but APPEAR to be done
💡 GST equivalent: If a DGGSTI officer investigated the case, he cannot also participate in the adjudication or review of the same case as part of any committee. Same principle from Ratna applies.
Ranjit Thakur v UOI (SC 1987)
CO against whom appellant had complained = member of court-martial that tried appellant
→ SC: "A judgment which is result of bias or want of impartiality is a nullity and the trial 'coram non judice'"
→ Test: "Proper approach for Judge is NOT to ask 'am I biased?' but to look at mind of party before him"
GST ADJUDICATION CONTEXT
(i) Officer who issued SCN cannot be the adjudicating authority for the same SCN
(ii) Officer who conducted audit/investigation cannot adjudicate on findings of that audit
(iii) Officer who was complainant/informant cannot be inquiry/adjudicating officer
These situations create personal bias — orders passed = vitiated
11.3A — Tests for Bias: Real Likelihood vs Reasonable Suspicion
🔍 Two Tests for Determining Bias
📝 Since actual bias cannot be proved (it is a mental state), courts have developed two tests to identify whether bias should be presumed. Both tests look at outward appearances and probability — not the judge's actual thoughts.
TEST 1: REAL LIKELIHOOD OF BIAS
(Lord Denning in Lannon | SC in Kraipak, Ashok Kumar Yadav)
Question: Would a REASONABLE MAN, looking at the facts from outside, think it likely or probable that the adjudicator was biased?
Court looks at IMPRESSION given to other people — not at judge's own mind
Even if judge is impartial — if right-minded persons think there is real likelihood → he must not sit
📝 "Right-minded" = Reasonable, balanced person with general knowledge. Not the paranoid litigant. Not the cold reviewing court.
TEST 2: REASONABLE SUSPICION OF BIAS
(De Smith | Some English Cases)
Question: Would a reasonable person SUSPECT bias from outward appearances?
Looks mainly to OUTWARD APPEARANCES — broader and less stringent than Real Likelihood test
DIFFERENCE
Real Likelihood = Court's own evaluation of probabilities
Reasonable Suspicion = Focus on outward appearance
In practice — both often lead to same result
SUPREME COURT — S. Parthasarathi v State of AP (1974)
"The tests of 'real likelihood' and 'reasonable suspicion' are really inconsistent with each other. The reviewing authority must determine whether a reasonable man would in the circumstances infer that there is real likelihood of bias. The Court must look at the impression which other people have... surmise or conjecture would not be enough. There must exist circumstances from which reasonable men would think it probable or likely that the inquiring officer will be prejudiced against the delinquent."
Cross LJ — Hannam v Bradford Corporation
"If a reasonable person who has no knowledge of the matter beyond knowledge of the relationship which subsists between some members of the tribunal and one of the parties would think that there might well be bias — then there is in his opinion a real likelihood of bias. The question is NOT whether the tribunal will in fact be biased, but whether a reasonable man with no inside knowledge might well think that it might be biased."
📝 Key: The test is from the perspective of an OUTSIDER reasonable man — not the insider who knows all members are honest, and not the litigant who is subjectively suspicious of everything.
GST HEARING APPLICATION
When challenging bias before GSTAT / High Court: Use "Real Likelihood" test — argue from the perspective of a right-minded reasonable person looking at the facts. The officer's honest belief in his own impartiality is irrelevant. What matters is the APPEARANCE of impartiality.
11.4 — Departmental / Official / Policy Bias
🏛️ Departmental / Official / Policy Bias
When Administrator Acts as Adjudicator in His Own Department's Dispute
📝 In many adjudicatory proceedings, the government/department is itself a party. Administrators who formulate and implement departmental policies inevitably develop a pre-disposition (policy bias) towards those policies. This creates an inherent conflict when they adjudicate disputes involving those same policies.
WHY POLICY BIAS IS INHERENT
Officials = Part and parcel of administrative department → Interested in pursuing & implementing departmental policies → They imbibe (consciously or unconsciously) pre-disposition vis-à-vis policies they administer → Cannot develop neutrality/objectivity of a judge
GENERAL RULE
Courts do NOT ordinarily treat mere policy or official bias as an infirmity
Officials not disqualified merely because their department is a party
(Otherwise entire adjudicatory system collapses)
📝 The law accepts that some policy bias is inevitable and tolerable — otherwise there would be no adjudication by government at all.
EXCEPTION — DISQUALIFICATION
Official BECOMES disqualified if:
→ He personally IDENTIFIED HIMSELF TOO MUCH with formulation of the policy
→ He exhibited ABNORMAL DESIRE to uphold/implement that specific policy
→ His mind is CLOSED to issues arising before him
→ Only BLATANT or VERY STRONG policy bias = disqualification
📝 Think of it as a spectrum: routine departmental loyalty = acceptable; being the actual author of the scheme you are adjudicating = unacceptable.
Key Comparison: Gullapalli-I (India) vs Franklin (UK)
FactorFranklin v Minister (UK — 1948)Gullapalli-I v APSRTC (SC — 1959)
Who heard objections?Independent Inspector (no policy bias)Secretary of Transport Dept (integral part of dept)
Was hearing function assigned by statute specifically?Yes — Minister's function by statute; cannot be reassignedNo — could have been assigned to any competent official
ResultPolicy bias tolerated — minister's function upheldSecretary DISQUALIFIED — policy bias found, hearing invalid
PrincipleIf statute compels the biased official = tolerated. If function could have been reassigned = must separate policy-making from adjudication.
GST APPLICATION
Joint Commissioner who formulated audit objections / issued SCN being asked to adjudicate those objections = Gullapalli situation. The function could have been assigned to another officer. Raise policy/official bias before GSTAT / High Court in Writ under Article 226.
11.4.1 — Combining Functions of Prosecutor and Judge
🚫 No Person Can Be Both Prosecutor AND Judge in the Same Matter
📝 This is the core of "nemo judex in causa sua." Being the accuser and the judge in the same matter is fundamentally incompatible with fair adjudication. It creates a conflict of interest that taints the entire proceeding.
FACTORY MANAGER
Manager conducts inquiry against workmen who allegedly assaulted him
→ He is accuser AND judge
→ Disqualified
STATE v NOOH (SC 1958)
Inquiry officer gave evidence against employee → resumed inquiry → passed dismissal order
→ SC QUASHED: Inquiry officer acted both as witness and judge
DCCS CASE — Arjun Chaubey (SC)
Deputy Chief Commercial Superintendent (DCCS) = charges related to conduct towards DCCS himself → DCCS decided the case
→ SC: "No person can be a judge in his own cause"
→ Dismissal quashed — "illegality so patent and loudly obtrusive"
APPELLATE BODY CASE
Chairman took original decision of dismissal → same Chairman participated as ex-officio member of Board hearing employee's appeal
→ Bias found: "Chairman's original order had great play in ultimate decision"
BAIDYANATH MAHAPTRA (SC 1989)
G = Chief Secretary → Administrative decision to compulsorily retire B → G later became Chairman of Administrative Tribunal → B challenged retirement before same Tribunal → G as Chairman participated in Tribunal hearing
SC: G "acted as judge of his own cause" — Tribunal's order vitiated
Though bona fide, principles of natural justice, fair play and judicial discipline required G to abstain from hearing B's case.
📝 Even if the officer is honest and bona fide — if he participated in the original administrative decision and then adjudicates the challenge to that decision = automatic disqualification. Good faith is irrelevant.
CRITICAL GST PRINCIPLE
(i) Officer who issued SCN cannot adjudicate the same SCN
(ii) Officer who passed provisional attachment cannot be part of decision-making on that attachment
(iii) Officer who recommended prosecution cannot also adjudicate the tax liability
All these violate the principle against combining prosecutor and judge roles.
11.4.2 — Combining Informant and Investigator in Same Person
🔎 Can the Person Who Framed Charges Also Be the Inquiry Officer?
📝 This is a grey area — different SC decisions have reached different conclusions. The correct principle: if the person who framed charges also ADVISED for holding the inquiry and was COMMITTED to the outcome, he should not conduct the inquiry. The test is real likelihood of bias from a reasonable man's perspective.
PARTHASARATHI (SC 1974)
Person who framed charges against civil servant = inquiry officer
→ SC held: WRONG — creates real likelihood of bias
📝 The charge-framing officer would be interested in substantiating those charges and proving his own initial judgment was correct.
SUNIL KUMAR (SC 1980)
Inquiry officer had earlier drafted charges AND advised for holding inquiry
→ SC held: Not necessarily acting as prosecutor + judge
(Debatable ruling — criticized in text)
📝 This ruling is considered incorrect. If the officer not only framed charges but also ADVISED that inquiry should be held — his commitment to the outcome is deeper. Real likelihood test would point to only one result.
CORRECT PRINCIPLE
(i) Person who merely frames charges on basis of evidence = possibly acceptable (like a magistrate)
(ii) Person who ADVISED for holding inquiry + framed charges = associated with idea of inquiry → his commitment to result is MORE DIRECT AND PERSONAL → real likelihood of bias exists
(iii) Best practice: Disciplinary authority draws charges → entrusts inquiry to completely independent person having no association with the matter
GST RELEVANCE
Anti-evasion / DGGSTI officer who identified and recommended the case for investigation should NOT be the adjudicating officer for the resultant SCN. His association with framing the case creates the very commitment that the real likelihood test identifies as problematic.
11.4.3 — Prior Utterances and Pre-judgment of Issues
📣 Prior Statements / Declarations = Pre-judgment = Bias
📝 When a decision-maker has already publicly declared his mind on the very issue he must adjudicate, he has pre-judged it. No amount of subsequent hearing can cure this — the mind is already closed. The purpose of hearing (to inform the adjudicator's open mind) is defeated.
Franklin v Minister of Town and Country Planning (UK HL)
Minister declared at public meeting: "It is no good jeering — it is going to be done" (re: designating Stevenage as new town) BEFORE hearing objections
→ Argument: Mind already made up → disqualified from hearing
→ House of Lords: Administrative function — no bias rule applies (questionable — "low-water mark of administrative law")
📝 The Franklin House of Lords decision is controversial and criticized. In India — with the Doctrine of Fairness now applicable to all administrative functions — prior utterances showing closed mind = real likelihood of bias even in administrative functions.
PRINCIPLE APPLICABLE IN INDIA
If a hearing officer has publicly committed to a policy / scheme / outcome BEFORE hearing objections → his mind is closed → purpose of hearing is frustrated → a reasonable man would see real likelihood of bias
Hearing = must be before person with OPEN MIND, no preconceived notions
GST RELEVANCE
If adjudicating officer had publicly stated in press statements / earlier orders / communications that the assessee has evaded tax / committed fraud — BEFORE the adjudication order — this constitutes pre-judgment. Use as ground in Reply to SCN and in Writ Petition.
💡 In ITC Ltd v UOI, the Director of Directorate of Anti-Evasion issued SCN + made press statement pre-adjudication + stood to get reward under reward scheme → Calcutta HC examined bias arguments on all three grounds.
11.5 — Bias on Account of Judicial Obstinacy
🔁 Bias by Prior Decision — Can the Same Person Decide Again After Remand?
📝 When a court sets aside an order for violation of natural justice and remands the matter, should the same officer/authority re-decide? There is a real risk that the officer will be biased in favour of his earlier decision — trying to vindicate himself.
KERALA HC VIEW
"It is not to be presumed that a person who has once decided a matter without due hearing would have such a bias in favour of his decision as not to be capable of reaching a fair decision after due hearing"
→ Same person can decide again
📝 This view is criticized as doubtful — the natural inclination is always to vindicate one's earlier conclusion.
RATTAN LAL SHARMA (SC)
Managing committee member who was biased in first inquiry → SC: fresh inquiry directed with NEW members
No member of old inquiry committee to be on new inquiry committee
→ Real likelihood that prior decision-maker will vindicate his earlier conclusion
CORRECT APPROACH
Where first decision was vitiated by bias → remand should be to a FRESH AUTHORITY with NO prior association with the matter. The natural inclination to vindicate earlier conclusions = real likelihood of bias in a reasonable person's assessment.
📝 P.V. Narasimharao v State (CBI/SPE): Even where apprehension of bias was baseless — trial transferred to another officer on principle that justice should not only be done but APPEAR to be done.
GST RELEVANCE
When SCN adjudication order is set aside by GSTAT/Court → specifically pray for adjudication by a DIFFERENT officer. Cite principle that same officer who originally adjudicated should not re-adjudicate — real likelihood of vindication of his earlier view.
11.6 — Group Decision — Bias of One Member Infects the Whole
👥 Group Decision Vitiation — One Biased Member = Entire Group Infected
📝 When a committee/board/panel takes a decision collectively, and even ONE member has a bias, the entire group decision is tainted. It is difficult — and impossible to prove — how the biased member subtly influenced the others during deliberations.
A.K. Kraipak v UOI (SC 1970) — on Group Decision
Acting Conservator was a candidate + member of Selection Board → Did not participate when his own name was being considered → Other members filed affidavits that THEY were not biased by his presence
→ SC: QUASHED SELECTIONS
"The bias of one member in a group would infect the whole group. In a group decision, it is difficult to say how one biased member may influence in a subtle manner the decision of other members."
📝 Even if the biased member sits silent while his own case is discussed — his presence in the room, his earlier participation, his relationships with other members — all of this subtly shapes the group's thinking. That contamination is impossible to isolate.
ADDITIONAL SITUATIONS
(i) Father of candidate was secretary of managing committee that selected her as teacher → became member of committee that confirmed her selection → SC: Flagrant violation of natural justice
(ii) Member of tribunal with pending promotion case participated in decision affecting another promotee = suspicion of undue favour
(iii) Independent members' bona fide + honesty "not sufficient to save the situation" when one biased member's presence infects the group
GST COMMITTEE PROCEEDINGS
If a Review Committee / Approval Committee / Committee under Section 75 CGST Act includes a member who has prior association with the case (e.g., original adjudicator, or complainant) — the entire committee's decision is vitiated. The Kraipak principle applies fully.
💡 Rattan Lal Sharma ruling: Even where dismissal order was set aside due to bias of ONE member of inquiry committee — fresh inquiry committee must be constituted from entirely different members. Old members cannot serve even in a supporting role.
11.7 — Actual Bias vs Apparent Bias
🎭 Actual Bias vs Apparent (Real Likelihood) Bias
ACTUAL BIAS
Decision-maker was in fact biased → sufficient to vitiate
But: Actual bias is extremely difficult to prove
(Bias = state of mind = internal)
📝 Proving actual bias is like proving what someone was thinking. Courts recognize this impossibility and have created the broader "real likelihood" test.
REAL LIKELIHOOD / APPARENT BIAS
Even without actual bias — proceedings vitiated if real likelihood of bias exists
Bias is "situational" or "circumstantial" — not the "reality" but the "appearance" that matters
📝 A judge from inside may know there was no bias. A reasonable man looking from outside may still feel justice was not done. The law accepts the reasonable man's view over the insider's claim.
RANJIT THAKUR FORMULATION (SC)
"Bias is 'situational' or 'circumstantial.' It is not the 'reality' of the matter, but the 'appearance' which is important. A judge knowing the facts from inside may feel that there was no bias in the decision-maker, but a reasonable man looking at the same facts externally may still feel that justice was not done in the instant case."
📝 Actual bias gives place to the broader test of "real likelihood of bias." Needless to say — where actual bias exists, administrative proceedings will be vitiated. But actual bias is NOT always necessary. Even real likelihood suffices.
NEMO JUDEX IN CAUSA SUA
"No man shall be a judge in his own cause"
Justice should not only be done but should MANIFESTLY BE SEEN TO BE DONE
Applies to: Judicial proceedings + Adjudicatory proceedings (including GST adjudication)
📝 Nemo judex in causa sua = Latin: No person [shall be] a judge in his own cause. This is the foundational principle — it is not merely a rule but a cornerstone of the rule of law.
11.9 — Statute May Exclude Bias
📜 Can a Statute Override the Rule Against Bias?
📝 Sometimes Parliament specifically empowers a particular officer — even one with policy bias — to adjudicate. When the statute itself creates the overlap between functions, the rule against bias may be impliedly excluded. But this only works where the statute COMPELS the overlap — not where the authority merely PERMITS the appointment of an independent officer.
WHEN STATUTE EXCLUDES
Statute specifically vests function in the very officer who has the bias → No alternative available → Bias rule impliedly excluded
(e.g., Minister alone can confirm new town under statute — Franklin case)
📝 If Parliament says "the Minister SHALL decide" — no one else can. So the minister's policy bias cannot disqualify him. The statute itself has accepted this overlap.
WHEN STATUTE DOES NOT EXCLUDE
Statute merely permits appointment of any competent authority → Function could have been assigned to independent officer → No legal compulsion for biased officer to adjudicate → Bias rule APPLIES
(e.g., Gullapalli — Secretary of Transport not compelled by statute)
RATNA PRINCIPLE — ICAI Case
"The objection on the ground of bias would have been excluded had the statute expressed itself to the contrary, but the Act makes no such exclusion" → Therefore bias principle applies → Members of Disciplinary Committee disqualified from Council meeting considering their own report
