📝 If the CGST Act had specifically said "the officer who issued the SCN shall adjudicate" — maybe statute exclusion could be argued. It doesn't. So the bias rule applies fully to GST adjudication.
11.10 — Waiver of Bias
✋ Waiver — Failure to Object at First Opportunity = Bias Waived
📝 If a party knows about the bias and yet continues to participate in the proceedings without objecting — the court may hold that the party has waived its right to object. You must raise the objection AS SOON AS you become aware of it.
RULE — PROMPT OBJECTION MANDATORY
Allegation of bias raised for the FIRST TIME in reply to show-cause notice issued by High Court → Held: AFTERTHOUGHT — to get over the report of the inquiry officer
Bias plea must be raised at inception of inquiry, not as an afterthought after an adverse result
WAIVER FOUND
Baidyanath Mahaptra: Appellant did NOT raise objection before Tribunal against G's participation → Court refused relief on this ground (waiver)
WAIVER FOUND
G. Sarana: Petitioner did not raise bias objection at selection stage → Court refused relief
NO WAIVER
Where bias plea is based on "admitted and uncontroverted facts" → goes to ROOT of question → need not be raised during inquiry; raising in High Court is sufficient
PRACTICAL RULE
(i) Know about the bias? → Object IMMEDIATELY — at first hearing / at inception of inquiry
(ii) Bias discovered later? → Object as soon as discovered — with an explanation of when you came to know
(iii) Bias based on documents / admitted facts → can be raised even in writ petition before High Court
📝 In GST adjudication: File a written objection against the adjudicating officer's bias at the very first hearing. Document it. Do not wait for the order — by then waiver may apply.
💡 If you discover that the adjudicating officer was the investigating officer of your case — file a written representation objecting to his adjudicating this matter, immediately, in the first personal hearing. Keep copy. This preserves the bias ground for appeal.
11.11 — Necessity Excludes Bias
⚙️ Necessity — When No Alternative Exists, Bias Cannot Disqualify
📝 If the only person legally empowered to take a decision has a bias — and there is genuinely no one else who can legally substitute — the doctrine of necessity kicks in. The biased person must decide, otherwise there would be no decision at all. But this is an exception of last resort, not a general rule.
PSC / UPSC EXAMPLE
If chairman or member of Public Service Commission has a close relative appearing for selection → must recuse → BUT if no other member available → PSC functioning would be affected → Necessity may apply → Bias rule relaxed
📝 A constitutional authority like PSC/UPSC cannot simply stop functioning because a member has a relative as candidate. If no substitute is possible, necessity doctrine saves the decision.
RATNA — NECESSITY REJECTED
ICAI disciplinary committee: SC rejected necessity argument because statute did NOT compel President/VP to be on committee → Rule could have been amended → Necessity not available when alternative is possible
📝 Necessity is truly a last resort. If the law merely permits (rather than compels) a biased officer to act — necessity does not apply and the bias rule stands.
CONDITIONS FOR NECESSITY DOCTRINE
(i) Genuinely NO OTHER person legally competent to decide
(ii) Statute COMPELS the particular officer to decide (not merely permits)
(iii) No possibility of amendment or reassignment
(iv) Absence of decision would be worse than biased decision
All four conditions must be met — not a routine escape hatch
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 11 | Rule Against Bias
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, First Appellate Authority, and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
A.K. Kraipak v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1970 SC 150 : (1969) 2 SCC 262 | Landmark on Rule Against Bias in Administrative Law
Key Ratio — Test of Bias
"The real question is not whether he was biased — because it is difficult to prove the state of mind of a person — but whether there is reasonable ground for believing that he was likely to have been biased. There must be a reasonable likelihood of bias. In deciding the question of bias, we have to take into consideration, human probabilities and ordinary course of human conduct."
Key Ratio — Group Decision
"The bias of one member in a group would infect the whole group. In a group decision, it is difficult to say how one biased member may influence in a subtle manner the decision of other members."
When to Use — When challenging GST adjudication/appellate order where the adjudicating officer had any prior association with the case (investigated, recommended, or was involved in issuing SCN). Cite to establish reasonable likelihood of bias without needing to prove actual bias.
Metropolitan Properties Co (FGC) Ltd v Lannon
Court of Appeal, UK | (1968) 3 All ER 304 | Lord Denning MR | Adopted by Supreme Court of India in multiple decisions
Key Ratio — Real Likelihood Test
"The court looks at the impression which would be given to other people. Even if he was as impartial as could be, nevertheless, if right-minded persons would think that, in the circumstances, there was a 'real likelihood' of bias on his part, then he should not sit. And if he does sit, his decision cannot stand. There must appear to be a 'real likelihood' of bias; mere surmise or conjecture is not enough."
When to Use — The universally cited test for establishing bias. Cite Lord Denning in Lannon when arguing before GSTAT / High Court that the adjudicating officer should not have decided the matter. The officer's own belief in his impartiality is irrelevant — what a right-minded person would think is the test.
Ranjit Thakur v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1987 SC 2386 : (1987) 4 SCC 611
Key Ratio — Nullity + Appearance Test
"A judgment which is the result of bias or want of impartiality is a nullity and the trial 'coram non judice'. As to the test of the likelihood of bias — what is relevant is the reasonableness of the apprehension in that regard in the mind of the party. The proper approach for the Judge is not to look at his own mind and ask himself, however honestly, 'am I biased?' but to look at the mind of the party before him."
Ratio on Bias Being Situational
"Bias is 'situational' or 'circumstantial.' It is not the 'reality' of the matter, but the 'appearance' which is important."
When to Use — When arguing that a GST adjudication order is a nullity (coram non judice) because the officer was biased. Emphasize that the order has no legal existence and cannot be enforced — stronger than arguing it is merely voidable.
Institute of Chartered Accountants of India v L.K. Ratna
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1987 SC 71 : (1986) 4 SCC 537 | Critical for Professional Disciplinary / Quasi-Judicial Bodies
Key Ratio — Appearance of Justice
"The council discharges a quasi-judicial function and the fundamental principle is that 'justice must not only be done but must also appear to be done.' To the member whose conduct has been investigated by the disciplinary committee, the possibility of the council disagreeing with the report of the disciplinary committee, in the presence of the President, Vice-President and other members of the disciplinary committee, would be rather remote."
When to Use — When challenging before GSTAT / High Court any multi-member committee / appellate body that included a member who had prior involvement in the same matter (as investigator, SCN-issuer, or original adjudicator). The Ratna principle = same persons cannot investigate AND adjudicate.
Ashok Kumar Yadav v State of Haryana
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1987 SC 454 : (1985) 4 SCC 417
Key Ratio — No Judge in Own Cause
"A fundamental principle of Administrative Law is that no one can be a judge in his own cause. The question is not whether the judge is actually biased, but whether there is a real likelihood of bias. What is objectionable in such a case, is not that the decision is actually tainted with bias, but that the circumstances are such as to create a reasonable apprehension in the mind of others, that there is a likelihood of bias affecting the decision."
On AK Kraipak
"AK Kraipak case is a landmark in the development of administrative law and it has contributed in a large measure to the strengthening of the rule of law in this country. Bias arises in varied situations. No person can be an advocate for or against a person in one proceeding, and at the same time sit as a judge in another proceeding to which that very person is also a party."
When to Use — When the GST officer who issued the SCN / conducted audit / filed complaint also adjudicates. The fundamental principle that no one is a judge in his own cause is a CONSTITUTIONAL principle — cannot be excluded by departmental convenience.
Rattan Lal Sharma v Managing Committee (SC 1993)
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1993 SC 2155 : (1993) 4 SCC 10 | On Bias of One Member Tainting Group + No Vindication in Re-inquiry
Key Ratio
"The test is not whether in fact a bias has affected the judgment — the test always is and must be whether a litigant could reasonably apprehend that a bias attributable to a member of the tribunal might have operated against him in the final decision of the tribunal. It is in this sense that it is often said that justice must not only be done but also appear to be done."
When to Use — When arguing from the perspective of the litigant (taxpayer) — would a reasonable taxpayer apprehend that the officer who investigated and then adjudicated was biased? This test validates the taxpayer's apprehension as legally relevant, not merely subjective suspicion.
Gullapalli Nageswara Rao v APSRTC (SC 1959) — Gullapalli-I
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1959 SC 308 : 1959 Supp (1) SCR 319 | Landmark on Policy Bias in Adjudication
Key Ratio — Separation of Policy-Making and Adjudication
"The Secretary of the Transport Department was disqualified to hear objections against the proposed scheme, as he was an integral part of the department concerned and therefore he was too much involved in the making of the scheme in question. He could not, therefore, have an open mind to hear objections against the scheme in question. The functions of policy-making and hearing of objections against the policy be separated."
When to Use — In challenges where the officer who participated in formulating departmental policy / circulars / investigation standards is also the officer adjudicating cases under those very standards. Policy bias disqualifies — if the function could have been assigned to another officer.
Ravi Yashwant Bhoir v District Collector, Raigad (SC 2012) — On Legal Malice
Supreme Court of India | AIR 2012 SC 1339 : (2012) 4 SCC 407
Key Ratio — Legal Malice in Administrative Action
"To constitute malice in law, the act must have been done in bad faith and the power must have been exercised not with the object of protecting the regulated entities or the public in general, but with the object of hitting those who form the target. The act must have been done wrongfully and wilfully, without reasonable and probable cause."
When to Use — When the GST SCN / demand order appears motivated by factors other than genuine tax recovery (e.g., targeting a specific taxpayer, acting on competitor's complaints, pre-determined conclusions). Cite to establish that the administrative action is vitiated by legal malice even without proving personal ill-will.
Nemo Judex in Causa Sua — Core Principle
Fundamental Principle of Natural Justice | Applied across all administrative proceedings including GST | Affirmed in J. Mohapatra (SC 1984), Kraipak (SC 1970), Arjun Chaubey (SC 1984)
The Foundational Principle
"Nemo judex in causa sua — no man shall be a judge in his own cause — is a principle firmly established in law. Justice should not only be done but should manifestly be seen to be done. Justice can never be seen to be done if a man acts as a judge in his own cause or is himself interested in its outcome."
Practical Application
"No person can be an advocate for or against a person in one proceeding, and at the same time sit as a judge in another proceeding to which that very person is also a party. A lawyer ought not to sit as an adjudicator in a case in which one of his clients is a party. A person should not act both as a judge and a witness, or as a judge and a prosecutor, or as a complainant and a judge at the same time."
When to Use — As the opening principle in any bias challenge. Cite at the very beginning of your written submission / writ petition. Establishes the constitutional dimension of the rule — not merely procedural but goes to root of jurisdiction.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter XI — Rule Against Bias

⚖️ Chapter XI — Rule Against Bias

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Bias Found
Condition / Test / Unless
Exception / Escape Route
Neutral / Principle
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
11.3 Policy Bias — Speaker Case
11.4.1 Combining Prosecutor & Judge
11.4.2 Informant & Investigator — Same Person
11.4.3 Prior Utterances & Pre-judgment
11.5 Judicial Obstinacy as Bias
11.6 Group Decision
11.7 Actual vs Apparent Bias
11.8 Initial Recommendation
11.9 Statute May Exclude Bias
11.9.1 Institutional Bias — CESTAT
11.10 Waiver of Bias Objection
11.11 Necessity Excludes Bias
11.12 Postscript — R v Gough Test
11.13 Natural Justice — History & Elaboration
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
11.3 — Policy Bias | Speaker's Role | Kihota Hollohon v. Zachilhu
⚖️ Policy Bias — Speaker's Adjudicatory Role
Kihota Hollohon v Zachilhu | AIR 1993 SC 412 | Schedule X — Anti-Defection
📝 Policy bias occurs when the person deciding a case has already formed a view on the policy behind it — not because of personal gain, but because of their institutional role. The Speaker deciding anti-defection cases is the classic Indian example.
CONTEXT
Schedule X of Constitution vests power to decide disqualification on grounds of defection in the Speaker of the House
MAJORITY VIEW (3:2)
Speaker's office is one of dignity & impartiality; held in highest respect in parliamentary traditions. He is guardian of rights of House and expected to decide impartially, objectively and non-partisan manner.
📝 The majority said: just because some Speakers acted badly doesn't mean we distrust the office itself.
Held: Vesting adjudicatory power in the Speaker is NOT exceptionable
"It is inappropriate to express distrust in the high office of the Speaker merely because some Speakers are alleged to have discharged functions not in keeping with traditions."
MINORITY VIEW (Preferred)
Speaker depends on majority party support; remains member of political party — cannot be free from political pressure. Decision made 'final' with no appeal to outside agency.
📝 The minority said: if there's no appeal and the Speaker owes his seat to the majority party, there's a built-in structural bias — you can't avoid it however honourable the person.
"The Speaker being an authority within the House and his tenure being dependent on the will of the majority therein, likelihood of suspicion of bias could not be ruled out."
PRAGMATIC CRITIQUE
Majority decision based on abstract & theoretical considerations; not on pragmatic Indian realities. In practice, speakers' decisions are invariably challenged and several have been quashed by courts. Minority view is preferable.
REFORM PRINCIPLE
Policy bias remains nebulous in India. Courts reluctant to apply it widely. Best solution: Keep roles of policy-maker and decision-maker separate. Appoint independent hearing officers with no policy-making involvement (as some States did post Gullapalli I).
11.4.1 — Combining Functions of Prosecutor and Judge
⚖️ Combining Prosecutor + Judge — When Is It Bias?
Hari v Dy. Commissioner of Police | AIR 1956 SC 559
📝 When the same person who accuses you also judges you, there is obvious bias. But what if two different officers in the same department — one prosecutes, one judges? Courts have drawn a careful line here.
✗ BIAS — CLEAR CASE
Same official discharges both prosecuting and adjudicating functions in the same matter → automatically biased unless clearly permitted by legislation
💡 GST analogy: If the same officer who drafted the SCN also decides the adjudication — bias is inherent. Challenge lies.
✓ VALID — DIFFERENT OFFICERS
If person adjudicating is different from person prosecuting → proceedings NOT flawed on ground of bias merely because both functions discharged within the same department
📝 Two officers in the same Income Tax / GST department — one investigates, another adjudicates — this is valid. The department's internal separation is enough.
HARI CASE — SC PRINCIPLE
Evidence collected by lower-rank police officer; externment order passed only by Commissioner of Police / District Magistrate (higher rank). Hence, satisfying authority ≠ prosecuting authority → No bias
DEPARTMENTAL PROCEEDINGS — DAILY REALITY
It is routine that the department conducts preliminary investigation AND the enquiry — but officer who conducted preliminary investigation does NOT sit in final judgment on charges. This is the correct arrangement.
⚠️ DANGER ZONE
Where statutory Reserve Police Force rules were silent on who shall present officer; and enquiry officer himself led examination-in-chief of prosecution witnesses AND acted as prosecutor → independent adjudicator capacity lost → bias inferred → enquiry vitiated
Pravin Kumar v UOI (2020) 9 SCC 471 | UOI v Rama Lakhan Sharma (2018) 7 SCC 670
📝 In plain terms: An enquiry officer questioning witnesses to unravel truth is fine. But if the same person is also acting as the prosecution, he has crossed the line — the enquiry is vitiated.
USA / APA MODEL
US Administrative Procedure Act, 1946 seeks physical separation within agency — separate Administrative Law Judges (ALJs), independent of agency, assigned by rotation, not subject to agency direction, pay controlled by Civil Service Commission. "Key to APA: independence of the hearing officer." — Schwartz
ENGLAND — FRANKS COMMITTEE
Recommended inspectors be placed under control of minister NOT directly concerned with subject-matter (Lord Chancellor). Emphasised: "This change would emphasize impartiality at an important stage of adjudication and do much to allay public misgiving." Government did NOT implement this recommendation.
11.4.2 — Combining Informant and Investigator in the Same Person
🔍 Informant = Investigator — Automatic Bias?
Mukesh Singh v State (NCT of Delhi) | (2020) 10 SCC 120
📝 If the same police officer who files the FIR (informant) also investigates the case, is there automatic bias? The Supreme Court settled this — it is NOT automatic. Each case must be examined on its own facts.
SC Ruling in Mukesh Singh — Two Key Propositions:
OLD CASES OVERRULED
Bhagwan Singh v State of Rajasthan (1976); Megha Singh v State of Haryana (1996); State v Rajangam (2010) — acquittals on ground that informant = investigator → confined to their OWN facts. NOT a general proposition of law
CORRECT RULE
Informant being investigator does NOT by itself vitiate investigation. Bias/prejudice depends on facts & circumstances of each case. Accused not automatically entitled to acquittal on this ground alone.
📝 Case-to-case basis — courts must look at whether actual prejudice was caused, not assume bias from the mere fact of dual role.
MOHAN LAL v STATE OF PUNJAB (2018) — OVERRULED
The contrary view that "informant cannot be investigator and accused is entitled to acquittal in such a case" is not good law and specifically overruled.
💡 GST relevance: If the same officer who issued SCN also conducted the investigation/survey — not automatic bias. Assessee must demonstrate specific prejudice caused by the dual role, not merely allege it.
11.4.3 — Prior Utterances and Pre-judgment of Issues
🗣️ Pre-Judgment — When Prior Statements Disqualify an Adjudicator
Oryx Fisheries Pvt Ltd v UOI | (2010) 13 SCC 427
📝 What if an officer has already made up his mind before hearing you? If his mind is "irrevocably closed" — he is disqualified. But having a prior view on general policy doesn't always disqualify.
✗ DISQUALIFIED
Prior policy statement shows a "final and irrevocable" decision — mind already foreclosed as to merits of the specific case before the authority
Premchand Jain v State of MP (AIR 1966 MP 117) — Enquiry officer did not consider objections raised at all; decided to approve scheme purely on policy → enquiry characterised as farcical → quashed
✓ PERMISSIBLE
Pre-disposition to certain issues known to parties but adjudicator's mind NOT "irrevocably closed" → may still decide. Prior statements of general policy → not disqualifying if person can still hear arguments with open mind
📝 A judge has no personal interest in the outcome — he can reconsider with open mind. An administrator has personal involvement in the policy — so the bar is higher for administrators.
FRANK J (USA) — HUMAN MIND CANNOT BE BLANK
"If 'bias' and 'partiality' be defined to mean the total absence of preconceptions in the mind of the judge, then no one has ever had a fair trial and no one will. The human mind, even at infancy, is no blank piece of paper." — In re Linahan, 138 F.2d 650 (1943)
The Critical Distinction:
PRE-JUDGING SPECIFIC FACTS
About a specific party / specific controversy → should DISQUALIFY
K.S. Rao v Hyderabad — Enquiry officer expressed strong view petitioner should be dismissed BEFORE enquiry → disqualified
PRE-CONCEPTIONS ON GENERAL POLICY
On general questions of law / policy affecting large number of persons → greater leeway permissible. Pre-conception does not mean the person will not listen fairly.
ORYX FISHERIES — SCN WITH CLOSED MIND
Show cause notice itself demonstrated authority had "a totally closed mind" at SCN stage — prejudged guilt before hearing. Order of cancellation also vitiated by bias. Court relied on Kranti Associates — "adequate and intelligent reasons must be given for judicial decisions." SCN quashed; authority given liberty to proceed afresh.
💡 GST Litigation: If the SCN itself contains language presupposing guilt ("the assessee has evaded tax") rather than alleging it ("there are grounds to believe..."), this is a ground for challenging the adjudication order — the adjudicator's mind was closed at the SCN stage itself.
11.5 — Bias on Account of Judicial Obstinacy
🏛️ Judicial Obstinacy — A New Form of Bias
State of WB v Shivananda Pathak | AIR 1998 SC 2050 | (1998) 5 SCC 513
📝 Judicial obstinacy means a judge refusing to follow what a higher court said and stubbornly clinging to his own earlier overruled view — in the same case. This is bias in one's own favour — an ego-driven refusal to accept the superior court's ruling.
Core Principle — When a Judgment is Overruled by Higher Court:
OBLIGATION
Judge whose judgment is overruled MUST submit to that judgment. Judicial discipline requires compliance.
PROHIBITION
Cannot in the same case or collateral proceedings between same parties rewrite or reiterate the overruled judgment — even on a pure question of law.
EXCEPTION
May reiterate dogmatic views in some other case (different matter) — but never in the same case at a subsequent stage.
EFFECT OF OBSTINACY
"If it is done, it would be exhibitive of his bias in his own favour to satisfy his egoistic judicial obstinacy." — SC in Shivananda Pathak
SK WARKOO v STATE OF J&K (1998) 9 SCC 677
When SLP was argued, not disclosed that one bench member had earlier heard the matter as CJ of J&K HC. SC recorded strong disapproval — the matter should have been disclosed and that judge should not have sat. SC directed matter to be listed before bench not including that judge.
📝 In GST proceedings: If the Revisional Authority who hears your revision is the same officer who passed the original order at the departmental level — this is grounds to challenge on bias. Disclose immediately; don't wait.
11.6 — Group Decision — Bias of One Member Vitiates All
👥 Group Decision — Bias Operates Subtly Through Influence
A.K. Kraipak v UOI | AIR 1970 SC 150 | G. Sarana v Lucknow University | AIR 1976 SC 2428
📝 When a biased person is on a committee that decides your case — the whole decision is tainted. Even if the biased person said nothing, voted separately or wasn't present — it doesn't matter. People on committees influence each other.
The Core Principle — G. Sarana v Lucknow University:
"In deciding the question of bias, human probabilities and ordinary course of human conduct have to be taken into consideration. In a group deliberation and decision like that of a Selection Board, the members do not function as computers. Each member of the group or board is bound to influence the others, more so if the member concerned is a person with special knowledge. His bias is likely to operate in a subtle manner."
DOES NOT MATTER THAT:
• Biased member did not actively participate
• He remained silent
• He did not seek to influence others
• Committee was aware of his relationship
• He did not personally attend the relevant meeting
📝 Hannam v Bradford City Council: Governors on school board + on council committee deciding teacher's dismissal. Three governors had not attended the meeting that dismissed Hannam. Still — bias found, decision quashed.
SELECTION COMMITTEE — RELATIVE ON BOARD
Son-in-law of selected candidate as committee member → selection quashed even though committee presided by UPSC Chairman (independent person). "Nearness of relationship could reasonably give impression of real likelihood of bias."
📝 Ashok Kumar Yadav v Haryana — if a close relative of a member is a candidate, the member must withdraw from the ENTIRE selection process, not just from interviewing that relative.
QUID PRO QUO — MOHAPATRA
J. Mohapatra — author-members on book selection committee. Mere non-participation or withdrawal insufficient — evil of quid pro quo cannot be eliminated. "Members deliberating would bear in mind that the turn for selecting their books would also come."
TEST — KRAIPAK PRINCIPLE
Question is NOT whether there was actual bias — question is whether there was a reasonable likelihood of bias. Bias of one member affects validity of the group decision.
💡 GST Tribunals: If a member of GSTAT Bench has previously been involved in the departmental adjudication of the same matter (e.g., as Additional Commissioner), the entire bench decision is challenged on basis of group bias — even if that member is not the author of the order.
11.7 — Actual Bias vs Apparent Bias
⚖️ Actual Bias vs Apparent Bias — The Critical Distinction
Bihar State Mineral Development Corp v Encon Builders | AIR 2003 SC 3688
ACTUAL BIAS
Decision-maker shown to have an interest in the outcome. Allows decision to be influenced by partiality / prejudice. Deprives litigant of fundamental right to fair trial. Leads to automatic disqualification. Rarely established.
📝 Actual bias = We can prove the judge was genuinely and consciously partial. Very hard to prove — minds are invisible.
APPARENT / UNCONSCIOUS BIAS
Suspicion of bias. Described as "apparent" or "unconscious" or "imputed" bias. Challenger does not suggest actual bias — only that there is objective apprehension of bias. More common in practice.
📝 Apparent bias = A reasonable person looking at the situation would worry that justice might not be done — even if the judge was actually fair internally.
Tests for Bias — Historical Debate:
TEST 1
Would a reasonable and fair-minded person knowing all facts have had a reasonable suspicion that a fair trial was not possible? (Reasonable Suspicion Test)
TEST 2
Was there a real likelihood of bias? (Real Likelihood Test)
R v GOUGH (1993) — UK
Lord Goff: Uniform test — "Real Danger of Bias" (not "real likelihood") — whether having regard to relevant circumstances, there was a real danger of bias in the sense that the member might unfairly regard the case
PECUNIARY / PROPRIETARY INTEREST — DIMES RULE
No investigation needed — "the nature of the interest is such that public confidence in the administration of justice requires that the decision should not stand." — Automatic disqualification regardless of likelihood or suspicion tests.
BIAS — ALWAYS A QUESTION OF FACT
Court has to be vigilant; depends on facts of each case. Court should act on real bias, not merely on likelihood. Apprehension alone without material is insufficient — Ashok Kumar Lingala v State of Karnataka (2012) 1 SCC 321 — No material to suggest real likelihood → prayer for entrusting demarcation to Geological Survey of India rejected.
APPARENT BIAS — SUJASHA MUKERJEE CASE (2015)
Senior-most judge on High Court bench who decided selection dispute was also one of the three judges who acted as examiners in the very selection test. "Tantamount to being a Judge in one's own cause — imperative for the learned Judge to recuse himself." — Highly objectionable.
11.8 — Initial Recommendation — Bias Taints the Chain
📋 Initial Recommendation — Does Bias at Early Stage Taint Final Decision?
A.K. Kraipak v UOI | Manak Lal v Prem Chand | G. Sarana v Lucknow University
📝 Even if the initial recommendation is not the final word (a higher authority still has to accept or reject it), bias at the recommendation stage taints the whole chain. The higher body is normally guided by the lower body's report — it's a necessary prerequisite of the final order.
DE SMITH PRINCIPLE — ADOPTED BY SC IN SARANA:
"On principle it would seem that where a report or determination lacking final effect may nevertheless have a seriously prejudicial effect on the legally protected interests of individuals (e.g. when it is a necessary prerequisite of a final order) the person making the report or preliminary decision must not be affected by interest or likelihood of bias."
MANAK LAL
Bar council tribunal report → submitted to High Court (not final). Argument: Rule against bias doesn't apply because tribunal not final. SC rejected — "justice must not only be done but must appear to be done." Rule against bias applies to initial quasi-judicial proceedings.
MOHAPATRA
Book selection committee → list not final; State Government could reject any recommendation. SC held: "State Government would normally be guided by the list approved by the committee." Bias of committee member → selection process vitiated.
PRINCIPLE
Courts respond in the affirmative — bias of initial decision maker CAN affect the ultimate decision taken by an independent body, because initial recommendation plays a significant part in making the final decision.
💡 GST context: If Joint Commissioner (biased) recommends SCN → Additional Commissioner issues SCN → Adjudicating authority decides. The bias at JC level taints the entire chain — the adjudicating order itself can be challenged on this ground.
11.9 — Statute May Exclude Bias
📜 When Statute Overrides the Rule Against Bias
B.K. Mehra v LIC | AIR 1991 Cal 256 | M. Madhavan Pillai v K.A. Balan | AIR 1979 Ker 120
📝 Normally, you can't be a judge in your own cause. But if a statute says specifically that a certain official must decide a certain matter — even if he has an interest — then the rule against bias yields to statute. Natural justice can supplement law but cannot override it.
GOLDEN RULE
"Legislation may override rules of natural justice, but the latter cannot efface a legislation. Natural justice can supplement but not supplant legislation." — BK Mehra v LIC
BK MEHRA — LIC ESTATE OFFICER
LIC officer appointed as estate officer for LIC premises. Argument: LIC employee would be biased in favour of employer. Court: Section 3 is "clear legislative authority" for the appointment. Statutory provision overrides natural justice.
MADHAVAN — PRIVATE COLLEGE MANAGEMENT
Statute authorised management (accuser + prosecutor) to also hold enquiry and take disciplinary action against teachers. To accept bias argument = total effacement of the statutory provision. Rejected.
CRITICAL CONDITIONS — FOR STATUTE TO OVERRIDE BIAS RULE:
(a) Intention of law must be clear to exclude bias rule; (b) In absence of clear indication → courts infer it as disqualification; (c) Even where statute requires interested person to decide, escape routes to dilute effect of disqualification should be taken, except where this cannot be done without paralysing administration; (d) Only in case of necessity may the statutory scheme have to be strictly followed.
NJAC CASE (2016) 5 SCC 1 — CONFLICT OF INTEREST
99th Constitutional Amendment and NJAC Act held unconstitutional. Law Minister as ex-officio NJAC member → likelihood of bias because executive has major stake in cases before higher judiciary. Participation of Law Minister in final judicial appointments = conflict of interest → provision held ultra vires.
📝 Even if law says a Minister "must" be on the committee — if the conflict of interest is so fundamental as to undermine a basic constitutional feature (judicial independence), the law itself can be struck down.
11.9.1 — Institutional Bias — CESTAT Former Members
🏛️ Institutional Bias — Restriction on Former Tribunal Members
N.K. Bajpai v UOI | AIR 2012 SC 1310 | (2012) 4 SCC 653
📝 Institutional bias = not personal prejudice, but structural conflict that arises because of the nature of the institution. Former CESTAT members practising before CESTAT creates an institutional risk of bias — the law can restrict this even retroactively (retroactively, not retrospectively).
FACTS
By amendment to Customs Act, former CESTAT members debarred from appearing/acting/pleading before CESTAT. Former member challenged: right to practise profession (Art. 19(1)(g)); disqualification not provided when he served; retrospective effect.
SC HELD — ALL CHALLENGES REJECTED
Bar on practice by former member = reasonable restriction permissible under Constitution. Restriction is reasonable to avoid possibility of institutional bias. Entire field of legal profession remains open except practice before CESTAT.
RETROSPECTIVE vs RETROACTIVE
Law not retrospective; operates retroactively. Takes away right to practice before a limited forum; all other forums remain open. Not a case of settled or vested rights incapable of interference.
COURT'S INSTITUTIONAL INTEREST DOCTRINE
"When you have been a member of a Tribunal over a long period, and other members have been your co-members whether judicial or technical, it is difficult to hold that there would be no possibility of bias or no real danger of bias. Even if we rule out this possibility, still, it will always be better advised and in the institutional interest that restrictions are enforced. Then alone will the mind of the litigant be free from a lurking doubt of likelihood of bias and this would enhance the image of the Tribunal."
💡 GSTAT relevance: Similar principles would apply to GSTAT members — restrictions on post-retirement practice before GSTAT are constitutionally defensible on institutional bias grounds. GST practitioners should factor this into their assessment of which tribunal forums are available to them in the long run.
11.10 — Waiver of Right to Object to Bias
🤝 Waiver — When You Lose the Right to Object to Bias
Manak Lal v Prem Chand | AIR 1957 SC 425 | Vidya Parkash v UOI | AIR 1988 SC 705
📝 If you know the adjudicator is biased — but you participate in the proceedings without raising the objection — you may lose your right to complain later. But waiver requires full knowledge of facts AND of your right to object. You can't waive what you don't know.
Conditions for Waiver to be Inferred:
CONDITION 1
Party knew of the disqualification arising out of bias in the adjudicator
CONDITION 2
Party was aware of his right to challenge / object to the presence of the biased member
CONDITION 3
Party acquiesced — appeared before the adjudicator, kept silent, knowing all the facts → deemed to have abandoned the right to object later on ground of bias
ESSEX JUSTICES PRINCIPLE
"Waiver on acquiescence presupposes that the person to be bound is fully cognizant of his rights, and that being so, he neglects to enforce them, or chooses one benefit instead of another... If the applicant was not fully cognizant of his right to take objection, he could not be said to have waived his right by failing to exercise it. Waiver presupposes an intention to forgo a right." — The King v Essex Justices (1927) 2 KB 475
EXCEPTIONS — WAIVER NOT INFERRED WHEN:
• Party prevented by fear of antagonising superiors
• Party had ignorance of full facts
• Party unrepresented by counsel and did not know of his right to object at the time
• Party surprised by the situation at the appropriate time
MANAK LAL — WAIVER APPLIED
Petitioner-advocate knew of chairman's disqualification. Being 10-year standing lawyer, deemed aware of legal rights. Raised bias plea first time in High Court after unfavourable report. SC: Petitioner "wanted to take a chance to secure a favourable report" — effective bar of waiver.
RATTAN LAL SHARMA — WAIVER RAISED PROPERLY
Objection to M's inclusion raised before managing committee (overruled). Raised again before Deputy Commissioner. Raised in writ petition. SC: Plea not raised for first time in writ — courts should entertain plea that goes to root of the lis. Managing committee's order quashed; fresh inquiry directed without any of the tainted members.
📝 GST Lesson: Raise bias objection at the FIRST HEARING before the adjudicating authority. Put it on record. If overruled, raise again in appeal. Don't wait for the outcome — don't "take a chance" and then plead bias only after an adverse order.
11.11 — Necessity Excludes Bias
⚙️ The Doctrine of Necessity — When Bias Must Be Overlooked
J. Mohapatra v State of Orissa | AIR 1984 SC 1572 | Election Commissioner v Subramaniam Swamy | AIR 1996 SC 1810
📝 Sometimes there is simply no one else who can decide. If the law empowers only one person to decide, and that person has bias — what to do? The doctrine of necessity says: let the biased person decide, because otherwise there would be no decision at all. The machinery of justice cannot be allowed to break down.
WILLIAM WADE PRINCIPLE
"But there are many cases where no substitution is possible, since no one else is empowered to act. Natural justice then has to give way to necessity; for otherwise there is no means of deciding and the machinery of justice or administration will break down."
MOHAPATRA — SUPREME COURT STATEMENT
If choice is between allowing a biased person to act OR stifling the action altogether → choice must fall in favour of the former as it is the only way to promote decision-making. Doctrine of necessity makes it imperative for authority to decide and considerations of judicial propriety must yield.
Conditions for Necessity Doctrine to Apply:
No other person is authorised or competent to adjudicate
OR Quorum cannot be formed without the biased person
OR No other competent tribunal can be constituted
Cases Where Necessity Doctrine Was REJECTED:
MOHAPATRA — BOOK SELECTION
Author-members on book selection committee. Nothing prevented them from pointing out conflict to State and resigning. State could have substituted them. Necessity doctrine rejected.
RATTAN LAL SHARMA
Managing committee sought to justify M's inclusion as necessity (staff representative required). No such rule existed. Inquiry committee could have been constituted with other members. Rejected.
RATNA — ICAI
Disciplinary committee rules required president and VP, but no such compulsion for council meeting. Council could meet without them. Rejected. Court suggested amendment to provide for committee without the interested officials.
ELECTION COMMISSIONER v SUBRAMANIAM SWAMY — CREATIVE SOLUTION
CEC had likelihood of bias. SC's approach: CEC to call meeting, chair it, announce recusal from opinion formation. If two ECs agree → unanimous opinion communicated. If they disagree → necessity doctrine compels CEC to express his view so majority opinion can be communicated to Governor as required by Art. 192(1).
📝 The Court found a middle path — biased member chairs but recuses from the substantive decision; necessity doctrine triggered only if even without him the commission cannot function.
💡 GST — When necessity might apply: If the only officer competent under GST law to adjudicate a particular case in a jurisdiction is the very officer who issued the SCN AND no other officer of equivalent rank is available, necessity might be argued. But departments must try to transfer the matter to another officer before invoking necessity.
11.12 — Postscript | R v Gough — Uniform Test for Bias
📖 R v Gough (1993) 2 All ER 726 — House of Lords
Lord Goff's Uniform Test | "Real Danger of Bias"
WHY ACTUAL BIAS NEED NOT BE PROVED
Two reasons: (1) Bias is an insidious thing — person may in good faith believe he was acting impartially yet unconsciously be affected. (2) Overriding public interest — there should be confidence in the integrity of the administration of justice.
Lord Goff's Uniform Test:
"Having ascertained the relevant circumstances, the court should ask itself whether, having regard to those circumstances, there was a real danger of bias on the part of the relevant member of the tribunal in question, in the sense that he might unfairly regard (or have unfairly regarded) with favour, or disfavour, the case of a party to the issue under consideration by him."
CHANGE FROM EARLIER TEST
"Real likelihood" test → replaced by "real danger" test. "Reasonable man" standard → OUT. Reviewing court itself personifies the reasonable man. Test is possibility of bias, not probability.
NARROWER IMPACT
New formula is somewhat narrower than Lord Denning's Metropolitan Properties test. Some cases that could be caught under the old rule will now be out of reach of the new formula.
INDIA — STATE OF CONFUSION
Indian case-law on bias is in a state of confusion — courts do not apply only one test uniformly but have propounded several tests. "It may lead to great advantage if the Supreme Court is able to evolve one uniform test for the purpose, as this would avoid unnecessary and avoidable confusion in this area."
11.13 — Natural Justice — History, Elaboration and Key Principles
⚖️ Natural Justice — The Foundation of All Fair Adjudication
Canara Bank v Debasis Das | AIR 2003 SC 2041 | (2003) 4 SCC 557
📝 Natural justice is simply common-sense fairness. It is not written in any statute — it is ingrained in the conscience of civilised society. When formal law fails to achieve fairness, natural justice fills the gap.
NATURE OF NATURAL JUSTICE — SC ELABORATION
Rules of natural justice are not codified canons but principles ingrained into conscience of man. Not embodied always expressly in statute — may be implied from the nature of the duty to be performed. Old distinction between judicial act and administrative act has withered away. Even administrative orders involving civil consequences must be consistent with natural justice. "Civil consequences" encompasses not merely property or personal rights but civil liberties, material deprivations, non-pecuniary damages — everything affecting a citizen in his civil life.
The Two Pillars of Natural Justice:
PILLAR 1
Nemo judex in causa sua
"No man shall be a judge in his own cause"
Nemo potest esse simul actor et judex — "No one can be at once suitor and judge"
📝 A judge cannot decide a case where he himself is interested in the outcome — whether financially, personally or institutionally. This is the entire law of bias.
PILLAR 2
Audi alteram partem
"Hear the other side"
Audietur et altera pars
Notice must be precise, unambiguous, apprise party of case to be met, time must be adequate. Absence = order wholly vitiated.
📝 Even God gave Adam a hearing before passing judgment! ("Adam, where art thou? Hast thou not eaten of the tree...") — Canara Bank v Debasis Das, quoting from the Scriptures.
MAGNA CARTA 1215 — FIRST STATUTORY RECOGNITION
Sir Edward Coke: Natural justice requires to "vocate, interrogate and adjudicate". Coopers v Wandsworth Board of Works (1863): Authority must listen to both sides before taking action.
COROLLARY — JUSTICE MUST NOT ONLY BE DONE
"Qui aliquid statuerit, parte inaudita altera, aequum licet dixerit, haud aequum fecerit" — he who shall decide anything without the other side having been heard, although he may have said what is right, will not have been doing what is right. → "Justice should not only be done but should manifestly be seen to be done."
The "Useless Formality Theory" — When Natural Justice Can Be Bypassed:
DOCTRINE
Where grant of opportunity in terms of principles of natural justice would make no difference — would not improve the situation — "useless formality theory" can be pressed into service. Applied in Dharampal Satyapal Ltd v Deputy Commissioner of Central Excise, Guwahati (2015) 8 SCC 519 — retrospective withdrawal of exemption notification; quantification not disputed; officer bound by RC Tobacco dictum; issuance of notice would be empty formality.
📝 The useless formality theory is controversial — courts are divided. SC in MC Mehta v UOI left the question open. The theory should be used sparingly — only where facts are admitted, indisputable, and the outcome is mathematically certain.
POST-DECISIONAL HEARING — Managing Director ECIL v B. Karunakar (1994) 4 SCC 727
Where enquiry officer's report not furnished to delinquent employee — courts must: (a) furnish copy; (b) give opportunity to show prejudice; (c) if non-supply made no difference to findings → do NOT set aside mechanically; (d) only if non-supply would have made a difference → set aside with liberty to proceed afresh from stage of furnishing report. Courts should avoid "resorting to short cuts."
💡 GST adjudication: If officer's preliminary inquiry report is not shared with assessee before passing adjudication order — this is a procedural deficiency. Assessee must demonstrate that non-supply actually prejudiced his case (changed outcome). Courts will not mechanically quash if outcome would have been same.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter XI | Rule Against Bias
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, First Appellate Authority and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Oryx Fisheries Pvt Ltd v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | (2010) 13 SCC 427 | On: SCN with Closed Mind
Key Ratio
The show cause notice itself demonstrated that the authority had "demonstrated a totally closed mind" at the stage of show cause notice itself, inconsistent with the scheme under which the impugned action had been taken. The authority had prejudged the guilt of the company at the stage of show cause notice itself and the order of cancellation was also vitiated by bias. The requirement of recording reasons in the decision making process on the 'doctrine of fairness' is a component of human right which requires that "adequate and intelligent reasons must be given for judicial decisions."
When to Use — When SCN language presupposes guilt ("the assessee has evaded tax" rather than "there are grounds to believe..."). Argue: mind of adjudicating authority was closed at the SCN stage itself; adjudication order flowing from such a SCN is tainted with bias and liable to be quashed.
G. Sarana v Lucknow University
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1976 SC 2428 | (1976) 3 SCC 585 | On: Group Decision Bias
Key Ratio
"In deciding the question of bias, human probabilities and ordinary course of human conduct have to be taken into consideration. In a group deliberation and decision like that of a Selection Board, the members do not function as computers. Each member of the group or board is bound to influence the others, more so if the member concerned is a person with special knowledge. His bias is likely to operate in a subtle manner."
When to Use — When challenging a committee decision (e.g., GSTAT bench, committee of officers, revisional authority) where one member had prior involvement in the matter. Cite to establish that bias of one member taints the entire decision — even if that member said nothing or did not vote.
A.K. Kraipak v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1970 SC 150 | (1969) 2 SCC 562 | On: Group Bias + Initial Recommendation
Key Ratio — Group Bias
In a group decision, each member of the group is bound to influence the other. Bias of one member affects validity of the group decision and it does not matter that the biased member did not actively participate in the decision, or that he remained silent and did not seek to influence the decision of other members in the group. The question is not whether there was actual bias or not, but whether there was a reasonable likelihood of bias.
Key Ratio — Initial Recommendation
Even where a report or recommendation lacks final effect, if it is a necessary prerequisite of a final order and may have a seriously prejudicial effect on the legally protected interests of individuals, the person making the report or preliminary decision must not be affected by interest or likelihood of bias.
When to Use — When the officer who conducted preliminary inquiry or investigation was biased, and the SCN/adjudication order flows from that report. Cite to establish that the bias at the preliminary stage taints the entire chain of decisions flowing therefrom.
Hari v Deputy Commissioner of Police
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1956 SC 559 | On: Combining Prosecutor and Judge
Key Ratio
It does not matter if both the prosecution and conviction are done by the one and the same department, so long as the two functions are discharged by separate officers. However, when such a dual function is discharged by one and the same official, then this is a clear case of bias unless clearly permitted by legislation.
When to Use — When the same officer who drafted/issued the SCN is also the adjudicating authority. Argue: identical officer discharging both prosecution and adjudication functions — structural bias — adjudication void unless statute specifically permits it. Particularly strong where officer has also conducted the investigation.
UOI v Sanjay Jethi
Supreme Court of India | 2013 (13) SCALE 82 | Armed Forces Tribunal | On: Prior Written Expression of Views
Key Ratio
Once a Court of Inquiry has been constituted to inquire into allegations relating to a person's character and military reputation subject to the Act, it should not be done by the persons who have expressed their views in writing behind the back of the person and assumed the role of the recommending authority which is statutory in nature to take disciplinary action. The law does not countenance the same. The Presiding Officer should have recused himself to preside over the Court of Inquiry. A recommendation made by a biased presiding officer, which forms the sole basis of disciplinary action, has an indelible stamp of infirmity.
When to Use — When the adjudicating authority or inquiry officer had previously expressed adverse views about the assessee/delinquent in internal communications or corres-pondence. Particularly powerful where the adverse prior view is on record (written) — cite to establish that officer had already decided the matter before hearing began.
Manak Lal v Prem Chand
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1957 SC 425 | On: Waiver of Bias Objection — When and How
Key Ratio on Waiver
"The alleged bias in a member of the tribunal does not render the proceedings invalid if it is shown that the objection against the presence of the member in question had not been taken by the party even though the party knew about the circumstances giving rise to the allegation about the alleged bias and was aware of his right to challenge the presence of the member in the tribunal. Waiver can be inferred only if and after it is shown that the party knew about the relevant facts and was aware of his right to take the objection in question."
Counter-argument on Waiver
Waiver cannot always and in every case be inferred merely from the failure of the party to take the objection. There can be no waiver of rights of which the person concerned is unaware. The courts should be reluctant to infer waiver, particularly when the affected person did not know of his right to object at the earliest stage. Waiver is an intentional act done with knowledge — not an unconscious act.
When to Use — Two ways: (1) Assessee must raise bias objection at the earliest stage before adjudicating authority and put it on record. (2) If Department raises "waiver" defence — counter with the "knowledge + intention" requirement — assessee must be shown to have known all facts AND known his right AND intentionally abandoned it — mere silence at one hearing is not enough.
State of WB v Shivananda Pathak
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1998 SC 2050 | (1998) 5 SCC 513 | On: Judicial Obstinacy + Open Mind Requirement
Key Ratio — Open Mind Requirement
An essential requirement of judicial adjudication is that the judge is impartial and neutral and is in a position to apply his mind objectively to the facts of the case put up before him. If he is predisposed or suffers from prejudices or has a biased mind, he disqualifies himself from acting as a judge. It is a fundamental principle of natural justice that the officer selected to make an enquiry should be a person with an open mind and not one who has already pre-judged the issue.
Key Ratio — Against Judicial Obstinacy
If a judgment is overruled by the higher court, the judicial discipline requires that the judge whose judgment is overruled must submit to that judgment. He cannot, in the same proceedings or in collateral proceedings between the same parties, rewrite the overruled judgment. If it is done, it would be exhibitive of his bias in his own favour to satisfy his egoistic judicial obstinacy.
When to Use — (1) Against adjudicating authority who has expressed prior views on the issue (pre-judgment). (2) Where Appellate Authority or Revisional Authority is re-affirming an already-overruled position — cite judicial obstinacy as a form of bias vitiating the order. (3) In proceedings before GSTAT where a member previously expressed views in departmental capacity — open mind requirement is compromised.
Trilok Sudhir Bhai Pandya v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 2012 SC 1668 | (2011) 10 SCC 203 | On: Financial Interest = Bias
Key Ratio
The test of likelihood bias is whether there is a reasonable apprehension in the mind of the party that the court or the tribunal would not act with fairness and without bias on account of certain objective circumstances. A competent authority whose salary, allowances, accommodation and transport were being borne by the private company for whom the acquisition was to be made was virtually an employee of the private company — thereby there were reasons for the affected party to entertain a reasonable apprehension that the competent authority may not act fairly and was likely to act with bias. The court made a distinction between authority conferred on a public authority and a private employee.
When to Use — When challenging the adjudication by an officer who has a financial, service or career interest in the outcome. Also use to challenge adjudication by officers whose promotions or performance appraisals are linked to the quantum of demands or recoveries — a strong ground for bias in high-demand GST adjudication matters.
Managing Director, ECIL v B. Karunakar
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1994 SC 1074 | (1993) 4 SCC 727 | On: Non-furnishing of Inquiry Report
Key Ratio
In all cases where the enquiry officer's report is not furnished to the delinquent employee in the disciplinary proceedings, the Courts and tribunals should cause the copy of the report to be furnished to the aggrieved employee and give the employee an opportunity to show how his case was prejudiced because of the non-supply of the report. If non-supply of the report would have made no difference to the ultimate findings and punishment, the Court should not interfere. The Court/tribunal should not mechanically set aside the order of punishment on the ground that the report was not furnished. The Courts should avoid resorting to short cuts.
When to Use — When the Department has not shared the investigation/inquiry report on the basis of which the SCN was issued. Demand a copy — if denied, raise the procedural violation. If the matter reaches Appellate Authority or GSTAT, argue: non-supply of inspection report, DGGI investigation report or audit report prejudiced representation; outcome would have been materially different. Show specifically how the non-supplied report affected the demand computation.
N.K. Bajpai v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 2012 SC 1310 | (2012) 4 SCC 653 | On: Institutional Bias
Key Ratio — Institutional Interest Doctrine
With the development of law, the Courts are expected to consider, in contradistinction to private and public interests, the institutional interest and expectations of the public at large from an institution. The element of bias by itself may not always necessarily vitiate an action. When you have been a member of a Tribunal over long period, and other members have been your co-members whether judicial or technical, it is difficult to hold that there would be no possibility of bias or no real danger of bias. Even if we rule out this possibility, still, it will always be better advised and in the institutional interest that restrictions are enforced. Then alone will the mind of the litigant be free from a lurking doubt of likelihood of bias and this would enhance the image of the Tribunal.
When to Use — In GSTAT proceedings where a technical member has departmental background and familiarity with the matter — raise institutional bias. Also relevant where same CBIC circular or policy under which SCN is issued was drafted by the same officer now adjudicating — structural conflict between policy-maker and decision-maker roles.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter 12 — Failure of Natural Justice

⚖️ Chapter 12 — Failure of Natural Justice

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Discretionary
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
12.1 Void vs Voidable
12.2 Position in England
12.3 Position in India
12.4 Ultimate Relief
12.4.1 Failure of Audi Alteram Partem
12.4.2 Denial of a Norm of Audi Alteram Partem
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
12.1 — Void vs Voidable | Foundational Concepts
⚖️ Failure of Natural Justice — Void or Voidable?
Audi Alteram Partem | Foundational & Fundamental Concepts (SC)
📝 Natural justice = the right to be heard before a decision is made against you. When this right is violated, the central question is: is the resulting order void (never existed) or voidable (exists until set aside)?
SETTLED LAW
Decision offending against principles of natural justice is outside jurisdiction of decision-making authority.
Parliament does not authorise exercise of power in breach of natural justice.
Rules of natural justice operate as implied mandatory requirements — non-observance invalidates the exercise of power.
Any order passed in violation, save certain contingencies = nullity.
Conceptual Distinction — Void vs Voidable
VOID ORDER
No order at all from inception
Nullity — void ab initio
Has no legal effect whatsoever
No right can be obtained under it
"Same as if it had never existed"
📝 Void ab initio = void from the very beginning. The order was never born in law — not just invalid, but non-existent.
💡 GST: SCN issued without opportunity of hearing → adjudication order passed = void ab initio; demand has no legal existence.
VOIDABLE ORDER
Legally valid at inception
Remains valid until set aside / quashed by court
Has legal effect up to time of quashing
Can only be challenged by person against whom passed
📝 Voidable = born alive but sick. It exists and binds you until a court kills it. You cannot ignore it — you must approach a court.
💡 A GST adjudication order passed without personal hearing — you must file appeal/writ. Cannot just disregard and not pay. Until set aside, it binds.
⚠️ PRACTICAL CAVEAT
Even if an order is void ab initio, it "bears no brand of invalidity upon its forehead." The affected person cannot simply ignore it as a nullity — must approach court for authoritative determination.
Court action is a necessity even when an order may be void.
📝 Even a void GST demand order cannot be simply ignored. You must challenge it — because until a court declares it void, practically it has effect (recovery, attachment, etc.).
9 Crucial Questions arising from Void/Voidable Distinction
(i) Can infringement of NJ be waived?
(ii) Effect of privative clauses?
(iii) Can defect be cured at appellate stage?
(iv) Certiorari without exhausting alternative remedy?
(v) Can affected person ignore order without liability?
(vi) Can government enforce void order pending court decision?
(vii) Who can challenge?
(viii) Challenge after limitation period?
(ix) Collateral proceedings or only direct proceedings?
KEY RULE Voidable order cannot be challenged in collateral proceedings — must be set aside in direct proceedings.
Void order can be challenged collaterally (in any proceedings).
📝 If you are prosecuted for disobeying a GST order, you can plead "the order is void" as a defence only if it was truly void, not merely voidable. For a voidable order, you needed to have challenged it separately first.
RELATIVITY PRINCIPLE
Voidness is a relative, not absolute, concept. Courts may in effect turn "void" acts into "valid" ones by refusing remedies. Meaning of "void" depends upon court's willingness to grant relief in particular situation.
12.2 — Position in England | Key Cases
🏛️ England — Void vs Voidable Controversy
📝 English courts debated this issue through landmark cases. The dominant view emerged that breach of natural justice = void, not merely voidable — though the Privy Council tried to push voidable approach in Durayappah.
RIDGE v BALDWIN (1964) AC 40
House of Lords | Lord Reid
Watch committee dismissed chief constable without hearing
→ House held dismissal "null and void"
No waiver by appealing to Home Secretary
📝 Lord Reid: "A decision given without regard to natural justice is void... The body with the power to decide cannot lawfully proceed without affording the person a proper opportunity to state his case."
💡 GST parallel: Adjudicating authority must give personal hearing before passing order. If denied, order = null and void. Appeal to Commissioner does not cure the original defect.
DURAYAPPAH v FERNANDO (1967) 2 AC 337
Privy Council | Jaffna Municipal Council
Minister dissolved council without hearing → held voidable, not void
Mayor alone had no locus standi
Widely criticised — does not represent good English law
📝 Locus standi = right to challenge. Privy Council said only the party against whom order was passed can challenge a voidable order. But this approach was rejected by majority of scholars.
ANISMINIC LTD. (1967) 3 WLR 382
House of Lords
Failure of NJ goes to jurisdiction of body
Decision = nullity
Even a privative clause cannot protect such decision
📝 Privative clause = a statutory provision that says "this order shall not be challenged in any court." Anisminic held that even such a clause cannot protect an order made in breach of natural justice — it is a nullity.
💡 CGST Act has no complete ouster clause — but even if there were one, a GST order violating natural justice would be a nullity per Anisminic.
Wade's Position — Categorically Against Voidable Approach
Holding action avoidable = depriving litigant of best remedy — declaratory judgment (only available against void acts)
Giving courts discretion to decide whether govt action is lawful = dangerous — citizens entitled to resist unlawful action as matter of right, not discretion
Administrative action irregular and ultra vires = void, not voidable
"Failure to give a hearing renders decision void, not voidable"
Natural justice has for centuries been enforced as matter of law, not discretion — right to natural justice = as firm as right to personal liberty
LORD WRIGHT — General Medical Council v Spackman [1943] AC 627

"If the principles of natural justice are violated in respect of any decision it is, indeed, immaterial whether the same decision would have been arrived at in the absence of the departure from the essential principles of justice. The decision must be declared to be no decision."
📝 This is the golden rule: if natural justice is violated, the order fails — period. It does not matter that the outcome might have been the same even with a proper hearing. The procedure itself is the vice.
LIMITATION EXCEPTION — R v Secy of State, ex p. Ostler (1977) QB 122
Where statute specifies time-limit to challenge an order — even a nullity for failure of NJ cannot be challenged after the specified period.
Public authorities need security before embarking on expensive schemes.
Concept of voidness = relative; even void order becomes unchallengeable after prescribed time-limit.
💡 GST parallel: If limitation for appeal/writ petition has expired, even an order passed without hearing may practically become final — though High Court under Art. 226 has broader discretion.
12.3 — Position in India | Leading Cases
🇮🇳 India — Position on Void / Voidable
Generally: Order without NJ = Void and Nullity (in line with Ridge v Baldwin majority)
GENERAL RULE IN INDIA
Judicial thinking: adjudicative order made without following natural justice = void and nullity.
In accordance with majority view in Ridge v Baldwin.
No need in India to distinguish between void/voidable for purposes of locus standi to challenge.
Key Indian Cases
Nawabkhan v State of Gujarat
AIR 1974 SC 1471
Order of externment affecting Fundamental Right (Art.19) made without hearing = void ab initio and of no legal efficacy from very beginning
"When competent court holds such act invalid, it operates from nativity"
📝 From nativity = from birth. The order was never valid even for a single day. Person cannot be convicted for disobeying such an order.
💡 GST: Where fundamental right is involved, GST order without hearing = void ab initio. Person cannot be prosecuted under Sec 132 for non-compliance with such a void order.
Nawabkhan — 3 Possible Answers
SC noted (without deciding definitively):
(1) Order void — can be defied, ignored, attacked collaterally
(2) Nullificability (not nullity) — court must first declare it invalid before disobeying
(3) Voidable — good until directly challenged by aggrieved party
📝 SC left this open — but confirmed: order in violation of NJ = void in the limited sense of being liable to be avoided with retroactive effect.
Nally Bharat Engg Co. v State of Bihar
AIR 1990 SC 325
Transfer of labour dispute without hearing management = null and void
"Denial of that opportunity is a fatal flaw to the decision"
Party denied NJ need not establish particular prejudice
📝 Non-observance of natural justice is itself prejudice. No need to prove separate harm. This is a critical principle for GST adjudication challenges.
💡 GST: If SCN does not give proper opportunity and adjudication order is passed, assessee need not prove specific monetary prejudice — the denial itself is sufficient.
LACHES / DELAY — India-specific Rule
Under Art. 226, no period of limitation prescribed. But SC trend: apply laches principle strictly.
When valuable rights have been created in others by lapse of time, or costly schemes implemented — courts may refuse to declare order nullity even if passed in violation of NJ.
Conceptual logic must give way to pragmatism, administrative exigencies and public interest.
📝 Laches = unreasonable delay in approaching court. Even if a GST order was void, if you wait years to challenge it, the court may refuse relief — especially where third-party rights have crystallised or the amount has been used in public interest.
SL KAPOOR v JAGMOHAN — AIR 1981 SC 136
Breach of NJ was in itself treated as prejudice — no other de facto prejudice needed to be proved.
Two exceptions: (1) upon admitted/indisputable facts only one conclusion was possible; (2) "useless formality" theory.
💡 GST application: If outcome of adjudication was inevitable regardless (e.g., admitted tax not paid, no defence possible), department may argue NJ violation was merely technical. But this exception is narrow — assessee must still show the result could have been different.
12.4 — Ultimate Relief | Judicial Discretion in Granting Relief
🔨 Ultimate Relief — What Will the Court Actually Do?
📝 Even if natural justice is violated and the order is technically void, courts in India exercise discretion in giving relief. They tailor the remedy to the totality of circumstances — not mechanical application of voidness.
ORDINARY CASE
Order is quashed
Affected party restored to original position
Concerned authority free to restart proceedings after observing NJ
💡 GST: Adjudication order quashed for no hearing → matter remanded → fresh SCN + hearing → fresh order.
COURT MAY INSTEAD
Maintain status quo
Direct authority to reconsider after completing NJ requirements
Affirm, modify or cancel earlier order
Give post-decisional hearing opportunity
📝 Post-decisional hearing = hearing given after the decision is made. But the decision must be tentative (not final) and authority must consider representation with open mind.
COURT MAY REFUSE RELIEF IF
No prejudice caused
Quashing would revive another illegal order
Restoration physically impossible
Position changed so fundamentally
Laches / delay on part of petitioner
Useless formality — outcome inevitable
💡 Gadde Venkateswara Rao v Govt of AP: Court refused to quash even though NJ violated — because quashing one defective order would revive another equally defective earlier order.
Miscellaneous Judicial Strategies — Tailoring Relief
Maneka Gandhi v UOI
Passport impounding — order violated NJ but court did NOT quash
Accepted AG's undertaking to give hearing and reconsider
Post-decisional hearing approach
Swadeshi Cotton Mills v UOI
Take-over without hearing — SC held decisions in violation of NJ = null and void
But instead of quashing, directed "full, fair and effective" hearing within 3 months
Pre-quashing remedy approach
SL Kapoor v Jagmohan
Suppression of New Delhi Municipality — NJ violated
Court found violation but did NOT quash — only 15 days left for committee's term
Time-expired — relief pointless approach
UP Warehousing Corp v Vijay Narain
Wrongful dismissal for breach of NJ
High Court ordered reinstatement with full wages
SC modified — whether gainful employment elsewhere = question of fact → writ not appropriate remedy for back wages
⚠️ DANGER OF JUDICIAL DISCRETION
Case-to-case approach makes Administrative Law uncertain, unsystematic, confused and unpredictable.
If court refrains from voiding the order, voidness principle for non-hearing becomes diluted in practice → compulsion on Administration to follow NJ becomes less pressing → significance of natural justice in administrative process is diluted.
💡 GST practical lesson: Even when natural justice is violated, do not assume the court will automatically quash. Courts look at: (a) was there actual prejudice? (b) would outcome have differed? (c) is quashing practical? Always argue prejudice strongly.
12.4.1 — Failure of Audi Alteram Partem | Specific Situations
👂 Audi Alteram Partem — Hear the Other Side
Specific Fact Situations Where Courts Have Acted
📝 Audi alteram partem = hear the other side before deciding. Courts have consistently held that any order passed without this opportunity is void. The cases below show how courts have applied this in practice.
Emerging Principles — Notice, Opportunity and Prejudice
(i) NOTICE DEFECT
Non-issue OR mistake in notice OR defective service of notice does NOT affect jurisdiction of assessing officer if otherwise reasonable opportunity of hearing has been given
(ii) NOTICE = PART OF OPPORTUNITY
Issue of notice as prescribed in Rules = part of reasonable opportunity of being heard
(iii) PREJUDICE TEST
If prejudice has been caused by non-issue or invalid service of notice → proceedings vitiated.
But irregular service of notice alone does not render proceedings invalid — especially if assessee's conduct rendered service impracticable
(iv) APPELLATE REMEDY
When NJ principles violated → Appellate Authority in appropriate cases can set aside the order and require de novo decision
📝 De novo = afresh, from scratch. The entire matter is reconsidered as if the earlier order never existed.
📌 Specific Fact Situations — Courts Held NJ Violated
SituationCourt's HoldingGST Relevance
Search of assessee — shortage detected — copy of check list not furnished despite request (ITO claimed documents not readily available) NJ violated — cannot deny vital information to person about to be visited with penalty. Tibhuvandas Bhimji Zaveri v CCE (1997) 11 SCC 276 GST inspection — documents seized — if relied upon in SCN, must be furnished. Non-furnishing = NJ violation.
Employee absented without leave — no notice for explanation given (as required by rules) NJ violated — rules require notice before action. Aligarh Muslim University v Mansoor Ali Khan (2000) 7 SCC 529 SCN must specifically state allegation — assessee must be given adequate time to respond.
Employee required to travel to distant city for disciplinary inquiry — subsistence allowance not paid — could not attend Clear breach of NJ — but if employee took no stand before authority that he was unable to participate, no prejudice in effectively defending = does not vitiate proceedings. Jagdamba Prasad Shukla v State of UP (2000) 7 SCC 90 Assessee must raise NJ objection at earliest in proceedings — else may lose the ground.
Brick kiln order passed without notice to landlords (third parties) NJ violated — persons adversely affected must be given opportunity. MC Mehta v UOI (2000) 7 SCC 422 Third-party adversely affected by GST order (e.g., ITC reversal affecting supplier) must also get opportunity.
Date of birth changed unilaterally without notice to employee Administrative order involving civil consequences must be made in conformity with NJ. Sarjoo Prasad v General Manager AIR 1981 SC 1481 GST registration cancellation / ITC blocking affecting civil rights — mandatory NJ compliance.
Order where opportunity afforded but not utilised No NJ violation — if opportunity was given and not availed, no grievance. Nagar Palika Nataur v UP PST (1998) 2 SCC 400 If assessee was given hearing date but did not attend and seeks to challenge later on NJ grounds — difficult to succeed.
⚠️ KEY RULE — When order struck down for NJ  Order vacated by virtue of inherent defect BUT proceedings are NOT terminated. Matter is left open for fresh hearing. Appellate Authority may set aside and require fresh decision de novo. [Guduthur Bros. v ITO AIR 1960 SC 1320]
12.4.2 — Denial of a Norm of Audi Alteram Partem
📋 Denial of a Norm — When Hearing Given but a Component Violated
📝 This section covers situations where a hearing was given — but one component of a fair hearing was denied: relevant documents not shown, cross-examination denied, reasons not given, or show cause notice was inadequate.
Components of Fair Hearing — Any One May Be Violated
Relevant documents not shown to party
Cross-examination of witnesses denied
Reasons not given in order
Show cause notice inadequate / vague
Personal hearing not given when required by statute
TWO RIDERS before giving relief for breach of norm:
(1) Whether any prejudice has been caused to the petitioner by breach of the norm of natural justice?
(2) Did the petitioner raise the matter of breach before the adjudicative body itself? (waiver question)
📝 Prejudice must be real — courts do not give relief for mere technical violations where no harm was actually caused. And if you stayed silent during the hearing about the breach, you may be held to have waived your right.
THEORETICAL POSITION
Denial of a norm of NJ is in itself sufficient prejudice
No further proof of prejudice should be needed
"It is no argument to deny natural justice that hearing would have made no difference"
📝 The denial of fair procedure is the harm itself. You should not need to show additionally that the outcome would have been different.
PRACTICAL POSITION
Courts have in practice required proof of real prejudice
K.L. Tripathi v State Bank of India (1984): real prejudice must be shown in case of cross-examination denial
Chandrama Tiwari v UOI: non-supply of documents — no quashing as "did not prejudice appellant"
💡 GST: Non-supply of relied-upon documents in SCN — assert prejudice: "We were unable to effectively defend because we could not verify/rebut the figures in documents." Mere assertion of non-supply is not enough — specify how outcome could have differed.
TRAVANCORE RAYONS v UOI — AIR 1971 SC 862
Central Government acting as appellate body from Collector of Excise — failed to give personal hearing to assessee company — Supreme Court quashed the decision.
💡 GST direct parallel: Commissioner (Appeals) must give personal hearing when required. If personal hearing opportunity is granted in notice but not actually given at hearing stage — order is liable to be quashed. Always attend hearings and record attendance.
SINE QUA NON PRINCIPLE
An adequate notice is the sine qua non of natural justice.
Inadequate notice = denial of natural justice.
A show cause notice that merely repeats statutory language without giving specific facts = inadequate.
Such a notice gives party no true idea of what will be considered → order should be quashed.
📝 Sine qua non = without which, nothing. An adequate SCN is the minimum foundation — without it, the entire proceeding is built on sand. The SCN must specifically state what you are charged with, based on what evidence, so you can respond meaningfully.
💡 GST: SCN which merely says "it appears you have suppressed facts — show cause why penalty should not be imposed" without specifying WHAT facts, WHICH transactions, WHICH period = inadequate SCN = NJ violation = order liable to be quashed. JMA Industries v UOI AIR 1980 Del 200.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 12 | Failure of Natural Justice
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, First Appellate Authority and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Ridge v Baldwin
House of Lords | [1964] AC 40 | Lord Reid
Key Ratio
"Time and again in the cases I have cited it has been stated that a decision given without regard to the principles of natural justice is void... I see no reason to doubt these authorities. The body with the power to decide cannot lawfully proceed to make a decision until it has afforded to the person affected a proper opportunity to state his case."
When to Use — Opening argument in any GST challenge where no hearing/inadequate hearing was given before passing SCN or adjudication order. Cite to establish that the foundational legal position is: no hearing = void order, not merely voidable.
General Medical Council v Spackman | Lord Wright
House of Lords | [1943] AC 627 | as cited in Wade and Forsyth, Administrative Law, 9th Edn., p.507
Key Ratio
"If the principles of natural justice are violated in respect of any decision it is, indeed, immaterial whether the same decision would have been arrived at in the absence of the departure from the essential principles of justice. The decision must be declared to be no decision."
When to Use — When Department argues "even if we had given you a hearing, the outcome would have been the same." Counter with this ratio: the violation itself is fatal — the outcome-irrelevance argument is expressly rejected by law.
Nawabkhan v State of Gujarat
