Supreme Court of India | AIR 1974 SC 1471 | (1974) 2 SCC 121
Key Ratio
"Where hearing is obligated by a statute which affects the fundamental right of a citizen, the duty to give the hearing sounds in constitutional requirement and failure to comply with such a duty is fatal." An order infringing a constitutionally guaranteed right made without hearing the party affected, where hearing was required, would be void ab initio and of no legal efficacy to bind the parties from the very beginning. When a competent court holds such official act or order invalid, it operates from nativity — the impugned act or order was never valid.
When to Use — Where GST order affects fundamental rights (e.g., arbitrary cancellation of registration without hearing — destroys right to trade under Art.19). Cite to argue order = void ab initio, operates from nativity.
Management, MS Nally Bharat Engg. Co. v State of Bihar
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1990 SC 325 | (1990) 2 SCC 48
Key Ratio
"Denial of that opportunity is a fatal flaw to the decision of the government." The Supreme Court very categorically stated that the party who is denied natural justice "need not establish particular prejudice for want of such opportunity" because "the principles of natural justice know no exclusionary rule dependent on whether it would have made any difference if natural justice has been observed. The non-observance of natural justice is itself prejudice to any man and proof of prejudice independently of proof of denial of natural justice is unnecessary."
When to Use — Strongest card when Department argues "no prejudice shown." Counter: non-observance of NJ is itself prejudice — no separate proof of prejudice is required. Use in GST adjudication challenges and writ petitions.
Anisminic Ltd. v Foreign Compensation Commissioner
House of Lords | [1967] 3 WLR 382 | Lord Diplock
Key Ratio
Failure of natural justice goes to the jurisdiction of the body concerned and gives rise to a jurisdictional error. Therefore, its decision is a nullity which a privative clause in the relevant statute cannot protect. A breach of the rules of natural justice should render the decision void.
When to Use — When Department argues CGST Act remedies are sufficient and writ is not maintainable. Cite to establish that breach of NJ = jurisdictional error → writ maintainable before High Court even if alternative remedy exists.
Tibhuvandas Bhimji Zaveri v Collector of Central Excise
Supreme Court of India | (1997) 11 SCC 276 | Para 9
Key Ratio
To say that the documents were not readily available with the officer is no ground to deny vital information to a person who is to be visited with a penalty. The Supreme Court held that in failure to supply that important piece of information the principle of natural justice would stand violated.
When to Use — In GST cases where GST officer relies on third-party data, DGGI reports, cross-verification data, ASMT-10 information etc. in SCN but refuses to provide copies. Cite to demand and obtain relied-upon documents before adjudication.
Allahabad High Court — MES Co. v State, AIR 1975 All 29
Allahabad High Court | AIR 1975 All 29
Key Ratio
"There is a world of difference between a hearing given before the determination is made by an authority and a hearing given after such determination has been made."
When to Use — When Department offers a post-order personal hearing as a cure for pre-order violation of NJ. Argue: post-decisional hearing is not equivalent to pre-decisional hearing. The decision has already been made — any subsequent hearing is an empty formality as the deciding officer is unlikely to change a concluded view.
Sine Qua Non Principle — Adequate Notice
Legal Principle | JMA Industries v UOI, AIR 1980 Del 200 | Board of Technical Education v Dhanwantari Kumar AIR 1991 SC 271
Key Principle
An adequate notice is the sine qua non of natural justice. A show cause notice that merely repeats statutory language without giving specific facts is inadequate — the party can have no true idea of what facts or circumstances the authority will consider. Such a notice gives the party no meaningful opportunity to respond and the order based on it should be quashed. A vague notice = denial of natural justice.
When to Use — When GST SCN does not specify the basis of tax demand, which transactions are disputed, what evidence is relied upon, or merely reproduces statutory language. Argue: SCN is inadequate, amounts to denial of NJ, order is liable to be quashed and remanded for fresh proceedings with adequate SCN.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter 12 — Failure of Natural Justice

⚖️ Chapter 12 — Failure of Natural Justice

Sections 12.4.3 to 12.8 | Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Violation
Condition / Unless / If
Optional / Discretionary
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
12.4.3 Giving of Reasons
12.4.3A Three Situations
12.5 Additional Grounds — Original Order
12.6 Curing at Appellate Stage
12.6A Leary v Calvin Principle
12.7 Waiver
12.7A SC Review — State Bank of Patiala
12.8 Audi Alteram Partem
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
12.4.3 — Giving of Reasons by Quasi-Judicial Authority
⚖️ Giving of Reasons — Core Principle
Quasi-Judicial Authority | Adjudicative Bodies
It is now well established: a quasi-judicial authority MUST give a reasoned decision
📝 Any government officer or tribunal deciding your case must explain WHY they decided against you. A bare "dismissed" or "rejected" without reasons is not acceptable in law.
GENERAL RULE
Judicial approach: quash administrative order for failure to give reasons
📝 Courts automatically set aside orders that give no explanation for the decision.
CAVEAT
Not always — in some cases where order was quashed, other factors also existed (non-application of mind, audi alteram partem failure, other illegality)
📝 Absence of reasons alone may not always be the sole ground of quashing — courts have looked at the whole picture.
DISTINCTION
Failure of audi alteram partem ≠ Failure to give reasons
(bilateral act vs unilateral act)
📝 Not being heard = bilateral (both you and authority must act). No reasons = unilateral (only authority's defect, you had no role left).
Advantages of Quashing Even Where Failure = Only Non-Supply of Reasons
(a) Administration obliged to give reasons WITH the order — introduces fairness element
(b) Individual gets incidental advantage (e.g. dismissal from service cases)
(c) Psychological satisfaction — vindicating rights
(d) By lapse of time, authority may change views
(e) Where multiple adverse parties — quashing forces fresh consideration
PRINCIPLE
Fairness of procedure is assured ONLY by passing a just decision supported by reasons.
💡 GST Example: SCN adjudication order saying "demand confirmed" without addressing your reply, without discussing each objection → non-speaking order → liable to be quashed. Officer cannot supply reasons later in an affidavit filed in court.
12.4.3A — Three Situations of Failure to Give Reasons
📋 Three Situations — Non-Supply of Reasons
SITUATION (i)
No reasons given at all — not to party, not to court
Goes to root of order — may indicate non-application of mind
Normal Rule: Non-speaking order be quashed; authority directed to examine matter afresh
📝 "Afresh" ≠ de novo hearing. Authority re-examines and gives reasoned order. You may get new opportunity for oral/written submissions.
KEY CASE
MP Industries v UOI (AIR 1966 SC 671) — Mining licence refused without reasons — first significant case; court did not quash due to fresh applications invited
KEY CASE
Bhagat Raja v UOI (AIR 1967 SC 1606) — Appellate authority used a pro forma rejection slip; no reasons. SC quashed and directed fresh decision
Travancore Rayons v UOI — Excise duty assessment; SC quashed for failure to give reasons and remanded
💡 GST: DRC-07 (demand order) with no discussion of assessee's reply → Situation (i) → quashable as non-speaking order.
SITUATION (ii)
Reasons on record but NOT communicated to party
No uniform judicial approach — cases go both ways
Upheld
Ahmedabad Municipality v Ramanlal — Eviction order; reasons on record not communicated. SC did NOT quash but warned municipality
Nandram Hunatram v UOI — Mine partners quarrelling; govt took over without reasons. Upheld — facts were clear
Strict View
Ajantha Industries v CBDT (AIR 1976 SC 437) — u/s 127 IT Act, recording reasons in file NOT sufficient — must give reasons to affected party. Order quashed.
📝 Reasons are NOT for the file. Reasons are for the person being decided against. If they're not told, how can they challenge the order?
Calcutta HC — Order without giving reasons = void ab initio; cannot become valid by giving reasons in affidavit filed in HC in response to writ petition.
💡 GST: Officer writes detailed reasons in his file but order says "demand confirmed". Department cannot now file affidavit in HC supplying those reasons — order remains void.
SITUATION (iii)
Reasons supplied to party after undue delay but before matter comes to court
Similar to Situation (ii) — delay causes financial loss; party does not know which remedy to take
Example: Customs duty dispute — authority decides against importer but delays reasons → importer cannot clear goods, suffers wharfage charges
📝 Late reasons = practical denial of justice even if reasons are eventually given. The damage — financial loss, missed limitation — is already done.
Suggested Rule (Academic / Reform Perspective)
REFORM SUGGESTION
Courts should propound a simple mandatory rule: giving reasons is mandatory as part of natural justice — otherwise the decision is not a 'decision' at all.
Exception: national security cases only.
📝 The existing judicial approach (upholding non-speaking orders in some cases) is criticized as soft — it encourages administrators to avoid giving reasons. A strict rule is needed to develop the culture of speaking orders.
💡 GST Litigation Relevance: In GST adjudication, Section 75(6) CGST Act requires the officer to pass a "speaking order". Non-compliance → order vulnerable. Use Bhagat Raja and Travancore Rayons to argue for quashing.
12.5 — Additional Grounds Not Contained in Original Order
🚫 Additional Grounds — Cannot Supplement Original Order
📝 If a government body passes an order based on specific reasons, it cannot add new reasons later when you challenge it in court. The order must stand or fall on the reasons given at the time it was passed.
CORE RULE
When a statutory functionary makes an order based on certain grounds, its validity must be judged by those reasons alone. CANNOT be supplemented by fresh reasons in shape of affidavit or otherwise.
📝 Department files reply in your writ petition adding 3 new reasons for the demand that were NOT in the SCN/adjudication order. These new reasons cannot save the order.
Key Case: 63 Technologies Ltd v UOI (2019) 18 SCC 401
Facts: FMC passed amalgamation order for NSEL-FTIL under S.396 Companies Act. Draft order published → objections heard → final order passed
Defect: First & second grounds in final order were NOT in draft order. Stakeholders had no opportunity to object to those new grounds
Held: Failure of natural justice. New grounds given before court were DIFFERENT from grounds stated in impugned order. Order set aside.
GST APPLICATION
Department's adjudication order raised demands on Ground A. In reply to writ petition, Department adds Grounds B and C. Assessee can argue: validity of order must be judged by Ground A alone; Grounds B and C cannot be imported to save the order. Cite 63 Technologies.
💡 SCN issued on the ground of "short payment of GST". Adjudication order confirmed demand on same ground. Department cannot then argue in HC that demand also justified on ITC reversal grounds (a ground not in SCN).
12.6 — Curing Failure of Natural Justice at Appellate Stage
🔄 Can Appellate Body Cure Original NJ Failure?
📝 If a lower authority decided without hearing you, can the higher appellate authority cure that defect by giving you a hearing on appeal? This question has two different answers depending on which principle applies — Leary or Calvin.
GENERAL RULE — LEARY PRINCIPLE
Failure of natural justice at original stage CANNOT be cured by giving sufficient natural justice at appellate stage.
Order of lower body = nullity. Even if affirmed by upper tribunal, BOTH orders fall.
📝 If you never got a fair trial in the first court, you cannot be forced to accept a fair appeal in the second court. The right to a fair trial AND a right of appeal are separate rights — you cannot be deprived of one.
LEARY PRINCIPLE — Exact Words (Megarry J)
"If one accepts the contention that a defect of natural justice in the trial body can be cured by the appellate body providing natural justice, then it deprives the person concerned of his right of appeal from the trial body. If the law gives a person the right to a fair trial and a right of appeal; why should he be satisfied with an unjust trial and a fair appeal?"
📝 Leary v National Union of Vehicle Builders (1970) 2 All ER 713 — member expelled from trade union without hearing → appellate hearing cannot cure that original defect.
Indian Cases Following Leary
Laxmidhar v State of Orissa (AIR 1974 Ori 127) — Trial body violated NJ → appellate hearing by same body cannot remedy → entire proceeding vitiated
Farid Ahmed v Ahmedabad Municipality (AIR 1976 SC 2095) — Commissioner didn't hear objectors; approval by State Govt cannot cure → Appeal to civil court also not complete substitute
Shri Mandir Sita Ramji v Govt of Delhi (AIR 1974 SC 1868) — Collector (recommendatory authority) gave no hearing → duty to hear cannot be excused merely because he doesn't decide
L.K. Ratna (AIR 1987 SC 71) — CA Institute punished member without hearing. HC appeal cannot cure → Damage to professional reputation immediate and far-reaching, can never be completely salvaged
💡 GST: Adjudication order passed without considering reply to SCN (audi alteram partem violation). Assessee appeals to First Appellate Authority (FAA). FAA gives full hearing. Can FAA cure original defect? As per Leary = NO. Assessee retains right to challenge original order as void. Apply Farid Ahmed.
12.6A — Leary v Calvin — Qualified Endorsement (Privy Council 1979)
⚖️ Calvin v Carr (1979) — Qualified Departure from Leary
📝 Calvin v Carr (1979) says: sometimes, a defect at the original stage can be cured by a fair appellate hearing — but ONLY in specific limited situations. This is a narrow exception to Leary.
PRIVY COUNCIL HELD
No clear, absolute or automatic rule can be laid down. Courts must examine the hearing process as a whole — original and appeal — and decide whether the complainant had a fair deal of the kind bargained for.
Three Inherent Limitations of Calvin Ruling
LIMIT 1
Applies ONLY to consensual bodies — social clubs, associations — NOT government / statutory bodies
LIMIT 2
Two-tier adjudicatory system — first tier decides, second tier hears appeal from that original decision
LIMIT 3
Appeal must be by way of rehearing de novo — not limited only to points of law
Leary vs Calvin — At a Glance
AspectLeary (Default Rule)Calvin (Exception)
Applies toAll bodies — government, statutory, domesticConsensual domestic bodies only (clubs, associations)
Type of appealAny appeal — even full rehearingMust be de novo rehearing
Original defect cured?NO — original order is nullityYES — if both levels together = fair deal
India preferenceDominant judicial trend — Leary appliesOnly in exceptional policy cases (Sahu/Olga Tellis)
WHY CALVIN NOT SUITABLE IN INDIA
(1) Adjudication in India done by government bodies / statutory bodies → outside Calvin's scope
(2) Even professional bodies like ICAI are statutory, not consensual
(3) Provisions for appeal are rare; rehearing de novo is rarer still
(4) Judicial review by writ petition ≠ rehearing de novo
💡 GST: First Appellate Authority is a statutory government authority. Calvin principle cannot apply. If adjudication order violated NJ, the FAA hearing cannot cure it. Assessee can maintain that original order is a nullity, even if FAA gave full hearing. Apply Leary.
12.7 — Waiver of Right to Be Heard
🙋 Waiver — When Can You Lose the Right to Complain?
📝 Waiver means voluntarily giving up a right. If you were given a chance to be heard but chose not to show up, you cannot later say your natural justice was violated. BUT — you cannot waive a right you didn't even know you had.
WHEN WAIVER APPLIES
Party given SCN/hearing notice → does NOT reply → does NOT appear
= Treated as having waived right to be heard
→ Authority may proceed ex parte
📝 Everyone has a right to waive a protection law gives him. If you ignore three notices and don't turn up, you cannot later claim denial of natural justice.
💡 GST: Assessee gets SCN, hearing notice. Does not respond. DRC-07 passed ex parte. Assessee cannot challenge on NJ grounds — deemed waiver (subject to condition of knowledge).
WHEN WAIVER CANNOT APPLY
(1) Failure of NJ makes decision void — void orders cannot become valid by acquiescence
(2) Basheshar Nath v ITO (AIR 1959 SC 149) — Right to be heard = Part of Art. 14 Constitution → cannot be waived by affected party
(3) Defect of jurisdiction cannot be cured by acquiescence of parties
(4) Mayes v Mayes: "A rule of natural justice going to the very basis of judicature CANNOT be waived. One cannot convert a nullity into a validity."
(5) Bias — existence of bias renders entire proceedings void; cannot be cured even if fairness of appellate authority is beyond dispute
📝 Bias is so fundamental that no waiver is possible — even if you didn't object to the biased officer at the time of hearing.
KEY CONDITION FOR WAIVER
For waiver to arise:
(A) Party must KNOW that NJ norm is being infringed
(B) Party must KNOW he has a right to object
Problem: Many parties are unrepresented by lawyers before adjudicating bodies → they don't know NJ norms are being violated → cannot be treated as having waived
📝 If you didn't know you had a right to object at that point, how can you be said to have given it up? A person cannot waive a right unless he knows he has such a right.
NJ norms are vague — even lawyers cannot always be sure whether a norm is being infringed
💡 GST Hearing: Officer conducts hearing but doesn't allow assessee to file written submissions (a facet of NJ). Assessee doesn't object then. Can Department later argue "waiver"? NO — assessee may not have known he had the right to insist on written submissions. Courts should be "extremely slow and circumspect" in entertaining waiver argument.
12.7A — SC Review of NJ Authorities — State Bank of Patiala v SK Sharma (1996) 3 SCC 364
🏛️ State Bank of Patiala v S.K. Sharma — Master Framework
Constitution Bench | Jeevan Reddy J | (1996) 3 SCC 364
📝 This landmark SC judgment surveyed all authorities on NJ violations and laid down a comprehensive set of principles governing disciplinary enquiries. While in a service law context, the principles apply equally to GST adjudication and quasi-judicial proceedings.
Critical Distinction — Two Categories of NJ Violations
CATEGORY 1 — TOTAL VIOLATION
"NO NOTICE" / "NO HEARING" / "NO OPPORTUNITY"

Ridge v Baldwin — dismissed without hearing altogether
RESULT: Order is INVALID / VOID. Authority must rehear matter afresh according to law.
💡 GST: SCN issued and adjudication order passed on the same day without waiting for reply — total violation. Order void.
CATEGORY 2 — VIOLATION OF A FACET
"NO ADEQUATE HEARING" / "NO FAIR OPPORTUNITY"

Examples: not supplying enquiry officer's report, not affording cross-examination opportunity
TEST: Touchstone of Prejudice — whether person received a fair hearing considering all things
💡 GST: Adjudicating authority didn't explicitly address one argument in reply but addressed all other major grounds — question is whether overall hearing was fair.
33 Principles Summarised (Disciplinary Enquiry Context — equally applicable to quasi-judicial proceedings)
#PrincipleTest / Consequence
(1)Violation of rules/regulations governing disciplinary enquiry → order should NOT be set aside automaticallyCourt must enquire: (a) Substantive or (b) Procedural provision?
(2)Substantive provision → must normally be complied withSubstantial compliance / prejudice test does NOT apply
(3)Procedural provision → designed for reasonable opportunityComplaint examined from standpoint of prejudice
(4a)Procedural provision — NOT mandatory characterExamine from standpoint of substantial compliance; set aside only if prejudice caused
(4b)Procedural provision — MANDATORY characterCheck: (i) conceived in interest of person or public? (ii) Was it waived? If not waived — make appropriate directions incl. setting aside
(5)No governing rules — only obligation = observe NJDistinguish: total violation (void) vs violation of facet (prejudice test)
(6)While applying audi alteram partemAlways keep in mind overriding objective: ensure no failure of justice
(7)Public/State interest considerationsCourt may balance public interest with NJ requirements — curtail rule of audi alteram partem if necessary
ULTIMATE TEST — ALWAYS
"The ultimate test is always the same, viz., test of prejudice or the test of fair hearing, as it may be called."
📝 The entire NJ jurisprudence reduces to one question: Was the person denied a fair hearing? If yes, and it caused prejudice — order falls. If no prejudice — order stands despite procedural irregularity.
💡 GST Adjudication: (a) Total violation = SCN not served at all / adjudication without any hearing → void order → Category 1. (b) Facet violation = hearing given but written submissions not allowed → test of prejudice → Category 2. Argue: "We were prejudiced because we could not submit the documents/submissions that would have changed the outcome."
12.8 — Audi Alteram Partem (Hear the Other Side)
👂 Audi Alteram Partem — The Right to Be Heard
📝 "Audi alteram partem" = Latin for "hear the other side". One of the two pillars of natural justice. Every person whose rights are being decided must be given notice of the case against him and a chance to respond to it.
Basic Requirements
Each party must have reasonable notice of the case he has to meet
Must be given an opportunity to state his case
Must be able to answer arguments put forward against him
SCOPE
Applies to ALL statutory functions — administrative, judicial or quasi-judicial — wherever holders of power are assigned duty to decide rights of individuals.
Forms of Unfairness Covered by this Maxim
Going back on an assurance given to party
Misleading advice given to party
Unreasonable manner in which decision is reached
Doctrine of Legitimate Expectation — courts apply when public bodies acted in a manner making it unfair to go back on the decision challenged
GST CONTEXT
Section 75(4) CGST Act: Opportunity of being heard SHALL be granted where request is made by person chargeable with tax / penalty. Section 75(6): Speaking order mandatory. Both are statutory codifications of audi alteram partem.
💡 Legitimate Expectation: CBIC issues Circular saying a particular activity is not taxable. Assessee acts on it. Later, CBIC withdraws circular and Department issues SCN for past periods. Assessee can argue legitimate expectation — it would be unfair to go back on the clear representation made by CBIC. Audi alteram partem + legitimate expectation = NJ violation.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 12 | Failure of Natural Justice
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, First Appellate Authority, and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Bhagat Raja v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1967 SC 1606 | (1971) 3 SCC 162
Key Ratio — Non-Speaking Order
The appellate authority (Central Government) used a pre-printed form to reject a mining lease application — this constituted no reasons at all. The Supreme Court quashed the order for failure to give reasons and directed the Central Government to decide the matter afresh. A pro forma rejection is no decision in law.
When to Use — When the adjudication order/OIO is a template order that does not address your specific reply, your specific submissions, or gives only bald conclusions. Argue: this is a non-speaking order equivalent to the pro forma form in Bhagat Raja — quashable on its face.
Travancore Rayons v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1971 SC 862 | (1969) 2 SCC 868
Key Ratio — Mandatory Speaking Order in Revenue Matters
In a case involving assessment of excise duty, the Supreme Court quashed the government order for failure to give reasons and remanded the matter. It was declared that giving of reasons for quasi-judicial orders is mandatory — in the absence of such reasons, the orders would be quashed by the courts.
When to Use — In GST adjudication orders where the officer merely confirms the demand without addressing the reply/submissions. This is the foundational authority for the rule that a quasi-judicial order in revenue matters must be a speaking order giving reasons. Section 75(6) CGST codifies this principle.
Ajantha Industries v Central Board of Direct Taxes
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1976 SC 437 | (1976) 1 SCC 1001
Key Ratio — Reasons Must Be Communicated, Not Merely Recorded
Merely recording reasons in the file is not sufficient. It is essential to give the reasons to the affected party. If reasons are not communicated to the person affected, how is he to know whether he ought to challenge the order or not, and if to challenge it, on what grounds?
When to Use — When the Department argues that the OIO file contains detailed reasons that were not mentioned in the order itself (reasons disclosed only in the reply affidavit to writ petition). Cite Ajantha Industries to argue: reasons in the file do not help the assessee; they must be in the order communicated to the assessee.
63 Technologies Ltd v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | (2019) 18 SCC 401
Key Ratio — No Additional Grounds in Original Order
When a statutory functionary makes an order based on certain grounds, its validity must be judged by the reasons so mentioned and cannot be supplemented by fresh reasons in the shape of affidavit or otherwise. Grounds not contained in the original order cannot be introduced later to validate it — failure of natural justice.
When to Use — When the Department, in the reply to your writ petition/appeal, tries to introduce new reasons for the GST demand that were not in the SCN or OIO. Argue: these grounds were not before the assessee at the time of adjudication; they cannot now be used to justify the order; failure of natural justice as assessee had no opportunity to respond to these new grounds.
Leary v National Union of Vehicle Builders
(1970) 2 All ER 713 | Megarry J | Followed extensively in India
Key Ratio — Appellate Cure of NJ Failure Impermissible
"If one accepts the contention that a defect of natural justice in the trial body can be cured by the appellate body providing natural justice, then it deprives the person concerned of his right of appeal from the trial body. If the law gives a person the right to a fair trial and the right of appeal; why should he be told that he ought to be satisfied with an unjust trial and a fair appeal?"
When to Use — When the Department argues before FAA/GSTAT that even if the OIO was passed without hearing, the appellate authority is now giving full hearing, so the defect is cured. Counter: Leary principle — original defect cannot be cured; the OIO remains void ab initio; assessee's right of appeal from original order is a separate right that cannot be traded for a fair appellate hearing.
Farid Ahmed v Ahmedabad Municipality
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1976 SC 2095 | (1976) 3 SCC 719
Key Ratio — Appellate Approval Cannot Cure Initial Invalidity
If the order was at its inception invalid, its invalidity cannot be cured by its approval by the standing committee and confirmation by the State Government. An appeal is not a complete substitute for the right to a personal hearing by the competent authority at the original stage.
When to Use — When the OIO was passed without proper hearing at adjudication level. Department argues that FAA hearing cures the defect. Use Farid Ahmed: initial invalidity cannot be cured by subsequent appellate approval. The proper course is to remit the matter to the original authority for fresh consideration.
Institute of Chartered Accountants of India v L.K. Ratna
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1987 SC 71 | (1986) 4 SCC 537
Key Ratio — Irreparable Damage from Initial NJ Violation
An order passed after an unfair trial causes to the person concerned immediate and serious injury which is not capable of being completely erased when the error committed in the initial proceedings is later corrected in appeal. It is necessary "to ensure that there is no breach of fundamental procedure in the original proceeding, and to avoid treating an appeal as an overall substitute for the original proceeding."
When to Use — When arguing that the damage caused by the invalid OIO (wrong demand on your record, reputational damage, attachment of bank accounts) cannot be erased even by a successful appeal. This supports seeking immediate stay of the OIO and not being forced to first exhaust appellate remedies against a void order.
State Bank of Patiala v S.K. Sharma — SLP Framework
Supreme Court of India | (1996) 3 SCC 364 | Constitution Bench | Jeevan Reddy J
Key Ratio — Ultimate Test = Fair Hearing
The principles of natural justice know of no exclusionary rule dependent on whether it would have made any difference if natural justice had been observed. The non-observance of natural justice is itself prejudice to any man. The ultimate test is always the same — test of prejudice or the test of fair hearing — whether the person has, in totality of circumstances, received a fair hearing. Courts do not act in vain — but where there is something of substance lost by the failure, a remedy lies.
When to Use — Comprehensive authority for all NJ-based challenges. When Department argues "no prejudice shown" in a GST hearing, cite S.K. Sharma (Category 1): "Non-observance of natural justice is itself prejudice." For facet violations (Category 2), cite the prejudice test: show specifically how the assessee was denied the opportunity to put forward a case that could have changed the outcome.
Bombay Oil Industries Pvt Ltd v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1984 SC 160 | (1984) 1 SCC 141
Key Ratio — Speaking Orders Essential for Public Faith
"The faith of the people in administrative tribunals can be sustained only if tribunals act fairly and dispose of matters before them by well considered orders." The government must "give good reasons in support of the order and not merely state its bald conclusion." Denial of opportunity to meet the other man's case is denial of natural justice.
When to Use — Before Commissioners/Adjudicating Officers and in appeal when urging the requirement of a speaking order. Particularly powerful in emphasizing that GST adjudication orders that merely confirm the demand figures without addressing the assessee's substantive legal arguments are not "well-considered orders" as required by this SC judgment.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter XIII — Administrative Adjudication (I)

⚖️ Chapter XIII — Administrative Adjudication (I)

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Advantage
Defect / Risk / Criticism
Condition / Requirement
Alternative / Optional
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
13.1 Reasons for Growth
13.2 Structure, Powers & Procedure
13.3 Tribunals
13.4 Franks Committee
13.5 Articles 323A & 323B
13.6.1 Direct Taxes — ITAT
13.6.2 Customs & Excise — CEGAT
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
13.1 — Reasons for Growth of Administrative Adjudication
⚖️ Administrative Adjudication — Growth & Reasons
Why adjudication power shifted from Courts → Administrative Bodies
📝 Courts were overwhelmed. Modern government had to tax, regulate, and settle millions of disputes quickly. Administrative bodies were created to handle these — faster, cheaper, and with more expertise than regular courts.
ROOT CAUSE: Expansion of Governmental Functions
Planning & Social Services — government took on welfare, employment conditions, health & safety
Tax Proliferation — new and new taxes → vast proliferation of assessing authorities → disputes with taxpayers
New Socio-economic Laws — modifying interpersonal rights & obligations → person at issue with state or another citizen
REASONS Legislature Entrusted Adjudication to Non-Court Bodies (inter alia)
✓ SPEED
Courts overburdened — law's delay proverbial. Admin bodies: speedy, cheap, decentralised disposal
📝 If all disputes went to courts, the system would collapse. As Franks Committee said: tribunals "positively contributed to the preservation of our ordinary judicial system."
✓ EXPERTISE
Judges are generalists. Admin bodies: experts in tax, accountancy, service law, technical subjects. Better fact-finding in specialised areas
💡 Under Tariff Act — determining customs duty on a commodity requires knowledge of science & technology, not just law. A specialist body decides better than a generalist judge.
✓ POLICY FLEXIBILITY
Courts bound by law. Admin bodies apply "public interest", "reasonable", "fair", "adequate" — policy-based standards that courts cannot adequately handle
📝 What is "fair" or "adequate"? These depend on socio-economic policy, foreign exchange position, competing priorities — policy decisions, not pure legal analysis.
✓ INFORMALITY
Court atmosphere formal, rigid, expensive. Admin adjudication: informal, accessible, no elaborate procedural rigidity — ideal for modern admin disputes
✓ UNIFORMITY
By continuous experience with specific activity, tribunal members develop expertise, consistency and uniformity of decisions
⚠️ PITFALL
Rule of law compromised when judicial powers transferred from courts to administrative bodies. Admin body member cannot have the same independence and objectivity as a judge. No cross-examination, no published law reports, no appeal on facts as in court system.
📝 Administrative adjudication is a necessary evil — useful but dangerous. Cases have gone on for 20+ years before adjudicatory bodies without being settled. Over-reliance has its own pitfalls.
13.2 — Structure, Powers and Procedure of Adjudicatory Bodies
🏛️ Adjudicatory Bodies — Structure, Nature & Procedure
NOMENCLATURE: Administrative Adjudication vs Quasi-Judicial
"Administrative Adjudication" / "Administrative Justice" — broad term for all adjudication by non-court bodies connected with administration
"Quasi-Judicial Bodies" — bodies following both judicial and administrative procedure. Term commonly used in India. They follow norms of natural justice.
📝 "Quasi" = almost. Quasi-judicial means almost-judicial — follows some court-like procedure (notice, hearing, reasoned order) but not all court rules.
"Administrative" bodies — take decisions affecting individuals without observing norms of natural justice. Technically different from quasi-judicial.
LEGAL BASIS — Adjudicatory Bodies Owe Existence to Statute
DIRECT Created by express statutory provision
INDIRECT Created by rules promulgated by executive under legislative power conferred by a statute
PROCEDURE — Common Features & Diversity
COMMON
All follow norms of natural justice at some stage — prescribed by parent Act, subsidiary legislation, or implied
CIVIL COURT POWERS
Many bodies vested with powers of civil court — summoning witnesses, examining on oath, compelling production of documents
SIMPLE PROCEDURE
Most follow simple procedure — examine without oath, legal representation not always permitted
NO UNIFORM STANDARD
Bewildering variety of procedures. No attempt made in India to establish minimum procedural standards uniformly applicable to all bodies
STRUCTURE — Hierarchical Adjudicatory System
Lower official/body → First Decision
Appeal → Higher official/body
OR: Review by higher official (instead of appeal)
Appeal to Court (on point of law)
Some decisions declared "final" — but constitutional provisions (Art 32/136/226/227) always available for judicial control
KEY PRINCIPLE An order passed on the dictates of a higher authority, being wholly without jurisdiction, is a nullity. Quasi-judicial authority must pose and answer the right question — has it jurisdiction in the matter?
📝 Even if a senior officer directs a quasi-judicial officer to decide a case in a particular way — that direction is illegal. The quasi-judicial officer must apply independent mind. If he obeys blindly, the order is void.
💡 Relevant to GST: Adjudicating Authority in SCN proceedings must independently apply mind. An order passed under pressure from Commissioner or directions of higher authority — can be challenged as nullity before Appellate Authority / High Court.
13.3 — Tribunals — Nature, Objectives & Contrasts with Courts
🏛️ Tribunal — Definition, Objectives & Characteristics
📝 A tribunal is the best dispute-settling mechanism outside the court system. It is separate from the Administration, decides cases impartially, and is more formal than bureaucrats but less formal than courts.
OBJECTIVES of Establishing Tribunals
(1) Provide dispute-settling mechanism as alternative to ordinary courts — court substitute to relieve work-load
(2) Transfer settling of disputes from administrative officials to autonomous body — independent element in adjudication — improve quality of decisions
(3) Provide appellate forum from first-instance official decisions — control mechanism over Administration
TRIBUNAL vs COURT — Key Contrasts
FeatureCourtTribunal
ProcedureBound by CPC, CrPC, Evidence ActNot generally bound by procedural/evidence laws
Public ProceedingsOpen to public as a matter of courseNot generally required to be conducted in public
JurisdictionGeneral jurisdictionSpecialised jurisdiction
Legal RepresentationLawyers entitled as a matter of courseMay have statutory prohibition on lawyers (though often not so)
Appointment/ControlJudges independent; removal by impeachment onlyMembers appointed & controlled almost entirely by executive
Security of TenureRetire at fixed age (65/62)Short term appointment (5-7 years); may not be re-appointed
Reasoned DecisionsMust give reasons; published in law reportsOften do not give reasons; decisions may not be published
Independence from BiasNo departmental bias; impartial and objectiveSome links with Administration — may put objectivity to test
ADVANTAGES of Tribunal over Departmental Adjudication
Free from departmental bias — autonomous of the dept charged with implementing the law
Insulates decision from current political pressures — handover to autonomous tribunal
Specialist — by deciding cases repeatedly in specific area, becomes expert body → uniformity, efficiency, predictability
Control mechanism — reviews departmental decisions; independent tribunal provides safeguard against administrative abuse
DEFECTS of Tribunal System
(a) Some links with Administration — objectivity tested when government is party; (b) Non-lawyer members may find interpreting statute difficult — base decisions on surmise; (c) Too much informality → whimsical decisions; (d) Tribunals often don't give reasons; (e) Commitment to departmental policies may cause ignoring individual case merits
FRANKS COMMITTEE PRINCIPLE
"Preference should be given to ordinary courts of law rather than to a tribunal unless there are demonstrably special reasons which make a tribunal more appropriate." — A tribunal is not as good a mechanism as a court for deciding disputes.
MOVEMENT PRINCIPLE
Movement of dispute settlement should be: Administrator → Tribunal → Court. Even if first decision given to administrator, provision should always be made for appeal to tribunal or court. Tribunal preferred to departmental adjudication except where overwhelming policy considerations are involved.
📝 Franks Committee: Tribunal decisions are predictable (apply principles of law) → citizen knows where he stands = Rule of Law. Minister's decision may be "unpredictable" and "arbitrary" = antithesis of rule of law. Where a decision can be made according to "rules or principles" — give it to a tribunal.
TRIBUNALS REFORMS ACT 2021 — Key Features
Non-obstante clause — Central Government may by notification prescribe qualifications, appointments, salaries, conditions of service of Chairperson & Members
Minimum age 50 years for appointment as Chairman/Member
Search-cum-Select Committee — CJI or SC Judge nominated by CJI as Chairman; two Government Secretaries as members
Term of office — Chairperson: 4 years or age 70, whichever earlier; Member: 4 years or age 67, whichever earlier
Challenged by Madras Bar Association — as damaging basic features of Constitution, violating separation of powers & judicial independence
Rojer Mathew v South Indian Bank Ltd. (2020) 6 SCC 1
Lack of judicial dominance in appointment of Members & Presiding Officers of tribunals = against constitutional scheme & independence of judiciary. Exclusion of executive control over judiciary extends to tribunals — tribunals are alternative to courts and perform judicial functions. Tribunals must have similar standards of appointment as courts to inspire confidence of public.
13.4 — Franks Committee — Openness, Fairness & Impartiality
📋 Franks Committee (UK, 1955-57) — Tribunal Reform Blueprint
Three Standards: Openness | Fairness | Impartiality
📝 UK appointed Franks Committee to review tribunals. Its report is the foundation of modern tribunal jurisprudence — even Indian courts and the Indian Constitution draw from it. The three standards it prescribed are now universal benchmarks.
BASIC APPROACH — Tribunals are NOT appendages of Government
Committee refused to treat tribunals as "part of the machinery of Administration" or "appendages of government departments." Anchored tribunals firmly within machinery of justice rather than Administration. Tribunals are independent of departments "in their independence of departments and in their functions they are akin to the regular courts."
THREE CORE STANDARDS — Meaning & Application
OPENNESS
Publicity of proceedings + knowledge of essential reasoning underlying decisions. Proceedings must not be wholly secret.
📝 You must be able to see the tribunal work and understand why it decided as it did. Secret proceedings lack basis for public confidence.
FAIRNESS
Clear procedure enabling parties to know their rights, present case fully, and know the case they have to meet.
📝 You must know the case against you and have a real opportunity to answer it. "If the objector were not allowed to state his case, there would be nothing to stop oppression."
IMPARTIALITY
Freedom of tribunals from influence (real or apparent) of departments concerned with subject-matter of decisions.
📝 "How can the citizen be satisfied unless he feels that those who decide his case come to their decision with open minds?" If the department is a party — it cannot also be the judge.
KEY RECOMMENDATIONS (Implemented in Tribunals & Inquiries Act 1958/1971)
Chairmen of tribunals should have legal qualifications — objectivity in treatment of cases and proper sifting of facts best secured by legally qualified Chairman
Hearings should be in public (exceptions only for good reason)
Parties must be allowed to be represented by lawyers (barring most exceptional circumstances)
Tribunal decisions must be reasoned and as full as possible
Council on Tribunals — standing permanent body to supervise constitution & procedure of all tribunals under constant review
CRITICAL WARNING — Arbitrary Decisions
A decision made "without principle, without any rules" is "unpredictable" and "arbitrary" — it is the antithesis of a decision taken in accordance with the rule of law. A tribunal as deciding authority (over Minister) is "itself a considerable step towards the realisation of openness, fairness and impartiality."
💡 GST Relevance: Adjudicating Officer who issues SCN and then passes OIO (Order-in-Original) must follow these three standards. An OIO which is unreasoned, biased toward the department (which framed SCN), or does not address assessee's submissions — violates Franks principles and natural justice. Ground for appeal.
13.5 — Articles 323A & 323B of the Indian Constitution
📜 Articles 323A & 323B — Constitutional Basis for Indian Tribunal System
Added by 42nd Constitutional Amendment, 1976
Article 323A
Parliament may establish tribunals for adjudication of disputes re: recruitment & conditions of service of persons in public service (Central, State, local authority, Govt corporation)

Only Parliament empowered (not State Legislature)

SC in UOI v Parma Nanda: Only ONE tribunal for Centre & ONE for each State or two or more States put together. No hierarchy of tribunals under 323A.
Article 323B
Empowers appropriate legislature (Parliament OR State Legislature) to provide for adjudication/trial of disputes re: (i) taxation; (ii) foreign exchange; (iii) import/export; (iv) industrial & labour disputes; (v) land reforms; (vi) ceiling on urban property; (vii) elections to Parliament/State Legislature; (viii) essential commodities; (ix) offences against above laws; (x) incidental matters

Can exclude jurisdiction of all courts except SC under Art 136. Can establish hierarchy of tribunals.
Sampath Kumar v UOI — AIR 1987 SC 386 — SEMINAL DECISION
KEY HOLDING
Judicial review is "part of the basic structure of the Constitution" and a "vital principle." BUT — Parliament CAN set up an alternative institution in place of High Court for providing for judicial review — provided the alternative is a "real substitute" — "not only in form and de jure but in content and de facto." The tribunal "must be a worthy successor of the High Court in all respects."
Bhagwati CJ Test
"If any constitutional amendment made by Parliament takes away from the High Court the power of judicial review in any particular area and vests it in any other institutional mechanism or authority, it would not be violative of the basic structure doctrine, so long as the essential condition is fulfilled, namely, that the alternative institutional mechanism or authority set up by the Parliamentary amendment is no less effective than the High Court."
Sampath Kumar Directions — To ensure CAT is "real substitute" for High Court
(1) Every bench = one judicial member + one administrative member. No preponderance of administrative members on any bench
(2) Chairman must have legal/judicial experience — ordinarily a retiring/retired Chief Justice of HC or senior Judge. Secretary to Govt having 2 years experience = unconstitutional Chairman
(3) Vice-Chairman — any one eligible to be HC Judge (district judge or advocate)
(4) Appointment of Chairman, Vice-Chairman & admin members — only after consultation with CJI; OR high-powered selection committee with sitting SC Judge nominated by CJI
(5) Five-year tenure too short — creates disincentive for competent persons; tribunal needs permanent bench at every HC seat
GST/TAX ADJUDICATION RELEVANCE
Under Art 323B, GSTAT (GST Appellate Tribunal) is established. The Sampath Kumar principles apply — GSTAT must be a real substitute for High Court. Independence from executive control in appointments, adequate judicial members, reasoned decisions — all are constitutional requirements for GSTAT as well.
💡 GSTAT — Goods & Services Tax Appellate Tribunal — is constituted under Sections 109–111 CGST Act, a body under Art 323B read with CGST Act. Challenge to composition, tenure, appointment of GSTAT members can be mounted citing Sampath Kumar / Rojer Mathew principles.
13.6.1 — Direct Taxes Assessment & Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT)
💰 Income Tax Adjudication Hierarchy & ITAT
ASSESSMENT HIERARCHY — Income Tax (same for Wealth & Gift Tax)
Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT)
Highest executive authority. Supervisory role. Issues orders, instructions, directions to subordinate officers for uniform implementation
Commissioners
Supervisory role. Also have revision power u/s 263 — can revise assessing officer's order within 2 years if erroneous & prejudicial to Revenue
Assessing Officer (AO)
Deputy Commissioner / Asst Commissioner / ITO. Passes assessment order. Quasi-judicial in nature — must give fair hearing. Best judgment assessment u/s 144 if assessee fails to respond
Appeal — Dy Commissioner (Appeals) or Commissioner (Appeals)
Within 30 days of assessment order. Quasi-judicial — natural justice must be followed. Power to confirm/reduce/enhance/annul assessment. Order must be in writing with reasons
ITAT — Income Tax Appellate Tribunal
Final appellate authority. Both assessee AND assessing officer can appeal. Power of widest amplitude — "it thinks fit" (s.254). Final fact-finding authority. No regular appeal — only reference to HC on question of law
ITAT — Constitution & Key Features
Established by Income Tax (Amendment) Act, 1939. Functions under Income-tax Act 1961. Started 1941. NOT an income tax authority — not subject to CBDT control or directions
Composition: Judicial Members (10 yrs judicial post / Central Legal Service / 10 yrs advocate) + Accountant Members (10 yrs CA practice / 3 yrs as Commissioner). Bench = 1 Judicial + 1 Accountant member
Independence ensured — functions under Ministry of Law, NOT Ministry of Finance. Section 119 CBDT cannot issue instructions TO Tribunal. Decides according to own wisdom & discretion without departmental interference
~45,000 cases per year. Only 10% go to High Courts. Hearing fees nominal. Informal atmosphere. Not bound by technical rules of evidence. Necessary expertise & specialisation in direct tax law & accountancy
ITAT — Powers & Jurisdiction
FACT Final fact-finding authority. Court has no jurisdiction to re-appreciate evidence on record. Findings can be challenged only if: (a) no evidence to support them; OR (b) finding is perverse
LAW Not final on questions of law. Party may apply for reference to High Court on question of law arising out of Tribunal's order. HC jurisdiction purely advisory — not appellate/revisional
Rectification u/s 254(2) — mistake apparent from record only. Cannot review its own order beyond rectification
Cannot pronounce on constitutional validity/vires of IT Act — such question to be raised only in suit or writ petition
PRINCIPLE — Best Judgment Assessment
AO must make an honest and fair estimate of income — must not be capricious. The "belief" of AO for reopening assessment u/s 147 must be based on "specific, reliable and relevant information" — not on vague, indefinite, remote or far-fetched information.
💡 GST Relevance — Parallel to GST: Section 144 best judgment assessment under GST is quasi-judicial. Same principle applies — cannot be capricious, must be based on reliable information. AO/Proper Officer must give notice and opportunity. Order must give reasons. GSTAT is equivalent to ITAT for GST disputes.
13.6.2 — Assessment of Customs & Excise | CEGAT | Adjudicatory Principles
🛃 Customs & Excise Adjudication — Historical Evolution & CEGAT
Including SC Principles on Quasi-Judicial Bodies
DEPARTMENTAL ADJUDICATION — Problems that led to CEGAT
Till 1980 — CBEC heard appeals; Central Government (Ministry of Finance) heard revision petitions. Purely departmental adjudication — officials acted in dual capacity as tax administrators AND Revenue Department
Revenue considerations inevitably supervened at every level — parties deprived of completely unbiased hearing
SC in Bharat Barrel & Drum v Collector — "no proper investigation of the dispute." Government in revision "should have given appellants adequate opportunity to prove" their case — did not do so
SUPREME COURT IN SIEMENS — Major Principles for Quasi-Judicial Bodies
PRINCIPLE 1
A quasi-judicial authority must record reasons in support of its order. Failure to give reasons is in "plain disregard of the requirement of law."
📝 Even if the decision is correct — if it doesn't explain WHY, it is legally defective. Reasons allow the aggrieved party to challenge the order in appeal.
PRINCIPLE 2
Administrative authorities and tribunals must accord fair and proper hearing to persons sought to be affected by their orders
PRINCIPLE 3
The rule requiring reasons in support of order is a basic principle of natural justice which must inform every quasi-judicial process. "Mere pretence of compliance" will not satisfy the requirement of law.
PRINCIPLE 4 (Collector of Customs v Ganga Setty)
Courts will give due deference to executive determination — will interfere only when executive adopts "a construction which no reasonable person could adopt" or which is "perverse"
CEGAT — Customs Excise & Gold (Control) Appellate Tribunal
Created in 1980 on lines of ITAT. Hears appeals from Customs, Excise & Gold Control authorities. Conceived on same model as ITAT. Reference procedure to High Court on question of law (same as ITAT). Presently: Customs, Excise & Service Tax Appellate Tribunal (CESTAT) — direct predecessor institution to GSTAT in terms of model and jurisprudence.
SIEMENS SUGGESTION — SC suggested creation of independent quasi-judicial tribunal for Customs & Excise — "like the Income Tax Appellate Tribunal or the Foreign Exchange Regulation Appellate Board — which would finally dispose of appeals and revision applications instead of leaving the determination to the Government of India. An independent quasi-judicial tribunal would definitely inspire greater confidence in the public mind."
💡 GST — GSTAT is the statutory successor to CESTAT for indirect taxes. All principles evolved for Customs/Excise adjudication (natural justice, reasoned orders, independence, impartiality) apply with full force to GST adjudication before Proper Officers, Appellate Authorities, and GSTAT.
KEY PRINCIPLE — Confiscation vs Criminal Prosecution
Confiscation of goods by Customs authorities does NOT bar prosecution for criminal offence — Customs authorities are not a "judicial tribunal." Action taken by them does not constitute "judgment or order of a court or judicial tribunal" — so Art 20(2) (double jeopardy) does not apply. [Maqbool Hussain v State of Bombay — AIR 1953 SC 325]
📝 In GST — this principle means: Even if goods are seized/confiscated and tax/penalty is paid in administrative proceedings, a separate criminal prosecution u/s 132 CGST Act is possible. Payment in quasi-judicial proceedings does not bar criminal prosecution.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter XIII | Administrative Adjudication
These ratios and principles are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, Appellate Authorities, GSTAT, and High Courts. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Siemens Engineering & Mfg. Co. v UOI — AIR 1976 SC 1785
Supreme Court of India | Bhagwati J | Quasi-Judicial Bodies — Duty to Give Reasons
Key Ratio
"The rule requiring reasons to be given in support of an order is a basic principle of natural justice which must inform every quasi-judicial process. It should be observed in its proper spirit and mere pretence of compliance with it would not satisfy the requirement of law."
Further Ratio
Administrative authorities and tribunals must accord fair and proper hearing to persons sought to be affected by their orders and give sufficiently clear and explicit reasons in support of their orders. That way, the quasi-judicial bodies will carry credibility with the people by inspiring confidence in the adjudicatory process.
When to Use — When OIO/OIA is unreasoned, cryptic, or does not address submissions made in reply to SCN. Cite to argue that an unreasoned order is void — violates basic principle of natural justice. Even appellate orders must be reasoned.
Collector of Customs v Ganga Setty — AIR 1963 SC 1319
Supreme Court of India | Classification / Expert Authority Determination
Key Ratio
"It is primarily for the Import Control authorities to determine the head or entry under which any particular commodity fell; but that if in doing so, these authorities adopted a construction which no reasonable person could adopt, i.e. if the construction was perverse, then it was a case in which the Court was competent to interfere."
When to Use — When challenging Department's classification/HSN determination as perverse. Argue: the construction adopted by the Proper Officer is one which no reasonable person could adopt. Triggers judicial review even on classification questions. Also useful when Department's interpretation of exemption notification is unreasonable.
Rojer Mathew v South Indian Bank Ltd. — (2020) 6 SCC 1
Supreme Court of India | Constitution Bench | Tribunal Independence & Separation of Powers
Key Ratio
Lack of judicial dominance in the appointment process of Members and Presiding Officers of tribunals is against the constitutional scheme and independence of judiciary. Exclusion of executive control over judiciary is not limited to traditional courts alone but extends also to tribunals because tribunals are formed as an alternative to courts and perform judicial functions. Tribunals constituted in substitution of courts should have similar standards of appointment, qualification and conditions of service to inspire confidence of the public at large.
When to Use — When challenging composition, appointment process, or independence of GSTAT. Cite to argue that a GSTAT Bench lacking adequate judicial members, or where appointments were made without CJI consultation, is not a "real substitute" for High Court and does not command constitutional legitimacy.
S.P. Sampath Kumar v UOI — AIR 1987 SC 386
Supreme Court of India | Constitution Bench | Tribunal as Substitute for High Court
Key Ratio — Bhagwati CJ
"If any constitutional amendment made by Parliament takes away from the High Court the power of judicial review in any particular area and vests it in any other institutional mechanism or authority, it would not be violative of the basic structure doctrine, so long as the essential condition is fulfilled, namely, that the alternative institutional mechanism or authority set up by the Parliamentary amendment is no less effective than the High Court."
Further Ratio
"We cannot afford to forget that it is the High Court which is being supplanted by the Administrative Tribunal and it must be so manned as to inspire confidence in the public mind that it is highly competent and expert mechanism with judicial approach and objectivity."
When to Use — In writ petitions challenging GSTAT orders or composition. Argue GSTAT must be a real, effective substitute for HC. If it is not adequately constituted (insufficient judicial members, executive-controlled appointments), HC jurisdiction is not ousted and writ lies despite the statutory scheme.
Franks Committee Principles — "Openness, Fairness and Impartiality"
UK Franks Committee Report 1957 | Adopted by Indian Courts | Natural Justice in Tribunal Procedure
Key Principle
"Openness" requires publicity of proceedings and knowledge of essential reasoning underlying decisions. "Fairness" requires adoption of a clear procedure enabling parties to know their rights, to present their case fully and to know the case which they have to meet. "Impartiality" demands freedom of tribunals from the influence, real or apparent, of departments concerned with the subject-matter of their decisions. A decision made "without principle, without any rules" is "unpredictable" and "arbitrary" — the antithesis of a decision taken in accordance with the rule of law.
When to Use — Universal ground for challenging any adjudicatory order. (a) SCN which does not disclose grounds = violation of Fairness. (b) OIO which does not address submissions = violation of Openness. (c) Adjudicating Officer who is also part of the investigating team = violation of Impartiality. These are natural justice grounds maintainable at every level.
Panel Narshi Thakershi v Pradumansinghji Arjunsinghji — AIR 1970 SC 1273
Supreme Court of India | Hedge J | Consequence of Entrusting Judicial Work Without Judicial Training
Key Ratio — Hedge J (biting comment)
"This case illustrates the consequence of entrusting judicial work to those who had no judicial training and background. A simple question whether the family of the respondent was divided or undivided has been pending for about 20 years."
When to Use — When arguing that adjudicating officers without legal/judicial background are not qualified to decide complex legal questions of GST law. Supports plea that GSTAT/Appellate Authority must be constituted with adequate judicial members. Also supports challenge to orders passed by technically untrained Proper Officers on complex questions of classification, valuation, or time of supply.
Mahadayal v Commercial Tax Officer — AIR 1958 SC 667
Supreme Court of India | Departmental Bias — Administrator as Judge in Own Cause
Key Principle
When an administrator sits as a decision maker, he is imbued, consciously or unconsciously, with bias towards official policies or administrative expediency. It is not possible for him to rise above a pro-departmental attitude because of his intimate relation with the department as he is its integral part. Neutrality of action in a controversy between the department and an individual is difficult to expect from him. Even if no directions may be legally issued to him while he discharges a quasi-judicial function, still his senior may, and often does, issue instructions to him behind the scene.
When to Use — When challenging Adjudicating Authority's order as biased. The Adjudicating Officer who issued SCN and also passes OIO is essentially a judge in his own cause. Cite to argue that the adjudication system within the Department cannot be trusted to be fair — strengthens case for GSTAT and HC jurisdiction. Also useful to show systemic bias when Department loses and the Proper Officer is under pressure from seniors.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter 13 — Administrative Adjudication (I)

⚖️ Chapter 13 — Administrative Adjudication (I)

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📄 Pages Covered: 818 – 861
Permitted / Valid / Powers
Prohibited / Restricted
Condition / Qualification / If
Optional / Appellate Route
Neutral Reference / Procedure
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
13.6 Overview — Types of Adjudicatory Bodies
13.6.1 Customs/Excise Tribunal (CEGAT)
13.6.3 Nationalisation Compensation Tribunals
13.6.4 Labour Disputes — Overview & Labour Codes
13.6.4.2–13.6.4.6 Labour Acts (Payment of Wages / ESI / Compensation / Min. Wages / IDA)
13.6.5 Motor Transport (Claims Tribunals & Licensing)
13.6.6 Railways Claims Tribunal
13.6.7 Electricity Regulatory Commission
13.6.8–13.6.9 Copyright & FERA Appellate Board
13.6.10–13.6.11 Court of Survey & Economic Regulation (IDR / NCLT / MRTP / Ess. Commodities)
13.6.12 Rent Control
13.6.13 Professional Regulation (Bar, ICAI, Press Council, Medical, Architecture, Dental)
13.6.14–13.6.15 Commodity Boards & CAT
13.6.16–13.6.18 Consumer Protection, FERA & Competition Commission
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
13.6 — Overview: Types of Administrative Adjudicatory Bodies
⚖️ Administrative Adjudication (I)
Chapter 13 | Pages 818–861 | Principles of Administrative Law
📝 "Administrative adjudication" means resolving disputes not through regular civil courts but through special tribunals or authorities created by specific laws. These bodies are faster, cheaper, and have domain expertise compared to ordinary courts.
Major Categories of Adjudicatory Bodies in India
1
Taxation Tribunals
Customs, Excise, Income Tax
2
Labour Tribunals
150+ labour enactments
3
Compensation Tribunals
Nationalisation laws
4
Service Tribunals
CAT / SAT / JAT
5
Consumer / Economic
MRTP, Consumer Forums, CCI
6
Professional Bodies
Bar Council, ICAI, MCI etc.
COMMON FEATURES
Guided by Natural Justice | Indian Evidence Act usually NOT applicable | Powers of Civil Court for summoning witnesses & documents | Lawyers can appear | Subject to writ jurisdiction of High Courts (Art. 226/227) and SC (Art. 32/136)
📝 Even though these bodies are not regular courts, they must follow principles of fairness (natural justice). The Evidence Act formalities don't apply, making proceedings more flexible. But High Courts and Supreme Court can still review their decisions through writs.
13.6.1 — Customs & Excise Tribunal (CEGAT) | Pages 818–819
🏛️ CEGAT — Customs, Excise & Gold (Control) Appellate Tribunal
📝 CEGAT was proposed as a specialized tribunal to replace the ad hoc mechanism for hearing customs and excise appeals. It would take over jurisdiction from the Customs & Gold Control Tribunal.
👥 Composition & Tenure
President + Members
President: cannot be removed except by order of President of India on ground of misbehaviour/incapacity
After ceasing office: Eligible only for Tribunal President reappointment — NOT other Central/State employment
CANNOT appear, act or plead before the Tribunal after ceasing office
Salaries fixed by Central Govt — cannot be varied to disadvantage after appointment
📝 These protections ensure the tribunal members are independent — they cannot be pressured by the government since their pay and service conditions are secure.
⚖️ Jurisdiction
Appeals against:
(a) Decisions by Collector of Customs as adjudicating authority
(b) Orders by Collector (Appeals) under Ss. 128A / 129D of Customs Act — where rate of duty or valuation is in issue
(c) Excise matters as well
Powers of Civil Court
Summon witnesses | Discovery & inspection of documents | Review own decisions
Can punish for contempt like a High Court under Contempt of Courts Act
📋 Procedure & Orders
NOT bound by Civil Procedure Code — guided by principles of natural justice
Regulates its own procedure
Orders: Confirm / Modify / Annul the decision appealed against OR remand back with directions for fresh adjudication
Difference of opinion → reference to President → majority decision of all members who heard the matter
🔍 Judicial Review Position
PROPOSED TRIBUNAL
Judicial review RESTRICTED
No High Court jurisdiction
Only Supreme Court jurisdiction
CONTRAST: CEGAT (1982)
Reference to High Court on point of law → then appeal to Supreme Court
High Court jurisdiction exists
📝 The proposed new tribunal was to be a "one-stop" forum with only SC review — this was the main departure from existing CEGAT where the High Court was also involved.
Law Commission 115th Report: Suggested Central Tax Court for indirect taxes under Art. 136 (NOT under the statute) — Commission NOT in favour of tribunal under the Act (1986)
13.6.3 — Nationalisation Laws: Compensation Tribunals | Pages 819–820
🏭 Compensation Tribunals Under Nationalisation Laws
📝 When the government took over (nationalised) private industries like airlines, insurance, coal mines — those private owners needed compensation. Special tribunals were created to determine the fair amount and settle disputes about it.
✈️ Air Corporations Act, 1953
Air India + Indian Airlines created
All air transport companies nationalised
Compensation first determined by Corporation with govt approval
If not accepted → reference to Tribunal (3 members: 1 HC/SC Judge + 1 expert)
Tribunal: Civil court powers | Can review own decision | Decision final — no appeal to any court
Decision final & not challengeable in any court — autonomous body
🏛️ LIC Act, 1956
Life Insurance business taken over by LIC
Compensation for insurers → similar procedure
Tribunal: 3 members (1 HC/SC Judge + 1 expert)
Amount fixed by Corporation + Central Govt approval first
If insurer rejects → reference to Tribunal → Tribunal acquires jurisdiction
Civil court powers: oath, discovery, witnesses, commissions
⛏️ Coal Bearing Areas Act, 1957
Central Govt can acquire coal bearing land + mining leases
Compensation → agreement first
If no agreement → Tribunal (1 HC-qualified person)
Central Govt may nominate mining expert to assist
Tribunal makes award: amount + person to whom payable
Appeal: to High Court within 30 days of award
🌾 Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863
Special Court: uneven number of persons (min. 3)
1 member = District Court Judge or principal civil court presiding officer
Indian Evidence Act does NOT apply
Regulated by Civil Procedure Code (as far as possible)
Judicial review by regular court EXCLUDED
BUT: Court may refer question of law/usage to High Court (mandatory if principle of general importance or affects rights of a class)
13.6.4 — Labour Disputes: Overview & Labour Codes 2019–2020 | Pages 821–822
👷 Labour Disputes — Adjudicatory Framework
📝 After 1947, the government deliberately kept labour disputes OUT of ordinary courts. The idea was that courts apply contracts strictly, but labour tribunals look at what the employer-employee relationship OUGHT to be — a more flexible, justice-based approach.
KEY FACT 150+ labour enactments (Central + State) | Each contains own judicial/quasi-judicial authorities | Named differently: Commissioner, Authority, Court, Tribunal | Functions overlap; no clear-cut procedures
The Four Labour Codes (2019–2020)
CODE 1
Code on Wages, 2019
Consolidates: Minimum Wages Act 1948 + Payment of Wages Act 1936
S.45: Authority (gazetted officer rank) to hear claims | S.49: Appellate authority (one rank higher) | Time limit: 3 years from cause of action | Appeal: 90 days
CODE 2
Industrial Relations Code, 2020
Consolidates 3 labour laws incl. IDA 1947
Abolishes Labour Courts → only Industrial Tribunals
S.44: Judicial Member + Administrative Member (Joint Secretary rank minimum)
Division Bench for: Standing Orders, dismissal, retrenchment, closure, strikes/lockouts, inter/intra union disputes
CODE 3
Code on Social Security, 2020
Consolidates 9 enactments incl. Employees' Compensation Act 1923 & ESI Act 1948
S.91: Competent authority for employees' compensation
Qualifications: Judicial service (5 yrs) OR gazetted officer (5 yrs) OR advocate (5 yrs) with HR/legal experience
S.99: Appeal to HC in 6 categories (lump sum, interest, damages, redemption, distribution, indemnity, registration)
CODE 4
Code on Occupational Safety, Health & Working Conditions, 2020
NOTE Post Roger Mathew v South Indian Bank Ltd (SC) — several Finance Act 2017 tribunal provisions set aside → changes made to conform to constitutional requirements for tribunals
📝 In Roger Mathew, SC held that tribunal members cannot be on short-term contracts — they need security of tenure to be truly independent. The Labour Codes incorporate these principles.
13.6.4.2 to 13.6.4.6 — Individual Labour Acts: Authorities & Procedure | Pages 823–829
Act Authority / Qualification Key Powers Time Limit Appeal
Payment of Wages Act, 1936 Commissioner for Workmen's Compensation OR officer with civil court / stipendiary magistrate / labour court experience — appointed by State Govt Exclusive jurisdiction for illegal deductions/delay in wages | Civil court powers: evidence, witnesses, documents | Fine recoverable as magistrate's fine | Can entertain applications beyond 6 months for sufficient cause 6 months from date of unauthorised deduction Appeal: District Court or Court of Small Causes → then High Court (questions of law)
Employees' Compensation Act, 1923 Commissioner appointed by State Govt — no formal qualifications prescribed Determines liability of employer to injured workman | Can take assistance of medical expert | Civil court powers: evidence on oath, witnesses, documents | Can recover as arrears of land revenue | Can award costs at discretion Application with prescribed fee; question dispute must have arisen first Limited appeal to HC on substantial question of law | Conflict among HCs: whether Commissioner is a "court" u/s 115 CPC — Bombay HC holds Commissioner is a court, MP HC holds otherwise
Minimum Wages Act, 1948 Commissioner for Workmen's Compensation OR Labour Commissioner OR Civil court/Stipendiary magistrate experience — appointed by appropriate govt Hears claims for less than minimum wages | Orders payment of discrepancy + compensation up to 10x discrepancy | Civil court powers | Deemed civil court for ss.195, Ch.XXXV CrPC 6 months from date minimum wage became payable (condonable) NO provision for appeal to any higher forum or court
ESI Act, 1948 Employees' Insurance Court | Judges: judicial officer OR legal practitioner of 5 years' standing — appointed by State Govt Decides disputes/claims under the statute | Civil court powers: witnesses, oath, discovery, documents | Can award costs | Order enforceable as civil court decree | Poor persons: can institute proceedings without court fees Prescribed by rules Submit question of law to HC | Any order involving substantial question of law → appealable to HC
Industrial Disputes Act, 1947 3 types: Labour Court, Industrial Tribunal, National Industrial Tribunal | Each = single person (presiding officer) with judicial experience | Two disqualifications: not independent OR age >65 | Govt maintains list of suitable persons (recommended by concerned HC) Jurisdiction only when REFERRED by Govt (not directly approachable) | Terms of reference determine scope | Ex parte award can be set aside on fresh merits application within 30 days of publication | Govt can appoint 2 assessors | S.23: General embargo on strikes/lockouts during proceedings and 2 months after Award submitted to Govt → published within 30 days → enforceable after 30 days of publication (or 90 days if Govt modifies) Awards enjoy FINALITY — IDA envisages NO appeal | But subject to writ jurisdiction HC (Art.226/227) and SC (Art.32/136)
⚠️ LABOUR APPELLATE TRIBUNAL A Central Labour Appellate Tribunal was set up in 1950 (4 benches) to bring uniformity but was abolished in 1956 as slow and expensive. Since then SC under Art.136 has been the appellate forum — criticised as SC lacks means to adequately deal with socio-economic labour questions. Law Commission 122nd Report: Suggested setting up Industrial Relations Commissions (Central + State) with original & appellate jurisdiction under Arts. 323B(2)(c) and 323B(3)(d).
💡 SC has said: "If the Labour Appellate Tribunal had continued by now the Labour Jurisprudence would have developed on much more satisfactory lines than what it is to-day. There is a great need today to revive and to bring into existence an All-India Labour Appellate Tribunal."
13.6.5 — Motor Transport: Claims Tribunals & Licensing Authorities | Pages 831–834
🚗 Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 — Two Adjudicatory Areas
⚕️ 13.6.5.1 Motor Accident Claims Tribunals (MACT)
PURPOSE
Cheap & quick remedy for accident compensation — avoiding protracted civil court suits & ad valorem court fees
COMPOSITION
Sitting/Retired HC Judges OR qualified to be so OR Sitting/Retired District Judges | Generally 1-member tribunals — District/Sessions Judges designated by notification
3 REGIMES OF COMPENSATION
(1) S.140: Absolute liability — fixed sum for death/permanent disablement (now proposed to be repealed)
(2) S.163A: Strict liability — annual income <₹40,000; no need to prove negligence; contributory negligence no bar
(3) S.168: Tort principle — "just" compensation
S.164 (Amendment 2019): Minimum ₹5 lakhs for death, ₹2.5 lakhs for grievous hurt (not yet notified)
Powers of civil court: oath, witnesses, discovery, documents | Deemed civil court u/s 195 & Ch.XXVI CrPC | S.168(3): Person ordered to pay MUST deposit entire amount within 30 days
Appeal to HC within 90 days | No appeal if amount <₹10,000 | No civil court jurisdiction once MACT established | Proceedings characterised as a "suit" (within Limitation Act s.6)
Claims Tribunal is a civil court subordinate to HC for purposes of CPC s.115 → HC revisional jurisdiction available
📝 Although the Tribunal's order is called an "award" it is NOT arbitration — it is more like a civil court decree. The Tribunal is an independent forum, not part of the administration.
🪪 13.6.5.2 Licensing of Transport Vehicles
AUTHORITIES
State Transport Authority (STA) + Regional Transport Authorities (RTA)
Each: Chairman with judicial experience + up to 4 members (State) or 2 (Regional) | No person with financial interest in transport can be member
Application for permit → to RTA
RTA must: state written reasons for refusal + give opportunity of being heard | Permit can be suspended/cancelled on specified grounds with reasonable opportunity
State Transport Appellate Tribunal
Hears appeals from STA and RTA orders | Composition: judicial officer not below District Judge rank | Aggrieved person may appeal within prescribed time | Decision FINAL
Revision: Tribunal can revise any order within 30 days | Even after 30 days if "good and sufficient" cause shown | Every Tribunal order: final
No civil court jurisdiction for permit matters | No injunction by civil court against authorities under the Act
INTERSTATE
Motor Vehicles Act 1939: Interstate Transport Commission (Chairman + ≥2 members) — appointed by Central Govt | New Act 1988 also incorporates provisions
13.6.6 — Railway Claims Tribunal | Pages 834–836
🚂 Railway Claims Tribunal — Railway Claims Tribunal Act, 1987
📝 Before this tribunal was set up, nearly 60,000 suits against railways were pending in civil courts. The tribunal was created to provide a special fast forum and settle claims within 6 months.
⚖️ Jurisdiction
(a) Claims relating to responsibility of railway administration as carrier:
— Compensation for loss/destruction/damage/deterioration/non-delivery of animals or goods
— Compensation under s.82A Railways Act
(b) Refund of fares or freight paid
Civil court jurisdiction BARRED in matters within Tribunal's jurisdiction (S.15)
👥 Composition
Chairman: Must be or have been HC Judge (OR 2 yrs as Vice-Chairman)
Vice-Chairmen (4): HC Judge / Indian Legal Service Grade I (5 yrs) / civil judicial post at Joint Secretary pay scale (5 yrs) / Railway administration post at Joint Secretary level (5 yrs) / Judicial or Technical Member (3 yrs)
Judicial Members: HC Judge / ILS Grade I (3 yrs) / civil judicial post at Joint Secretary level (3 yrs)
Technical Members: Railway post at Joint Secretary level (3 yrs) with adequate claims & commercial knowledge
No Chairman appointment without CJI consultation | Salaries cannot be varied to disadvantage
📋 Procedure & Powers
Indian Evidence Act does NOT apply | Powers of civil court: summon witnesses, documents | Bench: 1 Judicial + 1 Technical member | Can regulate own procedure | Can review own decisions | Lawyers can appear
Indigent person: Cannot be refused jurisdiction merely because he cannot pay fees — Tribunal must follow Order XXIII CPC to entertain his claim
Appeals from Tribunal lie to HC having jurisdiction over the place where the Bench is located
📝 The Tribunal is subordinate to the HC. It is hoped to settle claims in 6 months — far faster than civil courts where cases used to drag on for years.
13.6.7 — Electricity Regulatory Commission | Pages 836–837
⚡ WB Electricity Regulatory Commission — Electricity Regulatory Commission Act, 1998
NATURE OF COMMISSION
Expert body — determination of tariff requires knowledge of law, engineering, finance, commerce, economics and management. Technical and factual questions dominate.
🏛️ Key Supreme Court Principles on Appellate Forum for Electricity Commissions
SC OBSERVED
Neither HC nor SC would in reality be appropriate appellate forums for the factual and technical matters arising in electricity regulation
SC RECOMMENDED
Appellate power should be conferred on Central Electricity Regulatory Commission OR a similar expert body (having judicial + technical members with varied qualifications)
SIMILAR MODEL
TRAI Act, 1997: Special Appellate Tribunal → then further appeal to SC on questions of law only
