COMMISSION POWERS Applies principles of 1948 Electricity Supply Act (Ss.57, 57-A, Schedule VI) | Can weigh these with its own regulations | If error — HC under s.27 of 1998 Act can interfere as appellate authority | Not necessary to invoke s.30 after framing regulations — applies principles already incorporated in its regulations case-by-case
UP STATE ELECTRICITY BOARD — KEY RULING
An autonomous statutory board CANNOT be directed by the State Government to appoint/absorb a retrenched employee. Govt had no authority. Courts must avoid making stringent observations/strictures against officials except in exceptional circumstances.
13.6.8–13.6.9 — Copyright Board & FERA Appellate Board | Pages 837–839
📚 Copyright & Foreign Exchange Appellate Bodies
©️ 13.6.8 Copyright — Copyright Act, 1957
Two adjudicative bodies:
(1) Registrar of Copyrights: administrator + quasi-judicial powers
(2) Copyright Board (now substituted)
AMENDMENT Tribunal Reforms Act, 2021: Copyright Board SUBSTITUTED by Commercial Court constituted under Commercial Courts Act OR Commercial Division of HC
Reference to Appellate Board DELETED
Appeals against final decisions/orders of Registrar → HC | No further appeal | Both Registrar & Board have powers: summon witnesses, discovery, production of documents
📝 The Registrar's order confiscating goods imported from abroad (infringing copyright) has been held quasi-judicial in character (Gramaphone Co. of India v Birendra Bahadur Pandey, SC 1984).
💱 13.6.9 FERA Appellate Board (1973 Act — now replaced by FEMA 1999)
Director of Enforcement: Initial adjudication — adjudges penalty under FERA; can direct confiscation
Must give opportunity of being heard: appear in person, adduce evidence, examine/cross-examine witnesses
Fentandez Case
Inquiry QUASHED: Director failed to allow accused to cross-examine persons who gave statements against him → inquiry rendered unfair & invalid
Appellate Board: Chairman + max 4 members
Chairman qualifications: civil judicial post (10 yrs) OR Central Legal Service not below Grade I (3 yrs) OR advocate (10 yrs)
In practice: Addl. Secretary, Ministry of Law (Chairman) + 2 Joint Secretaries, Min. of Finance → all civil servants
CRITICISM: Board consists entirely of civil servants — CANNOT be characterised as an autonomous tribunal | BUT: For Art.136 purposes, Board IS regarded as a tribunal
Decision of Appellate Board final EXCEPT appeal to HC on questions of law | Contravention of Board/Director orders made penal
13.6.9.1 — Enforcement Directorate: Nature of Proceedings
KEY PRINCIPLE (SC ruling)
Proceedings under s.23(1)(a) FERA are ADJUDICATORY in nature — NOT criminal proceedings | ED officers are NOT judges of Criminal Courts | They perform quasi-judicial functions | They determine liability of contravener for breach of "civil obligations" — NOT commission of an offence | Penalty imposed ≠ fine as result of prosecution | Therefore, MENS REA is NOT an essential ingredient under s.23(1)(a) FERA
📝 This is crucial: since FERA proceedings are not criminal, the burden of proof is different and the accused cannot claim the protections of criminal law (like presumption of innocence in the same way). The officer adjudicates like a civil authority.
💡 Relevant for GST: GST adjudication proceedings (SCN/adjudication) are similarly adjudicatory — not criminal — so department need not prove mens rea for evasion. This principle from FERA has been applied in GST contexts to uphold penalties even without fraudulent intent being conclusively proved.
13.6.10–13.6.11 — Court of Survey & Economic Regulation (IDR / NCLT / MRTP / Essential Commodities) | Pages 839–843
🏭 Economic Regulation & Specialized Adjudicatory Bodies
⚓ 13.6.10 Court of Survey
(Merchant Shipping Act, 1958 Ss.383–387)
Hears appeals from surveyor's actions: (i) inspection report disputed by ship owner (ii) notice of defect (iii) refusal to grant certificate
Composition: 1 Judge + 2 Assessors (possessing nautical/engineering/special skill) | Hearing in open court | Court NOT authorised to make binding order — judge merely reports to Central Govt
🏗️ 13.6.11.1 IDR Act, 1951
Industries (Development & Regulation)
S.10: Registration of industrial undertakings
S.10-A: Central Govt can cancel registration — must give opportunity of hearing first
Cancellation of licence = adjudicatory function
Licensing authority must adjudge: Has licensee infringed any condition? Has licensee become unfit to hold licence?
⚖️ 13.6.11.2 NCLT
Companies Act, 2013 S.408 (w.e.f. 1.6.2016)
Consolidates: Company Law Board + BIFR + IBC adjudicatory body
Principal Bench: New Delhi + Regional Benches (16+)
NCLT: President + Technical Member | NCLAT: Chairperson + Judicial/Technical Member
President NCLT: HC Judge (5 yrs) | Judicial Member: HC Judge / District Judge (5 yrs) / Advocate (10 yrs) | Technical Member: 15 yrs in ICLS/ILS / Secy or Addl. Secy GOI / CA/CMA/CS with 15 yrs experience / Labour Court/National Tribunal Presiding Officer (5 yrs)
NCLAT Chairperson: SC Judge or HC Chief Justice | Members appointed by Selection Committee (CJI + senior SC Judge + HC CJ + Secretary MCA + Secretary MoLJ)
NCLT Order → NCLAT → Supreme Court (further appeal)
Winding up & amalgamation jurisdiction formerly with HC now vested in NCLT
🏬 13.6.11.3–13.6.11.4 Essential Commodities & MRTP
Essential Commodities Act, 1955:
Vast administrative apparatus | Licence cancellation judicialised for some commodities | e.g. Jute Textiles Control Order: Jute Controller can cancel licence → must give hearing → written order with reasons → appeal to Central Govt
MRTP Act, 1969 (replaced by Competition Act, 2002):
Two authorities: Director-General of Investigation & Registration + MRTP Commission (Chairman + 2-8 members)
Chairman: SC or HC Judge qualification
Functions: Investigatory + Advisory + Adjudicatory | Decisions by majority opinion | Can punish for own contempt
MRTP Commission Tenure: Not exceeding 5 yrs | Max 2 terms (total 10 yrs) or age 65 | Removal: 2 key grounds → (i) financial interest prejudicial to functions OR (ii) abused position → must refer to SC for inquiry first
Can issue interim injunctions in MONOPOLISTIC / RESTRICTIVE / UNFAIR trade practice cases | Can award compensation for loss caused | Suo motu enquiry in monopolistic trade practice possible | Final orders on concentration of economic power vest with Govt
Appeals to SC under S.55 on grounds in CPC S.100 | Commission can amend/revoke own orders under S.13(2)
💡 NOIDA Case: Commission found compelling customers to make additional payments due to construction delay = restrictive trade practice under ss.2(o) and 10(a)(i) — issued notice of inquiry
13.6.12 — Rent Control | Pages 844
🏠 Rent Control — Delhi Rent Control Act, 1958 (Illustrative)
📝 Housing shortage since WWII led to restricting landlords from evicting tenants freely or fixing rents arbitrarily. Rent controllers were created as specialist adjudicators for landlord-tenant disputes.
👤 Rent Controller
Appointed by Central Govt | Qualification: Judicial office in India for 5 yrs OR legal practitioner for 7 yrs
Jurisdiction: Fixation of standard rent, eviction, other disputes between landlord-tenant | Exclusive jurisdiction — civil court excluded | Must give opportunity of being heard before any prejudicial order
Procedure: "As far as may be" — practice & procedure of court of small causes | Lawyers can appear | Civil court powers: summon/examine persons, discovery, production of documents | Can award costs
Orders executable by controller as civil court decree
🏛️ Rent Control Tribunal
1 person appointed by Central Govt from: District Judges OR persons with judicial office in India for ≥10 yrs
Hears appeals from Controller orders | Order involving substantial question of law → appealable to HC
📝 Interesting: In practice, HC suggests names for appointment of rent control authorities, and the government invariably accepts HC's suggestions. So judicial independence is practically maintained.
⚠️ Special Provisions
S.25B(8): For eviction on ground of landlord's bona fide necessity → NO appeal to Tribunal — ONLY revision to HC
SC suggestion: National Rent Tribunal (all-India basis, quicker procedure) to resolve acute housing shortage litigation and relieve HC + SC burden
13.6.13 — Regulation of Professions | Pages 844–848
Professional Body Disciplinary Mechanism Key Procedural Features Appeals
Press Council
(Press Council Act)		 Council can warn, admonish, or censure a newspaper/news agency/editor/journalist for journalistic misconduct | Powers only: admonition + censure — NO other punishment Inquiry + opportunity of being heard + recording of reasons required | Civil court powers: summon witnesses, discovery | Body genuinely independent of government — no Govt power to select any member No appeal provision (censure is the maximum)
Bar Council
(Advocates Act, 1961)		 State Bar Council Disciplinary Committee (DC) | "Has reason to believe" standard required before referral to DC | Prima facie case needed for investigation | Bar Council of India has ultimate supervisory + appellate role Quasi-criminal in character → must follow natural justice | DC can: summarily reject (no prima facie case) OR fix hearing date | Advocate must be given opportunity to represent | DC may: dismiss complaint / reprimand / suspend from rolls | 1 year to dispose; if not — transferred to BCI | Proceedings deemed judicial proceedings (IPC ss.193, 228) | DC deemed civil court (Ss.346, 349 CrPC) Within 60 days → appeal to Bar Council of India DC | Within 60 days of BCI DC order → appeal to Supreme Court (law + fact) | State Bar Council = "person aggrieved" for appeal to SC (SC held)
ICAI (Chartered Accountants)
(CA Act, 1949)		 Council refers to Disciplinary Committee | DC holds enquiry + reports to Council | If no misconduct → case dismissed | If Schedule I misconduct → opportunity of hearing → remove from rolls / reprimand Civil court powers: summon, attend, oath, discovery, documents | Appeal against Council penalties → HC within 30 days | HC may: modify / remit / set aside / confirm / reduce or enhance penalty | If HC modifies/sets aside/enhances → Council/individual must be given opportunity to represent Removal beyond 5 years: Council can only forward to HC with recommendation — cannot decide itself | Misconduct outside Schedule I → must forward to HC
Council of Architecture
(Architects Act, 1972)		 Can remove name from register or take disciplinary action for professional misconduct | Inquiry as per rules + hearing to architect must be given Prescribed rules govern enquiry procedure Not specified in the excerpt
National Medical Commission
(NMC Act, 2019 — repeals IMC Act 1956)		 NMC: Chairperson + 10 ex-officio + 22 part-time members | Chairperson: Medical professional with 20 yrs experience (10 yrs as leader in medical education) + postgraduate medical degree | Appointment by Committee headed by Cabinet Secretary NMC exercises appellate jurisdiction over decisions of autonomous boards (UGMEB, PGMEB, MARB, Ethics and Medical Registration Board) | Medical Registration Board maintains national register + every State Medical Council maintains updated State register (electronic format, public) Chairperson term: Max 4 yrs, not eligible for re-appointment | Ex-officio term: As long as holding qualifying office
Dental Council
(Dentists Act, 1948)		 State Dental Council can remove dentist from register on grounds of misconduct after reasonable opportunity of being heard and such inquiry as it deems fit Inquiry as Council deems fit | Reasonable opportunity of being heard mandatory Appeal to State Government | State Govt order upon such appeal: FINAL
13.6.14–13.6.15 — Commodity Boards & Central Administrative Tribunal (CAT) | Pages 849–856
🌾 Commodity Boards & ⚖️ CAT
☕ 13.6.14 Commodity Boards
Coffee Act, 1942; Tea Act, 1953; Rubber Act, 1947; Coir Industry Act, 1953; Cardamom Act, 1965; Seeds Act, 1966; Tobacco Act, 1975
Each board: self-regulatory mechanism including producers & trade representatives
Functions: Regulate production, price, sale, import/export, storage, licensing
Coffee Board (example): Chairman (Central Govt) + 3 MPs + 29 members representing coffee states, growers, trade, curing establishments, labour | Functions under Central Govt control | Any order of board refusing/cancelling curing establishment licence → appeal to Central Govt within 60 days
🏛️ 13.6.15 Central Administrative Tribunal (CAT)
GENESIS
Art. 323A → Administrative Tribunals Act, 1985 | KK Dutta v UOI (1980): SC suggested setting up | Sampath Kumar (1987): SC upheld constitutionality | Types: CAT (Central Govt servants) / SAT (State — on request) / JAT (Joint for 2 States) — NOT a hierarchy, no appeal from SAT/JAT to CAT
JURISDICTION (CAT)
All India Service | Any civil service/civil post under Centre | Posts connected with defence (civilians) | Service matters of authority/corporation/society owned or controlled by GOI
EXCLUDED: Armed forces members | Servants of HC or SC | Secretarial staff of Parliament / State / UT Legislatures
COMPOSITION
Chairman (HC Judge — mandatory) + Vice-Chairmen + Judicial Members + Administrative Members | Bench: 1 Judicial + 1 Administrative member | Seats: New Delhi (Principal), Allahabad, Bombay, Bangalore, Chandigarh, Cuttack, Calcutta, Madras, Ernakulam, Jodhpur, Guwahati, Hyderabad, Patna, Ahmedabad, Jabalpur (SC Sampath Kumar: Bench at every HC seat)
TENURE
5 yrs (re-appointment eligible) | Chairman/VC: 65 yrs | Members: 62 yrs | No removal except by President order on proved misbehaviour/incapacity after inquiry by SC Judge | Salaries/allowances: cannot be varied to disadvantage | CJI consultation mandatory for all appointments
KEY PROVISIONS
S.14: CAT takes over ALL jurisdiction of all courts (except SC) in service matters | HC CANNOT issue Art.226 writ in service matters within CAT jurisdiction | S.28: After CAT established — no court except SC or labour tribunal for recruitment/service matters | S.20: Must exhaust departmental remedies first | S.21: Application within 1 year of final order OR within 6 months after appellate authority disposes/fails to dispose (SS Rathore case)
POWERS OF CAT
Civil court powers (CPC): summon witnesses, enforce attendance, examine on oath, discovery & production of documents | S.14: Jurisdiction = powers exercisable by all courts (except SC) | Can declare departmental recruitment rules unconstitutional for violating Arts. 14 & 16 (JB Chopra v UOI) | Contempt powers as HC
LIMITATIONS
CAT has NO power to reduce quantum of punishment imposed by disciplinary authority (UOI v Parma Nand) | CAT CANNOT reappreciate evidence and reach a different finding — only judicial review of procedural correctness | CAT is NOT an appellate authority over competent authority — jurisdiction equivalent to HC's writ jurisdiction (UOI v Abbas) | Tribunals CANNOT delegate their essential function to decide service disputes (ab initio void)
Only subject to SC under Art.32 (fundamental rights) and Art.136 (special leave) | High Courts lost Art.226 jurisdiction in service matters → taken away | S.24: Interim orders only after giving copies + opportunity to other party (14-day expiry if not complied)
📝 Baidyanath Mahapatra case: CAT Chairman who had been ex-Chief Secretary and had recommended B's premature retirement CANNOT participate in hearing B's challenge. This is a serious breach of the rule against bias — "Members must not sit in judgment on their own decisions."
13.6.16–13.6.18 — Consumer Protection, FERA & Competition Commission | Pages 856–861
🛒 Consumer Protection Act, 2019 (repeals 1986 Act) | Competition Commission of India
🏗️ Three-Tier Structure
District Commission: President + min 2 + max as prescribed by Central Govt
State Commission: President + min 4 + max as prescribed
National Commission: President + min 4 + max as prescribed | Functions at national capital region | President max age: 70 yrs
SECTION 55 Prescribes qualifications of President of National Commission — determined by Central Govt | Max age of other members: 67 yrs
Jurisdiction: Deficiency in service — including services by State, public sector undertakings, trading organisations that provide public service on payment of consideration | Government body equally amenable to Consumer Protection Act (Lucknow Dev. Authority v MK Gupta)
📝 This is transformative: Even electricity boards, railways, telephone departments, banks, insurance companies can be sued in the consumer forum. You don't need to file expensive civil suits anymore.
💡 Lucknow Dev. Authority (SC 1994): Housing board delayed handing over flat despite full payment → ordered to pay interest + ₹10,000 compensation for harassment, mental torture and agony. Officials who caused harassment personally liable to pay the compensation the authority pays.
⚡ Powers & Limitations
Can award: value of goods/services + compensation for injustice suffered | Can order discontinuance of unfair/restrictive trade practices | Can grant "cease and desist" order | Can award compensation for harassment if mala fide/oppressive acts of public servant
CANNOT issue interim injunctions | O.XXXIX CPC NOT applicable | Commission cannot issue injunctions | Only final orders | Prospective investor in company NOT a consumer (Morgan Stanley Mutual Fund case)
Consumer forum = NOT a "judicial authority" for s.34 Arbitration Act (National Commission ruling) | BUT IS a quasi-judicial body — appeal from National Commission lies to SC under Art.136 → proves it is a tribunal with natural justice principles
Advantages over civil courts: Simplified procedure | No court fees | Multi-disciplinary members | Expeditious disposal | Cheaper | Broader perspective (not purely technical legal approach)
HC retains Art.226 power over consumer forums — BUT High Courts reluctant to interfere (will defeat speedy remedy purpose) | HC Art.227 supervisory jurisdiction also available
Time limits: National Commission — decide complaint within 3 months of notice to other party | Decision appeal within 90 days
🏛️ 13.6.18 Competition Commission of India
(Competition Act, 2002)
Quasi-judicial body to curb negative aspects of competition | Powers: Passing interim orders | Imposing penalty | Awarding compensation | Protecting consumer interest | Ensuring fair trade | Preventing adverse effect on competition
Ex Parte Ad Interim Orders (S.33)
CCI can pass ex parte ad interim restraint orders BUT must be exercised SPARINGLY and under COMPELLING AND EXCEPTIONAL circumstances
CCI must record a REASONED ORDER and: (a) record satisfaction (higher degree than prima facie view under S.26(1)) that contravention has been/continues to be/about to be committed; (b) restraint is necessary; (c) from record — very likely party to lis would suffer irreparable/irretrievable damage OR definite apprehension of adverse effect on market competition
📝 CCI's power to grant interim injunctions is much stricter than a regular court. The standard of proof is higher — mere prima facie case (like under S.26(1)) is NOT enough. CCI must satisfy a higher level of satisfaction before restraining a company.
💡 Competition Commission of India v Steel Authority of India (SC 2010): Laid down that interim orders under S.33 require stricter satisfaction — not just prima facie view. Relevant for GST practitioners: CCI proceedings on anti-competitive pricing/discounting can affect GST base.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 13 | Administrative Adjudication (I)
These ratios and principles are for use in personal hearings before GST adjudicating authorities, first appellate authorities, and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name or provision.
Principle: Nature of Adjudicatory Proceedings — Not Criminal
Supreme Court | Director of Enforcement v M.C.T.M. Corporation Pvt. Ltd., AIR 1996 SC 1100 : (1996) 2 SCC 471 | Section 23(1)(a) FERA, 1947
Key Ratio
Officers of the Enforcement Directorate and administrative authorities exercise quasi-judicial functions and do not act as 'Courts' but only as 'administrators' and 'adjudicators'. In proceedings before them, they do not try 'an accused' for commission of 'any crime' but determine the liability of the contravener for the breach of his 'obligations' imposed under the Act. They impose 'penalty' for the breach of 'civil obligations' and not impose any 'sentence' for commission of an offence. Hence, mens rea is not an essential ingredient under such provisions.
When to Use — At adjudication/first appeal stage: When assessee argues that penalty cannot be imposed without proving intent/mens rea. Cite to show that GST adjudication is a civil obligation proceeding, not criminal — and penalty flows from breach of obligation, not criminal intent. Also use to establish that standard of proof in GST adjudication is not "beyond reasonable doubt" but preponderance.
Natural Justice — Opportunity to Cross-Examine (Fentandez Principle)
Kerala High Court | Director, E.D. FER Act v Alfred James Fernandez, AIR 1987 Ker 179 | Applicable to all adjudicatory proceedings
Key Ratio
An inquiry by the Director was quashed because of his failure to afford to the accused an opportunity to cross-examine those persons who had given statements against him, and this rendered the inquiry unfair and invalid.
When to Use — At adjudication stage: When the adjudicating officer relies on third-party statements or statements of witnesses without giving the assessee an opportunity to cross-examine. Argue: the statements cannot be used as evidence — entire adjudication is vitiated. Highly relevant where statements of co-noticees, suppliers, or purchasers are used against an assessee.
Rule Against Bias — Member Must Not Sit in Judgment on Own Decision
Supreme Court | Baidyanath Mahapatra v State of Orissa, AIR 1989 SC 2218 : (1989) 4 SCC 664 | CAT proceedings
Key Ratio
"The members of the Tribunal must follow rules of natural justice in administering justice like Judges. They should not sit in judgment on their own decisions."
When to Use — At first appeal or GSTAT stage: When the appellate authority or member had earlier participated in the investigation, inspection, or issued notices in the same matter. Argue: the authority is disqualified by reason of bias. Demand recusal or challenge the order passed. Applicable where the adjudicating officer who issued the SCN also passes the order.
Proportionality — Disproportionately High Punishment Can Be Challenged
Supreme Court | UOI v Parma Nand, AIR 1989 SC 1185 : (1989) 2 SCC 177 | CAT jurisdiction; proportionality as ground of review
Key Ratio
CAT has no power to reduce the quantum of punishment imposed on an employee by the disciplinary authority on the ground that the punishment was disproportionate to the employee's proved misbehaviour. The Court has argued the same as the High Court could not have done so on a writ petition; so CAT also cannot do so as CAT's powers are synonymous with High Court's powers. However, as shall be seen later, it is now an evolving ground to challenge a discretionary decision that it imposes a disproportionately high punishment on the concerned person.
When to Use — At GSTAT / High Court stage: When penalty imposed is grossly disproportionate to the nature and extent of default. Argue that proportionality is now a recognised ground of judicial review (the Supreme Court has applied it in several cases). The penalty must bear a rational relationship to the gravity of the default.
CAT Equivalence Principle — Tribunal as Substitute for High Court
Supreme Court | S.P. Sampath Kumar v UOI, AIR 1987 SC 386 : (1987) 1 SCC 124 | Administrative Tribunals Act, 1985
Key Ratio
The Central Administrative Tribunal is equivalent to a High Court as a forum for adjudication of service disputes; it can do whatever a High Court can do. The Tribunal's power is neither more nor less because it is just a substitute for these courts.
When to Use — When jurisdiction of an adjudicatory body is challenged or when arguing that a specialised tribunal has the same powers as HC to declare delegated legislation as ultra vires. Also use to establish that a tribunal specifically created for a subject area has the same quality of adjudication as a High Court.
Lucknow Development Authority Principle — Government / Statutory Bodies Amenable to Consumer Forum
Supreme Court | Lucknow Development Authority v M.K. Gupta, AIR 1994 SC 787 : (1994) 1 SCC 243 | Consumer Protection Act
Key Ratio
The test is not whether the person against whom complaint is made is a statutory body but whether the nature of the duty and function performed by it is service or even facility. Public Authorities acting in violation of constitutional or statutory provisions oppressively are accountable for their behaviour before authorities created under the statute. Consumer forums are empowered to not only award value of goods/services but also to compensate a consumer for the injustice suffered by him.
Further — Official Accountability
Where harassment is caused by mala fide or oppressive or capricious acts of a public servant, it is he who should be held responsible for such behaviour. The authority shall fix responsibility on its officials and make them pay the amount of compensation paid by the authority.
When to Use — In SCN replies and appeals where government departments or public sector undertakings claim they are not amenable to quasi-judicial proceedings. Cite to establish that statutory bodies providing services to citizens are accountable in appropriate forums. Also supports argument that GST officers are personally accountable for malafide actions in adjudication.
Principles of Natural Justice — Flexible, Not Embodied Rules
Supreme Court | C.B. Boarding & Lodging v State of Mysore, AIR 1970 SC 2042 : (1970) 1 SCC 43 | Employees' Compensation proceedings
Key Ratio
The principles of natural justice are not "embodied rules" and what particular principle applies to a given case depends, to a great extent, on the facts and circumstances of that case, the framework of the law under which the inquiry is held and the constitution of the tribunal or body of persons appointed for the purpose.
When to Use — In SCN reply: To argue that the specific natural justice requirement applicable to the given GST proceeding must be assessed based on the gravity of the charge, the consequences for the assessee, and the procedure laid down in CGST Act. A perfunctory 15-day notice to respond to a major demand is not adequate natural justice in the circumstances.
Competition Commission — Ex Parte Interim Orders: High Standard Required
Supreme Court | Competition Commission of India v Steel Authority of India Limited, (2010) 10 SCC 744 | Competition Act, 2002 Section 33
Key Ratio
The power to pass ex parte ad interim orders under Section 33 of the Competition Act has to be exercised sparingly and under compelling and exceptional circumstances. The Commission must record its satisfaction (of much higher degree than formation for prima facie view under Section 26(1)) that: (a) an act in contravention of the stated provisions has been committed and continues to be committed or is about to be committed; (b) it is necessary to issue order of restraint; and (c) there is a very likelihood that the party would suffer irreparable and irretrievable damage, or there is definite apprehension that it would have adverse effect on competition.
When to Use — When challenging provisional attachment orders or ex parte orders in GST proceedings under Section 83 CGST Act. Argue by analogy: even in competition law (which expressly permits interim orders), the SC has held that the standard for ex parte orders must be high — irreparable damage + compelling circumstances. GST provisional attachment should meet the same exacting standard.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter 14 — Administrative Adjudication (II)

⚖️ Chapter 14 — Administrative Adjudication (II)

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📄 Pages Covered: 862 – 898
Permitted / Valid / Principle Upheld
Prohibited / Quashed / Invalid
Condition / Unless / Exception
Optional / Alternative Rule
Neutral Reference / Key Principle
📝 Plain English
💡 Example / Case
📋 Index of Sections
14.1 Norms Applicable to Adjudicatory Bodies
14.2 Evidence — Indian Evidence Act Not Applicable
14.2.1 Standard of Proof in Disciplinary Enquiries
14.2.1.1–14.2.1.2 Evidence on Oath & Perjury
14.3 Natural Justice — Duty to Record Reasons
14.4 Official Notice — Expert Knowledge of Adjudicator
14.5 Burden of Proof — Customs & Income Tax
14.6 Res Judicata / Estoppel
14.7 Limitation Act — Applicability to Tribunals
14.8 Change in Personnel of Adjudicatory Body
14.9 Acting Arbitrarily
14.10 Acting under Dictation
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
14.1 — Norms Applicable to Adjudicatory Bodies | Page 862
⚖️ Chapter 14 — Norms for Adjudicatory Bodies
Principles of Administrative Law | Pages 862–898
FOUNDATIONAL RULE
All adjudicatory bodies are bound to follow principles of natural justice when the parent Act and rules are silent on procedure.
Courts have developed additional norms over time — these are covered in this chapter.
📝 Even if a statute creating a tribunal says nothing about HOW it should conduct proceedings, the tribunal is not free to do as it pleases. The courts have developed minimum standards — like giving a fair hearing, recording reasons, not acting on whims — that every tribunal must follow.
14.2 — Evidence: Indian Evidence Act Not Formally Applicable | Pages 862–868
📋 Evidence Before Adjudicatory Bodies — The Ground Rules
📝 The Indian Evidence Act, 1872 with all its technical rules about admissibility, cross-examination etc. does NOT formally apply to tribunals and quasi-judicial bodies. But this does not mean they can act on anything — quality and probity of evidence still matters.
✗ CANNOT
Act on pure conjectures and surmises without any legal evidence
Base decisions on bare suspicion alone
Make "best judgment" assessment on pure imagination
Use evidence not disclosed to the party
📝 Surmises = guesses. Conjectures = assumptions. Even without technical rules of evidence, there must be SOMETHING of probative value to support a finding. Zero evidence = order gets quashed.
💡 Dhakeshwari Cotton Mills v CIT (SC 1955): ITO cannot make a pure guess and act on suspicion; there must be something more than bare suspicion. Assessment orders set aside when based on bare suspicion, conjectures and surmises.
✓ CAN
Act on all material which is logically probative to a prudent mind
Rely on hearsay evidence IF it has reasonable nexus and credibility
Use circumstantial evidence
Use own expert knowledge within limits (official notice doctrine)
📝 The test is NOT whether evidence would be technically admissible in a civil court. The test is: "Was there some evidence? Would men of understanding and worldly wisdom accept it as fair commonsense?" If yes — the finding stands.
💡 State of Haryana v Rattan Singh (SC 1977): "In a domestic enquiry the strict and sophisticated rules of evidence under the Indian Evidence Act may not apply. All materials which are logically probative for a prudent mind are permissible. There is no allergy to hearsay evidence provided it has reasonable nexus and credibility."
HEARSAY — SPECIAL RULES
Hearsay evidence CAN be received by adjudicatory bodies
But: must be disclosed to the party so he can contradict it
Cannot be relied upon SOLELY — there must be something beyond hearsay (Residuum Rule — USA approach)
Kishinchand (SC): ITAT findings quashed — based on hearsay, evidence not disclosed to assessee, so assessee could not contradict it
📝 Residuum Rule (USA): Even though hearsay is allowed, there must be a "residuum" — some legally valid evidence at the bottom. You can't build an entire finding on hearsay alone.
💡 Balance-sheet produced in court doesn't by itself prove truth of entries — the writer must be produced or affidavit filed and opportunity given to opposite party to challenge. (B.E. Supply Co. v Workmen — SC)
SPECIAL: PROFESSIONAL MISCONDUCT
Higher standard than civil suit BUT lower than criminal prosecution
"There should be convincing preponderance of evidence"
Government servants: preponderance of probabilities (not beyond reasonable doubt)
Departmental proceedings ≠ criminal prosecution
💡 Khandekar v Bar Council of Maharashtra (SC): Bar Council DC decision quashed — two advocates held guilty of professional misconduct on evidence which fell SHORT of the convincing preponderance standard required.
💡 State of AP v C. Venkata Rao (SC 1975): "The adequacy or sufficiency of evidence led on a point and the inference of facts to be drawn from the said finding are within the exclusive jurisdiction of the Tribunal."
⚠️ KEY WARNING FROM SC
Adjudicatory authorities should not become smug that as courts do not interfere with findings of fact, they can come to any conclusion. Courts CAN interfere when findings are based on no evidence OR evidence examined in a perverse manner.
14.2.1 — Standard of Proof in Disciplinary Enquiries | Pages 869
⚖️ Standard of Proof — The Three-Level Framework
CRIMINAL PROSECUTION
Proof beyond reasonable doubt
Highest standard
Conviction must be certain
PROFESSIONAL MISCONDUCT
(Bar Council, ICAI, Medical)
Convincing preponderance of evidence
Higher than civil suit, lower than criminal
(Khandekar — SC)
DOMESTIC / DISCIPLINARY ENQUIRY
(Government servants, employees)
Preponderance of probabilities
NOT proof beyond reasonable doubt
(UOI v Sardar Bahadur; SBI v Ramesh Dinkar Punde; SBI v Narendra Kumar Pandey — SC)
📝 Preponderance of probabilities = More likely than not. A 51% probability is enough in a domestic enquiry. This is why many departmental punishments survive even without conclusive proof.
CIVIL SUIT
Preponderance of probabilities
(same as disciplinary but degree may vary)
SC RULE (GM SBI v R. Periyasamy, 2015)
The standard of proof that must be employed in domestic enquiries is that of preponderance of probabilities and not proof beyond reasonable doubt. A disciplinary proceeding is not a criminal trial. A disciplinary authority is expected to prove charges against a bank officer on the preponderance of probabilities and not on proof beyond reasonable doubt.
14.2.1.1–14.2.1.2 — Evidence on Oath & Perjury | Pages 869–871
🔏 Evidence on Oath & Perjury Before Adjudicatory Bodies
🔏 Evidence on Oath (Oaths Act)
NOT every adjudicatory body has legal authority to take evidence on oath | Only bodies with specific legal authority can do so | Oaths Act S.4: Oath/affirmation shall be made by all witnesses lawfully examined by or before any court or person having authority to examine or receive evidence
Oath taking = voluntary act | S.7 Oaths Act: Omission to administer oath does NOT invalidate proceedings | Evidence not given on oath still has evidentiary value — enquiry authority can weigh it accordingly
Key disadvantage if evidence NOT on oath: party against whom it is used CANNOT prosecute witness for perjury if he tells a lie — loss of one significant safeguard
📝 Practical tip: In GST adjudication, statements recorded by officers are generally not on oath (unless specifically authorised). This means if a statement of a third party is used against an assessee and the statement-maker lies, there is no perjury remedy. This weakens the evidentiary value of such statements.
⚠️ Perjury (IPC S.193)
Giving false evidence intentionally in a "judicial proceeding" is punishable | "Judicial proceeding" = NOT necessarily before a court; can include proceeding before an adjudicatory body
Safe harbour: Parent statute typically declares proceedings before the body to be "judicial proceedings" for s.193 IPC — avoids uncertainty | Example: MRTP Commission proceedings declared judicial proceedings under s.193 IPC
S.195(1)(b) CrPC: If perjury committed before a "court" → cognizance only on written complaint of that court | S.195(3): "court" = civil, revenue or criminal court + any tribunal declared to be a court
IT Officer = court for s.195 purposes (s.136 IT Act declares proceedings before ITO to be judicial proceedings) | Sales Tax Officer = NOT a court → no complaint needed to proceed against person producing forged documents
📝 Income Tax Officer cases: S.136 of the IT Act declares IT proceedings to be "judicial proceedings" — so ITO = court. In GST, similar effect should follow since GST proceedings are quasi-judicial in nature.
14.3 — Natural Justice: Duty to Record & Communicate Reasons | Page 871
📝 Natural Justice — Recording & Communication of Reasons
FOUNDATIONAL PRINCIPLE
The requirement of recording reasons by every quasi-judicial or even an administrative authority entrusted with passing an order adversely affecting an individual and communication thereof to the affected person is one of the recognised facets of the rules of natural justice. Violation thereof has the effect of vitiating the order passed by the authority concerned.
WHY Reasons Must Be Given
Public confidence in judicial/quasi-judicial authorities | Without reasons — how can a person know if authority applied its mind? | Reasons minimise arbitrariness | Essential requirement of the rule of law | Even an order of affirmation must give some reasons
Order Must "SPEAK"
The face of an order passed by a quasi-judicial authority or even an administrative authority affecting the rights of the parties must speak. It must not be like the "inscrutable face of a sphinx". The distinction between administrative and quasi-judicial orders has reached a vanishing point.
📝 "Inscrutable face of a sphinx" = an order with no reasoning that gives no clue as to WHY the decision was made. Such an order cannot be sustained.
Customs — NJ Principles
Statutory authorities under Customs Act, 1962 exercise quasi-judicial functions. Properties can be confiscated, redemption fine and personal penalty imposed. Because civil consequences may follow, principles of natural justice were required to be complied with. (Kothari Filaments v Commissioner of Customs, Port, Kolkata — SC 2009)
💡 Directly applicable in GST: Adjudicating officers under CGST Act impose penalties, confiscation, demand — all quasi-judicial. SC has held NJ principles must be followed in Customs; same principle applies to GST adjudication.
14.4 — Official Notice: Adjudicator's Expert Knowledge | Pages 871–874
🧠 Doctrine of Official Notice — Expert Knowledge of Adjudicator
📝 Judges in regular courts can take "judicial notice" of obvious facts (like India is in Asia) without requiring proof. Adjudicatory bodies have a wider equivalent — "official notice" — because they develop deep expertise in their subject area over time. But this power has limits and safeguards.
SCOPE — What Adjudicator CAN Use
Take official notice of obvious and notorious facts (same as judicial notice) | Use expert knowledge and professional expertise to assess and evaluate evidence presented | Act on own impressions and knowledge gained from sitting day after day in a specialised area | Supply deficiencies in a party's case from professional experience (Fairmount principle)
💡 Metropolitan Properties Co. v Lannon (UK): Rent assessment committee entitled to use its own knowledge and experience — but cannot overthrow the evidence altogether. Tribunal sitting day after day is bound to acquire experience and knowledge of conditions.
LIMIT — What Adjudicator CANNOT Do
Cannot overthrow the evidence produced altogether | Cannot base a decision entirely on facts NOT known to the concerned parties | Cannot decide something that would surprise the parties without giving them a chance to respond
SAFEGUARD
If a NEW POINT emerges — something the parties may not know about — fairness dictates informing them and giving opportunity to deal with it | If facts from background knowledge will be RELIED UPON — must be disclosed in advance for discussion and rebuttal
The General Approach (Schwartz Rule)
"Principles of natural justice require factual information within the knowledge of a tribunal to be disclosed to the parties for comment if the tribunal is going to rely on it to supplement or supplant the evidence tendered. But it does NOT have to disclose its accumulated background of experience, skill, specialised knowledge in analysing and evaluating evidence properly presented to it."
📝 Distinction: Accumulated background expertise → no need to disclose. Specific fact from own knowledge relied upon to decide → must disclose and give chance to rebut.
💡 GST relevance: An adjudicating officer's knowledge of trade practices in a sector (e.g., textile industry) can inform his analysis. But if he relies on a specific industry report or data that the assessee doesn't know about — he must disclose it first.
14.5 — Burden of Proof: Customs & Income Tax | Pages 874–879
Area / Section Who Bears Burden Standard Required Key Cases & Principles
General Rule
(Evidence Act S.101)		 Party who asserts a claim in his favour must prove all facts necessary for the purpose Burden of establishing a case never shifts. Burden of evidence may shift constantly. Party must produce sufficient proof which, if not contradicted, leads to inference in his favour. Need not produce all conceivable evidence.
Customs — Smuggling
(S.123 Customs Act)		 Initially on Department to prove goods were smuggled Not "mathematical precision to a demonstrable degree" — but "such a degree of probability that a prudent man may, on its basis, believe in existence of the fact in issue" (Bhoormull) Amba Lal v UOI (SC): Customs proceedings = penal in nature. Principles of criminal jurisprudence and natural justice apply. Burden on Customs to prove goods brought after partition (customs barrier established). They failed — confiscation set aside.

Bhoormull (SC 1974): Department discharged burden by: (a) import banned for long; (b) highly suspicious circumstances of seizure; (c) dubious conduct of party. Prima facie proof sufficient — then burden shifts to other party.
Customs — Gold & Diamonds
(S.123 Customs Act)		 REVERSED: Burden on individual to prove goods are NOT smuggled once seized in "reasonable belief" Reasonable belief creates rational connection between presumption and fact to be proved (Sampathu Chetty — SC: constitutional validity upheld) SC reluctant to probe "reasonable belief" — if prima facie grounds exist, courts accept officer's belief. But mens rea on smuggling charge → may be inferred from circumstances of seizure and subsequent conduct of accused.
Income Tax — Assessment
(S.106 Evidence Act applicable by analogy)		 Facts especially within knowledge of assessee → burden on assessee. Source of cash credit, truth of entry, claim to agricultural income, expenditure for business, losses, allowances/deductions → all on assessee. Preponderance — initial burden on assessee to explain; then authority can draw inferences. BUT authority cannot reject accounts arbitrarily — must have valid material. Apparent state of affairs is presumed real unless contrary proved. Chuharmal v CIT: Even though Evidence Act not formally applicable, nothing prevents taxing authority from invoking S.110 Evidence Act (title follows possession). S.110 "embodies a salutary principle of common law jurisprudence."

S.69/69A IT Act: Where assessee has investments/assets/expenditure not recorded in books → burden on assessee to explain source.
Income Tax — PENALTY
(S.271 IT Act — quasi-criminal)		 Burden entirely on DEPARTMENT to show: (a) concealment of income OR (b) deliberate furnishing of inaccurate particulars
Higher burden than mere assessment		 "The entirety of circumstances must reasonably point to the conclusion that the disputed amount represented income and that the assessee had consciously concealed particulars." Conscious and deliberate concealment required. CIT v Anwar Ali (SC 1970): Penalty proceedings are quasi-criminal. Mere best judgment assessment or saying assessee's explanation is false does NOT furnish basis for penalty. Department must show independently that penalty was warranted.
14.5.3 Additional Evidence Before Appellate Body Administrative appellate bodies may permit additional evidence not presented to the original authority. SC in B. Prabhakara v D. Panakala (SC 1976): Administrative bodies operating "quasi-judicially at the public-interest level" should enjoy "more liberal powers and less formal and more flexible processes if they are to fulfil the statutory behest efficaciously." Not bound by CPC procedural rules. Full opportunity to be given to other party to rebut such additional evidence.
⚠️ GST PARALLEL The penalty provisions under CGST Act (S.74 — fraud/suppression) are analogous to quasi-criminal proceedings. Following the IT penalty doctrine: mere demand and confirmation does NOT automatically justify penalty. Department must separately establish conscious suppression/fraudulent intent. Best judgment assessment ≠ automatic penalty.
💡 Burden shifts in GST: Where GSTIN-holder fails to explain the source of undisclosed turnover or mismatch in GSTR-1 vs GSTR-3B — the initial burden may shift to him under the analogy of S.106/S.69 principles.
14.6 — Res Judicata / Estoppel | Pages 880–881
🔒 Res Judicata & Estoppel in Adjudicatory Proceedings
📝 Res judicata = once decided, cannot be re-opened. It prevents parties from litigating the same issue twice. Although adjudicatory bodies are not courts, the principle applies to them in some situations.
GENERAL RULE
Principle of res judicata (not exact terms of CPC S.11) applies to decisions of adjudicatory bodies | Decision of a tribunal = res judicata if within competence of tribunal | If tribunal EXCEEDED jurisdiction → decision void → cannot be res judicata | Hope Plantations Ltd. v Taluk Land Board (SC 1999): Estoppel and res judicata equally applicable in proceedings before administrative authorities — based on public policy and justice
INCOME/SALES TAX — EXCEPTION
General approach: res judicata does NOT apply to income/sales tax assessments | Reason: Each year's assessment is final ONLY for that year — does not govern later years (Instalment Supply v UOI — SC 1962) | ITO can depart from decision in earlier year | "There is no estoppel in these matters" | But: Cannot reopen capriciously — earlier decision cannot be reopened if it was not arbitrary, arrived at after due enquiry, no fresh facts
📝 GST parallel: Each tax period (month/quarter) assessment is independent. A GST officer can take a different view for a later period even if earlier SCN on same issue was dropped. But cannot change view capriciously without fresh facts/change of law.
INDUSTRIAL ADJUDICATION
Res judicata APPLIES to industrial adjudication when matter in dispute in subsequent case had been directly and substantially in issue between same parties and heard and finally decided | Reason: Multiplicity of litigation between same employer and employees not conducive to industrial peace | BUT: extreme technical considerations of civil proceedings must not outweigh substantial justice | Certain disputes (wage structure, service conditions) can be re-agitated when circumstances change (e.g. spiralling prices)
BAR COUNCIL DECISION vs CIVIL SUIT
Bar Council dismissed complaint against advocate → subsequent civil suit for damages by same complainant for same negligence → Rajasthan HC: BARRED by res judicata | S.11 CPC not exhaustive — general principle of res judicata applies beyond its strict terms | Court cannot take cognizance of matter already finally decided by Bar Council
14.7 — Limitation Act: Applicability to Tribunals | Pages 881–885
⏱️ Limitation Act, 1963 — Does It Apply to Adjudicatory Bodies?
DOMINANT VIEW — Does NOT Apply
Limitation Act applicable ONLY to proceedings in "courts" — not to tribunals and other adjudicatory bodies
Sakuru v Tanaji (SC 1985): "Provisions of Limitation Act, 1963, apply only to proceedings in 'courts' and NOT to appeals or applications before bodies other than courts such as quasi-judicial tribunals or executive authorities — notwithstanding the fact that such bodies may be vested with certain specified powers conferred on courts under CPC/CrPC."
Article 137 of Limitation Act does not apply to applications before Labour Courts (Athani Municipality v Labour Court — SC 1969)
📝 KEY GST PRINCIPLE: The Limitation Act as such does NOT apply to GST adjudication proceedings. The specific time limits in CGST Act (S.73/74 for SCN, S.107 for appeal) are self-contained. Courts would apply the statutory period, not Limitation Act provisions by analogy.
EXCEPTIONS — Cases Where Limitation Act Applied
(1) Where relevant statute expressly makes specific provisions of Limitation Act applicable (e.g. Advocates Act makes S.5 and 12 applicable to appeals to BCI and SC)
(2) Where statute prescribes a limitation period — that period must be observed (ITAT: appeal within 60 days of communication of order)
(3) Central Coalfields v UOI (Pat HC): Article 137 applied to Railway Claims Tribunal for short supply claims — since RCT Act did not cover this category
(4) Madan Lal Das (SC): S.12(2) Limitation Act applied to UP Sales Tax proceedings via S.29(2) Limitation Act
💡 Key tension: Commissioner of Sales Tax v Parson Tools (SC 1975) refused to apply Limitation Act principles even on equity grounds to Sales Tax proceedings. But Madan Lal Das reached opposite result on S.12(2) via S.29(2). These cases are difficult to reconcile.
CONDONATION OF DELAY
Gujarat HC in Mahesh Harilal v B.N. Narasimham: When quasi-judicial authority enforces a limitation period for appeals/applications, it has an implicit power to condone delay. S.3-24 of Limitation Act apply via S.29(2). S.5 (sufficient cause) is necessarily included.
MM Rubber (SC): Limitation starts running when the aggrieved person comes to know, either actually or constructively, of the order against which he seeks remedy. Communication of impugned order to affected party assumes great importance.
Suo motu revision: If department seeks to revise order suo motu → limitation runs from date of making the order — irrespective of communication to affected person
📝 GST relevance: This principle directly supports arguments where SCNs are issued years after the transactions — if the assessee was not put on notice (no communication), limitation may not have started running from the date of assessment.
14.8 — Change in Personnel of Adjudicatory Body | Pages 885–886
👥 Change in Personnel — Must a Case Be Heard De Novo?
📝 "De novo" = from the beginning. If the adjudicator who heard arguments leaves and a new one comes, must the whole hearing start again? The answer depends on whether the evidence was documentary or oral (where demeanour matters).
Cardinal Principle
Case SHOULD be decided by the authority that heard the arguments. A successor CANNOT decide without fresh hearing if arguments were advanced before predecessor. (Amir Singh v Government of India — Punjab HC)
Exception — Documentary Evidence
Change in personnel = IMMATERIAL where evidence is documentary and demeanour of witnesses is not important. (General Manager, E. Railway v Jawala Prasad — SC 1970)
Here: Disciplinary committee — one member replaced, no fresh hearing, report submitted to disciplinary authority who decided on the record. SC upheld proceedings as not vitiated.
Supplementary Enquiry
No irregularity if supplementary enquiry held by same persons who held original enquiry even if they were meanwhile promoted to higher posts | It is NOT necessary that enquiry begun by one officer must be continued by same officer until end — members may retire, become unavailable etc.
Suo Motu Revision Power
Higher authority's suo motu revision power of a lower authority's decision must be exercised within a reasonable period even without prescribed limitation | "Reasonable" = question of fact in the circumstances of each case — no hard and fast rule
GST NOTE Where an adjudicating officer is transferred mid-proceedings and a new officer takes over — the new officer CAN pass the order on the basis of the record/submissions already made. He need not rehear the case from scratch IF the evidence is on record. But if fresh oral submissions are to be made, natural justice requires opportunity to make them.
14.9 — Acting Arbitrarily | Pages 886–890
🚫 Acting Arbitrarily — Judicial Scrutiny of Discretion
FOUNDATIONAL RULE An adjudicatory body should not act arbitrarily. Its decision should not be based on pure conjectures and surmises. A tribunal must exercise its discretion judicially and not mechanically — weighing the circumstances of the case according to well-recognised principles. (Hindustan Steels Ltd. v A.K. Roy — SC 1970)
GROUNDS FOR QUASHING
(1) Decision based on irrelevant considerations
(2) Decision without regard to relevant considerations
(3) Decision so wholly arbitrary that no reasonable person could have arrived at it
(4) Tribunal disabled itself from reaching a fair decision by extraneous considerations
(Mahindra & Mahindra v UOI — SC: Discretion cannot be exercised in "arbitrary, vague and fanciful" manner)
SOME IRRELEVANT GROUNDS + SOME VALID GROUNDS
Zora Singh v J.M. Tandon (SC 1971): Even if some reasons were extraneous or non-existent, IF remaining relevant reasons were sufficient to justify order, conclusion is NOT vitiated
Exception: This applies only where conclusions based on objective facts/evidence. Where conclusions are based on subjective satisfaction — if any ground is extraneous, the whole order may be vitiated (because impossible to isolate which reason caused the satisfaction)
📝 Distinction: Tax assessment (objective facts) → some irrelevant grounds but rest sufficient → order stands. Grant of permit (discretionary, quasi-subjective) → any irrelevant ground may vitiate the whole order.
KEY CASES
Prem Chand v State of Punjab: Best judgment tax assessment must not be arbitrary — must be based on some reasonable data, not imagination. Tax officer must make honest enquiries from other sources, communicate material to assessee, give opportunity to show material is faulty.
Ajantha Transports v T.V.K. Transports (SC 1975): "Quasi-judicial powers have to be exercised fairly, reasonably and impartially. Capricious or dishonest preferences on purely personal grounds are necessarily excluded here." Grant on totally irrelevant grounds = ultra vires / excess of power.
Patiala Bus v State Transport Appellate Tribunal: Decision VOID if tribunal fails to take into account relevant considerations.
💡 GST parallel: Best judgment assessment under S.62/63 CGST Act — cannot be based on imagination. Officer must make honest enquiry, communicate data to assessee, give opportunity to show data is wrong. Arbitrary best judgment assessment = quashable.
NANJUNATH PRINCIPLE Every quasi-judicial body must follow HC rulings unless reversed by SC. Even the government while functioning as a quasi-judicial body in deciding appeals or revisions is obligated to follow HC rulings. Disregarding HC rulings = ground for quashing the order.
14.10 — Acting under Dictation | Pages 890–898
🚫 Acting Under Dictation — Independence of Adjudicatory Bodies
📝 "Dictation" = an instruction from a senior authority directing the lower adjudicatory authority HOW to decide a particular case. This is fundamentally incompatible with quasi-judicial functioning. It is equivalent to no hearing at all.
CARDINAL PRINCIPLE
An adjudicatory body is NOT to be directed or dictated to by senior authorities as to how it should exercise its adjudicatory function. The courts insist that an adjudicator should apply his own mind to the matter before him, exercise his own discretion, decide the matter according to his own lights, and NOT follow the dictates of his superior in discharging his adjudicatory functions. No one — not even the concerned department — is entitled to control the functioning of an adjudicatory authority.
Classic Dictation Cases
Mahadayal Prem Chandra v CTO (SC 1970): CTO was of opinion assessee not liable to sales tax — but referred to senior officer who instructed him to impose tax. Done behind assessee's back without opportunity to meet the point. SC: Procedure unfair, contrary to NJ, calculated to undermine confidence in fair administration. CTO should have exercised own mind.
Orient Paper Mills v UOI (SC 1970): Assessment under Central Excises and Salt Act — assistant collector bound to act in judicial and independent manner. If collector issues directions to assistant collector as to how to assess → directions invalid → proceedings vitiated → collector becomes "empty formality".
Purtabpur Co. v Cane Commissioner (SC 1970): Cane Commissioner's order modifying sugar area reservation was actually made at the instance of Chief Minister. SC quashed — order of quasi-judicial body cannot actually be made by the Chief Minister (even if signed by Commissioner). Also: affected party not given notice of proposed modification.
Sirpur Paper Mills v Commissioner of Wealth Tax (SC 1970): Commissioner sought instructions from Board of Revenue before deciding revision application. Board gave instructions under S.13 Wealth Tax Act. SC: S.13 empowers Board to control "administrative" — NOT "quasi-judicial" — matters. Board CANNOT give instructions to Commissioner in discharge of his quasi-judicial function.
CVC Directives to Banks Case
Ministry of Finance directed all nationalised banks to invariably follow CVC advice in disciplinary matters. Bank imposed compulsory retirement purely on CVC advice — bank itself thought punishment too harsh but could not deviate from Ministry directive. SC quashed Ministry directive: "The disciplinary authority must exercise its own judicial discretion to impose punishment and not act under the dictates of the CVC. An unfettered discretion has been completely fettered by the Ministry's directive."
📝 Even when CVC/IB/vigilance body recommends action — the disciplinary/adjudicatory authority must apply ITS OWN mind. Rubber-stamping external advice = acting under dictation = invalid order.
Rajgopala Naidu Principle (SC 1964)
Government order laying down standards for grant of permits (under S.43A MVA) declared VOID after 8 years of operation | S.43A confined to administrative — not quasi-judicial — functions | Issue of compulsive, obligatory instructions to quasi-judicial body = infringement of concept of rule of law | Tribunals "must be left absolutely free to deal with the matter according to their best judgment"
NUANCE Non-compulsive instructions (guidelines for consideration, not binding) may be acceptable | What SC objects to: Tribunal holds itself bound to follow the instruction unless it expressly chooses to depart and records reasons | Even general directions cannot be issued to adjudicatory bodies
Maharaja Dharmander Prasad Singh Principle
"It is true that an exercise of powers of revoking or cancelling the permission is akin to and partakes of a quasi-judicial complexion. The authority must bring to bear an unbiased mind, consider impartially the objections raised by the aggrieved party and decide the matter consistent with the principles of natural justice. The authority cannot permit its decision to be influenced by the dictation of others as this would amount to abdication and surrender of its discretion. If an authority 'hands over its discretion to another body it acts ultra vires'. Such interference by a person or body extraneous to the power would plainly be contrary to the nature of the power conferred upon the authority."
💡 Deepak Babariya v State of Gujarat (SC 2014): Minister dictated to Collector on land transfer for industrial purpose. SC: "The present case is clearly one of dereliction of his duties by the Collector and dictation by the Minister, showing nothing but arrogance of power." Development must respect the inbuilt wisdom of the statute which is the mandate of the legislature.
GST APPLICATION
The dictation doctrine applies directly to GST adjudication. A senior officer (AC/DC/ADC/Commissioner) cannot direct the adjudicating officer to make a particular demand or drop a case. The adjudicating officer must apply his own mind. Similarly, internal instructions about minimum demand amounts or rate of penalty fixation — if binding — would be invalid under this principle. "Review" by seniors who then direct the adjudicating officer to pass a fresh order in a particular manner is equally impermissible.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 14 | Administrative Adjudication (II)
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST adjudicating authorities, first appellate authorities, and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Evidence — "Logically Probative" Test | Hearsay Disclosure Requirement
Supreme Court | State of Haryana v Rattan Singh, AIR 1977 SC 1512 : (1977) 2 SCC 491 | Domestic Enquiry
Key Ratio
In a domestic enquiry the strict and sophisticated rules of evidence under the Indian Evidence Act may not apply. All materials which are logically probative for a prudent mind are permissible. There is no allergy to hearsay evidence provided it has reasonable nexus and credibility. It is true that departmental authorities and tribunals should be careful in evaluating such evidence and should not glibly swallow what is strictly speaking not relevant under the Evidence Act. The test to apply is: was there some evidence or was there no evidence — not in the sense of technical rules governing regular court proceedings, but in a fair commonsense way as men of understanding and worldly wisdom will accept? Absence of any evidence in this sense in support of a finding is certainly a matter for a court to look into as it amounts to "an error of law apparent on the record."
When to Use — When challenging GST adjudication/appellate orders based on (a) no proper evidence; (b) reliance on third-party statements not disclosed to assessee; (c) orders based on pure surmises. Also use to challenge where statements of co-noticees used against assessee without giving opportunity to cross-examine or rebut. "There must be something more than bare suspicion."
Standard of Proof — Preponderance, Not Beyond Reasonable Doubt
Supreme Court | General Manager (Operations), State Bank of India v R. Periyasamy, (2015) 3 SCC 101 | UOI v Sardar Bahadur, 1972 (4) SCC 618
Key Ratio
The standard of proof that must be employed in domestic enquiries is that of preponderance of probabilities and not proof beyond reasonable doubt. A disciplinary proceeding is not a criminal trial and thus, the standard of proof required is that of preponderance of probabilities and not proof beyond reasonable doubt. A disciplinary authority is expected to prove the charges levelled on the preponderance of probabilities and not on proof beyond reasonable doubt.
When to Use — When responding to SCN replies: Argue that even in GST adjudication (analogous to domestic/disciplinary enquiry), the burden on the department is preponderance of probabilities — NOT mathematical proof. However, for penalty under S.74 (fraud/suppression), argue that quasi-criminal penalty proceedings require a HIGHER standard — conscious deliberate concealment must be independently established.
Penalty Requires Independent Proof of Concealment — Not Mere Assessment
Supreme Court | CIT v Anwar Ali, AIR 1970 SC 1782 : (1970) 2 SCC 185 | Income Tax Penalty — Quasi-Criminal Proceedings
Key Ratio
Before penalty can be imposed the entirety of circumstances must reasonably point to the conclusion that the disputed amount represented income and that the assessee had consciously concealed the particulars of his income or had deliberately furnished inaccurate particulars. Penalty on ground of concealment could be imposed only if there was conscious and deliberate concealment on the part of the assessee. Independently of the assessment, the department has to show that the imposition of penalty was warranted.
Further Principle
Penalty proceedings are quasi-criminal in nature. The burden is on the department to show that the assessee has concealed the particulars of income or deliberately furnished inaccurate particulars of such income. A best judgment assessment, or merely saying that the assessee's explanation is false, does not furnish a basis for imposing penalty.
When to Use — When challenging penalty orders under S.74 CGST Act (fraud/suppression). Argue: The mere confirmation of demand or rejection of explanation does NOT automatically justify S.74 penalty. Department must independently show that there was conscious, deliberate suppression. If the matter involved a bona fide dispute on classification/valuation, penalty under S.74 is not sustainable — S.73 (no intention) is the applicable provision.
Duty to Record Reasons — Order Must "Speak" | Natural Justice
Supreme Court | Kranti Associates Pvt. Ltd. v Masood Ahmed Khan, (2010) 9 SCC 496 | Customs Act Adjudication | Natural Justice
Key Ratio
The requirement of recording reasons by every quasi-judicial or even an administrative authority entrusted with the task of passing an order adversely affecting an individual and communication thereof to the affected person is one of the recognised facets of the rules of natural justice and violation thereof has the effect of vitiating the order passed by the authority concerned. The purpose of disclosure of reasons is that the people must have confidence in the judicial or quasi-judicial authorities. Hence, it is an essential requirement of the rule of law that some reasons, at least in brief, must be disclosed in a judicial or quasi-judicial order, even if it is an order of affirmation. The face of an order passed by a quasi-judicial authority must speak. It must not be like the "inscrutable face of a sphinx."
When to Use — At FIRST APPEAL stage: When the adjudicating authority's order is cryptic, non-speaking, or does not deal with the submissions made in reply to SCN. An order that simply says "reply not satisfactory" or "taxpayer fails to substantiate" without reasons = violates NJ = liable to be set aside. Also use when appellate order affirms lower order without independent reasons.
Acting under Dictation — Adjudicator Must Apply Own Mind
Supreme Court | Mahadayal Prem Chandra v Commercial Tax Officer, AIR 1958 SC 667 | Sirpur Paper Mills v Commissioner of Wealth Tax, AIR 1970 SC 1520 | Orient Paper Mills v UOI, AIR 1970 SC 1498
Key Ratio
An adjudicatory body is not to be directed or dictated to by senior authorities as to how it should exercise its adjudicatory function. The courts insist that an adjudicator should apply his own mind to the matter before him, exercise his own discretion, decide the matter according to his own lights, and not follow the dictates of his superior in discharging his adjudicatory functions. No one — not even the concerned department — is entitled to control the functioning of an adjudicatory authority. The Board cannot give instructions to an officer in the discharge of his quasi-judicial function. The distinction between "administrative" and "quasi-judicial" functions must be maintained.
Further Ratio (Maharaja Dharmander Prasad Singh)
The authority cannot permit its decision to be influenced by the dictation of others as this would amount to abdication and surrender of its discretion. It would then not be the authority's discretion that is exercised, but someone else's. If an authority 'hands over its discretion to another body it acts ultra vires'. Such an interference by a person or body extraneous to the power would plainly be contrary to the nature of the power conferred upon the authority.
When to Use — At adjudication/first appeal when: (a) adjudicating officer appears to have followed internal circular/instruction about minimum demand levels; (b) order is based on a review note by senior officer directing a particular outcome; (c) a senior officer's opinion has been adopted verbatim without independent analysis. Argue: The authority has abdicated its function by acting under dictation — the order is ultra vires. Particularly relevant where internal audit para or CERA objection is the basis of the demand with no independent DRO analysis.
Arbitrariness — Best Judgment Assessment Must Be Honest Estimate
Punjab & Haryana High Court | Prem Chand v State of Punjab, AIR 1971 P&H 151 | Best Judgment Assessment — Tax
Key Ratio
The best judgment assessment of a tax should not be arbitrary and must be based on some reasonable data and not merely on imagination. The best judgment assessment order was quashed as being arbitrary and not being in accordance with the principles of natural justice. The Court emphasised that the tax assessment officer should make honest enquiries from sources other than the assessee before assessing the tax. After having collected the necessary information, the tax assessing officer should communicate the same to the assessee and give him an opportunity to show that the material was faulty and that it could not be made the basis of the assessment. The 'best judgement' assessment should be an 'honest estimate'.
When to Use — When challenging ex parte or best judgment assessments under S.62/63 CGST Act. Argue: Best judgment assessment cannot be on imagination — must rest on honest enquiry from multiple sources. Officer must communicate the data relied upon and give opportunity to show it is faulty. An order that simply takes highest available GSTR-1 data without cross-verification and without giving opportunity = arbitrary.
Res Judicata — Tax: Each Year Independent | But No Capricious Reopening
Supreme Court | Instalment Supply (Pvt.) Ltd. v UOI, AIR 1962 SC 53 | Income/Sales Tax Proceedings
Key Ratio
Each year's assessment is final only for that year and does not govern later years, because it determines only the tax for a particular period. In other words, a question settled in relation to one assessment year may be reopened during the assessment for the subsequent year. There is no estoppel in these matters, and the officer is not bound by the method followed in the earlier years. Similarly, the failure of an assessee to raise a particular objection does not bar him from raising the same objection in a subsequent assessment proceeding.
Safeguard — Cannot Reopen Capriciously
An earlier decision cannot be reopened if that decision is not arbitrary or perverse; if it has been arrived at after due enquiry; if no fresh facts are placed before the authority giving the later decision; if the authority giving the earlier decision has taken into consideration all material evidence. Tax authorities can reopen capriciously or arbitrarily a question previously settled.
When to Use — At FIRST APPEAL stage: When department issues a second SCN on the same issue for a different tax period after a previous SCN was dropped or adjudicated in favour of the taxpayer. Argue: (a) If the law and facts are the same — department cannot capriciously take a different view without fresh facts or change in law; (b) Earlier favourable adjudication, though not technically binding, creates a legitimate expectation. Also argue estoppel (Hope Plantations principle) where department's consistent prior practice creates reliance.
Limitation — Starts Running from Communication to Affected Party | Suo Motu from Date of Order
Supreme Court | M.M. Rubber & Co. v Collector of Central Excise, Madras, AIR 1991 SC 2141 | Limitation in Administrative Proceedings
Key Ratio
When a statute fixes a time limit for an aggrieved person to seek his remedy, limitation starts running when he comes to know, either actually or constructively, of the order against which he is seeking his remedy. Thus, communication of the impugned order to the affected party assumes a great importance. On the other hand, if the department concerned seeks to revise the order suo motu, then limitation starts running from the date of making the order in question irrespective of its communication to the affected person.
When to Use — In SCN reply and first appeal: When department argues that limitation period for filing appeal has expired — counter by showing actual date of receipt/communication of order (not just date of passing) as the start of limitation. Particularly relevant where orders are served by affixture or publication and actual knowledge is a later date. Also use when challenging CGST S.107 or S.108 review/revision — show that limitation for revision runs from date of order, not communication.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter XIV — Administrative Adjudication (II)

⚖️ Chapter XIV — Administrative Adjudication (II)

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Discretionary
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections

14.13 Disciplinary Action against Officer-Adjudicators
14.14 Government cannot Suspend a Tribunal
14.15 Lower Body to Follow Higher Body's Orders
14.16 Overruling Decision of Lower Authority

14.17.1 Principle of Consequential Orders
14.18 Adjudicatory Body — No Review of Own Orders
14.19 Court Fees
14.20 Breach of Statutory Procedural Rules
14.21 Jurisdiction
14.22 & 14.23 Appeal / No Appeal by Tribunal
14.24 Communication of Adjudicatory Order
14.25 Doctrine of Stare Decisis
14.26 Contempt of Adjudicatory Bodies
14.27 Judicial Review
14.28 Reforms & Proposals
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
14.12 — Executive Interference with Tribunal Justice
⚖️ Executive Interference with Tribunal Justice
Supreme Court's consistent stand — Keep fountain of justice pure
Core Principle: Executive cannot direct / control / override Tribunal decisions
📝 The government appoints tribunals and pays their salaries — but once appointed, the tribunal must decide independently. The government cannot tell the tribunal how to decide or undo its decisions.
CASE 1
P. Sambamurthy v State of AP
AIR 1987 SC 663
Art. 371D — Proviso to Cl.5 gave State Govt. power to modify or annul any order of Administrative Tribunal
HELD: UNCONSTITUTIONAL
"How can a party to litigation be given power to override the tribunal decision? It would be a mockery of the entire adjudicative process."
📝 The State was almost always a party in service disputes. Giving it power to cancel tribunal orders = giving a party power to reverse verdicts against it. That is not justice.
CASE 2
Karnataka S.T.D. Corp. v Karnataka S.T.A. Tribunal
AIR 1988 SC 1089
S.67(7) Motor Vehicles Act — Tribunal "shall give preference" to Central Govt. approved operators for tourist vehicle permits
HELD: INVALID
"To the extent it goes, the clause pre-empts the decision of the designated tribunal by executive discretion."
📝 A tribunal deciding permit disputes cannot be compelled by statute to prefer persons handpicked by the government. That destroys merit-based adjudication.
CASE 3
B.B. Rajwanshi v State of UP
AIR 1988 SC 1089
S.6(4) UP Industrial Disputes Act — Govt. could remit Labour Court award for reconsideration without reasons, without hearing, no guidelines
HELD: VOID
"Uncontrolled and unguided power" over executive to remit award. "When once a decision is given by a quasi-judicial authority it would not be safe to confer on any executive authority the power of review or remission without imposing any limitation."
K.S. Ramamurthy v Chief Commissioner, Pondicherry — AIR 1963 SC 1464
PRINCIPLE
Government may direct administrative/executive authorities. But it cannot control the functioning of a quasi-judicial body or direct it to decide a matter in a particular manner.
Word 'control' in parent statute = control in administrative matters only — never adjudicatory functions.
📝 Even if a law says the govt. has "control" over a body — courts read "control" narrowly. It means control over admin/housekeeping functions (budgets, offices) but not over how the body decides cases.
MP Irrigation Karamchari Sangh v State of MP — AIR 1985 SC 860
Govt. refused to refer industrial dispute to tribunal. SC: Govt. has only administrative power to determine if dispute "exists or is apprehended." On disputed facts / law → must refer to tribunal. Cannot usurp tribunal's adjudicatory jurisdiction by making unilateral decision.
💡 GST Application: A GST Commissioner deciding a dispute involving contested facts / law cannot shortcut the adjudicatory process. Refusing adjudication or pre-determining the issue = usurpation of tribunal jurisdiction — challenge by writ.
14.13 — Disciplinary Action against Officers Acting as Adjudicators
🛡️ Disciplinary Action vs Adjudicator — Where is the Line?
📝 In India, tax officers (like GST adjudicating authorities) are also departmental employees subject to service discipline. If they can be punished for what they decide, they will always decide in favour of their department. The Supreme Court has drawn a protective line.
✗ CANNOT — Disciplinary Action NOT Permissible for:
Rendering a wrong / erroneous / palpably erroneous decision
"Merely because he gives a judicial or quasi-judicial decision which is erroneous or even palpably erroneous no disciplinary proceedings would lie."
UOI v R.K. Desai (1993)
Error correctable on appeal / revision — not by disciplinary action
📝 If an ITO or GST officer gives a refund that the dept. thinks is wrong — the remedy is to file appeal, not to punish the officer. Punishment for decisions destroys judicial independence.
💡 UOI v R.K. Desai: ITO gave refund to assessee. Dept. initiated disciplinary action. SC quashed it — wrong decision → appeal remedy, not disciplinary action.
✓ PERMITTED — Disciplinary Action CAN be taken for:
(i) Acting in a manner reflecting on integrity / good faith / devotion to duty
(ii) Prima facie material showing recklessness / misconduct
(iii) Unbecoming conduct of a Govt. servant
(iv) Negligence / omission of prescribed conditions essential for statutory power
(v) Acting to unduly favour a party
(vi) Actuated by corrupt motive — however small the bribe
"Though the bribe may be small, yet the fault is great." — Lord Coke
📝 A corrupt or dishonest adjudicator has no protection. The shield of judicial independence covers honest errors — not corrupt or mala fide actions.
⚠️ SUPREME COURT CAUTION — UOI v A.N. Saxena
"When an officer is performing judicial or quasi-judicial functions disciplinary proceedings regarding any of his actions in the course of such proceedings should be taken only after great caution and a close scrutiny of his actions and only if the circumstances so warrant. The initiation of such proceedings... is likely to shake the confidence of the public in the officer concerned and also if lightly to undermine his independence."
ZUNIJARRAO BIKAJI NAGARKAR v UOI — AIR 1999 SC 2881
Collector/Commissioner not imposing penalty on assessee (using statutory discretion) → disciplinary action initiated.
SC: Penalty not imposed ≠ favour shown. Mere non-imposition + CBEC filing appeal ≠ enough basis to proceed. "If every error of law were to constitute a charge of misconduct, it would impinge upon the independent functioning of quasi-judicial officers." Charge-sheet against quasi-judicial authority must allege something more than a mere mistake of law — e.g., extraneous consideration.
💡 GST Application: If a GST adjudicating authority drops demand / grants relief which Dept. disagrees with — mere filing of appeal by DGGI/Board cannot form basis for disciplinary action. Challenge is by appeal — not by intimidating the adjudicator.
14.14 — Government Cannot Suspend a Tribunal
🚫 Government Cannot Suspend / Direct Suspension of Tribunal's Work
📝 Once a tribunal is constituted by statute and starts functioning, the government cannot press a pause button on its work. The tribunal is a quasi-judicial body — under High Court's supervision, not govt. control.
CASE Mallappa Murigeppa Sajjan v State — AIR 1980 Kant 53
