Karnataka Land Reforms Act Tribunal directed by State Govt. to suspend working. Non-official members challenged.
Govt. had control over Dy. Commissioner as administrative/revenue officer
But this control does NOT extend to the tribunal as such
Direction to suspend work = direction to tribunal (not to officer personally)
QUASHED
Government has no power to interfere with judicial functioning of tribunal
"The kind of power claimed by the government is fraught with grave danger and will unduly shake the confidence of the litigant public in the impartiality and status of the members of the tribunal and is destructive of our legal and judicial system."
💡 GST Application: GSTAT / Appellate Authority constituted under CGST Act cannot be directed by the government to stop hearing cases or defer its functioning. Any such direction is challengeable by writ.
14.15 — Lower Adjudicatory Body to Follow Orders of Higher Adjudicatory Body
📐 Lower Body Must Carry Out Orders of Superior Quasi-Judicial Body
CASE Dharam Chand v State of Bihar — AIR 1976 SC 1433
Mining lease dispute — shuttlecock between State and Central Govt. for years
RULE
Lower authority must carry out orders of superior quasi-judicial body
If new ground arises → make reference to superior authority
✗ CANNOT
State Govt. refusing to implement Central Govt.'s order (superior tribunal) "on some ground which it thinks proper"
"To take the view that the State Govt. could decline to carry out the order of the Central Govt. on some ground which it thinks proper would be subversive of judicial discipline"
ADDITIONAL RULE
Superior body cannot re-decide a matter it already decided without fresh ground. Going back on its own order without fresh ground = no legal justification. A tribunal is also bound by the decisions of the concerned High Court.
💡 GST Application: Once GSTAT or Appellate Authority passes an order — Commissioner cannot refuse implementation citing his own view. Also, a revision/review of that order requires fresh ground under the statute — not merely departmental disagreement.
14.16 — Overruling Decision of Lower Administrative Authority
📋 Higher Body May Overrule Lower Body — No Stigma Attaches
When a higher adjudicatory body (e.g. Supreme Court) overrules the decision of a lower authority (e.g. Screening Committee), it does NOT cast any stigma on the judicial officer or the lower administrative authority. Calibre of the lower body is not doubted — the higher body simply exercises its own jurisdiction.
📝 Being overruled on appeal is part of the system. An officer who is overruled by a superior court has done nothing wrong. The higher court's power to correct errors is the very purpose of the appellate hierarchy.
M.S. Bindra v UOI — AIR 1998 SC 3058
Screening Committee recommended premature retirement (upheld by Revenue Committee). SC set aside the order, but affirmed — overruling the committee "no particle of mud is slung on any member of the Screening Committee."
💡 GST Application: When High Court or GSTAT overrules an Adjudicating Authority / Appellate Authority order, no adverse inference arises against that officer. The officer can continue to function — no disciplinary consequence follows merely from being overruled on appeal.
14.17 — Statutory Adjudicatory Body Cannot Assess Vires of the Statute
🏛️ Tribunal Cannot Decide Constitutionality of the Very Statute Under Which it Operates
📝 Vires = constitutional validity. A tribunal created by an Act cannot declare that Act to be unconstitutional — it is itself the creature of that Act. It would be like a child declaring its parent invalid.
CANNOT
Raise question of constitutional validity of the statute under which tribunal functions
Question of vires is foreign to scope of tribunal's jurisdiction
EXTENDED PRINCIPLE
High Courts have also held: tribunal cannot go into vires of rules made under the Act under which it functions
NOTE
Author's view: this extension is debatable — tribunal is creature of the Act but not necessarily of the rules made under it
GST APPLICATION
GSTAT / First Appellate Authority under CGST Act cannot declare any provision of CGST Act unconstitutional. If constitutional validity of a provision is challenged, the forum is the High Court (Article 226) — not the GST tribunal.
💡 Example: Assessee argues Section 16(4) CGST Act is unconstitutional — GSTAT cannot adjudicate this. Assessee must file writ petition in High Court for constitutional challenge, separate from the GSTAT appeal on factual/legal grounds.
14.17.1 — Principle of Consequential Orders
🔗 When Primary Order Falls — All Consequential Orders Fall Too
📝 If the foundation of a proceeding is set aside by a superior authority, all subsequent steps taken on the basis of that foundation automatically collapse — like pulling out a card from the bottom of a card house.
Applicable to: judicial proceedings, quasi-judicial proceedings, and administrative orders
CONDITION
Primary authority passes Order A
Superior authority is reconsidering Order A
Meanwhile, Order B is passed on basis of Order A
CONSEQUENCE
If superior authority sets aside Order A → Order B automatically falls to the ground
Even UPSC recommendations / actions taken thereon fall if basis is gone
GST APPLICATION
If SCN is set aside by High Court — all proceedings initiated on basis of that SCN (DRC-07, attachment, prosecution etc.) fall automatically. Consequential orders cannot survive when their foundation is quashed.
💡 Example: Provisional attachment order passed under S.83 CGST on basis of SCN. High Court quashes SCN → attachment order automatically falls as a consequential order. Assessee need not separately challenge attachment.
14.18 — Adjudicatory Body Cannot Revise/Review Its Own Orders
🔒 Quasi-Judicial Order Once Passed — Becomes Final Unless Review Power Expressly Conferred
📝 Functus officio = having discharged one's office. Once a quasi-judicial authority passes an order, it is done — it cannot change its mind unless a law specifically gives it power to do so.
General Rule — No Inherent Power of Review
NO INHERENT POWER
Cannot review / revise own orders
Cannot reopen the case
Cannot nullify earlier order
EXCEPTION 1
Express statutory provision conferring power of review — specifically (not by implication)
EXCEPTION 2
Inherent jurisdiction to rehear if party was prevented from appearing by reasonable and sufficient cause (Grindlays Bank case)
EXCEPTION 3
Inherent jurisdiction to recall orders obtained by fraud / misrepresentation — not functus officio in such cases
Key Cases on Review Power
CaseSituationHeld
Kuntesh v Management, H.K. Mahavidyalaya Vice-Chancellor approved dismissal → disapproved → later re-approved (reviewed own order) Quashed — power of review must be "expressly conferred" — not implied
Varma v UOI — AIR 1980 SC 1461 Review power May be conferred "by statute either specifically or by necessary implication" (earlier position)
Kuntesh / Suman — later position Discrepancy resolved Power of review cannot be implied — must be specifically conferred by relevant statute
Speaker of Goa Legislative Assembly case Speaker disqualified member → office became vacant → Deputy Speaker reversed order Speaker cannot review his own order — no review power given under Anti-Defection Law
Bachhittar Singh v State of Punjab Revenue Minister reduced punishment → Government enhanced it back Revenue Minister's order was provisional (not yet communicated) → Government could consider matter. But once communicated → no review without power
Mahindra & Mahindra v UOI — S.13(2) MRTP Act Statutory review power given in widest amplitude Even where power conferred — being quasi-judicial, cannot be exercised in arbitrary/vague/fanciful manner; must be guided by relevant considerations
💡 GST Application: Once a GST adjudicating authority passes an order under S.73/74 CGST — it cannot recall or modify it at its own instance. The remedy for the department is to file a revision under S.108 or direct appeal. The assessee's remedy is S.107/108 or writ. The adjudicator itself has no power to re-open unless a specific statutory provision exists.
14.19 — Court Fees
💰 Court Fees — Applicability to Tribunals
S.4 Court Fees Act — High Court not to receive documents unless fees paid as prescribed
Term 'court' in Court Fees Act = interpreted broadly to include tribunals performing judicial functions
Tribunal subject to High Court's superintendence under Art. 227 = "court" for S.4 purposes
Court fees payable on appeal from tribunal decision to High Court
EXCEPTION — Displaced Persons
Tribunal adjudication = not a "decree" under CPC. Appeal from such tribunal to HC → no ad valorem court fees. Fees per Schedule II, Art.11 only.
📝 Proceedings start with application (not plaint) → decision not a decree → lighter fee regime for beneficent legislation tribunals.
Fee Modes for Various Tribunals
ITAT — fees prescribed by parent statute | Railway Claims Tribunal — govt. prescribes by rules | Railway Rates Tribunal — self-prescribes by regulations | Tribunal can permit indigent person to file claim under Order XXIII CPC
14.20 — Breach of Statutory Procedural Rules
📜 Tribunal Must Follow Statutory Procedural Rules — Violation May Invalidate Proceedings
RULE
Tribunal bound to observe statutory procedural rules as well as principles of natural justice
CONSEQUENCE
If mandatory procedural rule is not observed → may invalidate entire proceedings
EXCEPTION
Mere non-compliance ≠ automatic vitiation
Rule similar to S.313 CrPC → non-examination under it does not invalidate unless prejudice established
📝 If an officer fails to follow a procedural rule but the accused suffered no actual harm, the proceedings are not automatically void. Prejudice must be shown.
💡 GST Application: If proper opportunity of hearing was not given under S.75(4) CGST — assessee must show actual prejudice (not mere technical non-compliance) to get order quashed. But if prejudice is clear (e.g., no opportunity given before ex parte order), proceedings are void.
14.21 — Jurisdiction of Adjudicatory Bodies
⚡ Jurisdiction — The Foundation of Every Adjudicatory Power
📝 Jurisdiction = the authority and power to hear and decide a matter. Without jurisdiction, even a correct decision is void. With jurisdiction, even an incorrect decision can only be corrected on appeal — not treated as a nullity.
WITHOUT JURISDICTION
Decision is inoperative in law
Void — even if not challenged
Party's consent / acquiescence cannot confer jurisdiction
"What is without jurisdiction will remain so"
Failure to object to jurisdiction during hearing cannot be used to estop party from filing writ
📝 Even if you never objected during the hearing — you can still challenge a decision on grounds of lack of jurisdiction. Acquiescence cannot create jurisdiction.
WITHIN JURISDICTION
Power to decide → includes power to decide rightly and wrongly
Erroneous decision = error within jurisdiction — correctable on appeal
Not void — remains operative until corrected by superior authority
DISTINCTION
Jurisdictional error = body has no power to act at all → void
Error within jurisdiction = body had power, but made mistake → voidable on appeal
Incidental / Ancillary Powers — Implied Jurisdiction
PRINCIPLE
Adjudicatory bodies have not only express statutory powers but also incidental and ancillary powers necessary to make effective the express grant of statutory powers. Courts may imply such powers. Examples: adjournment, ex parte rehearing, allowing indigent party, recalling orders obtained by fraud.
UOI v Paras Laminates — AIR 1991 SC 696
CEGAT Chairman referred question from 2-member bench to 5-member bench even though S.129C(5) only expressly covered difference within a bench. SC upheld: implied power from express grant to constitute benches for effective functioning.
💡 GST Application: GST adjudicating authority has implied power to grant adjournment, call for additional documents, and hear parties de novo if initial hearing was defective — even if no express rule covers these. The power to adjudicate includes incidental powers to do so effectively.
14.22 & 14.23 — Appeal / No Appeal by Tribunal
📤 Right of Appeal — Creature of Statute / Tribunal Cannot Appeal Against Its Own Order
14.22 — Nature of Appeal
Right of appeal is NOT a natural or inherent right
Cannot be assumed — must be expressly provided by statute
Mere omission or error in quoting statutory provisions does not affect maintainability of appeal if order is otherwise amenable to appeal
14.23 — No Appeal BY Tribunal
RULE
Quasi-judicial tribunal has NO RIGHT TO APPEAL against its own order quashed by High Court
Tribunal has no personal interest in confirmation or reversal of its order
"An appeal by a tribunal would virtually amount to saying that the decision of the High Court is wrong while its own decision is correct."
BUT
Tribunal must be made a necessary party in writ proceedings under Art. 226 (to produce records). Under Art. 227 → not a necessary party.
💡 GST Application: If High Court quashes a GST Adjudicating Authority's order, the AO has no right to file SLP in Supreme Court against that quashing. The Department (Union of India / Commissioner) is the proper party to appeal — not the adjudicating officer himself.
14.24 — Communication of an Adjudicatory Order
📬 Communication of Order is Mandatory — Limitation Runs from Knowledge, Not Passing
📝 An order that is not communicated to the person affected is, in law, not yet effective against that person. No obligation can arise (including limitation for appeal) until the person has knowledge of the order.
DARA SINGH v DIRECTOR OF ENFORCEMENT — AIR 1981 SC 427
Ex parte order imposing penalty → appellant denied knowledge → criminal prosecution for non-payment → SC held appellant not guilty
PRINCIPLES
Order effectively made only when person comes to know of it — by supply of copy or any other means
No penal action for non-compliance without proof of knowledge of order
Criminal proceedings for non-compliance = substitute for, not supplement to, knowledge of order
LIMITATION FOR APPEAL
If order not pronounced in presence of affected person → limitation period runs from date of his knowledge, not date of passing
"It would be wholly unjust to compute the period of limitation to file an appeal from a date earlier than the date on which the party who is entitled to prefer an appeal has the knowledge of the order."
📝 If you never received the order, the clock for appeal has not started. You cannot be said to have missed the limitation period for something you didn't know about.
Collector of Central Excise, Madras v M.M. Rubber & Co — AIR 1991 SC 2114
One-year limitation for Board to direct Collector to appeal against own order — runs from date Collector passed his order, not from date of communication to respondent. Literal meaning of "date of decision" given.
💡 GST Application: If SCN or order under S.73/74 is passed but not served, limitation for appeal does not run against assessee. Equally, if demand confirmed by DRC-07 and assessee claims no service — assessee can seek condonation of delay on grounds of no knowledge. Date of knowledge = start of limitation, not date of DRC-07.
14.25 — Doctrine of Stare Decisis vis-à-vis Adjudicatory Bodies
⚖️ Stare Decisis — Adjudicatory Bodies Not Bound by Own Past Decisions (Unlike Courts)
📝 Stare decisis = "stand by decisions already made." Courts are strictly bound by their precedents. Tribunals are not — but they should maintain consistency for public confidence and orderly administration of justice.
NOT BOUND BY
Own earlier decisions
May differ if circumstances of case warrant
BOUND BY
Decisions of higher body in hierarchy
Decisions of concerned High Court
Decisions of the Supreme Court
SHOULD (NOT MUST)
Maintain consistent approach
One bench should not lightly disregard earlier decision of another bench — esp. larger bench
Erroneous earlier decision → refer to larger bench for reconsideration (not ignore it)
UOI v PARAS LAMINATES — AIR 1991 SC 696
"The rationale of this rule is the need for continuity, certainty and predictability in the administration of justice. Persons affected by decisions of tribunals or courts have a right to expect that those exercising judicial functions will follow the reason or the ground of the judicial decision in identical matters." Judicial inconsistency shakes public confidence.
💡 GST Application: GSTAT is not bound by its own earlier decisions but should follow them for consistency. But it is BOUND by High Court decisions of the jurisdiction and Supreme Court. If GSTAT bench disagrees with earlier GSTAT bench — must refer to larger bench, not simply overrule. Inconsistent GSTAT orders on same issue = strong ground for High Court challenge.
14.26 — Contempt of Adjudicatory Bodies
🔨 Contempt of Adjudicatory Bodies — High Court's Power Under S.10 Contempt of Courts Act
📝 Contempt power ensures orders are actually obeyed. A tribunal without contempt power is toothless — its orders may be ignored. S.10 allows High Court to deal with contempt of courts subordinate to it.
Two Questions for S.10 Contempt of Courts Act
Q1: Is the adjudicatory body a 'court' for purposes of the Act?
Q2: Is it subordinate to the High Court?
'Subordination' under S.10 = judicial subordination (Art.227 superintendence) — NOT administrative subordination within court hierarchy
4 Tests for Adjudicatory Body to be 'Court' — Gujarat HC (Sk. Mohammedbhikhan)
TestRequirement
(i) Nature of powerMust be judicial power of the State — must adjudicate disputes between parties; must have a lis between contesting parties
(ii) Source of powerMust emanate from a statute — not based merely on agreement between parties
(iii) Manner of exerciseMust partake of essential attributes of a court — though minor trappings may be absent
(iv) Binding decisionEnd product must result in a binding decision between parties
Test from Brajnandan Sinha v Jyoti Narain — AIR 1956 SC 66
"In order to constitute a court in the strict sense of the term, an essential condition is that the court should have, apart from having some of the trappings of a judicial tribunal, power to give a decision or a definitive judgment which has finality and authoritativeness which are the essential tests of judicial pronouncement."
Key Holdings
• Assistant Registrar under Co-op Societies Act = court for S.10 (Jugal Kishore)
• Board of Revenue under UP Zamindari Abolition Act = court for S.10 (Vinay Chandra)
• Bar Council Disciplinary Committee = court for S.10
• Labour Courts / Industrial Tribunals under IDA = court for S.10 (Gujarat HC)
TRIBUNALS WITH OWN CONTEMPT POWER
Some tribunals empowered by parent statute to punish own contempt like a High Court:
• Administrative Tribunal (CAT) under S.17 Administrative Tribunals Act — High Court substitute
• CEGAT / MRTP Commission — given equivalent powers
💡 GST Application: GSTAT, being constituted under CGST Act with judicial characteristics, may qualify as a 'court' for contempt purposes under S.10. If any party defies GSTAT orders, High Court can deal with contempt under Art.227 superintendence. GSTAT Procedure Rules 2025 will likely address contempt jurisdiction.
14.27 — Judicial Review of Adjudicatory Orders
🔍 Judicial Review — Scope and Limits of Writ Court's Intervention
Review of own orders by Quasi-Judicial Body
No statutory power of review → review application not maintainable
Order in review = ultra vires, illegal, without jurisdiction
Application in garb of clarification / modification / correction also not permissible
Writ Court's Intervention
SELDOM INTERFERES
Writ court seldom interferes with quasi-judicial orders unless:
• Serious procedural illegality or irregularity, OR
• Authority acted in excess of jurisdiction
RULE ON TESTING
Quasi-judicial order tested on reasoning contained therein — not on plea of person seeking to sustain it. Order cannot be supplemented by fresh reasons in affidavit.
📝 The department cannot file a counter-affidavit in writ proceedings and give brand new reasons for the demand that were never in the original order. The order stands or falls on its own reasoning.
💡 GST Application: If adjudicating authority passes demand order with poor reasoning — department cannot file counter-affidavit in HC with fresh grounds not in the order. HC will test the order on its own merits. Also: S.73/74 adjudicating authority has no power to "review" its own confirmed demand — the only route is revision under S.108 by Commissioner.
14.28 — Comments on Present Adjudicatory System & Proposals for Reform
🔧 Indian Administrative Adjudicatory System — Diagnosis & Reform Agenda
📋 Current Problems (Diagnosis)
System lacks coherence, symmetry and uniformity
Bodies set up sporadically, no overall plan or blueprint
Most decisions by departmental officials — not independent tribunals
Adjudicators not trained in law — no credibility in public eye
🛠️ Reform Proposals (7 Key Recommendations)
#ReformRationale
1Uniform retirement age of 70 years for all tribunal membersEnables experienced Judges to serve full terms; eliminates irrational disparity
2No reappointment of tribunal members for another term — appoint till retirement ageReappointment compromises independence; follow High Court model
3Move from departmental adjudication to tribunal adjudicationTribunals are independent, less susceptible to bureaucratic pressure, have legal expertise, open procedures
4Central Appellate Tribunal (on Australian AAT model) to hear all original decisions from adjudicatory bodiesAt least one full-fledged appeal on law, facts and merits; review on merits, not just legality
5Mandatory legal representation + reasoned decisions by all adjudicatory bodiesNatural justice essentials; reduces arbitrary decisions
6Council on Tribunals (like UK) to constantly monitor adjudicatory system and suggest reformsContinuous supervision; RK Jain case illustrated need
7Uniform code of fair hearing procedure — on lines of US Federal Administrative Procedure Act, 1946Reduce confusion; give guidance to adjudicators not trained in law; codify irreducible minimum norms of natural justice
RK JAIN v UOI — AIR 1993 SC 1769 (CEGAT Case)
SC criticized CEGAT: non-judge appointed President; 4000 appeals pending; work culture missing; SC directed Law Commission to study tribunal constitutions and recommend reforms. Chairman should be retired/sitting HC Judge appointed in consultation with CJI. Appeals from tribunal should lie to HC bench (not directly to SC under Art.136 — too costly and inaccessible).
💡 GST Relevance: Establishment of GSTAT under CGST Act addresses several of these reform demands. GSTAT President must be retired SC/HC Judge. But the wider problem of departmental adjudication at the level of GST Officers (S.73/74 orders by same department) continues — the systemic critique of lack of independence remains valid.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter XIV | Administrative Adjudication (II)
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, Appellate Authority, GSTAT and High Courts. State them confidently and cite the case name.
P. Sambamurthy v State of Andhra Pradesh
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1987 SC 663 | Constitution Bench
Key Ratio
"How can a party to the litigation be given the power to override the decision given by the tribunal in the litigation, without violating the basic concept of justice? It would be a mockery of the entire adjudicative process."
When to Use — When challenging any provision / circular / instruction that gives the Revenue / Department power to set aside, modify or override orders of a quasi-judicial tribunal or appellate authority. Equally applicable when Commissioner issues instructions to subordinates directing them how to decide a specific case.
K.S. Ramamurthy v Chief Commissioner, Pondicherry
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1963 SC 1464
Key Ratio
"Although the government may have appointed it, may be paying it salary, and may even have a right to take disciplinary action against it in certain eventualities yet in the very nature of things, where the rule of law prevails, it is not open to the government to control the functioning of a quasi-judicial authority and to direct it to decide a particular matter before it in a particular manner."
When to Use — When arguing that Circular/Instruction/DG's direction to field officers on how to decide pending adjudications is illegal. CBIC circulars that "direct" outcomes (not just clarify law) are challengeable on this principle.
B.B. Rajwanshi v State of Uttar Pradesh
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1988 SC 1089
Key Ratio
"When once a decision is given by a quasi-judicial authority it would not be safe to confer on any executive authority the power of review or of remission, in respect of the said decision without imposing any limitation on the exercise of such power."
When to Use — When challenging revisionary powers exercised by Commissioner under S.108 CGST without guidelines / without hearing / without fresh ground. Also when Commissioner calls for records of appeal order and modifies it without a legitimate basis — this ratio supports challenge to such exercise.
Zunijarrao Bikaji Nagarkar v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1999 SC 2881 | Paras 41-43
Key Ratio
"To maintain any charge-sheet against a quasi-judicial authority something more has to be alleged than a mere mistake of law, e.g., in the nature of some extraneous consideration influencing the quasi-judicial order... The entire system of administrative adjudication whereunder quasi-judicial powers are conferred on administrative authorities would fall into disrepute if officers performing such functions are inhibited in performing their functions without fear or favour because of the constant threat of disciplinary proceedings."
When to Use — When GST officer (adjudicating authority or appellate authority) faces departmental pressure or show-cause notice for giving relief to assessee. This ratio protects the officer AND the assessee — because an intimidated adjudicator cannot give independent justice. Assessee can cite this to counter the argument that the adjudicating officer's favourable order was "motivated."
Dharam Chand v State of Bihar — Judicial Discipline
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1976 SC 1433
Key Ratio
"In fact to take the view that the State Government could decline to carry out the order of the Central Government on some ground which it thinks proper would be subversive of judicial discipline."
When to Use — When GSTAT / Appellate Authority has passed an order and field officers / Commissioner is refusing to implement or is delaying implementation. Also when GSTAT's order of remand is being ignored and the authority is re-adjudicating without following GSTAT's directions.
UOI v A.N. Saxena — Protection of Adjudicator
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1992 SC 1233
Key Ratio
"When an officer is performing judicial or quasi-judicial functions disciplinary proceedings regarding any of his actions in the course of such proceedings should be taken only after great caution and a close scrutiny of his actions and only if the circumstances so warrant. The initiation of such proceedings... is likely to shake the confidence of the public in the officer concerned and also if lightly to undermine his independence."
When to Use — When CBIC/DRI/DGGI initiates adverse action against a field officer who granted relief to an assessee. Also relevant when assessee argues that the adjudicating officer's decision in assessee's favour was made under pressure — and the principle of independent adjudication must be upheld.
UOI v Paras Laminates — Stare Decisis for Tribunals
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1991 SC 696
Key Ratio
"The rationale of this rule is the need for continuity, certainty and predictability in the administration of justice. Persons affected by decisions of tribunals or courts have a right to expect that those exercising judicial functions will follow the reason or the ground of the judicial decision in identical matters. Judicial inconsistency would shake public confidence in the administration of justice."
When to Use — When GSTAT / Appellate Authority is departing from its own earlier decisions on the same legal issue without referring to a larger bench. Cite this to argue that the prior decision of the tribunal should have been followed or at minimum referred to a larger bench — and the departure without doing so is contrary to judicial discipline.
Dara Singh v Director of Enforcement — Communication & Limitation
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1981 SC 427
Key Ratio
"It would be wholly unjust to compute the period of limitation to file an appeal from a date earlier than the date on which the party who is entitled to prefer an appeal has the knowledge of the order."
When to Use — When Revenue argues appeal is time-barred and assessee claims no proper communication of SCN / demand / order. Date of actual knowledge = start of limitation, not date of passing. Also use when S.73/74 order was passed but never properly served — assessee can argue limitation has not even started running.
Mallappa Murigeppa Sajjan v State — Independence of Tribunal
Karnataka High Court | AIR 1980 Kant 53
Key Ratio
"The kind of power claimed by the government is fraught with grave danger and will unduly shake the confidence of the litigant public in the impartiality and status of the members of the tribunal and is destructive of our legal and judicial system."
When to Use — When executive interferes with functioning of GSTAT or Appellate Authority through administrative directions, circulars, or by withholding staff/resources to cripple the tribunal. Also when arguing that GSTAT's independence cannot be curtailed by Ministry of Finance's administrative control.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter 15 — Administrative Bodies and Powers

⚖️ Chapter 15 — Administrative Bodies and Powers

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
15.1 Administrative Bodies
15.1.1 Jurisdiction
15.1.2 Presumption — Bona Fide
15.1.3 Finality of Order
15.1.4 Norms for Exercise of Powers
15.1.4.1 Two Authorities — Same Power
15.1.4.2 Doctrine of Public Trust
15.1.4.3 Acceptance Sub Silentio
15.1.4.4 Withdrawal from Acquisition
15.1.4.5 Recall and Review of Order
15.1.5 Accountability
15.1.6 Judicial Control
15.1.7 Designated Authority
15.1.8 High Ranking Authority
15.1.9 Seeking Opinion
15.1.10 Delegation of Power
15.1.11 Recording of Reasons
15.1.12 Uncommunicated Order
15.1.13 Illegal Order
15.1.14 Unreasonableness
15.2 Nature of Administrative Powers
15.3 Admin vs Quasi-Judicial Power
15.4 Executive Power — Govt of India
15.5 Admin vs Adjudicatory Orders
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
15.1.1 — Jurisdiction
⚖️ Jurisdiction of Statutory / Administrative Body
§15.1.1 | Four Corners Doctrine
GOLDEN RULE — Statutory authority must act ONLY within the four corners of the statute
📝 "Four corners of the statute" means the exact boundary drawn by the law. If the law says you can do X, you cannot do X+1. Any step outside is illegal, automatically.
VOID
Order passed by State Government without jurisdiction = coram non judice
📝 Coram non judice = Before one who is not a judge. The order has no legal force — as if it was never passed.
VOID
Designated authority created under a scheme cannot act beyond provisions of that scheme
DUTY
Officers of local authorities — bounden duty to act within statute and constitutional scheme
PRINCIPLE
Statutory authority exercising conferred jurisdiction must apply its own mind and follow prescribed procedures scrupulously
POLICY DECISIONS
State is entitled to alter/amend/rescind its policy decision. Policy taken in public interest shall be given effect. State action must be supported by valid reasons and upon due application of mind. Retrospective operation of policy must be stated expressly or by necessary implication.
GST LINK
When GST Officer's order travels beyond the SCN or beyond the subject matter of inquiry — it is coram non judice — can be challenged as without jurisdiction in writ.
💡 SCN issued for FY 2018-19 ITC denial. Order also covers FY 2019-20 not mentioned in SCN → excess portion is without jurisdiction → void — use this principle in writ petition.
15.1.2 — Presumption as of Exercise of Statutory Power by Statutory Functionary
🏛️ Presumption of Bona Fide — Statutory Functionary
PRESUMPTION
There exists a presumption that a statutory functionary would act honestly and bona fide.
📝 Courts presume that a government officer follows the law honestly. But this presumption can be rebutted if there is evidence of malice, arbitrariness or violation of procedure.
FLIP SIDE
High-ranking authority appointed by Central Government → law presumes it would act bona fide. Misuse of power in such situation should not be readily inferred. [§15.1.8]
💡 In GST adjudication — if assessee alleges malice/bias against the officer — this presumption must first be rebutted with concrete evidence before the argument is taken up.
15.1.3 — Finality of Order
📋 Finality of Order — When Does an Order Attain Finality?
RULE
Once an order is signed by a competent authority AND an entry in the requisite register is made as per statute — the order cannot be changed or altered. It attains finality (subject to other provisions of the Act).
📝 An order becomes final when two things happen: (1) it is signed, and (2) it is entered in the official record. Until both are done, it can be changed. After that, only appeal/revision can alter it.
CAVEAT
Subject to other provisions in the relevant Act — finality is not absolute if the Act provides for appeal, revision or review.
💡 GST demand order under Sec 73/74 — once signed and communicated — attains finality. Department cannot modify it without following the appeal route. Useful when Department tries to informally revise a passed order.
15.1.4 — Norms for Exercise of Powers
📜 Norms for Exercise of Statutory Powers
Reasonableness | Written Orders | Application of Mind | Fairness
MANDATORY
Written orders required — oral direction not contemplated in Administrative Law. Statutory functionary must act in manner laid down in statute.
📝 No verbal orders in law. Every direction must be in writing. An oral instruction by an officer has no legal force.
💡 GST officer orally asks to pay tax — not enforceable. Only written SCN/order counts. If department acts on oral direction → challenge as void.
VOID
Action outside domain of statutory authority = illegal and without jurisdiction. Authority cannot act beyond what statute confers.
REQUIRED
Every statutory authority bound by rule of reasonableness and fairness. Actions must be free from arbitrariness and favouritism.
📝 Even if the officer has power — the way it is exercised must be reasonable. Arbitrary or biased exercise can be struck down by court.
APPLICATION OF MIND
Application of mind best demonstrated by disclosure of reasons. Absence of reasons = order is arbitrary = legally unsustainable.
💡 GST order without reasons for rejecting assessee's reply — violates this norm. Reason is the heartbeat of every conclusion.
PROHIBITED
No legal fiction can be created by an administrative order. Official memorandum contrary to statutory provisions = ineffective; cannot be enforced in quo warranto.
📝 An officer's circular or office memo cannot override what the law says. Only Parliament/Legislature can create legal fictions.
WRONG PROVISION PRINCIPLE
Where authority passes an order within its competence — order cannot fail merely because it purports to be made under a wrong provision, if it can be shown to be within power under any other provision. Validity judged on substance not form. Principle: ascribe the act of a public servant to an actual existing authority under which it would have validity.
📝 If an officer's order cites the wrong section of CGST Act but the action was still legally permissible under another section — the order survives. However the reverse (citing correct section but acting wrongly) does NOT save the order.
💡 GST officer issues recovery notice citing Sec 79 instead of Sec 78 — if recovery was still permitted — order survives. But if power itself was absent — wrong section citation cannot save it.
15.1.4.1 — Two Authorities Having the Same Power
👥 Two Authorities — Same Statutory Power
BASIC RULE OF ADMINISTRATIVE LAW
Where two statutory authorities could exercise the same power, and if a matter has been heard by one authority — the other cannot exercise that power.
📝 Once one officer/authority has already dealt with the case, the second officer of equal rank cannot take it up again. This prevents double jeopardy and harassment.
💡 In GST: If CGST officer has already adjudicated SCN for a period — SGST officer of concurrent jurisdiction cannot issue fresh SCN for the same period on the same issue. Use this against parallel proceedings.
15.1.4.2 — Doctrine of Public Trust
🌊 Doctrine of Public Trust
Roman Law → English Common Law → Indian Constitutional Law
📝 The government holds public resources (rivers, forests, air, seashore, minerals) in trust for the people — not as its own property. It cannot give these away to private parties for commercial use. If it does, citizens can challenge it in court.
ORIGIN
Ancient Roman law → Roman Empire held rivers, seashore, forests, air in trust for free public use. English common law restricted Crown ownership. American courts expanded the doctrine to all eco-systems.
Indian Position — M.C. Mehta v Kamal Nath [1996 SC]
State is trustee of all natural resources meant for public use. Public at large = beneficiary of seashore, running waters, air, forests, ecologically fragile lands.
Resources meant for public use cannot be converted into private ownership.
Key Applications of Doctrine
LAND
Once land acquired under Land Acquisition Act → becomes State property. Doctrine prevents disposal below market value. Cannot be given back for anything less than market value.
OPEN SPACES
Area reserved for garden in development plan cannot be converted for other use (MRTP Act, 1966). Statutory mockery not permitted.
NATURAL RESOURCES
2G Spectrum case: State holds natural resources as legal owner but distributes guided by constitutional principles of equality and public good. Auction not the ONLY permissible method — but must be non-arbitrary.
BREACH
Bikram Chatterji [2019]: Noida/Greater Noida Authorities allotting further land to defaulting builder = breach of public trust. Citizens empowered to question it.
INDIBILY CREATIVE — ARTICLE 21 LINK
"The Public Trust Doctrine is a part of the law of the land. The doctrine has grown from article 21 of the Constitution. Power vested by the State in a public authority should be viewed as a trust coupled with duty to be exercised in larger public and social interest. An authority is under a legal obligation to exercise the power reasonably and in good faith to effectuate the purpose for which power stood conferred."
GST / MINING LINK
Mining lease grants are subject to Public Trust Doctrine. Government decision to refuse deemed extension of mining lease = public law domain → correctness challengeable only in superior court vested with power of judicial review — NOT before NCLT. [Embassy Property Developments case, 2020]
💡 State refuses mining lease extension. Company goes to NCLT under IBC. SC held: NCLT has no jurisdiction — this is a public law matter. Only High Court writ jurisdiction applies. Use this to challenge wrong forum issues in GST/revenue disputes.
15.1.4.3 — Doctrine of Acceptance Sub Silentio
🔇 Doctrine of Acceptance Sub Silentio
📝 Sub silentio = "in silence." If a regulatory body keeps ignoring repeated requests without deciding — and the party proceeds based on that silence — the body may be deemed to have accepted the proposal by its inaction.
FACTS
Electricity Regulatory Commission took no decision on repeated requests by Power Corporation (licensee) for alteration in tariff. Licensee proceeded with its proposal.
HELD
Commission was required to take decision forthwith — not while away time and allow licensee to proceed with proposal. Such conduct of the Commission may invite the Doctrine of Acceptance Sub Silentio.
💡 GST Department does not respond to representation for several months — assessee proceeds. If Department later acts adversely, assessee can argue acceptance by silence / legitimate expectation created by inaction.
15.1.4.4 — Withdrawal from Acquisition
📰 Withdrawal from Acquisition — Publication Requirement
RULE
When land is acquired by government for public purpose → notified in Official Gazette.
When government withdraws from acquisition → must also be notified in Official Gazette.
Both situations require publication for public information.
📝 Government cannot quietly undo or reverse a public action. Both the acquisition and its withdrawal must be in the public domain through the Official Gazette — for transparency and to protect affected parties.
15.1.4.5 — Recall and Review of Order
🔄 Recall and Review of Order
MANDATORY
Municipal corporation cannot recall/review its earlier order without:
(a) Assigning reasons
(b) Explaining what material on record led to change
Failure to record reasons + violation of natural justice = order vitiated.
📝 If a government body wants to take back or change its earlier decision, it must explain WHY. Without reasons, the new order (recalling the earlier one) is itself illegal.
ADMINISTRATIVE ORDER BY MISTAKE
An administrative/executive order passed by way of mistake can be recalled by the said authority, after complying with principles of natural justice. [§15.1.4 — General Norms]
💡 Department issues refund order by mistake — recall must follow natural justice — notice to assessee, opportunity to be heard — cannot be recalled unilaterally without notice.
15.1.5 — Accountability
📌 Accountability of Government Officers / Statutory Authorities
PERSONAL LIABILITY
Government officer may be personally liable if in discharge of official duties he acts maliciously, with oblique motive or mala fide. Action not bona fide / malicious = NOT protected.
📝 Officers cannot hide behind government immunity if they act dishonestly or with malice. Personal liability can be fastened.
NEGLIGENCE
Government departments often do not reply to notices or send vague/evasive replies. Apex Court directed governments to nominate an officer responsible to ensure timely replies within stipulated period.
DUTY
Statutory authorities duty-bound to act in manner preferring public interest over private interest. Must perform duties expeditiously.
COST IMPOSED
Apex Court imposed cost of Rs. 25,000/- on State of J&K for lapses in not producing relevant evidence — resulting in quashing of detention order.
CONNIVANCE PRINCIPLE
M.C. Mehta v UOI: Large-scale flagrant violation of building/land use/town planning law in Delhi — not possible without active connivance of officers concerned. Officers to show cause + payment of compensation directions.
💡 In GST litigation — where Department consistently loses cases due to wrong orders — argue officer's connivance / non-application of mind — seek cost imposition as accountability measure.
15.1.6 — Judicial Control
⚖️ Judicial Control over Administrative Bodies
EXPANSION OF AUTHORITY
Supreme Court in TN Godavarman — directed relevant clause of notification of Compensatory Aforestation Fund Management Authority be amended to include more experts. Courts can direct restructuring of statutory bodies.
MONITORING COMMITTEE
M.C. Mehta v UOI — SC appointed monitoring committee to oversee implementation of law. Court can retain oversight via monitoring committees in public interest matters.
EXPERT BODY DEFERENCE
Court does not normally substitute its own views for those of expert bodies (pay commission, NEERI) unless glaring infirmities are established. Decision of expert body supported by materials — courts cannot interfere without adequate contra material.
📝 Courts defer to expert bodies. To challenge expert body's decision — you must produce contrary expert evidence. Mere argument not enough.
COVID / RBI / MSME
Small Scale Industrial Manufactures Association v UOI [2021]: Even for extraordinary circumstances (Covid-19), extent of judicial control over financial policy maker (RBI) is limited — though some oversight exists. Relief claims over RBI moratorium mostly dismissed.
15.1.7 — Designated Authority
🎯 Designated Authority — Who Must Perform the Statutory Function?
GENERAL RULE
If authority designated by statute to perform function → that authority alone must do it. Where Article 311(1) of Constitution requires major penalties be imposed only by appointing authority → that authority becomes the designated authority. Authority higher than appointing authority is also the designated authority for Art. 311.
RAJESH AWASTHI CASE [AIR 2013 SC 78]
UP State Electricity Regulatory Commission — Selection committee under Sec 85(5) of the Act must itself satisfy about financial interest of selectee. Committee cannot give go-by to that function and entrust it to State Government. Selection process without intellectual objectivity = set aside by SC unanimously.
📝 The authority designated by law for a specific task cannot delegate or bypass that task. If the wrong person performs it — the action is legally invalid.
💡 GST SCN issued by CGST officer of rank not empowered under CGST Act/Rules — challenged as without jurisdiction. Designated authority rule applies — order liable to be quashed.
15.1.10 — Delegation of Power by Statutory Authority
🔗 Delegation of Power — Rules and Limits
CONDITION
A statutory authority can delegate its power only if there exists a provision in the Act for delegation. Power to delegate, being a statutory requirement, must find its place in the principal Act itself — NOT in regulations.
📝 An authority cannot simply give away its powers to another person or body unless the original law specifically allows it. And that permission must be in the main Act — not just in rules or regulations made under it.
💡 CGST Commissioner delegates power of adjudication to Additional Commissioner via an administrative instruction not backed by the CGST Act provision → delegation is invalid → order passed by Additional Commissioner can be challenged as without authority.
15.1.11 — Recording of Reasons
📝 Recording of Reasons — The Heartbeat of Every Order
Administrative Orders | Judicial Orders | Quasi-Judicial Orders
WHY REASONS ARE MANDATORY
Reason = heartbeat of every conclusion. Without it → order becomes lifeless.
Reasons substitute subjectivity by objectivity. Absence = order indefensible/unsustainable when subject to challenge.
Enables appellate/higher courts to exercise jurisdiction appropriately and in accordance with law.
Reasons are the soul of orders. Non-recording = prejudice to affected party + hamper administration of justice.
SCOPE OF DUTY — Applies to ALL:
Administrative orders
Quasi-judicial orders
Judicial pronouncements (with greater precision)
Both at admission stage AND final hearing
KEY PRINCIPLE — Asst Commissioner v Shukla [2010] 4 SCC 785
"Reason is the very life of law. When the reason of a law once ceases, the law itself generally ceases. Giving reasons furthers the cause of justice as well as avoids uncertainty and helps in the observance of law of precedent. Concept of reasoned judgment has become an indispensable part of basic rule of law and is a mandatory requirement of procedural law."
NATURAL JUSTICE — TWIN INGREDIENTS
(1) Person adversely affected must be given notice to show cause and opportunity of hearing.
(2) Orders passed must give reasons for arriving at conclusions — showing proper application of mind.
Violation of either vitiates the order.
💡 GST adjudication order dismissing reply in one line "reply not found satisfactory" — violates duty to record reasons. Challenge before First Appellate Authority / GSTAT — order is legally unsustainable.
15.1.12 — Uncommunicated Order
📭 Uncommunicated Order — No Legal Effect
RULE
An order passed but not communicated to the party concerned — does not create any legal right which can be enforced through court of law. Order does not become effective till communicated.
📝 A government order sitting in a file, not sent to the affected party — has zero legal force. Limitation periods, rights, and obligations begin only from the date of communication — not from the date the order is signed.
💡 GST refund rejection order signed on 15 Jan but communicated on 20 Feb — limitation for appeal runs from 20 Feb, not 15 Jan. Use this when Department tries to count limitation from internal signing date.
15.1.13 — Illegal Order
🚫 Illegal / Ultra Vires Order
CANNOT BE REPEATED OR LEGALISED
Illegal or ultra vires order cannot be repeated by public officers. Cannot be legalised.
A wrong action cannot be allowed to be perpetuated on the ground of discrimination — discrimination principle is not applicable to illegal acts.
📝 Ultra vires = beyond powers. If an order was illegal from the start, the fact that the same wrong was done to others cannot make it right. Two wrongs don't make a right in administrative law.
BAD INCEPTION PRINCIPLE
If an order is bad in its inception, it does not get sanctified at a later stage. Subsequent action/development cannot validate an action which was not lawful at inception. Illegality strikes at the root of the order. Beyond competence of any authority to validate such order.
💡 GST SCN issued without DIN (Document Identification Number) — illegal from inception. Even if assessee replied — the foundational illegality cannot be cured by subsequent steps. Order passed on such SCN = challengeable.
15.1.14 — Unreasonableness (Wednesbury Standard)
⚡ Unreasonableness — Wednesbury Standard
Secretary of State v Tameside | Associated Provincial Picture v Wednesbury Corp [1948]
WEDNESBURY TEST — Lord Diplock
"Unreasonable" as descriptive of the way a public authority has purported to exercise a discretion vested in it by statute — must be conduct which no sensible authority acting with due appreciation of its responsibilities would have decided to adopt.
EXTENDED STANDARD
A decision which is so outrageous in its defiance of logic or of accepted moral standards that no sensible person who had applied his mind to the question to be decided, could have arrived at it.
📝 Wednesbury unreasonableness = the decision is so absurd that no reasonable authority could have made it. Not merely wrong — it must be so irrational that no reasonable person would agree with it. This is a high threshold for courts to interfere.
REQUIREMENT
Public bodies with statutory public power must exercise them reasonably. Every statutory authority is bound by the rule of reasonableness and fairness.
💡 GST penalty of 100% imposed on a minor clerical error without any tax loss to revenue — can be challenged as Wednesbury unreasonable. Courts intervene where penalty is grossly disproportionate to the default.
15.2 — Nature of Administrative Powers
🏛️ Nature of Administrative Powers — Classification
CONTEXT
With demise of laissez faire and advent of welfare state — administrative organ now performs vast regulatory and managerial functions impinging increasingly on rights and interests of citizens.
Traditional Classification of Powers
LEGISLATIVE
Lays down general rule/policy; studied as "Delegated Legislation"
QUASI-JUDICIAL
Obligation to adopt judicial approach + comply with basic requirements of justice
ADMINISTRATIVE (PURELY)
No obligation to follow either natural justice or fairness. No hearing required.
EVOLUTION — Neelima Misra v Harinder Kaur [AIR 1990 SC 1402]
"An administrative function is called quasi-judicial when there is an obligation to adopt a judicial approach and to comply with the basic requirements of justice. When there is no such obligation, the decision is called 'purely administrative' and there is no third category."
CURRENT POSITION
Dichotomy between 'quasi-judicial' and 'administrative' functions has become blurred with emergence of 'Fairness' concept. Natural justice is being transformed into fairness and applied over a much larger area — including some functions earlier regarded as purely administrative. Term quasi-judicial is now falling into disuse.
IDENTIFYING ADMINISTRATIVE FUNCTION
Test = non-applicability of audi alteram partem rule. But rule against bias may still apply even if audi alteram partem is not applicable (if not invoked — patently partisan administrative decisions may go unchallenged).
📝 Audi alteram partem = hear the other side. If the function does not require hearing — it is administrative. But even then, the officer must not be biased.
15.3 — Dividing Line: Administrative Power vs Quasi-Judicial Power
⚖️ Admin Power vs Quasi-Judicial Power — How to Draw the Line?
FIVE FACTOR TEST [Automotive Tyre Manufacturers Association v Designated Authority, (2011) 2 SCC 258]
For determining whether a power is administrative or quasi-judicial, regard must be had to:
(i) Nature of the power conferred
(ii) Person or persons on whom it is conferred
(iii) Framework of the law conferring that power
(iv) Consequences ensuing from exercise of that power
(v) Manner in which power is expected to be exercised
DIVIDING LINE IS THIN AND BLURRING
The aim of both — quasi-judicial and administrative function — is to arrive at a just decision. The dividing line is quite thin and is being gradually obliterated.
SEBI CASE — National Securities Depository Ltd v SEBI
SEBI circular under Sec 11(1) = administrative order → outside appellate jurisdiction of tribunal.
Orders under Sec 11(4), 11(b), 11(d), 12(3), 15-I = quasi-judicial orders → subject to appeal under Sec 15T.
Administrative orders are outside appellate jurisdiction.
💡 GST Circular issued by CBIC = administrative order. Cannot be challenged before GSTAT. Only writ petition in High Court is proper remedy. Quasi-judicial order (adjudication order) = subject to appeal before Commissioner (Appeals) / GSTAT.
15.4 — Extent of Executive Power of Government of India
🇮🇳 Extent of Executive Power — Central Government
RULE
Executive power of Central Government extends to the same subjects and to the same extent as that of Parliament — as long as it does not infringe any provision of any law made by Parliament or the Constitution.
📝 Government's executive power is as wide as Parliament's legislative power over that subject. But it cannot override laws already made by Parliament or Constitutional provisions.
💡 Government can issue executive instructions (circulars, notifications) on GST matters — but if they contradict the CGST Act, the Act prevails. Executive power cannot override statute.
15.5 — Administrative vs Adjudicatory Orders
⚖️ Administrative Order vs Adjudicatory Order — Key Differences
Feature Administrative Order Adjudicatory Order
Hearing No requirement of giving hearing to affected party. Means of informing himself left to administrator's discretion. Concerned party must be given a hearing before making the order.
Flexibility Far more flexibility in exercising administrative powers. Less flexibility — bound by merits of controversy after hearing.
Res Judicata Does NOT apply. Authority making order can change or review it later. No finality. Applies. Decision becomes binding on all parties and on the decision-making authority itself.
Review / Reconsideration Authority can reconsider at any time. Government can refer a dispute even after earlier refusal. [Western India Match Co / A.S. Production Agencies cases] Can be reviewed only by appellate authority if provision for revision/appeal made by law.
Remedy for Challenge Writ petition in High Court under Article 226/227 of Constitution. Appeal provided under relevant Act (for quasi-judicial orders). Writ also available.
R.R. VERMA v UOI [AIR 1980 SC 1461]  "To extend the principle of review to pure administrative decisions would indeed lead to untoward and startling results. The government should remain free to alter its policies or decisions in administrative matters from time to time."
💡 GST refund rejection = adjudicatory order → res judicata applies → Department cannot re-examine same grounds after appeal. But Department's internal circular on refund policy = administrative → can be changed at any time.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 15 | Administrative Bodies and Powers
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Asst Commissioner, Commercial Tax Dept v Shukla and Brothers
Supreme Court of India | (2010) 4 SCC 785 | Recording of Reasons
Key Ratio
"Reason is the very life of law. When the reason of a law once ceases, the law itself generally ceases. Such is the significance of reasoning in any rule of law. Giving reasons furthers the cause of justice as well as avoids uncertainty and helps in the observance of law of precedent. Concept of reasoned judgment has become an indispensable part of basic rule of law and, in fact, is a mandatory requirement of procedural law."
Further Ratio
"Reason is the heartbeat of every conclusion. Without the same, it becomes lifeless. Reasons substitute subjectivity by objectivity. The absence of reasons either in the order passed by the authority or in the record contemporaneously maintained, is clearly suggestive of the order being arbitrary, hence, legally unsustainable."
When to Use — When GST adjudication order dismisses reply without reasons. Cite: order without reasons = legally unsustainable. Press for a speaking order with specific reasons for each ground of reply.
M.C. Mehta v Kamal Nath
Supreme Court of India | JT 1996 (11) SC 467 | Public Trust Doctrine
Key Ratio
The State is the trustee of all natural resources which are by nature meant for public use and enjoyment. Public at large is the beneficiary of the seashore, running waters, air, forests and ecologically fragile lands. The State as a trustee is under a legal duty to protect the natural resources. These resources meant for public use cannot be converted into private ownership.
When to Use — In disputes involving mining lease, forestry rights, 2G spectrum allotment, or any State largesse given without fair procedure. Argue: State holds this as trustee — action without competitive procedure / non-arbitrary method = breach of public trust.
Indibily Creative (P) Ltd v State of WB
Supreme Court of India | (2020) 12 SCC 436 | Public Trust — Article 21
Key Ratio
"The Public Trust Doctrine is a part of the law of the land. The doctrine has grown from article 21 of the Constitution. Power vested by the State in a public authority should be viewed as a trust coupled with duty to be exercised in larger public and social interest. Power is to be exercised strictly adhering to the statutory provisions and fact situation of a case. Public authorities cannot play fast and loose with the powers vested in them. A decision taken in an arbitrary manner contradicts the principle of legitimate expectation. An authority is under a legal obligation to exercise the power reasonably and in good faith to effectuate the purpose for which power stood conferred."
When to Use — When GST authority exercises power in an arbitrary or mala fide manner. "Public authorities cannot play fast and loose with the powers vested in them" — powerful line for oral arguments before GSTAT / High Court.
Neelima Misra v Harinder Kaur Paintal
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1990 SC 1402 | Classification of Functions
Key Ratio
"An administrative function is called quasi-judicial when there is an obligation to adopt a judicial approach and to comply with the basic requirements of justice. When there is no such obligation, the decision is called 'purely administrative' and there is no third category."
When to Use — When Department argues that a particular action (e.g., internal circular, policy decision) need not follow natural justice. Counter: the function is quasi-judicial because it has consequences on citizen's rights — obligation to adopt judicial approach arises.
Ritesh Tewari v State of UP [+ Chain of Cases on Bad Inception]
Supreme Court of India | (2010) 10 SCC 677 | Illegality from Inception
Key Ratio
"It is a settled legal position that, if an order is bad in its inception, it does not get sanctified at a later stage. A subsequent action/development cannot validate an action which was not lawful at its inception, for the reason that illegality strikes at the root of the order. It would be beyond the competence of any authority to validate such an order. It would be ironical to permit a person to rely upon a law, in violation of which he has obtained the benefit."
When to Use — When Department issues SCN without DIN, or without following prescribed procedure (e.g., no approval before search, SCN beyond limitation) — argue: bad at inception = cannot be cured by subsequent steps. Entire proceeding collapses.
R.R. Verma v UOI
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1980 SC 1461 : (1980) 3 SCC 402 | Admin vs Quasi-Judicial
Key Ratio
"To extend the principle of review to pure administrative decisions would indeed lead to untoward and startling results. The government should remain free to alter its policies or decisions in administrative matters from time to time, so that it can carry on its day-to-day administrative functions effectively. When the government exercises quasi-judicial powers vested in it by a statute, the principle is that the power to review, must be conferred by a statute either specifically or by necessary implication. But this principle is not applied to a purely administrative order."
When to Use — When Department tries to review its earlier quasi-judicial order without statutory authority. Argue: power to review must be conferred by statute — cannot be implied for quasi-judicial orders. Use also when challenging consistency of Department's policy changes.
Rajesh Awasthi v Nand Lal Jaiswal
Supreme Court of India | AIR 2013 SC 78 : (2013) 1 SCC 501 | Designated Authority
Key Ratio — Deepak Misra J (Concurring)
"It is manifest in the selection of the appellant that there is absence of 'intellectual objectivity' in the decision-making process. It is to be kept in mind that constructive intellect brings in good rationale and reflects conscious exercise of conferred power."
When to Use — When GST order passed by wrong authority or without proper application of mind. Argue: absence of intellectual objectivity in exercise of statutory power = legally infirm. Also use when designated authority delegated function to subordinate without statutory backing.
Embassy Property Developments Pvt Ltd v State of Karnataka
Supreme Court of India | (2020) 1 MLJ 65 | Forum — NCLT vs High Court
Key Ratio
NCLT, being a special forum to discharge certain specific functions, cannot be elevated to the status of a superior court having power of judicial review over administrative action. Wherever corporate debtor has to exercise a right that falls outside purview of IBC, especially in the realm of public law, they cannot, through resolution professional, take a bypass and go before NCLT for enforcement of such a right.
When to Use — In cross-forum disputes where party tries to enforce public law rights (mining lease, GST refund, administrative action) before NCLT/IBC process. Argue: public law remedy lies only before High Court in writ jurisdiction — not before special tribunals.
Secretary of State for Education v Tameside Borough Council / Wednesbury Corp
House of Lords, UK | 1977 AC 1014 / (1948) 1 KB 223 | Unreasonableness Standard
Key Ratio — Lord Diplock
"Unreasonable" as descriptive of the way in which the public authority has purported to exercise a discretion vested in it by statute has become a term of legal art. To fall within this expression, it must be conduct which no sensible authority acting with due appreciation of its responsibilities would have decided to adopt. A decision which is so outrageous in its defiance of logic or of accepted moral standards that no sensible person, who had applied his mind to the question to be decided, could have arrived at it.
When to Use — When GST penalty or demand is grossly disproportionate or based on irrational grounds. Argue Wednesbury unreasonableness before High Court in writ jurisdiction. Accepted by Indian courts in all administrative law challenges.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter 15 — Administrative Bodies and Powers

⚖ Chapter 15 — Administrative Bodies and Powers

Source: Principles of Administrative Law — M.P. Jain | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

📄 Pages 965 – 992
Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void
Condition / If / Unless
Exception / Optional
Neutral Reference
Caution / Warning
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
Power to Review / Modify Administrative Orders
Section 21 — General Clauses Act, 1897
Limits on Review — Natural Justice & Equitable Constraints
Functus Officio & Power to Revoke Permissions
Rectification of Mistakes
15.6 — Some Examples of Administrative Powers
15.6.1 — Non-Statutory Powers
15.6.2 — Statutory Administrative Powers
15.6.2.1 — Doctrine of Full Faith and Credit
15.7.1 — Mid-Term Review
15.8 — Communication of Administrative Orders
Judicial Review — Administrative vs Quasi-Judicial
⚔ Hearing Arsenal
Power to Review / Modify Administrative Orders
📄 Pages 965–966
⚙ Can an Administrative Order Be Reviewed or Revised?
General Principle | Shrimdam Case | Bool Chand Case
✓ GENERAL RULE
An administrative order CAN be reviewed, revised or changed even when no such power is specifically conferred on the concerned authority by statute
📝 Unlike courts, administrative bodies are not permanently locked into past decisions. The government can revisit its own orders — especially when fresh circumstances arise.
📋 Shrimdam Case
Govt refused to release premises to landlady under AP Buildings (Lease, Rent & Eviction) Control Act, 1960
FRESH CIRCUMSTANCES
Landlady made another representation citing additional and new circumstances
COURT HELD
Govt CAN review its own order taking subsequent events into account — not a nullity
"In case of bona fide need, subsequent events must be taken into account if they are relevant on the question of release or possession of the premises."
📋 Bool Chand Case
AIR 1968 SC 292
State Govt appointed Vice-Chancellor of Kurukshetra University for 3 years. No express power in Act to remove before term expiry
ISSUE
Can tenure be terminated before expiry in absence of specific statutory provision?
SC HELD
Absence of specific provision does NOT mean tenure cannot be determined. Inherent power exists.
"Unable to hold that a person appointed Vice-Chancellor is entitled to continue for the full period, even if it turns out that he is physically decrepit, mentally infirm, or grossly immoral."
📝 Power to appoint carries an incidental power to respond to gross unfitness — even without an express removal clause in the statute.
Section 21 — General Clauses Act, 1897 — Power to Modify Orders
📄 Pages 965–967
📜 Section 21 — General Clauses Act, 1897
Statutory Source: Power to Add / Amend / Vary / Rescind Orders
📝 This provision says: if a law gives power to make an order, that same power automatically includes the power to later change or cancel that order. No separate law is needed for amendment/cancellation.
STATUTORY TEXT
A statutory power to make an order includes "a power, exercisable in the like manner and subject to the like sanction and conditions (if any), to add, amend, vary or rescind" any orders so issued.
ADD
Supplementary conditions to existing order
AMEND
Modify existing order partially
VARY
Change scope / applicability
RESCIND
Cancel / withdraw entirely
⚠ KEY CONDITION
Power to modify must be exercised in the like manner and subject to the like sanction and conditions as the original power — cannot modify through a lower-level authority or bypassing original procedure
📝 If the original order needed Secretary-level approval, the amendment also needs the same. A junior officer cannot sneak in a modification.
💡 GST: A CBIC Circular granting a benefit must be withdrawn/amended by the same Board-level authority following the same procedure. A field formation circular cannot override a Board circular.
Limits on Review Power — Natural Justice & Equitable Constraints
📄 Pages 966–968 | RR Verma v UOI — AIR 1980 SC 1461
⚖ Caveats on the General Power to Review / Revise
SCENARIO A
Order unfavourable to person cancelled → Favourable order made
NO DIFFICULTY
Natural justice NOT required when revision benefits the person
📝 Changing an adverse order to a beneficial one — the person has nothing to complain about. No prior hearing needed.
SCENARIO B
Order favourable to person reversed / modified to his disadvantage or cancelled
Natural Justice MUST be followed
Hearing mandatory before adverse revision
📝 Audi alteram partem = hear the other side. Reversing a granted benefit requires giving the person a chance to respond.
💡 GST Sec 108 CGST: Revisionary officer proposing to revise a favourable adjudication order against taxpayer must give prior notice and hearing.
Three Additional Constraints on Revision Power
1
Grounds for change must justify the drastic action — mere change of opinion not sufficient
2
Promissory Estoppel — if person acted in reliance on earlier favourable order, equity may prevent reversal
📝 You cannot go back on a promise if the other party already acted on it to their detriment.
3
Third-party rights created on basis of earlier order — court may deny power to cancel on equitable principles
RR VERMA v UOI — AIR 1980 SC 1461
If an administrative decision is reviewed, the revised decision remains subject to judicial review on all grounds on which any administrative decision may be questioned in a court. Review does not insulate the new order from scrutiny.
Functus Officio & Power to Revoke Permissions
📄 Pages 965–967 | State of UP v Maharaja Dharmander Prasad Singh — AIR 1989 SC 997
🔄 Functus Officio — Is Power Exhausted After Granting Permission?
State of UP v Maharaja Dharmander Prasad Singh | AIR 1989 SC 997 : (1989) 2 SCC 505
📝 Functus officio = having discharged one's duty and therefore having no further power. Question: once a permission is granted, is the authority done forever — or can it later cancel that permission?
Three Grounds Where Revocation Power EXISTS (SC Held)
GROUND 1
Permission obtained by misrepresentation or fraud by grantee
Power to grant includes power to revoke even without express statutory provision
GROUND 2
Violation of terms & conditions of the grant by grantee
Power to grant includes power to revoke even without any other express statutory provision
GROUND 3
Officers who granted permission betrayed trust — acted corruptly / improperly
Grant may be voidable — authority may initiate declaratory action to nullify the permission
SC RATIO — REGULATORY POWER NOT EXHAUSTED
"The power to regulate with the obligations and functions that go with and are incidental to it are not spent or exhausted with the grant of permission. The power to grant must be held to include the power to revoke or cancel the permit, even in the absence of any other express statutory provisions in that behalf."
⚠ FUNCTUS OFFICIO ARGUMENT REJECTED
Functus officio applies to quasi-judicial / adjudicatory orders — NOT to regulatory / administrative permissions. Regulatory power is ongoing — not a one-time event exhausted by a single grant.
📝 Courts cannot reopen their final judgments (functus officio). But administrative bodies are different — their regulatory power is continuous and does not exhaust with each individual grant.
💡 GST: Registration obtained by fraud or misrepresentation — cancellation valid even if GST provision on cancellation appears narrow. Inherent power + Section 21 GCA + fraud ground collectively support cancellation.
Rectification of Mistakes in Administrative Orders
📄 Pages 988–989 | UOI v KM. Shankarappa — AIR 2000 SC 3678
✎ When Can an Error in an Administrative Order Be Corrected?
✓ GENERAL PRINCIPLE
If a mistake is committed in passing an administrative order — it may be rectified. However, whether a hearing is needed depends on the nature of the mistake.
✓ APPARENT MISTAKE
Mistake apparent on the face of the record
Rectification PERMISSIBLE without any hearing to aggrieved party
📝 Clerical error, arithmetic mistake, obvious inconsistency on the face of order — correctable at any time without full hearing process.
💡 Assessment order: Rs 1,00,000 in words but Rs 10,00,000 in figures — clear clerical error. Rectifiable without notice.
✗ NON-APPARENT MISTAKE
Mistake requires inquiry / analysis beyond the face of record
Rectification requires principles of natural justice — hearing must be given to aggrieved party
📝 If correcting the "mistake" requires re-examining facts, evidence or legal questions — it is essentially a review. The affected person must be heard first.
UOI v KM. Shankarappa — AIR 2000 SC 3678 : (2001) 1 SCC 582
QUASI-JUDICIAL BODY — FINALITY PRINCIPLE
Once a quasi-judicial body (Appellate Tribunal — retired HC Judge / qualified expert) gives its decision, that decision is final and binding on the executive and government. Permitting the executive to review/revise a quasi-judicial decision = interference with judicial functions.
📝 A quasi-judicial body's order is treated like a court's order. The government cannot override or revise it. Only an appellate authority or superior court can interfere.
💡 GST: Once GSTAT passes its order (quasi-judicial body), Department cannot administratively nullify it. Only High Court on further appeal can interfere.
Bihar State Co-operative Marketing Union v Dindyalsingh — 1995 Supp (4) SCC 647
As a principle of law, there is no legal bar or prohibition against an administrative body reviewing its earlier decision — provided parties likely to be affected are afforded an opportunity of hearing.
15.6 — Some Examples of Administrative Powers
📄 Page 973
📚 15.6 — Some Examples of Administrative Powers
Overview | Two Main Categories
§15.6.1
Non-Statutory Powers
(without specific legislative sanction)
§15.6.2
Statutory Administrative Powers
(backed by legislation)
GUIDING PRINCIPLE
Administrative power — whether statutory or non-statutory — cannot infringe any person's legal rights without the authority of law. The distinction between the two determines the scope of permissible action and intensity of judicial review.
15.6.1 — Non-Statutory Powers
📄 Pages 973–975
🏛 15.6.1 — Non-Statutory Administrative Powers
Executive Powers Without Specific Legislative Sanction
📝 The government has inherent executive powers beyond what statutes explicitly say. It can act administratively without needing a separate law for each action — provided it does not harm anyone's legal rights.
What Government CAN Do Without Specific Statutory Power
#Power / ActionExample / Case Reference
1Issue or re-issue administrative directions from time to timeGeneral executive power — no separate law needed for each direction
2Establish fair price shopsSarkari Sasta Anaj Vikreta Sangh v State of MP — AIR 1981 SC 2030
3Issue directions and instructions to subordinate authoritiesArticle 162 of Constitution — executive power of State
4Appoint an ad hoc enquiry officer or committeePreliminary inquiry — not a formal Commission of Inquiry
5Appoint an enquiry commission to enquire into a matter of public importanceHarekrishna Mahtab v Chief Minister, Orissa — AIR 1971 Ori 175
6Suspend a student pending enquiry for breach of disciplineAbhay Kumar v K. Srinivasan — AIR 1981 Del 381
7Constitute a body by resolution (e.g. Sanskrit Council) to hold examinations and publish resultsG. Misra v Orissa Association of Sanskrit Learning — AIR 1971 Ori 212
8Create administrative bodies and assign functions — provided they cannot infringe legal rightsPlanning Commission of India — created under administrative power, no statutory basis, purely advisory. Also Criminal Injuries Compensation Board (UK).
9Terminate a contract according to its termsNo statutory power needed; question is only whether action is in consonance with contract terms
⚠ HARD LIMIT
Non-statutory administrative actions cannot infringe any person's legal rights. The moment action encroaches on legal rights, statutory authority becomes mandatory (see 15.6.2).
EQUITIES MAY ARISE
Even when government acts only administratively and without statutory sanction, equities may arise in favour of an affected person (e.g. promissory estoppel). The non-statutory nature of the action does not prevent equitable remedies.
15.6.2 — Statutory Administrative Powers
📄 Pages 975–978
📋 15.6.2 — Statutory Administrative Powers
Rule of Law | Naraindas Principle | Satwant Singh Case | RS Dass Case
FIRM PRINCIPLE OF ADMINISTRATIVE LAW
Executive action which prejudices the legal right or fundamental right of any person must have the authority of law to support it. Without statutory backing, such action is void.
📝 If government wants to restrict or take away a citizen's rights — there must be a law empowering it to do so. Executive convenience alone is not enough.
NARAINDAS v STATE OF MP — APEX COURT RATIO
"If the executive action taken by the State Government encroaches on any private rights, it would have to be supported by legislative authority, for under the rule of law which prevails in our country every executive action which operates to the prejudice of any person must have the authority of law to support it."
Actions That MUST Have Statutory Backing
Restricting person to reside in specified place — cannot be imposed by mere executive order
Affecting right to property adversely — must have statutory backing
Discharge of legislative or adjudicatory function — needs authority of law
Imposing tax or expenditure from Consolidated Fund — needs authority of law
📋 Satwant Singh v APO — AIR 1967 SC 1836 — Passport Case
EARLIER POSITION
Passports issued/refused by administrative power — no law on the subject
SC OBJECTION
Art 21 guarantees right to travel abroad — "no person can be deprived except according to procedure established by law." Unfettered discretion = discrimination under Art 14
RESULT
Parliament enacted Passport Act, 1967 — converting administrative power into a statutory regulated one
📋 RS Dass v UOI — Selection / Promotion = Pure Administrative Power
"No vested legal right of a member of the State Civil Service who, after being considered, is not included in the select list, is adversely affected. Non-inclusion in select list does not take away any right that may have accrued to him as a government servant. No opportunity to make representation is necessary to be afforded to him for making representation against the proposed supersession."
📝 Selection/promotion is a privilege subject to competent authority's assessment — not a right. Since no existing right is taken away, natural justice does not apply to selection decisions.
💡 GST: Transfer/posting of officers = administrative power, no natural justice required. But cancellation of registration (affecting legal rights) = statutory power + natural justice mandatory.
15.6.2.1 — Doctrine of Full Faith and Credit; Public Duty and Public Obligations
📄 Pages 986–988 | Canara Bank Case | Delhi International Airport Case
📜 15.6.2.1 — Full Faith and Credit Doctrine & Public Accountability
Full Faith and Credit Doctrine
Official acts done / performed by officials carry presumptive evidence of regularity in faithful discharge of duties
📝 Official acts carry a presumption they were done properly. Burden is on the person challenging them to show irregularity.
Public Duty & Public Accountability
