"Principles of public accountability apply to officers/officials — responsibility to be just and fair. Greater the power to decide, higher is the responsibility."
Delay in decision-making in govt hierarchy = growing concern. Delayed decisions can cause prejudice to rights besides violating statutory rules
Every officer in state hierarchy = "public servant" accountable to public as well as State. Dual responsibility must be applied with rigour for proper governance.
📋 Canara Bank v M. Mahesh Kumar — Retrospective Effect of Administrative Orders
FACTS
Employee died in 1998 under Dying-in-Harness Scheme (1993). Compassionate appointment claimed. Bank refused — relied on ex-gratia payment scheme of 2005 replacing earlier scheme.
SC HELD
Ex-gratia payment scheme of 2005 being an administrative/executive order cannot have retrospective effect so as to take away rights already accrued under the scheme of 1993.
KEY PRINCIPLE
An administrative or executive order cannot operate retrospectively to destroy vested / accrued rights. Legislative change may have retrospective effect if expressly stated; administrative orders cannot.
📝 Rights that have crystallised under an existing scheme cannot be taken away by a later administrative order. That would be unfair and legally impermissible.
💡 GST: A retrospective notification withdrawing an exemption cannot take away ITC / refund rights already accrued under the earlier exemption notification. Vested rights cannot be destroyed retrospectively by administrative order.
📋 Delhi International Airport Ltd v International Lease Finance Corporation — AIR 2015 SC 1903
KEY PRINCIPLE — MINUTES ≠ STATUTORY ORDER
Minutes of a meeting of an authority cannot be treated as a general or special order passed by the Central Government unless statutory rules themselves so provide. Without compliance with applicable rules of business of government, no general or special order in writing can be treated as having been passed by Central Government. Administrative instructions cannot supersede statutory rules.
📝 A decision taken in a meeting and recorded in minutes is NOT a statutory order. Formal procedures for issuing government orders must be followed — informality cannot override statute.
💡 GST: A meeting between CBIC officers and trade bodies recording consensus — cannot be treated as a circular or notification. Only formally issued notifications/circulars under Sections 164/168 CGST Act carry statutory force. FAQs and press notes are not law.
ADDITIONAL PRINCIPLE — RULES NOT FRAMED
Even where a statute provides for things to be done subject to rules, action taken without framing the rules would not render that action invalid — if the statute is workable even without the rules, it must be given effect.
📝 Absence of delegated rules does not paralyse a statute. If the law can function without subsidiary rules — the absence of rules does not void actions taken under it.
15.7.1 — Mid-Term Review
📄 Pages 989–990 | Tagin Litin v State of Arunachal Pradesh — AIR 1996 SC 2121
🔄 15.7.1 — Mid-Term Review
Altering Order Before Communication | Change of Government
✓ BEFORE COMMUNICATION
An order can be altered or rescinded by the authority before it is communicated to the concerned person
📝 Like a letter being drafted — you can change it before it is sent. Authority retains full power to modify until communication happens. Tagin Litin case.
✗ AFTER COMMUNICATION
Once communicated to the person — principles of natural justice and legitimate expectation apply before any adverse modification
📝 Once the person has received the order and begun to rely on it — taking it back affects their rights. Natural justice kicks in.
MID-TERM REVIEW PRINCIPLE
An authority is entitled to reconsider the matter even during pendency — provided the parties likely to be affected are afforded an opportunity of hearing where the revised order adversely affects them.
CHANGE OF GOVERNMENT
Merely because there was a change in the government — there is no necessity to review all decisions of the previous government. The authority must assign good reasons for reviewing earlier orders.
📝 A new government cannot simply undo all decisions of the previous government without reason. Each order reviewed needs a proper factual or legal basis — change in political power alone is insufficient.
💡 GST: A new Commissioner cannot cancel earlier favourable adjudication orders merely on change of charge. Proper grounds for revision under Section 108 CGST must exist.
15.8 — Communication of an Administrative Order
📄 Pages 990–992 | Lim Chin Aik v R | State of Punjab v Khemi Ram — AIR 1969 SC 214
📤 15.8 — When Does an Administrative Order Become Effective?
Legislative Orders vs Administrative Orders | Dispatch vs Receipt
LEGISLATIVE ORDERS
(general nature — affects many persons known or unknown)
Must be notified in the Official Gazette
Communication to each individual person NOT necessary — gazette publication = constructive notice to all
📝 GST notifications published in Official Gazette: taxpayers cannot say "I didn't receive it personally" — constructive notice through gazette is sufficient. State of Gujarat v Panch of Nani Mamas Pole (SC).
INDIVIDUAL ADMINISTRATIVE ORDERS
(specific to identified person)
QUESTION
Effective from date of dispatch OR date of actual receipt?
"No uniform rule can be laid down for the purpose."
GENERAL — RECEIPT REQUIRED:
Especially when non-compliance leads to punishment
Lim Chin Aik v R (Privy Council): "Common sense presumes a man was aware of the order before he can be said to have contravened it."
EXCEPTION — DISPATCH RULE:
Suspension order effective from date of dispatch, not receipt
State of Punjab v Khemi Ram — AIR 1969 SC 214: Suspension dispatched 31 July, employee due to retire 4 Aug → effective from 31 July → validly suspended before retirement
📝 Penal/punishment orders: receipt required (fairness demands awareness). Status-changing orders like suspension: dispatch may be enough — once dispatched, order is out of govt's hands.
PRE-COMMUNICATION MODIFICATION — Tagin Litin (AIR 1996 SC 2121)
An order can be altered or rescinded by the authority before it is communicated to the person concerned. Once communicated, legitimate expectation / natural justice applies before any adverse modification.
💡 GST: SCN dispatched by speed post — limitation for taxpayer's reply runs from date of service (receipt), not dispatch. Adjudication order: effective from date of communication — important for filing revision/appeal within time.
Judicial Review — Administrative vs Quasi-Judicial Functions
📄 Pages 968–972
🔍 Scope of Judicial Review — Depends on Nature of Function
"The grounds, depth, form and content of judicial review vary from administrative to adjudicatory functions."
ADMINISTRATIVE FUNCTION
Decision based on policy, convenience, expediency — no legal rights of parties being adjudicated
Judicial review: More deferential
Courts respect government's policy choices
Standard: Wednesbury unreasonableness / patent arbitrariness (Art 14)
📝 Courts will not substitute their judgment for government's on policy — only strike down what is patently unreasonable, mala fide, or arbitrary.
💡 GST Council rate recommendations — primarily policy. Courts generally defer unless manifestly arbitrary.
QUASI-JUDICIAL FUNCTION
Decision determines legal rights of parties — authority must hear parties, consider evidence, apply law
Judicial review: More intensive
(i) Natural justice followed
(ii) Decision on relevant material
(iii) No extraneous considerations
(iv) Reasons given
📝 When authority acts like a judge — deciding rights in a dispute — courts look more closely. It must act fairly and on evidence.
💡 GST adjudication (Sec 73/74) and GSTAT = quasi-judicial. SCN reply ignored = void. Order without reasons = void.
Tests — What Makes an Authority Quasi-Judicial?
TEST 1
Authority must decide a lis (dispute between parties)
TEST 2
Decision affects legal rights of specific individuals
TEST 3
Obligation to hear parties and give reasoned decision
TEST 4
Duty to act on relevant facts and applicable legal principles
PRIMA FACIE INQUIRY — NO HEARING AT INVESTIGATION STAGE
A person need NOT be given a hearing when investigation is conducted merely to determine whether there is a prima facie case against him to undertake a formal inquiry. The decision to investigate is purely an administrative one.
📝 Before a formal charge-sheet, there is a preliminary inquiry stage. At this stage no hearing is needed. The right to be heard arises only when formal notice/charge is issued.
💡 GST: DGGI conducting preliminary inquiry under Section 67 — no prior notice needed. But when SCN under Section 73/74 is issued, full natural justice is mandatory. Investigation stage ≠ adjudication stage.
⚔ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 15 | Administrative Bodies and Powers
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before adjudicating officers, appellate authorities, and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
State of UP v Maharaja Dharmander Prasad Singh
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1989 SC 997 : (1989) 2 SCC 505 | Chapter XV
Key Ratio — Regulatory Power Not Exhausted After Grant | Functus Officio Rejected
"The power to regulate with the obligations and functions that go with and are incidental to it are not spent or exhausted with the grant of permission. The power to grant must be held to include the power to revoke or cancel the permit, even in the absence of any other express statutory provisions in that behalf" — where permission was obtained by fraud or conditions were violated.
When to Use — When Department argues a GST registration or approval once granted is irrevocable for want of express cancellation power. Counter: regulatory power is not exhausted by grant; inherent power of revocation exists on grounds of fraud or violation of conditions. Also use when assessee argues cancellation order is bad for want of express statutory provision.
Section 21 — General Clauses Act, 1897
Statutory Provision | Applicable to all administrative orders, notifications, and circulars
Key Principle — Power to Add, Amend, Vary or Rescind
A statutory power to make an order includes "a power, exercisable in the like manner and subject to the like sanction and conditions (if any), to add, amend, vary or rescind" any orders so issued. An administrative power to make an order includes power to modify the same.
When to Use — (a) When Department issues a circular modifying an earlier one: argue it must follow "like manner and like conditions" as original. (b) When assessee argues cancellation invalid for want of express revocation power: counter with Section 21 GCA. (c) To establish that modifications to CBIC circulars must follow the same procedure as original issuance.
Naraindas v State of MP — Rule of Law Principle
Supreme Court of India | Affirmed in multiple decisions | Chapter XV §15.6.2
Key Ratio — Statutory Authority Mandatory for Adverse Executive Action
"If the executive action taken by the State Government encroaches on any private rights, it would have to be supported by legislative authority, for under the rule of law which prevails in our country every executive action which operates to the prejudice of any person must have the authority of law to support it."
When to Use — When Department seeks to enforce a circular/direction that prejudices assessee's existing legal right (ITC entitlement, exemption) without backing in the CGST Act. A circular cannot override statutory rights. Section 168 power does not authorise override of Section 16 rights conferred by statute.
UOI v KM. Shankarappa — Quasi-Judicial Finality
Supreme Court of India | AIR 2000 SC 3678 : (2001) 1 SCC 582
Key Principle — Executive Cannot Revise Quasi-Judicial Orders
Once a quasi-judicial body like the Appellate Tribunal gives its decision, that decision would be final and binding, so far as the executive and the government is concerned. To permit the executive to review and/or revise that decision would amount to interference with the exercise of judicial functions by a quasi-judicial board.
When to Use — When Department tries to review or nullify a favourable GSTAT/appellate order through administrative action. Once GSTAT decides, the executive cannot administratively unsettle it — only High Court/Supreme Court on further appeal can interfere.
Natural Justice — Mandatory Before Adverse Revision of Favourable Order
Administrative Law Principle | Chapter XV | Multiple SC decisions
Key Principle — Hearing Mandatory Before Adverse Modification
If an order favourable to a person is reversed or modified to his disadvantage or cancelled, then it may become necessary for the concerned authority to comply with the principles of natural justice, and the grounds for the change must be such as would justify such drastic action.
When to Use — When revisionary officer under Section 108 CGST proposes to revise a favourable adjudication order against taxpayer. Before reversing a favourable order, natural justice mandates full opportunity to the assessee. Grounds must justify the drastic step. Mere change of opinion or change of charge is insufficient.
Canara Bank v M. Mahesh Kumar — No Retrospective Effect of Administrative Orders
Supreme Court of India | Chapter XV §15.6.2.1
Key Principle — Administrative Order Cannot Destroy Accrued Rights Retrospectively
An administrative or executive order cannot have retrospective effect so as to take away rights already accrued to a person under an earlier scheme in force at the time of the relevant event. The ex-gratia payment scheme being an administrative/executive order could not retrospectively take away the right accrued to the dependent of the employee under the compassionate appointment scheme of 1993.
When to Use — When Department relies on a retrospective notification/circular to deny ITC or exemption benefit already accrued under an earlier notification. Argue: administrative orders cannot operate retrospectively to destroy vested rights. Rights that crystallised under an earlier notification cannot be taken away by a later administrative order with retrospective effect.
Lim Chin Aik v R — Awareness Required Before Penalty for Non-Compliance
Privy Council | (1963) 1 All ER 223 | Principle adopted by Supreme Court of India | Chapter XV §15.8
Key Ratio — Order Must Be Known Before Penalty Can Be Imposed
"Where a man is said to have contravened an order or an order of prohibition, the common sense of the language presumes that he was aware of the order before he can be said to have contravened it. It will be unfair to subject a person to any punishment for infringing an order of which he knows nothing."
When to Use — When penalty is imposed for non-compliance with a notice/order that was never properly served on the taxpayer. Argue: penalty for "contravention" requires actual knowledge — mere dispatch without proof of receipt does not suffice where penal consequences follow. Particularly useful in Section 73/74 demands where SCN was not properly served.
Delhi International Airport Ltd v International Lease Finance Corporation
Supreme Court of India | AIR 2015 SC 1903 | Chapter XV §15.6.2.1
Key Principle — Minutes of Meeting Not Statutory Order | Admin Instructions Cannot Override Statute
Unless the statutory rules themselves permit an exception, the general or special order, in writing, without compliance with applicable rules of business of government, cannot override the statutory regulation. Such administrative instructions cannot supersede the statutory rules.
When to Use — When Department relies on minutes of meetings, press releases, FAQ documents, or trade notices as equivalent to a circular or notification. Argue: only formally issued notifications/circulars under Sections 164/168 CGST Act carry statutory force. FAQs and informal communications are administrative instructions — they cannot supersede statutory rules or override taxpayer's statutory rights.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter XVI — Some Typical Administrative Powers and Procedures

⚖️ Chapter XVI — Some Typical Administrative Powers and Procedures

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Safeguard
Prohibited / Caution / Problem
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Discretionary
Neutral / Statutory Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
16.1 Introductory
16.2 Inquiries & Investigations
16.2.1 Administrative Inquiries
16.2.2 Statutory Inquiries
16.2.3 Parliamentary Inquiry
16.3 Commissions of Inquiry Act, 1952
16.3.1 Legal Issues Under the Act
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
16.1 — Introductory: Administrative Powers — Nature & Scope
⚖️ Typical Administrative Powers & Procedures
Informational in Nature | Coercive in Effect
PURPOSE — Why Administration Needs These Powers
📝 The government needs information to do its job. These powers — inquiry, search, summons, inspection — are its tools to gather facts before taking action.
Hold inquiries & investigations
Search and seize
Require filing of reports & returns
Inspect premises & records
Summon witnesses & compel production of documents
DUAL NATURE — Two Faces of the Same Power
FOR ADMINISTRATION
Basically INFORMATIONAL
Gathers facts to decide course of action
FOR INDIVIDUAL
Essentially COERCIVE
May harm reputation, business, finances even before any action is taken
💡 GST officer summons a trader under Sec 70 CGST — no demand yet, but trader's bank may refuse credit, suppliers may panic. Coercive effect precedes the legal action.
GOLDEN RULE
An administrative authority can employ ONLY such coercive processes as the statute sanctions.
No authority of law = No coercion.
Without legal power → authority can only issue letters requesting appearance / production — individual may comply or refuse.
📝 If a law does not give the officer the power to force someone to appear or produce documents, the officer cannot force it. The person receiving the letter can simply say no.
💡 GST officers conducting surveys u/s 67 CGST — if they enter premises without authority, evidence collected is tainted. Assessee can challenge the very foundation of demand.
NECESSITY
These powers are of great consequence in the modern administrative process.
They must be subject to proper safeguards so they are not misused or abused.
16.2 — Inquiries and Investigations — Nature & Purpose
🔍 Inquiries & Investigations
"Inquiry" and "Investigation" — No Generic Difference in Indian Statutes
📝 In Indian law these two words are used interchangeably. Both mean the administration trying to find out facts.
SCOPE — What is "Governmental Investigation"?
BROAD — EXCLUDED
Scientific, commercial, agricultural investigations by Planning Commission, Bureau of Economics, Dept of Labour etc.
NARROW — INCLUDED
Administrative investigations & public inquiries
(Administrative Law's concern)
WHY INQUIRIES? — Multiple Purposes Served
Formulate policies & legislate
Unearth evidence for prosecution
Determine causes of accidents / calamities
Gauge public reaction to proposed policy
"Illuminate obscure areas" — find out what if anything should be done
⚠️ DISADVANTAGE — Coercive Impact on Individual
Even when inquiry does not result in any follow-up action or liability:
→ Physical inconvenience, mental agony, expenses
→ Reputation & business suffers adversely
→ Prospect of further action
Therefore: safeguards must be incorporated in the technique of inquiry to reconcile administrative exigencies with individual's interests & rights.
✓ POSITIVE FACE OF INQUIRY
Inquiry can be individual's FIRST LINE OF DEFENCE — gets early opportunity to clear himself against suspicion.
If statute requires inquiry before action → provision is MANDATORY → action without inquiry must be quashed.
📝 Think of inquiry as a blessing in disguise — instead of the government acting blindly, the individual gets to present his side. If inquiry is mandatory by statute, skipping it is fatal to the action.
💡 Under Industries (Dev & Reg) Act — action u/s 18-A without prior investigation u/s 15 is liable to be quashed. Same principle applies to GST adjudication orders passed without proper hearing.
16.2.1 — Administrative Inquiries (Informal / Ad Hoc)
📋 Administrative Inquiries
Initiated without Statutory Authority | Ad Hoc | Informal
FeatureAdministrative InquiryFormal CI Act Commission
Authority Executive / Administrative power — no statute needed Section 3, Commissions of Inquiry Act, 1952
Coercive Power NONE — cannot compel attendance or document production YES — civil court powers under Sec 4 of CI Act
Confidentiality Can be private, away from public gaze — witnesses may speak freely Usually held in public
Immunity NO immunity — statements can be used against witness in civil/criminal proceedings Section 6 immunity — statement before commission cannot be used against witness (except perjury)
Punishment Recommendation CAN recommend punishment/redress — not barred CANNOT recommend punishment for wrongs already committed (ultra vires: Dalmia case)
Public Confidence Credibility doubted — especially if conducted by a government official Open inquiry commanded greater public confidence
NATURAL JUSTICE — Applies Even to Administrative Inquiries
RULE
Natural justice rules operate in areas not covered by any law. They do NOT supplant the law of the land but supplement it.
An unjust decision in an administrative inquiry may have a MORE FAR-REACHING effect than a court decision in a quasi-judicial matter.
The rule that enquiry must be held in good faith and without bias or unreasonable conduct is now part of natural justice.
📝 Even if no law specifically says "follow natural justice," the inquiry officer must act fairly — give notice, hear the person, not be biased. Courts will quash findings that violate this basic fairness.
CASE: Sachin Kumar v DSSSB (2021) 4 SCC 631
Facts: Irregularities in GNCTD Grade-II DASS exam. Two committees appointed sequentially. Second committee found no irregularity in 281 candidates. But Vigilance report indicated serious flaws in entire Tier-I exam. Government cancelled entire exam.

SC held: Report of Second Committee was only ONE FACET of the matter. Deputy Chief Minister was justified in going beyond it.
"Recruitment to public services must command public confidence. Where the entire process is found to be flawed, its cancellation may undoubtedly cause hardship to a few who may not specifically be found to be involved in wrongdoing. But that is not sufficient to nullify the ultimate decision to cancel an examination where the nature of wrongdoing cuts through the entire process."
CASE: Prafulla Chandra v State of Orissa — AIR 1988 Ori
Fire at Paradip — Administrative inquiry → findings made without opportunity to explain materials.
Court: Though fact-finding body, not judicial — yet DUTY to follow natural justice.
"It has a duty to act following the principles of natural justice... which duty had been cast upon it by the notification itself."
Findings quashed — based on presumption lacking reasonable foundation.
📝 Even if the notification setting up the inquiry is silent on natural justice, the duty exists as a general principle. The officer CANNOT say "I wasn't told to follow fair procedure."
SECTION 11 BRIDGE — Converting Ad Hoc to Statutory
Section 11 of CI Act: Government may apply any provision of the CI Act to an inquiry authority appointed under administrative power.
On notification: That authority is DEEMED to be a commission appointed u/s 3.
Used in: Newspaper Fact Finding Committee (1972), Paradip fire inquiry.
📝 If an informal inquiry needs coercive powers (to force documents/witnesses), the government can use Section 11 to upgrade it to a statutory commission — without starting afresh.
16.2.2 — Statutory Inquiries
📜 Statutory Inquiries
Powers Conferred by Specific Statutes for Specific Purposes
TYPES OF STATUTORY INQUIRIES
Investigatory
Post-accident/post-event inquiry
(Railways, Motor Vehicles, Shipping, Aircraft)
Regulatory
To supervise private commerce
(Industries D&R Act, Banking Act, Companies Act)
Law Enforcement
Tax evasion, customs smuggling
(Income Tax, Customs Act)
Adjudicatory
Pre-decision hearing on proposed govt action
(Land Acquisition, Motor Vehicles route nationalisation)
🔑 CUSTOMS ACT INQUIRY — Critical Parallel for GST
S.107
Customs officer empowered by collector may examine any person acquainted with facts during smuggling inquiry
S.108
Gazetted customs officer can summon any person to give evidence in smuggling inquiry
Such inquiry = JUDICIAL PROCEEDING (IPC ss. 193, 228)
📝 When summoned under S.108, you MUST appear and tell the truth — it's treated like giving evidence in court. Silence = contempt. Lies = perjury.
PROTECTION
Customs officer ≠ Police officer
Statements u/ss 107-108 are NOT covered by Ss. 24-25 Evidence Act (no confession bar)
Person is NOT treated as accused
💡 Similar principle applies to GST summons u/s 70 CGST — GST officer is not a police officer, statement recorded cannot be retracted claiming it was coerced. SC has upheld this in multiple cases.
⚠️ VAGUE INVESTIGATION GROUNDS — DESTRUCTIVE OF INDIVIDUAL LIBERTY
Investigations initiated on vague or flimsy grounds in hope of finding incriminating material are destructive of individual liberty and may have serious impact on the person.
It is necessary to lay down in the statute the circumstances when inquiry may be initiated.
Where statute specifies definite circumstances → courts can quash inquiry if those grounds are not met.
[Rohtas Industries Ltd v S.D. Agarwal, AIR 1969 SC 707]
📝 The government cannot go on a fishing expedition — "let's investigate and see if we find anything wrong." If the law sets conditions for inquiry, those conditions must actually exist. Otherwise courts will stop the inquiry.
💡 Parallel in GST: SCN issued without DIN (Document Identification Number) or summons issued without jurisdiction — analogous to investigation initiated without statutory authority. Can be challenged at the threshold.
16.2.3 — Parliamentary Inquiry (Joint Parliamentary Committee)
🏛️ Parliamentary Inquiry
Joint Parliamentary Committee (JPC) | Bofors | Bombay Stock Exchange
POWERS
Take oral / written evidence
Call for documentary evidence
Send for persons, papers & records
Non-compliance = Contempt of House
LIMITATIONS — WHY JPCs ARE INEFFECTIVE
Composed on proportional party strength → divided on party lines
Ruling party tries to mitigate govt responsibility
Opposition tries to magnify the same
Truth becomes a casualty
Commission of Inquiry preferred for serious matters
USE OF PARLIAMENTARY COMMITTEE REPORTS IN COURT
[Kalpana Mehta v UOI, (2018) 7 SCC 1 — upheld in Bhaskar Shrachi Alloys v Damodar Valley Corp, (2018) 8 SCC 281]

Court CAN use Parliamentary committee reports for:
✓ Appreciating historical background of statutory provisions
✓ Resolving ambiguity / incongruity in enactment

Court CANNOT use for:
✗ Arriving at factual findings (invites contest)
📝 A JPC report can help understand why a law was passed, but it cannot substitute for court's own fact-finding. If you use JPC report to prove a fact, the other side can dispute it.
16.3 — The Commissions of Inquiry Act, 1952 (CI Act)
🏛️ Commissions of Inquiry Act, 1952
The statute par excellence for public inquiries | Wide Variety of Subjects
SECTION 3 — Appointment of Commission
CONDITIONS
Central or State Govt may appoint commission:
(i) Of its own motion
(ii) If of opinion that it is necessary to do so
(iii) For purpose of inquiry into any "definite matter of public importance"
(iv) Must be notified in Official Gazette (imperative — cannot be dispensed with)
[Shammbhu Nath v Kedar Prasad, AIR 1972 SC 1515]
📝 The "Official Gazette" publication is mandatory — it gives publicity to the notification and authenticates its contents. A commission not published in gazette is not valid.
DISCRETIONARY — NOT OBLIGATORY
Government is under NO legal or statutory obligation to appoint a commission.
No one can compel the government to appoint a commission.
[Peoples Union for Democratic Rights v Ministry of Home Affairs, AIR 1985 Del 268]
[Rajendran v Home Secretary, AIR 1983 Ker 59]
📝 Even if there is a major public tragedy and people demand an inquiry, the government may refuse. Courts traditionally cannot compel the appointment — though this view has been challenged post-Padfield.
EXCEPTION — MANDATORY APPOINTMENT
Government IS obligated to appoint when:
→ Resolution passed by House of People (Lok Sabha) / State Legislative Assembly
"MAY" CAN BECOME "SHALL"
When a power is coupled with duty, "may" can be interpreted as "shall" — discretion almost ceases.
If there arises a matter of great public importance → government is duty bound to institute an inquiry.
Refusal to exercise discretion on irrelevant grounds = invalid.
📝 "The government may appoint" doesn't always mean the government can simply refuse. If the facts are serious enough and the public interest is grave, courts may hold the discretion to be non-existent.
LEGISLATIVE COMPETENCE — Centre vs State
Central Govt
Can inquire into matters in List I, II or III of VII Schedule
State Govt
Can inquire into matters in List II or III only
No Overlap
If Centre appointed first → State cannot appoint on same matter without Centre's approval, and vice versa
POWERS OF COMMISSION — Sections 4 & 5
SECTION 4 — AUTO-CONFERRED CIVIL COURT POWERS
Every commission has automatic powers of civil court (while trying a suit under CPC) for:
(i) Summoning & enforcing attendance
(ii) Requiring discovery & production of documents
(iii) Examining persons on oath
(iv) Requisitioning public records
(v) Receiving evidence on affidavits
(vi) Issuing commissions for examining witnesses
(vii) Any other matter prescribed by rules
Can require attendance from ANY PART OF INDIA.
SECTION 5 — ADDITIONAL POWERS (If Govt Notifies)
S.5(1): If govt of opinion that nature of inquiry warrants → may apply Ss. 5(2)-(5) to commission
S.5(2): Can require any person to furnish information relevant to inquiry (legally bound — IPC Ss. 176-177)
S.5(3): Search & seizure powers (documents relating to inquiry)
S.5(4): Commission deemed civil court for IPC Ss. 175, 178, 179, 180, 228
S.5(5): Proceedings before commission = judicial proceeding (IPC Ss. 193, 228)
📝 Section 4 powers come automatically with every commission. Section 5 powers need the government to specifically extend them. If Section 5 is not applied, the commission cannot search or seize.
SECTION 6 — PROTECTION FOR WITNESSES
Statement made before commission → CANNOT be used against the person in any civil or criminal proceeding
EXCEPT: prosecution for giving false evidence (perjury)
Conditions: (i) Made in reply to question required by commission; OR (ii) Relevant to subject-matter of inquiry

Restriction: Extends only to ORAL statements — NOT documentary production
S.6 does NOT confer protection against self-incrimination under Art. 20(3) — Art. 20(3) applies only to accused of offence
[Kehar Singh v Delhi Admn, AIR 1988 SC 1883]
📝 If you truthfully answer the commission's questions, those answers cannot be used against you later in a court case. But if you lie — you can be prosecuted for perjury. And producing documents doesn't get this protection.
SECTION 8B — NATURAL JUSTICE — NOT TO CONDEMN UNHEARD
Although commission is not an adjudicatory body, adverse observations by such high-powered body can be irremediable.
Section 8B: Commission must give reasonable opportunity of being heard if:
→ It is of opinion that any person's reputation may be prejudicially affected, OR
→ It proposes to inquire into conduct of any person
Section 8C: Such person entitled to cross-examine witnesses deposing against him.
[Kiran Bedi & Jinder Singh v Inquiry Committee, AIR 1989 SC 714]
📝 The commission's report is not a court judgment — but if it says bad things about you, it can destroy your reputation permanently. So you have a right to be heard before that happens.
⚠️ CRITICAL — NATURE OF COMMISSION: NOT A COURT
Commission is a FACT-FINDING body — it does NOT decide any dispute.
It is NEITHER a civil court NOR a criminal court.
NO power of adjudication.
NO lis (dispute between parties).
NO accused, no accusers, no specific charges for trial.
Report = merely recommendatory, NOT binding on government.
NOT enforceable proprio vigore.
[Ram Krishna Dalmia v Justice Tendolkar, AIR 1958 SC 538]
📝 "Proprio vigore" = by its own force. The commission's report cannot be directly enforced — it's a recommendation to the government. The government may or may not act on it.
EVIDENTIARY VALUE — NIL IN CRIMINAL TRIAL
SC has ruled: Report of commission of inquiry has NO evidentiary value in trial of a criminal case.
[Thakkar Commission — Kehar Singh case — court refused to summon commission report]

SC: Findings of commission are NOT BINDING on a court — court must arrive at its own conclusions after independent assessment.
[Shambkant v State of Maharashtra, AIR 1992 SC 1879]
INDEPENDENCE — GOVERNMENT CANNOT RECONSTITUTE COMMISSION
[State of MP v Ajay Singh, AIR 1993 SC 825]
MP Govt replaced Justice Ramalinga (who was willing to continue) with another retired HC judge — without any vacancy.
SC: Government cannot reconstitute or substitute commission member — unless vacancy arises.
Only government control after appointment: Power to wind up under Section 7.
Commission must function as independent body to enjoy public confidence.
⚠️ SECTION 3(5) — WITHHOLDING REPORT (1986 AMENDMENT — CRITICISED)
Before 1986: Govt must place report before Lok Sabha within 6 months.
1986 Amendment: Govt may withhold report if satisfied it affects "sovereignty, integrity, security, foreign relations or public interest."

Criticism: "Public interest" is so vague — government can withhold any inconvenient report.
Public interest becomes synonymous with interest of government in power.
Majority in House automatically supports government → veto is no safeguard.
This amendment is against the tenet of open government.
16.3.1 — Legal Issues Arising Under the CI Act
⚖️ Legal Issues Under CI Act
Constitutional Validity | Jurisdiction | "Definite Matter of Public Importance" | Mala Fides | Bias
TWO TYPES OF COMMISSIONS
Advisory Commissions
Gather information, study problems, advise on policy
Eg: Monopolies Inquiry Commission
Investigative Commissions
Inquire into conduct of individuals & officials
Eg: Chagla Commission, Shah Commission, Thakkar Commission
Here public concern vs individual interests conflict
ISSUE 1 — CONSTITUTIONAL VALIDITY OF CI ACT
UPHELD: Ram Krishna Dalmia v Justice Tendolkar, AIR 1958 SC 538
Arguments raised AND rejected by SC:
(a) Outside Parliament's legislative competence → REJECTED — entries 94/List I and 45/List III empower Parliament to hold inquiries for legislative AND administrative purposes
(b) Usurps judicial functions → REJECTED — commission has no adjudicatory power, no order enforceable proprio vigore
(c) Arbitrary discretion under S.3 = Art.14 violation → REJECTED — preamble to Act controls the discretion; power vested in Government (high-powered body); limited to "definite matter of public importance"

BUT — Ultra vires exception: Term of reference requiring commission to recommend "redress or punishment for wrongs already committed" = ultra vires — punishment for past wrongs must be by courts, not commissions.
ISSUE 2 — "DEFINITE MATTER OF PUBLIC IMPORTANCE"
WHAT IT MEANS
[Ram Krishna Dalmia case]:
"A definite matter of public importance does not necessarily mean only some matter involving public benefit in the abstract. Quite conceivably the conduct of an individual person or a group of persons may assume such dangerous proportion as to make such conduct a matter of public importance urgently calling for a full inquiry."
📝 Even the misconduct of one person can be a matter of public importance if it affects enough people or undermines public confidence. The courts have interpreted this very broadly in favour of inquiry.
Held as Definite Matters:
✓ Individual's conduct/abuse of office [Bakshi Gulam Mohammad]
✓ Mismanagement of public endowments
✓ Economics of newspaper industry
✓ Conduct of legislators/public servants
✓ Affairs of industrial groups (Dalmia-Jain)
Held as Vague / NOT Definite:
✗ Allegations not appended to notification [P.K. Kunju v State of Kerala]
✗ Salaries to relatives, "contact men" engagement, hospital free treatment — general fishing [Orient Paper Mills v UOI]
✗ Notification not specifying matter but only category of persons → vagueness challenge
ISSUE 3 — CENTRE-STATE JURISDICTION
State of Karnataka v UOI, AIR 1978 SC 68
Can Central Govt inquire into conduct of State Ministers?
SC: YES — CI Act gives Central Govt wide power; inquiry into conduct of State Ministers = "definite matter of public importance."
Appointing commission ≠ interference with State executive functions or collective responsibility.
BUT: Centre & State cannot both inquire into SAME matter — if areas of reference differ, both can co-exist.
CAN STATE INQUIRE INTO EX-MINISTER'S CONDUCT?
YES — Ministers are collectively responsible to Legislature while in office. But once out of office, successor ministry CAN inquire.
[Krishna Ballabh Sahay — AIR 1969 SC 258]: "Otherwise each Ministry will become a law unto itself and the corrupt conduct of its Ministers will remain beyond scrutiny."
ISSUE 4 — MALA FIDES
GENERAL RULE: MALA FIDES IS HARD TO PROVE
Raised in several cases — invariably rejected by courts.
Courts refuse to accept mala fides based solely on "acute political rivalry between two governments."
If political rivalry = litmus test for mala fides → CI Act would be crippled, no government could inquire into its predecessor.
EXCEPTION — P.K. Kunju v State of Kerala (AIR 1970 Ker 252)
Kerala: Coalition govt — CM and Finance Minister from different parties. Commission appointed against Finance Minister only, though similar allegations existed against other ministers.
HC quashed: Mala fide — dominant object was to drop Finance Minister from Ministry, not to maintain purity of administration.
ISSUE 5 — NON-APPLICATION OF MIND
State of MP v Arjun Singh
Formation of opinion must be based on OBJECTIVE / REAL material — not merely on vague allegations or hearsay evidence or to make a fishing enquiry.
SC quashed a term of reference as no relevant material existed before the State Govt to form its subjective opinion.
ISSUE 6 — BIAS AGAINST COMMISSION MEMBERS
Ghanshyam Upadhyay v State of UP, (2020) 16 SCC 811
Allegations that Commission Chairman's brother was a legislator of party in power, and member was Inspector General of Police.
SC: Standard for applying test of personal bias against Enquiry Commission is very high.
Enquiry would be in public domain; petitioner already given liberty to participate; report to be filed before Court and complied with — these were sufficient safeguards.
Allegations were held insufficient to constitute bias.
NO TWO COMMISSIONS ON SAME MATTER
By necessary implication, S.3 does NOT confer power to appoint more than one commission to inquire into same charges/allegations.
Risk of two commissions arriving at different findings.
State Govt cannot appoint two separate commissions for same allegations.
PROCEDURE — NATURAL JUSTICE EVEN WHERE STATUTE SILENT
Kiran Bedi & Jinder Singh v Inquiry Committee, AIR 1989 SC 714:
Police officers required to testify at very inception of inquiry — BEFORE other witnesses were examined.
SC: Calling upon a person to produce evidence in his defence at the very inception = contradiction in terms — would disprove statements of witnesses NOT YET examined.
RIGHT to cross-examine accrues only when witness gives evidence ORALLY — not when done through affidavits.
COMPANIES ACT — INVESTIGATION INTO AFFAIRS (Parallel Principle)
Rohtas Industries Ltd v S.D. Agarwal, AIR 1969 SC 707
Investigation under S.237(b) Companies Act must not be ordered except on SATISFACTORY GROUNDS.
Inspector's report does not bind anybody — but appointment itself receives press publicity, adversely affecting reputation and business.
Board must proceed reasonably — must not be actuated by bad faith, must exclude irrelevant considerations.
"Formation of opinion is subjective, but existence of circumstances relevant to the inference as the sine qua non for action must be demonstrable."
💡 Parallel in GST: Arrest under S.69 CGST or provisional attachment under S.83 — "reason to believe" must be based on objective material, not a mere whim. Absence of material can be challenged by writ. Rohtas Industries principle applies.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 16 | Administrative Powers & Procedures
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Ram Krishna Dalmia v Justice Tendolkar
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1958 SC 538 | 1959 SCR 279
Key Ratio — Nature of Commission
"A clear distinction must be drawn between a decision which, by itself, has no force and no penal effect and a decision which becomes enforceable immediately or which may become enforceable by some action being taken. In the former case, the function could not be characterised as judicial."
Key Ratio — Scope of Commission
A commission appointed under the CI Act is not an adjudicatory body. Its report is purely recommendatory and not effective proprio vigore. Redress or punishment for wrongs already committed should be imposed by properly constituted courts — a commission cannot recommend punitive action for past wrongs.
When to Use — When challenging the report of a GST/Revenue inquiry commission or DG-GSTI investigation report being used as if it were a court finding. Cite to establish that such reports are not binding, have no evidentiary value in adjudication, and cannot substitute the adjudicating officer's independent application of mind.
Rohtas Industries Ltd v S.D. Agarwal
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1969 SC 707 | (1969) 1 SCC 325
Key Ratio — Investigation Must Be on Satisfactory Grounds
"From the provisions contained in sections 235 and 236 it is clear that the legislature considered that investigation into the affairs of a company is a very serious matter and it should not be ordered except on good grounds... the appointment of an Inspector is likely to receive much press publicity as a result of which the reputation and prospects of the company may be adversely affected. It should not therefore be ordered except on satisfactory grounds."
Second Ratio
"The formation of the opinion under section 237 of the Companies Act by the Central Government is subjective, but the existence of circumstances relevant to the inference as the sine qua non for action must be demonstrable."
When to Use — When challenging GST provisional attachment (S.83 CGST), arrest (S.69), or survey (S.67) on the ground that "reason to believe" is absent. The sine qua non — existence of circumstances — must be demonstrable by the officer, not imagined.
State of MP v Ajay Singh
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1993 SC 825 | (1993) 1 SCC 302
Key Ratio — Independence of Commission
"The scheme of the enactment is that the appropriate government should have no control over the commission after its constitution under section 3 of the Act except for the purpose of filling any vacancy which may have arisen in the office of a member of the commission apart from winding up the commission by issuance of a notification under section 7 of the Act if the continued existence of the commission is considered unnecessary."
Further Ratio
The commission should function as an independent body free from any government control so that it may enjoy public confidence.
When to Use — When challenging the reconstitution or interference with an inquiry authority by the appointing authority. Also cite as principle that fact-finding bodies (like DGGI investigation wings) must function independently without direction from the adjudicating authorities.
Kiran Bedi & Jinder Singh v Inquiry Committee
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1989 SC 714 | (1989) 1 SCC 494
Key Ratio — Right to Cross-Examine Before Adverse Findings
"Calling upon a person governed by section 8B to produce evidence in his defence at the very inception of the inquiry is a contradiction in terms in as much as in this situation such a person would really be required to disprove statements prejudicial to him of such witnesses who are yet to be examined, it would also reduce the right of cross-examination by such person to a mere formality for the obvious reason that by the time the witnesses who are to be cross-examined and produced, the defence of such person which would normally constitute the basis for the line and object of cross-examination would already be known to such witnesses and they are likely to refashion their statements accordingly."
When to Use — In GST adjudication when department calls for assessee's reply before completing its own inquiry/investigation, denying assessee the opportunity to cross-examine witnesses or confront adverse statements. Cite for mandatory sequence: accusation → evidence by department → then reply by assessee.
Prafulla Chandra v State of Orissa
Orissa High Court | AIR 1988 Ori 18 | Administrative Inquiry — Natural Justice
Key Ratio
"Though it was a fact finding body and did not perform any judicial function and the findings arrived at by such commission are not enforceable proprio vigore and it was to determine and regulate its own procedure of enquiry and there was no lis nor any determination of lis, it has a duty to act following the principles of natural justice... which duty had been cast upon it by the notification itself."
Further Ratio
A finding of fact based on no evidence can be quashed by the Court by issuing a writ. The adverse findings were based on "presumption which lacks a reasonable foundation from the materials on record."
When to Use — When challenging GST order passed without opportunity of hearing, or based on statements/documents not provided to assessee. Even a fact-finding officer (like a survey report) must follow natural justice. Adverse observations based on no material can be quashed by writ.
Sachin Kumar v Delhi Subordinate Service Selection Board (DSSSB)
Supreme Court of India | (2021) 4 SCC 631
Key Ratio — Scope of Inquiry — Looking Beyond the Narrow Report
"Recruitment to public services must command public confidence. Persons who are recruited are intended to fulfil public functions associated with the functioning of the Government. Where the entire process is found to be flawed, its cancellation may undoubtedly cause hardship to a few who may not specifically be found to be involved in wrongdoing. But that is not sufficient to nullify the ultimate decision to cancel an examination where the nature of wrongdoing cuts through the entire process so as to seriously impinge upon the legitimacy of the examinations which have been held for recruitment."
When to Use — When Revenue argues that since no specific act of evasion is found against this assessee in a narrowly conducted inquiry, the demand must be dropped. Counter: if the systemic fraud permeates the entire transaction chain, the government can look at the entire process — not just one committee's report.
Krishna Ballabh Sahay v Commission of Inquiry
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1969 SC 258 | (1969) 1 SCR 387
Key Ratio — "Definite Matter of Public Importance"
"If the charges were vague or speculative suggesting a fishing expedition we would have paused to consider whether such an inquiry should be allowed to proceed. A perusal of the grounds assures us that the charges are specific, and that records rather than oral testimony will be used to establish them."
Further Ratio
"It is of public importance that public men failing in their duty should be called upon to face the consequences... The cleanliness of public life in which the public should be vitally interested must be a matter of public importance."
Further Ratio — Mala Fides
The question of mala fides could be decided if it could be held that the allegations were false, and it was for the commission to find facts. "Whether they lead to the conclusion that the inquiry was justified or was malicious, cannot be said just now, when there are only allegations and recriminations but no evidence."
When to Use — When challenging GST summons or investigation on the ground that allegations are vague — cite the "fishing expedition" test. If charges are specific and document-based, inquiry is justified. Also cite the mala fides ratio when alleging that SCN is issued for extraneous reasons.
State of Karnataka v UOI
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1978 SC 68 | (1977) 4 SCC 608
Key Ratio — Commission = Fact-Finding Only
The commission is merely a fact-finding body having no power to pronounce a binding or definitive judgment. It collects facts through the evidence laid before it, and after considering the same, it submits its report which the appointing government may or may not accept. The inquiry commission is not a court — under section 4 of the Act, it is merely clothed with certain powers of a civil court but it does not have the status of a court.
When to Use — When Revenue relies on a DRI/DGGI investigation report as conclusive evidence in adjudication. Argue: investigation reports are purely fact-finding, have no judicial character, do not bind the adjudicating officer, and cannot substitute independent adjudication. Adjudicating officer must reach his own conclusion on the evidence before him.
General Principle — Coercive Powers Require Statutory Sanction
Constitutional Principle | Administrative Law | Deoman v State of Bombay, AIR 1959 Bom 284 | Capital Multi-Purpose Co-op Societies v State of MP, AIR 1967 SC 1815
Key Ratio
Law assumes the existence of rights and liberties of the individual and steps in for regulating those liberties in the interest of social living. No coercive method can be employed against any person without the authority of law. If an administrative body does not have legal power to compel the attendance of witnesses or production of documents, it can only issue letters requesting persons to appear and/or produce documents, and it is then open to the persons so requested to comply with the request or not.
When to Use — The foundational challenge to any coercive GST action — summons, survey, search, arrest — that lacks statutory authority. "No authority of law = no coercion" is the bedrock principle of administrative law applicable to all revenue actions.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter 16 — Some Typical Administrative Powers & Procedures

⚖️ Chapter 16 — Some Typical Administrative Powers & Procedures

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Caution
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
16.3.2 Contempt of Commission
16.3.3 Perjury Before Commission
16.4 Search & Seizure — Overview
16.4.1 Search — Power & Safeguards
16.4.2 "Reason to Believe" — Meaning
16.4.3 CrPC Applicability to Searches
16.5 Seizure
16.5.1 Illegally Obtained Evidence
16.6 Confiscation
16.7 Constitutional Provisions
16.7.1 Self-Incrimination
16.8 Power of Inspection
16.9 Public Auctions
16.10 Reports, Returns & Summoning
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
16.3.2 — Contempt of Commission of Inquiry
🏛️ Contempt of Commission of Inquiry
Can High Court take cognisance under Contempt of Courts Act?
HELD — NEGATIVE
High Court CANNOT take cognisance of contempt of a commission of inquiry under the Contempt of Courts Act
(Brajnandan Sinha v Jyoti Narain, AIR 1956 SC 66)
📝 A commission is NOT a "court." It cannot punish anyone for disrespecting it. If someone defies the commission, it must go to a magistrate's court — it cannot act itself.
Why Commission is NOT a Court
REASON 1
Commission is neither a court nor an adjudicatory body — it is merely a fact-finding body
REASON 2
Parliament cannot make a law to confer contempt power on commission under Entry 14, List III
REASON 3
Article 19(1): Contempt power cannot be used to restrict free speech where body is not a court
Limited Fiction Under CI Act — Sections 5(4) & 5(5)
§5(4)
Commission deemed to be a civil court for purposes of ss. 179, 180, 193, 228 IPC only
📝 A limited legal fiction — just for specific IPC sections. It does NOT make the commission a full court for all purposes.
§5(5)
Commission proceedings = judicial proceedings for certain purposes ONLY — cannot be extended beyond specified purposes
MINIMUM TEST
A "court" must be able to give a definitive judgment — commission can only instruct the government's mind; it has no adjudicatory functions
Procedure When Contempt Occurs
STEP 1
If person disobeys commission order → commission refers matter to magistrate's court (cannot deal itself)
STEP 2
Trial before magistrate under ss. 193 & 228 IPC
§10A (Added)
Law Commission recommended — making it a criminal offence to bring commission or its members into disrepute → Section 10A added to CI Act
Key Case References
Bansi Lal v State — Refused oath → complaints filed under ss. 178 & 179 IPC before Chief Metropolitan Magistrate
Indira Gandhi v J.C. Shah Commission — Delhi HC quashed contempt proceedings; flaws found in commission's procedure
Kiran Bedi & Jinder Singh — Police officers entitled to benefit of §8B of CI Act; commission order directing prosecution was not lawful
16.3.3 — Perjury Before Commission of Inquiry
📋 Perjury Before Commission — Baliram v Justice B. Lentin, AIR 1988 SC 2267
Is perjury before commission punishable on commission's own complaint u/s 195(1)(b) CrPC?
HELD — NO
Commission of Inquiry is NOT a court for purposes of Section 195 CrPC
Prosecution for perjury cannot be launched on commission's own complaint
📝 If someone lies before a commission, the commission CANNOT file a perjury complaint itself. Only the State Government can initiate prosecution for false evidence (ss. 193, 228 IPC).
FICTION — AVAILABLE
Under ss. 5(4) & 5(5): Commission = civil court for ss. 179, 180, 193, 228 IPC
FICTION — NOT AVAILABLE
Section 195 CrPC: Commission is NOT a civil court or criminal court "properly so called"
SC OBSERVATION
"A Commission of Inquiry is not a Court properly so called. A Commission is obviously appointed by the appropriate Government 'for the information of its mind'... It is therefore a fact-finding body and is not required to adjudicate upon the rights of the parties and has no adjudicatory functions."
FURTHER
"The least that is required of a Court is the capacity to deliver a 'definitive judgment', and merely because the procedure adopted by it is of a legal character and it has power to administer an oath will not impart to it the status of a Court."
EXCEPTION — SOME STATUTES
Specific statutes may declare inquiry official = deemed civil court for §195(1)(b) CrPC
E.g., Section 116, Railways Act — Commissioner of Railway Safety deemed court for §195 & Ch. XXVI CrPC during accident inquiry
16.4 — Search and Seizure — Overview & Nature
🔍 Search and Seizure — Administrative Power
M.P. Sharma v Satish, AIR 1954 SC 300: "An overriding power of the State for the protection of social security"
Three Stages in the Process
STAGE 1
SEARCH
Investigatory in nature — conducted on belief of contravention
STAGE 2
SEIZURE
Physical possession transferred to Administration — ownership NOT transferred
📝 Seizure means the officer takes physical custody. You still own the goods; they just hold them.
STAGE 3
CONFISCATION
Ownership transferred to government — requires prior notice & hearing (natural justice)
Key Principles
DRASTIC POWER
Serious invasion of privacy, property rights, reputation, business and freedom of the affected person
PREREQUISITE
Power can be exercised ONLY when conferred by statutory provision — no general law of search & seizure by administrative officers
SAFEGUARD
Conditions of statutory provision must be duly observed — otherwise search declared illegal
JUDICIAL TREND
Courts have tilted more toward Administration than toward affected individual in interpreting these powers
Major Statutes Conferring Search & Seizure Powers
StatuteSectionPower / Trigger
Customs Act, 1962§100, 105, 106, 110Search body/aircraft/vessel/premises; seize goods on "reason to believe" confiscation
FERA / FEMA§37, 43Search premises if reason to believe documents secreted
Income Tax Act, 1961§132, 133A, 133BSearch on "reason to believe" — Commissioner/Director to authorise
Imports & Exports (Control) Act, 1947§4C, 4DSearch premises; seize imported goods liable to confiscation
PMLA, 2002§17Director/authorised officer on "reason to believe" of money laundering — belief must be recorded in writing
Central Excise Act, 1944§18; Rule 201Officer empowered to enter/search where excisable goods processed/stored in contravention
⚠️ GST RELEVANCE Under GST: Section 67 CGST Act confers search & seizure powers on Joint Commissioner and above. The principles in this chapter on "reason to believe," recording of reasons, safeguards, and evidence admissibility apply mutatis mutandis to GST searches.
16.4.1 — Search: Power, Implied Power & Procedural Safeguards
🔎 Search Power — Express & Implied
Purpose: Unearth evidence of suspected violations of governing statutes
EXPRESS POWER
Statute explicitly confers power of search → must strictly follow all conditions stipulated
IMPLIED POWER
Even if statute does not say "search" — court may imply it from other provisions
📝 If a statute says officers can "inspect" business premises, courts have held this implies a right to "enter and search" — without warrant (except residential places).
💡 Madras General Sales Tax Act §41(2): Power of "inspection" of accounts implied power to "enter and search" — Supreme Court in Jhaver
Section 165 CrPC — Key Safeguards (for police searches during cognisable offence investigation)
(1) Reasonable grounds to believe something needed for investigation may be found in the place
(2) Thing cannot be obtained without undue delay otherwise
(3) Record in writing the grounds of belief — MANDATORY, not directory
(4) Specify in writing (as far as possible) things to be searched for
(5) Conduct search in person if practicable
(6) If not practicable to search personally — record reasons & authorise subordinate in writing
Section 100 CrPC — General Provision (All Searches)
Two or more independent respectable inhabitants of locality must be present during search
List of all things seized during search to be prepared & signed by witnesses
📝 This ensures no officer can "plant" any article. Independent witnesses verify what was actually found.
⚠️ JUDICIAL APPROACH — INCONSISTENT
Courts have NOT developed a uniform rule on when §165 CrPC applies to statutory searches. The approach is ad hoc — decided statute by statute. Courts have interpreted search provisions liberally and safeguards narrowly.
Key Case Divergence — §165 CrPC Applicability
CaseStatute§165 Applied?Reason
State of Rajasthan v Rehman (1960)Central Excise Act — Rule 201YES — §165 appliedSearch during investigation of offence — parallel to police search
Board of Revenue, Madras v R.S. Jhaver (1968)Madras GST Act §41(2)YES — §165 appliedNo "reason to believe" words → §165 essential; search without it held illegal
Sheonath Prasad v State of Bihar (1968)Bihar Sales Tax Act §17NO — §165 not appliedOfficer acting under §17 (not §27 investigation); strict statutory interpretation
ITO v Seth Bros (1970)Income Tax Act §132NO — §165 not appliedRules under IT Act already require recording reasons; §165 not needed
Partap Singh v Director, FERA (1985)FERA §37PARTIALLYMethodology of §165 to be "generally followed" but duty to record reasons not made applicable
💡 GST: Section 67 CGST Act requires Joint Commissioner to have "reason to believe" before authorising search. Following Rehman principle, reasons should be recorded in writing. Search without recorded reasons is vulnerable to challenge.
16.4.2 — "Reason to Believe" — Meaning, Scope & Judicial Review
📖 "Reason to Believe" — The Trigger for Search Power
Partap Singh v Director of Enforcement, FERA — AIR 1985 SC 989
IS NOT
Subjective satisfaction of the officer — "I think there is something" is not enough
📝 Officers cannot act on a "hunch" or "gut feeling." The belief must be based on actual material/information.
IS NOT
Merely a pretence — belief cannot be a sham or cover-up
IS
Belief held in good faith based on adequate material for forming a reasonable belief
IS
Reasons must have rational nexus to the formation of belief and must not be extraneous or irrelevant
Judicial Review — Scope
✓ COURT CAN EXAMINE
Whether reasons for the belief have a rational nexus to the purpose of the statutory provision
✗ COURT CANNOT SUBSTITUTE
Its own opinion for the officer's belief — judicial review is limited
PRINCIPLE
If search finds nothing incriminating — that by itself CANNOT conclude that inception of search was mala fide
SPECIAL NOTES
(1) "Reason to suspect" = "Reason to believe" — no semantic difference [judicial holding]
(2) Reasons need not be disclosed in the authorisation letter — but must be revealed if writ petition is filed
(3) Material from intelligence or informants (even oral) can ground the belief [Partap Singh]
(4) SC in Narayan v ITO: Power under §132 IT Act is drastic — exercise only after serious application of mind to information
CONDITION PRECEDENT
P. Ramachandra v Secretary, Ministry of Food: "Reason to believe" is a condition precedent. Absence vitiates entry and search. Subsequent discovery of material cannot be equated to initial reasonable belief.
💡 GST §67: If officer conducts search without recording reasons or on irrelevant grounds — entire search can be challenged. Subsequent finding of discrepancy does not cure the initial absence of belief.
16.4.3 — CrPC Provisions: "So Far as May Be" — Applicability to Statutory Searches
📜 Do CrPC Safeguards Apply to Administrative Searches?
Depends entirely on the specific statute
IF STATUTE PROVIDES
"Provisions of CrPC relating to searches shall apply, so far as may be" → CrPC safeguards apply to that search
IF STATUTE SILENT
CrPC provisions do NOT automatically apply — individual gets no CrPC protection in that search
📝 The CrPC safeguards do not follow you automatically. Your protection depends on whether the specific law (GST, Customs, IT) incorporates CrPC provisions.
EXPRESSION "SO FAR AS MAY BE"
Has always been construed to mean those provisions may be generally followed to the extent possible — not a verbatim incorporation
RECORDING OF REASONS — KEY SAFEGUARD
The requirement of "recording of reasons" is a safeguard of some substance against arbitrary exercise of power of search, but its efficacy is largely diluted by courts being easily satisfied by general statements made by the authority without being specific as to the information in possession.
DISTINCTION
(i) Recording of reasons for authorising search
(ii) Mentioning of those reasons in the authorisation letter / search warrant

Courts hold: Officer is not obligated to disclose reasons in the authorisation letter itself. Reasons need only be disclosed when writ petition is filed.
LORD DENNING'S CAUTION
"Once great power is granted, there is a danger of it being abused. Rather than risk such abuse, it is the duty of the courts so to construe the statute as to see that it encroaches as little as possible upon the liberties of the people."
16.5 — Seizure: Nature, Prerequisites & Key Provisions
📦 Seizure — Legal Foundation & Safeguards
"It is axiomatic that there can be no seizure of documents or any other property without legal authority"
FUNDAMENTAL RULE
No seizure without legal authority — legal power to seize gold does not warrant seizure of documents
Seizure — Key Principles from Case Law
"Reason to believe" must relate to the time of seizure — any subsequent acquisition of belief is of no avail
[Hindustan Aluminium v Controller of Aluminium]
Exercise of statutory power interfering with property rights requires strict compliance with pre-conditions
[Hindustan Aluminium]
"Reason to believe" cannot exist unless there is application of mind as to whether particular state of affairs exists — not merely a pretence
[Barium Chemicals v A.J. Rana]
Seizure Under Specific Statutes — Notable Provisions
Statute / SectionKey Rule
Customs Act §110(1)"Reasonable cause to believe" goods liable to confiscation → seizure permitted. Satisfaction not absolutely subjective — reasons must be relevant, not extraneous.
Customs Act §110(2)Goods seized must be returned within 6 months unless notice given u/s 124. Collector may extend by 6 more months — but extension is quasi-judicial requiring notice & hearing [Chuharmal v UOI]
IT Act §132(8)Documents seized shall NOT be retained beyond 180 days unless reasons recorded in writing AND Commissioner's approval obtained. After 180 days: communication of recorded reasons to assessee is obligatory [CIT, West Bengal v Oriental Rubber Works]
IT Act §132(10)Person entitled to books of account may file objections with Board of Revenue against Commissioner's approval for retention beyond 180 days
PMLA §17Belief must be recorded in writing; after search/seizure or open issuance of freezing order, authorised officer must forward copy of reasons to Adjudicating Authority in sealed envelope within 30 days [Opto Circuit India v Axis Bank]
Gold Control Act §66Section 66 does NOT permit indiscriminate seizure "to fish out material to form a belief and justify it by reasons culled therefrom" [Collector, Central Excise v LKN Jewellers]
💡 GST §67(7): Documents seized during GST search cannot be retained beyond 30 days without approval. Principles of §132(8) IT Act (communication to assessee, recording reasons) apply by analogy.
Seizure — What Can Be Seized (IT Act §132)
Books of account and other documents
Place marks of identification on documents
Make extracts/copies from documents
Inventory of articles/things found
Examine on oath any person in possession of books/assets
KEY SC RULING — Seth Bros
IT Act and rules do NOT require warrant of authorisation to specify particulars of documents — general authorisation to search for relevant/useful materials is sufficient. Officer's judgment at search to decide what is relevant.
16.5.1 — Evidence Not Obtained Legally — Admissibility
⚖️ Can Evidence from an Illegal Search be Used?
Indian Law vs USA / UK Approach
INDIA — DOCUMENTS
Jhaver: Illegal search → documents must be returned to assessee
BUT
Pooran Mal v Director of Inspection (AIR 1974 SC 348):
Even if search illegal — information gathered from seized documents CAN be used as evidence. No constitutional or statutory bar.
📝 In India, an illegal search is not a free pass. You get the documents back, but the department can use what they learned from those documents against you.
USA — EXCLUSIONARY RULE
Evidence obtained illegally must be excluded in agency hearing (due process clause / Fourth Amendment)
RELAXED IN
INS v Lopez-Mendoza: Exclusionary rule does NOT apply in civil deportation proceedings — social cost-benefit analysis applied
UK (LORD GODDARD)
"The test to be applied... is whether it is relevant to the matters in issue. If it is, it is admissible and the Court is not concerned with how it was obtained." [Kuruma]
SETTLED PRINCIPLE — INDIA
"Illegality of the search does not vitiate the evidence collected during such illegal search."
The only requirement: court/authority must be cautious and circumspect in dealing with such evidence.
[Pushpa Devi M Jatia v M.L. Wadhavan: "If evidence is relevant, the court is not concerned with the method by which it was obtained."]
ALSO
Assuming witnesses at search were NOT respectable inhabitants of locality (§100 CrPC non-compliance) — this does NOT invalidate the search. It only affects the weight of evidence supporting search/recovery.
💡 GST: Even if a search under §67 CGST Act is procedurally defective, statements/admissions/documents seized may still be used against assessee. Officers must still be challenged on admission of such evidence — court must be cautious.
16.6 — Confiscation: Natural Justice & Safeguards
📋 Confiscation — Transfer of Ownership to Government
Requires natural justice; distinct from seizure
SEIZURE
Administrative act — no hearing needed before seizure
CONFISCATION
Cannot be ordered WITHOUT giving affected person a reasonable opportunity of being heard
📝 This is a key difference. Seizure can happen without a hearing. But confiscation — permanently taking away your property — requires you be given a fair chance to oppose it.
Three Standard Conditions Before Confiscation (Section 124, Customs Act — Standard for All Statutes)
(1) Written notice stating the grounds on which confiscation is proposed
(2) Opportunity to make written representation against grounds
(3) Reasonable opportunity of being heard (oral hearing)
PRINCIPLE
Even if statute is SILENT on hearing, courts will imply natural justice norms when confiscation order is to be passed. The three conditions in §124 are standard safeguards imposed by virtually every statute authorising confiscation.
💡 GST §130: Confiscation of goods/conveyances — followed by §122 penalty proceeding. Natural justice (notice, opportunity to be heard) is mandatory before any confiscation order. An order without hearing is void.
QUASI-JUDICIAL NATURE
Gunwantlal Godawat v UOI (2018) 12 SCC 309: Adjudgment of liability of seized goods and exercise of discretion to determine redemption fine in lieu of confiscation is a quasi-judicial function — cannot be arbitrary even if rules give unfettered discretion.
16.7 — Relevant Constitutional Provisions — Search & Seizure
🏛️ Constitutional Protection Against Unreasonable Search & Seizure
Articles 19(1)(g) and 21 — Principal Provisions
ART. 19(1)(g)
Right to practise profession/occupation/trade/business — subject to reasonable restrictions by law
Search of business premises + seizure of account books affects this right
ART. 21
No deprivation of personal liberty except by procedure established by law
After Maneka Gandhi: "Procedure" = due and fair procedure
Search of residence disturbs personal liberty
DELETED
Art. 19(1)(f) — Right to acquire/hold/dispose of property — Deleted by 44th Constitutional Amendment, 1978
KEY LIMITS OF CONSTITUTIONAL CHALLENGE
Courts have found search & seizure provisions constitutionally valid in view of procedural safeguards provided in statutes. However: courts have not spelled out from constitutional provisions any minimum procedural safeguards to be followed uniformly.
WHY PRE-SEARCH HEARING IS IMPOSSIBLE
Swiftness and surprise are necessary elements of search — if assessee is given notice of impending search, he will dispose of relevant materials and frustrate the purpose. Hence, no right to pre-search representation can be conceded.
MINIMUM SAFEGUARD COURTS INSIST ON
Some reasons for ordering search must be recorded (though not necessarily disclosed to the party) — provides important safeguard because court can examine them when action is challenged.
16.7.1 — Self-Incrimination: Article 20(3) and Administrative Inquiries
🛡️ Privilege Against Self-Incrimination
Article 20(3): "No person accused of any offence shall be compelled to be a witness against himself"
ARTICLE 20(3) — SCOPE
Available only to person formally accused of an offence at the time of compulsion. The word "accused" has been construed strictly by Supreme Court → makes protection practically inapplicable to administrative proceedings.
📝 If you are being investigated but no formal charge is filed against you yet, you cannot claim the right to silence under Art. 20(3). This is a major limitation in tax/customs investigations.
Raja Narayanlal Bansilal v Mistry
Company inspector examining director on oath — no formal accusation yet → Art. 20(3) NOT applicable. Report to government ≠ accusation.
Ramesh Chandra Mehta v State of West Bengal
Customs officer investigation for smuggling — no formal accusation → Art. 20(3) NOT applicable. Confessional statement admissible in subsequent trial.
Poolpandi v Supdt., Central Excise
Persons questioned during Customs/FERA investigation are NOT entitled to presence of lawyer. Guarantee against self-incrimination only for person already accused.
Art. 20(3) and Documents / Search & Seizure
PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS
State of Bombay v Kathi Kalu Oghad: "To be a witness" = giving oral testimony. Production of pre-existing documents ≠ compelled testimony (unless document conveys personal knowledge relating to the charge)
SEARCH WARRANT
Sharma v Satish: Search & seizure of documents under search warrant is NOT "compelled production" within Art. 20(3). Seizure under warrant does not violate Art. 20(3) even if documents might incriminate.
BEST APPROACH — SECTION 6, CI ACT
Section 6 of the Commissions of Inquiry Act: Person gives evidence on stipulation that it will NOT be used against him in any subsequent proceedings (except prosecution for giving false evidence). This balances state interest with individual protection and discourages administration from browbeating the individual.
💡 GST §70: Summons to person to give evidence or produce documents. Art. 20(3) protection is narrow — statements given under summons before GST officer may be used in proceedings. Assessee should be cautious about making any admission in response to summons.
16.8 — Power of Inspection
🔬 Power of Inspection — Less Drastic than Search
Purpose: Ensure continual compliance with law
INSPECTION
Enter premises, examine books/records — but officer cannot take away materials with him
Only extracts & copies; place marks of identification
SEARCH
Enter, examine AND seize — officer can take documents/goods away
KEY RULE
Use of word "inspection" in statute does NOT signify power of search unless context indicates otherwise [Jhaver]
Statutes Conferring Inspection Power
StatuteSectionScope of Inspection
Income Tax Act, 1961§133-AEnter business premises during business hours; inspect books, documents; take extracts/copies; place marks. Cannot take materials away.
FERA§43Authorised officer can inspect books/accounts of authorised dealers. Dealer must produce documents.
Imports & Exports (Control) Act, 1947§4BEnter premises at reasonable time; inspect imported goods/account books; take notes or extracts
Factories Act, 1948§2Inspectors enter any factory premises to find violations. May result in prosecution.
IMPLIED POWER TO SEIZE DURING INSPECTION
Mangat Rai v State of MP: Tax official during shop inspection — assessee's son tried to run away with documents. SC ruled official was justified in seizing those documents — otherwise assessee could snatch away material leaving official helpless.
💡 GST §71: Authorised officer may have access to business premises, accounts etc. during business hours. If assessee tries to hide or remove books during such visit — officer can be justified in retaining them. Cannot be obstructed.
16.9 — Public Auctions
🏷️ Public Auctions — Administrative Decision Must Match Stated Reasons
State of Punjab v Bandeep Singh, (2016) 1 SCC 724
Facts — Bandeep Singh
Properties of Punjab State Leather Development Corporation put to public auction → Highest bid rejected on ground of "marginal excess over reserve price" → Government later claimed "cartelization/pooling" as real reason
HELD
Administrative decision must be judged by reasons stated in the decision/order itself. Subsequent explanations or reasons cannot be accepted to sustain the decision/order.
📝 You cannot give one reason at the time and a different, better reason later. The decision stands or falls by what you said when you made it.
SC RATIO — Mohinder Singh Gill v Chief Election Commissioner
"Every decision of an administrative or executive nature must be a composite and self-sustaining one, in that it should contain all the reasons which prevailed on the official taking the decision to arrive at his conclusion. It is beyond cavil that any authority cannot be permitted to travel beyond the stand adopted and expressed by it in the impugned action."
ALSO — NO VESTED RIGHT IN HIGHEST BIDDER
Highest bidder has no vested right to have auction concluded in his favour. Government retains power to accept or reject the highest bid in public revenue. [HUDA v Orchid Infrastructure Developers (P) Ltd.]
16.10 — Calling for Reports, Returns, Information & Summoning of Persons
📂 Regulatory Powers — Reports, Returns, Information & Summons
Continuous compliance mechanism under various statutes
Power to Call for Information & Returns
Purpose: Monitor operation of regulations; enable adjustments based on actual working
IT Act §139
Annual return of income — starting point of assessment process
IT Act §133A
Income tax authority can require certain information from assessees
IT Act §133B
Enter business premises during office hours to collect relevant information
Companies Act §206
Registrar can call for information/explanation; can order production of books if explanation inadequate
16.10.1 — Summoning of Persons — Power & Enforcement
PURPOSE
Without coercive summons power, inquiry body can only request — ineffective if persons choose not to cooperate
FORMULA
Designated authority has powers of a Civil Court under CPC for: enforcing attendance, examining on oath, requiring discovery & production of documents
LIMITATION
Administrative authority having powers of civil court — it is doubtful whether it can itself impose CPC sanctions (arrest, attachment, civil prison). It may need to approach civil court.
Key Statutory Summons Powers
StatuteSectionPower
Customs Act§107, §108Gazetted officer of Customs can summon any person for evidence/documents in smuggling inquiry
FERA§39, §40Authorised Enforcement Officer to require production of documents; gazetted officer to summon for evidence/documents during investigation
CI Act, 1952§5(2), §5(4)Person required to furnish information — legally bound u/ss. 176, 177 IPC. Commission deemed civil court for ss. 175, 178, 179, 180, 228 IPC offences — must refer to magistrate.
Income Tax Act§131Powers of civil court — ITO can impose fine up to Rs. 500 for failure to produce documents when summoned
NEXUS REQUIREMENT — BARIUM CHEMICALS
Barium Chemicals Ltd. v A.J. Rana (AIR 1972 SC 591): Documents required must have some nexus with the purpose of the Act. Where such nexus is missing and document has no relevance for the purpose of the Act, the condition precedent for making an order is non-existent — order will be quashed.
💡 GST §70 Summons: If officer calls for documents having no connection to GST proceedings pending/contemplated — assessee can challenge the summons on "nexus" ground. Call for documents must relate to specific proceeding.
GOVERNMENT PRIVILEGE
Government may claim privilege under §123 of Indian Evidence Act for unpublished official records. However, this privilege CANNOT be claimed before a Commission of Inquiry as the Evidence Act does not apply to commissions. Commission has power to require production of relevant documents. [State of J&K v Anwar Ahmed Aftab]
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 16 | Administrative Powers & Procedures
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, Appellate Authorities and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
M.P. Sharma v Satish Chandra
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1954 SC 300
Key Ratio
"A power of search and seizure is in any system of jurisprudence an overriding power of the State for the protection of social security."
Nature of Power
The power of search and seizure is a drastic power and constitutes a serious invasion of the affected person's privacy, property rights, reputation, business and freedom. Therefore, the power can be exercised by an administrative authority only when it is conferred on it by a statutory provision.
