When to Use — When challenging a GST search u/s 67 CGST Act where no proper statutory authority existed or preconditions were not met. Establish that search power is a drastic exception, not a routine tool.
Partap Singh v Director of Enforcement, FERA
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1985 SC 989 | (1985) 3 SCC 72
Key Ratio — "Reason to Believe"
The expression "reason to believe" is not synonymous with subjective satisfaction of the officer concerned. The belief must be held in good faith; it cannot be merely a pretence. There should be material adequate for forming the reasonable belief to issue search warrant. It is open to the court to examine the question whether the reasons for the belief have a rational nexus or connection or a relevant bearing to the formation of the belief and are not extraneous or irrelevant to the purpose of the statutory provision in question.
When to Use — When challenging GST search where officer acted on vague "information" or "suspicion" without adequate material. Establish that "reason to believe" is not subjective satisfaction — court can examine the nexus of reasons to the belief.
P. Ramachandra v Secretary, Ministry of Food
Andhra Pradesh High Court | AIR 1979 AP 28
Key Ratio — Condition Precedent to Search
"Even at the time of entering or searching the premises, there must be reasonable belief, that any contravention has been or is likely to be committed." That "reason to believe" is a condition precedent to vesting any jurisdiction in any officer to enter any premises or make a search. The subsequent discovery of any material cannot be equated to the initial reasonable belief.
When to Use — When department argues that subsequent discovery of discrepancy during search validates the search. Counter: absence of prior reasonable belief voids the entire proceeding — subsequent discovery does not cure initial illegality.
State of Rajasthan v Rehman
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1960 SC 210 | (1960) 1 SCR 991
Key Ratio — Recording of Reasons Mandatory
The "recording of reasons is an important step in the matter of search and to ignore it is to ignore the material part of the provisions governing searches." A search conducted without recording the reasons therefor (as envisaged by §165 CrPC) was held to be illegal.
When to Use — When GST search was conducted without the authorising officer recording reasons in writing. Establish that recording of reasons is not a formality — its omission renders the search illegal.
ITO v Seth Bros.
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1970 SC 292 | (1969) 2 SCC 324
Key Ratio — Search Not Arbitrary, But Conditions Must Be Met
Section 132 does not confer any arbitrary power upon the income tax officers. The commissioner must have, in consequence of information, reason to believe that the statutory conditions for the exercise of the power to order search exist. He must record the reasons for his belief and then issue an authorization in favour of a designated officer to search the premises.
On Bona Fide Exercise
"Since by the exercise of the power a serious invasion is made upon the rights, privacy and freedom of the taxpayer, the power must be exercised strictly in accordance with the law and only for the purposes for which the law authorizes it to be exercised. If the action of the officer is maliciously taken or power under the section is exercised for a collateral purpose it is liable to be struck down by the Court."
When to Use — When establishing that a GST search was conducted for collateral purpose (harassment, pressure for settlement) or without genuine recorded belief. Power exercised for collateral purpose must be struck down.
Collector, Central Excise v LKN Jewellers
Allahabad High Court | AIR 1972 All 231
Key Ratio — No "Fishing Expedition" Seizures
The provision does not permit an indiscriminate seizure with a view to fishing out material to form a belief and justify it by reasons culled therefrom. The expression "reason to believe" is usually held to mean a belief held in good faith and not a mere pretence. Section 66 does not permit "a belief which may subsequently be found to be reasonable."
When to Use — When department conducts a broad, sweeping search/seizure without specific reason and justifies it post-hoc by what was found. Establish that pre-existing reasonable belief is mandatory — post-facto justification is not acceptable.
CIT, West Bengal v Oriental Rubber Works
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1984 SC 230 | (1984) 1 SCC 700
Key Ratio — Communication of Reasons for Retention Mandatory
Under §132(8) of the Income-tax Act, it is obligatory on the revenue to communicate to the person concerned the recorded reasons and the commissioner's approval for retaining the books beyond 180 days, and that such communication must be made as expeditiously as possible after the passing of the approval by the commissioner. Otherwise any further retention of the seized books or documents would become invalid and unlawful.
When to Use — When GST-seized documents are retained beyond the prescribed period (30 days u/s 67(7) CGST) without proper approval or communication. Demand immediate return — retention without communication is unlawful.
Baliram v Justice B. Lentin
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1988 SC 2267 | (1988) 4 SCC 419
Key Ratio — Commission of Inquiry is NOT a Court
"A Commission of Inquiry is not a Court properly so called. A Commission is obviously appointed by the appropriate Government 'for the information of its mind' in order for it to decide as to the course of action to be followed. It is therefore a fact-finding body and is not required to adjudicate upon the rights of the parties and has no adjudicatory functions. The mere fact that the Government is not bound to accept its recommendations or act upon its findings... and merely because the procedure adopted by it is of a legal character and it has the power to administer an oath will not impart to it the status of a Court."
When to Use — When dealing with any inquiry body (GST adjudication officer) exercising hybrid functions — establish the minimum requirement of a "court" is capacity to give a definitive judgment. Relevant also for Art. 20(3) arguments.
Barium Chemicals Ltd. v A.J. Rana
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1972 SC 591 | (1972) 1 SCC 240
Key Ratio — Nexus Required for Document Production Orders
"There has to be some nexus between the documents sought to be obtained and the purpose of the Act." Where such nexus is missing, and the document has no relevance for the purpose of the Act, the condition precedent to the making of an order under section 19(2) would be non-existent.
On "Reason to Believe" / "Considers it Necessary"
"The words 'considers it necessary' postulate that the authority concerned has thought over the matter deliberately with care and it has been found necessary as a result of such thinking to pass the order... 'Reason to believe' cannot be said to exist unless there is application of mind as to whether a particular state of affairs exists or does not exist at a particular time."
When to Use — When challenging GST summons u/s 70 or notices calling for documents unconnected to any pending proceedings. Establish nexus requirement; expose absence of application of mind to the specific state of affairs.
Mohinder Singh Gill v Chief Election Commissioner
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1978 SC 851 | (1978) 1 SCC 405
Key Ratio — Administrative Decision Must Stand on Its Own Reasons
"Every decision of an administrative or executive nature must be a composite and self-sustaining one, in that it should contain all the reasons which prevailed on the official taking the decision to arrive at his conclusion. It is beyond cavil that any authority cannot be permitted to travel beyond the stand adopted and expressed by it in the impugned action."
When to Use — When GST SCN, adjudication order or rejection order gives one reason but department tries to defend it before Appellate Authority on a different ground. Cite to establish that department cannot travel beyond reasons stated in its own order.
Chuharmal v UOI
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1988 SC 1474 | (1988) 3 SCC 697
Key Ratio — Extension of Seizure Period is Quasi-Judicial
"This Court reiterated that the right to restoration of the seized goods is a civil right which accrues on the expiry of the initial six months... an ex parte determination by the collector would expose his decision to be one-sided and perhaps one based on an incorrect statement of facts. How then can it be said that his determination that a sufficient cause exists is just and fair if he has done it before by one-sided picture without any means to check it unless there is an opportunity to the other side to correct or controvert it?"
When to Use — When department extends period of seizure of goods (Customs) or documents (GST) without giving assessee opportunity to be heard. Extension without notice violates natural justice — assessee has a civil right to return of property.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter 17 — Discretionary Powers (I)

⚖️ Chapter 17 — Discretionary Powers (I)

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Danger
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative Route
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
17.1 Discretion — Meaning & Core Definitions
17.2 Nature of Discretionary Powers
17.2.1 Ministerial Functions
17.3 Reasons for Growth of Discretionary Powers
17.4 Need for Safeguards
17.4.1 Network of Restrictive Principles
17.4.2 Rationality and Proportionality
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
17.1 — Discretion: Meaning & Core Definitions
⚖️ DISCRETION — Core Concept
Chapter XVII | Principles of Administrative Law
📝 Discretion means the power to make a choice — but in law, that choice must follow reason, law, and justice. It is never a free ticket to do what you like. The courts have always said: discretion must be guided by rule, not by personal whim.
📖 Classic Judicial Definitions of "Discretion"
ROOKE'S CASE (1598)
Coke J: "Discretion is a science or understanding to discern between falsity and truth, between wrong and right, between shadows and substances, between equity and colourable glosses — and NOT to do according to their wills and private affections."
R. v WILKES (1770) — LORD MANSFIELD
"Discretion, when applied to a Court of Justice, means sound discretion guided by law. It must be governed by rule, not by humour; it must not be arbitrary, vague, and fanciful, but legal and regular."
LORD WREDNBURY
"A discretion does not empower a man to do what he likes merely because he is minded to do so; he must do not what he likes but what he OUGHT."
CORPUS JURIS SECUNDUM (as cited in Aero Traders v Ravinder Kumar Suri)
Discretion implies absence of a hard-and-fast rule; requires actual exercise of judgment, consideration of facts, and knowledge of facts upon which discretion may properly operate.
SHARPE v WAKEFIELD — LORD HALSBURY L.C.
Discretion = discernment of what is right and proper; knowledge and prudence; judgment directed by circumspection. "When it is said something is to be done within the discretion of the authorities — that something is to be done according to the rules of reason and justice, not according to private opinion; according to law and not humour."
As referred in National Insurance Co. Ltd. v Keshav Bahadur & UOI v Kuldeep Singh.
🔑 What Discretion IS and IS NOT
✓ DISCRETION IS
• Sound judgment guided by law
• Exercise of reason to ascertain what ought to be done
• Choice within the limits allowed by positive rules of law
• Coupled always with a duty
• Governed by rule, legal and regular
✗ DISCRETION IS NOT
• Freedom to do what one likes
• Arbitrary, vague, or fanciful
• Governed by humour or private affection
• Absolute or unfettered
• A discriminatory power (not necessarily)
• A tool to circumvent legal obligations
📝 Abuse of discretionary power is NOT to be easily assumed — courts require clear evidence of arbitrariness or mala fides before intervening.
⚠️ PERMISSIVE vs OBLIGATORY LANGUAGE
Hallmark of discretionary power = permissive words: "may" / "it shall be lawful"
vs. obligatory power = "shall"
BUT: this distinction is NOT always a sure guide — courts have construed "may" as obligatory in several decisions.
📝 Just because a law says "may" doesn't always mean the authority has a free choice. Courts look at the purpose and context. Example: If a statute says a collector "may" refer a matter to court when conditions are met — that "may" has been read as a duty (Lal Deen v State of Rajasthan).
💡 GST context: When a GST officer "may" conduct an inquiry u/s 70 CGST Act — an adjudicating authority cannot treat non-exercise of this power as absolute. Natural justice principles still apply to the manner of exercise even where "may" gives a choice.
17.2 — Nature of Discretionary Powers
🏛️ Nature of Discretionary Powers
Modern Administrative Governance
📝 Modern government cannot function without giving officials the power to decide things case by case. This is inevitable — but dangerous if unchecked. The larger the discretion, the greater the risk of abuse.
📌 Key Characteristics of Discretionary Powers in Modern Administration
SCALE
Literally tens of thousands of discretionary powers found in statutes and delegated legislation
CONFERRAL
Vested in: Government, Minister, Official, or Instrumentality constituted to discharge state functions
TRIGGER WORDS
"necessary", "reasonable", "if it is satisfied", "if it is of the opinion", "adequate", "fair", "expedient", "proper"
📝 When a law uses these words — it is conferring discretion. The official must still exercise real judgment, not rubber-stamp an outcome.
ESSENCE
Essence of discretion = CHOICE
Right to choose between more than one possible course of action where reasonable persons may hold differing opinions (Davis)
BLAND COMMITTEE (AUSTRALIA) — TYPES OF DISCRETION IN LAW
Powers to: admit/accept/refuse claims · grant less than maximum benefit · determine degrees of disablement · select beneficiaries · seize and forfeit goods · exempt from statutory obligations · remit & make rebates · advance or prejudice a career · impinge deeply on property rights — sometimes with no redress for persons affected.
⚖️ Advantages vs Disadvantages of Discretion
✓ ADVANTAGES
• Flexibility — individual case merits considered
• Concerned with spirit not letter of law
• Allows effective implementation of government policy
• Useful in new/novel areas of government activity
• Enables dealing with unforeseen circumstances
✗ DISADVANTAGES
• Citizens at mercy of administrators
• Opens way for inconsistent decisions
• Demands higher care/attention from administrators
• Expensive on time and money
• May be used to off-load politically contentious choices
• Judicial control may become very difficult
📝 Justice Douglas (US SC): "Absolute discretion is more destructive of freedom than any of man's other inventions." And: "Absolute discretion, like corruption, marks the beginning of the end of liberty."
DAVIS — GREATEST DANGER
"I think the greatest and most frequent injustice occurs at the discretion end of the scale, where rules and principles provide little or no guidance, where emotions of deciding officers may affect what they do, where political or other favouritism may influence decisions, and where the imperfections of human nature are often reflected in the choices made."
17.2.1 — Ministerial Functions (Contrasted with Discretionary)
🔧 Ministerial Functions vs Discretionary Functions
The Contrast | Section 17.2.1
MINISTERIAL POWER
Law prescribes function in definite and specific terms
No choice left; nothing to discretion or judgment
No investigation into disputed facts
EXAMPLE
Issue of radio/TV licence — when form correctly filled and fee tendered, licence is issued automatically by post office. No discretion exercised.
📝 A robot following a checklist. If conditions A, B, C are met — outcome X must follow. No judgment, no choice, no discretion.
💡 GST registration: When a taxpayer meets all conditions of Section 22 CGST Act, the officer has no discretion to refuse — it is ministerial. Refusal would be challengeable as exceeding jurisdiction.
DISCRETIONARY POWER
Range of options available to authority
Personal judgment exercised in making choice
Choice to act, not act, how to act, when to act
DANGER
Broader the discretion, greater the chance of abuse. Dominant modern trend.
SHARIF AHMAD v R.T.A. MEERUT (SC)
A minor discretionary element does not make a function non-ministerial. The Court stated: "The minor discretionary element given to it (R.T.A) for finding out whether the terms of the Appellate Order had been complied with or not is not enough to deter the Courts from characterising the function as ministerial."
📝 Even if there is a tiny element of judgment involved, the function can still be classified as ministerial — if the essential action is prescribed by law and the judgment is only incidental to verifying compliance.
KAVITA v STATE OF MAHARASHTRA (SC)
Task of referring a detention case to advisory board under COFEPOSA = mechanical/ministerial act involving no exercise of discretion — though the government has full liberty to revoke the order at that stage.
17.3 — Reasons for Growth of Discretionary Powers
📈 Why Have Discretionary Powers Grown So Much?
Modern Welfare State Context
📝 Discretionary powers have exploded in modern law because governments now regulate nearly every aspect of life. Rigid rules cannot cover all situations, so officials must be given judgment. But this comes with serious risks.
🌱 Root Causes — Why Discretion is Inevitable
REASON 1
Welfare State Philosophy
Tremendous state regulation over human affairs — health, transport, electricity, planning, coal mining, urban development all brought under state control
REASON 2
Complexity of Problems
Problems are of varying nature; difficult to comprehend all within scope of general rules. Many arise fresh — no prior experience to adopt general rules
REASON 3
Case-to-Case Individualization
Circumstances differ case to case — applying one rule mechanically to all cases may itself result in injustice. Need for individualization of power exercise
REASON 4
Technical Expertise
Need for technical expertise in regulating particular activities; expertise develops on case-to-case basis
REASON 5
Power to Do Nothing
Davis: "All along the line an enormous discretionary power is the power to do nothing… the omnipresent power"
INDIA SPECIFIC
Being a democratic country with regulated (not free) economy and centralised planning, India has witnessed much faster and more pervasive growth of discretionary powers. A demonstrable contemporary trend is that large volumes of case law arise around discretionary powers — indicative of widespread use of the technique of conferring discretionary powers on Administration.
17.4 — Need for Safeguards over Discretionary Powers
🛡️ Safeguards over Discretion — Multi-Pronged Strategy
Controlling the Administration
📝 Because discretion can be abused, law has developed several layers of protection. The goal is a "government of laws, not of men." Courts, tribunals, delegated legislation, and internal departmental checks all form parts of this safety net.
THE PROBLEM
Legislature usually confers discretion in too general terms without specifying any policy to be followed. Legislation is often sketchy/skeletal — leaving gaps. Administration does not use delegated legislation to fill in gaps or lay down norms — there is reluctance to control and define its own powers.
🔐 Four-Pronged Safeguard Strategy
① LAW ITSELF
The law conferring discretion should lay down standards, circumstances and criteria for its exercise — confine and structure the discretion
📝 Best safeguard — Parliament specifies the rules in the statute itself. Makes exercise predictable. Fundamental Rights (Arts. 14 and 19) used in India to compel this.
② DELEGATED LEGISLATION
If legislature fails, Administration ought to use its rule-making power to lay down norms — channelise broad statutory discretion into a narrow stream
📝 The administration makes its own rules about how it will exercise discretion — creating predictability and uniformity across similar cases.
💡 GST: CBIC circulars and instructions guiding officers on how to exercise discretion in scrutiny (Section 61) and assessment (Section 63) proceedings — these are delegated legislation safeguards.
③ HIGHER AUTHORITY DIRECTIONS
In some statutes, power to issue general directions setting standards to guide discretion is vested in a superior authority (e.g., Board of Film Censors)
④ SELF-ISSUED GUIDELINES
Authority having discretion may itself issue guidelines informing persons how it proposes to exercise its discretion
📝 Lowest safeguard — the officer sets his own rules. Has limitations (see "Fettering Discretion").
🔍 Supervision Mechanisms — Beyond Standard & Guidelines
Judicial Control
Top level — Courts lay down norms to protect individuals when legislature leaves discretion wide open. Ultra vires doctrine — officials must not exceed power given by law.
Tribunals
Unlike courts, tribunals may go into the merits of a discretionary decision — more effective control mechanism. AAT in Australia — reviews decision of officials on merits including policies.
Ombudsman
Institution to strengthen supervision over discretionary decisions — discussed separately.
Intra-Departmental Supervision
Internal hierarchical appeals within department. Superior officers supervise junior officers. Reduces cases going to tribunals/courts.
Legislative Supervision
Parliament can supervise Administration. On the whole — mechanism is very weak and ineffective.
K.S. PUTTASWAMY (AADHAR-5) v UOI — (2019) 1 SCC 1
Supreme Court on abridgement of discretion through law — Proportionality Test:
(i) Existence of a just, fair and reasonable law
(ii) Abridgement must serve a legitimate State aim
(iii) Abridgement must be proportionate to the need for such interference
(iv) Law must provide procedural guarantees against abuse of such interference
📝 Even when the government restricts a right through a discretionary power, that restriction must pass a four-part test. If any one of these four limbs is missing — the exercise of discretion can be struck down by courts.
GUJARAT MAZDOOR SABHA v STATE OF GUJARAT — (2020) 10 SCC 459
Administrative action through subordinate legislation (extending working hours under Factories Act during COVID-19) must be: (i) pursuant to legitimate State aim; (ii) rational connection between measures and object; (iii) measures must not infringe rights beyond necessity; (iv) restrictions must be necessary to protect legitimate purposes; (v) sufficient safeguards against abuse must be provided.
💡 GST parallel: When GST officers are given discretion to conduct special audits (Section 66 CGST), or to extend investigations (Section 67), the exercise must be proportionate. Blanket issuance of notices without material basis can be challenged as disproportionate exercise of discretion.
17.4.1 — Network of Restrictive Principles
🕸️ Network of Restrictive Principles
Courts "Weaving" Controls Over Discretion
📝 Courts have woven a network of restrictive principles — a web of rules that every authority must follow when exercising discretion. These principles bind even where the statute appears to give wide unfettered power.
CORE RULE
When a statute vests discretion in an authority to exercise a statutory power, such authority CANNOT exercise the same in an unfettered manner — otherwise courts are constrained to intervene.
🌐 Restrictive Principles Woven by Courts
PRINCIPLE 1
Implicit requirement that discretion shall be exercised in a reasonable and rational manner free from whims, vagaries and arbitrariness
UOI v Jesus Sales Corporation, AIR 1996 SC 1509
PRINCIPLE 2
Discretion must be exercised within the limitations of the statute. Wider the power — greater the circumspection required
Vijayabai v Shreram Tukaram, AIR 1999 SC 431
PRINCIPLE 3
Discretion may be qualified by express and implied legal duties — both substantive and procedural requirements before a decision is taken
PRINCIPLE 4
Discretion is always coupled with a duty. It cannot be used to circumvent obligations cast under law or contract governing the parties
PRINCIPLE 5
Discretion must be truly exercised (not abdicated) and exercised reasonably — two-fold consequences of statutory construction (Maxwell)
VIJAYABAI v SHRERAM TUKARAM — AIR 1999 SC 431 | (1999) 1 SCC 693
Tahsildar exercising suo motu power u/s 49-B, Bombay Tenancy & Agricultural Lands (Vidarbha Region) Act, 1958 — must initiate on the basis of materials before him and not arbitrarily. The suo motu power "is not an omni power to be exercised on the likes and dislikes of the authority." Wider the power, the greater circumspection has to be exercised.
💡 GST: Where GST officer exercises suo motu power of reopening, revision, or special audit — must be based on material on record. A vague satisfaction or rubber-stamping is challengeable. Higher the power (Commissioner level), stricter the scrutiny of its exercise.
17.4.2 — Rationality and Proportionality
⚖️ Rationality & Proportionality — Judicial Review Tests
The Wednesbury Standard and Beyond
📝 Courts can invalidate discretionary decisions if they are unreasonable or disproportionate. Two key tests have evolved: (1) The Wednesbury test — was the decision so unreasonable no reasonable body could have reached it? (2) The Proportionality test — was the action proportionate to the aim? Human rights have sharpened these tools.
🏛️ The Wednesbury Standard — Associated Picture Houses Ltd. v Wednesbury Corporation (1948) 1 KB 223
TEST 1 — WEDNESBURY
Decision nullified if: illegality + irrelevancy + unreasonableness of a magnitude that no reasonable body could have reached such a decision
TEST 2 — GCHQ CASE
Council of Civil Services Unions v Minister for Civil Services (1985) AC 374: "Decision is so outrageous in its defiance of logic or of accepted moral standards that no sensible person, who had applied his mind to the question, could have arrived at it"
EVOLUTION
Over time: judicial review on unreasonableness/irrationality came to be applied in proved cases of "abuse of power"
📐 Proportionality Test — R v Secretary of State, Home Dept. ex p Brind (1991) 1 AC 696
WHAT COURTS WEIGH
Whether action taken in law was necessary to meet an intended social need AND was proportionate to the aim evinced by the provision of the law
HUMAN RIGHTS DIMENSION
The greater the interference with human rights, the more the Court can enquire by way of justification. Courts examine whether interference was the least restrictive possible
CONSTITUTIONAL LIMITS
Courts accept that social and political choices are for Parliament and executive. Courts will not substitute the decision of law-making bodies — except where human/fundamental rights are invaded
PUTTASWAMY PRIVACY CASE — SCRUTINY STANDARDS
Debate: Whether court applies "strict scrutiny standard" OR "just, fair and reasonableness standard"
• Majority approved: "just, fair and reasonable standard"
• Justice Chelamewar: carved out exception — heightened scrutiny could apply; "compelling State interest" standard = highest scrutiny — to be evolved on case-to-case basis
✓ PROPORTIONALITY APPLIED — Internet & Mobile Association of India v Reserve Bank of India (2020) 10 SCC 274
RBI circular directing banks not to deal with crypto currency businesses — tested on rationality/proportionality.
Supreme Court: Circular was a disproportionate restriction — struck it as bad.
Cited European Union Parliament's decision NOT to ban crypto-bank interaction as a whole.
📝 Even the RBI — one of the most powerful regulatory authorities — was held to have exceeded proper exercise of discretion. Proportionality is not just for small authorities; it applies to the highest regulatory bodies.
💡 GST: Show Cause Notices demanding disproportionately high penalties or invoking extended limitation without adequate nexus to evidence — challengeable under proportionality. A penalty of 100% where tax short-paid is 2% can be challenged as disproportionate exercise of discretion.
INDIA — PROPORTIONALITY IN COURTS
Indian Higher Courts applied proportionality (expressly or impliedly) particularly where penalty imposed was excessive and out of proportion to the offence.
With development of human rights jurisprudence — the Court now insists that what the administration has done should be compatible with human and fundamental rights.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 17 | Discretionary Powers (I)
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Rooke's Case (1598) 5 Co 99b — Coke J
Classic Common Law Authority | Foundational Definition of Discretion | As cited in Wade & Forsyth, Administrative Law, 9th Edn.
Key Ratio / Principle
"Discretion is a science or understanding to discern between falsity and truth, between wrong and right, between shadows and substances, between equity and colourable glosses and pretences, and not to do according to their wills and private affections."
When to Use — When challenging an adjudicating authority's order as arbitrary, whimsical, or based on personal perception rather than law. Argue: discretion was exercised not on reason and law but on private affection — against the foundational definition of discretion in law.
R. v Wilkes (1770) 98 ER 327 — Lord Mansfield
English Common Law | Classic Authority on Guided Discretion | As referred in Ramji Dayawala & Sons v Invest Import AIR 1981 SC 2085
Key Ratio / Principle
"Discretion, when applied to a Court of Justice, means sound discretion guided by law. It must be governed by rule, not by humour; it must not be arbitrary, vague, and fanciful, but legal and regular."
When to Use — Universally applicable. Use when an adjudicating officer, appellate authority, or tribunal has given a decision that appears to be guided by convenience, departmental pressure, or personal preference rather than legal principle. This ratio lays down the irreducible minimum standard for any exercise of discretion.
Vijayabai v Shreram Tukaram
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1999 SC 431 : (1999) 1 SCC 693, 698 (para 9)
Key Ratio / Principle
Wider the power, the greater circumspection has to be exercised. A suo motu discretionary power is not an omni power to be exercised on the likes and dislikes of the authority. It has to be exercised within the limitations of the statute.
When to Use — Where a Commissioner or senior GST authority exercises suo motu revision, special audit, or reopening power. Argue: wider the power vested, greater is the obligation on the authority to act with circumspection and on material. Mere administrative convenience is not a ground for suo motu exercise.
Justice Douglas — United States Supreme Court
United States v Wunderlick, 342 US 98, 101 (1951) | New York v United States, 342 US 882, 884 (1951)
Key Ratio / Principle
"Absolute discretion is more destructive of freedom than any of man's other inventions." And further: "Absolute discretion, like corruption, marks the beginning of the end of liberty."
When to Use — When arguing against an authority that claims unfettered or unchecked discretion — e.g., an officer claiming total discretion to reject an application, grant or deny a refund, or impose a penalty without reasoned order. Use to establish that absolute discretion is constitutionally and legally impermissible.
Associated Picture Houses Ltd. v Wednesbury Corporation
Court of Appeal, England | (1948) 1 KB 223 | Lord Greene MR
Key Ratio — Wednesbury Unreasonableness Test
A court is entitled to invalidate an administrative decision where the decision is so unreasonable — illegality, irrelevancy, unreasonableness of such magnitude — that no reasonable body could have reached such a decision.
Further — GCHQ Test (Council of Civil Services Unions v Minister, (1985) AC 374)
"The decision is so outrageous in its defiance of logic or of accepted moral standards that no sensible person, who had applied his mind to the question, could have arrived at it."
When to Use — Where a GST demand order, penalty order, or rejection of refund/ITC is factually or legally so perverse that no reasonable adjudicating officer applying his mind could have reached that conclusion. Also used to challenge excessive demands, rejection without consideration of submissions, or orders ignoring material evidence.
K.S. Puttaswamy (Aadhar-5) v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | (2019) 1 SCC 1 | Constitution Bench
Key Ratio — Proportionality Test for Discretionary Interference
Any abridgement of rights through law or administrative action must satisfy: (i) existence of a just, fair and reasonable law; (ii) abridgement must serve a legitimate State aim; (iii) abridgement must be proportionate to the need for such interference; (iv) law must also provide procedural guarantees against abuse.
When to Use — In hearings where a sweeping tax demand, extended period invocation, or heavy penalty is imposed. Argue: the action fails the proportionality test — even if the power exists, it must be proportionately exercised. A demand out of proportion to the breach, or limitation invoked where no suppression is established, cannot survive proportionality scrutiny.
Internet & Mobile Association of India v Reserve Bank of India
Supreme Court of India | (2020) 10 SCC 274
Key Ratio — Proportionality Applied Against Regulatory Discretion
Even a regulatory authority of the stature of the RBI must exercise its discretionary power proportionately. A circular that amounted to a disproportionate restriction was struck down as bad — even though the RBI had the power to regulate, the manner of exercise failed the proportionality test.
When to Use — Where CBIC/GST Council issues circulars or notifications that impose disproportionate burdens — e.g., blanket denial of ITC in an entire sector, or restrictions on refund that are broader than the mischief sought to be prevented. Use to challenge the exercise of statutory discretion as disproportionate.
Sharpe v Wakefield — Lord Halsbury L.C.
House of Lords | (1891) AC 173 | As referred in National Insurance Co. Ltd. v Keshav Bahadur AIR 2004 SC 1581 & UOI v Kuldeep Singh AIR 2004 SC 827
Key Ratio
"When it is said that something is to be done within the discretion of the authorities, that something is to be done according to the rules of reason and justice, not according to private opinion; according to law and not humour. It is to be not arbitrary, vague and fanciful, but legal and regular. And it must be exercised within the limit, to which an honest man, competent to the discharge of his office ought to confine himself."
When to Use — In any GST hearing where an officer's order appears influenced by perception, surmise, or personal view rather than material on record. The "honest man competent to his office" standard is a powerful check — argue the impugned order fails this minimum standard of bona fide exercise of discretion.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter 18 — Discretionary Powers (II)

⚖️ Chapter 18 — Discretionary Powers (II)

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Invalid
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
18.1 Fundamental Rights & Administrative Discretion
18.2 Doctrine of Excessive Delegation of Discretion
18.3 Article 14: Right to Equality
18.3.1 Provisions Held Invalid
18.3.2 Provisions Held Valid
18.3.3 Special Courts
18.3.4 Two Procedures
18.4 Article 19: The Six Freedoms
18.5 Articles 19(1)(A) & 19(2): Freedom of Speech
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
18.1 — Fundamental Rights and Administrative Discretion
⚖️ Fundamental Rights & Administrative Discretion
Chapter XVIII | Principles of Administrative Law
Modern Trend — Wide Discretion Given to Administration
Legislation drafted in broad/general terms; leaves wide choice to administrator
📝 Governments pass laws that say "the officer may do X if he thinks fit" — without saying when or how. This gives officers huge freedom to act — and huge power to misuse.
DANGER "Absolute power corrupts absolutely" — broad powers breed risk of arbitrary & discriminatory exercise. Statute conferring discretionary power rarely creates any oversight mechanism.
Two Systems Compared — England vs India
ENGLAND
Parliament is sovereign → courts CANNOT review legislation → Administration may receive absolute discretion with no procedural safeguards → Courts control only on grounds of ultra vires, mala fides
📝 In England, if Parliament gives you unbridled power, courts largely cannot strike it down. The law is supreme.
INDIA
Fundamental Rights (Arts. 12–35) = Constitutional limitation on legislature & executive → Courts control discretion at TWO levels
📝 In India, Fundamental Rights act as a fence around administrative power. Even if Parliament grants wide discretion, courts can strike it down if it violates Art. 14, 19 or 21.
Two Levels of Judicial Control in India
LEVEL 1 — Conferral Stage
Court examines the LAW ITSELF — if it confers too broad discretion without policy/principle → declares law unconstitutional. Court tests both substantive (what power?) and procedural (what safeguards?) aspects.
📝 Before anyone uses the power — courts check: does the law itself give enough guidance to the officer? If not, the law is bad.
LEVEL 2 — Exercise Stage
Even if law is valid → court examines SPECIFIC ADMINISTRATIVE ACTION — arbitrary action is invalid even under a valid law. No law can clothe administrative discretion with COMPLETE FINALITY.
📝 Even if the law is okay, the officer's specific order can be struck down if it is arbitrary, discriminatory or violates fundamental rights.
KEY PRINCIPLE Fundamental Rights are real and substantial — not notional or fictional. Balance must be drawn between public power and individual freedom. Courts must ensure balance is not tilted too much in favour of Administration.
18.2 — Doctrine of Excessive Delegation of Discretion
📋 Doctrine of Excessive Delegation of Discretion
Fundamental Rights as Control on Uncanalised Discretion
CORE RULE Conferral of too broad and uncanalised discretion on the Administration is INVALID. Discretionary power ought to be hedged by policy, standards, guidelines and/or procedural safeguards.
📝 Uncanalised = no channel/direction given. Just like water without a canal floods everywhere — uncanalised power floods into arbitrariness. The law must channel when, why and how the power is to be used.
BHAGWATI J — Sheo Nandan Paswan v State of Bihar (AIR 1987 SC 877)
"The law always frowns on uncanalised and unfettered discretion on any instrumentality of the State and it is the glory of administrative law that such discretion has been through judicial decisions structured and regulated."
Judicial Approach — Flexible & Tolerant
COURTS UPHOLD IF:
• Vague/general standards found in preamble or other provisions
• Procedural safeguards exist
• Court implies a standard to save statute
• Policy ascertainable from context
COURTS STRIKE DOWN IF:
• Extreme case: too broad, absolutely no policy or procedural safeguards
• After utmost effort — no standard discoverable
• Power conferred without any guidelines whatsoever
IMPORTANT Courts show greater tolerance to delegated legislation (general orders) than to administrative discretion (individual cases) — because discretion applied to individual cases creates greater risk of discrimination and arbitrariness.
📝 A rule that applies to everyone equally is less dangerous. But when one officer can decide differently for each person — that's where fundamental rights are most at risk.
💡 GST: An officer given power to condone delay "if satisfied" without any guideline on what counts as sufficient cause — such power is uncanalised and can be challenged under Art. 14 if exercised discriminatorily between taxpayers similarly situated.
18.3 — Article 14: Right to Equality — General Principles
⚖️ Article 14 — Right to Equality
Guarantee against Administrative Arbitrariness
Article 14 Operates at TWO Levels
LEVEL 1
Challenge to the LAW — delegation of extremely broad powers may be challenged as violating Art. 14 at the stage of conferral
LEVEL 2
Challenge to the ORDER — exercise of valid power in a concrete factual situation may be challenged as discriminatory
Core Principles — Equality & Arbitrariness
Art. 14 strikes at ARBITRARINESS in executive/administrative action — "Equality is antithetical to arbitrariness"
📝 Arbitrary means random, whimsical, without reason. An arbitrary act is by definition unequal — it treats similarly placed people differently for no good reason.
Art. 14 PERMITS classification — if based on intelligible differentia having rational relationship with object of Act
📝 Not all differences in treatment are bad. If there is a sensible reason to treat Group A differently from Group B, and that reason connects to the law's purpose — that is valid classification.
Art. 14 FORBIDS class legislation — treating differently those who are similarly situated without reasonable basis
A.L. Kalra v P & E Corporation of India Ltd (AIR 1984 SC 1361)
"Article 14 strikes at arbitrariness in executive/administrative action because any action that is arbitrary must necessarily involve the negation of equality. One need not confine the denial of equality to a comparative evaluation between two persons... An action per se arbitrary itself denies equality of protection by law."
SC — Another Leading Case
"Absolute discretion uncontrolled by guidelines which may permit denial of equality before law is the anti-thesis of rule of law. Absolute discretion not judicially reviewable inheres the pernicious tendency to be arbitrary and is therefore violative of article 14."
GENERAL PRINCIPLE Conferment of too broad, sweeping, uncanalised, uncontrolled discretion on an administrative authority VIOLATES Article 14 — because uncontrolled discretion may degenerate into arbitrariness, creating danger of discrimination among similarly situated persons.
📝 If a law gives power to an officer but tells him nothing about when to use it, whom to target, or how to decide — that law is bad under Art. 14. It enables pick-and-choose treatment of citizens.
💡 GST: An officer empowered to extend time for filing appeal "as he deems fit" without any guidelines — can extend for one taxpayer but refuse another similarly placed. Such a provision violates Art. 14 unless policy-guided.
How Courts Apply — Flexibility & Deference
Policy need NOT be in the specific provision — may be found in preamble, long title, other provisions, or even rules made under the Act
Court may SUPPLY a standard itself — or read down / give restrictive interpretation to uphold validity
Onus on CHALLENGER to show law is discriminatory — presumption in favour of validity
ONLY in EXTREME case — if after utmost effort no policy found — discretion declared unguided and bad under Art. 14
HIGH OFFICIAL ARGUMENT Courts have at times upheld broad discretion by arguing power conferred on high-ranking official who is unlikely to misuse it — but this is criticised as a tenuous and class-distinction-based reasoning.
📝 Saying "the Minister won't misuse power" is not a constitutional safeguard. The Supreme Court itself has warned: "wide discretion is fraught with tyrannical potential even in high personages, absent legal norms and institutional checks." (Mohinder Singh Gill v Chief Election Commissioner, AIR 1978 SC 851)
State of Punjab v Rafiq Masih (Whitewasher) — AIR 2015 SC 696
Discretion in recovery of excess payment from employee must balance social justice (Arts. 14–18, 38, 39, 39A, 43, 46) — Recovery impermissible in these cases:
(i) Class III & IV employees; (ii) Retired / due to retire within 1 year; (iii) Excess payment for period exceeding 5 years; (iv) Employee wrongfully made to discharge higher post duties; (v) Any case where recovery would be iniquitous or harsh far outweighing employer's right.
Roger Mathew v South Indian Bank — (2020) 6 SCC 1
Delegation of power under Finance Act, 2017 (Sections 183–188) to prescribe qualifications & service conditions of tribunal members — test of validity: "policy and guidelines test" — if found contrary, could be struck down.
18.3.1 — Provisions Held Invalid (Article 14)
🚫 Provisions Struck Down — Article 14
Unguided / Uncanalised Discretion — Leading Cases
Case Power Challenged Why Struck Down GST/Tax Relevance
State of Punjab v Khan Chand
AIR 1974 SC 543		 State power to requisition any movable property if "necessary or expedient" & pay compensation as determined by government No purpose stated; no requirement of public purpose; no compensation principles; power delegable to petty official; no guidelines whatsoever → "pick and choose according to the notion of individuals" Principle: Revenue power without any guideline on what constitutes "public interest" or "expedient" is bad under Art. 14
Air India v Nargesh Meerza
AIR 1981 SC 1829		 Managing Director empowered to extend retirement age of air hostesses from 35 to 45 years at his option — no guidelines, no procedural safeguard, no obligation to record reasons, no appeal Uncanalised and unguided discretion — "where a statute confers a power on an authority to decide matters of moment without laying down any guidelines or principles or norms the power has to be struck down as being violative of article 14" Any GST provision giving officer power to grant/deny waiver "as he thinks fit" without any policy — squarely hit
Suman Gupta v State of J&K
AIR 1983 SC 1235		 State Government nominating candidates to reserved medical seats in other states — in "absolute and unfettered choice" Exercise of all administrative power must be structured within system of controls informed by both relevance and reason — "it is impossible to conceive of legitimate power which is arbitrary in character and travels beyond the bounds of reason" Any licensing/registration power exercised without any criteria = violates Art. 14
Delhi Transport Corporation v DTC Mazdoor Congress
AIR 1991 SC 101		 DTC regulation permitting termination of permanent employees by giving 1 month notice without recording reason and without opportunity of hearing "naked hire and fire" rule — uncanalised and indiscriminate power to terminate without any reason and without opportunity of hearing — arbitrary and discriminatory per se SCN/adjudication: Order without reasons and without hearing = violates Art. 14 + natural justice
Gold Control (Licensing of Dealers) Rules, 1969 Licensing officer could refuse renewal if "too low a turnover" — no guideline on what constitutes "too low" No guideline to determine what exactly is too low — capable of arbitrary application — different officers could work rule differently for different dealers Any GST rule empowering cancellation/suspension of registration without defined criteria on what constitutes sufficient ground — equally bad
Dramatic Performances Act, 1876 District magistrate empowered to prohibit dramatic performances — final authority — no obligation to give reasons — no appellate forum No obligation to give reasons; no provision for higher authority to review; no opportunity for aggrieved party to represent — complete absence of procedural safeguards Adjudication order without reasons and without appellate remedy = procedural infirmity
Senior Supdt. of Post Offices v Izhar Hussan
AIR 1989 SC 2262		 Rule conferring absolute power to compulsorily retire employee without any guidelines — not even words "public interest" Absolute power without even the phrase "public interest" — no restriction/limitation — fell foul of Art. 14 and 16 SCN: Officer must be guided by specific grounds — "if satisfied" or "if it appears" without any further criterion is insufficient
18.3.2 — Provisions Held Valid (Article 14)
✅ Provisions Upheld — Article 14
Sufficient Policy / Safeguards Found
Factors Courts Look for to Uphold Discretionary Power
Policy in preamble / long title / other provisions of the Act
Procedural safeguards — opportunity of hearing, reasons in writing, appeal
Power vested in high official (less weight now — but still a factor)
Quasi-judicial nature of proceedings — natural justice implied
Floor/ceiling fixed for quantum of penalty / damages
Case Power Upheld Why Upheld
Organo Chemical Industries v UOI
AIR 1979 SC 1803		 Section 14-B, EPF Act — Central PF Commissioner empowered to impose damages (0–100% of arrears) for default in contribution — no appellate forum High official; quasi-judicial; must make speaking order; subject to Art. 226; concept of "damages" itself limits the power; Commissioner "usually takes into consideration number of defaults, period of delay, frequency and amounts involved"
Maneka Gandhi v UOI
AIR 1978 SC 597		 Passport Act Section 10(3)(c) — impound passport "in interests of general public" Court read in natural justice requirement; words in Article 19(5) provide sufficient guidelines; reasons to be recorded in writing; copy to affected person; power in high authority; appeal to government
P.J. Irani v State of Madras
AIR 1961 SC 1731		 Section 29, TN Buildings Act — government may exempt any building from provisions "subject to such condition as they may deem fit" Preamble laid down purpose and policy of Act — furnished adequate guidance for exercise of power of exemption
Babubhai & Co. v State of Gujarat
AIR 1985 SC 613		 Section 54, Bombay Town Planning Act — Municipal Corporation power to summarily evict persons from land required for town planning scheme Cumulative factors: (i) high responsible body; (ii) objective exercise (by reference to final scheme); (iii) quasi-judicial — speaking order required; (iv) subject to writ jurisdiction. Mere absence of appellate forum does NOT alone make power arbitrary.
CB Gautam v UOI
(1993) 1 SCC 78		 Section 269-UD, Income Tax Act — compulsory purchase of property on undervaluation of 15% or more Several restrictions: (i) only on 15%+ undervaluation; (ii) reasons in writing; (iii) reasons must be germane to purpose; (iv) right to be heard
Indian Oil Corporation Ltd v Shashi Prabha Shukla
(2018) 12 SCC 85		 Allotment of petroleum dealership — discretion of authority "A public authority in its dealings has to be fair, objective, non-arbitrary, transparent and non-discriminatory. The discretion vested in such an authority is coupled with duty and can never be unregulated or unbridled."
⚠️ BABUBHAI TEST — Key Factors for Cumulative Assessment
(1) On whom is power conferred — high official or petty officer?
(2) Nature of power — subjective satisfaction OR objective reference to facts/tests?
(3) Is it quasi-judicial? — requires natural justice + speaking order?
(4) Is the order subject to writ jurisdiction?
→ It is the CUMULATIVE EFFECT of all these factors that determines validity — not any single factor alone.
18.3.3 — Special Courts
🏛️ Special Courts — Validity under Article 14
Classification of Offences / Cases for Trial by Special Courts
Core Principle — From the Cases
"If the statute itself does not disclose a definite policy or objective and it confers authority on another to make selection at its pleasure, the statute would be held on the face of it to be discriminatory."
INVALID
State of West Bengal v Anwar Ali Sarkar (AIR 1952 SC 75)
Law permitting special courts for "speedier trial" of such offences as the government might direct — no yardstick for grouping cases/offences — government could pick any individual case — uncontrolled authority to discriminate
VALID
Kathi Raning v State of Saurashtra (AIR 1952 SC 123)
Similar provision — but preamble stated policy: security of state, maintenance of public order, essential supplies — government could specify only a CLASS of offences (not individual case) → sufficient guidance found
VALID
Special Courts Act, 1979 (SC ruled in State (Delhi Admn) v VC Shukla — AIR 1980 SC 1382)
Trial of holders of high public/political offices for misuse of power — detailed guidelines in preamble; prima facie evidence required; power in very high authority; clear connotation of "high public office"; sitting HC judge to hold court
KEY DISTINCTION Anwar Ali vs Kathi Raning — difference was: (a) preamble in Saurashtra Act more detailed; (b) Saurashtra: government could specify only a CLASS of offences — not pick individual cases like in Bengal Act. Classifying offences vs selecting individual cases makes all the difference.
📝 For GST: An officer empowered to select specific taxpayers for special audit "as he deems fit" without any criterion — similar to Anwar Ali situation = bad under Art. 14. But if the law says "where turnover exceeds X crore" or "where risk parameters are met" — that is a valid class-based selection.
18.3.4 — Two Procedures
🔀 Two Procedures — Discrimination Risk
When Two Laws/Procedures Available — One Harsher Than Other
📝 Sometimes an officer can proceed against a person under two different laws — one stricter, one lighter. If there is no rule about when to use which — the officer can discriminate: use the harsh law against one person, the lenient law against another similarly placed person.
The Problem
When administrative authority has option to proceed under different provisions — one laying down more onerous procedure — discrimination likely if left to "sweet will" of authority to invoke the prejudicial law. Two similarly situated persons may be differently treated.
The Rule
The basis on which the harsher law is to be applied should be rational and have a reasonable nexus with the purpose sought to be achieved by the law. If statute fails to do so → bad under Article 14.
EARLIER — STRICT VIEW
Northern India Caterers Ltd v State of Punjab
Where two procedures available and no guidance given — harsher law declared invalid as manifestly violating Art. 14
LATER — TOLERANT VIEW
M. Chhagganlal v Greater Bombay Municipality (AIR 1974 SC 2009)
Overruled Northern India Caterers — if two procedures exist, striking down harsher one would only lead to its repeal and the general rule would become harsher. Act upheld where purpose (speedy eviction) provided sufficient guidance. Art. 14 does not demand a fanatical approach.
GST APPLICATION Where CGST Act provides both adjudication route and prosecution route — or both regular assessment and best judgment assessment — the officer must be guided by defined criteria on which route to take. If no criteria exist and one taxpayer is subjected to prosecution while another similarly placed is only assessed — Art. 14 challenge is available.
💡 GST: Section 73 (non-fraud) vs Section 74 (fraud/suppression) of CGST Act — officer cannot invoke Section 74 without sufficient material showing fraud/willful misstatement. Invoking the harsher provision without basis = Art. 14 violation + vitiation of SCN.
18.4 — Article 19: The Six Freedoms
🗽 Article 19 — Six Freedoms of Citizens
Art. 19(1)(a) to (e) and (g) | Reasonable Restrictions under Art. 19(2)–(6)
The Six Freedoms — Article 19(1)
(a) Speech & Expression
(b) Assemble Peaceably
(c) Form Associations
(d) Move Freely
(e) Reside & Settle
(g) Trade & Profession
Key Principles Applicable to All Six Freedoms
✗ CANNOT Curtail freedoms merely by executive fiat — must have a LAW to back administrative action
TEST Restriction must be REASONABLE — courts examine both substantive (what restriction?) and procedural (what safeguards?) aspects
NEXUS Restriction must have rational relation with one of the constitutionally stated purposes under relevant clause (2) to (6)
JUDICIAL APPROACH Courts deprecate legislative attempts to confer unguided discretion on authorities without standards or policies — same approach as Art. 14, but efficacy diluted by tendency to accept vague policy statements. Courts demand stronger procedural safeguards for freedoms of trade, speech and association than for freedoms of movement or residence.
📝 The hierarchy of protection: Right to form association and right of trade get the most protection — they affect economic and democratic rights of many. Right of movement only affects the individual personally.
💡 GST: Suspension/cancellation of GST registration directly restricts the taxpayer's right to trade (Art. 19(1)(g)). Any such action without following the procedure prescribed in Rule 21/21A = violation of fundamental right. Maneka Gandhi principle: must follow natural justice even where not explicitly stated.
18.5 — Articles 19(1)(A) & 19(2): Freedom of Speech and Expression
🗣️ Freedom of Speech & Expression — Art. 19(1)(a)
Reasonable Restrictions under Art. 19(2)
PERMITTED RESTRICTIONS — Art. 19(2)
Sovereignty and integrity of India | Security of the State | Friendly relations with foreign States | Public order | Decency or morality | Contempt of court | Defamation | Incitement to an offence
General Principles
INVALID Executive interference with freedom of speech without support of a law — not valid (Indira Jai Singh v UOI, AIR 1989 Bom 25)
INVALID Unguided or arbitrary discretion to regulate freedom of speech — without procedural safeguards or legislative policy — should not be given to administrative officer
VALID — IF SAFEGUARDS EXIST Broad power can be saved if adequate safeguards against improper exercise are provided (Virendra v State of Punjab, AIR 1957 SC 896)
STRUCK DOWN — Dramatic Performances Act, 1876
District magistrate = final authority to prohibit performances. No obligation to give reasons. No provision for higher authority to review. No opportunity to aggrieved party to make representation → Unconstitutional — complete absence of safeguards
UPHELD — Virendra v State of Punjab (AIR 1957 SC 896)
Punjab Special Powers (Press) Act, 1956 — power to prohibit publication for up to 2 months — saved because: (i) power for specific stated purposes (communal harmony, public order); (ii) temporary (2 months); (iii) aggrieved party could make representation; (iv) government could modify/confirm/rescind
UPHELD — Section 144 CrPC (Babulal Parate v State of Maharashtra, AIR 1961 SC 884)
District magistrate may order person to abstain from certain act — saved because: (i) magistrate must act judicially; (ii) only temporary (max 2 months); (iii) emergency situation; (iv) material facts must be stated in order; (v) appeal to HC under CrPC ss. 435/439
SECTION 144(6) — STRUCK DOWN (State of Bihar v K.K. Misra, AIR 1971 SC 1667)
State government power to extend life of magistrate's order beyond 2 months — "independent power" not to be exercised judicially — open to arbitrary exercise — no provision for party to make representation — order not temporary → Unconstitutional
Anuradha Bhasin v UOI — (2020) 3 SCC 637
Freedom of speech and expression through internet enjoys constitutional protection under Art. 19(1)(a). Any restriction by clamping Section 144 through executive magistrate's directions must be in accordance with Art. 19(2) inclusive of test of proportionality. Must set out reasons. Right to access internet was not held to be a fundamental freedom of licence.
📝 You cannot shut down internet or block a website just because you want to. There must be a law justifying it, the restriction must be proportional, and reasons must be given.
Maneka Gandhi v UOI (AIR 1978 SC 597)
Order impounding passport under Section 10(3)(c) — could be challenged under Art. 19(1)(a) and (g) if so drastic as to impose unreasonable restrictions (e.g., impounding for indefinite period). Concept of unreasonableness under Art. 19 is much broader than Wednesbury unreasonableness.
💡 GST: Suspension of GST registration for an indefinite period without fixing a definite period or prescribing the review mechanism — would violate Art. 19(1)(g) on the Maneka Gandhi principle of disproportionate restriction.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 18 | Discretionary Powers (II)
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
A.L. Kalra v P & E Corporation of India Ltd
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1984 SC 1361 | Article 14 — Arbitrariness
Key Ratio
"Article 14 strikes at arbitrariness in executive/administrative action because any action that is arbitrary must necessarily involve the negation of equality. One need not confine the denial of equality to a comparative evaluation between two persons to arrive at a conclusion of discriminatory treatment. An action per se arbitrary itself denies equality of protection by law."
When to Use — When challenging a GST SCN, adjudication order, or penalty order that is issued without reasons, without following the prescribed procedure, or where similarly placed taxpayers have been treated differently. Cite to establish that an arbitrary action per se violates Art. 14 — no need to show discrimination against another person.
Suman Gupta v State of Jammu & Kashmir
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1983 SC 1235 | Article 14 — Structured Discretion
Key Ratio
"We think it beyond dispute that the exercise of all administrative power vested in public authority must be structured within a system of controls informed by both relevance and reason—relevance in relation to the object which it seeks to serve, and reason in regard to the manner in which it attempts to do so." In a system founded on Rule of Law "it is impossible to conceive of legitimate power which is arbitrary in character and travels beyond the bounds of reason."
When to Use — When challenging exercise of discretionary power by GST officer (e.g., selecting taxpayer for scrutiny, rejecting refund, imposing condition on registration) without any criteria or reasons — the power must be structured by relevance and reason.
Sheo Nandan Paswan v State of Bihar — Bhagwati J
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1987 SC 877 | Doctrine of Excessive Delegation
Key Ratio
"It is significant to note that the entire development of Administrative law is characterised by a consistent series of decisions controlling and structuring the discretion conferred on the State and its officers. The law always frowns on uncanalised and unfettered discretion on any instrumentality of the State and it is the glory of administrative law that such discretion has been through judicial decisions structured and regulated."
When to Use — When arguing that a provision of CGST Act or Rules conferring wide power on an officer (e.g., Section 83 provisional attachment, Section 67 search) without specific conditions is being exercised in an uncanalised manner. Cite to show that law frowns on unfettered discretion.
Air India v Nargesh Meerza
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1981 SC 1829 | Article 14 — Guidelines Mandatory
Key Ratio
"It is true that a discretionary power may not necessarily be a discriminatory power but where a statute confers a power on an authority to decide matters of moment without laying down any guidelines or principles or norms the power has to be struck down as being violative of article 14."
When to Use — When challenging an SCN or adjudication order where the officer has exercised power without any guidelines on the decision criteria — for example, rejecting an application for extension of time, or imposing penalty, without specifying the factors considered. The power without guidelines is per se violative of Art. 14.
Indian Oil Corporation Ltd v Shashi Prabha Shukla
Supreme Court of India | (2018) 12 SCC 85 | Duty Coupled with Discretion
Key Ratio
"A public authority in its dealings has to be fair, objective, non-arbitrary, transparent and non-discriminatory. The discretion vested in such an authority, which is a concomitant of its power is coupled with duty and can never be unregulated or unbridled. Any decision or action contrary to these functional precepts would be at the pain of invalidation thereof."
When to Use — When arguing that the GST adjudicating authority has not been fair, objective or transparent in its adjudication — e.g., ignoring documents submitted, passing copy-paste orders, not addressing contentions. Discretion is coupled with duty to be fair and non-arbitrary.
Babubhai & Co. v State of Gujarat
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1985 SC 613 | Cumulative Test for Validity
Key Ratio / Test
"It is the cumulative effect of all these factors that will render the provision unreasonable or arbitrary and liable to be struck down." Factors: (i) on whom power is conferred; (ii) whether exercise depends on subjective satisfaction or objective reference to facts; (iii) whether quasi-judicial requiring natural justice and speaking order; (iv) whether subject to judicial review.
When to Use — When challenging validity of a specific GST provision or its exercise — run the Babubhai cumulative test: who has the power, how subjective is it, is there a speaking order requirement, is writ remedy available? If the cumulative picture is adverse — the provision or its exercise is bad.
Maneka Gandhi v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1978 SC 597 | Articles 14, 19, 21 — Unreasonableness
Key Ratio
An order under a statutory power could be declared invalid under Articles 19(1)(a) and (g) if it was so drastic in nature as to impose unreasonable restrictions on the individual's freedom — such as an order of impounding a passport for an indefinite period. The concept of unreasonableness under article 19 is much broader than that laid down in Wednesbury.
When to Use — When challenging indefinite suspension of GST registration or prolonged blocking of Electronic Credit Ledger. Argue that an indefinite restriction on the right to trade (Art. 19(1)(g)) without any review mechanism is disproportionate and violates Art. 19 on the Maneka Gandhi principle.
Delhi Transport Corporation v DTC Mazdoor Congress — Ray J
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1991 SC 101 | Article 14 — Speaking Orders & Hearing
Key Ratio
"Thus a regular, temporary or permanent employee of the State Transport Authority can be dismissed or removed from service at the whims and caprices of the concerned authority without any reason whatsoever and undoubtedly this evidences that such unbridled, indiscriminate and uncanalised power to terminate the services even of a permanent employee without assigning any reason and without giving any opportunity of hearing as fairplay and justice demands a reasonable procedure is per se, arbitrary and discriminatory."
When to Use — When challenging adjudication/demand orders passed without reasons and without adequate opportunity of hearing. An order without reasons and opportunity of hearing is per se arbitrary and discriminatory under Art. 14.
Mohinder Singh Gill v Chief Election Commissioner
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1978 SC 851 | Limits of "High Official" Argument
Key Ratio
"Wide discretion is fraught with tyrannical potential even in high personages, absent legal norms and institutional checks."
When to Use — When the Department argues that the officer is a senior official and will not misuse power. Counter: the Supreme Court has itself warned that wide discretion carries tyrannical potential even for high-ranking officials in the absence of legal norms. Status of officer is not a constitutional safeguard.
State of Punjab v Rafiq Masih (Whitewasher)
Supreme Court of India | AIR 2015 SC 696 | Equitable Exercise of Discretion
Key Ratio — Five Situations of Impermissible Recovery
Recovery from employees would be impermissible where: (i) Class III/IV employees; (ii) retired or due to retire within 1 year; (iii) excess payment for period exceeding 5 years; (iv) employee wrongfully made to discharge higher post duties; (v) any case where recovery would be iniquitous or harsh or arbitrary to such an extent as would far outweigh the equitable balance of the employer's right to recover. The spirit of Articles 14 to 18, 38, 39, 39A, 43 and 46 requires the State to assure a social order providing justice.
When to Use — In GST adjudication involving recovery of tax paid on a wrong understanding where recovery would cause undue hardship — argue that equitable principles emanating from constitutional provisions require balancing the revenue interest with the taxpayer's hardship, especially for small traders and MSMEs.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter XVIII — Discretionary Powers (II)

⚖️ Chapter XVIII — Discretionary Powers (II)

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Invalid
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Discretionary
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
18.8 Externment — Art. 19(1)(D) & (E)
18.9 Art. 19(1)(F) — Property Rights
18.10 Art. 19(1)(G) — Trade & Commerce
18.10.1 Licensing — Core Principles
18.10.1A Licensing — Case Matrix
18.10.2 Miscellaneous Situations
18.10.3 Price Fixing
18.11 Art. 21 & 22 — Personal Liberty & Preventive Detention
18.12 Administrative Discrimination & Arbitrariness
18.12.1 Right to be Heard
18.12.2 Viva Voce Examination
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
18.8 — Externment: Articles 19(1)(D) & (E) — Movement & Residence
🚫 Externment — Discretionary Power to Remove / Restrict Persons
Articles 19(1)(d) & (e) | Movement & Residence within India
📝 Externment = Government order forcing a person to leave or stay away from an area. This is a drastic restriction on fundamental rights of movement and residence. Courts have scrutinised these laws strictly.
Procedural Safeguards Required for a Valid Externment Law
✓ MINIMUM REQUIREMENT
Grounds of externment must be communicated to the person
✓ REQUIRED
Opportunity to make representation — even if before executive officer only
✗ NOT NECESSARY
Presence of Advisory Board — courts do NOT regard it as essential for externment
VOID IF ABSENT
No hearing provided for selecting the place where externee to reside → Fatal flaw (Baldeo Pd. case)
Standard for Valid Externment — Prem Chand v UOI [AIR 1981 SC 613]
STRICT TEST
Externment = "Economic harakiri and psychic distress"
Police apprehension alone is NOT enough
Some ground or other is NOT adequate
Must be: Clear and present danger based on creditable material making movements/acts alarming, dangerous or fraught with violence
Natural justice must be fairly complied with
📝 Even if the law allows externment based on official satisfaction, the standard is high — vague police suspicion will not do. There must be real, credible evidence of clear danger before a person can be expelled from an area.
Contrast: Right of Association vs Right of Movement
HIGHER PROTECTION
Right of Association (Art. 19(1)(c))
Advisory board NOT sufficient
Judicial tribunal required to declare association unlawful
Base of democratic society
LOWER PROTECTION (in practice)
Right of Movement & Residence (Art. 19(1)(d)&(e))
Courts permit large executive discretion
Advisory board not insisted upon
Affects individual only — courts more permissive
JUDICIAL CRITIQUE
Right of movement and residence has received less protection than it deserves. Forceful dissent of Mukherjea J. in the Khare case is indicative of unsatisfactory features in this area.
SPECIAL CASE
Section 7, Influx from Pakistan (Control) Act, 1949 — empowered Central Govt. to direct removal of any person on "reasonable suspicion" of illegal entry
→ Held INVALID in Ibrahim Vazir v State of Bombay [AIR 1954 SC 229] as regards Indian citizens
→ Invoked Fundamental Right of citizen to reside and settle in any part of India [Art. 19(1)(e)]
📝 A person could be removed merely on suspicion without any opportunity to clear his conduct — the Supreme Court struck this down as a "travesty of the right of citizenship."
18.9 — Articles 19(1)(F) & 19(5): Freedom to Hold Property
🏛️ Property Rights — Constitutional Framework
Articles 19(1)(f) & 19(5) [REPEALED] | Article 300-A [CURRENT]
REPEALED
Articles 19(1)(f) & 19(5) along with Article 31 — REPEALED
Right to property as Fundamental Right: No longer exists
📝 Earlier, property was a fundamental right enforceable by courts. After repeal, it is only a constitutional right — weaker protection.
CURRENT LAW — Art. 300-A
"No person shall be deprived of his property save by authority of law"
Executive action without law support = UNWARRANTED
📝 Property can only be taken by proper law, not by a mere executive order. An officer cannot just seize your property without legal backing.
CHALLENGE ROUTE
Law conferring unguided and arbitrary powers on Administration in relation to property → Can be challenged under Article 14 and Article 19(1)(g)
💡 In GST context: If an officer seizes goods or blocks ITC refund without any statutory basis or guideline, this arbitrary executive action can be challenged under Art. 14 (equality) and Art. 300-A (property deprivation without law).
18.10 — Articles 19(1)(G) & 19(6): Freedom of Trade and Commerce
🏪 Freedom of Trade & Commerce — Art. 19(1)(g) & Reasonable Restrictions u/Art. 19(6)
Article 19(1)(g): Right of ALL citizens to practise any profession, carry on any occupation, trade or business
PERMITTED
Art. 19(6): State may impose reasonable restrictions in the interests of general public
PERMITTED
Law nationalising any trade or business — NOT hit by Art. 19(1)(g)
INVALID
Law conferring arbitrary, unregulated power on executive without any check or control → Unconstitutional
Core Constitutional Standard for Executive Discretion in Trade Regulation
GOLDEN RULE
Power conferred on executive must NOT be:
(i) Arbitrary | (ii) Unregulated by any rule or principle
It should NOT be left entirely to discretion of authority to do anything it likes without any check or control
— General principle laid down across numerous Supreme Court cases
📝 The state can regulate trade — but the rules for that regulation must themselves be defined. You cannot give a government officer a blank cheque to decide who can trade and who cannot.
EXCEPTION
For trades which are illegal, dangerous, immoral or injurious to health/welfare of people:
Greater discretionary authority may be left with executive than is permissible in normal trades
INTERNET / PUBLIC EMERGENCY
Anuradha Bhasin v UOI [2020] — Power to restrict movement & internet use in J&K on "public emergency" explained:
Authorities must triangulate necessity → Satisfy proportionality requirement
Public emergency involves different stages of restriction
📝 Even during emergencies, restrictions must be proportionate to the danger. A blanket internet shutdown for months needs proper justification at each stage.
18.10.1 — Licensing: Core Principles & Dwarka Pd. Foundation
📋 Licensing — Permissible Limits on Administrative Power
Dwarka Pd. v State of UP [AIR 1954 SC 227] — The Foundational Case
Basic Proposition Laid Down by Courts on Licensing
INVALID
Licensing officers must NOT be given uncontrolled power to grant, revoke or cancel a licence
A law conferring arbitrary and unguided powers on administrative authorities = INVALID under Art. 19(1)(g)
VALID IF —
(i) Reasonable norms / policy / principles to guide administrative power
(ii) Some procedural safeguards against misuse
(iii) At least one stage of appeal to higher officer as matter of right
(iv) Reasons required to be recorded in writing
Dwarka Pd. v State of UP — UP Coal Control Order, 1953
Cl. 3(1) — VALID
Licence required for stocking/selling coal
Reasonable to regulate essential commodities through licensed vendors → equitable distribution at fair prices
Cl. 3(2) — INVALID
Coal Controller could exempt any person from licensing
Grounds for exemption nowhere mentioned
Unrestricted power → No check, no redress if acted arbitrarily
Cl. 4(3) — INVALID
Absolute power to grant/revoke/suspend licence
No rules to guide discretion
Committed to unrestrained will of single individual
No check against injustice from improper exercise
📝 The Dwarka Pd. case is the Magna Carta of licensing law in India. The court drew a clear line: regulating trade through licences is fine, but the power to give or take away a licence must itself be bound by rules — not left to one person's whim.
💡 GST Application: When GST registration is cancelled or suspended u/s 29/30 CGST Act, the officer must follow prescribed grounds and give opportunity to be heard. An officer cancelling registration without following Rule 22 procedure — applying Dwarka Pd. — acts without valid guidance = arbitrary = challengeable.
Key Distinction: Licence to Trade vs Permit to Transport
STRICTER STANDARD
Licence to Trade — without it, person cannot carry on trade at all
Must have clear norms + procedural safeguards + appeal
MORE FLEXIBLE
Permit for Transportation/Movement of Goods — restricts only one aspect of total trade, leaving rest free
General policy of order can provide sufficient guidance
📝 Harishankar Bagla v State of MP: Cotton Textiles transport permit upheld because general policy (ensure even distribution at fair price) gave sufficient guidance — distinct from Dwarka Pd. where no such policy existed.
HIGH OFFICER PRINCIPLE — Chinta Lingam
Courts often hold broad powers valid on the ground that power is conferred on a "high and responsible officer" expected to discharge duties in a responsible manner
→ Criticised: Authority may be misused by high OR low officer alike
→ Courts should insist on at least ONE stage of appeal as a matter of right
📝 Courts should not be satisfied with "the officer is senior, so he won't misuse power." Control mechanism is a guarantee against improper use of power regardless of rank.
18.10.1A — Licensing: Case Matrix — Valid vs Invalid
Case Provision Decision Reason GST/Tax Relevance
Chandrakant v Jasjit Singh
AIR 1962 SC 204		 Customs House Agents Licensing Rule — "not otherwise considered suitable" INVALID Rejection must state reasons + provision for appeal against rejection GST practitioner/broker licensing cannot have vague "suitability" clause without objective criteria
Hari Chand Sarda v Mizo District Council
AIR 1967 SC 829		 Non-tribal cannot trade without council licence; no principles; no appeal to superior INVALID No principle/standards on which committee was to act; "hardly a safeguard" to record reasons without appeal Any licensing regime with no objective criteria = constitutionally vulnerable
Kishan Chand Arora v Commissioner of Police
AIR 1961 SC 705		 Commissioner to grant licences to eating houses in his discretion — no norms, no safeguards INVALID (later overruled — does not represent good law) Minority: "Arbitrariness is writ large." Later decisions confirmed this view. Historical — shows evolution of judicial thinking towards stricter control
Gold Control Act — Harak Chand v UOI
AIR 1970 SC 1453		 Conditions for issue of licences to gold dealers INVALID — conditions "vague, uncertain and unintelligible" Region undefined; "anticipated demand" vague; "suitability" and "public interest" — no objective standard Similar to vague GST show-cause notices — "public interest" without definition = arbitrary
Manick Chand v UOI
AIR 1984 SC 1249		 Gold Control Act — Sec. 52 (firm composition change) & Sec. 79 (extension of seizure period) VALID — adequate safeguards found Rules 2 & 3 provided guidelines; provision for appeal to Collector; Sec. 79 — inbuilt safeguards: notice, reasoned order Detention of goods/documents under GST: must give notice, record reasons, allow appeal — else Sec. 79 analogy applies
Fedco v Bilgrami
AIR 1960 SC 415		 Cl. 9(a) Imports Control Order — cancel licence for fraud/inadvertence; Cl. 10 — hearing before cancellation VALID — reasonable restriction Safeguards: cancellation only on specified grounds + hearing before action = no scope for arbitrary cancellation GST registration cancellation must follow SCN + hearing — Fedco rationale directly applicable
Mineral Development Ltd. v State of Bihar
