AIR 1960 SC 468		 Bihar Mica Act — State Govt. cancels mica licence on prescribed grounds after show cause VALID Highest executive + clear grounds + opportunity to show cause = adequate safeguard GST licence/registration cancellation with grounds + SCN + hearing = constitutionally valid
Parasuraman (Tamil Nadu Act)
AIR 1990 SC 40		 Private educational institution requires government permission; govt. may grant or refuse; only hearing before refusal INVALID "Unrestricted and unguided discretion" — no criteria to apply/grant or refuse; hearing alone not enough safeguard Any GST provision giving officer absolute discretion without criteria = constitutionally infirm
Rice-Milling — Chandrakant Saha v UOI
AIR 1979 SC 314		 Rice-Milling Industry (Regulation) Act — licensing with adequate guidelines for revocation VALID Adequate guidelines incorporated in statutory provision → not arbitrary power Statutory framework with clear grounds = valid licensing/cancellation regime
⚖️ SYNTHESIS RULE  If safeguards (norms + hearing + reasons + appeal) are provided against arbitrary exercise of power to grant or cancel licences, the law may be upheld. The function of cancelling a licence is quasi-judicial — natural justice must be followed. Suspension without hearing is permissible as it is merely a preliminary stage to cancellation.
18.10.2 — Miscellaneous Situations: Dwarka Pd. Proposition in Action
⚙️ Miscellaneous Situations — Dwarka Pd. Applied
Factory Closure | Government Contracts | Income Tax Search | Calcutta Corporation
INVALID
Factory Closure (Sec. 25-O)
Excel Wear v UOI [AIR 1979 SC 25]
Order passed on govt's subjective satisfaction
No requirement to record reasons
Authority could whimsically refuse permission → STRUCK DOWN
📝 Sec. 25-O gave government absolute power to block factory closure without reasons — courts invalidated it as pure arbitrary power.
VALID
Retrenchment (Sec. 25N)
Meenakshi Mills [AIR 1994 SC 2696]
Order on objective considerations
Speaking order required
High Court review available u/Art. 226
Govt power = quasi-judicial
VALID
Lay-off Permission (Sec. 25M)
Papnasam Labour Union v Madura Coats [AIR 1995 SC 2200]
Safeguards: (i) Decision within 2 months else deemed granted (ii) Reasons in writing (iii) Order communicated to applicant
Procedural reasonableness satisfied
💡 GST: Parallel — under GST, deemed approval if not disposed within time limit = same principle of procedural reasonableness.
VALID
Search & Seizure (Sec. 132 IT Act)
Pooran Mal v Director of Inspection [AIR 1974 SC 348]
Directed against tax evaders on "reason to believe"
Summary enquiry within 90 days
Books retained max 180 days with reasons
Safeguards on lines of CrPC
💡 GST Search u/s 67: Must be based on "reason to believe" — not general suspicion. Pooran Mal analogy: safeguards must be CrPC-level.
Two More Significant Cases
INVALID
Calcutta Municipal Corporation
Banned use of premises "in the opinion of" corporation — opinion made conclusive and non-justiciable
Court: "Vice is it makes opinion of Corporation, howsoever capricious or arbitrary, conclusive"
= Unreasonable restriction on right to carry on trade
📝 Making any authority's opinion "final and unchallengeable" is unconstitutional. Courts must always be able to review whether the opinion was reasonable.
💡 GST: If an adjudicating officer's finding is made non-challengeable by a statute — that provision itself would be unconstitutional on this analogy.
FREEDOM OF EXPRESSION UPHELD
Vijay Shankar Pandey v UOI [AIR 2015 SC 326]
IAS Officer charged for NGO's writ criticising government
SC: Public servant NOT denuded of fundamental right of freedom of expression under Art. 19(1)(a)
Charge-sheet = "attempt to suppress probe into black money"
📝 Even government employees retain fundamental rights. Disciplinary action for speaking truth to power = abuse of process = unconstitutional.
18.10.3 — Price Fixing: Discretionary Power of Executive
💰 Price Fixing — Courts Grant Wide Deference to Administration
General Judicial Approach to Price-Fixing Challenges
GENERAL RULE — DEFERENCE
"In the ultimate analysis, the mechanics of price fixation has necessarily to be left to the judgment of the executive and unless it is patent that there is hostile discrimination against a class of operators, the processual basis of price fixation has to be accepted in the generality of cases as valid."
— Courts concede a good deal of flexibility to Administration in price-fixing area
📝 Courts rarely strike down price-fixing orders. They will intervene only if: (i) there is hostile discrimination against a class, or (ii) the price is so patently unreasonable as to be beyond the price-fixing power altogether.
Price-Fixing Cases — Key Outcomes
CaseSubjectOutcomePrinciple
Dwarka Pd. Coal Control Order — price formula VALID Detailed statutory formula = discretion neither unfettered nor unlimited
Diwan Sugar Mills v UOI
AIR 1959 SC 626		 Sugar price — Cl. 5, Sugar (Control) Order, 1955 VALID Multiple factors (sugarcane price, costs, taxes, margin of profit) = adequate check against abuse
Premier Automobiles v UOI
AIR 1972 SC 1690		 Car prices under Industries Act, 1951 VALID + periodic review "Takes in all elements which make it fair for consumer leaving reasonable margin of profit to manufacturer"
Shree Meenakshi Mills v UOI
AIR 1974 SC 366		 Yarn price fixation VALID — no intervention Price not shown "arbitrary" or "so grossly inadequate as to threaten supply position"
Saraswati Syndicate v UOI
AIR 1975 SC 460		 Sugar ex-factory price VALID Basis not "so patently unreasonable as to be in excess of price-fixing power"
State of Rajasthan v Nath Mal
AIR 1954 SC 307		 Rajasthan Foodgrains Control Order — power to freeze + dispose at rate fixed by govt. PARTLY INVALID Power to dispose of stock at rate fixed by it in unrestrained authority = unreasonable
Bijay Cotton Mills v State of Ajmer
AIR 1955 SC 33		 Minimum Wages Act, 1948 VALID Advisory committee + representation + Central Advisory Board + equal representation of employers/employees = adequate safeguards despite large powers
18.11 — Articles 21 & 22: Personal Liberty and Preventive Detention
🔒 Personal Liberty & Preventive Detention
Articles 21 & 22 | Supreme Court's Interpretation
Article 21 — Standard of Procedure
Maneka Gandhi v UOI [AIR 1978 SC 597]
Art. 21 envisages "reasonable, fair and just procedure"
Absence of arbitrary power = first postulate of rule of law
In re Special Courts Bill, 1978
Procedure must be "right and just and fair and NOT arbitrary, fanciful or oppressive"
Otherwise = no procedure at all → Art. 21 requirement NOT satisfied
Satwant v Asst. Passport Officer [AIR 1967 SC 1836]
No law governed issue of passports → Refusal violated Art. 21
Led to enactment of Passports Act, 1967
Preventive Detention — Framework & Safeguards
📝 Preventive detention = Detaining a person merely on suspicion (without trial or conviction) to PREVENT future prejudicial acts. Unusual in democracies — England and Canada use it only in wartime. India permits it even in peacetime due to unstable law and order.
PARLIAMENT EXCLUSIVELY
Detention for:
• Defence of India
• Foreign affairs
• Security of India
[7th Schedule, List I, Entry 9]
PARLIAMENT + STATE CONCURRENTLY
Detention for:
• Security of a State
• Maintenance of public order
• Essential supplies & services
[List III, Entry 3]
RESIDUARY
Parliament can legislate for preventive detention on any other ground under residuary power
Article 22 — Constitutional Safeguards for Detenu
Art. 22(5) — GROUNDS
Detaining authority SHALL communicate grounds of detention as soon as may be
SHALL afford earliest opportunity of making representation
Govt. MUST consider representation at earliest opportunity (SC implied obligation)
Art. 22(5) — LIMITS
RIGHT conferred = Make representation
NOT a right to: Oral hearing / Lawyer / Lead evidence
(unless govt. side represented by lawyer — then Art. 14 & 21 give corresponding right to detenu)
Art. 22(6) — DILUTION
Authority may withhold disclosure of facts considered against public interest to disclose
→ Whittles down effectiveness of Art. 22(5)
Art. 22(4) — ADVISORY BOARD
Detention beyond 3 months requires Advisory Board report
Board = persons qualified as HC judges
Board determines ONLY: Is detention justified or not? (NOT: period of detention)
Judicial Control Over Preventive Detention — Marginal but Existing
KEY PRINCIPLE
Making of preventive detention order = ENTIRELY left to subjective satisfaction of executive
Legislature need NOT set up objective standard or test
Detention is preventive NOT punitive — based on suspicion/anticipation, not proof
Judicial control is ONLY MARGINAL
GROUNDS ON WHICH COURTS CAN REVIEW:
(i) Mala fides
(ii) Irrelevant or extraneous considerations
(iii) Non-application of mind by detaining authority
(iv) Compliance with prescribed procedure
(v) Whether kept within confines of law
(vi) Adequacy of grounds — whether grounds are vague/indefinite making representation difficult
(vii) Whether grounds furnished to detenu within reasonable time
(viii) Whether there is live and proximate link between past conduct and imperative need to detain (Sama Aruna — stale incidents of 9-14 years back = insufficient)
📝 Courts cannot substitute their own satisfaction for that of the detaining authority. But they can check if the authority followed the rules and if the grounds are real and recent — not old incidents dug up to justify detention.
A.K. ROY v UOI [AIR 1982 SC 710] — National Security Act, 1980
SC accepted: "Vagueness and consequent uncertainty of a law of preventive detention bears upon the reasonableness of that law"
BUT: Did NOT hold NSA invalid — held expressions relate to concepts "difficult to encase within strait-jacket of a precise definition" — Act must be restricted to as few situations as possible
"Maintenance of supplies" clause — requires prior notification specifying which supplies are regarded essential
18.12 — Administrative Discrimination and Arbitrariness
⚖️ Article 14 — Guarantee Against Arbitrariness & Discrimination
Two Dimensions: (1) Excessive Delegation | (2) Discriminatory Exercise of Power
DIMENSION 1 (Already covered)
Statute confers too broad and unregulated discretionary power → Statute itself void u/Art. 14
DIMENSION 2 (This section)
Statute is valid BUT authority entrusted with discretion acts in discriminatory or arbitrary manner
→ Treats equals differentially
→ Does not follow policy/principle in Act
→ Individual order quashed u/Art. 14 (easier — court does not strike down law, only the order)
📝 Two ways to challenge under Art. 14: (1) Attack the law itself as giving unguided power. (2) Accept the law but say the officer used the power in a biased/unequal manner. The second is easier to succeed in courts because they only quash the specific order, not the whole law.
Core Propositions on Arbitrariness — E.P. Royappa & Maneka Gandhi
⚖️ ROYAPPA DOCTRINE
"From a positivistic point of view, equality is antithetic to arbitrariness. Article 14 strikes at arbitrariness in state action and thus ensures fairness and equality of treatment."
— Bhagwati J. in E.P. Royappa v State of Tamil Nadu [AIR 1974 SC 555]

"Where an act is arbitrary, it is implicit in it that it is unequal both according to political logic and constitutional law, and is therefore violative of Article 14."
An arbitrary decision is per se violative of Article 14
📝 Arbitrariness is a broader concept than discrimination. Discrimination requires comparing two persons treated differently. Arbitrariness can arise even in action against ONE person if that action has no reasonable basis.
MANEKA GANDHI ELABORATION
"Discretion means sound discretion guided by law or governed by known principles or rules, not by whim or fancy or caprice of the authority."
"If discretion is exercised without any principle or without any rule, it is a situation amounting to the antithesis of Rule of Law."
— Maneka Gandhi v UOI [AIR 1978 SC 597]
Yick Wo v Hopkins — Classic Case on Discriminatory Administration
AMERICAN CASE — Applied in India
San Francisco Ordinance: No laundry without Board's consent except in brick/stone building
Result: 200 Chinese launderers denied permission despite complying with all conditions
80 non-Chinese under similar circumstances — permitted
Court: "Though the law itself be fair on its face and impartial in appearance, yet, if it is applied and administered by public authority with an evil eye and an unequal hand, so as practically to make unjust and illegal discriminations... the denial of equal justice is still within the prohibition of the Constitution."
📝 A law can be perfectly worded but if officers implement it selectively to favour some and target others — that is still unconstitutional discrimination. The motive and impact of application matters, not just the text of the law.
💡 GST Application: If a show-cause notice is issued to one trader for a practice commonly followed across an industry, but only that trader is targeted — Yick Wo principle applies. Discriminatory enforcement = violation of Art. 14.
Burden of Proof in Discrimination Claims — Government Branch Press Case
STARTING PRESUMPTION
Administration of a particular law has NOT been discriminatory
Abuse of power not easily assumed where discretion is vested in high officials
COMPLAINANT'S BURDEN
Must prove abuse of power — heavy burden
Must point out circumstances which prima facie make out discriminatory exercise of power
SHIFT OF BURDEN
Once complainant makes out prima facie case → Authority's duty to disclose reason or motive for impugned action
Court will then scrutinise circumstances
Illustrative Cases — When Art. 14 Applied to Quash Administrative Action
Situation / CaseAction Quashed Under Art. 14Reason
Quota of raw material — small scale industrial units Quashed — Discriminatory Granted to some, denied to petitioners though similarly situated
Service termination — temporary servant senior to retained persons Quashed — Discriminatory No distinguishing feature between terminated and retained employees
Retirement at 58 while others at 60 Quashed — Discriminatory Unequal treatment in same cadre without justification
Vellore Educational Trust v AP Quashed — Discriminatory Permission granted to two other societies for engineering college while denied to Trust — "not at all tenable"
Bihar Government — releasing piece of land from acquisition for influential family Quashed — "Pure and simple act of favouritism" All similarly situated must be treated alike under acquisition proceedings
Common Cause v UOI — Petrol pump allotment Quashed — Wholly arbitrary & discriminatory Allotted without criteria, without applications, without guidelines — "lack denotes nepotism and arbitrariness"
Liberty Oil Mills v UOI — Secret circular keeping licence applications in abeyance Quashed — Arbitrary Order of abeyance not communicated to affected parties = violates Art. 14 & 19(1)(g) + Natural Justice
A.L. Kalra v P&E Corporation — Removal without specific rule defining misconduct Quashed — Arbitrary Conduct not defined as misconduct under rules; "ex post facto interpretation of incident camouflaged as misconduct"
Disproportionate penalty — students in fracas Quashed — "Arbitrary and discriminatory" Serious misconduct → nominal punishment; self-defence → harsher punishment = arbitrary and discriminatory
Shrilekha Vidyarthi v State of UP — Wholesale termination of all govt. counsels Quashed — "Arbitrariness is writ large" Omnibus order treating posts as "bounty to be distributed at sweet will of appointing authority"
18.12.1 — Right to be Heard: Article 14 & Natural Justice
👂 Right to be Heard — Article 14 as Constitutional Guardian of Natural Justice
The Syllogism — Tulsiram Patel [AIR 1985 SC 1416]
THE LOGICAL CHAIN:
(1) Violation of rule of natural justice → results in arbitrariness
(2) Arbitrariness = same as discrimination
(3) Discrimination from state action → Violation of Article 14
∴ Violation of natural justice by State = Violation of Article 14

BUT: "Article 14, however, is NOT the sole repository of the principles of natural justice"
"The principles of natural justice are NOT the creation of Article 14. Article 14 is not their begetter but their constitutional guardian."
📝 Natural justice existed before Article 14. But Art. 14 gives it constitutional teeth — any state action that violates principles of hearing (audi alteram partem) is now also unconstitutional arbitrariness.
💡 GST Application: Show cause notice under GST without proper opportunity to be heard = violation of natural justice = violation of Art. 14. Even Section 75(4) CGST Act mandates hearing before any adverse order — this is Art. 14 in action.
D.K. Yadava v J.M.A. Industries [(1993) 3 SCC 259]
COMPREHENSIVE STATEMENT
"Article 14 has a pervasive processual potency and versatile quality, equalitarian in its soul and allergic to discriminatory dictates. Equality is the antithesis of arbitrariness. It is, thereby, conclusively held by this Court that the principles of natural justice are part of Article 14 and the procedure prescribed by law must be just fair and reasonable."
Specific Applications of Right to be Heard u/Art. 14
CANTONMENT BOARD
Respondent adversely affected by cancellation of permission given to her neighbour — entitled to be heard
"Audi alteram partem is a part of Art. 14"
No order shall be passed at the back of a person when it entails civil consequences
Sec. 269UB Income Tax — C.B. Gautam
Compulsory purchase of property:
Before order u/s 269UD — Intending purchaser AND seller must be given reasonable opportunity of showing cause
Opportunity must be read into the provision
SUSPENSION — Jan Mohd.
Suspension pending inquiry: NOT punitive
Hearing NOT required before suspension
BUT hearing required before cancellation (quasi-judicial function)
Two types: (i) Penalty → audi alteram partem MUST; (ii) Interim measure → hearing NOT insisted
💡 GST Critical: Section 83 CGST (provisional attachment) — no pre-attachment hearing required (interim measure). But Section 73/74 adjudication — hearing mandatory. Rule 142 SCN and reply = Art. 14 compliance. Failure to grant hearing before assessment order = void order on Art. 14 grounds.
18.12.2 — Viva Voce Examination: Arbitrariness in Selection
🎤 Viva Voce (Oral Interview) — Arbitrary Power Risk & Judicial Control
Ajay Hasia v Khalid Majid [AIR 1981 SC 487] — Leading Case
Court's Findings — Viva Voce as Selection Tool
VALID USE
Viva voce = supplementary method for assessing personal traits
NOT irrelevant or irrational for selection
Both written + oral are essential features of proper selection
DANGER ZONE
Highly subjective and impressionistic test
Result influenced by many uncertain factors
Susceptible to misuse if:
• Too high % of marks allotted
• Not properly conducted
• Only 2-3 minutes per candidate
Rules Prescribed by Court — Reducing Arbitrariness in Viva Voce
RULE 1
Oral interview must be used ONLY as additional/supplementary test — NOT as exclusive test
(For college admission AND public employment)
RULE 2
Persons of high integrity, calibre and qualification must be appointed to conduct the test
RULE 3 — MARKS CEILING
For college admission / fresh recruitment:
Viva voce marks must NOT exceed 15% of total marks
More than 15% = arbitrary and unreasonable
33.33% allocation in Ajay Hasia = STRUCK DOWN
RULE 4
Test must be conducted fairly — sufficient time per candidate
Questions must be relevant to factors required for selection
Tape-recording of interview suggested
RULE 5
Candidates called for viva voce must NOT exceed twice or thrice the number of vacancies (at highest)
Key Distinction: College Admission vs Service Recruitment
COLLEGE ADMISSION
Viva voce marks must be minimal
Candidate's personality in formative stage, yet to develop
Too early to identify personal qualities
Written test must carry greater weight
SERVICE RECRUITMENT
Higher viva voce % acceptable for senior posts
Mature personality traits are assessable
Interview = only way to judge suitability for responsible positions
E.g., 33.33% upheld for senior state administrative services (Mehmood Alam Tariq)
PURE VIVA VOCE SELECTION
If selection = ONLY viva voce (no written test) → No limit on marks for interview
Ashok Raju [AIR 1995 SC 39]
But: Highly subjective — real risk of misuse
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter XVIII | Discretionary Powers (II)
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, Appellate Authorities and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Dwarka Pd. v State of UP
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1954 SC 227 | Foundational Licensing Case
Key Ratio / Principle
A law conferring arbitrary and unguided powers on the administrative authorities will be invalid under article 19(1)(g). There should be reasonable norms, policy or principles to guide administrative power as well as some procedural safeguards. There was nothing to ensure a proper execution of the power, and no check against injustice resulting from its improper exercise.
When to Use — When challenging show-cause notice, licence suspension, registration cancellation or ITC denial orders where the officer had no objective criteria to apply. Argue: the officer exercised power without norms = arbitrary = invalid u/Art. 19(1)(g).
E.P. Royappa v State of Tamil Nadu
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1974 SC 555 | Bhagwati J. | Landmark on Arbitrariness
Key Ratio / Principle
From a positivistic point of view, equality is antithetic to arbitrariness. Article 14 strikes at arbitrariness in state action and thus ensures fairness and equality of treatment in state action for arbitrary action necessarily involves negation of equality. Where an act is arbitrary, it is implicit in it that it is unequal both according to political logic and constitutional law, and is therefore violative of article 14. An arbitrary decision is per se violative of article 14.
When to Use — When GST order is passed without objective reasons, without following procedure, or with selective targeting. Cite to establish that any arbitrary administrative action = per se violation of Art. 14 = void.
Maneka Gandhi v UOI
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1978 SC 597 | Constitutional Landmark
Key Ratio — Discretion Must Not Be Arbitrary
The absence of arbitrary power is the first postulate of rule of law upon which our whole constitutional edifice is based. In a system governed by Rule of Law, discretion when conferred upon an executive authority must be confined within clearly defined limits... If the discretion is exercised without any principle or without any rule, it is a situation amounting to the antithesis of Rule of Law. Discretion means sound discretion guided by law or governed by known principles or rules, not by whim or fancy or caprice of the authority.
When to Use — When Department officer passes order based on whim, selective reading of facts, or without following prescribed principles. Cite to argue: the officer's "discretion" was no discretion at all — it was arbitrary power = violates Rule of Law.
Prem Chand v UOI
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1981 SC 613 | CrLJ 5 | Externment / Strict Powers Case
Key Ratio / Principle — Standard for Coercive State Action
Externment provisions have to be read strictly. Any police apprehension is not enough. Some ground or other is not adequate. There must be a clear and present danger based upon creditable material which makes the movements and act of the person in question alarming or dangerous or fraught with violence. Natural justice must be fairly complied with.
When to Use — In any case where Department takes coercive/drastic action (search, arrest, attachment, cancellation) citing vague "apprehension" or "suspicion." Cite Prem Chand: mere apprehension is not enough; there must be credible, specific, current material. Natural justice must be fairly observed.
Yick Wo v Hopkins
United States Supreme Court | 118 U.S. 356 (1885) | Applied in India — State of West Bengal v Anwar Ali Sarkar [AIR 1952 SC 75]
Key Ratio — Discriminatory Administration
Though the law itself be fair on its face and impartial in appearance, yet, if it is applied and administered by public authority with an evil eye and an unequal hand, so as practically to make unjust and illegal discriminations between persons in similar circumstances, material to their rights, the denial of equal justice is still within the prohibition of the Constitution.
When to Use — When the same provision is being used against one taxpayer while others committing identical acts are not touched. Cite to argue: selective/discriminatory enforcement = violation of Art. 14 even if the underlying law is valid.
UOI v Tulsiram Patel
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1985 SC 1416 | Natural Justice & Article 14
Key Ratio — Natural Justice as Part of Article 14
The principles of natural justice have thus come to be recognized as being a part of the guarantee contained in article 14 because of the new and dynamic interpretation given by this Court to the concept of equality. Shortly put, the syllogism runs thus: violation of a rule of natural justice results in arbitrariness which is the same as discrimination; where discrimination is the result of state action, it is a violation of article 14: therefore, a violation of a principle of natural justice by a State Action is a violation of article 14. The principles of natural justice are not the creation of article 14. Article 14 is not their begetter but their constitutional guardian.
When to Use — When GST order passed without proper SCN, without opportunity of personal hearing, or without considering the reply. Cite to argue: Violation of natural justice = violation of Art. 14 = order void. Section 75(4) CGST Act codifies this right.
D.K. Yadava v J.M.A. Industries
Supreme Court of India | (1993) 3 SCC 259
Key Ratio — Procedure Must Be Just, Fair and Reasonable
Article 14 has a pervasive processual potency and versatile quality, equalitarian in its soul and allergic to discriminatory dictates. Equality is the antithesis of arbitrariness. It is, thereby, conclusively held by this Court that the principles of natural justice are part of article 14 and the procedure prescribed by law must be just fair and reasonable.
When to Use — In any hearing where procedural fairness is in question. Cite to emphasise that the procedure followed by the officer must itself be just, fair and reasonable — not a rubber stamp exercise.
A.L. Kalra v P&E Corporation of India Ltd.
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1984 SC 1361 | Desai J.
Key Ratio — Misconduct Must Be Defined with Precision
What is unbecoming of a public servant may vary with individuals and expose employees to vagaries of subjective evaluation. Where misconduct when proved entails penal consequences, it is obligatory on the employer to specify and if necessary define it with precision and accuracy so that any ex post facto interpretation of some incident may not be camouflaged as misconduct.
When to Use — When Department recharacterises a business transaction as "fraud," "suppression" or "willful misstatement" without specific predefined criteria. Cite: penal consequences require precise definition of the conduct — vague allegations of mala fide intent = camouflaged arbitrary action.
Shrilekha Vidyarthi v State of Uttar Pradesh
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1991 SC 537 | (1991) 1 SCC 212
Key Ratio — Arbitrariness in Government Action
It is now too well settled that every state action, in order to survive, must not be susceptible to the vice of arbitrariness which is the crux of article 14 of the Constitution and basic to the rule of law, the system which governs us. Arbitrariness is writ large in the impugned circular.
When to Use — Against omnibus orders, circulars or instructions affecting multiple parties without individual application of mind. Cite to argue: blanket coercive action without individual assessment = arbitrariness writ large = Art. 14 violation.
Corporation of Calcutta v Calcutta Tramways Co. Ltd.
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1964 SC 1279
Key Ratio — Making Opinion Conclusive and Non-Justiciable is Invalid
The vice in the provision is that it makes the opinion of the Corporation, howsoever capricious or arbitrary it may be or howsoever unreasonable on the face of it may be, conclusive and non-justiciable.
When to Use — When a GST provision or instruction purports to make the officer's finding/opinion final and non-challengeable. Cite to argue: any provision making administrative opinion conclusive without judicial review = unconstitutional restriction on right to trade.
Ajay Hasia v Khalid Majid
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1981 SC 487 | (1981) 1 SCC 722
Key Ratio — Subjective Tests Must Be Controlled
Having regard to the drawbacks and deficiencies in the oral interview test and the conditions prevailing in the country, particularly when there is deterioration in moral values and corruption and nepotism are very much on the increase, allocation of a high percentage of marks for the oral interview as compared to the marks allocated for the written test, cannot be accepted by the Court as free from the vice of arbitrariness.
When to Use — In GST adjudication context: when the officer's personal assessment (without objective criteria) becomes the decisive factor in denial of ITC, exemption or classification. Subjective satisfaction without objective criteria = arbitrary.
Chandrakant v Jasjit Singh + Hari Chand Sarda v Mizo District Council
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1962 SC 204; AIR 1967 SC 829
Key Ratio — Rejection Must State Reasons + Provide Appeal
If a candidate is found fit under the other rules and has successfully passed the examination, he should only be rejected under a rule which requires the Customs Collector to state his reasons for the rejection and the rules must provide for an appeal against the order. The power conferred on the committee was held to be unbridled and unrestrained — recording reasons for refusal is "hardly a safeguard against an arbitrary refusal" without a provision for appeal.
When to Use — When GST registration is rejected, or ITC claim denied, without stated reasons or without providing appeal mechanism. Cite to argue: reasons alone without appeal = no real safeguard. Both reasons AND appeal right are constitutionally necessary.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter XIX — Discretionary Powers (III)

⚖️ Chapter XIX — Discretionary Powers (III)

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Pass-on route
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
19.1 Norms for Exercise of Discretionary Power
19.2 Exercise of Discretionary Power Obligatory
19.3 Instances
19.4 Courts Do Not Probe Into Merits
19.5 Direction by Court on Exercise of Discretionary Power
19.6 Doctrine of Ultra Vires
19.6.1 Substantive Ultra Vires
19.6.2 Procedural Ultra Vires
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
19.1 — Norms for the Exercise of Discretionary Powers
⚖️ Judicial Control of Discretionary Powers
Chapter XIX — Principles of Administrative Law
📝 This chapter is about the second stage: how courts control the way authorities actually use their discretion. It's not about whether discretion was delegated — it's about whether the authority used it properly.
⚖️ Pattern of Judicial Control — Reconciling Two Conflicting Values
VALUE 1
Legislature placed trust in the judgment of the authority → Courts not given appellate power
VALUE 2
To preserve democratic values & Rule of Law → authority must act within bounds of law
Legislature cannot be the final judge of its own powers
RESULT
Courts have role to keep authority within bounds of law
Interaction of these two values determines scope of judicial review
📝 Courts don't run the government — they referee it. An authority can be forced to exercise its power but not forced to exercise it in a particular way.
🚫 There is NOTHING Like Absolute/Unfettered Discretion
ABSOLUTE PRINCIPLE
However broadly the statute phrases the discretionary power — it can NEVER be completely uncontrolled.
(All courts from Coke 1598 to Supreme Court of India have affirmed this)
📝 Even if a law says an authority can do something "as it deems fit" — that doesn't mean it can do anything it likes. Every power is finite and purposive.
💡 Rooke's Case (1598): Commissioners had unlimited power to levy charges — but the Court disallowed a charge that threw the whole burden on one owner instead of distributing it equitably. "Discretion is a science to discern truth — not to act on private affections."
📋 Norms the Administration Must Follow in Exercising Discretion
✓ Act within scope of statutory powers
✓ Act in good faith (bona fide)
✓ Act reasonably and objectively
✓ Consider all relevant factors
✗ Do NOT consider irrelevant factors
✗ Do NOT act arbitrarily or capriciously
✗ Do NOT promote purposes alien to the legislation
✗ Do NOT act under dictation of another body
🔍 Wednesbury Principle — Test of Reasonableness
WEDNESBURY TEST
Discretion cannot be exercised in a manner so devoid of plausible justification that no authority having reasonable persons could have taken the said decision.
Court must check: Did the administrator leave out relevant factors OR take into account irrelevant factors?
📝 Named after the Wednesbury Corporation case in England. In India too, a decision must be within the four corners of the law, and not one which no sensible person could have reasonably arrived at. The court does not ask "was this the best decision?" — only "was this a decision that could reasonably be made?"
💡 Warrington LJ's "Red Hair Teacher" example: A teacher dismissed because she had red hair — this is so unreasonable that it is Wednesbury unreasonable. It also takes into consideration extraneous matters.
Discretion Always Coupled with Duty
Every power vested in a public authority is coupled with a duty to exercise it.
Authority cannot refuse to exercise power at will or pleasure.
Courts can compel exercise of discretion — but NOT compel it in a particular manner.
📝 Having discretion doesn't mean you can sit on it forever. The authority must exercise it. But the court can only say "decide" — not "decide this way."
19.2 — Exercise of Discretionary Power Obligatory
⚡ No Body Vested with Power Can Abstain from Exercising It
RULE
Every power vested in a public authority is coupled with a duty to exercise it when an occasion warranting such exercise arises.
The authority in which a discretion is vested can be compelled to exercise that discretion, but NOT to exercise it in any particular manner.
📝 Even a Ministry or Bar Council cannot sit idle when power is conferred. If the Bar Council ignores its duty to discipline advocates, a court can compel it to act — but not tell it what discipline to impose.
EXAMPLE Supreme Court in Ex-Capt. Harish Uppal v UOI — Bar Councils must take disciplinary action when advocates call for strikes. They cannot abstain from exercising their statutory power even if unpopular.
EXAMPLE State Government's refusal to exercise discretion to issue notification abolishing milk tax was held not reasonably exercised — court could compel it to consider exercising the power.
19.3 — Instances of Discretionary Power Exercise
📌 Key Instances Where Courts Have Tested Discretion
Voluntary Retirement
Discretion to accept/reject must be exercised reasonably & judiciously — not ignored for 2+ months & then used as weapon
💡 Assam Industrial Dev. Corp. officer: VRS ignored 2 months, then show cause issued for political activity. Court held: employee deemed voluntarily retired from date of application.
Transfer
Discretionary power should not be exercised in frequent/routine manner — sparingly, in public interest or administrative exigency
Public Interest
Discretion exercisable in public interest must be for everyone in the area — not for a few particular persons
💡 Kerala State Beverages case: Government's shift from regularising daily workers to fresh selection justified — counterveiling interest of larger pool of unemployed youth could not be ignored.
Grant of Largesse
Must be open, fair, honest, completely above board
Allocation of land/grant/permit: no favouritism or nepotism
Contracts
Even in commercial contracts with revenue augmentation element — must follow norms prescribed by Courts
Doctrine of 'executive necessity' does not justify ignoring procurement norms
💡 Rashmi Metaliks v KMDA: Income tax return filing in tender — held collateral term, not essential term. Tenderer given benefit of doubt, lower financial bid noted in final analysis.
KEY PRINCIPLE — De Smith
"The authority in which a discretion is vested can be compelled to exercise that discretion, but not to exercise it in any particular manner. It must genuinely address itself to the matter before it; must not act under the dictation of another body; must act in good faith; must have regard to all relevant considerations and not be swayed by irrelevant considerations; must not seek to promote purposes alien to the letter or spirit of the legislation."
19.4 — Courts Do Not Probe into the Merits of Exercise of Discretion
🏛️ The Negative Proposition — What Courts Do NOT Do
📝 A court is not an appeal forum on how well an authority used its discretion. It only checks whether the process was legal. It cannot say "you should have decided differently" — only "you did not follow the law in deciding."
✗ COURTS WILL NOT ASK:
Whether the decision should have been taken or not
Whether some other decision ought to have been taken
Whether the decision is correct or proper
Whether grounds are sufficient or adequate for the purpose
Supervisory Capacity — NOT Appellate Capacity
JUDICIAL REVIEW IS
Review of the manner in which the decision was made
📝 Courts ask: "Did the authority follow a fair process and stay within the law?" — not "Did they make the right call?"
NOT AN APPEAL FROM
The decision itself
Courts cannot substitute their wisdom for the authority entrusted by law
SCOPE LIMITED TO
Deficiency in decision-making process — not the decision of the administrator.
High Court cannot impede exercise of discretion by issuance of mandatory order.
"It is not within the purview of a court to substitute a decision taken by a constituted authority simply because the decision sought to be substituted is a better one."
SUBJECTIVE SATISFACTION
Where statute confers power in subjective terms ("if satisfied", "in his opinion") — Courts do not sit as appellate court to reappraise the facts. However, opinion must be formed honestly and after applying mind to relevant materials.
The words "reason to believe" do not confer purely subjective power — existence of reasonable grounds is an objective limitation.
📝 Even "subjective satisfaction" requires some material basis. Courts won't question what conclusion the authority reached — but they will check whether there was any credible material to reach any conclusion at all.
💡 Nakkuda Ali v Jayaratne (Privy Council): Controller's belief that a textile dealer was unfit was subjective — BUT existence of reasonable grounds on which that belief could be founded was objective and a limitation on power.
19.5 — Direction by Court on Exercise of Discretionary Power
🧭 What Can Courts Direct — and What They Cannot
✓ CAN DIRECT
Court CAN direct authority to exercise its discretion in accordance with law
Court CAN quash illegal decision and ask authority to re-decide
📝 If a permit was given for 1 year but statute required 3–5 years, court directs re-exercise within 3–5 year range. Court doesn't pick the number.
✗ CANNOT DIRECT
Court CANNOT direct authority to exercise discretion in a particular manner
Court CANNOT take over the decision-making power of the statutory authority
📝 Courts are referees, not players. Once they quash a decision, the ball goes back to the authority to play again — lawfully.
EXCEPTION — Very Rare
Court may take over decision-making power only in exceptional circumstances — such as when proved external/internal pressure was wielded on the authority making independent decision impossible.
Reasons for disabling the authority must be stated and cogent.
"This must only be in exceptional circumstances and not as a routine."
CASE D.N. Jeevaraj v Chief Secretary, Govt. of Karnataka (2016): High Court had virtually taken over BDA's function. Supreme Court set aside — court had not given reasons for disabling statutory authority.
19.6 — Doctrine of Ultra Vires
🔒 Doctrine of Ultra Vires — "Beyond the Powers"
Basic tool of courts to control discretionary power
📝 Ultra vires literally means "beyond the powers." A body has no power except what the statute gives it. If it acts beyond that — the action is void. This is the most fundamental concept in Administrative Law.
Ultra Vires Has TWO Aspects
1. Substantive Ultra Vires
Decision-maker acted beyond the substantive power conferred by statute
→ See 19.6.1
2. Procedural Ultra Vires
Decision-maker failed to follow mandatory procedural requirements
→ See 19.6.2
CONSTITUTIONAL ANCHOR
An absolute discretion is anti-democratic and may lead to arbitrary decisions — abuse of power violates Rule of Law.
Krishna Iyer J (SC): "Absolute power is anathema under our constitutional order; naked and arbitrary exercise of power is bad in law."
Khudiram (SC): "There is nothing like unfettered discretion immune from judicial reviewability."
ORDER WITHOUT AUTHORITY
An order passed by an officer having no authority of law has no effect. It neither creates any right in favour of a party nor imposes any obligation on the opposite party.
19.6.1 — Substantive Ultra Vires
⚡ Substantive Ultra Vires — Decision-Maker Lacked the Power
📝 This happens when an authority acts outside the scope of what the statute allows. It's not about how power was used — but whether the authority had the power at all. Even a statutory body with wide powers cannot wander outside its defined legal boundary.
When Does Substantive Ultra Vires Apply?
Authority acts in excess of its statutory powers
Condition precedent for exercise of power not fulfilled
Action taken by wrong authority (power sub-delegated without legal basis)
Statutory body exercises a power not conferred on it expressly or by necessary implication
📌 Illustrative Cases
CASE
Marathwada University v S.B.R. Chavan: V-C's power to "regulate work and conduct" of officers did NOT include power of removal → dismissal ultra vires
CASE
State of Gujarat v Krishna Cinema: Government cannot refuse no-objection certificate on ground that building was constructed before applying — that ground was outside the permitted reasons
CASE
Naraindas v State of MP: Board of Secondary Education prescribed textbooks — power belonged to State Government, not the Board. Board being creature of statute could only exercise powers expressly invested in it
CASE
D.V. Capoor v UOI: President withheld pension — condition precedent (grave misconduct finding) not proved → order illegal and in excess of jurisdiction
IMPLIED POWER
Power to appoint includes power to dismiss. Power to grant permission includes power to cancel in appropriate circumstances — even without specific provision
📝 Some powers are implied by necessity — e.g., examination board can cancel exam if unfair means used, even if statute doesn't say so. "Unwritten rule" of fair examination.
SETTLED PRINCIPLE
When a statute designates a specific body to exercise a power, it must be exercised only by that body. It cannot be exercised by any other body unless the power is sub-delegated.
A statutory body cannot travel beyond the power conferred on it by law. Any action without power has no legal validity and it is void ab initio.
19.6.2 — Procedural Ultra Vires
📋 Procedural Ultra Vires — "Where a power is given to do a thing in a certain way, the thing must be done in that way or not at all."
Nazir Ahmad v King Emperor (Privy Council) — Lord Roche
📝 If the law prescribes a procedure for using a power — you must follow that procedure exactly. Ignoring mandatory steps voids the action. This is different from acting outside the substantive power: here you had the power, but used it the wrong way.
19.6.2.1 — Mandatory vs Directory Requirements
MANDATORY
Non-compliance renders the decision ultra vires
→ Action void
📝 Like a deadline. Miss it and the action simply has no legal effect.
DIRECTORY
Non-compliance is only an irregularity — does NOT vitiate the decision
→ But must show actual prejudice
📝 Like a guideline. Bending it doesn't automatically void the action — but if it causes real harm, it may still be set aside.
TEST — Dalchand v Municipal Corporation
"There are no ready tests. The broad purpose of the statute is important. The object of the particular provision must be considered. The weighing of the consequence of holding a provision to be mandatory or directory is vital and, more often than not, determinative of the very question."

If enforcement literally would defeat the design of the statute → provision is directory (need prejudice also).
If enforcement is needed to protect rights of persons → provision is mandatory.
Procedural Requirements Generally Held MANDATORY
19.6.2.2 Consultation
Consultation with specified authority must be full, real and effective — not merely formal or unproductive
Must cover essential points and core of subject
💡 Narayanan v State of Kerala: Electricity licence revocation required consultation with Electricity Board AFTER receiving licensee's representation — consulting before was insufficient.
19.6.2.3 Publication
Section 4 notification under Land Acquisition Act must be: (1) published in Official Gazette AND (2) gist notified in local area — BOTH mandatory
Failure vitiates notification and ALL subsequent proceedings
19.6.2.4 Notice
Requirement to give notice to affected person is mandatory
Person being deprived of property must be given time to vacate
Requirement to Record Reasons
Where statute requires reasons to be recorded before taking administrative action → mandatory
📝 Absence of reasons = no way to know if discretion was exercised lawfully. Courts can quash orders with no reasons or non-speaking orders where reasons are required.
Inquiry
Where statute requires holding an inquiry before action → mandatory; skipping it voids the action
📌 Consultation — Key Rulings
Consultation with Chairman of Board ≠ Consultation with the Board itself
Naraindas v State of MP
Article 320(3)(c): PSC must be consulted on disciplinary matters — but held directory in Manbodhan Lal Srivastava as PSC opinion not binding on government
📝 Exceptional case where consultation held directory because PSC's opinion was not binding — so non-compliance cannot nullify final order. Does not affect the general rule.
Transfer of High Court judges under Art. 222 — consultation with Chief Justice of India is condition precedent and must be "real, full and effective"
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter XIX | Discretionary Powers (III)
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Khudiram v State of West Bengal
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1975 SC 550
Key Ratio
"There is nothing like unfettered discretion immune from judicial reviewability. The truth is that in a Government under law, there can be no such thing as unreviewable discretion."
When to Use — When Department or officer claims their satisfaction / opinion under a statute is final and unchallengeable. Cite to establish that no discretion is beyond review.
Nazir Ahmad v King Emperor
Privy Council | AIR 1936 PC 253 — Lord Roche
Key Ratio
"Where a power is given to do a certain thing in a certain way, the thing must be done in that way or not at all."
When to Use — When challenging any order where mandatory procedural requirements (consultation, notice, publication, inquiry, reasons) were not followed. Cite to establish that the order is void ab initio for procedural ultra vires.
Wednesbury Principle — Associated Provincial Picture Houses Ltd. v Wednesbury Corporation
Court of Appeal, England | (1948) 1 KB 223 — Lord Greene MR | Applied consistently by Supreme Court of India
Key Ratio
"A person entrusted with a discretion must direct himself properly in law. He must call his own attention to the matters which he is bound to consider. He must exclude from his consideration matters which are irrelevant to what he has to consider. If he does not obey those rules, he may truly be said, and often is said, to be acting 'unreasonably'."
When to Use — When challenging an order where authority ignored relevant material (ITC denial without examining books, arbitrary rejection without applying mind). Cite to show Wednesbury unreasonableness.
Bangalore Medical Trust v B.S. Muddappa
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1991 SC 1902 : (1991) 4 SCC 54
Key Ratio
The action or decision must not only be reached reasonably and intelligibly but it must be related to the purpose for which power is exercised. Even where statutes are silent and only power is conferred to act in one or the other manner, the authority cannot act whimsically or arbitrarily. It should be guided by reasonableness and fairness. The legislature never intended authorities to abuse the law or use it unfairly.
When to Use — In challenges to tax notices, orders, or assessments where the officer's action is disconnected from the stated purpose, or where no relevant material was applied to mind.
United States v Wunderlich
US Supreme Court | 342 US 98 (1951) — Justice Douglas
Key Ratio
"Law has reached its finest moments when it has freed man from the unlimited discretion of some ruler, some... official, some bureaucrat.... Absolute discretion is a ruthless master. It is more destructive of freedom than any of man's other inventions."
When to Use — As an opening statement in oral hearings where the officer claims unreviewable absolute power. Also used by Indian Supreme Court as a rhetorical anchor for the no-absolute-discretion principle.
Nakkuda Ali v Jayaratne
Privy Council | (1952) AC 66
Key Ratio on "Reason to Believe"
Even where an officer can act if he has "reason to believe" — this does not confer a purely subjective power. There must in fact exist reasonable grounds known to the officer before he can validly exercise the power. The belief may be subjective, but existence of reasonable grounds on which the belief could be founded is objective and a limitation on his power.
When to Use — When challenging searches, seizures, arrests, or assessments under GST/Income Tax where the officer claims "reason to believe" without disclosing any material. Cite to show existence of grounds is an objective pre-condition.
Narayanan v State of Kerala
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1974 SC 175 : (1974) 1 SCC 68
Key Ratio on Consultation
Consultation with the specified authority (Electricity Board) was a condition precedent for exercising the power of revocation of licence and was mandatory. The Board must be consulted after receipt of the licensee's representation so that the Board could consider the same before giving its advice. Consulting before receipt of representation would not fulfil the requirements.
When to Use — Where a show cause notice or adverse order was issued without seeking the mandatory consultation / opinion required by statute, or where consultation was token rather than real. Cite to argue the entire process was vitiated.
Rooke's Case / Coke's Words (1598) — Applied in All Indian Courts
(1598) 5 Co. Rep. 99b | Repeatedly applied and affirmed by Supreme Court of India
Key Ratio — The Oldest Statement on Discretion
"Notwithstanding the words of the commission give authority to the commissioners to do according to their discretions, yet their proceedings ought to be limited and bound with the rule of reason and law. For discretion is a science or understanding to discern between falsity and truth, between wrong and right... and not to do according to their wills and private affections; for as one sayeth, talis discretio discretionem confundit."
When to Use — As a foundational statement in any challenge to arbitrary exercise of power. Translates to: "Such a discretion destroys discretion." Use when discretion was exercised for private/personal/biased reasons.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter XIX — Discretionary Powers (III)

⚖️ Chapter XIX — Discretionary Powers (III)

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Mandatory Complied
Prohibited / Invalid / Vitiated
Condition / Unless / If
Optional / Directory / Exception
Neutral Statutory Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example / Case
📋 Index of Sections
19.6.2.5 Inquiry
19.6.2.6 Reasons — General Rule
19.6.2.6A Reasons — Recording & Communication
19.6.2.7 Miscellaneous Situations
19.6.2.8 Preventive Detention
19.7 Extended Ultra Vires — Doctrine & Grounds
19.7.1 Wrong Provision of Law
19.8.1 Political Questions
19.8.2 Prerogative Powers
19.8.3 Non-Statutory Powers
19.8.4 Policies — Judicial Review
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
19.6.2.5 — Inquiry (Pre-decisional)
🔍 Pre-Decisional Inquiry — Mandatory Procedural Safeguard
📝 Before making certain decisions, the law may require the authority to hold an inquiry first. If it skips this inquiry and decides anyway, the decision is invalid — it cannot be saved by saying the outcome would have been the same.
GENERAL RULE
Where a statutory provision requires the authority to hold an inquiry before taking a specified decision → Holding such inquiry is MANDATORY
PURPOSE
Enables objectors to have their say & allows authority to collect relevant materials for a right decision
📝 Two aims: let people object, and let the authority gather facts before deciding. Both together prevent arbitrary action.
CONSEQUENCE
Decision/order made without such inquiry → invariably invalid
💡 Kapur Chand v ITC (AIR 1981 SC): ITO passed order without inquiry required under S.25A of Income-tax Act → SC quashed it.
Key Case Illustrations
LAND ACQUISITION
§5A — Collector must hear objectors before acquiring property. This duty is mandatory. Collector cannot leave matter to government without making recommendation — even if government has final say.
📝 Even if the collector's recommendation is non-binding on the government, the law still requires the collector to hear objections and make a recommendation. The government's final power doesn't excuse the collector.
💡 Shri Mandir Sita Ramji v Lt. Governor of Delhi (AIR 1974 SC): Acquisition quashed. Collector directed to re-hear objections and recommend to government.
MOTOR VEHICLES ACT §43
Before issuing directions to State Transport Authority, draft must be published + objections heard from "representatives of interests affected" — includes general public, not just vested/pressure groups
💡 Akhil Bharatiya Grahak Panchayat (Bombay) v State (AIR 1985 Bom): Court held general public can file objections in the name of public interest — "interests affected" is wide.
GST / TAX APPLICATION
Where statute prescribes pre-decisional inquiry before passing adjudication/assessment order, an order passed without following such procedure is invalid and liable to be quashed on writ. This applies equally to SCN adjudication hearings, anti-profiteering inquiries, and GST audit proceedings.
19.6.2.6 — Duty to Give Reasons (General Rule)
📋 Statutory Requirement to Give Reasons — Mandatory in Public Interest
📝 When a law says the authority must state reasons for its action, this is not a formality — it is a real obligation. Without reasons, the action is invalid. Reasons kept in secret files are not enough — they must be communicated.
Why is the Duty to Give Reasons Mandatory?
Avoids arbitrary or capricious exercise of discretion
Excludes discrimination and extraneous considerations
Enables affected party to effectively challenge the order in court
✗ NOT VALID
Grounds implied from the order but not expressly stated
Reasons stored only in government files — not communicated
Merely repeating statutory language without actual grounds
📝 Three traps: implied reasons, file-only reasons, copy-paste of statute. None of these satisfy the legal duty to give reasons.
MINORITY VIEW (OUTDATED)
Some cases held duty to give reasons is merely directory — omission is irregularity only
BUT This view does NOT represent good law in modern times
KEY QUOTE — Rajamallaiah v Anil Kishore (AIR 1980 SC)
"Generally one may say that a provision which insists upon recording of reasons before an action is taken must prima facie be considered to be mandatory, as it is aimed at preventing arbitrariness. Where the rights of citizens are involved there can be no question that such a provision should be regarded as mandatory. Where the provision involves the public interest also the provision must be regarded as mandatory."
📝 The Supreme Court laid down a two-step test: (i) If citizens' rights are at stake — always mandatory. (ii) If public interest is involved — also mandatory. It is hard to find cases where neither applies.
⚠️ RECORDING EX POST FACTO = INVALID
Reasons recorded after the action is taken (ex post facto) do not satisfy the statutory requirement. Reasons must be recorded before and not after the action is taken.

"The object of the insistence upon the recording of reasons is to eliminate arbitrariness. Reasons, when given, substitute objectivity for subjectivity. When reasons are set down in writing greater thought goes into it and greater objectivity is attained."
📝 Ex post facto = After the fact. Writing reasons after deciding is like writing your working after copying someone's answer — it doesn't prove you actually thought it through before deciding.
💡 In GST adjudication: Where an adjudication order is signed first and reasons are filled in later, the order is bad in law and courts will quash it. The officer must apply mind and record reasons BEFORE signing the order.
19.6.2.6A — Recording & Communication of Reasons (Case Law)
📨 Must Reasons Be Communicated? — Ajantha View vs Satyavir View
AJANTHA VIEW — PREFERRED
Ajantha Industries v CBDT (AIR 1976 SC)
S.127 Income-tax Act — Board transferred assessee's case. No reasons given. SC quashed.
Reasons must be:
(i) Recorded before the order
(ii) Communicated to assessee — mere file recording not enough
📝 The assessee has no remedy except writ under Art.226. So unless reasons are communicated, he cannot even challenge the order — defeating the entire purpose of the right.
SATYAVIR VIEW — LUKEWARM
Satyavir Singh v UOI (AIR 1986 SC)
Art.311(2) — dismissal without inquiry. Court held reasons must be recorded before order but was not emphatic about communication
Advisable to record in final order to avoid fabrication allegation but not strictly mandatory to communicate
SETTLED — GAUTAM CASE CONFIRMS AJANTHA
C.B. Gautam v UOI (1993) 1 SCC 78 — S.269UD(1) IT Act: reasons must be in order served on party. Court: "It is inconceivable that the order required to be served would not contain reasons. The order would be incomplete unless reasons are incorporated therein or served separately. We are of the view that the reasons for the order must be communicated to the affected party."
📝 The Gautam case settled the debate: Ajantha wins. Reasons must be communicated. The affected party must know WHY the order was passed — both to challenge it and to deter arbitrary action.
TWO-FOLD PURPOSE OF COMMUNICATING REASONS (per Gautam SC)
(1) Aggrieved party can challenge the reasons before court as erroneous, irrational or irrelevant
(2) Operates as a deterrent against arbitrary action
Important Case Studies on Reasons
CaseFact SituationHeld
State of UP v Lalai Singh (AIR 1977 SC) Govt forfeited publication under §99A CrPC; grounds not expressly stated, only implied in appendix Order invalidated. Formal authoritative statement of grounds is statutorily mandatory. Grounds cannot be merely implied. If grounds not stated — right of appeal to HC is defeated.
Narayan Das v State of MP (AIR 1972 SC) Book forfeited under §4(1) Criminal Law Amendment Act; Govt merely repeated statutory language as reason Order quashed. Merely repeating statutory language is not enough. Actual grounds on which opinion is formed must be stated.
UOI v H.P. Chothia (AIR 1978 SC) Selection board did not give reasons for not selecting respondent for State Forest Service Selections vitiated. Requirement of giving reasons is not an idle formality — it is mandatory to prevent arbitrary/hostile discrimination and to enable UPSC to know the board's views.
Laxmibai v Collector (2020) 12 SCC 186 Disqualification of election candidate for failure to submit election expenses — requires reasoned order by Election Commission. "May disqualify" ≠ "shall disqualify". Reasons why candidate failed to file expenses are relevant to type of punishment. Election Commission has discretion — must record satisfaction and pass reasoned order.
Rajamallaiah v Anil Kishore (AIR 1980 SC) Auctioning authority changed entire auction scheme without recording reasons in writing as required by rules Action quashed. Recording of reasons is condition precedent to taking action. Ex post facto recording = not compliant. No words in action.
GST APPLICATION Adjudication orders, appellate orders (FAA/GSTAT), anti-profiteering orders — all must contain reasons. A template order with no reasoning on specific facts is bad in law and challengeable by writ. The officer cannot later supply reasons in a counter-affidavit before the court that were absent in the original order.
19.6.2.7 — Miscellaneous Procedural Situations
📌 Prescribed Procedure — Mandatory or Directory? Miscellaneous Rules
APPOINTMENT WITHOUT TEST
Selection for post requiring qualifying written test — made only on oral interview = QUASHED
WRONG COMMITTEE
Teacher selected by committee without VC's nominee, Edu. Director nominee & expert — NOT VALID (Bombay University directive)
TIME-LIMIT FOR APPLICATIONS
Authority has no power to consider applications beyond prescribed time-limit — unless statute separately permits considering unqualified applicants
📝 If the law says apply by X date, the authority cannot accept late applications. But if the mode of posting is not prescribed, postal delay doesn't defeat an application posted on time.
REASONS EVEN WITHOUT STATUTE
Consumer Action Group v State of TN (AIR 2000 SC): Even if statute confers wide discretion and is silent about reasons — reasons must be recorded for exercising discretionary power
📝 Silence of the statute does not excuse the authority from giving reasons when it exercises wide discretion. The constitutional requirement of non-arbitrariness (Art.14) fills the gap.
💡 Kerala Abkari shop shift permission granted without satisfying conditions precedent = without authority. Conditions precedent must be satisfied before exercising power.
SUBSTANCE OVER FORM — RESERVE BANK CASE
Burn Standard Co. v McDermott International (AIR 1991 SC): Company challenged permission granted by RBI on ground that application was not on prescribed form. SC rejected: "We are concerned with the factum of permission and not the procedure followed by the RBI for granting the same. The prescription of the form is merely to aid the RBI to process the application for permission. Emphasis must be laid on substance and not on mere form."
📝 Form-filing is an administrative convenience, not a substantive right of the other party. If permission was actually granted and the purpose was served, a technical defect in form is not fatal — substance prevails over form in procedural matters.
19.6.2.8 — Preventive Detention — Procedural Safeguards (Strictly Mandatory)
🔒 Preventive Detention — All Procedural Safeguards Are Mandatory
📝 Preventive detention means locking someone up without a trial — to PREVENT future crime, not punish past crime. Because it takes away personal liberty without conviction, courts treat every procedural safeguard as mandatory and apply them strictly. Even minor lapses in procedure = detention quashed.
Core Constitutional Safeguards (Article 22 — All Mandatory)
ART.22(5)
Detenu must be given grounds of detention — at earliest opportunity
(Any delay in communicating grounds = detention bad)
REPRESENTATION
Detenu has right to make representation.
Government MUST consider representation itself before forwarding to advisory board.
"Consideration by the Board is an additional safeguard — NOT a substitute for government consideration."
📝 Even if the board considers it, the government must also independently apply its mind. If the government rubber-stamps the board's recommendation without itself considering the representation — detention is bad.
ADVISORY BOARD
Three-member board required → two-member board = not properly constituted
Board MUST hear detenu in person if he so desires = peremptory requirement
NATIONAL SECURITY ACT §8
Grounds must be communicated within five days.
Any delay in communicating grounds = detention bad.
Officers must act with utmost expedition.
Stale Incidents — Preventive Detention Based on Old Events
⚠️ STALE INCIDENTS DOCTRINE
Detention order based on incidents too far in the past (with no live/proximate link to present threat) = order of punishment for crime without trial — NOT preventive detention.

Sama Aruna v State of Telangana (2018) 12 SCC 150: Detenu charged for offences in 2002-07; arrested 2016; detention order based on events 2-3 years before order. Quashed.

SC held: "The detention order must be based on a reasonable prognosis of the future behaviour of a person based on his past conduct in light of the surrounding circumstances. The live and proximate link that must exist between the past conduct of a person and the imperative need to detain him must be taken to have been snapped."
📝 Preventive detention is about preventing future danger. If the past events are so old that they cannot reasonably predict a future threat, the detention is just punishment in disguise — and that is unconstitutional without a trial.
JUDICIAL APPROACH — STRINGENT REVIEW
Courts apply principles of Administrative Law to preventive detention more rigorously because personal freedom is at stake.
Even though courts don't probe merits, they check:
(i) Relevancy and proximity of grounds → challengeable (Vijay Narain Singh SC 1984)
(ii) Adequacy and sufficiency → NOT challengeable (subjective satisfaction of authority)
📝 Two separate tests: Courts can ask "Is this ground relevant and recent?" — Yes. But courts cannot ask "Was this ground enough to justify detention?" — No (that's the detaining authority's subjective call). The distinction is critical for framing writ petitions against detention orders.
MANDATORY VS DIRECTORY — BREAKING DOWN
Of late, the mandatory-directory dichotomy in procedure is being criticised. Lord Hailsham's spectrum theory (cited with approval by SC in Pankaj Bhargava v Mohinder Nath AIR 1991 SC):
"Not so much a stark choice of alternatives but really a spectrum of possible procedural defects ranging in the degree of seriousness."
At one end — flagrant defiance of procedure; at other end — trivial defect that may be ignored.
19.7 — Extended Ultra Vires — Doctrine & Grounds of Invalidity
⚖️ Extended Ultra Vires — Judicial Control Over Discretionary Power
📝 Ultra vires traditionally means "beyond the power given by law." Courts expanded this concept to cover not just absence of power but also how power is used — because legislatures give broad discretion without guidelines. Courts fill this vacuum by implying limits. An act can be ultra vires not because there was no power, but because of the way it was done.
WHY EXTENDED DOCTRINE NEEDED
Legislatures confer broad discretionary powers without prescribing standards. Traditional ultra vires (literal interpretation of statute) cannot control misuse of such open-ended powers. Courts developed extended doctrine to ensure:
(i) Executive cannot be sole judge of extent of its own power (= negation of Rule of Law)
(ii) Legislature cannot have intended to authorise misuse of power or unreasonable action
📝 The reasoning: Parliament gives discretion to be exercised properly. If Parliament wanted to authorise arbitrary action, it would have said so. Since it didn't, courts imply reasonable limits. Power is never absolute in India.
Two Major Categories of Grounds — Discretionary Decision Invalidated
CATEGORY 1 — ABUSE OF POWER
• Mala fides / Bad faith
• Improper purpose
• Irrelevant/extraneous considerations taken into account
• Relevant considerations NOT taken into account
• Colourable exercise of power
• Unreasonableness
• Misdirection of law and fact
📝 Mala fides = bad faith / dishonest motive. Colourable exercise = using power for A to achieve B (what you can't do directly, you can't do indirectly).
CATEGORY 2 — FAILURE TO EXERCISE DISCRETION
• Acting mechanically (no application of mind)
• Acting under dictation (surrendering discretion to another)
• Fettering discretion (self-binding by rigid policy without exceptions)
📝 Fettering discretion = adopting a rule so rigid that you refuse to hear even exceptional cases. You can have a policy but must be willing to hear any case that's exceptional.
IMPORTANT — GROUNDS OVERLAP
The line of demarcation between these grounds is NOT sharp but blurred. In one fact-situation, multiple grounds may apply simultaneously. Classic illustration (English case): "The example of the red-haired teacher, dismissed because she had red hair. This is unreasonable in one sense. In another sense, it is taking into consideration extraneous matters. It is also so unreasonable that it might almost be described as having been done in bad faith."
SC IN CIT v MAHINDRA & MAHINDRA (AIR 1984 SC)
"Indisputably, it is a settled position that if the action or decision is perverse or is such that no reasonable body of persons, properly informed could come to, or has been arrived at by the authority misdirecting itself by adopting a wrong approach or has been influenced by irrelevant or extraneous matter, the Court would be justified in interfering with the same."
LORD DIPLOCK'S THREE-FOLD CLASSIFICATION (GCHQ Case 1984)
(i) Illegality — decision-maker made error of law; did not understand/give effect to the law
(ii) Irrationality — Wednesbury unreasonableness
(iii) Procedural Impropriety — failure to observe procedural rules / natural justice

These are grounds of judicial review. Judicial review is NOT STATIC — further grounds may develop in course of time.
CRITICAL LIMITATION — COURTS DO NOT SIT IN APPEAL
Courts examine the decision-making process — NOT the merits of the decision.
Court cannot substitute its decision for that of the administrator.
Even if another decision would have been better, wiser or fairer — court will not interfere unless a justiciable flaw exists in the process of making the decision.
📝 The difference between appeal and judicial review: In appeal, you can say "wrong decision." In judicial review, you can only say "wrong process." If the process was fair, the decision stands — even if the court disagrees with it.
19.7.1 — Mentioning a Wrong Provision of Law
📌 Wrong / Missing Statutory Reference — Does it Invalidate Order?
📝 Sometimes an order or notice cites the wrong section of law. Does that automatically make the order bad? The answer depends on whether power actually existed under some provision and whether the two provisions operate in different fields requiring different conditions.
DOES NOT INVALIDATE — IF
• Power actually exists under any provision of law
• Two sources of power exist — one may be traced to another
• Non-reference to statutory provision with actual compliance — legal effect preserved
💡 GST context: An order citing wrong sub-section but correct section, where power exists under the correct sub-section too, may survive — if all pre-conditions of the correct sub-section are met.
DOES INVALIDATE — IF
• Two statutory provisions operate in two different fields requiring compliance with different pre-requisites
• Wrong provision cited AND the correct provision has different conditions that were not met
💡 If an SCN cites §73 (non-fraud) but the case requires §74 (fraud) with its different limitation and penalty provisions — citing wrong provision and proceeding on §73 standards when §74 applies = bad order.
KEY PRINCIPLE — Chandra Singh v State of Rajasthan (AIR 2003 SC)
Mentioning wrong provision or omission to mention correct provision would not invalidate an order so long as the power exists under any provision of law — but this principle would not apply where the two statutory provisions operate in two different fields requiring compliance with different pre-requisites.
19.8.1 — Political Questions — Justiciability & Limits
🏛️ Political Questions — Can Courts Review Them?
📝 The political question doctrine says courts should not decide purely political matters. But in India with a written Constitution and fundamental rights, nearly every government decision has legal dimensions. Courts have been cautious but have developed a nuanced position: purely political ≠ beyond review; mala fide political action = reviewable.
COURTS WILL NOT REVIEW
Purely political question not involving legal/constitutional rights
Adequacy of grounds for President's subjective satisfaction
Correctness of policy decisions on merits
(Appeal to polls, not courts — Bhut Nath v State of WB AIR 1974 SC)
COURTS WILL REVIEW
Any discretionary decision (including President's) if:
• Mala fides / bad faith
• Based wholly on extraneous or irrelevant grounds
• No satisfaction at all (vs. improper satisfaction)
(S.R. Bommai v UOI AIR 1994 SC — Art.356 proclamation)
📝 Difference between "satisfaction being improper" (not reviewable) vs "NO satisfaction at all" (reviewable). If there was some subjective satisfaction — courts won't probe its correctness. If there was simply nothing — courts can step in.
BHAGWATI J IN STATE OF RAJASTHAN v UOI (AIR 1977 SC)
"It is true that if a question brought before the court is purely a political question not involving determination of any legal or constitutional right or objection, the court will not entertain it... But merely because a question has a political complexion, that by itself is no ground why the court should shrink from performing its duty under the Constitution if it raises an issue of constitutional determination."
HIGH STATE POLICY — NON-JUSTICIABLE WITHOUT MALA FIDES
• Assent of President to bills — not justiciable
• Reason why Bill reserved by Governor under Art.200 — courts will not enquire
• International treaties / cession of territory — courts slow to interfere
• But: High office power must be exercised for public good only — not personal benefit
19.8.2 — Prerogative Powers — Judicial Review
👑 Prerogative Powers — Evolving from Immunity to Reviewability
📝 Prerogative powers are powers the Crown (in Britain) or executive (in India) exercises as common law powers, not under any statute. Historically they were immune from judicial review. Courts have now moved to reviewing them like any discretionary power — based on subject matter, not source.
STILL NOT REVIEWABLE
• High political overtones — treaty-making, defence policy, dissolution of Parliament, appointment of ministers
• Not because prerogative — but because they raise political rather than legal questions
NOW REVIEWABLE
Prerogative power that determines an individual's rights or interests is subject to judicial review
Lord Diplock in GCHQ: "I see no reason why simply because a decision-making power is derived from a common law and not a statutory source it should for that reason alone be immune from judicial review."
💡 R. v Secretary of State, ex p. Everett: Refusal of passport (a prerogative act) is judicially reviewable. If refused for bad reason — court can inquire.
LORD SCARMAN IN GCHQ
"Today the controlling factor in determining whether the exercise of prerogative power is subject to judicial review is not its source but its subject-matter."
📝 Subject-matter test: Courts ask "What is this decision about?" — not "Where does this power come from?" If the subject matter affects individual rights, it is reviewable regardless of whether it comes from a statute or the prerogative.
INDIA — PREROGATIVE = CONSTITUTIONAL POWERS
In India most prerogative powers of England are codified in the Constitution. Power to pardon (Art.72/161), dissolution of Parliament, appointment of PM/CM — are constitutional powers, not common law. Indian courts have greater powers than English courts (written Constitution + fundamental rights). Even power to pardon under Art.72/161 is reviewable for mala fides, discrimination or arbitrariness — but not on merits.
19.8.3 — Non-Statutory Powers — Judicial Review
📜 Non-Statutory Discretionary Powers — Equally Subject to Review
📝 Government can do many things without a specific statute — under Articles 73 and 162 of the Constitution which vest executive power in the Centre and States. These non-statutory powers are NOT on a higher pedestal than statutory powers. Courts can review them for arbitrariness.
BASIS Arts.73 & 162 vest general executive power — wider than prerogative powers in Britain
Non-statutory power cannot be placed on a pedestal higher than statutory power — all norms applicable to statutory power apply equally
LESS SCRUTINY — NON-LEGAL FACTORS
Rayalseema Paper Mills v Govt of AP: No law prescribed royalty criteria for forest produce. SC: scope of judicial scrutiny less where no law exists — but ANY arbitrary action still subject to scrutiny
REASONS STILL REQUIRED
Royal Medical Trust v UOI (2017): Debarment from medical college admission (non-statutory) still requires adequate reasons. Court's duty in judicial review is to zealously guard human rights and fundamental rights even over non-statutory powers.
19.8.4 — Judicial Review of Government Policies
📊 Policy Decisions — Limited Judicial Review; Merits Not Reviewable
📝 Government makes policy — courts don't. Judges lack technical expertise in economics, defence, education etc. Courts will examine the process of policy-making (Was it fair? Was law followed? Was it based on relevant facts?) — but will not say "a better policy should have been adopted."
When Can Courts Interfere with Policy?
COURT WILL INTERFERE
• Mala fides
• Unreasonableness / arbitrariness
• Violation of constitutional provisions (Art.14, 19 etc.)
• Violation of statutory law
• Extraneous/irrelevant considerations
• Policy applied in discriminatory manner
COURT WILL NOT INTERFERE
• Mere factual disputes
• Policy not the best/wisest choice
• Court prefers different approach
• Technical/expert matters where judges lack expertise
• Policy hurts business interest (alone)
• Change of policy (even if inconvenient)
KEY PRINCIPLES — POLICY REVIEW
• Policy not immutable — government can change it at any time for just, valid and cogent reasons
• Change must be Wednesbury reasonable and free from arbitrariness, irrationality, bias and malice
• Policy after being made should be made known to concerned people: "What is done in secret is often suspected of being capricious or mala fide"
• Policy change must be made fairly — Art.14 implicit imperative: "If it does change its policy, it must do so fairly and should not give the impression that it is acting by any ulterior criteria or arbitrarily" (Sangwan SC)
• Vested/accrued right cannot be taken away by mere policy amendment
FETTERING DISCRETION — POLICY & ITS LIMITS
Authority CAN adopt policy for exercising discretion — valid approach. BUT must still:
(i) Be willing to hear applicants and consider their case
(ii) Not adopt a rigid rule that refuses ALL applications of a particular character (R. v Port of London Authority, ex p. Kynoch: "Wide distinction to be drawn between having a policy and refusing to hear any application of a particular character")
