(iii) Not fetter its discretion by unlawful policy (R. v Forest Betting Licensing Committee)
📝 Having a policy is fine. Applying the policy without even listening = bad. The authority must always be willing to hear exceptional cases. Only then is the policy lawful.
ECONOMIC & FISCAL POLICY — JUDICIAL RESTRAINT
Bajaj Hindustan v Sir Shaadi Lal Enterprises (2011): "Economic and fiscal regulatory measures are fields where judges should encroach upon very warily as judges are not experts in these matters."

In tax and economic regulation cases, there are good reasons for judicial restraint, if not judicial deference, to judgment of the executive. Courts will not weigh pros and cons of economic policy.
💡 GST rate changes, exemption notifications, circulars on classification — all are policy decisions. Courts will not substitute their view on what rate should apply or what should be exempt. Challenge is permissible only if notification is arbitrary, mala fide, or violates Art.14/19 on its face.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter XIX | Discretionary Powers (III)
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, FAA, GSTAT and High Courts. State them confidently and cite the case name and court.
Rajamallaiah v Anil Kishore
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1980 SC 1502 | Reasons — Mandatory; Recording Ex Post Facto = Invalid
Key Ratio on Recording Reasons Before Action
"Generally one may say that a provision which insists upon recording of reasons before an action is taken must prima facie be considered to be mandatory, as it is aimed at preventing arbitrariness. Where the rights of citizens are involved there can be no question that such a provision should be regarded as mandatory. Where the provision involves the public interest also the provision must be regarded as mandatory."
Further Ratio on Objectivity
"The object of the insistence upon the recording of reasons is to eliminate arbitrariness. Reasons, when given, substitute objectivity for subjectivity. It is common experience that when reasons are set down in writing greater thought goes into it and greater objectivity is attained."
When to Use — When adjudication order, assessment order or appellate order does not contain reasons or reasons were recorded after the order was signed. Cite to show the order is bad in law and liable to be quashed.
Ajantha Industries v Central Board of Direct Taxes
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1976 SC 437 | S.127 Income-tax Act | Reasons Must Be Communicated
Key Ratio
Section 127(1) requires mandatory recording of reasons prior to passing order of transfer. Not only that, these reasons should also be communicated to the assessee concerned. Communication of the order transferring the case is an essential requirement. Mere recording of reasons in the file is not sufficient. The non-communication of the reasons is not saved by showing that the reasons exist in the file. The assessee has no other remedy against an order of transfer except approaching the High Court in its writ jurisdiction under article 226 — and therefore the communication of reasons to him becomes essential so as to enable him to seek his remedy.
When to Use — When GST adjudication/demand order is passed without reasons being communicated to taxpayer, or where reasons in the order are cryptic/absent. Cite to argue the order is invalid and writ maintainable. Also use when CBDT/CBIC circular transfers a case without reasons.
C.B. Gautam v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | (1993) 1 SCC 78 | S.269UD Income-tax Act | Reasons in Order Must Be Served
Key Ratio — Gautam Confirms Ajantha
"It is therefore inconceivable that the order which is required to be served by the appropriate authority under sub-section (2) would be one which does not contain the reasons recorded in writing. It may be permissible to record the reasons separately but the order would be an incomplete order unless either the reasons are incorporated therein or are served separately along with the order on the affected party. We are of the view that the reasons for the order must be communicated to the affected party."
Two-Fold Purpose Ratio
The procedure by way of recording and communicating the reasons for the impugned order to the affected party serves a two-fold purpose: (1) the aggrieved party can challenge the reasons before a court as erroneous, irrational or irrelevant; (2) it operates as a deterrent against any arbitrary action.
When to Use — When SCN/adjudication order/demand notice does not contain reasons, or contains boilerplate language. Cite to argue: order is incomplete and invalid; right of challenge is defeated; writ must lie.
State of Uttar Pradesh v Lalai Singh
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1977 SC 202 | §99A CrPC | Grounds Must Be Expressly Stated — Cannot Be Implied
Key Ratio
Under section 99A, a formal authoritative statement of the grounds was statutorily mandatory and this condition was not fulfilled. It was not sufficient that the grounds on which the government formed its opinion could be implied from the order. Failure to state the grounds expressly was not a compliance with the statutory provision. The Court insisted that when the statutory provision said that the government must state the grounds, it was no answer to say that they need not be stated because they were implied. A formal authoritative setting forth of the grounds was statutorily mandatory. If the grounds were not stated, what would the court examine? The court "cannot make a roving inquiry" into the grounds beyond what was set forth in the impugned order. Omission to state the grounds would thus defeat the valuable right of appeal to the court.
When to Use — When SCN or adjudication order contains vague grounds, or where the officer says "you knew the reasons." Cite to establish: implied reasons ≠ stated reasons; the court cannot supply what the officer omitted; right of reply/appeal is defeated; order is bad.
Shri Mandir Sita Ramji v Lt. Governor of Delhi
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1974 SC 1868 | §5A Land Acquisition Act | Pre-decisional Inquiry is Mandatory
Key Ratio
The duty to afford a hearing has been held to be mandatory and any acquisition of property without this procedure having been undergone would be invalid. The fact that the ultimate decision has to be made by the government and not the collector did not exonerate the collector from his statutory duty to hear the objections and make recommendation. The observance of the procedure laid down by statute before depriving a person of his property is necessary to generate the feeling that rule of law prevails in this country. When a procedure is prescribed by the legislature, it is not for the court to substitute a different procedure.
When to Use — When adjudication order is passed without following pre-decisional inquiry/show cause procedure prescribed by CGST Act. Cite to argue: the officer cannot shortcut the statutory procedure; order passed without following procedure is invalid regardless of outcome.
Sama Aruna v State of Telangana
Supreme Court of India | (2018) 12 SCC 150 | Preventive Detention | Stale Incidents Cannot Sustain Detention
Key Ratio — Live & Proximate Link Required
"The detention order must be based on a reasonable prognosis of the future behaviour of a person based on his past conduct in light of the surrounding circumstances. The live and proximate link that must exist between the past conduct of a person and the imperative need to detain him must be taken to have been snapped in this case. A detention order which is founded on stale incidents, must be regarded as an order of punishment for a crime, passed without a trial, though purporting to be an order of preventive detention. The essential concept of preventive detention is that the detention of a person is not to punish him for something he has done but to prevent him from doing it."
When to Use — In GST/custom arrests or detention orders where the alleged evasion events are old and stale, or where the "nexus" between past conduct and present detention is broken. Also use as analogy in adjudication — a show cause on stale events beyond limitation with no live nexus to current period should not sustain demand.
CIT v Mahindra & Mahindra Ltd.
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1984 SC 1182 | Extended Ultra Vires — Standard for Judicial Interference
Key Ratio — When Court Is Justified to Interfere
"Indisputably, it is a settled position that if the action or decision is perverse or is such that no reasonable body of persons, properly informed could come to, or has been arrived at by the authority misdirecting itself by adopting a wrong approach or has been influenced by irrelevant or extraneous matter, the Court would be justified in interfering with the same."
When to Use — When challenging an arbitrary GST demand, anti-profiteering order or classification ruling. Cite to establish: even if the officer had the power, the way it was exercised (wrong approach, extraneous considerations, perverse finding) makes it bad in law. Applies in writ jurisdiction.
Consumer Action Group v State of Tamil Nadu
Supreme Court of India | AIR 2000 SC 3060 | Reasons Required Even Without Statutory Mandate
Key Ratio
Even if the statute confers wide discretionary power on an authority or Government, reasons must be recorded for exercising the power even though the statute is silent about it.
When to Use — When Revenue argues that since no specific section requires reasons in the order, the order without reasons is valid. Cite to destroy this argument: statutory silence on reasons does not excuse the authority — constitutional non-arbitrariness mandate (Art.14) independently requires reasons for any exercise of discretion.
Chandra Singh v State of Rajasthan
Supreme Court of India | AIR 2003 SC 2889 | Wrong Statutory Provision — When Order Survives
Key Ratio
Mentioning of a wrong provision or omission to mention the correct provision would not invalidate an order so long as the power exists under any provision of law — but this principle would not apply where the two statutory provisions operate in two different fields requiring compliance with different pre-requisites.
When to Use — Dual use: (A) For taxpayer: When SCN cites §73 but facts clearly require §74 (different prerequisites — extended limitation, intent to evade) — argue wrong provision cited; §73 conditions not satisfied; order bad. (B) For Revenue: When order cites wrong sub-section but power exists elsewhere in the same section — argue order survives as power existed.
Kapur Chand v ITC (Income Tax Cases)
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1981 SC 1965 | S.25A Income-tax Act | Order Without Mandatory Inquiry = Quashed
Key Ratio
An order passed without any inquiry as required by a statutory provision is invalid and must be quashed. Where a statutory provision requires the authority to hold an inquiry before taking a specified decision, holding of the pre-decisional inquiry is mandatory and a decision or order made without such an inquiry is invariably regarded as invalid.
When to Use — When GST adjudication order is passed without granting opportunity for personal hearing, or where DGGI/Anti-Profiteering investigation proceeds without giving assessee opportunity to present their case. Cite: mandatory pre-decisional procedure skipped = order invalid from inception.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter XIX — Discretionary Powers (III)

⚖️ Chapter XIX — Discretionary Powers (III)

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Root / Header
Level 1 Branch
Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void / Warning
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Exception
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
19.8 Scope of Judicial Review
19.9 Policy Decision & SC Judgment
19.10 Policy Decision — Compliance
19.11 Forming an Opinion
19.12 Commercial Matters
19.13 Commodity Classification
19.14 Onus of Proof
19.15 Disclosure of Reasons
19.16 Misuse of Discretion
19.17 Abuse of Discretion
19.17.1 Mala Fides
19.17.2 Improper Purpose
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
19.8 — Scope of Judicial Review | State of UP v Joharimal [AIR 2004 SC 3800]
⚖️ Discretionary Powers — Judicial Review: Six Limiting Principles
Recapitulated in State of UP v Joharimal | Applies to all executive discretionary decisions
📝 Judicial review is not an appeal — courts do not re-examine the merits of a government decision. They only check if the process was lawful, fair, and within authority. These six rules define where the court draws its boundary.
Six Grounds / Limits of Judicial Review in Executive Decisions
(i)
Court does NOT sit in appeal over Administrative Body decisions
(ii)
Judicial review lies only on well-defined grounds
(iii)
Discretionary order interfered with only if exercise of discretion itself was perverse or illegal
(iv)
A mere wrong decision without more is not enough for judicial review
(v)
Supervisory jurisdiction: Court checks if Tribunal acted within limits of authority — no miscarriage of justice
(vi)
Court shall not ordinarily interfere with a policy decision of the State
POLICY DECISIONS — SPECIAL RULE
When a policy decision is valid (not arbitrary, not based on irrelevant considerations, not mala fide, not contrary to statute), judicial review narrows further to: Is the impugned decision in conformity with the said policy?
📝 For GST/tax authorities: if a policy (like rate fixation or exemption) is validly made, challenging a specific assessment under it requires showing either the policy itself is bad OR the assessment departs from the policy — not merely that the outcome is unfavourable.
DOCTRINE — Wednesbury Unreasonableness
Where the exercise is policy-based and involves expert deliberation, Courts apply minimal interference — "Wednesbury" standard: only interfere if decision is so unreasonable that no reasonable authority would have made it.
📝 The Wednesbury test is the last resort for challengers — it means the decision must be so absurd or irrational that it shocks conscience. Courts are especially reluctant in economic policy decisions made by domain experts.
💡 Spectrum allocation policy, mining policy, GST rate setting — all policy-based expert decisions. Courts will not second-guess them unless arbitrary or malicious. Only challenge: show the policy violates a statute or is discriminatory.
19.9 — Policy Decision with due Regard to Supreme Court Judgment
📋 Policy Decision Must Respect Existing SC Judgments
Mohd. Gani v National Geophysical Research Institute (1998) 8 SCC 458
BACKGROUND
National Geophysical Research Institute failed to pay even minimum prescribed pay scales to casual workers for 15+ years
SC JUDGMENT
Surinder Singh v Engineer-in-Chief, CPWD (1986): wages of regularly employed workers directed from Jan 1986
GOVERNMENT POLICY (1988)
Union Government reviewed recruitment policy of casual workers — fixed minimum pay + DA from 7 June 1988
PROBLEM
Director modified order: wages payable only from 7 June 1988 — ordered recovery of excess paid from Jan 1986 to June 1988
SC RULING
Policy decision taken by Union Government regarding casual workers' wages cannot be construed as effective from 7 June 1988 in light of earlier SC judgment. Director's modification order quashed.
📝 A government policy, however validly made, cannot override or cut back the effect of a Supreme Court order. The SC judgment is paramount — a later policy must operate consistent with, not in derogation of, an existing SC ruling.
💡 In GST: if SC has ruled on a refund right or ITC entitlement, a later circular limiting it cannot override the ruling. The circular must be read down or struck down.
19.10 — Policy Decision to be Complied with — Bindingness & Discrimination
📌 Once Policy is Laid Down — It Binds All Functionaries
Multiple SC decisions | Virender S. Hooda v State of Haryana AIR 1999 SC 1701
📝 The government is not free to follow its own policy selectively. Once it sets a rule or policy, everyone in the same situation has a right to the same treatment. Departing from the policy for some people while applying it to others = Article 14 (Equality) violation.
BINDING RULE
Policy decision is binding on all government functionaries if not contrary to rules
(Virender S. Hooda)
DISCRIMINATION RULE
Once policy is taken — it must be implemented fairly and uniformly without any discrimination
If not → Article 14 violation
(Indian Charge Chrome Ltd. v UOI)
📝 Even if there is a valid policy, applying it differently to similar persons is illegal. Consistency is constitutional.
SURPLUS LAND RULE
When government acquires land and declares surplus, its own policy of giving priority to landowners must be followed — cannot divert land to another department free of cost in derogation of stated policy
ADVISORY BODIES
Views of National Commission / advisory bodies expressed in reports are NOT binding on Government — only advisory in nature
MINING LEASE — SPECIFIC EXAMPLE
Government cannot renew mining lease with policy conditions, then act contrary to its own policy to allot certain work type to a contractor, while retrenching casual labourers who relied on the original policy — such contradictory action is unjustified and upheld as bad by SC.
💡 GST relevance: If Department issues a circular granting benefit to a class of assessees, it must apply it uniformly. A selective denial to similarly-placed assessees = arbitrary and liable to be struck down on Article 14 grounds.
19.11 — Forming an Opinion
🧠 Forming an Opinion — Must be Objective on Relevant Considerations
NOT ALLOWED
Opinion need NOT be formed in a subjective or casual manner
REQUIRED
Opinion must be formed objectively on relevant considerations
📝 Even when a statute uses words like "is satisfied" or "has reason to believe" (subjective-sounding), it does not mean the authority can form any opinion it likes. The opinion must have a rational, objective basis — relevant facts must be actually considered.
💡 In GST: A GST officer forming an opinion for arrest, provisional attachment, or search must base it on relevant material — not suspicion or pressure. An opinion formed without examining records = subjective and invalid.
19.12 — Commercial Matters | Karnataka SFC v Cavalet India Ltd. (2005) 4 SCC 456
🏦 Courts in Commercial Matters — Nine Principles for Financial Corporation Decisions
📝 When a Financial Corporation (like a bank or DRT authority) exercises its commercial judgment, Courts apply a lighter touch — they do not replace the Corporation's decision with their own commercial wisdom. Nine specific rules govern this restraint.
(i)
High Court under Art. 226 does NOT sit as appellate authority over Financial Corporation acts
(ii)
Writ Court intervenes in Corporation–debtor disputes ONLY IF:
(a) Statutory violation by Corporation
(b) Corporation acts unfairly/unreasonably
(iii)
Courts should NOT risk their judgments for those of bodies to which task is assigned
(iv)
Unless Corporation acts mala fide — even a wrong decision by it is not open to challenge by courts or third party
(v)
In sale of public property → dominant consideration = best price → requires maximum public participation
(vi)
Public auction is NOT the only mode — tender and negotiation can also be adopted
(vii)
Financial Corporation must realise maximum sale price by transparent, publicly-advertised procedure; consider reasons for default
(viii)
Fairness is NOT one-way — Corporation cannot be shackled from recovering dues in name of fairness to borrower
(ix)
Reasonableness tested against dominant consideration: best price for public property
CONTRACT TENDERS
In contracts awarded by State through tender — no need to give reasons; neither judicial nor quasi-judicial conduct. State may choose its method for bona fide reasons. Lowest price alone is not decisive — public interest and quality also matter. (Silppi Constructions Contractors v UOI, (2020) 16 SCC 489)
💡 GST relevance: Demand of best price and transparency principles apply to government auctions of mineral rights, spectrum, etc. Arbitrary tender cancellation or non-transparent allotment may be judicially reviewable.
19.13 — Commodity Classification
📦 Commodity Classification — Heading/Duty Rate Dispute
PRINCIPLE
Classification (heading/rate) must be decided on facts arising in each case. Even if a prior classification decision existed, new investigation or changed law may lead to re-examination.
COURT RESTRAINT
High Court should not interfere in classification matters at the stage of show-cause notice — factual investigation must be completed first.
📝 Classification disputes (HSN code, rate category) are fact-heavy. Courts wait for the adjudication process to complete before interfering. Filing a writ at SCN stage to stop classification proceedings will generally fail.
💡 GST: When Department issues SCN proposing re-classification of a product (e.g., from 5% to 18%), assessee cannot get a writ to quash the SCN at that stage itself. Must reply, participate in adjudication, and then challenge the order.
19.14 — Onus of Proof in Challenging Administrative Action
⚖️ Who Bears the Burden of Proving an Administrative Decision is Bad?
Land Acquisition Collector v Durga Pada AIR 1980 SC 1678 | Narayan v State of Maharashtra AIR 1977 SC 183
General Rule — Onus on Challenger
PRESUMPTION
Administrative act is presumed valid. Onus on person challenging to prove flaw in decision-making process
DIFFICULTY
Petitioner has no access to government files; Administration rarely gives reasons → discharging onus is very difficult
§114 EVIDENCE ACT
Presumption that official acts have been regularly performed — raised if recital in order is technically correct
§106 EVIDENCE ACT
If facts are specially within government's knowledge — burden of proving those facts shifts to government
When Onus Shifts to Government — Narayan v State of Maharashtra
DEFECTIVE RECITAL
If recital in order is defective — does not indicate material/circumstances on which order is based → §114 presumption stands displaced → petitioner has discharged initial onus
SPECIAL KNOWLEDGE
Facts constituting urgency / reason for exercise of power lie especially within knowledge of official → §106 burden to prove those facts rests on that official
COMBINATION TEST
Petitioner discharges burden if: bare assertion + failure of State to indicate facts easily disclosable + defective recital → taken together = petitioner succeeds
⚠️ DANGER — STATE'S NON-DISCLOSURE
If State fails to disclose facts especially within its knowledge without giving sufficient reason → it must bear the consequences of non-production. Remaining silent and relying on order's sufficiency is unsafe when recital can be challenged.
📝 For taxpayers: if Department passes an order with a vague or generic recital (e.g., "material available suggests evasion") without specifying the material — the §114 presumption is displaced. The taxpayer has discharged initial onus; the burden shifts back to the officer to show what specific material was considered.
💡 GST: A provisional attachment order under §83 CGST Act that says only "protecting revenue interest" without specifying the material considered is a defective recital. After Narayan — onus shifts to Department. Attach without reason → vulnerable to challenge.
ANURADHA BHASIN v UOI (2020) 3 SCC 637 — PROACTIVE DISCLOSURE
Where fundamental rights are curtailed by State action not easily available — State must take a proactive approach: place all relevant orders before Court, unless specific privilege or countervailing public interest is specifically claimed on affidavit. Court then balances privilege against petitioner's interest.
19.15 — Disclosure of Reasons for Discretionary Decisions
📝 Must an Administrative Body Give Reasons?
Padfield v Minister of Agriculture (1968) AC 997 | Barium Chemicals v Company Law Board AIR 1967 SC 295
GENERAL RULE
In absence of statutory requirement → administrative order need NOT be a speaking order
(Mahabir Jute Mills v Shibban Lot Sasena)
📝 Unlike quasi-judicial bodies, a pure administrative body is not legally required to give reasons for every decision. But...
EXCEPTION 1
If there is a provision for appeal to higher administrative authority or tribunal → reasons must be given
PADFIELD PRINCIPLE (UK HoL)
If no reasons are stated → Court will presume the authority had no good reasons → decide accordingly
"Failure to give reasons does not preclude judicial review"
📝 Silence on reasons is not a shield — it can be a sword against the authority. Courts treat unexplained decisions with suspicion.
IF STATUTE REQUIRES REASONS
Then it becomes obligatory to state them. Absence of required reasons → administrative action liable to be quashed
India — Barium Chemicals Standard: Courts Insist on Reasons in Affidavit
Courts insist on disclosure of reasons to the court (not necessarily the party) when discretionary order is challenged — even without legal obligation to give reasons to individuals
A mere mechanical recital or executive declaration that "material exists" is NOT sufficient
— Barium Chemicals: Court unsatisfied with bare claim of opinion formation
LIMITATION
Courts generally reluctant to go beyond order and affidavits to examine government record — but may look into files in exceptional cases, without showing them to the party
FRESH REASONS IN AFFIDAVIT — NOT ALLOWED
When an order is based on certain grounds, its validity must be judged with reference to those reasons. Reasons cannot be supplemented by fresh reasons in the affidavit to justify the order after the fact.
📝 Government cannot do an adjudication order saying "goods liable to confiscation" and then, when challenged, file an affidavit giving entirely new grounds not mentioned in the order. The order must stand or fall on its own stated grounds.
💡 GST: SCN must state reasons for demand. Order must reflect those reasons. If adjudication order gives a reason not in SCN — it is void (violates natural justice). Equally, Dept cannot supplement a cryptic order with fresh reasons in court affidavit.
19.16 — Misuse of Discretion
🚫 Misuse of Discretion — When is it a Ground for Challenge?
SETTLED LAW
A discretionary power CANNOT be struck down on grounds of mere likelihood of misuse
NO PRESUMPTION
There cannot be a presumption of misuse merely because discretion is conferred on a public authority
BUT IF ACTUALLY MISUSED
If discretionary power is actually misused or used arbitrarily in a specific case → subject to judicial review
(Food Corporation of India v A. Prahalada Rao)
📝 You cannot challenge a power (like §83 attachment or §67 search) just because it CAN be misused. You must show it WAS misused in your specific case — with evidence of arbitrariness, irrelevant considerations, or mala fide.
19.17 — Abuse of Discretion — Restrictions & Core Principles
⚠️ Abuse of Discretion — Comprehensive Framework
Diecey's Commentary | Multiple SC Judgments | Various English Authorities
📝 All discretion has limits. Courts will not tolerate unfettered, arbitrary power. Abuse occurs when discretion is exercised for wrong purposes, in bad faith, under pressure from others, or with irrelevant considerations. The primary rule: discretion must serve the policy and objects of the empowering Act.
Core Restrictions on Exercise of Discretion
✗ Must NOT act in bad faith
✗ Must NOT discriminate between persons on irrelevant criteria
✗ Discretion once conferred CANNOT be restricted or fettered
✗ Authority CANNOT refuse to exercise discretion when statute confers it
✗ Authority CANNOT act at dictates of higher authority — must maintain independence & impartiality
✗ Must NOT be influenced by improper motive or purpose
✗ Decision-makers must NOT allow personal interest/beliefs to influence them
✗ Must NOT pre-judge the case or hold pre-determined views regardless of merits
PRIMARY RULE
Discretion should be used to promote the policies and objects of the governing Act. Discretionary power must NOT be used to achieve a purpose not contemplated by the Act that confers the power.
📝 The Act giving the power defines its outer limit. Exercising power to achieve any other purpose — however well-intentioned — is abuse.
💡 GST: §83 provisional attachment power is for protecting revenue. Using it to coerce compliance or settlement = improper purpose = abuse = challengeable.
ABUSE EXAMPLE — Rocarelli v Duplessis (1959 Canada)
Liquor licence revoked because owner had posted bail for persons prosecuted under municipal bylaws — had nothing to do with liquor licensing. Court held: gross abuse of legal power expressly intended to punish for an act wholly irrelevant to the statute conferring the power.
19.17.1 — Mala Fides (Bad Faith) — Nature, Proof & Limits
🎭 Mala Fides — Two Senses, Two Standards, Extraordinarily Difficult to Prove
Partap Singh v State of Punjab AIR 1964 SC 72 | Express Newspapers v UOI AIR 1986 SC 872
Mala Fides — Two Distinct Meanings
BROAD SENSE
May denote any improper exercise of power, abuse of discretion, or any of the grounds of invalidity — used loosely
📝 In broad sense, Courts sometimes use "mala fides" to mean "bad exercise of power" even without personal animosity.
NARROW SENSE (CORRECT)
Mala fides = Dishonest intention or corrupt motive — deliberately malicious or fraudulent purpose on part of decision-maker.
Includes personal animosity, spite, vengeance, personal gratification.
📝 Real mala fides requires a corrupt/dishonest mind — not just a wrong decision or even a foolish one. The person must be acting out of spite, personal grudge, or corrupt purpose.
Malice in Law vs. Malice of Fact — Distinction
MALICE IN LAW
= Doing a wrongful act intentionally without just cause or excuse, without reasonable or probable cause. Not necessarily from ill feeling — can be "innocently" (acting ignorantly in violation of law).
Legal mala fides = exercising statutory power for purposes foreign to those for which law intended it
Can be inferred; need not be direct.
📝 Malice in law does not require spite. It means: you used a legal power for a purpose the law did not authorise. Even if you meant well, wrong purpose = malice in law.
MALICE OF FACT
= Actual personal ill-will, spite, vengeance — driven by personal animosity against the party.
Must be specifically pleaded and proved. High standard of proof required.
📝 Malice of fact = the official genuinely hated or wanted to harm the person. Very difficult to prove — needs specific evidence of personal grudge or vendetta.
Proof of Mala Fides — Burden & Standard
VERY HEAVY BURDEN
Burden of establishing mala fides lies on the person who alleges it — very heavy, difficult to discharge
HIGH CREDIBILITY NEEDED
Requires proof of high degree of credibility — suspicion is not proof. "Suspicion cannot take the place of proof" — EP Royappa
SPECIFIC ALLEGATIONS
Vague, broad, general allegations do not suffice. Must be specific, pointed, and based on factual matrix — not surmises or conjectures
IMPLEADING REQUIREMENT
Person against whom mala fides alleged must be made a party to proceedings
INFERENCE ALLOWED
Mala fides need not be proved only by direct evidence — can be deduced as reasonable and inescapable inference from proved facts
(public utterances, failure to file affidavit, sequence of events)
SHIFT OF BURDEN
Once petitioner makes out prima facie case of mala fides with sufficient material → burden shifts to authority to justify the order
WHERE ALLEGATIONS ARE UNREBUTTED — Express Newspapers v UOI
Where mala fides are alleged, the person against whom allegations are made must come forward with an answer refuting them. If specific allegations remain unrebutted → Court will accept them on test of probability.
📝 Government silence on specific mala fides allegations is dangerous. Vague counter-affidavit saying "allegations not relevant" without denying them = Court treats them as unrebutted = accepted against the government.
Sub-Doctrines within Mala Fides
§19.17.1.1 — PRESUMPTION
When thing is done in post-haste manner → mala fide may be presumed
(S.P. Kapoor v State of HP, AIR 1981 SC 2181)
§19.17.1.2 — PREVENTIVE DETENTION
Detention order also liable to be set aside on mala fides of detaining authority. Malice of subordinate reporting officer alone insufficient unless detaining authority failed to independently examine material.
(G. Sadanandan v State of Kerala)
§19.17.1.2 — STALENESS IN DETENTION
Events 9-14 years old cannot form basis for preventive detention in the present. Only past activities that lead to conclusion of likely future engagement can be considered.
(Sama Aruna v State of Telangana, 2018)
§19.17.1.3 — MISUSE/ARBITRARY USE
Discretionary power must be exercised case-by-case. If misused or used arbitrarily → subject to judicial review.
(Food Corporation v A. Prahalada Rao)
MALA FIDES — RARELY SUCCEEDS
While mala fides is frequently pleaded, it rarely succeeds — because of the heavy burden, high proof standard, difficulty of establishing the state of mind of an official, and the presumption of bona fide official action. Courts are slow to attribute malice to holders of high office on basis of vague pleadings.
19.17.2 — Improper Purpose | Sydney Municipal Council v Campbell (1925) AC 338
🎯 Improper Purpose — Power Used for Different Purpose than Statute Intended
A ground distinct from mala fides — purpose may be public-interest motivated but still improper if outside statutory scope
📝 Improper purpose differs from mala fides: the authority may be acting honestly and even in what it believes is the public interest — but if the purpose falls outside what the empowering statute authorized, the action is void. No corrupt intent needed; just wrong purpose.
DEFINITION
Improper purpose = purpose lying outside the scope and purpose of the enabling statute.
If statute confers power for Purpose A, using it for Purpose B = not a valid exercise → quashable
BROADER THAN MALA FIDES
Mala fides requires corrupt/malicious intent.
Improper purpose: the motivation may be benign, public-interest driven — but if purpose is not what the statute authorised → still bad exercise of power
Good faith is immaterial
📝 Even if the official had the best intentions, using power for a purpose the law didn't sanction = improper purpose = void.
KEY TEST
Was the purpose in view one sanctioned by the statute that confers power on the authority?
If NO → improper purpose → power abused
Landmark Examples of Improper Purpose
Sydney Municipal Council v Campbell
Power to acquire land for improving city — used to benefit financially from land value increment expected due to nearby street extension = improper purpose → acquisition quashed
Attorney General v Fulham Corporation
Power to establish warehouse for non-commercial use of local residents — used to open commercial laundry = ultra vires = improper purpose
Hull & Co v Shoreham-by-Sea UDC
Condition to construct public road imposed as pre-condition to building plan sanction — no fair/reasonable relation to development itself = beyond power = improper purpose
Porter v Magill (2002) UK HoL
Local authority power to dispose of land — used to promote electoral advantage of dominant party = improper purpose = unlawful
INDIA — SC STATEMENT OF LAW
"It is trite law that if a discretionary power has been exercised for an unauthorised purpose it is generally immaterial whether its repository was acting in good faith or in bad faith."
(S.R. Venkataraman v UOI AIR 1979 SC 49)
💡 GST: Provisional attachment order (§83) used by Department to coerce taxpayer into settling a disputed amount — the purpose (coercion) is not what §83 authorises (protecting revenue interest during proceedings). = Improper purpose = order liable to be quashed even if officer acted in "good faith."
LORD REID in Mixnam Properties Ltd. v Buckingham County Council
"General words and phrases, therefore, however wide and comprehensive they may be in their literal sense, must, usually, be construed as being limited to the actual objects of the Act." — Power given in broad statutory language still cannot be used beyond the Act's actual objects.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter XIX | Discretionary Powers (III)
These ratios are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the case name.
State of UP v Joharimal | AIR 2004 SC 3800 : (2004) 4 SCC 714
Supreme Court of India | 2004 | Relied upon for scope of judicial review in executive decisions
Key Ratio / Six Principles
Court while exercising judicial review does not sit in appeal over administrative bodies. Review lies only on well-defined grounds. An order cannot be interfered with unless the exercise of discretion itself was perverse or illegal. A mere wrong decision without more is not sufficient. Supervisory jurisdiction is limited to checking that the Tribunal functions within authority and that its decisions do not occasion miscarriage of justice. Court shall not ordinarily interfere with a policy decision of the State.
When to Use — When opposing writ petitions that challenge GST/tax adjudication orders on the ground that the officer reached the "wrong" conclusion. Argue: judicial review does not re-examine merits; only perverse or illegal exercise justifies interference.
Center for Public Interest Litigation v UOI | AIR 2016 SC 1777 : (2016) 6 SCC 408
Supreme Court of India | 2016 | Policy decisions by technical experts — minimal judicial interference
Key Ratio
A policy decision, when not found to be arbitrary or based on irrelevant considerations or mala fide or against any statutory provisions, does not call for any interference by courts in exercise of judicial review. When the decision-making is policy-based, judicial approach to interfere with such decision-making becomes narrower. Minimal interference is called for when the policy is the outcome of deliberations of technical experts in the field, as courts are not well equipped to fathom into such domain.
When to Use — When challenging arbitrary classification of goods/services under GST, or challenging GST Council recommendations/notifications on rate. The challenge must show the policy is arbitrary/contrary to statute — mere economic disagreement is insufficient.
Narayan v State of Maharashtra | AIR 1977 SC 183 : (1977) 1 SCC 133
Supreme Court of India | 1977 | Comprehensive ruling on burden of proof in challenging discretionary power
Key Ratio — Burden of Proof & Recital Defect
Even when formation of an opinion by an administrative authority is a subjective matter, the validity of the opinion will still be judged by applying the well established principles of administrative law — consideration of relevant material, absence of perversity or arbitrariness, non-application of mind, unreasonableness. Where a recital is defective — as not indicating material or circumstances on which the order was based — the Section 114, Illustration (e) presumption stands displaced and the petitioner is said to have discharged his general onus. The burden then shifts to the authority to show what exceptional circumstances existed.
When to Use — When GST order of attachment, assessment or search has a defective or vague recital. Argue: defective recital displaces the presumption of regularity; burden shifts to Department to show what specific material was considered. Also use when opposing orders that merely repeat the statutory formula without disclosing the actual basis.
Padfield v Minister of Agriculture | (1968) AC 997 : (1968) 1 All ER 694
House of Lords, UK | 1968 | Non-disclosure of reasons — inference of no good reasons
Key Ratio — Inference from Absence of Reasons
Failure to give reasons by an administrative authority for taking a particular decision does not preclude judicial review thereof. If the Minister gives no reasons for his action, the court may infer that he has no good reason and that he is not using the power given by Parliament to carry out its intentions. Non-revelation of reasons by the Administration for a decision may lead the reviewing Court to infer that there were no reasons to disclose and thus treat the matter accordingly.
When to Use — When GST adjudication order or Department communication gives no reasons for rejection of refund, ITC reversal demand, or penalty levy. Argue: absence of reasons = Court/Tribunal may infer no good reason existed. The Department cannot remain silent on the specific basis for its conclusion.
Barium Chemicals Ltd. v Company Law Board | AIR 1967 SC 295 : (1966) 36 Comp Cas 639
Supreme Court of India | 1967 | Mechanical repetition of statutory formula insufficient
Key Ratio
A mere mechanical repetition of the words of the section in the affidavit filed by the government in support of its action — i.e., a mere executive declaration that there was material on which the authority could form its opinion — is not satisfactory. The court will not be satisfied with a bare mechanical recital. The circumstances specifically mentioned must relate to the grounds mentioned in the order of the government, and the court will consider whether the circumstances mentioned in the affidavit were irrelevant or extraneous to the grounds in the order.
When to Use — When Department supports its assessment or attachment order by filing an affidavit that merely repeats the statutory language ("reason to believe," "material available") without specifying what the material actually was. Cite Barium Chemicals: bare formula is insufficient — specific circumstances must be stated and relevant.
Express Newspapers Ltd. v UOI | AIR 1986 SC 872 : (1986) 1 SCC 133
Supreme Court of India | 1986 | Mala fides — unrebutted specific allegations accepted on probability
Key Ratio — Unrebutted Mala Fides
Where mala fides are alleged, it is necessary that the person against whom such allegations are made should come forward with an answer refuting or denying such allegations. For otherwise, such allegations remain unrebutted and the Court would in such a case be constrained to accept the allegations so remaining unrebutted and unanswered on the test of probability.
When to Use — When Department officials or Commissioner is alleged to have acted with mala fides and the Department does not specifically deny the allegations. Argue: failure to specifically rebut specific mala fide allegations = Court accepts them on probability. Government cannot hide behind vague counter-affidavits.
Sydney Municipal Council v Campbell | (1925) AC 338
Privy Council | 1925 | Improper purpose — statutory power for different object
Key Ratio — Improper Purpose Doctrine
A body authorised to take land compulsorily for specified purposes will not be permitted to exercise its powers for different purposes, and if it attempts to do so, the Courts will interfere. The power must be exercised for the purposes and objects of the empowering statute, not for a purpose outside that scope, even if the authority genuinely believed it was acting in the public interest.
When to Use — When Department uses GST enforcement powers (search, arrest, attachment) for a purpose outside the statute's scope — e.g., pressuring taxpayer to pay disputed amount or surrender pending litigation. Argue: statutory power used for improper purpose = void, regardless of bona fide intent of the officer.
S.R. Venkataraman v UOI | AIR 1979 SC 49 : (1979) 2 SCC 491
Supreme Court of India | 1979 | Malice in law — good faith immaterial when purpose is unauthorised
Key Ratio
It is trite law that if a discretionary power has been exercised for an unauthorised purpose it is generally immaterial whether its repository was acting in good faith or in bad faith. Mala fide exercise of power does not necessarily imply any moral turpitude as a matter of law. It only means that the statutory power is exercised for purposes foreign to those for which it is in law intended.
When to Use — When Department argues that its action (however outside the statute's scope) was done in good faith. Counter: good faith is irrelevant when the purpose is not authorised by the statute. The test is not the officer's intention but the statutory scope of the power exercised.
Karnataka State Industrial Investment & Development Corporation v Cavalet India Ltd. | (2005) 4 SCC 456
Supreme Court of India | 2005 | Commercial decisions — courts restrained; fairness is not one-way
Key Ratio — Principle (viii) on Fairness
Fairness cannot be a one-way street. The fairness required of Financial Corporations cannot be carried to the extent of disabling them from recovering what is due to them. While not insisting upon the borrower to honour the commitments undertaken by him, the Financial Corporation alone cannot be shackled hand and foot in the name of fairness. The courts should not risk their judgments for the judgments of the bodies to which that task is assigned.
When to Use — When assessee argues that Revenue's enforcement action is "harsh" or "unfair" without pointing to specific legal infirmity. Counter: fairness is not one-way. Revenue has a legitimate duty to recover tax. Courts will not replace Revenue's commercial/enforcement judgment with their own view of "what is fair."
Virender S. Hooda v State of Haryana | AIR 1999 SC 1701 : (1999) 3 SCC 696
Supreme Court of India | 1999 | Policy decisions — binding on functionaries; deviations = Article 14 violation
Key Ratio
The policy decision is binding on all its functionaries if it is not contrary to the rules. Once the Government has taken a policy decision, it must implement it fairly and uniformly without any discrimination. Any deviation or departure from the policy would attract the wrath of Article 14 of the Constitution. Merely because a little higher revenue can be earned by the Government, that cannot be a ground for not enforcing the obligation of the Government in accordance with its own policy decision.
When to Use — When Department applies a circular/instruction, notification or exemption selectively — giving benefit to some taxpayers but denying to similarly-placed others without any rational distinction. Cite: policy must be applied uniformly; selective denial = Article 14 violation.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter XIX — Discretionary Powers (III)

⚖️ Chapter XIX — Discretionary Powers (III)

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void / Quashed
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Alternate / Test
Neutral Reference / Statute
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
19.17.1 Improper Purpose — Core Rule
19.17.2 Padfield — No Unfettered Discretion
19.17.3 Relevant / Irrelevant Considerations
19.17.3.1 Compulsory Retirement
19.17.3.2 Transfer
19.17.3.3 Preventive Detention — Irrelevant Grounds
19.17.3.4 Mixed Considerations
19.17.3.5 Improper Purpose vs Irrelevant Considerations
19.17.4 Ignoring Relevant Considerations
19.17.4.1 Preventive Detention — Ignoring Relevant Facts
19.17.5 Colourable Exercise of Power
19.17.6 Misdirection of Fact and Law
19.17.7 & 19.17.8 Misrepresentation / No Material
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
19.17.1 — Improper Purpose — Core Rule
⚖️ Improper Purpose — Grounds to Quash Discretion
Administrative Law | Chapter XIX | Discretionary Powers (III)
Discretionary power is always conferred for a specific statutory purpose
— it CANNOT be used to achieve any other end
📝 Every power given by law has a purpose. If the authority uses the power to reach a different goal — even a desirable one — the order is illegal. "Absolute" power is not truly absolute.
CORE RULE
Conditions placed on grant of permission must "fairly and reasonably relate to the permitted development"
Authority cannot impose conditions to achieve ends unrelated to statutory purpose
🇮🇳 Indian Cases — Improper Purpose Invoked
QUASHED
Sama Aruna v State of Telangana
(2018) 12 SCC 150
Preventive detention based on stale instances 9–14 years old — improper purpose
📝 Using very old events to justify detention today = improper purpose. Time-gap makes the basis stale and legally unsound.
QUASHED
63 Moon Technologies v UOI
(2019) 18 SCC 401
Order for merger of companies in public interest — fell outside authorised realm of merger grounds
QUASHED
In re Manick Chand
LR 1921 Cal 916
Municipal corporation acquired property not for itself but for Improvement Trust — outside scope of acquisition power
📝 Corporation's power to acquire land is only for its own statutory purposes — acting as a proxy for another body is improper.
QUASHED
R.L. Arora v State of UP
AIR 1962 SC 764
Land acquired for textile machinery factory — "useful to public" means direct utility of WORK, not product of company
📝 "Public" in public purpose must be directly benefited by the work itself — not indirectly by the factory's commercial output.
⚖️ Mixed Purpose — Dominant Purpose Test
TEST
When authority pursues two or more purposes — one authorised, one unauthorised — legality is determined by the DOMINANT purpose
If dominant purpose is unlawful → act is invalid
📝 Think of it like the main ingredient in a recipe. If the main ingredient is poison, the dish is poisonous — even if some ingredients are healthy.
PRINCIPLE
Conditions attached to permits must have a nexus with the statutory purpose of the Act.
Under Motor Vehicles Act §51(2)(x) — only conditions with nexus to Act purpose can be imposed.
e.g. Condition that bus must not be more than 7 years old — upheld as promoting human safety
💡 GST Parallel: Conditions in notices/orders must have nexus to the specific provision invoked. E.g., a condition in registration cancellation order that goes beyond §29 CGST Act can be challenged as having no nexus to the statutory purpose.
RULE ON EXEMPTION POWER
Power to exempt must be exercised to advance the object and policy of the Act — not to frustrate it.
P.J. Irani v State of Madras AIR 1961 SC 1731: Govt exempted building to evict protected tenant — not for hardship to landlord/abuse by tenant as Act intended → exemption invalid
📝 Exemption power is a scalpel — to be used for the reasons the law intended. Using it for unrelated purposes (like evicting a specific tenant) is improper.
💡 GST angle: Notifications granting conditional exemptions under §11 CGST must serve the purpose of reducing burden on a genuine category — not to create discrimination. If it is shown that the exemption was designed to benefit a specific class with no policy rationale, it can be attacked on this ground.
19.17.2 — Padfield v Minister of Agriculture — No Unfettered Discretion
🏛️ Padfield v Minister of Agriculture & Fisheries
(1968) 1 All ER 694 — House of Lords | Lord Reid
📝 This is THE landmark case establishing that no statutory discretion is truly "unlimited." A Minister who refuses to use a power for self-serving reasons (like avoiding embarrassment) is acting unlawfully.
📋 Facts
Milk Marketing Board fixed prices — regional milk producers complained prices didn't account for transport cost variations
Requested Minister to refer complaint to investigation committee — Minister empowered but refused
Reason given: matter raised "wide issues" — Minister would be "embarrassed" if referred
Minister argued: "unfettered discretion" — can refer or not as he thinks fit
⚖️ LORD REID: Parliament confers discretion on Minister with intention it should promote the policy and objects of the Act. Policy and objects must be determined by construing the Act as a whole — always a matter of law for the court.

"If the Minister so uses his discretion as to thwart or run counter to the policy and objects of the Act, then our law would be very defective if persons aggrieved were not entitled to the protection of the court."
✗ BAD REASON
Refusing to refer because it would put Minister in "embarrassing situation" = acting on a bad reason
→ misdirection of himself
📝 Convenience of the officer is NOT a valid legal reason. The law doesn't exist to protect officers from embarrassment.
RULE
"Absolute" before discretion does NOT fetter judicial control over the executive
Executive must act lawfully — matter determined by the court
📝 "Absolute discretion" in a statute means wide — not uncontrollable. Courts always have the last word on whether the law was followed.
PRINCIPLE
Statutory construction is always a matter of law for the court
→ Courts can assess whether discretion was used to promote the Act's policy
GST APPLICATION
When a GST officer refuses to allow inspection of records, grant adjournment, or consider a specific defence — claiming it is his "discretion" — Padfield is your weapon: the discretion must be exercised to promote the object of the CGST Act (just and fair determination of tax liability), not to suit administrative convenience.
19.17.3 — Relevant / Irrelevant Considerations — Wednesbury Framework
🔍 Relevant / Irrelevant Considerations
Wednesbury Corporation | Lord Green M.R. | (1948) 1 KB 223
Wednesbury Triple Requirement — Authority exercising discretion MUST:
✓ MUST
Take all relevant factors into account
✗ MUST NOT
Take into account irrelevant or extraneous considerations
✓ MUST
Reach decision which is neither perverse nor irrational
CORE RULE
Power must be exercised taking into account considerations mentioned in the statute.
If statute mentions no specific considerations → power to be exercised on considerations relevant to the purpose for which it is conferred.
Authority must act in good faith, have regard to all relevant considerations, must not be influenced by irrelevant/alien considerations, must not act arbitrarily or capriciously.
📝 In simple terms: the officer must have the right reasons for his decision. Not just any reasons — the right ones connected to what the law is trying to achieve. Wrong reasons = invalid decision.
🇮🇳 Key Cases — Irrelevant Considerations Applied
CaseIrrelevant Consideration FoundOutcome
State of Bombay v K.P. Krishnan
AIR 1960 SC 1222		Govt refused to refer bonus dispute to tribunal because "workmen resorted to go slow" — punitive reason unrelated to dispute meritQuashed — Punitive spirit contrary to the Act's objective of investigating disputes
Rohtas Industries v S.D. Agarwal
AIR 1969 SC 707		Investigation of company — complaints of misconduct against director of OTHER companies (not the company being investigated)Quashed — Not a relevant circumstance for investigating THIS company
Congreve v Secy of State
(1976) 1 All ER 697		TV licence revoked after 12 months (valid period) — reason: Minister needed more money. "Want of money was no reason for revoking a licence."Quashed — Financial compulsion of government is not a ground to revoke a valid licence
Bromley LBC v Greater London Council
(1982) 2 WLR 62		GLC reduced bus/tube fares 25% based on election manifesto — gave undue weight to manifesto; failed to balance ratepayers' interestsQuashed — Election manifesto is not a gospel; decision must be fair and rational, not manifesto-driven
State of UP v Raja Ram Jaiswal
AIR 1985 SC 1108		Cinema licence refused based on objection of Hindi Sahitya Sammelan that cinema noise would disturb them. "Dislike of a body howsoever prestigious is not adequate substitute for public interest."Quashed — Relevant ground was only unsuitable location in public interest; noise from an air-conditioned sound-proof building irrelevant
Barium Chemicals v Company Law Board
AIR 1967 SC 295		Investigation ordered due to: project delays, share value fall, resignations of directors — no connection to fraud by companyQuashed (majority) — Facts not relevant to the question of fraud which is the ground for investigation
Hukam Chand v UOI
AIR 1976 SC 789		Custom classification based solely on "ultimate use" of imported commodity — "end use is absolutely irrelevant" — taxing event is importQuashed — Classification must be based on condition of article at time of import, not future use
Shalini Soni v UOI
AIR 1981 SC 431		Supreme Court emphasised: "whenever a decision-making function is entrusted to subjective satisfaction, there is implicit obligation to apply mind to pertinent and proximate matters only, eschewing the irrelevant and the remote"Foundational principle — applies to all subjective satisfaction provisions
Nepal Singh v State of UP
AIR 1985 SC 84		Services terminated on vague grounds: "corrupt, not straight forward, married two wives" — allegation of two wives not proved, other grounds vagueQuashed — Vague and unspecific grounds vitiate administrative action
GST APPLICATION  When a GST officer issues SCN citing vague grounds like "transactions appear suspicious" or "returns show discrepancy" without specifying WHICH transactions, WHICH discrepancy — the SCN is vulnerable. Shalini Soni + Nepal Singh principle: subjective satisfaction must be on specific and proximate material, not vague or remote impressions. Every ground in SCN must be concrete and relevant to the specific allegation of tax evasion.
19.17.3.1 — Compulsory Retirement — Relevant Considerations
👤 Compulsory Retirement — Rule 56(j) Fundamental Rules
Principles applicable to all subjective satisfaction provisions in GST too
RULE 56(j)
Appropriate authority has right to retire any Government servant "if in public interest" — but this is NOT absolute.
Baldev Raj v UOI AIR 1981 SC 70: Opinion must be "not subjective satisfaction but objective and bona fide and based on relevant material"
📝 "Public interest" in the rule means the interest of public service — not personal, political, or any other interest. "Absolute power is anathema under our constitutional order."
INVALID — STALE MATERIAL
Retirement based on old/stale confidential reports ignoring recent satisfactory reports
Baldev Raj v UOI: old adverse entries considered, last 5 years' clean record ignored → quashed
📝 "Legality depends on regard to the totality of material facts viewed in a holistic perspective." — Krishna Iyer J
INVALID — POST-PROMOTION
Retirement passed immediately after promotion to higher post based on one adverse entry from years before promotion
Ramaswami v Tamil Nadu: promotion wipes out effect of past entry
INVALID — EXTRANEOUS GROUND
Retirement on ground that "better talent" available in department
"Better talent" is relative — does not mean incumbent has become "dead wood" → quashed
VALID
Employer duly complied with statutory procedure — no political/personal victimisation shown
UOI v P. Chandra Mouli (2003) 10 SCC 196: Court's interference not called for
NOTE — UNCOMMUNICATED ADVERSE REMARKS
Baikuntha Nath Das AIR 1992 SC 1020: Compulsory retirement is not punishment — principles of natural justice do not fully apply. Uncommunicated adverse remarks can be considered. Retirement need not await disposal of representation against those remarks. But adverse remarks ought to be communicated in normal course.
💡 GST Parallel: When adjudicating officer passes adverse order citing "precedents" or "audit findings" that were never put to assessee before the order — same principle applies. Vital material must be disclosed and opportunity given before it influences the decision.
19.17.3.2 — Transfer — When Courts Can Interfere
🔄 Transfer Orders — Scope of Judicial Review
GENERAL RULE — NO INTERFERENCE
Courts/tribunals normally CANNOT interfere with transfer orders as if they were appellate authorities — unless mala fides shown or statutory prohibition violated
M.A. Rauoof (1998) 9 SCC 466
📝 Transfer is a routine administrative exigency. Courts don't substitute their judgment for management's on where an employee should work.
EXCEPTION — EXTRANEOUS FACTORS
T.P. Senkumar v UOI (2017) 6 SCC 801
Senior police officer transferred from sensitive post mid-tenure — while defending order, government cited "poor public support in temple fire case" — not mentioned in transfer order itself
→ Court interfered: extraneous factors used to justify transfer
📝 The real reason for the transfer emerged outside the order — which was the extraneous factor used to punish an inconvenient officer.
19.17.3.3 — Preventive Detention — Irrelevant Grounds
🔒 Preventive Detention — Irrelevant / Non-existent Grounds
National Security Act, 1980 | Preventive Detention Act, 1950
CORE RULE
Grounds of detention must have reasonable relation / nexus / proximate connection with the objects for which detention can be ordered under the relevant law.
Extraneous factors cannot sustain a detention order.
📝 There is a sharp distinction between "public order" (wide, community-level) and "law and order" (local, individual). Detention is valid only for "public order" breaches — not ordinary crimes that disrupt one person or a small group.
✗ ONLY "LAW AND ORDER" — DETENTION BAD
One murderous attack on person → only a "law and order" problem, not public order threat
One solitary act of wagon-breaking → cannot persuade a reasonable person that detention is needed
(Debu v West Bengal AIR 1974 SC 816)
✓ "PUBLIC ORDER" — DETENTION VALID
Series of acts paralysing entire university (indefinite closure) = public order breach
Inciting communal hatred causing closure of schools/colleges/shops in Town of Moradabad = public order
(Wasi Uddin Ahmed AIR 1981 SC 2166)
STALE GROUNDS — DETENTION BAD
Series of acts 3 to 5 years old used as basis for preventive detention — held bad. "No rational authority can be satisfied of future mischief merely because long ago the detainee had done something evil."
Undue time-lag between offending act and detention order may vitiate the order.
💡 GST Parallel: SCN issued for FY 2018-19 based on audit observations made in 2023 — without considering subsequent compliances, refunds processed, or returns filed for intervening years — is vulnerable as stale/remote material cannot drive satisfaction to issue demand.
19.17.3.4 — Mixed Considerations — Dominant Purpose Rule
⚖️ Mixed Considerations — When Both Relevant and Irrelevant Grounds Exist
GENERAL TEST
Whether mixed considerations lead to quashing depends on: whether "the exclusion of the irrelevant or non-existent grounds would have affected the ultimate decision."
If court is satisfied authority would have passed order on relevant grounds alone → order upheld
GENERAL AREA (non-detention)
If order can be supported on any one valid finding showing grave misconduct → not invalid even if other grounds fail (dismissal of servant)
Severable portions: if invalid part is not integral and its deletion leaves order viable → court can quash only invalid part
State of Mysore v K.C. Adiga AIR 1976 SC 853: land assignment valid, illegal condition quashed — "fall of illegal condition does not lead to fall of assignment sans that condition"
📝 Courts can do surgical removal — strike the bad part, keep the rest. Like removing a defective clause from a contract while keeping the valid ones.
PREVENTIVE DETENTION — STRICTER RULE
If detention order based on MULTIPLE grounds — some good, some bad → order is INVALID even if remaining grounds are good.
Shibbanlal v State of UP AIR 1954 SC 179
📝 Personal liberty demands the highest standard. Courts cannot predict what the detaining authority's mind would have been if it had known one ground was bad — so the entire order falls.
RIDER
Court must be satisfied the vague/irrelevant ground, if excluded, "might reasonably have affected the subjective satisfaction of the authority"
If ground is comparatively unessential → order may survive
COFEPOSA §5A
Parliament legislated to change this rule for COFEPOSA: each ground deemed independent — one bad ground doesn't vitiate entire order. But questioned as possibly unconstitutional under Articles 21 and 22.
EMERGING PRINCIPLE
Administrative order based on both relevant and irrelevant (or non-existent) considerations is NOT invalid if court is satisfied that the authority would have passed the order even on the basis of the relevant and existing grounds alone, and exclusion of irrelevant grounds would not have affected its ultimate decision.
💡 GST SCN: Suppose an SCN has 5 grounds — 3 good (real ITC mismatch) and 2 bad (turnover comparison with unrelated industry). If the 3 good grounds are independently sufficient to sustain the demand, the demand survives. But if the entire demand is on the flawed comparison alone — it falls entirely.
19.17.3.5 — Difference: Improper Purpose vs Irrelevant Considerations
🔍 Improper Purpose vs Irrelevant Considerations — Are They Different?
SHORT ANSWER
The difference is "blurred and often imperceptible" — (Taylor, (1976) 36 Camb LJ 272). In practice, the same act can be characterised either way. Both make the authority ultra vires — exceeding its authority.
IMPROPER PURPOSE
Applied when irregularity is NOT apparent on face of order / reasons
Authority had power but used it for a different undisclosed objective
Used for latent irregularity
IRRELEVANT CONSIDERATIONS
Applied when wrong considerations appear on the face of the order or stated reasons
Used for apparent irregularity
COMMON RULE
If improper motive is established AND particularly when improper motive is the dominant motive → order quashed
Immaterial which ground is cited
📝 In Collector Allahabad v Raja Ram (AIR 1985 SC 1622) — SC used both labels interchangeably: "colourable exercise of power" = "improper purpose" = "extraneous and irrelevant purpose." The substance is that the officer misused the power, whatever label you give it.
19.17.4 — Ignoring Relevant Considerations
🔍 Ignoring Relevant Considerations — Distinct Ground for Invalidity
RULE
If authority ignores or does not take into account considerations relevant to the purpose of the statute → action invalid.
Even where statute does not prescribe specific considerations, court may spell them out from subject-matter, scope and purpose of the statute and quash order if authority ignored them.
📝 Not only must the officer avoid wrong reasons — he must also affirmatively consider the right ones. Missing a mandatory consideration is just as bad as considering a wrong one.
Key Principles and Cases
Case / ProvisionRelevant Consideration IgnoredOutcome
Kerala Cashew Factories Act
(Indian Nut Products v UOI (1994) 4 SCC 269)		Pre-acquisition notice did not comply with §3(1) requirements — grounds not based on relevant material. Satisfaction must be on grounds mentioned. "Person aggrieved can question the satisfaction by showing it was wholly based on irrelevant grounds — amounts to no satisfaction at all."Quashed — Pre-acquisition notice invalid
State of Bombay v KP Krishnan
AIR 1960 SC 1222		Bonus dispute — government could consider: stale dispute, inconsistency with Act or agreement. Cannot use power punitively to discipline workers for go-slow.Quashed — Refusal to refer was punitive; ignored proper factors
Rampur Distillery v Company Law Board
AIR 1970 SC 1789		Board refused managing agency renewal considering only past misconduct of director — did not consider "present acts and activities" which is also a relevant factorQuashed — Present conduct is a mandatory relevant factor ignored
Ranjit Singh v UOI
AIR 1981 SC 461		Gun manufacturing quota curtailed citing Industrial Policy Resolution — but IPR contained specific commitment to permit existing factories to continue. Relevant considerations: production capacity, quality, economic viability, law and order needs — all ignoredQuashed — "If all relevant factors are not considered, or irrelevant considerations allowed to find place, the decision is vitiated by arbitrary judgment"
Sugar Price Fixation — Essential Commodities Act §3(3C)
(Shri Sitaram Sugar Co. v UOI AIR 1990 SC 1277)		"Having regard to" = must address specified factors. Cannot ignore prescribed factors. But expression is NOT "having regard only to" — additional relevant factors may also be considered.Court will not strictly scrutinise extent if Government has addressed its mind to specified factors. BUT if specified factors not considered at all — order invalid.
Renusagar Power Co.
AIR 1988 SC 1737		State refused electricity duty exemption considering only ONE factor out of several specified in §3(4). Court: mandatory duty to consider ALL specified factors separately. Factors must be judged in adjunct to public interest — not disjointedly.Quashed — Cannot refuse after considering only one factor; must consider all factors plus other relevant ones brought to its notice
Brij Behari Lal v High Court of MP
AIR 1981 SC 594		Two conflicting confidential reports by successive CJs for overlapping periods — fact escaped High Court's attention; did not take into account all relevant materials before recommending compulsory retirementQuashed — High Court could reconsider afresh
Environmental Clearance Cases
(Shri Sachidanand Pandey v West Bengal AIR 1987 SC 1109)		"Proposition that a decision must be arrived at after taking into account all relevant considerations, eschewing all irrelevant considerations cannot for a moment be doubted." Environmental pollution is now a relevant factor in industrial sanctioning.Court upheld clearance where all relevant aspects including environment were considered
⚠️ KEY DISTINCTION — EXHAUSTIVE vs NON-EXHAUSTIVE LIST
If statute spells out considerations as an exclusive list → no other considerations can be brought in.
If list is non-exhaustive (expression: "having regard to", "inter alia", "other relevant factors") → court may be justified in referring to additional relevant considerations. Government is not the final judge of what is relevant — courts decide.
19.17.4.1 — Preventive Detention — Ignoring Relevant Considerations
🔒 Preventive Detention — Ignoring Relevant Facts = Invalid
CORE RULE
Non-consideration of any relevant material which could have influenced the mind of the detaining authority one way or the other on the question whether to make the detention order → vitiates subjective satisfaction → order illegal
FACT: ALREADY IN JAIL
If detenu is already in jail → this is a relevant fact the detaining authority must consider.
"Awareness must find its place either in the detention order or in the affidavit justifying detention." If order silent — vitiated.
📝 How can you be detained to prevent future mischief if you are already locked up? The authority must address this contradiction.
FACT: POSSIBILITY OF CRIMINAL PROSECUTION
Detaining authority must consider whether person could be prosecuted under ordinary criminal law instead of being detained.
Kanchanlal v State of Gujarat AIR 1979 SC 1945: Possibility of prosecution is relevant — failure to consider it may lead to conclusion that authority didn't apply its mind
📝 Preventive detention is not a substitute for criminal prosecution. If a person can easily be tried in court, why detain him without trial? The authority must have a reason why criminal process was inadequate.
Ashadevi Case — Non-communication of vital facts
Customs officer did not communicate to detaining authority that: (a) confessional statement was retracted; (b) detenu's lawyer's request to be present during interrogation was denied.
→ Detaining authority's satisfaction vitiated — "total non-application of mind to most material and vital facts"
KEY PRINCIPLE
Detaining authority must exercise due care and caution and act fairly and justly.
Taking into account extraneous matters → vitiates subjective satisfaction.
Equally: failure to take into account the most material facts → vitiates subjective satisfaction → detention order invalid.
💡 GST Summons/Arrest: If GST officer effects arrest under §69 CGST without considering whether the person has already filed detailed reply to SCN or has already paid the disputed tax + interest — failure to consider these relevant mitigating facts would vitiate the subjective satisfaction for arrest.
19.17.5 — Colourable Exercise of Power
🎭 Colourable Exercise of Power — What It Means
📝 "Colourable" = wearing the colour/appearance of legality while actually being illegal. Like a wolf in sheep's clothing. The authority ostensibly does X (which is legal) but really achieves Y (which is not).
Three Senses in Which Courts Use This Doctrine
SENSE 1
Power exercised ostensibly for authorised end but really to achieve another unauthorised purpose
= Overlaps with "improper purpose"
💡 Land acquired under Land Acquisition Act ostensibly for "public purpose" but really to prevent a landowner from building a cinema — Collector Allahabad v Raja Ram
SENSE 2
Power exercised for extraneous or irrelevant considerations
"Unquestionably a colourable exercise of power or fraud on power and exercise of power is vitiated" — Collector Allahabad v Raja Ram
SENSE 3
Power used as a substitute for another power — achieving through one power what should be done through a different statutory power.
e.g. Using requisition power for permanent acquisition
Key Cases on Colourable Exercise
CaseColourable Exercise Found
Collector Allahabad v Raja Ram
AIR 1985 SC 1622		Land acquired for Hindi Sahitya Sammelan "sangrahalaya" — but another plot already allotted for same purpose lying vacant unutilised. Real purpose: prevent landowner from building cinema. "Need of land for sangrahalaya is a figment of imagination conjured up to provide an ostensible purpose for acquisition." — Quashed.
Internet & Mobile Assn. of India v RBI
(2020) 10 SCC 274		RBI's 2018 crypto circular — exercise of power achieving one objective that incidentally caused collateral damage to activities of entities outside purview of RBI's statutory authority — NOT colourable exercise where RBI acted in interest of banking policy.
State of Gujarat v Suryakant Chunilal Shah
(1999) 1 SCC 529		Opinion on integrity of employee formed on material NOT part of record — arbitrary and colourable exercise of power.
Scheduled Castes Officers Welfare Council v UP
AIR 1997 SC 1451		Eligibility criteria for promotion modified to deprive reserved category officers, then restored after 6 months once general category became eligible — colourable and mala fide.
Municipal Council — Ruling Party Committee
(Hargovind Tamrakar v State of MP (2003) 10 SCC 626)		Administrator replaced with committee of 7 from ruling party only (including those with criminal cases pending) while elections kept being postponed — "not only arbitrary but also unjustified as it gives undue advantage to ruling party members by giving them back door entry" — quashed as colourable exercise.
V. Krishnamurty v State of TN
(2020) 14 SCC 408		Government withdrew land acquisition notification — builders had paid substantial amounts just before withdrawal — unholy nexus found — decision to drop proceedings was to confer advantages on builders, not effectuate public purpose — colourable exercise → quashed.
NOTE  "Colourable exercise of power" is NOT a distinct and identifiable ground of invalidity in itself — it is a colourful way of denoting already-recognised grounds (improper purpose, irrelevant considerations, mala fides, unreasonableness). But it is retained because it may catch residuary grounds of abuse not covered elsewhere.
💡 GST Parallel: Provisional attachment under §83 CGST — if attachment is of immovable property worth crores for a demand of ₹5 lakhs, or of assets of a person not connected to the investigation — this may be characterised as colourable exercise of attachment power to coerce the taxpayer rather than protect revenue.
19.17.6 — Misdirection of Fact and Law
🔍 Misdirection of Fact and Law — Ground for Judicial Review
📝 Even if an officer acts bona fide and has the right to exercise discretion — if he got the law wrong or misunderstood the facts, his order can be set aside. Good faith doesn't save a legally wrong decision.
MISDIRECTION IN LAW
Discretion is vitiated if decision-maker misconstrues the limits imposed by law on the scope of the power.
Tameside test (Lord Diplock): Whether decision-maker directed himself properly in law and in consequence took into consideration what he ought to have considered, excluding what was irrelevant
Arora case: "The Government cannot both give meaning to the words and also say that they are satisfied on the meaning given by them." — Interpretation of law is the court's function.
📝 The officer cannot be the judge of what the law means and also judge whether he complied with it. Courts decide what the law means — officers must comply.
MISDIRECTION IN FACT
Lord Scarman in Tameside: "Misunderstanding or ignorance of an established and relevant fact" can be a ground for judicial review
Laker Airways (Lord Denning): "Mistakenly" = "under the influence of a misdirection in fact or in law"
Court may inquire whether facts exist and have been taken into account; whether judgment made on proper self-direction as to facts; whether judgment not made upon other facts which ought not to have been taken into account
📝 Evaluation of facts = officer. Existence of facts and whether they were correctly assessed = court can check. Error in reading the record = reviewable mistake.
KEY CASES
CIT v Mahindra & Mahindra AIR 1984 SC 1182: Decision quashed — government "had misdirected" itself in law "by adopting wrong approach and proceeding on wrong assumption" and completely ignored legal provisions.

State of Gujarat v Krishna Cinema AIR 1971 SC 1650: Order quashed — government "did not correctly apprise itself of the facts."

Harshit Agarwal v UOI (2021) 2 SCC 710: NEET cutoff reduction — High Court misdirected itself about fact that dental seats had sufficient takers — discretion wrongly exercised.
💡 GST Application: Adjudicating officer in order under §74 states "assessee did not furnish reply" when in fact a detailed reply was filed and is on record — this is misdirection in fact = order challengeable. Officer applied his mind to non-existing state of affairs ("no reply") = error of fact vitiating satisfaction.
19.17.7 & 19.17.8 — Misrepresentation of Vital Facts | No Material
📋 Misrepresentation of Vital Facts | No Material — Two Final Grounds
§19.17.7 — Misrepresentation of Vital Facts
A.L. Ranjane v Ravindra Ishwardas Sethna
AIR 2003 SC 300

Any administrative or executive decision based on misrepresentation of vital facts is vitiated and of no avail
📝 If the facts placed before the decision-maker were wrong or twisted, even a well-intentioned decision fails. The foundation (facts) was faulty — the building (decision) must fall.
💡 GST: If the show-cause notice is based on data obtained through inspection where panchnama records were incorrectly recorded — the SCN's foundation (misrepresented material) is weak and challengeable.
§19.17.8 — No Material
Courts quash discretionary action when there existed NO MATERIAL on record for taking the action.

Any action/decision/opinion/subjective satisfaction based on NO material = invalid arbitrary exercise of power.

"There will be an error of fact when a public body is prompted by a mistaken belief in the existence of a non-existing fact or circumstance." — (S.R. Venkataraman v UOI AIR 1979 SC 49)
📝 You cannot pull a decision out of thin air. There must be actual material on record — documents, statements, records — to justify the action. "No material" = arbitrary action = invalid.
💡 GST: SCN for ₹50 lakh demand where the only material is "intelligence input" with no corroborating document, no statement, no books seized — constitutes action on "no material." This is a strong ground to challenge under Article 226.
D. Nataraja Mudaliar v STA Madras
AIR 1979 SC 114 — Carriage permit renewal refused because ITDC buses were adequate and new permit would cause "unhealthy competition"

SC: "Some objective assessment to exclude the petitioner based on tangible data is the minimum for a judicial negation of a fundamental right. The reference to 'unhealthy competition' is baffling. If there are 300 buses and one more is sought to be added, what is the ill-health in the traffic system?" → Decision based on "no material" — quashed.
