💡 GST parallel: Registration cancellation on ground that "business appears non-genuine" without visiting the premises, without calling the person, without specific document showing non-genuineness — is action on "no material" and is legally unsustainable.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter XIX | Discretionary Powers (III)
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, First Appellate Authority, GSTAT, and High Court. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Padfield v Minister of Agriculture & Fisheries
House of Lords | (1968) 1 All ER 694 | Lord Reid
Key Ratio / Principle
"Parliament must have conferred the discretion on the Minister with the intention that it should be used to promote the policy and objects of the Act. The policy and objects of the Act must be determined by construing the Act as a whole, and statutory construction is always a matter of law for the court... If the Minister, by reason of his having misconstrued the Act or for any other reason, so uses his discretion as to thwart or run counter to the policy and objects of the Act, then our law would be very defective if persons aggrieved were not entitled to the protection of the court."
When to Use — When GST officer claims "it is my discretion" to refuse something (adjournment, inspection of file, consideration of a specific plea). Cite Padfield: the discretion must be exercised to promote the CGST Act's objective of correct tax determination — not for administrative convenience or officer's preference. Also cite when officer ignores a mandatory ground and uses an extraneous one.
Wednesbury Corporation — Lord Green M.R.
Associated Provincial Picture Houses Ltd. v Wednesbury Corporation | (1948) 1 KB 223
Key Ratio / Principle
An authority exercising discretion must: (a) take all relevant factors into account; (b) exclude all irrelevant factors from its consideration; (c) reach a decision which is neither perverse nor irrational. "If, in the statute conferring the discretion, there is to be found, expressly or by implication, matters to which the authority exercising the discretion ought to have regard, then, in exercising the discretion, they must have regard to those matters. Conversely, if the nature of the subject-matter and the general interpretation of the Act make it clear that certain matters would not be germane to the matter in question, they must disregard those matters."
When to Use — Foundational citation for any attack on an adjudication order or appellate order. Enumerate: (i) which relevant factor the officer ignored; (ii) which irrelevant factor the officer considered. Then cite Wednesbury as the test the order fails.
Shalini Soni v Union of India
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1981 SC 431 | (1980) 4 SCC 544
Key Ratio / Principle
"It is an unwritten rule of law, constitutional and administrative, that whenever a decision-making function is entrusted to the subjective satisfaction of a statutory functionary, there is an implicit obligation to apply his mind to pertinent and proximate matters only, eschewing the irrelevant and the remote."
When to Use — Every GST provision uses "is satisfied" or "has reason to believe" — these are subjective satisfaction provisions. Cite Shalini Soni to establish that the officer's satisfaction must be based on specific, proximate, material facts — not vague impressions, remote data, or irrelevant comparisons. Especially powerful against Orders-in-Original that cite sweeping grounds without document-level specificity.
Baldev Raj v Union of India — "Absolute Power is Anathema"
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1981 SC 70 | (1980) 4 SCC 321 | Krishna Iyer J
Key Ratio / Principle
"The appropriate authority must form the requisite opinion — not subjective satisfaction but objective and bona fide and based on relevant material... Absolute power is anathema under our constitutional order. 'Absolute' merely means wide, not more. Naked and arbitrary exercise of power is bad in law... Any order which materially suffers from the blemish of overlooking or ignoring wilfully or otherwise, vital facts bearing on the decision is bad in law. Likewise, any action which irrationally digs up obsolete circumstances and obsessively reaches a decision based thereon, cannot be sustained. Legality depends on regard of the totality of material facts viewed in a holistic perspective."
When to Use — (a) When old audit reports/old SCN from several years ago are being used as basis for a new demand without considering recent compliances. (b) When officer ignores the assessee's recent clean track record. (c) When arguing that "absolute" discretion given to an officer under CGST Act does not mean the officer can ignore all rules of fair play.
Collector Allahabad v Raja Ram — Colourable Exercise of Power
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1985 SC 1622 | (1985) 3 SCC 1
Key Ratio / Principle
"Where power is conferred to achieve a certain purpose it has been repeatedly reiterated that the power must be exercised reasonably and in good faith to effectuate the purpose. Where power is exercised for extraneous or irrelevant considerations or reasons, it is unquestionably a colourable exercise of power or fraud on power and the exercise of power is vitiated." The power to acquire land must be exercised bona fide for the statutory purpose and for none other. If exercised for an extraneous, irrelevant or non-germane consideration, the acquisition authority can be charged with legal mala fides.
When to Use — When attacking provisional attachments made under §83 CGST without nexus to the pending proceeding; search and seizure under §67 where documents seized have no connection to the alleged evasion; or registration cancellation targeting a specific taxpayer for non-tax reasons. Cite to establish: exercise of statutory power for extraneous reasons = colourable exercise = vitiated = quashable.
R.L. Arora v State of Uttar Pradesh — Courts Interpret Law, Not Government
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1962 SC 764 | (1962) 32 Comp Cas 268
Key Ratio / Principle
"The Government cannot both give meaning to the words and also say that they are satisfied on the meaning given by them." It was for the Court to interpret the words in the statute and to say what those words meant and to see whether the government was satisfied according to the meaning given by the Court.
When to Use — When Department proposes its own interpretation of "supply," "exempt," "works contract," "composite supply" etc. and then says it is "satisfied" that the assessee violated the Act based on that self-serving interpretation. Cite Arora: the Department cannot be both the interpreter of law and the judge of compliance. Courts decide what the law means.
State of Bombay v K.P. Krishnan — Punitive Spirit Contrary to Act
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1960 SC 1222 | 1961 (1) SCR 227
Key Ratio / Principle
"A claim for bonus is based on the consideration that by their contribution to the profits of the employer the employees are entitled to claim a share in the said profits, and so any punitive action taken by the Government by refusing to refer for adjudication an industrial dispute for bonus would, in our opinion, be wholly inconsistent with the object of the Act." The government had acted in a punitive spirit and this was contrary to the objective of the statute.
When to Use — When GST officer uses the adjudication process punitively (e.g., repeatedly summoning compliant taxpayers, selectively invoking §74 when §73 applies, demanding stay of refunds without adequate legal basis). Cite KP Krishnan: statutory power cannot be used to punish — it must serve the Act's object of correct determination of tax liability, not harassment.
Shibbanlal v State of Uttar Pradesh — Multiple Grounds, One Bad = Order Falls (Detention)
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1954 SC 179 | 1954 CrLJ 456
Key Ratio / Principle
"Where power is vested in a statutory authority to deprive the liberty of a subject on its subjective satisfaction with reference to specified matters, if that satisfaction is stated to be based on a number of grounds or for a variety of reasons, all taken together, and if some out of them are found to be non-existent or irrelevant, the very exercise of that power is bad. This is so because the matter being one for subjective satisfaction, it must be properly based on all the reasons on which it purports to be based. If some out of them are found to be non-existent or irrelevant, the Court cannot predicate what the subjective satisfaction of the said authority would have been on the exclusion of those grounds or reasons."
When to Use — When attacking SCN or Order that has multiple grounds — and you can demolish some of them as non-existent or irrelevant. Where the demand rests on multiple grounds holistically (not independently), demolishing even one key ground should bring down the whole demand. While the strict Shibbanlal rule applies to preventive detention, the general principle (subjective satisfaction must be based on all stated reasons) is useful in GST arrest / provisional attachment challenges.
D. Nataraja Mudaliar v STA Madras — "No Material" Ground
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1979 SC 114 | (1978) 4 SCC 290
Key Ratio / Principle
"Some objective assessment to exclude the petitioner based on tangible data is the minimum for a judicial negation of a fundamental right. The reference to 'unhealthy competition' is baffling. If there are 300 or more buses and one more is sought to be added, what is the ill-health in the traffic system that will be injected by this addition? The decision may be regarded as being based on 'no material' and taking into account irrelevant considerations."
When to Use — When the SCN or order relies on vague assertions ("transactions appear suspicious," "profile does not match") without any specific document, statement, or quantified material. Cite Nataraja Mudaliar: at minimum there must be tangible, objective material — subjective impressions not backed by any record = "no material" = invalid exercise of power.
Sama Aruna v State of Telangana — Stale Past Instances = Improper Purpose
Supreme Court of India | (2018) 12 SCC 150
Key Ratio / Principle
The so-called satisfaction for preventive arrest was taken on the basis of irrelevant or extraneous facts in disregard of the purpose and procedure of preventive detention law which the detaining authority must be presumed to know. The detention order was passed by relying on stale incidents which had no relevance in the immediate context for preventive detention affecting the detenu's right of personal liberty.
When to Use — When Department relies on: old SCN that was dropped without issuance of order; old audit objections from prior years that were resolved; transactions from years ago in a period-barred assessment. Argue: stale incidents have no proximate relevance — using them as the basis for a fresh demand in current period is an exercise of power based on extraneous and remote material = improper and invalid.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter XIX — Discretionary Powers (III)

⚖️ Chapter XIX — Discretionary Powers (III)

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Upheld
Prohibited / Quashed / Invalid
Condition / Test / Unless
Optional / Alternative / Discretion
Neutral Reference / Principle
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
19.17.A — No Material / Subjective Satisfaction
19.17.9 — Judicial Discretion
19.17.10 — Unreasonableness (Wednesbury)
19.17.10.1 — Preventive Detention
19.17.10.2 — Article 14 & Unreasonableness
19.17.12 — Proportionality (England)
19.17.12.2/3/4/5/6 — Proportionality (Global)
19.17.12.7 — Proportionality in India
19.18 — Proportionality & Arts. 14/19/21
19.18.1 — Proportionality & Service Law
19.18.2/3/4 — Guidelines / Delegation / Environment
19.19 — Failure to Exercise Discretion
19.19.1/2 — Mechanical Exercise / Non-Application of Mind
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
19.17.A — "No Material" / Subjective Satisfaction — Order Without Material is Invalid
⚖️ Discretionary Power Requires Material to Form Requisite Satisfaction
Orders passed without material on record are invalid — Court may quash
📝 When a law gives an authority power to act "if satisfied" — the authority cannot just pass an order out of the blue. There must be real facts / evidence on the file before it acts. If there is nothing on file, the court can strike down the order.
GENERAL RULE — Courts can go into the question:
"Was there in fact any material or evidence for formation of the requisite opinion?"
(Court CANNOT probe adequacy / sufficiency — only existence)
📝 Courts won't say "this evidence is too weak." They will only check — was there ANY evidence at all? If none, order falls. If some evidence exists, courts won't substitute their view.
QUASHED
Rohtas Industries v S.D. Agarwal
AIR 1969 SC 707
No material to form subjective opinion → order passed without applying mind → INVALID
QUASHED
Nasir Ahmad v Asst. Custodian Evacuee Property
AIR 1980 SC 1157
Notice issued when no material/evidence existed for initiating proceedings → notice + all subsequent proceedings quashed
QUASHED
S.R. Venkataraman v UOI
AIR 1979 SC 491
Compulsory retirement — nothing on government file against him — quashed as abuse of power
QUASHED
D. Ramaswami v State of Tamil Nadu
AIR 1980 SC 269
"In the face of promotion just a few months earlier and nothing even mildly suggestive of ineptitude... impossible to sustain."
Income Tax — "Reason to Believe" under S.34, IT Act 1922 (now S.147)
TWO CONDITIONS
(i) ITO must have "reason to believe"
(ii) In consequence of "information in his possession"
→ that income underassessed due to non-disclosure by assessee
STANDARD
Chhugamal Rajpal: Belief must be of an honest and reasonable person based on reasonable grounds
May act on direct or circumstantial evidence
NOT on mere suspicion, gossip or rumour
MAVJI — Two Points on S.34(1)(b)
(i) Word "information" = widest amplitude
(ii) Discretion of ITO — not plenary — controlled by "reason to believe"
No application where ITO merely reopens without fresh facts/material
QUASHED
MP Industries Ltd. v ITO
AIR 1970 SC 1011
No material to show ITO had reason to form belief → ITO filed no affidavit → proceedings quashed
GST APPLICATION
SCN / Assessment / Search / Seizure powers under GST require "reason to believe" — if officer passes order without any material on file, or if SCN is issued on mere suspicion, the entire proceeding is vulnerable to challenge on "no material" ground. Demand of tax without any material = quashable.
💡 GST officer issues SCN for alleged suppression without any specific transaction identified — no documents, no witness, no audit report cited. Assessee can challenge: "No material exists on which the officer could have formed the requisite belief — proceedings are void ab initio."
19.17.9 — Judicial Discretion
⚖️ Judicial Discretion — Meaning, Scope & Limits
📝 "Judicial discretion" means a judge can choose from lawful options — but not act like a king. The word "judicial" limits the discretion; the word "discretion" gives some freedom. Together: the judge must follow law, not personal whim.
DEFINITION
Certain latitude/liberty accorded by statute to a judge / ministerial/administrative official in adjudicating matters brought before him
📝 It's like a referee who has rules to follow but also some judgment calls allowed within those rules.
EFFECT OF "JUDICIAL"
Limits and regulates the exercise
Prevents it from being wholly absolute, capricious, or exempt from review
EFFECT OF "DISCRETION"
Permits the judge to consider — what are all the circumstances? What is the purpose for which power is given? Allows considerations of convenience/utility
LIMITS
NOT personal discretion
NOT whim/caprice
Must be "sound discretion guided by fixed legal principles"
Usually on procedure / punishment / costs — NOT vested substantive rights
Key Principles from Cases
Registrar, Trademarks v Ashok Chandra Rakhit
AIR 1955 SC 558
"Exercise of power conferred on Registrar always remained a matter of discretion — not capriciously or arbitrarily but according to sound principles laid down for exercise of all judicial discretion."
Rampur Distillery v Company Law Board
AIR 1970 SC 1789
Investment of power in Government under S.326(2) Companies Act carries duty to act judicially — hold inquiry consistent with natural justice, consider relevant, ignore irrelevant, reach conclusion without bias
M.I. Builders v Redhey Shyam Sahu
AIR 1999 SC 2468
Judicial discretion CANNOT be exercised to encourage illegality. Courts are free from statutory fetters but "Judges are not entitled to exercise power wearing the robes of judicial discretion and pass orders based solely on their personal predilections."
Aharon Barak (as cited in Food Corp. of India v Jagdish Balaram Bahira)
(2017) 8 SCC 670
"Discretion assumes the freedom to choose among several lawful alternatives. Discretion does not exist when there is but one lawful option." Courts cannot give direction contrary to a statute in exercise of judicial discretion.
GST APPLICATION
When Appellate Authority / GSTAT exercises discretion in granting stay or reducing penalty — it must follow this principle. A stay cannot be refused arbitrarily. Equally, interest/penalty cannot be waived beyond what law permits — "discretion does not exist when there is but one lawful option."
19.17.10 — Unreasonableness — The Wednesbury Standard
⚖️ Unreasonableness as Ground of Judicial Review
Associated Provincial Picture Houses Ltd. v Wednesbury Corporation (1948) 1 KB 223
📝 Just because you disagree with a government decision does not mean it is illegal. The court can only strike down a decision that is SO unreasonable that NO sensible person could ever have made it. This is a very high bar — the decision must border on absurdity.
LORD GREENE'S TEST
"If a decision on a competent matter is so unreasonable that no reasonable authority could ever have come to it, then the courts can interfere."
"Something so absurd that no sensible person could ever dream that it lay within the power of the authority"
"To prove a case of that kind would require something overwhelming."
📝 Imagine a teacher dismissed only because she has red hair. THAT is Wednesbury unreasonableness — not just wrong, but shockingly absurd.
LORD GREENE — COMPREHENSIVE MEANING
Authority must:
• Direct itself properly in law
• Call attention to matters it must consider
• Exclude irrelevant matters
Failure = "unreasonably" acting
Also: acting on extraneous considerations / improper purpose / mala fide
LORD DENNING in Breen v Amalgamated Engineering Union
"Discretion of a statutory body is never unfettered. The statutory body must be guided by relevant considerations and not irrelevant. If influenced by extraneous considerations — decision cannot stand, even if in good faith."
Padfield v Minister of Agriculture — landmark.
COURT CANNOT
Exercise overriding power to decide what is reasonable
Two reasonable persons may reach opposite conclusions without being unreasonable
Court must NOT substitute its view for that of the administrator
GCHQ — Lord Diplock's "Irrationality" Formulation
Council of Civil Service Unions v Minister for Civil Service (CCSU) (1985) 1 AC 374
Lord Diplock classified grounds of judicial review as: (i) Illegality, (ii) Irrationality, (iii) Procedural Impropriety
"Irrationality" = Wednesbury unreasonableness = decision "so outrageous in its defiance of logic or accepted moral standards that no sensible person who had applied his mind to the question could have arrived at it."
📝 "Irrationality" is just a new name for the same old Wednesbury test. Lord Diplock also hinted that "proportionality" may be added as a fourth ground in the future.
Key Applications — Wednesbury Upheld / Applied
Hall & Co. v Shoreham UDC (1964)
Condition to construct road on plaintiff's land — no compensation — held "utterly unreasonable" — condition void
Evans v Collins (1964)
LCC charging "unreasonably low" rents — court showed reluctance to interfere in social policy — no prima facie unfairness found
Tameside Case
Secretary of State disagreed with Council's policy — NOT unreasonableness. Council's scheme was "within range of alternatives that a reasonable local council might choose."
Roberts v Hopwood (1925) 1 AC 578
Borough Council paying fixed wages regardless of work/cost of living index — Lord Wrenbury: "discretion does not empower a man to do what he likes... he must do not what he likes but what he ought."
GST APPLICATION
When a GST officer makes an assessment / penalty order that is grossly disproportionate to the facts, or considers irrelevant matters (e.g., political connections, unrelated transactions), or ignores relevant materials — the order is vulnerable under Wednesbury unreasonableness. This ground is available in writ jurisdiction before High Court.
💡 GST officer imposes maximum penalty under S.122 ignoring that the assessee had bona fide belief and had deposited tax voluntarily before SCN — penalty is "unreasonable" as no sensible officer could have imposed maximum penalty in such circumstances.
19.17.10.1 — Unreasonableness in Preventive Detention
🔐 Unreasonableness — Preventive Detention Cases
Courts apply more scrutinising attitude where personal liberty is involved
📝 Preventive detention = locking someone up WITHOUT a trial, to prevent them from doing something in the future. Courts check this extra carefully — the "reasonable man" test is applied with greater strictness where liberty is at stake.
QUASHED
Debu v State of West Bengal
AIR 1974 SC 816
One solitary act of wagon-breaking → Court "failed to see how one solitary isolated act could possibly persuade a reasonable person to reach the satisfaction that unless petitioner detained, he would in all probability indulge in further acts."
Satisfaction = "colourable" — no basis
📝 One mistake = not enough to lock someone up preventively. You need a pattern suggesting future risk.
EXCEPTION — Single Act May Suffice
Theft of sophisticated signal material of railways — required technical skill — single act implied course of previous conduct → detention upheld
KEY TEST
Whether in circumstances, a reasonable man would come to the decision to detain on the basis of material available
Courts come very near probing subjective satisfaction on merits in personal liberty cases
Test of "reasonable man" is a flexible test
Pushpadevi v Wadhwan
AIR 1987 SC 1748
Court CANNOT examine adequacy/sufficiency of material
BUT CAN always go into whether there was in fact material/evidence — apart from its adequacy — for formation of requisite opinion
19.17.10.2 — Article 14 of Constitution as Broader Unreasonableness Ground
🇮🇳 Article 14 — Equality Clause as Control over Arbitrary Administrative Action
India has broader doctrine than Wednesbury — Article 14 strikes at arbitrariness
📝 In England, courts can only review on the narrow Wednesbury ground. In India, Article 14 (equality) independently controls arbitrary state action. Any arbitrary decision = violates equality = unconstitutional. This gives Indian courts wider power.
GENERAL PROPOSITION
Article 14 strikes at arbitrariness in state action and ensures fairness
Arbitrary action = negation of equality = violation of Article 14
Maneka Gandhi v UOI: "Principle of reasonableness pervades Article 14 like a brooding omnipresence"
Express Newspapers v UOI
AIR 1986 SC 872
Government order forfeiting lease + demolition of press building — held arbitrary — "Arbitrariness was the antithesis of Article 14."
H.D. Vora v State of Maharashtra
AIR 1984 SC 866
Requisition order of 30 years — by nature of temporary duration — cannot continue indefinitely — held unreasonable
INDIA v ENGLAND — TEST DIFFERENCE
England: Is the action unreasonable? So absurd no reasonable man could reach it?
India: Is the impugned decision reasonable? Courts quash if no "reasonable" body would have reached it on the material before the decision-maker
INDIAN STANDARD — More Positive
Courts quash if "no reasonable body would have reached the same on the basis of the material on which the decision-maker has reached the decision" (Rasheed Case)
An action which is oppressive, palpably absurd or perverse is not reasonable — India's test can cover administrative action below the line of absurdity (broader than Wednesbury)
💡 GST: Assessment order made without considering assessee's reply or documents submitted — ignoring relevant material — violates both Wednesbury standard AND Article 14 arbitrariness doctrine. Writ under Art. 226 maintainable.
19.17.12 — Proportionality — Position in England
⚖️ Doctrine of Proportionality
Is there a responsible relationship between the objective sought and the means used?
📝 Proportionality asks: was the government's action proportionate to the problem it was trying to solve? Like using a sledgehammer to crack a nut — even if the action is technically within the law, if it is wildly excessive compared to the objective, it can be struck down.
Halsbury's Laws of England — Basic Statement
Court will quash exercise of discretionary powers where there is NOT a responsible relationship between the objective sought to be achieved and the means used — or where punishments imposed by administrative bodies are wholly out of proportion to the relevant misconduct
CCSU Case — Lord Diplock's Three Grounds + Proportionality as Possible Fourth
Ground 1
Illegality
Ground 2
Irrationality
(Wednesbury)
Ground 3
Procedural Impropriety
FUTURE?
Ground 4
Proportionality
Recognised in European Community administrative law — Lord Diplock mooted its possible adoption
Debate — Separate Ground or Facet of Unreasonableness?
VIEW 1 — Courts (Cautious)
Proportionality = only a facet of unreasonableness, NOT independent ground
Risk: Courts tempted to forget supervisory nature and substitute their own view
VIEW 2 — Scholars
Proportionality ought to be separate ground — gives flexibility to veto harsh administrative decisions
Jowell & Lester: "The requirement that the decision-maker must have a sense of proportion seems ripe for full recognition as an English principle of administrative law."
Strict Scrutiny — Recent England Position (Fundamental Freedoms)
Administrative action affecting freedom of expression or liberty → courts apply "strict scrutiny" test
Wednesbury principles NO LONGER applied in such cases
Spycatcher Case: Lord Goff — no inconsistency between Convention and common law
R v Secy. for Home Dept. ex p. Simms: Right of prisoner to interview journalist upheld — Lord Hobhouse: administrator's policy was disproportionate
📝 For ordinary admin decisions — Wednesbury test. For decisions affecting fundamental freedoms — courts use a stricter "proportionality" or "strict scrutiny" test, giving much less deference to the administrator.
19.17.12.2–12.6 — Proportionality — Global Comparative Position
Jurisdiction Key Principle / Standard Leading Case / Source GST Relevance
France Overriding principle — admin act is proper only if reasonable (opposite of capricious / arbitrary). Administrator must produce reasons before Conseil d'Etat. Also: détournement de pouvoir = using power for purpose outside its intended scope. Broader than common law. Violation de la loi — Conseil d'Etat can quash action not in accord with French legal system even if no statute infringed. In GST: if officer uses inspection power (S.67) for a purpose other than its intended scope = détournement de pouvoir analogy
Germany Three elements: (1) Suitable — means must rationally fit the objective; (2) Necessary — least restrictive means available; (3) Proportionate — injury to individual not disproportionate to benefit to public Federal German Constitutional Court GST penalty must be suitable + necessary + not grossly excessive compared to the default
Israel Three elements: (1) Means should rationally fit the legislative purpose; (2) Authority must adopt means that do not injure individual more than necessary; (3) Injury to individual not disproportionate to benefit to public Recognised as separate ground — different from unreasonableness. Cited in Om Kumar v UOI (2001) 2 SCC 386 Assessment or penalty under GST must satisfy all three tests — not just "some legal basis exists"
Canada Court assesses: gravity of offence, personal characteristics of offender, particular circumstances — to determine what range of sentence is appropriate to punish, rehabilitate, deter. Grossly disproportionate punishment = unconstitutional Smith v Queen, 40 DLR (4th) 435 — S.5(2) Narcotic Control Act minimum 7-year sentence held unconstitutional as grossly disproportionate In GST prosecution — minimum mandatory penalty challenged as disproportionate to the specific offence
European Community "Most important general legal principle in Common Market Law." Means must correspond to the importance of the aim AND must be necessary for its achievement. Differential treatment must be proportionate (Lithgow v UK — Art.14 ECHR) Council of Europe Recommendation 1980; Denkavit France v Fonds D'Orientation (1987) 3 CLR 202 In anti-dumping / customs / trade cases — proportionality is key challenge tool
19.17.12.7 — Proportionality in India — Position
🇮🇳 Proportionality in India — Applied Since 1950 (Legislative Action) & Limited Administrative Application
📝 India started applying proportionality to laws (legislation) since 1950. For administrative actions — courts usually apply Wednesbury (secondary review). For fundamental freedoms — courts apply proportionality as primary review. For service punishment — Wednesbury only unless shockingly disproportionate.
Om Kumar v UOI — AIR 2000 SC 3689 — Definitive Framework
By 'proportionality' we mean: whether, while regulating exercise of fundamental rights, the appropriate or least-restrictive choice of measures has been made by the legislature or the administrator so as to achieve the object of the legislation or purpose of the administrative order. Court will see that legislature and administrative authority 'maintain a proper balance between adverse effects which the legislation or administrative order may have on the rights, liberties or interests of persons keeping in mind the purpose which they were intended to serve.'
WHERE NO FUNDAMENTAL FREEDOMS INVOLVED
Courts/tribunals play only secondary role
Primary judgment = reasonableness → with administrative authority
Secondary judgment of Court = Wednesbury + CCSU principles
Court checks if executive reasonably arrived at decision
WHERE FUNDAMENTAL FREEDOMS AFFECTED
Whether Courts will apply proportionality and assume primary role — left open — to be decided in appropriate case
Will have primary role only if freedoms under Arts. 19/21 involved — NOT for Art.14
AN ADMINISTRATIVE DECISION IS PROPORTIONATE IF:
(a) Objective to curtail fundamental rights = important
(b) Measures taken have rational connection with objective
(c) Means impair rights of individuals no more than necessary
Key Indian Cases — Proportionality Applied
Mineral Development Ltd. v State of Bihar
AIR 1960 SC 468
Licence cancelled for trivial technical non-compliance — "contraventions alleged, even if true, appear to be too trivial for the drastic action taken" — quashed on proportionality
Ranjit Thakur v UOI
AIR 1987 SC 2386
Court-martial punishment "strikingly disproportionate" — quashed. "The sentence has to suit the offence and the offender. It should not be vindictive or unduly harsh." Doctrine of proportionality ensures even in matters exclusively with Court-Martial, outrageous defiance of logic = reviewable.
Akbar Badruddin Giwani v Collector of Customs
AIR 1990 SC 1579
Appellant imported calcareous stone believing it importable under OGL — customs imposed Rs.10 lac fine + confiscation — held "extremely harsh, excessive and unreasonable bearing in mind the bona fides" — penalty quashed
Internet and Mobile Association v Reserve Bank of India
RBI circular banning banks from serving crypto exchanges — tested on proportionality — no evidence any regulated entity suffered loss on account of banking services to VC exchanges — circular read down/struck
GST APPLICATION
Maximum penalty / full interest / full confiscation imposed in bona fide error cases without considering degree of harm, mental element, or actual loss to revenue = disproportionate. Challenge: penalty must have rational connection to gravity of default — "all powers have legal limits" (Ranjit Thakur).
💡 GST: Transporter's vehicle seized and goods confiscated — driver had e-way bill but minor technical defect (one digit wrong in vehicle number). Maximum penalty imposed. Proportionality challenge: harm = nil; error = bona fide; measure = wildly excessive. Akin to Mineral Development rationale.
19.18 — Proportionality & Administrative Action under Articles 19(1), 21 & 14
⚖️ Proportionality as Primary Review — Art. 19(1) / 21 Administrative Action
📝 India has fundamental rights guaranteed since 1950. When government restricts these rights through administrative action — courts don't just check Wednesbury. They apply proportionality as PRIMARY reviewers — meaning courts look at the merits of the balance struck, not just whether it is wholly absurd.
PRIMARY vs SECONDARY REVIEW
Primary review = Court itself weighs the balance — is restriction proportionate to fundamental right affected?
Secondary review = Court only checks if administrator reasonably arrived at decision (Wednesbury)
📝 Primary = court acts as a judge of the merits of the balance. Secondary = court only checks if the decision is within the range of reasonableness.
FOR ART. 14 — ARBITRARY ACTION
Question = whether order is rational/reasonable
Test = Wednesbury (secondary review)
Court checks: did administrator act illegally? Omit relevant? Take irrelevant? Absurd/perverse?
NOT: Is the decision the best possible decision?
FOR ARTS. 19/21 — FUNDAMENTAL FREEDOMS
Courts apply proportionality as primary reviewing authority
Court asks: is the restriction the least restrictive? Is it proportionate to the fundamental right affected?
Supreme Court Summary (Om Kumar — Canara Bank v VK Awasthy)
To judge validity of any administrative order → Wednesbury test normally applied → find if decision was illegal / procedurally improper / no sensible decision-maker could have arrived at it on material available
Court cannot substitute its decision for that of administrator
Court will not interfere unless illegal / procedurally improper / irrational (outrageous defiance of logic)
💡 GST: Pre-show cause notice consultations (under CBIC Circular) — if officer refuses to give opportunity under Art.19/21 principles — court applies proportionality as primary reviewer. But for a simple assessment dispute under S.73 — court uses Wednesbury secondary review only.
19.18.1 — Proportionality and Punishments in Service Law
⚖️ Quantum of Punishment in Disciplinary Proceedings — Proportionality Rules
GENERAL RULE
Disciplinary authority + Appellate Authority have exclusive power to determine quantum of punishment
Courts normally DO NOT substitute their own view on quantum
Jurisdiction = limited — cannot be exercised without sufficient reasons
WHEN COURT INTERFERES
Only when punishment "shocks the conscience of the Court"
Ranjit Thakur: "shockingly disproportionate"
Sardar Singh: "severe and wholly disproportionate"
B.C. Chaturvedi: punishment awarded shocks conscience → normally remit back to authority → in exceptional rare cases only, substitute punishment
CANNOT INTERFERE
Where no issue of fundamental freedoms
Where Art.14 arbitrariness is questioned — court confined to Wednesbury (secondary review)
Court will NOT apply proportionality as primary reviewing court in disciplinary cases
CORRUPTION / SERIOUS CHARGES
Pravan Kumar v UOI (2020) 9 SCC 471
Charges of corruption + fabrication + intimidation — dismissal NOT disproportionate
"For grave offences — need to send clear message of deterrence. Corruption/misappropriation/gross indiscipline — deal with strictly."
Factors Court Considers in Proportionality of Punishment
Mental set-up of delinquent
Type of duty performed
Nature & culpability of misconduct
Financial effect & societal implication
Position of trust? Honesty + integrity inbuilt? → must deal with iron hands
GST APPLICATION
GST officer imposing maximum penalty for a technical violation (e.g., one day delay in filing GSTR-3B, no revenue loss) = disproportionate punishment. GST law must be applied with the principle that "varying degrees of misdeeds" cannot be dealt with "the same broad stroke." Penalty must match the gravity of the default.
19.18.2/3/4 — Violation of Guidelines | Unauthorised Delegation | Environmental Law
⚖️ Three Specific Grounds of Invalidity of Discretionary Action
19.18.2 — Violation of Guidelines
Exercise of discretionary power by Government NOT justiciable merely on ground of violation of guidelines framed by Government — purely executive instructions
Exception: Unless mala fides, extraneous consideration, or arbitrariness also present
(J.R. Raghupatiy v State of AP, AIR 1988 SC 1681)
📝 Government circular / guideline violation alone does not get you to court. You need something more — like bad faith or irrelevant consideration.
💡 GST: CBIC Instruction on computation of interest rate — deviation alone not challengeable unless officer also acted mala fide or arbitrarily.
19.18.3 — Unauthorised Delegation
Where statute entrusts function to a particular authority → function must be exercised by THAT authority → cannot be delegated to another
Anirudhsinhji v State of Gujarat: discretion exercised under direction/instruction of higher authority = failure to exercise discretion
Vine v National Dock Labour Board (HL): delegation to Port Manager = ultra vires → void
📝 If Parliament gave power to Officer A, Officer A cannot pass it to Officer B — even if B is senior. The law chose A for a reason.
💡 GST: S.75(1) — proper officer defined strictly. If SCN issued by officer not designated as "proper officer" for that assessee — delegation is unauthorised → SCN is void.
19.18.4 — Environmental Law
Lafarge Umiam Mining Private Limited v UOI (2011) 7 SCC 335
Chief Justice S.H. Kapadia: Doctrine of proportionality must be applied to environmental matters as part of judicial review
Principles:
• Have all relevant factors been taken into account?
• Any extraneous factors influenced decision?
• Strictly in accordance with legislative policy?
• Consistent with principles of sustainable development?
Court must review decision-making process to ensure MoEF decision is fair, fully informed, correct, free from bias
💡 GST: Mining lease / forest clearance decisions — challenge ground available if MoEF / state government failed to apply proportionality in restricting mining rights — connects with GST on royalties.
19.19 — Failure to Exercise Discretion
⚖️ Failure to Exercise Discretion — Core Principle
Discretion conferred = Duty to exercise it independently — cannot be divested or delegated
📝 When law gives an authority discretion, it MUST use that discretion itself — by genuinely applying its mind to the facts. It cannot rubber-stamp someone else's decision, act mechanically, or follow orders from a superior without independent thought. If it does, the decision is as if no discretion was exercised at all.
LORD ESHER MR — Queen of Prosecution v Verstry of St. Pancras (1890) 24 QBD 371
"If the people who have to exercise public duty by exercising their discretion take into account matters which the Courts consider not to be proper for the guidance of their discretion then, in the eye of law they have not exercised their discretion."
Anirudhsinhji v State of Gujarat AIR 1995 SC 2390
A statutory authority vested with jurisdiction must exercise it according to its OWN discretion. Discretion exercised under direction or instruction of some higher authority = failure to exercise discretion altogether
Indian Railway Construction Co. Ltd. v Ajay Kumar AIR 2003 SC 1843
"In general, a discretion must be exercised only by the authority to which it is committed. The authority must genuinely address itself to the matter before it, it must not act under the dictates of another body or disable itself from exercising a discretion in each individual case."
Situations where failure to exercise discretion arises
(1) Mechanical Exercise — acting automatically on subordinate's recommendation without own mind application
(2) Non-Application of Mind — casual exercise without due care and consideration of merits
(3) Acting under Dictation — following higher authority's direction to exercise discretion in a particular manner
(4) Fettering Discretion — adopting a rigid policy that prevents case-by-case consideration
GST APPLICATION
GST assessment orders must reflect independent application of mind by the proper officer — not just copy-paste of audit objections. If SCN/Order is a verbatim reproduction of audit para without any independent evaluation = failure to exercise discretion = order void. Also: if Commissioner grants approval for search under S.67 merely on police recommendation without own application of mind = failure.
💡 Assessment order under S.73 that merely adopts SCN allegations without considering assessee's reply = non-application of mind. Courts can quash such orders under Art. 226 — "the authority has not genuinely addressed itself to the matter before it."
19.19.1 & 19.19.2 — Mechanical Exercise of Power & Non-Application of Mind
⚖️ Two Related but Distinct Grounds of Invalidity
19.19.1 — Mechanical Exercise of Power
Authority passes order mechanically without itself considering facts and circumstances
Cause: Inertia / laziness / total reliance on subordinates
📝 Rubber-stamping = mechanical exercise. The authority signs off without thinking — just follows whatever the subordinate recommends, automatically.
Emperor v Sibnath Banerji AIR 1945 PC 156
Home Secretary issued preventive detention order automatically whenever police recommended it — never personally satisfied himself from materials — Privy Council: personal satisfaction = condition precedent → order quashed
Merugu Satyanarayana v State of AP AIR 1982 SC 1543
Affidavit in justification of detention filed by subordinate — not detaining authority — subordinate "does not say how he came to know about subjective satisfaction of DM" → authority completely abdicated functions
19.19.2 — Non-Application of Mind
Authority exercises discretion in casual manner without taking due care and caution
Does not consider the merits of the case carefully
Thin difference from mechanical: here, authority tries to apply mind but does so carelessly
📝 Mechanical = doesn't think at all. Non-application = thinks briefly but casually, not seriously. Both are equally invalid — order is quashed.
Nandlal v Bar Council of Gujarat AIR 1981 SC 477
Bar Council forwarded case to Disciplinary Committee without applying mind to find if prima facie case existed
"Reason to believe" = barrier against frivolous enquiries
→ Reference incompetent → suspension order set aside
Sanction for Prosecution — Prevention of Corruption Act
Sanction NOT an automatic formality
Facts of offence must be placed before sanctioning authority
Authority must decide after applying mind
Application of mind must appear from sanction itself OR proved by other evidence
Bansilal Lubadia — sanction quashed as material facts not brought to notice of sanctioning authority
Search Warrant — Blank Form
Authority issued search warrant without striking out irrelevant columns in printed form / blanks not filled → indicated warrant issued without applying mind → invalid
GST APPLICATION
Search under S.67 CGST / Arrest under S.69 / Provisional attachment under S.83 — all require formation of "reason to believe" by the competent officer. If the Commissioner signs an authorization on a pro forma without independent evaluation → mechanical exercise → void. SCN must show application of mind to assessee's specific facts — not a template.
💡 GST provisional attachment order under S.83 issued in a printed format with blanks filled — no mention of which specific transactions led to formation of belief — identical to the search warrant case above. Such attachment is liable to be quashed for non-application of mind.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter XIX | Discretionary Powers (III)
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officer, Appellate Authority, GSTAT, High Court. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Chhugamal Rajpal v S.P. Chaliha — AIR 1971 SC 730
Supreme Court of India | 1971 | On "Reason to Believe" Standard
Key Ratio
"The belief must be that of an honest and reasonable person based upon reasonable grounds and the income-tax officer may act on direct or circumstantial evidence but not on mere suspicion, gossip or rumour."
When to Use — Challenge SCN under S.73/74 CGST where the basis is mere suspicion or information without any tangible material. Argue: the officer lacked the requisite honest and reasonable belief — the SCN is void at the threshold.
Rohtas Industries v S.D. Agarwal — AIR 1969 SC 707
Supreme Court of India | 1969 | No Material — Wholly Irrational Opinion
Key Ratio
"The opinion formed by the Government was a wholly irrational opinion." — Whether any reasonable person much less any expert body could have reasonably made the impugned order on the basis of the material before it.
When to Use — Where GST assessment / penalty / search / arrest authorization is made on completely inadequate material. Cite to establish the order is irrational — no reasonable officer could have passed it on material before him.
Associated Provincial Picture Houses Ltd. v Wednesbury Corporation — (1948) 1 KB 223
England, Court of Appeal | Lord Greene MR | The Wednesbury Standard
Key Ratio
"If a decision on a competent matter is so unreasonable that no reasonable authority could ever have come to it, then the courts can interfere... but to prove a case of that kind would require something overwhelming." "Something so absurd that no sensible person could ever dream that it lay within the power of the authority."
Second Ratio — Comprehensive Unreasonableness
"A person entrusted with a discretion must... direct himself properly in law... call his own attention to the matters which he is bound to consider... exclude from his consideration matters which are irrelevant. If he does not obey those rules, he may truly be said, and often is said to be acting 'unreasonably'."
When to Use — Challenge GST order that ignores assessee's submissions, considers irrelevant factors (e.g., competitor's statement), or reaches a conclusion no reasonable officer could reach. Cite both ratios — the specific unreasonableness AND the comprehensive definition.
Council of Civil Service Unions v Minister for Civil Service (GCHQ Case) — (1985) 1 AC 374
House of Lords | Lord Diplock | Three Grounds of Judicial Review
Key Ratio
"Judicial review has I think developed to a stage today when, without reiterating any analysis of the steps by which the development has come about, one can conveniently classify under three heads the grounds on which administrative action is subject to control by judicial review. The first ground I would call 'illegality', the second 'irrationality' and the third 'procedural impropriety'."
Irrationality Defined
"So outrageous in its defiance of logic or of accepted moral standards that no sensible person who had applied his mind to the question to be decided could have arrived at it."
When to Use — In writ petitions challenging GST orders — frame your grounds under these three heads. "Irrationality" is useful where the GST officer's conclusion is logically inconsistent with the material before him.
Ranjit Thakur v Union of India — AIR 1987 SC 2386 | (1987) 4 SCC 611
Supreme Court of India | 1987 | Proportionality in Judicial Review
Key Ratio
"The doctrine of proportionality, as part of the concept of judicial review, would ensure that even on an aspect which is, otherwise, within the exclusive province of the Court-Martial, if the decision of the Court even as to sentence is an outrageous defiance of logic, then the sentence would not be immune from correction. Irrationality and perversity are recognised grounds of judicial review."
Core Principle
"The sentence has to suit the offence and the offender. It should not be vindictive or unduly harsh. It should not be so disproportionate to the offence as to shock the conscience and amount in itself to conclusive evidence of bias."
"The point to note and emphasise is that all powers have legal limits."
When to Use — Challenge maximum GST penalty imposed for technical defaults. Also: penalty u/s 129/130 for e-way bill errors where goods are perfectly legitimate and tax is paid. "All powers have legal limits" — the officer's discretion is not unlimited.
Bhagat Ram v State of Himachal Pradesh — AIR 1983 SC 454 | (1983) 2 SCC 442
Supreme Court of India | 1983 | Proportionality & Article 14
Key Ratio
"It is equally true that the penalty imposed must be commensurate with the gravity of the misconduct, and that any penalty disproportionate to the gravity of the misconduct would be violative of article 14 of the Constitution."
When to Use — Challenge maximum penalty (S.122 CGST) or compounded amount where the underlying default is minor. Frame: penalty disproportionate to gravity of default = violation of Article 14.
Mineral Development Ltd. v State of Bihar — AIR 1960 SC 468
Supreme Court of India | 1960 | Proportionality — Trivial Violations / Drastic Action
Key Ratio
"The contraventions alleged, even if true, appear to be too trivial for the drastic action taken by the State." "It is obvious that the licence affecting rights of great magnitude was cancelled to say the least, for trivial reasons."
When to Use — GST registration cancellation for minor procedural default; revocation refused disproportionately. Cite: the action taken is grossly disproportionate to the alleged default — same ratio as Mineral Development.
Emperor v Sibnath Banerji — AIR 1945 PC 156
Privy Council | 1945 | Mechanical Exercise / Non-Application of Mind
Key Ratio
Home Secretary issued detention order automatically whenever police recommended it — never personally satisfied himself from materials — "The Home Secretary's personal satisfaction in each case of detention was a condition precedent to the issue of the order, otherwise the order would be invalid."
When to Use — GST search authorization / arrest / provisional attachment — challenge on ground that the Commissioner did not independently apply mind — merely acted on DGGI / audit recommendation mechanically. Personal satisfaction of authorized officer = condition precedent.
Indian Railway Construction Co. Ltd. v Ajay Kumar — AIR 2003 SC 1843 | (2003) 4 SCC 579
Supreme Court of India | 2003 | Failure to Exercise Discretion — Must Exercise Own Mind
Key Ratio
"In general, a discretion must be exercised only by the authority to which it is committed. The authority must genuinely address itself to the matter before it, it must not act under the dictates of another body or disable itself from exercising a discretion in each individual case."
When to Use — Where SCN / order has been issued by an officer acting under instructions from superior (e.g., field officers following central directions to issue demands across the board) — each case must be individually considered. Blanket / template orders are invalid.
Lord Denning MR in Breen v Amalgamated Engineering Union — (1971) 2 QB 175
England, Court of Appeal | 1971 | Discretion Must Follow Relevant Considerations — Giving Reasons
Key Ratio — Relevant Considerations
"The discretion of a statutory body is never unfettered. It is a discretion which is to be exercised according to law. That means at least this: the statutory body must be guided by relevant considerations and not by irrelevant. If its decision is influenced by extraneous considerations which it ought not to have taken into account then the decision cannot stand. No matter that the statutory body may have acted in good faith nevertheless the decision will be set aside."
Key Ratio — Giving Reasons
"The giving of reasons is one of the fundamentals of good administration."
When to Use — Two situations: (1) Officer considers irrelevant factors (hearsay statements, third-party complaints without verification) → decision cannot stand despite good faith. (2) Order passed without reasons / with cryptic reasons → "giving of reasons is one of the fundamentals of good administration."
Om Kumar v Union of India — AIR 2000 SC 3689 | (2001) 2 SCC 386
Supreme Court of India | 2000 | Definitive Framework on Proportionality
Key Ratio
"By 'proportionality', we mean the question whether, while regulating exercise of fundamental rights, the appropriate or least-restrictive choice of measures has been made by the legislature or the administrator so as to achieve the object of the legislation or the purpose of the administrative order. Under the principle, the Court will see that the legislature and the administrative authority 'maintain a proper balance between the adverse effects which the legislation or the administrative order may have on the rights, liberties or interests of persons keeping in mind the purpose which they were intended to serve'."
When to Use — GST actions restricting business activity (registration cancellation, provisional attachment, arrest) — frame the challenge under proportionality: is the measure the least restrictive? Does it maintain proper balance? Is it commensurate with the objective?
Akbar Badruddin Giwani v Collector of Customs — AIR 1990 SC 1579
Supreme Court of India | 1990 | Penalty Must Consider Bona Fides
Key Ratio
"Moreover, the quantum of penalty and fine in lieu of confiscation are extremely harsh, excessive and unreasonable bearing in mind the bona fides of the appellant... The imposition of such a heavy fine was 'not at all warranted and justifiable' in view of his bona fide belief that the stone being imported was not marble."
When to Use — GST cases where assessee acted in bona fide belief (classification dispute, exemption claim, valuation issue) and penalty is imposed as if intentional evasion occurred. Bona fides must reduce quantum of penalty — "not at all warranted" to impose maximum in bona fide cases.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter XIX — Discretionary Powers (III)

⚖️ Chapter XIX — Discretionary Powers (III)

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Ground
Prohibited / Defect / Quashed
Condition / Test / If
Optional / Alternative Route
Neutral Reference / Principle
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
19.19.1 Non-Application of Mind — Core Doctrine
19.19.2.1 Preventive Detention
19.19.2.2 Fettering Discretion
19.19.2.3 Undue Haste
19.19.3 Acting under Dictation
19.19.3.1 Political Pressure
19.19.4 Seeking Advice vs Dictation
19.19.5 Usurpation of Power
19.20 Administrative Delay — Overview
19.20.1 No Time-Limit in Statute
19.20.2 Time-Limit Without Consequence
19.20.3 Time-Limit With Consequence
19.20.4 Preventive Detention Delay
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
19.19.1 — Non-Application of Mind: Core Doctrine
🧠 Non-Application of Mind
A New & Developing Ground to Challenge Discretionary Administrative Action
📝 When a government authority is given a power to decide something, it must actually think about it — read the file, examine the facts, consider the merits. If it acts mechanically, rubber-stamps, or issues an order without genuine thought, courts will quash it as "non-application of mind."
Core Principle — What Courts Require
✓ REQUIRED
Authority must deliberate carefully — examine all relevant facts, consider merits of individual case, and form a genuine opinion
📝 The authority must show it actually thought about your specific case — not just follow a template or rubber-stamp what someone above told it.
✗ FORBIDDEN
Mechanical action, one-line orders without reasoning, acting on audit report alone, or applying policy blindly without examining individual merits
💡 GST SCN responded with one-line order "not acceptable" — courts have quashed such orders as non-application of mind (Prayag Das Agarwal).
CONDITION
Words like "considers it necessary or expedient" postulate that the authority has thought over the matter deliberately and with care — cannot be treated as unfettered discretion
📝 Even when the statute seems to give unlimited power ("if it considers necessary"), courts imply a duty to actually think before acting.
📌 Key Judicial Illustrations — Non-Application of Mind
Case Fact Situation Court Held GST / Admin Law Takeaway
Prayag Das Agarwal
(AIR 1981 SC 1263)		 Accountable person offered property to Government to pay estate duty. Request rejected by one-line reply without any inquiry. Quashed. Government cannot act mechanically. Must consider application on merits with sound administrative judgment. SCN reply / adjudication order in one line = non-application of mind. Assessee can challenge.
Barium Chemicals v Rana
(AIR 1972 SC 591)		 Order to produce documents under FERA. Many documents had no bearing on Act. Quashed. Application of mind required for each document in the order. Omnibus order = non-application of mind. Summons/notices requiring documents must specify each document with application of mind. Blanket notice is bad.
New Central Jute Mills v Triloki Nath
(AIR 1974 Cal 146)		 Omnibus order to produce documents — no specification. Non-application of mind. Notice without specifying documents is invalid. SCN must specify exact allegations for each claim. Omnibus demand = non-application of mind.
UOI v Jesus Sales Corporation
(AIR 1996 SC 1509)		 Appellate authority had discretion to condone/dispense on conditions. Levied penalty based only on CAG audit report. Cannot levy penalty solely on audit report. Authority must apply own mind to find if breach actually occurred. GST demand based only on GSTIN data / GSTR-2A mismatch (without examining actuals) = non-application of mind.
ITO, Jodhpur v Purushotttam Das Bangur
(1997) 3 SCC 253		 ITO issued notice to reopen assessment the very next day of receiving letter from Directorate. Upheld. Speed alone does not mean non-application of mind — ITO could form belief on information received. Speed of SCN / audit objection does not itself prove non-application of mind, if authority shows it examined the information.
Mohd. Iqbal Shaikh v Maharashtra
(AIR 1998 SC 2864)		 Sanction for prosecution accorded same day all papers received. Valid — if authority perused all records before according sanction, same-day action does not indicate non-application of mind. GST SCN issued quickly after inspection = valid if officer actually examined material during inspection.
Onkar Lal Bajaj v UOI
(AIR 2003 SC 2562)		 PM directed mass cancellation of all petroleum dealerships (post-newspaper controversy) without examining individual cases. Quashed. En masse cancellation without examining individual case = no application of mind to any specific case. Blanket GST notices/demands against entire sector without individual examination = non-application of mind.
Aslam Mohammad Merchant v Competent Authority
(2008) 14 SCC 186		 Large number of properties mentioned in SCN as illegally acquired. One property already struck down in another case. Large SCN list alone ≠ non-application of mind (para 37). BUT inclusion of property already struck down = non-application of mind (para 54). GST SCN including turnover/ITC already settled in prior proceedings = ground to challenge as non-application of mind.
⚠️ IMPORTANT QUALIFIER  Absence of a formal recital ("I have formed the opinion...") in the order does NOT automatically prove non-application of mind. Government can prove by other means that requisite opinion was formed before making the order. The formation of opinion is the condition precedent — not its formal recording.
19.19.2.1 — Non-Application of Mind: Preventive Detention
🔒 Non-Application of Mind — Preventive Detention Context
Courts take strictest view — liberty of citizen at stake
DEFECT 1
Use of disjunctive "or" instead of conjunctive "and" between grounds of detention
📝 Detention order must be definite — "detained for ground A and B" not "A or B." Using "or" shows the authority was not sure which ground applied — that is non-application of mind.
Jagannath Misra v State of Orissa
(AIR 1966 SC 1140) — Use of "or" = casualness / non-application of mind = order bad
Abhay Shridhar Ambukar v S.V. Bhave
(AIR 1991 SC 397) — "use of disjunctive word 'or' indicates non-application of mind and obscurity in thought"
DEFECT 2
Detaining authority signed order mechanically without applying mind
📝 Even if all grounds are correctly stated, if the authority just signed it without actually reading and thinking — the order is bad.
Teka Bahadur v State of W.B.
(AIR 1975 SC 1000) — Mechanical signing = bad order
DEFECT 3
Serious factual mistakes in grounds of detention = evidence of casualness
📝 Wrong dates, wrong amounts, wrong acts attributed — indicates the authority never really examined the file.
DEFECT 4
Detention order passed without awareness that person is already confined in prison
📝 If authority doesn't even know the person is already in jail when passing fresh detention order — it clearly hasn't applied its mind to the basic facts.
Merugu Satyanarayana v State of AP
(AIR 1982 SC 1543) — Such order liable to be set aside as invalid
DEFECT 5
Casual & cavalier manner of exercising detention powers — treating wide powers as routine
G. Sadanandan v State of Kerala
(AIR 1966 SC 1925) — SC said: "continuous exercise of very wide powers… likely to make conscience of authorities insensitive"
GST APPLICATION
In GST proceedings: Adjudication orders / SCN confirmations issued mechanically (rubber-stamped), without examining reply of assessee, without addressing specific contentions raised, or including demands already paid/dropped — are squarely covered by this doctrine. Challenge on ground of non-application of mind is well established.
19.19.2.2 — Fettering Discretion
⛓️ Fettering Discretion
Self-Imposed Restraint by Authority on Its Own Statutory Discretion — Invalid in Administrative Law
📝 When Parliament gives a discretion to an authority, it expects that authority to decide each case on its own merits. If the authority instead says "we have a blanket policy — we never grant X in class Y cases," it has tied its own hands — "fettered" its discretion. This is as bad as not exercising discretion at all.
✓ PERMITTED
Laying down a general policy for guidance in exercising discretion — promotes consistency, predictability, coordination
Policy may be applied to individual cases — but authority must still listen to each applicant and examine whether policy should apply to that specific case
📝 Having a policy is fine — like having a standard checklist. But you must still read each person's actual situation and consider if the policy fits them.
✗ FORBIDDEN — FETTERING
Applying policy rigidly and inflexibly to all cases irrespective of individual merits — refusing to even listen to the applicant
Authority must NOT "shut its ears" to an applicant who wishes to say why his case is exceptional or why the policy should not apply to him
📝 If the adjudicator says "our policy is to confirm all demands where GSTR-2A mismatch exists — no exceptions" — that is illegal fettering of discretion.
💡 GST: Appellate authority dismissing all appeals citing department circular without examining individual merits = fettering discretion. Circular cannot override the duty to apply mind to each appeal.
📌 Key Cases — Fettering Discretion
CaseWhat Was FetteredOutcome
H. Lavender v Minister of Housing
(1970) 3 All ER 871		 Minister of Housing refused permission for mineral extraction wherever Minister of Agriculture objected — inflexible policy, effective decision left with Agriculture Minister. Quashed. Minister must keep actual decision in own hands. Cannot delegate by policy to another ministry's veto.
R v Stepecny Corporation
(1902) 1 KB 317		 Local authority calculated compensation by blindly following Treasury formula instead of applying statutory criteria itself. Unlawful. Authority under obligation to exercise its own discretion applying statutory criteria — acting under dictation of appellate authority is invalid.
British Oxygen Co. v Ministry of Technology
(1970) 3 All ER 165		 Lord Reid — Minister may formulate policy for future exercise of discretion if good administration requires it, provided he listens to any applicant having something new to say. Policy valid — BUT authority must not refuse to listen; it should always be willing to hear those having something to say, including representations to change policy.
Gell v Teja Noora
(1903) 27 ILR Bom 307		 Police Commissioner required all new carriages to conform to a model outside his office — without examining each carriage's individual fitness. Illegal. Discretion must be exercised after examining each individual carriage. Cannot fetter with rules preventing free consideration of each case.
Coal Mines PF Commissioner v J.P. Lalla
(AIR 1976 SC 676)		 Commissioner imposed 25% penalty on PF arrears mechanically by formula, without hearing employer's explanation for delay. Quashed. Determination of damages not a mechanical process. Words "as it may think fit" require application of mind to facts and circumstances. Opportunity to be heard essential.
Shri Rama Sugar Industries v State of AP
(AIR 1974 SC 1745)		 Government policy to grant tax exemption only to co-operative sugar factories — private factories denied without examining merits of expansion claims. Policy was irrelevant to Act's object (promote sugar production). Fettering discretion by policy extraneous to object of Act = invalid.
BALANCE PRINCIPLE (Prof. Craig)  A public body endowed with discretionary powers is NOT entitled to adopt a policy or rule allowing it to dispose of a case without any consideration of the merits of the individual applicant. A general policy is allowed provided: (a) due consideration of individual merits takes place, and (b) the content of the policy is intra vires. Laying down a policy is NOT ipso facto invalid — what is invalid is applying it so rigidly as to exclude individual consideration.
19.19.2.3 — Undue Haste
⚡ Undue Haste — Flip Side of Delay
Premature Action Before Awaiting Statutory Approval = Also Defective
📝 Just as inordinate delay vitiates administrative action, undue haste — acting before required approvals are in place — is equally a defect. An officer cannot jump ahead of the process because he is eager or pressured.
CASE — Jasbir Singh Chhabra v State of Punjab
(2010) 4 SCC 192

MD of State Corporation — who himself participated in Plan Approval Committee — issued advertisement for provisional draw of lots for residential plots WITHOUT waiting for competent authority's approval to change land use from industrial to residential.

Court deprecated such hasty action.
GST APPLICATION
Issuing demand order before statutory reply period expires, or before personal hearing is held, or before SCN reply is examined = undue haste = violation of natural justice + non-application of mind. Assessee can challenge on both grounds.
19.19.3 — Acting under Dictation
📢 Acting under Dictation
Firm Rule: Discretion Must Be Exercised By the Authority In Whom It Is Vested — No Abdication Permitted
📝 If Parliament gave the power to Officer A, only Officer A can exercise it — using his own judgment. If Officer A just does what Officer B (his boss) tells him to do, that is NOT Officer A exercising his discretion — it is Officer B exercising it illegally through Officer A. The decision is void.
🧭 Core Rule — Two Principles for Valid Exercise of Discretion
PRINCIPLE 1
Decision must be made on the personal bona fide satisfaction of the authority making it — not on the dictates of any one else or on being influenced by any one
📝 The officer must genuinely believe, based on his own examination, that the order is correct. He cannot just be a messenger for his superior's decision.
PRINCIPLE 2
Authority invested with power must exercise it for the purpose for which it has been conferred — not for any collateral purpose
📝 The discretion is given for a specific legal purpose. It cannot be used for political reasons, personal reasons, or to please a superior officer.
RATIO — State of UP v Maharaja Dharmander Prasad Singh (AIR 1989 SC 997)
"The authority cannot permit its decision to be influenced by the dictation of others as this would amount to abdication and surrender of its discretion. It would then not be the authority's discretion that is exercised, but someone else's. If an authority 'hands over its discretion to another body it acts ultra vires.'"
📌 Key Cases — Acting under Dictation
CaseWho Dictated WhomOutcome
Anirudhsinghji Jadeja v State of Gujarat
(AIR 1995 SC 2390)		 Discretion exercised under direction/instruction of higher authority. Failure to exercise discretion altogether. Authority committed with power must genuinely address matter — cannot act under dictates of another body.
Nagaraj Karjagi v Syndicate Bank
(AIR 1991 SC 1507)		 Bank's disciplinary authorities mechanically obeyed CVC advice (made binding by Finance Ministry circular). Petitioner compulsorily retired. SC quashed Finance Ministry circular, set aside punishment, directed bank to reconsider. Classic: both dictation AND non-application of mind.
Chandrika Jha v State of Bihar
(AIR 1984 SC 322)		 Chief Minister usurped functions of Registrar of Co-operative Societies. Registrar passed order at Chief Minister's behest — not own discretion. Quashed. Neither Chief Minister nor Minister had power to arrogate statutory functions of Registrar. Order at behest of Minister = invalid.
Purtabpore Co. v Cane Commissioner of Bihar
(AIR 1979 SC 1896)		 Chief Minister imposed opinion on Cane Commissioner, who excluded 99 villages from sugar company's reserved area. Quashed. Power was exercisable by Cane Commissioner alone. He should not have abdicated responsibility in favour of Chief Minister.
Simms Motor Units v Minister of Labour
(1946) 2 All ER 201		 National service officer issued order under dictation from Minister. Quashed. If Minister wants to lay down policy, he must do so through rules — not through administrative directions. Concerned official must exercise own mind.
Raj Prakash v ADM New Delhi
(AIR 1978 Del 17)		 ADM issued preventive detention order under dictates of superior officers who had no statutory power. ADM acted mechanically in obedience. Quashed. Satisfaction must be free from vice of acting under dictate or mechanical adoption of superior's views. No grounds existed on record.
Sheonandan Paswan v State of Bihar
(AIR 1983 SC 194)		 Public prosecutor applied for withdrawal of prosecution. State had decided to withdraw but made clear PP could take his own decision. Valid. PP clearly indicated he had looked into matter himself and taken his own decision. Court found no dictation.
LIC v Escorts
(AIR 1986 SC 1370)		 RBI changed position on NRI investments after high-level discussions with Finance Ministry. Valid. After high-level policy discussions, change of view ≠ acting under pressure. Genuine policy reconsideration is permitted.
19.19.3.1 — Acting under Political Pressure
🏛️ Political Pressure on Senior Officers — Prohibited
LEADING CASE — Tarlochan Dev Sharma v State of Punjab (AIR 2001 SC 2524)

Principal Secretary (Local Government), without applying mind to facts and relevant law, allegedly under political pressure, removed elected President of a Municipality.

Supreme Court held:
"In the system of Indian democratic governance... senior officers occupying key positions such as Secretaries are not supposed to mortgage their own discretion, volition and decision making authority and be prepared to give way or being pushed back or pressed ahead at the behest of politicians for carrying out commands having no sanctity in law."
GST APPLICATION
In GST proceedings — adjudicating/appellate officer who confirms demand because "department practice is to confirm" or because of instructions from Commissioner/superiors to "not give reliefs" — is acting under political/administrative pressure. Decisions so made are liable to be challenged as non-application of mind + acting under dictation.
19.19.4 — Seeking Assistance or Advice vs. Being Dictated To
🤝 Seeking Advice — Permissible vs. Dictation — Prohibited
Critical Distinction in Administrative Law
✓ PERMITTED — Seeking Advice
Advice or assistance may be taken so long as authority: (1) does NOT mechanically act on it, and (2) itself takes the final decision in the matter
📝 Getting a second opinion is fine. What is NOT fine is treating that opinion as binding or as a replacement for your own judgment.
Commissioner of Police v Gordhandas Bhanji
(AIR 1952 SC 16) — Cinema licence granted on recommendation of advisory committee = valid because "there is no suggestion that his will was overborne or that there was dishonesty"
Consulting Vigilance Commissioner on inquiry findings = valid — government is superior authority to commissioner
✗ FORBIDDEN — Dictation
Will of authority is "overborne" if advice comes from: (a) a superior authority, or (b) advisory committee having an official superior to the deciding authority — as in practice it is nearly impossible to disregard superior's opinion
📝 If your boss sits on the "advisory" committee, it's no longer really advisory — you cannot practically say no to him. That becomes dictation in practice.
Mount Corporation v Director of Industries
(AIR 1965 Mys 143) — Director acted as conduit pipe of committee chaired by deputy minister = will overborne = not own discretion
Tahsildar concluded petitioner entitled to land grant — but sought instructions from senior officer instead of granting himself = wrong. Could not seek instructions from superior for discharging own statutory duty.
RULE
It is wrong for a statutory authority to seek guidance from a superior authority even of its own accord and to act on its instructions. Statutory authority must function independently in each case coming before it without any interference or dictation from any higher officer.
19.19.5 — Usurpation of Power
👑 Usurpation of Power
Higher Authority Cannot Exercise Power Vested by Statute in Lower Authority
📝 Usurpation = when a higher authority takes over and decides a case that legally should be decided by a lower authority. The legislature deliberately created a two-tier system. If the higher authority jumps into the first tier, it upsets the whole scheme.
Landmark — State of Punjab v Hari Kishan (AIR 1966 SC 1081)
Cinema licence application made to Licensing Authority → forwarded to State Government (under Government instructions) → State Government rejected
SC HELD
State Government comes into picture only as appellate/revisional authority after licensing authority acts. Government cannot assume original powers of licensing authority.
"Control" power cannot justify Government to completely oust licensing authority and usurp its function. Result of instructions was to "obliterate the licensing authority from the Statute-book altogether."
What "Control" Power DOES and DOES NOT Permit
✓ PERMITTED
Issue general instructions to licensing authorities on how to deal with applications; exercise revisional power over orders passed by them
✗ FORBIDDEN
Take original action by itself; exercise original jurisdiction; decide applications at first instance (bypassing licensing authority)
GST APPLICATION
In GST — if Commissioner issues direction to adjudicating officer to confirm a demand, and officer confirms without independent examination = usurpation + dictation + non-application of mind — three grounds in one. Similarly, if Board (CBIC) issues circular directing that all cases of a particular type be decided in a particular way — and officer applies it without examining individual case — that is fettering + usurpation.
19.20 — Administrative Delay: Overview & Classification
⏰ Administrative Delay
Bureaucratic Inertia — A Common Malady & Hidden Form of Arbitrariness
📝 Government cannot take its time indefinitely just because the statute does not specify a deadline. Courts have said: even without a time-limit, decisions must be taken within a "reasonable time." Delay is a hidden form of arbitrariness — it hurts the citizen just as much as a bad order.
Three Statutory Models — How Indian Law Deals with Administrative Delay
MODEL 1
Statute prescribes NO time-limit
→ Action must be taken within reasonable time
→ Mandamus available in High Court for inordinate delay
→ Action after unreasonable delay may be quashed
MODEL 2
Statute prescribes time-limit but silent on consequence of default
→ Time-limit may be mandatory (jurisdiction lost) or directory (action valid but late)
→ Mandamus available to compel action
→ Action after prescribed time = court examines each case
MODEL 3
Statute prescribes time-limit AND specifies consequence
→ Two sub-models: (a) Deemed rejection after time, (b) Deemed grant/approval after time
→ Mandatory provision — government loses power after prescribed period
→ No estoppel against statute
KEY PRINCIPLE — Mansaram v S.P. Pathak (AIR 1983 SC 1239)
"When the power is conferred to effectuate a purpose, it has to be exercised in a reasonable manner. Exercise of power in a reasonable manner inheres the concept of its exercise within a reasonable time."
PRINCIPLE — Yogeshwar Jaiswal (AIR 1985 SC 516)
"Delay in performance of statutory duties amounts to an abuse of process of law and has to be remedied by the court particularly when the public interest suffers thereby."
19.20.1 — When Statute Prescribes No Time-Limit
📋 No Statutory Time-Limit — "Reasonable Time" Standard Applies
RULE
Even without statutory time-limit, administration ought not to delay decisions for unduly long time. Mandamus available in High Court for inordinate delay.
Gram Panchayat, Kakran v Additional Director of Consolidation
(1997) 8 SCC 484

Objection to land partition filed 40 years after event — no satisfactory explanation. Should not have been entertained even though no time-limit prescribed.
Yogeshwar Jaiswal v State Transport Appellate Tribunal
(AIR 1985 SC 516)

Draft scheme under MVA 1939 published 1971 — no final order under Sec 68D till 1985 (14 years). SC held unreasonable delay = abuse of process. Court can quash scheme if government fails to act within specified time.
State of Kerala v M. Padmanabhan Nair
(AIR 1985 SC 356)

Pension and gratuity paid 2+ years after retirement. SC: "culpable delay" — pension is a right/property, not bounty. Any culpable delay must be visited with penal interest at current market rate till actual payment.
O.P. Gupta v UOI
(AIR 1987 SC 2257)

Employee kept under suspension for 11 years, departmental inquiry alive for 20 years. SC: "no occasion whatever to protract the departmental inquiry for 20 years... unless actuated with mala fide intention." Interest at 12% on delayed pension ordered.
GST APPLICATION
Refund application pending for years without action → mandamus available. Adjudication order not passed for years after SCN → assessee can approach court. GST officers cannot "sleep over" SCNs. Delay in processing is a right-based grievance — not just a procedural complaint.
19.20.2 — Time-Limit Without Stating Consequences of Delay
⚖️ Statute Prescribes Time-Limit but Silent on Consequences
Court examines whether provision is Mandatory or Directory
