📝 "Mandatory" means the authority loses its power to act after the time expires. "Directory" means the authority should ideally act within that time, but if it doesn't, the action is still valid — though the court can compel action by mandamus and may impose interest for delay.
MANDATORY — Jurisdiction Lost After Expiry

N. Setharamiah v Kotaiah
(AIR 1970 SC 1354)
Mineral Concession Rules — application for mining lease not disposed of within 12 months = deemed rejected. State Government lost power to deal with applications pending before it. Mandatory provision = no estoppel against statute.
DIRECTORY — Late Action Valid but Delay May Have Consequences

Dalchand v Municipal Corporation
(AIR 1983 SC 303)
Food Adulteration Rules: 10 days to supply copy of Public Analyst's report. Held directory — not every time prescription = limitation with painful consequences. If no prejudice caused to person, no cause for complaint.
Income Tax — Director of Inspection v Pooran Mall (AIR 1975 SC 67)
Period of limitation under Sec 132(5) = mandatory for initial order. BUT if order set aside on appeal and fresh order directed, fresh order need NOT be within original 90-day limit. Limitation applied only to initial order — not to consequential orders under court/appellate direction.
GST APPLICATION
Section 73/74 CGST Act: Time-limit to issue order is prescribed. If adjudication order not passed within time → whether mandatory or directory is a live GST litigation question. Courts have held similar provisions as mandatory in analogous tax laws — strong ground for challenging time-barred adjudication orders.
19.20.3 — Time-Limit With Stated Consequences (Deemed Grant / Deemed Rejection)
✅ Statute Prescribes Time-Limit AND Specifies Consequence
Two Sub-Models: Deemed Rejection | Deemed Grant
MODEL A — Deemed Rejection
If government does not dispose of application within prescribed period → deemed rejected → applicant can appeal/revise against deemed rejection

N. Setharamiah v Kotaiah — Mineral Concession Rules: 12 months → deemed rejection. Mandatory — government loses power thereafter.
MODEL B — Deemed Grant
If government does not refuse within prescribed period → deemed to have granted permission → applicant gets absolute right to deal with property/matter

P.M. Kalwar v Deputy Commissioner, Belgaum (AIR 1981 SC 1363) — Urban Land Ceiling Act Sec 27: 60 days → "When once the power to refuse is lost, for whatever reason, it is lost once and for all."
📌 Key Deemed-Grant Cases
Case / ProvisionTime-LimitDeemed Grant Rule
Urban Land Ceiling Act, Sec 27
P.M. Kalwar v Dy. Commissioner, Belgaum
(AIR 1981 SC 1363)		 60 days to refuse or communicate refusal Deemed permission granted. Once power to refuse lost, lost once and for all. Applicant gets absolute right to deal with property.
Bihar Regional Development Authority Act, Sec 37
Uma Shyam Parivar Dust v State of Bihar
(AIR 1990 Pat 174)		 4 months to scrutinise building plan. Then 30 days' notice period. If no action within 30-day notice period → plan deemed sanctioned. Provisions of Sec 37(5) mandatory in nature.
Bombay Land Revenue Code, Sec 65
State of Gujarat v Patel Raghav Natha
(AIR 1969 SC 1297)		 3 months to refuse conversion of agricultural land to non-agricultural use Deemed permission. Legislature treated matter as urgent. Revisional authority must also act promptly — power of revision after undue delay quashed.
Motor Vehicles Act, Sec 100(4) 1 year from publication to approve scheme Scheme deemed to have lapsed. Object: avoid delay in finalising transport schemes which caused public interest to suffer under old 1939 Act.
WELCOME TREND (Court's View)  "What is the point of the legislature imposing a time-limit... if the time-limit is not to be observed in practice. Law imposes a limitation period within which a person can assert his right, and he loses the remedy if he oversteps the period of limitation. On the same analogy, the Administration should also lose its right to say 'no' if it does not do so within the prescribed period. This is only fair administration."
19.20.4 — Administrative Delay: Preventive Detention
🔒 Delay in Preventive Detention Cases
Courts take strictest view — Article 21 (Personal Liberty) at stake
📝 In preventive detention, speed is a duty — not a choice. Any unexplained delay in communicating grounds or considering representation strikes at the detenu's right to challenge the detention. "Undue administrative delay will invariably prove fatal to the validity of preventive detention."
ARTICLE 22(5)
Grounds of detention must be communicated "as soon as may be" = with reasonable despatch

Failure to communicate within reasonable time vitiates detention
(Ujagar Singh v State of Punjab — AIR 1952 SC 350)
REPRESENTATION
Detenu must be afforded "earliest opportunity" to make representation

Government must consider representation promptly — unexplained delay of even 17 days in considering representation = vitiates detention
(K.I. Singh v State of Manipur — AIR 1972 SC 1198)

56 days delay by Chief Minister = "utter callousness" — SC criticised strongly
ADVISORY BOARD
Advisory board must report and government must confirm order within 3 months from date of detention

Failure to confirm within 3 months = detention invalid
(Art 22(4) — Madan Malik v State of W.B.)
NSA — 5 DAYS
National Security Act: 5 days to communicate grounds

Mandatory — any delay beyond 5 days = detention invalid
(Hem Lall Bhandari v State of Sikkim — AIR 1987 SC 762)
NSA Sec 3(5) — 7 DAYS
State Government must report preventive detention order to Central Government within 7 days

Mandatory — infringement vitiates detention
GST PARALLEL
Under GST — Section 69 arrest: grounds of arrest must be communicated immediately. Delay in producing before magistrate / delay in following Section 70 procedure = ground to challenge detention on liberty grounds. Constitutional guarantee of Article 22 applies to GST arrests too.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter XIX | Discretionary Powers (III)
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, Appellate Authorities, GSTAT, and High Courts. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Barium Chemicals Ltd. v Rana
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1972 SC 591 | (1972) 1 SCC 240 | Non-Application of Mind
Key Ratio
The words "considers it necessary or expedient" postulate "that the authority concerned has thought over the matter deliberately and with care and it has been found necessary as a result of such thinking to pass the order." An application of mind regarding the necessity to obtain and examine only a few of the many documents mentioned in the order, while there has been no such application of mind in respect of the remaining documents, would not be a sufficient compliance with the requirements of the statutory provision.
When to Use — When Department issues omnibus SCN or summons covering all transactions without examining each specifically. Argue: there is no application of mind to individual allegations. Each allegation in an SCN must reflect deliberate examination of relevant material.
UOI v Jesus Sales Corporation
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1996 SC 1509 | (1996) 4 SCC 69, para 6 | Penalty — Non-Application of Mind
Key Ratio
When the appellate authority has been vested with the discretion to dispense with a matter unconditionally or on conditions, then it has to apply its mind on that question like a quasi-judicial authority taking into consideration all the facts and circumstances of the case including undue hardship. Authorities could not have sought to levy penalty relying on or on the basis of the audit report only. They were required to apply their own mind for the purpose of finding out as to whether the licensee companies in law had committed any breach.
When to Use — In penalty proceedings under Section 122/125 CGST Act where Department has confirmed penalty solely based on GSTR-2A mismatch, audit report, or data mismatch without examining actual transactions and the assessee's reply. Argue: penalty requires independent application of mind — not rubber-stamping of audit finding.
Assistant Controller of Estate Duty v Prayag Das Agarwal
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1981 SC 1263 | (1981) 3 SCC 181 | Discretion — Application of Mind
Key Ratio
The Government must consider the application on the merits and in the exercise of sound administrative judgment. The Government could not act mechanically in refusing to accept such a request by the person concerned and proceed as if "its discretion was unfettered." The one-line refusal without any probe into the matter showed there was no proper exercise of the discretion.
When to Use — When adjudication order / appellate order gives a one-line dismissal of assessee's reply without engaging with specific contentions raised. The order must show application of mind to each defence taken by assessee. A form order / template order = invalid.
H. Lavender v Minister of Housing & Local Government
England | (1970) 3 All ER 871 | Fettering Discretion | Followed in Indian Courts
Key Ratio
Where a Minister is entrusted by Parliament with the decision of any particular case, he must keep that actual decision in the last resort in his own hands. The Minister "must be open to persuasion" that the general policy should not be applied in a particular case. By adopting and applying his stated policy inflexibly, the Minister had in effect inhibited himself from exercising a proper discretion. Everything else might point to the desirability of granting permission, but by applying and acting on his stated policy, the Minister had fettered himself.
When to Use — When Department/Appellate Authority cites CBIC Circular/Instruction as sole reason for rejecting assessee's claim, without examining facts of assessee's specific case. Argue: Circular cannot fetter statutory discretion. Authority must examine whether, on facts of this case, circular should apply or individual merits warrant a different outcome.
Coal Mines Provident Fund Commissioner v J.P. Lalla
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1976 SC 676 | (1976) 1 SCC 964 | Fettering — Penalty
Key Ratio
The determination of damages is not an inflexible application of a rigid formula. The words "as it may think fit to impose" show that the concerned authorities were required to apply their mind to the facts and circumstances of the case. In such a case having regard to the particular situation it would be unfair for the body or authority not to have allowed a reasonable opportunity to be heard.
When to Use — In GST penalty/interest proceedings where Department applies maximum penalty mechanically (e.g., 100% under Section 122) without considering assessee's intent, cooperation, and circumstances. Penalty determination requires application of mind — not mechanical formula application. Opportunity to be heard before quantifying penalty is essential.
State of Uttar Pradesh v Maharaja Dharmander Prasad Singh
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1989 SC 997 | (1989) 2 SCC 505 | Acting under Dictation
Key Ratio
"The authority cannot permit its decision to be influenced by the dictation of others as this would amount to abdication and surrender of its discretion. It would then not be the authority's discretion that is exercised, but someone else's. If an authority 'hands over its discretion to another body it acts ultra vires.' Such an interference by a person or body extraneous to the power would plainly be contrary to the nature of the power conferred on the authority."
When to Use — When adjudicating/appellate officer confirms demands citing "instructions from Commissioner" or "Department's policy not to give relief in such cases." Argue: that is not the officer exercising statutory discretion — it is the Commissioner exercising it through the officer = ultra vires. The adjudicating officer must exercise own mind independently.
State of Punjab v Hari Kishan
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1966 SC 1081 | (1966) 2 SCR 982 | Usurpation of Power
Key Ratio
The Act conferred the jurisdiction to deal with applications for licences on the licensing authority which could grant or reject the same. The State Government only came into the picture as an appellate or revisional authority. The result of the instructions issued by the government "is to change the statutory provision" and "to obliterate the licensing authority from the Statute-book altogether." However wide its ambit of control, the government could not completely oust the licensing authority and itself usurp its function.
When to Use — When CBIC/Commissioner issues circular directing adjudicating officers to decide cases in a particular way on merits (not just procedure). Argue: merits-decision power is vested in adjudicating officer — circular cannot usurp that function. Government's "control" extends to policy/procedure, not to deciding each case's merits in advance.
Yogeshwar Jaiswal v State Transport Appellate Tribunal & O.P. Gupta v UOI
Yogeshwar: AIR 1985 SC 516 | O.P. Gupta: AIR 1987 SC 2257 | Administrative Delay
Key Ratio — Yogeshwar
"Delay in performance of statutory duties amounts to an abuse of process of law and has to be remedied by the court particularly when the public interest suffers thereby."
Key Ratio — O.P. Gupta
"It is a clear principle of natural justice that the delinquent officer when placed under suspension is entitled to represent that the departmental proceedings should be concluded with reasonable diligence and within a reasonable period of time. If such a principle were not to be recognised, it would imply that the Executive is being vested with a totally arbitrary and unfettered power of placing its officers under disability and distress for an indefinite duration."
When to Use — When adjudication proceedings / refund proceedings are deliberately kept pending for years. File writ for mandamus. Also cite when Department argues limitation against assessee — counter: if Department itself sat on the matter, it cannot take advantage of delay it caused. Delay in refund processing → interest payable under Section 56 CGST Act compulsorily.
State of Gujarat v Patel Raghav Natha & P.M. Kalwar v Deputy Commissioner, Belgaum
AIR 1969 SC 1297 | AIR 1981 SC 1363 | Mandatory Time-Limit — Deemed Grant
Key Ratio — P.M. Kalwar
"When once the power to refuse an application is lost, for whatever reason it may be, that power is lost once and for all and cannot be exercised after the expiry of the period specified by the Act. On the expiry of the specified period an applicant who gets what is a deemed grant in his favour, has an absolute right to deal with the property."
Key Ratio — Patel Raghav Natha
Although no period of limitation was prescribed for revision, the power must be exercised within a reasonable time. The revisional authority should have exercised power of revision within a few months. Time taken was more than reasonable — concerned person might have spent money after grant of permission and would be put to loss if permission was withdrawn after undue delay.
When to Use — When Department issues order under Section 73/74 CGST Act after statutory time-limit has expired. Argue: if time-limit is mandatory, the demand order is void. Also cite Patel Raghav Natha when Department invokes revision/reopening proceedings after unreasonable delay — assessee having acted on earlier order cannot be prejudiced by belated revision.
G. Sadanandan v State of Kerala + Jagannath Misra v State of Orissa
G. Sadanandan: AIR 1966 SC 1925 | Jagannath Misra: AIR 1966 SC 1140 | Detention / Liberty / Non-Application
Key Ratio — G. Sadanandan
"We feel rudely disturbed by the thought that continuous exercise of the very wide powers... is likely to make the conscience of the said authorities insensitive, if not blunt, to the paramount requirement of the Constitution that even during Emergency, the freedom of Indian citizens cannot be taken away without the existence of the justifying necessity specified by the rules themselves."
Key Ratio — Jagannath Misra
"The use of the word 'or' instead of 'and' indicated casualness, or non-application of the mind by the authority, or that the authority was not sure as to which of the grounds was applicable to the detenu in question and in either case the order was bad."
When to Use — In GST arrest cases under Section 69 CGST Act: grounds of arrest must be specific ("and" not "or"). Arrest for "evasion or non-payment or suppression" without specifying exact ground = non-application of mind. Also cite G. Sadanandan to emphasise that continuous, mechanical exercise of wide enforcement powers under GST (arrest, search, seizure) without genuine application of mind is constitutionally impermissible.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter XX — Administrative Duties

⚖️ Chapter XX — Administrative Duties

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | Pages 1416–1445 | Application: GST Litigation Practice

Permitted / Valid / Duty Enforced
Prohibited / Omission / Failure
Condition / Depends on / Test
Optional / Discretionary
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections — Chapter XX | Pages 1416–1445
20.1 Duty vs Discretion
20.2 Judicial Response — Case Law
20.2.23 Discretionary Power
20.3 Directive Principles
20.4 Enforcement of Duties
20.5 Public Interest Litigation
20.6 Is State Bound by Statute?
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
20.1 — Duty, Discretion, or Discretion Coupled with Duty
📄 Pages Covered: 1416–1418
⚖️ Administrative Duties — The Core Concern
Administrative Law ensures not only that authority does not misuse power — but also that it DOES what it is REQUIRED to do
📝 So far, Admin Law asked: "Is the authority overstepping?" Now it also asks: "Is the authority doing what the law COMMANDS it to do?" Control = prevention of abuse + compulsion to perform.
Three Expressions — Courts Must Determine Which Applies
DUTY
Prescriptive — authority MUST act
No choice — non-performance is a legal wrong
📝 "Duty" = Law commands. The authority has no option but to act. Like a tax officer who MUST issue a refund order — refusing is illegal.
💡 GST: Officer MUST pass adjudication order within prescribed time. Failure = violation of statutory duty → writ of mandamus lies.
DISCRETION
Permissive — authority MAY act
Choice remains with authority — but must be exercised on proper principles
📝 "Discretion" = Law permits but does not command. But even discretion must be exercised reasonably, not arbitrarily.
DISCRETION COUPLED WITH DUTY
Authority MUST exercise its discretion
AND must exercise it in a particular manner when conditions are met
📝 Hybrid: Authority has a choice HOW to act — but it is compelled to ACT when the trigger conditions arise. Cannot simply sit on the file.
💡 GST: Commissioner "may" extend time under Sec 168A — but if taxpayer has made out a case, the Commissioner is duty-bound to consider it on merits. Rejection without consideration = improper exercise.
The Critical Interpretive Question — "MAY" vs "SHALL"
GENERAL RULE
"SHALL" → Mandatory duty
"MAY" → Permissive / Discretionary
NOT INFLEXIBLE
Court can interpret "MAY" as "SHALL" depending on context, purpose, and object of the statute
📝 The word used is not conclusive. Courts look at the INTENT — what was Parliament trying to achieve? If the purpose requires action, "may" becomes "shall."
AND VICE VERSA
Court can interpret "SHALL" as "MAY" (directory not mandatory) if the context does not require strict compliance
RAJAN — Supreme Court Propositions
(1) "Shall" or "may" alone does NOT determine mandatory/directory — read text and context.
(2) Where a statutory functionary is asked to perform a duty within prescribed time — it is directory (not mandatory) as to time.
(3) "Shall" in a procedural statute may not be mandatory if no prejudice is caused by non-compliance.
💡 GST Application: Section 73/74 CGST — "shall" issue SCN within 3/5 years → directory as to time (no strict invalidity for delay beyond limitation if no prejudice); BUT duty to issue SCN itself is mandatory.
20.2 — Judicial Response: Key Cases on Duty vs Discretion
📄 Pages Covered: 1418–1428
🏛️ Courts Progressively Shifting: Interpreting "Discretion" as "Duty"
Landmark Cases — Julius to Dinakar — showing evolution
Case / Section Key Facts Court's Holding Principle Extracted GST Relevance
20.2.1 Julius
Julius v Bishop of Oxford
(1880) 5 AC 214
House of Lords — Locus Classicus		 Bishop had power to hold inquiry on complaint against a clergyman. Refused to act on complaint. Lords held: Provision was permissive + obligatory. Bishop must act on every complaint made against a clerk. "May" was interpreted as duty. Locus classicus: Even a permissive "may" becomes a duty if the provision is intended to benefit a class of persons who invoke it. Authority cannot refuse to exercise the power. GST Adjudicating Authority: Once a taxpayer's reply to SCN is received, the officer "may" adjudicate — but is duty-bound to consider it. Ignoring the reply = violation of statutory duty.
20.2.4 Padfield
Padfield v Minister of Agriculture
[1968] All ER 694 — House of Lords		 Act said Minister "may think fit" to refer complaints against Milk Marketing Board to Committee. Minister refused to refer, fearing adverse finding against Board. Lord Reid: Act imposes responsibility on Minister whenever there is a relevant and substantial complaint. Duty to have it investigated. Failure renders safeguard nugatory. Mandamus granted. Purpose-based interpretation: Discretion must be exercised for the PURPOSE for which Parliament conferred it. Using discretion to DEFEAT the purpose of the Act = misuse. Court can compel exercise. GST: Commissioner using discretion to protect revenue rather than apply law correctly = Padfield-type abuse. Court can mandamus to decide on merits.
20.2.5 Jogendra Singh
State of UP v Jogendra Singh
AIR 1963 SC 1618		 Rule 4(2) — Government "may" refer cases to tribunal. Question: Is "may" mandatory or discretionary? SC: Where discretion is conferred on public authority coupled with an obligation, "may" should be construed as a command. Legislature may use "may" out of deference to high status of authority. Discretion + Obligation = Command. "May" = "shall" when obligation is coupled. Three-part test: (i) purpose of provision, (ii) benefit intended, (iii) prejudice to intended beneficiary. GST: Section 107 — Appellate Authority "may" condone delay. If assessee establishes sufficient cause, the discretion becomes a duty to condone. Blanket rejection = mandamus-able.
20.2.6 Gordhan
Commissioner of Police v Gordhandas Bhanji
AIR 1952 SC 16		 Regulatory power conferred on Commissioner. Public interest considerations required. SC: Regulatory power vis-a-vis public = discretion for public reasons. But authority must genuinely consider, not act arbitrarily. Court can direct re-exercise on correct principles. Discretion involving public safety/convenience must be exercised bona fide for those purposes — not for extraneous reasons. GST: Provisional attachment u/s 83 — Commissioner has discretion but must have genuine satisfaction of necessity. Mechanical attachment = colourable exercise of power.
20.2.8 Common Cause
Common Cause (Registered Society) v UOI
SC directed state governments		 Consumer Protection Act 1986 required State Governments to establish district consumer fora. States did not act for years. SC: Section 9 imposes responsibility on State Government. Government "cannot absolve itself" of responsibility. Directed compliance within one year. Statutory obligation cannot be evaded by inaction. Where Act establishes a right, creating the forum to enforce that right is itself a duty. GST: GSTAT (GST Appellate Tribunal) operationalization — State is duty-bound to constitute and operationalize the tribunal. Delay → mandamus lies.
20.2.18 Stamp Act
Chief Controlling Revenue Authority v Maharashtra S. Mills
AIR 1950 SC 218		 Section 57 Stamp Act — authority "may" refer question of law to High Court. Revenue Authority refused to refer, saying it was its discretion. SC: Power under Section 57 is in the nature of an obligation — coupled with duty. Where important intricate question of law arises on construction of document, it is the duty of the authority as public officer to make the reference. Power to refer difficult legal questions = duty, not merely discretion. Even if statute uses "may", courts can compel reference when legal complexity demands it. GST: Section 97 CGST — Authority for Advance Ruling. Similar logic: once a genuine legal question is raised, AAR is duty-bound to examine and answer it — cannot refuse arbitrarily.
20.2.19 Registration Act Registrar had no duty to prevent undervaluation of properties under Registration Act. Rules prescribed time-limit for inquiry into undervaluation. SC: Rules regarding time for completion of undervaluation inquiry are directory — not mandatory. No strict duty to prevent undervaluation; power to investigate is directory as to time. Not all powers are duties. Some powers (especially those relating to procedural time-limits) remain directory. Courts will not mandamus every procedural step. GST: Limitation periods for issuing SCN — directory as to time, but mandatory as to substance. Adjudication order time-limit (Section 75 CGST) — procedural, not going to jurisdiction.
20.2.20 Mirchumal
Mirchumal v UOI		 Rule 42 — Displaced Persons Rules — property may be transferred to government if displaced person fails to hold it. Court analyzed whether "may" imposed duty. HC: Rule 42 not merely permissive — vesting of property imposes duty on authority to complete process. Discretion + public good = duty. When discretion is coupled with public interest / beneficiary class protection, courts will read it as a duty to act. GST: Refund provisions (Section 54) — Officer "may" sanction refund — but once eligibility is established, duty to sanction arises. Withholding = mandamus-able.
20.2.22 Dinakar
Dinakar Anna Patil v State of Maharashtra
AIR 1999 SC 152		 Maharashtra rule said government "may" consult Public Service Commission before certain appointments. Government argued "may" = discretionary. SC: "May" here means "shall." Rendering consultation discretionary would defeat the very purpose of the Public Service Commission. Purpose-based interpretation mandates consultation. Where rendering a provision discretionary would defeat its purpose, "may" = "shall." The court will not allow authority to bypass a safeguard by classifying it as "discretionary." GST: Pre-SCN consultation requirement — if made mandatory by board instruction, cannot be bypassed as "discretionary." Failure to consult = procedural defect vitiating demand.
20.2.23 — Discretionary Power: Even Pure Discretion Has Limits
📄 Pages Covered: 1428–1430
🔍 Discretionary Power — Even When NOT Coupled with Duty, Must Be Exercised Lawfully
Key Propositions from the Chapter
PRINCIPLE 1
Even pure discretion does not escape obligation to exercise it on proper principles (as discussed in previous chapters on abuse of discretion)
📝 "Pure" discretion merely means no specific duty to exercise in one way. But the authority STILL must not act arbitrarily, for wrong reasons, or ignore relevant considerations.
PRINCIPLE 2
Abuse = Taking irrelevant consideration into account / using discretion for improper purpose
Excess = Making an unreasonable decision going outside limits of discretion
📝 Two wrongs: (i) Wrong reason → abuse; (ii) Unreasonable outcome → excess. Both can be judicially reviewed.
FETTERED DISCRETION
If the decision is NOT the result of decision-maker's own choice → discretion is fettered → ultra vires
📝 Fettered = Pre-determined / bound by policy blindly. Authority that says "We always reject this type of claim" without examining individual cases = fettering its own discretion.
💡 GST: If a GST officer says "We never grant refund on this HSN" without examining the specific taxpayer's facts — that is fettering discretion = writ-able.
Policies / Guidelines / Non-Statutory Rules — Judicial Approach
ADVANTAGE
Guidelines save time, promote consistency, give citizens guidance, and prevent arbitrary variation
DANGER
Non-statutory rules need not be published, more easily changed, not scrutinized by Parliament, may implement controversial policy covertly
RULE
Policies must be applied flexibly, not rigidly. Authority must always allow the affected person to "put their side of the case" — policy cannot override fairness.
💡 GST: CBIC Circulars are non-statutory guidelines. Beneficial circulars bind Revenue (equity) but department cannot blindly apply restrictive circulars ignoring individual facts.
JUDICIAL REVIEW OF DISCRETION
Courts will intervene when: (1) Decision-maker acted ultra-vires; (2) Acted "illegally" (irrelevant factors); (3) Stepped outside limits of discretion.
Ultimate question: Has the discretion been abused (wrong reason) or exceeded (unreasonable outcome)?
20.3 — Directive Principles of State Policy as Source of Duties
📄 Pages Covered: 1430–1435
📜 Directive Principles — Non-Enforceable as Rights, But Courts Use Them to Imply Government Duties
📝 Directive Principles (Articles 36–51) are goals for the State — not directly enforceable like Fundamental Rights. But courts have developed strategy: read them together with Fundamental Rights / statutory provisions to impose ENFORCEABLE DUTIES on the government.
Constitutional Synthesis — Fundamental Rights + Directive Principles = Complementary
Kesavananda Bharati (1973) — Hedgeji JJ: "There is no antithesis between Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles — they constitute the conscience of the Constitution." One supplements the other.
Unni Krishnan (1993) — "Fundamental Rights are but a means to achieve the goal indicated in Part IV. Directive Principles + Fundamental Rights = supplementary and goal-oriented."
Illustrative Cases Where Directive Principles Created Enforceable Duties
Case Directive Principle Used Duty Imposed GST / Tax Relevance
Samir Kumar Das v State
AIR 1982 Pat 66		 Article 41 — Right to education State and medical colleges have public duty to fill ALL seats offered for admission. Creation of infrastructure without filling seats is contrary to reason. Principle: Creation of a statutory right creates a corresponding duty in the State to make that right effective — not just formal compliance.
Umed Ram Article 21 — Right to Life + Right to livelihood State is duty-bound to provide bonded labourers released from bondage with alternative means of livelihood — mere release not enough. Duty extends beyond the literal text of a statute to its PURPOSE. GST refund: Officer must not just process — must ensure effective relief reaches the taxpayer.
Environment Cases
MC Mehta v UOI
AIR 1996 SC 2231		 Articles 48A, 51A — Protect environment; duty of citizens Courts directed government to enforce law against polluting industries. Administration equally responsible for allowing illegal operation. Chief Secretary directed to fix responsibility of negligent officers. GST: If tax officers consistently allow evasion in a sector without action, this "administrative failure" principle becomes relevant. Senior officers can be held accountable for deliberate non-enforcement.
Dr BL Wadehra v UOI
AIR 1996 SC 2969		 Article 21 read with Article 48A Delhi Municipal bodies directed to perform statutory duties of garbage collection, removal and disposal. Government of India directed to install incinerators in hospitals. Statutory duty + Directive Principle = Enforceable combination. Failure to perform statutory duty even for financial reasons is no excuse.
Stray Cases
Various Supreme Court decisions		 Article 39A — Equal justice; free legal aid; Article 47 — Food standards State must give grants to recognised private law colleges on same basis as government institutions. Food Corporation of India must not issue sub-standard food. Constitutional obligations on statutory bodies are enforceable. Even commercial/quasi-commercial bodies of the State must comply with constitutional norms.
20.4 — Enforcement of Duties Against Public Authorities
📄 Pages Covered: 1435–1440
⚙️ Enforcement of Duties — How to Compel an Authority to Act
📝 If an authority is obligated by law to do something but refuses or neglects to do it — the courts step in. The primary remedy is mandamus. But the concept extends further: even constitutional rights must be respected regardless of budgetary constraints.
IF DOES UNAUTHORISED ACT
Action is ULTRA VIRES → Courts quash it
IF FAILS TO DO OBLIGATED ACT
Courts intervene → Mandate it to perform its legal duty
20.4.1 — Mandamus: The Primary Remedy
CONSTITUTIONAL BASIS
High Court: Article 226 of the Constitution (broader jurisdiction)
Supreme Court: Article 32 of the Constitution
MANSUKHIAL — SC PRINCIPLE
"Mandamus is an order to compel the performance of public duties which may be administrative, ministerial, or discretionary, under Article 226 of the Constitution. It may be issued to an administrative body to exercise a discretion or to quash a decision taken without authority."
But mandamus cannot be issued to control HOW the discretion is exercised — only to COMPEL the discretion to be exercised.
📝 Mandamus says: "You MUST ACT." It does not say: "You MUST ACT THIS WAY." It opens the door — the authority must then walk through it on proper principles.
20.4.2 — Rampal v State | Rajasthan HC
Mandamus issued against municipal board for failure to perform statutory duties. Court: "When the Statute imposes a duty, the performance and non-performance of which is not a matter of discretion, the court has the power to issue a mandamus directing the local body to do what it is required to do."
20.4.5 — Ratlam Municipality v Vardhichand | AIR 1980 SC 1622
⚖️ RATLAM — MOST POWERFUL RATIO ON ENFORCEMENT OF DUTIES:

"Discretion becomes a duty when the beneficiary brings home the circumstances for its benign exercise."

"This is a public duty implicit in the public power to be exercised on behalf of the public and pursuant to a public proceeding."

"The Criminal Procedure Code operates against statutory bodies and others regardless of the cash in their coffers, even as human rights under Part III of the Constitution have to be respected by the State regardless of budgetary provision."
📝 The municipality pleaded it had no money to clean up the mess. The Supreme Court rejected this: A statutory body cannot escape duty by self-created bankruptcy or perverted budget. The law does not exempt it because of financial inability.
💡 GST Arsenal: When department claims inability to process/decide refund for "resource" or "staff shortage" reasons — cite Ratlam. "A responsible statutory body cannot run away from its principal duty by pleading financial/administrative inability."
⚠️ PLEA OF FINANCIAL INABILITY — REJECTED BY COURTS
Statutory duty is not conditional on available funds. A proligate statutory body or pachytermic governmental agency may not legally defy duties under the law by urging in self-defence a self-created bankruptcy or perverted expenditure budget. That cannot be.
20.5 — Public Interest Litigation as Tool for Imposing Duties
📄 Pages Covered: 1440–1442
📢 PIL — Courts Spell Out New Duties for Government Through Affirmative Action Orders
MECHANISM
Public interest groups expose administrative failure through PIL. Courts then issue positive directions — not just "stop this" but "do this."
📝 PIL is the activist tool. Where individual cannot sue (no locus standi), public groups can invoke court's jurisdiction to make government act according to law.
LEGAL BASIS
Statutory provisions + Fundamental Rights + Directive Principles — courts invoke all three together to create and enforce duties
RESULT
Courts impose duties not expressly stated in statutes — implied from constitutional scheme, purpose, and justice
💡 PUDR v UOI — Court spelled out detailed duties of state re bonded labour: release, rehabilitation, education, livelihood. Entire scheme of obligations created judicially.
KEY LIMITATION NOTE — De Smith
"Judicial redress can more readily be obtained for wrongful acts than for wrongful omissions [of public authorities] and the effectiveness of judicial remedies is limited either by their intrinsic characteristics or by restrictive technical rules."
→ Courts historically more willing to stop wrong actions than compel positive actions. PIL changes this balance.
20.6 — Is the State Bound by a Statute?
📄 Pages Covered: 1442–1445
👑 Can the State Claim Exemption from Its Own Statutes?
📝 Common law rule (from English monarchy): Crown is not bound by statute unless expressly named. India inherited this from British law. But in republican India, the courts have significantly eroded this principle.
OLD RULE (Common Law)
Crown/State NOT bound by statute unless expressly named in the statute
Based on doctrine of royal prerogative from England
📝 English monarchy principle: The King cannot be sued or bound without consent. India inherited this — but has a Republic, not a monarchy. Courts have questioned its relevance.
MODERN RULE (India)
General presumption: State IS bound by statute
Burden on State to prove it is exempt
Exemption only by (i) Express words OR (ii) Necessary implication
📝 Post-Remembrancer case — the default flipped. Now State must prove exemption, not the citizen prove inclusion. "Necessary implication" gives courts flexibility.
Remembrancer of Legal Affairs v Corporation — Three Indicia for State Bound by Statute
(1) There is no canonical law rule binding in India that the Crown is not bound by a statute
(2) The relevant question is construction of the particular statute concerned
(3) The rule based on the prerogative of the Crown has no relevance in India where there is no Crown
Sub-sections: Application of the Principle
20.6.2.1 JUBBI
UOI v Jubbi | AIR 1968 SC 360
HP Abolition of Big Landed Estates Act — Tenant of State could acquire proprietary rights under Act.
SC: State NOT excluded by implication. "Object of Act = abolish big landed estates. No discrimination between state and citizen intended. Act binds every landowner whether individual or government."
📝 Object-based test: If the object of the statute would be defeated by excluding the State, the State IS bound. Legislature cannot have intended discrimination.
20.6.2.2 ESIC Case
State v ESIC | Madras HC
State running transport bus — employee injured due to driver negligence. State argued it need not reimburse ESIC.
Court: Government bound by necessary implication. "Absurd to exclude state from liability as statute is intended for public good." State employing itself in road transport cannot claim exemption from ESIC liability.
📝 "Public good" test: When statute is for public benefit, excluding the State would be absurd — courts will bind the State by necessary implication.
💡 GST: State PSUs / government companies — cannot claim exemption from GST on the ground of state ownership. They are "persons" within CGST Act. Jubbi + ESIC principle applies.
20.6.3 CRIMINAL PROSECUTION
In criminal cases involving imprisonment — State may normally be exempt (it cannot imprison itself). But for statutory duties with financial consequences, the State is generally bound.
The Remembrancer principle applies: State is not exempt from statutory obligations merely because it is the State.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter XX | Administrative Duties
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, First Appellate Authority, and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Julius v Bishop of Oxford + Padfield v Minister of Agriculture
House of Lords | (1880) 5 AC 214 | [1968] All ER 694 — Locus Classicus on Discretion as Duty
Key Ratio — Julius
"In every case of an application of any party complaining of an offence, it shall be lawful for the bishop... to issue a commission for the purpose of making inquiry as to the grounds of such charge." — Even where the provision uses "may" or "shall be lawful", where the purpose is to benefit a class of persons, the authority IS obligated to exercise the power when invoked.
Key Ratio — Padfield (Lord Reid)
"The Act imposes on the Minister a responsibility whenever there is a relevant and substantial complaint... If he does not do that he is rendering nugatory a safeguard provided by the Act and depriving complainers of a remedy which I am satisfied that Parliament intended them to have."
When to Use — When GST Officer/Authority uses "discretion" as excuse to avoid deciding a legitimate issue. Cite Julius + Padfield: The discretion given by Section 73/74/107 etc. is not a license to avoid. When a taxpayer brings home the grounds, the authority is DUTY-BOUND to examine and decide. Refusal to decide = mandamus-able.
Ratlam Municipality v Vardhichand
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1980 SC 1622 | (1980) 4 SCC 162 | V.R. Krishna Iyer J
Key Ratio 1 — Discretion Becomes Duty
"Discretion becomes a duty when the beneficiary brings home the circumstances for its benign exercise."
Key Ratio 2 — Financial Incapacity No Defence
"The Criminal Procedure Code operates against statutory bodies and others regardless of the cash in their coffers, even as human rights under Part III of the Constitution have to be respected by the State regardless of budgetary provision."
Key Ratio 3 — Public Duty Principle
"This is a public duty implicit in the public power to be exercised on behalf of the public and pursuant to a public proceeding."
When to Use — (a) When GST Officer/Department delays or avoids granting refund citing "administrative" or "financial" constraints — cite Ratlam: statutory duty is not excused by resource constraints. (b) When appellate authority avoids deciding, cite: "discretion becomes a duty when the beneficiary brings home the circumstances." (c) In GSTAT — when departmental appeal is filed only to delay — argue public duty requires expeditious hearing.
Common Cause v UOI — Statutory Forum Must Be Constituted
Supreme Court of India | State Governments directed to constitute district consumer fora
Key Principle
"It is the responsibility of the State Government to set up a district consumer forum and the State Government cannot absolve itself of this responsibility." — Where a statute creates a right and provides a forum, constituting and operationalizing the forum is itself a DUTY of the State.
When to Use — GSTAT non-constitution / delayed constitution writ petitions. Argue: Section 109 CGST Act creates GSTAT — Union and States CANNOT absolve themselves of responsibility to constitute and operationalize it. Every day of delay deprives taxpayers of statutory right to appeal. Common Cause principle directly applies.
Chief Controlling Revenue Authority v Maharashtra S. Mills — Power Coupled with Obligation
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1950 SC 218 | Stamp Act Section 57 Case
Key Ratio
"In our opinion, the power contained in section 57 is in the nature of an obligation or is coupled with an obligation and under the circumstances can be demanded to be used also by the parties affected by the assessment... If he omits to do so it is within the power of the Court to direct him to discharge that duty."
Core Principle
"It does not appear, on principle, sound to hold that these difficult questions should be left under the Stamp Act to the final decision of the appellants, and if the party affected by the assessment has a grievance, there is no relief at all in law for him."
When to Use — When a GST authority refuses to refer a question of law (e.g., refuse to state a case, or refuse to consider a legal argument on merits). Cite: Power to examine and decide legal questions = power coupled with obligation. Officer cannot simply ignore legal complexity and pass a summary order. "It is his duty, as a public servant, to discharge that duty."
UOI v Jubbi + Remembrancer of Legal Affairs — State is Bound by Statute
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1968 SC 360 | AIR 1967 SC 997
Key Ratio — Jubbi
"It is clear that the object of the Act was to... [achieve its purpose]. That being the paramount object of the legislature, it is hardly likely that it would make any discrimination between the state and the citizen in the matter of the application of the Act."
Modern Rule (Remembrancer)
The rule that the Crown is not bound by statute unless expressly named has no application in republican India where there is no Crown. The state will be bound by a statute. The burden is on the state to prove that it is exempt from it.
When to Use — When government companies, PSUs, or state departments claim they are exempt from GST provisions: (a) Section 9 (levy applies to all "persons" including Government); (b) Section 23 exemptions must be specifically established. Default: State IS bound. Jubbi establishes: where object of statute would be defeated by excluding State, State is bound. Use against claims by government entities that they are not "persons" under CGST Act.
PT. Rajan v TPM Sabir — Interpretation of "May" and "Shall"
Supreme Court of India | AIR 2003 SC 4603 | (2003) 8 SCC 498
Key Propositions
(1) A statute must be read in text and context. Whether directory or mandatory does not depend solely on "shall" or "may" — it depends on purpose and object sought to be achieved.
(2) Where a statutory functionary is asked to perform a duty within prescribed time, the time-limit is directory and not mandatory.
(3) "Shall" in a procedural statute may not be mandatory if no prejudice is caused.
When to Use — (a) Defending against department claims that time-barred SCN is valid (Section 73/74 time-limits are directory — no prejudice caused to Revenue). (b) Attacking time-barred SCN — where failure to issue SCN within stipulated period causes PREJUDICE to assessee (cannot gather records, limitation has run), the time-limit becomes mandatory. (c) Opposing department argument that "may grant extension" under Sec 168A is mandatory — it is discretionary. Context determines the result.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter XX Administrative Duties — Pages 1416–1445
⚖️ Chapter 21 — Conduct of Government Business

⚖️ Chapter 21 — Conduct of Government Business

Source: GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative Route
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
21.1 Introductory — Conflicting Values
21.1.1 Under Rules of Business
21.1.2 Sub-Delegation
21.3 President
21.3 Cases — Samsher, Sripati, Mitter
21.4 Governor
21.5 Lt. Governor of NCT Delhi
21.6 Government/Ministers
21.6 Carltona Principle
21.7 Rules of Business
21.7 Key Cases on Rules of Business
21.7.13 — Order not in conformity = Ultra Vires
21.7.14 Rules of Business v Sub-delegation
21.8 Form of the Order
21.9 Rules of Authentication
21.10 Challenging an Authenticated Order
21.11 Duly Authenticated Order — Still Challengeable
21.12 Notings in File ≠ Order
21.13 Rules of Allocation
21.14 Conferment of Power on Designated Authorities
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
21.1 — Introductory | Two Conflicting Values in Administrative Law
🏛️ Conduct of Government Business
Chapter XXI | Principles of Administrative Law
Two Almost Conflicting Values Come into Play
VALUE 1
Delegatus non potest delegare
The authority to whom power is given by law should exercise it by itself — not by anyone else
📝 Delegatus non potest delegare = A delegate cannot further delegate. If Parliament gave power to Person A, only Person A should exercise it — not Person B.
Justification: Legislature placed its trust in the judgment and suitability of that specific authority for the purpose
VALUE 2
Practical Exigencies of Modern Administration
High officials cannot personally discharge every power — that would cause administrative breakdown
📝 In plain terms: The Government is a massive machine. If the Minister had to personally sign every file, nothing would ever get done. A mechanism is needed to pass work to lower officials.
Senior officer may have powers under many laws; impossible burden if personally required to decide each
SOLUTION — BALANCE
Two legal methods available to enable a lower authority to exercise power vested in a higher authority:
(1) Under the Rules of Business | (2) Through Sub-Delegation
21.1.1 — Under the Rules of Business
📜 Rules of Business — Constitutional Basis
CONSTITUTIONAL SCHEME
Powers conferred on the President / Governor can, in actual practice, be exercised by Ministers and other government officials.
Similarly, powers conferred on Central/State Government or Ministers can be exercised by officials in government departments without always needing approval of the concerned Minister or Cabinet.
📝 This is the heart of how Indian government actually runs. The President/Governor/Minister does not personally sign every order. Officials lower in the hierarchy do it — and that is constitutionally valid because of the Rules of Business.
CENTRAL
Article 77(3): President shall make Rules of Business and Rules of Allocation among Ministers
STATE
Article 166(3): Governor shall make Rules of Business and Allocation among Ministers
KEY PRINCIPLE
Decision of any Minister or Officer under Rules of Business made under Article 77(3)/166(3) is the decision of the President/Governor. These Articles do NOT provide for any delegation — it is the Government itself acting through its limbs.
💡 GST Application: When a GST Show Cause Notice (SCN) is issued or a demand order is passed by a Joint Commissioner / Deputy Commissioner under Rules of Business — that IS the order of the Central Government. Challenge on ground "President/Minister didn't personally decide" is legally untenable.
21.1.2 — Sub-Delegation: When Rules of Business Do NOT Apply
🔀 Sub-Delegation vs Rules of Business — Key Distinction
RULES OF BUSINESS APPLY WHEN
Powers conferred on:
• President / Governor
• Central / State Government
• Ministers
📝 When the law gives power to the Government as a whole or to the President/Governor, the Rules of Business enable officials to exercise it on their behalf.
RULES OF BUSINESS DO NOT APPLY WHEN
Powers are vested in:
• Designated officials and authorities (e.g., Commissioner of Income Tax, Settlement Commissioner)
📝 If law gives power directly to a specific officer by name or designation — only that officer can exercise it. Rules of Business cannot help. Sub-delegation under the statute is needed.
Sub-delegation operates — requires specific statutory authorisation
GST RELEVANCE
CGST Act confers powers on specific designated officers (Proper Officer, Commissioner, etc.) — these officers exercise power in their own right, not under Rules of Business. Challenge based on wrong officer is a valid challenge of jurisdiction.
21.3 — The President: Constitutional Head, Not Effective Decision Maker
🇮🇳 The President — Constitutional Position
ARTICLE 53(1)
Executive power of the Union is vested in the President.
He may exercise it "either directly or through officers subordinate to him".
Article 77 — Three Limbs
Art 77(1)
All executive action of Central Government — expressed in the name of the President
Art 77(2)
Manner in which order is to be authenticated
Art 77(3)
President to make Rules of Business + Allocation Rules
CRITICAL RULE — "SATISFACTION" OF PRESIDENT
Wherever Constitution requires "satisfaction" of the President for exercising any power — it is NOT his personal satisfaction, but the satisfaction of his Council of Ministers on whose aid and advice he generally exercises all powers.

President's "opinion, satisfaction or decision is constitutionally secured when his Ministers arrive at such opinion, satisfaction or decision." [Samsher Singh]
📝 In plain terms: When a statute says "President is satisfied" — it really means "the relevant Minister/Government is satisfied." The President himself need not personally apply his mind to each file. He acts as a constitutional figurehead, not an active decision-maker.
Exceptions — Functions President Must Discharge Personally
(i) Appointment of Prime Minister
(ii) Dissolution of Lok Sabha
(iii) Assent to Bills
(iv) Proclamation of Emergency — Art 352
(v) President's Rule — Art 356
📝 Even for these functions, the President acts on advice of Council of Ministers (except appointing PM). The difference is he must personally sign/act — not just have a Minister do it.
21.3 — Key Cases on Presidential/Gubernatorial Power
CaseCourt / CitationKey HoldingGST/Litigation Relevance
Samsher Singh v State of Punjab SC | AIR 1974 SC 2192 President need not be personally satisfied. Satisfaction = satisfaction of Council of Ministers. Decision by Minister/officer under Rules of Business = decision of President. Article 77(3) does NOT provide for delegation. Foundational — any order by competent govt officer under Rules of Business is a valid Presidential/Central Govt order. Defeats challenges on "Minister didn't personally decide."
UOI v Sripati Ranjan SC | AIR 1975 SC 1755 Minister of Finance validly decided appeal addressed to the President. Constitutional scheme contemplates a constitutional President — not a ruling monarch. "Any reference to the President... must need be to the President as the constitutional head." Even when a rule says "appeal to President" — Minister's decision under Rules of Business is the President's decision.
UOI v J.P. Mitter SC | AIR 1971 SC 1093 Exception: Under Article 217(3), President must personally decide question of age of High Court Judge after consulting Chief Justice — cannot act on ministerial advice. Narrow exception — judiciary independence requires personal Presidential action here.
Common Cause v UOI SC | AIR 1999 SC 2979 Order in name of President does NOT become order passed by him personally — it is essentially an order of the Government passed in exercise of executive functions. Not immune from judicial review under Article 361. Courts can examine its validity. Important: GST orders expressed in name of President/Governor are judicially reviewable. Immunity under Article 361 does NOT extend to such orders.
State of Maharashtra v R.S. Nayak SC | AIR 1982 SC 1248 Governor sanctioning prosecution of Chief Minister must act in his own discretion (not on advice of Council of Ministers) — as Ministers themselves are interested. Doctrine of necessity fills the gap. Exception where entire Council is interested — Governor acts personally. "Doctrine of necessity" allows departure from normal cabinet advice rule.
21.4 — The Governor: Position, Powers & Discretion
🏛️ The Governor — State Executive Head
ARTICLE 154 Executive power of State vested in Governor
Counterpart to Article 77 at Centre = Article 166 in States
Rules of Business in States under Article 166(3)
SIMILARITY WITH PRESIDENT
Governor's "satisfaction" = satisfaction of his Council of Ministers (not personal)
What is said of President applies mutatis mutandis to Governor
📝 Mutatis mutandis = with the necessary changes made. The President rules apply to Governor with State-specific tweaks.
TWO DIFFERENCES FROM PRESIDENT
(1) Constitution confers some discretionary functions on Governor (not on President)
(2) Article 163(1) does NOT make advice of Council of Ministers binding on Governor in his discretionary functions
Governor's Discretionary Functions — Must Act Personally
Assent / reserving bills — Article 200
Report to President under Article 356 (Constitutional breakdown)
Matters under 6th Schedule, para 9(2) & 18(3)
Appointment/dismissal of Chief Minister
GENERAL RULE [Samsher / Shamsher]
"The Governor exercises his powers on the aid and advice of his Council of Ministers, save in spheres where the Governor is required by or under the Constitution to exercise his functions in his discretion."

Whenever power is conferred on Governor by constitutional/statutory provision — same can be exercised by the State Government except where power is to be exercised by the Governor in his discretion.
💡 UPPS Commission v Suresh Chandra: Appeal against punishment by State Public Service Commission — Governor need not personally hear it. State Government can dispose under Rules of Business as it is not a discretionary function.
21.5 — Lt. Governor of NCT Delhi: Unique Constitutional Position
🏙️ Lt. Governor of NCT Delhi — Special Position
NCT OF DELHI 1993 RULES
Council of Ministers shall inform all its decisions to the LG — mandatory.
But NO requirement of seeking concurrence of LG for every decision.
📝 The LG must be kept in the loop (informed) about all decisions — but he need not approve them. The idea is to keep him aware so he can exercise power under Article 239AA(4) if warranted.
LG's ROLE
To be informed → Kept in loop → Enabled to exercise power under Art 239AA(4) → Reference to President only if warranted
LIMIT [Nabam Rebia]
Requiring prior concurrence of LG = contrary to Parliament's intention to treat Delhi as a representative government
Governor's order preponing assembly session without Council's advice = breach of constitutional mandate
21.6 — Government/Ministers: Who Exercises Power?
🏢 Government of India — Powers Conferred by Statute
STATUTORY DEFINITION
Section 3(60) of General Clauses Act, 1879 → "Government of India" = "President"
Legally, no material difference whether power conferred on Government of India / Central Government / President
📝 So when GST law says "Central Government may" or "Commissioner of Central Government" — it ultimately traces back to the President exercising power through the administrative machinery. The Act applies to interpretation of the Constitution as well via Article 367.
Vesting of power in Central/State Government does NOT mean:
President must personally decide each matter
Governor must personally decide each matter
Cabinet must personally dispose every case
Minister must personally read every file
ACTUAL POSITION
Government officials — acting under Rules of Business and standing orders — exercise powers on behalf of the Government. These officials are limbs of the government, not its delegates. Their decision IS the Government's decision.
21.6.1 — The Carltona Principle: Civil Servant as Alter Ego of Minister
⚖️ Carltona Principle [UK → Adopted in India]
📝 Alter ego = The other self. In law, this means the civil servant IS the minister — not a delegate, not an agent, but the same legal entity acting. So when a Deputy Secretary signs an order, it is as if the Minister signed it personally.
CARLTONA [UK CA, 1943]
"The duties imposed upon ministers and the powers given to ministers are normally exercised under the authority of the ministers by responsible officials of the department. Public business could not be carried on if that were not the case. Constitutionally, the decision of such an official is, of course, the decision of the minister. The minister is responsible."
Indian Law Principle [Samsher / Sanjeevi / Rukmani Bai]
Civil servant does NOT act as a delegate of his Minister — he acts on behalf of Government
Officials are the limbs of the Government — not persons to whom power has been delegated
When official acts under Rules of Business — no delegation — constitutionally the act IS that of the Minister
Minister retains power to call any file, pass orders, issue directions — subject to which officers decide on behalf of Government
KEY LIMIT
Wadhwa Rule: Rules of Business CANNOT override provisions of an Act or statutory rule.
[State of Haryana v P.C. Wadhwa, AIR 1987 SC 1201]

Chaman Rule: Order passed by an unauthorised officer CANNOT be validated retrospectively by subsequently amending standing orders.
💡 GST Application: A demand order passed by a Superintendent where the power was vested in an Assistant Commissioner by notification — is an order by an unauthorised officer. Cannot be validated retrospectively. Challenge on jurisdiction is valid.
21.7 — Rules of Business: How Power Flows Through the Hierarchy
📋 Rules of Business — Architecture
CENTRAL
Government of India (Allocation of Business) Rules, 1961
+ Transaction of Business Rules
(under Article 77(3))
STATE
Each State has separate Rules of Business under Article 166(3)
Governor makes state-specific rules
INDIA vs BRITAIN
In India: power devolves through formal Rules of Business
In Britain: no formal rules — devolution by customs, conventions and departmental practices
What Types of Power Can Be Exercised Through Rules of Business?
Administrative powers
Quasi-judicial powers
Quasi-legislative powers
Statutory functions and duties
Usual Pattern of Disposal Under Rules of Business
NORMAL
Business disposed of by / under direction of Minister-in-charge
ESCALATE TO CABINET
Matters involving policy / administrative importance
PRACTICAL REALITY
Section Officer / Under Secretary / Deputy Secretary / Joint Secretary / Secretary — independently decide matters per standing orders
VALIDITY OF DECISIONS
Validity of any decision by a competent official under Rules of Business cannot be challenged on the ground that it is not the decision of the President.

The "satisfaction" of an officer duly authorised under the Rules = satisfaction of the Minister, or even of the President/Governor.

Rules of Business have statutory force — binding even though not published in official gazette.
21.7 — Key Cases on Rules of Business (Sanjeevi to Capital)
Case (§)CitationKey PrincipleOutcome/Relevance
Sanjeevi §21.7.1
A. Sanjeevi v State of Madras		 SC | AIR 1970 SC 1102 "Government" means Governor aided and advised by Ministers. Governor cannot deal with each and every matter. Business Rules authorise allocation to Ministers and even to designated officials. Civil servant acts not as delegate but as limb of Government. Rule 23A under which Secretary was empowered = valid. Joint responsibility does not require Cabinet to take each decision.
Zalani §21.7.2
G.D. Zalani v UOI		 SC | AIR 1995 SC 1178 "President" in article 117 of HAL's Articles = Government of India. HAL is a government instrumentality. Directive approved by Minister & authenticated by Deputy Secretary = valid. "To say this power should be exercised by the President himself is neither practicable nor consistent with the dignity of the President." Even directives to PSUs in President's name can be issued by competent officer under Rules of Business.
Raverdy §21.7.5 SC | AIR 1982 SC 311 Under Rules of Business, both Secretary and Home Minister could deal with preventive detention. Order made by Secretary, reviewed by Minister — immaterial which one finally issued order. Validates dual-level handling of matters under Rules of Business.
Bal Kalyan §21.7.6 Bom HC | AIR 1993 Rules of Business required prior consultation with Finance Department before granting land lease. Omission = "not merely a technical infirmity but a basic illegality." Subsequent concurrence NOT a valid substitute for prior consultation. Where Rules of Business prescribe a mandatory procedure — non-compliance = illegality.
Wadhwa §21.7.7 SC | AIR 1987 SC 1201 State Government could not authorise Home Secretary to write ACR of IGP. Reporting authority must be higher in rank than person being reported on. Rules of Business under Art 166 cannot override provisions of the Police Act or statutory rules. Golden rule: Rules of Business CANNOT override statute. If statute prescribes a specific authority — Rules of Business cannot substitute a lower one.
Mohan §21.7.8 Cal HC | AIR 1974 Cal 253 Deputy Secretary under standing orders disposed of land acquisition matter. Held valid — Secretary's satisfaction = Governor's satisfaction. "His satisfaction will amount to the satisfaction of the Governor as required under the law, not being a matter requiring his personal discretion." Illustrates filtration of power down the hierarchy through standing orders under Rules of Business.
Labh Singh §21.7.9 Del HC | AIR 1970 Del 171 Deputy Secretary reviewing order of Chief Settlement Commissioner (higher rank officer) — valid. Every officer acting under Business Rules acts not for himself but for the Government. Decision of Deputy Secretary = decision of Government of India = superior entity to Commissioner. Officer of lower rank can review order of higher-ranking officer if acting under Rules of Business on behalf of Government.
Rukmani Bai §21.7.11 SC | AIR 1975 SC 991 Officer under Rules of Business does NOT act as delegate — he acts in exercise of the power of the State Government itself. His order = order of Government itself. No infirmity for lack of "delegation." Clarifies: Rules of Business ≠ delegation. The official IS the Government when acting under Rules of Business.
Capital §21.7.12 SC | AIR 1967 SC 1815 Government may act under Rules of Business even when statutory provision permits sub-delegation. Both mechanisms co-exist. Government has option to use either. Business Rules are substantive (not procedural) — made under Constitutional authority — cannot be modified by statutory provision. Even if statute provides for sub-delegation, Government can still use Rules of Business. Both are valid pathways.
21.7.13 — Order Not in Conformity with Rules of Business = Ultra Vires
🚫 Non-Conformity with Rules of Business = Ultra Vires
📝 Ultra vires = Beyond power. If Rules of Business say only a Joint Secretary or above can make an order, and a Deputy Secretary makes it — the order is wholly void. No court will enforce it.
FONSECA CASE [SC]
Under Defence of India Rules, only Central Government could order eviction. Under Rules of Business, this power vested in Secretary / Special Secretary / Addl Secretary / Joint Secretary.

Deputy Secretary made the order — wholly illegal, ineffective and void — quashed by SC.
RAJURESHWAR CASE [SC]
Under Business Rules, decision in the specific matter was required to be taken by the Cabinet. Instead, a Department took the decision.

Departmental decision = not valid — not in conformity with Rules of Business.
THREE WAYS TO CHALLENGE AN ORDER UNDER RULES OF BUSINESS
(1) Condition precedent to making the order has not been fulfilled
(2) Principles of natural justice have not been observed where required
(3) Order does not correctly denote the actual decision made by the Government
💡 GST Application: If Rules of Business require that a matter above ₹X crore demand must be decided by Commissioner, but an Additional Commissioner passes the order — ultra vires. Challenge on jurisdiction = maintainable. The order can be quashed.
21.7.14 — Rules of Business vs Sub-Delegation: Key Distinctions
Parameter Rules of Business Sub-Delegation
Basis Constitutional — Articles 77(3) / 166(3) Statutory — requires specific statutory provision authorising it
Who Acts Official acts IN THE NAME OF the Government/President/Governor/Minister — as a limb Sub-delegate acts IN HIS OWN NAME — as a person to whom power has been delegated
Nature of action No delegation — official IS the Government. Decision = Government's decision Actual delegation of power from higher to lower — sub-delegate exercises power in his own right
Prior authorisation needed? No specific prior authorisation in advance — officials go on acting per standing orders Requires a distinct act by which higher authority confers power on lower officer
Co-existence Both mechanisms co-exist — even where statute permits sub-delegation, Government may still use Rules of Business Supplementary to Rules of Business — not exclusive. Sibnath Banerji (PC): statutory sub-delegation provision is "merely supplementary"
Applies to Powers vested in President/Governor/Central/State Government/Ministers Powers vested in DESIGNATED officials — Rules of Business cannot help here
21.8 — Form of the Order: Article 77(1) / 166(1) Requirements
📝 Form of Government Order — Constitutional Requirements
ARTICLE 77(1) / 166(1)
All executive action of Central/State Government shall be expressed to be taken in the name of the President/Governor.
Until this formality is observed → action cannot be regarded as that of the Government.
DIRECTORY, NOT MANDATORY [Supreme Court — Consistent View]
Requirements of Articles 77(1) and 166(1) are DIRECTORY — not mandatory — even though the word "shall" is used.

Substantial compliance with the substance of the requirements suffices — strict compliance is not necessary.

"The Constitution does not require a magic incantation which can only be expressed in a set formula of words. What we have to see is whether the substance of the requirements is there." [Naik, SC]
📝 So even if an order says "Government of Bombay" instead of "Governor of Bombay" — if signed by the proper authority and the substance is clear — the order is valid. Courts look at the substance, not the magic words.
Illustrative Cases on Form of Order
CaseIssueHeld
Naik — State of Bombay v Purushottam Jog Naik [SC 1952] Order said "Government of Bombay" instead of "Governor" Valid. "By order of the Governor" at bottom + Secretary's signature = substantial compliance with Art 166(1)/(2)
Orient Paper [SC 1979] Notification in name of Central Government, not President Art 77(1)/(2) = merely directory. Government can lead evidence to prove compliance.
Chitralekha [SC 1964] Letter from Under Secretary not conforming with Art 166 formalities Not quashed — nobody denied it was a Government order. Substance test satisfied.
Ghaio Mal [SC 1959] Inter-departmental communications Cannot be regarded as orders of the Government.
Sripal Jain [SC 1963] Order conveyed through "I am directed..." letter by subordinate authority Valid if made by competent authority — mode of communication does not affect validity.
Bansal — J.P. Bansal v State of Rajasthan [SC 2003] Mere cabinet decision not couched in formal order Constitution requires action in name of Governor — not enforceable till formality observed. Cabinet decision is only tentative till translated into formal authenticated order.
BURDEN OF PROOF
Burden to prove validity of the order falls on the party concerned — whether government or individual — whoever invokes such an order in their favour must prove affirmatively that it was made by the competent authority according to the Rules of Business.
21.9 — Rules of Authentication of Orders
🔏 Rules of Authentication — Who Can Sign Government Orders?
ARTICLE 77(2) / 166(2)
Orders in President's/Governor's name shall be authenticated in the manner prescribed.
Once duly authenticated — validity cannot be called in question on the ground that it is not an order made or executed by the President/Governor.
📝 Authentication = the signature of an authorised officer on an order expressed in the name of President/Governor. Once this is done, there is an irrebuttable presumption that the order IS the President's order. The authenticating signature signifies consent of the Governor and acceptance of the advice of the concerned Minister.
AUTHENTICATION RULES, 1958 — WHO CAN SIGN?
Orders made in President's name authenticated by signatures of:
Secretary / Special Secretary / Additional Secretary / Joint Secretary / Deputy Secretary / Under Secretary / Assistant Secretary to Government of India
EFFECT OF AUTHENTICATION
Irrebuttable presumption that order = made and executed by President/Governor
Art 77(2)/166(2) immunizes from ONE SPECIFIC CHALLENGE ONLY: that order was not made by the President/Governor
WHAT AUTHENTICATION DOES NOT PROTECT
Non-compliance with Art 77(2)/166(2) does not invalidate the order — but precludes drawing irrebuttable presumption
Order can still be proved valid by other evidence
TRIMBAK CASE [SC]
Authentication of order by officer authorised under Authentication Rules "signifies the consent of the Governor as well as the acceptance of the advice rendered by the concerned Minister."

Personal satisfaction of Governor not required for executive functions — any function may be allocated to Ministers under Art 166(3) Rules of Business, except functions dischargeable in his personal discretion.
21.10 — Challenging an Authenticated Order
⚔️ When Can a Duly Authenticated Order Be Challenged?
ARTICLE 77(2) / 166(2) — SCOPE OF IMMUNITY
Immunises from ONE GROUND ONLY: that order has not been made by the President/Governor.

Does NOT immunise from ALL other challenges.
✗ NOT CHALLENGEABLE
Order not made by President/Governor — protected once duly authenticated
✓ STILL CHALLENGEABLE
• Condition precedent not fulfilled
• Natural justice violated
• Order not made by duly authorised officer under Rules of Business / Allocation Rules
• Order does not correctly reflect actual Government decision
Key Cases on Challenging Authenticated Orders
CaseKey PointOutcome
Sonabati [SC] Order expressed "in name of Governor" and authenticated per Art 166(2) Cannot be called in question on ground it was not made by the Governor — Art 166(2) protection applies.
Ananda Nambiar [SC 1966] Orders suspending Fundamental Rights — signed by Additional Secretary Valid. President's personal satisfaction not essential. Properly authenticated order cannot be questioned on that ground.
Joshi — I.G. Joshi v State of Gujarat [SC 1968] Secretary's determination under Land Acquisition Act Determination by Secretary = determination of Government. Orders by civil servant = valid if power exercised per Rules of Business.
Godavari [SC 1964] Preventive detention order — said "Government of Maharashtra satisfied" — didn't say "Governor" Valid — order passed by Chief Minister who was in charge of department under Allocation Rules.
Yashwant / Trimbak [SC 1996] Pension Rules said "sanction of Governor" needed for departmental proceedings against retired employee — Under Secretary signed Valid. Granting sanction = executive function of Government. Personal satisfaction of Governor not required. May be allocated to Minister under Rules of Business.
PRACTICAL DIFFICULTY FOR CHALLENGER
There is a presumption in favour of recitals in duly authenticated orders. Burden of proof to show recitals are wrong = on the challenging party. Difficult because facts are within Government's exclusive knowledge.
💡 GST Hearing Tip: When challenging an order of an officer as being from wrong authority — you must affirmatively prove the officer was not authorised under Rules of Business / notification. The burden is heavy. Best strategy: get the Rules of Business / Notification and demonstrate clearly which officer is authorised for which monetary limit.
21.11 — Duly Authenticated Order May Still Be Struck Down (Sibnath, Gullapalli, Royappa)
⚠️ Authentication Does Not Sanitise All Defects
Three Key Cases Where Duly Authenticated Orders Were Set Aside
SIBNATH BANERJI [PC 1945]
Detention order signed by competent officer under Rules of Business — but authority acted mechanically on police recommendation without applying its mind.

Condition precedent = "satisfaction" — not fulfilled in fact.
Order quashed.
📝 Even if the right officer signs — he must actually apply his mind. Mechanical/rubber-stamp signing = nullity.
GULLAPALLI [SC 1959]
Duly authenticated order quashed because quasi-judicial principles of natural justice had not been observed by the Government in making the order.
📝 Natural justice requirement survives authentication. If you should have given a hearing but didn't — the authenticated order is still bad.
ROYAPPA [SC 1974]
Authenticated order did not correctly embody the real decision — it said "promoted as Chief Secretary" while actual decision was different.

"The formal expression of the order cannot be given such sanctity that even if it is found to be mistaken, it must prevail over the actual order made."
📝 The authenticated document is evidence of the decision — if it incorrectly records the decision, the actual decision prevails. Authentication is form, not substance.
GROUNDS FOR CHALLENGE SUMMARY
(1) Officer not duly authorised under Rules of Business / Allocation Rules
(2) Condition precedent to making the order not fulfilled
(3) Natural justice violated where required
(4) Order does not correctly reflect actual Government decision
(5) Mind not applied / mechanical action (Sibnath principle)
💡 GST Application: A GST demand order where the proper officer passes the order without actually examining the documents, relying entirely on a departmental recommendation without independent application of mind = challengeable on Sibnath principle. Also useful where SCN and Order are copy-paste of each other — no DRP mind applied.
21.12 — Notings in the File Do NOT Constitute an Order
📄 Noting ≠ Order: The Essential Distinction
📝 Noting = What an officer or Minister writes in the file while processing a matter. This is an internal record — not yet an order. Even if the Minister notes "Please approve" or "Approved" — that is NOT a binding order until it is (a) expressed in name of Governor and (b) communicated to the affected person.
NOTINGS ARE:
• Provisional in character
• Can be reconsidered, changed, varied, cancelled at any time
• Not binding on the Government
• Not enforceable by the individual
A BINDING ORDER REQUIRES:
(1) Expression in name of Governor/President [Art 166(1) / 77(1)]
(2) Communication to the person concerned
Until both: only a tentative decision — can still be changed
Key Cases on Notings
CaseKey Point
Bachittar Singh [SC 1963] Revenue Minister's noting in file (reducing dismissal to reversion) = NOT a binding order. Chief Minister could review and restore dismissal. "Merely writing something in the file does not amount to a binding order." Matter is not final till formally authenticated and communicated.
Kedar Nath [SC 1979] Chief Minister's minute agreeing to confirmation = not effective as no formal order issued. Later order reverting = valid.
Lakshmikutty [SC 1987] Chief Minister's Press Conference announcement of district judge appointments = no authenticated order issued = candidates not entitled to be appointed merely by announcement.
Gulabrao [SC] Revenue Department letter proposing to drop land acquisition = not final decision. No properly authenticated order issued. Decision of Minister under Business Rules not final till Art 166(1)/(2) complied with.
Mondal [SC 2001] Order passed but retained in file without being communicated = no force or authority whatever. "An uncommunicated order has no valid existence in the eye of law."
Nareshbhai Bhagubhai [SC 2019] Handwritten note in file of competent authority (disallowing all 59 objections) = could not be considered an order on objections under the Railways Act. Formal order required.
GST CRITICAL APPLICATION
In GST proceedings: Internal notings, DIN communications, or internal recommendations do NOT constitute valid orders. The final demand order or order-in-original must be properly expressed in the name of the authority concerned and communicated. Uncommunicated orders — even if made — have no force of law.
💡 GST: If proper officer uploads an order on portal but does not communicate it physically or via email/portal to taxpayer — can the taxpayer be bound? The Mondal principle says uncommunicated order has no legal existence. Limitation for appeal would also not run from an uncommunicated order.
21.13 — Rules of Allocation
📊 Rules of Allocation vs Rules of Business: The Difference
RULES OF ALLOCATION
Specify items of business allotted to each Ministry / Department
Government of India (Allocation of Business) Rules, 1961
Published / available to public
Ultra vires to do something NOT assigned by Allocation Rules
RULES OF BUSINESS
Provide for disposal of work WITHIN a Ministry/Department
How decisions are made internally
Usually confidential (though binding once constitutionally made)
KEY RULE — ULTRA VIRES UNDER ALLOCATION RULES
A Ministry can perform only such tasks as are assigned to it by the Allocation Rules.
Action by wrong Ministry = without jurisdiction = ultra vires.

If sanction is given by Ministry A when Ministry B has exclusive power under Allocation Rules — sanction by Ministry A is void.
CaseIssueResult
