Datta [SC 1981] Sanction to prosecute ZSI employee granted by Ministry of Home Affairs — should have been by Ministry of Science & Technology per Allocation Rules Sanction void — granted by incompetent authority. Special judge had no jurisdiction. Conviction set aside.
Singh [SC 1971] Sanction to prosecute given by Ministry of Industrial Development — Home Ministry was competent Sanction void — wrong Ministry.
Godavari [SC 1964] Order of preventive detention on ground of both public order AND defence of India — different Departments had exclusive jurisdiction over each Valid — order made by Chief Minister who was in charge of BOTH departments. If you have authority over both subjects, one order covering both = valid.
Express Newspapers [SC 1986] Lt. Governor of Delhi exercising power over Union's land in Delhi Invalid — all matters relating to Union property / government lands in Delhi = exclusively vested in Ministry of Works and Housing under Allocation Rules. LG has no power.
Wadhwa §21.13.8 [SC 1987] Business Rules placing IGP under Home Secretary — inconsistent with All India Services Act Business Rules CANNOT override statute. Rules of Allocation also cannot override specific statutory provisions.
BUREAUCRACY WARNING — Trilochan / Kunwar Pal Singh
Supreme Court has warned: Bureaucrats exercising governmental powers must NOT "mortgage their own discretion, volition and decision-making authority."

No bureaucrat is bound to obey an illegal order of Minister/Chief Minister. Civil servants cannot shelter behind Cabinet decision to justify illegal acts. Cabinet decision does not give immunity to act in derogation of rules and regulations.

Court criticised "spineless bureaucracy acting as domestic servants of the Chief Minister" — acting as "deaf, dumb, silent and passive spectators... puppets."
💡 GST Application: A proper officer who issues an SCN or order under political/administrative pressure without independent mind application cannot shelter behind "instructions from Commissioner" or "Board Circular." The Sibnath + Trilochan + Kunwar Pal Singh trilogy together create a complete challenge: officer must independently apply mind AND not act mechanically on superiors' directions.
21.14 — Conferment of Power on Designated Authorities
⚡ Designated Authorities — Rules of Business Do NOT Apply
📝 When Parliament confers power directly on a specified person or body (e.g., Commissioner of Customs, Settlement Commission, NCLT) — that is NOT a power of the President or Government. It is a power of that specific authority. Rules of Business cannot help to transfer it further.
RULES OF BUSINESS DO NOT APPLY
When power conferred on designated authority outside government apparatus (e.g., SEBI, Income Tax Commissioner, Railway Board)
RECOURSE: SUB-DELEGATION
If needed to transfer power from designated authority to lower authority → Must use sub-delegation mechanism under the relevant statute — if statute permits it
GST CRITICAL RELEVANCE
CGST Act confers powers on designated officers — Commissioner, Additional Commissioner, Joint Commissioner, Deputy Commissioner, Assistant Commissioner, Superintendent, Inspector.

Each has a defined monetary limit and jurisdiction per the Act and Notifications. These are designated authorities — Rules of Business mechanism is irrelevant.

If wrong-level officer passes an order (e.g., Superintendent passes order when law requires Assistant Commissioner) → order is without jurisdiction → not curable by Rules of Business → void.
💡 Example: Section 73/74 CGST Act — proper officer is defined as per Notification 02/2017-CT. If an officer not assigned that jurisdiction issues an order — it is not saved by Rules of Business. Challenge on territorial jurisdiction / subject-matter jurisdiction is a complete answer. See also: Filco Trade Centre — wrong officer = without jurisdiction.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 21 | Conduct of Government Business
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, Appellate Authority, and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name and constitutional provision.
Samsher Singh v State of Punjab
Supreme Court | AIR 1974 SC 2192 | Constitution Bench | On Rules of Business, Presidential Satisfaction
Key Ratio — Presidential/Ministerial Satisfaction
"The President's opinion, satisfaction or decision is constitutionally secured when his Ministers arrive at such opinion, satisfaction or decision." Any decision made by a Minister or officer under the Rules of Business made under Article 77(3) is the decision of the President. Article 77(3) does not provide for any delegation of power.
Further Ratio — No Delegation Under Rules of Business
"Where functions entrusted to a Minister are performed by an official employed in the Minister's Department there is, in law, no delegation because constitutionally the act or decision of the official is that of the Minister. The official is merely the machinery for the discharge of the functions entrusted to a Minister."
When to Use — When assessee argues that the GST order is not valid because the President/Government/Commissioner did not personally apply mind. Counter: Under Rules of Business, the concerned officer's decision IS the Government's decision. No separate ministerial approval needed.
UOI v Sripati Ranjan
Supreme Court | AIR 1975 SC 1755 | Constitutional President — Not a Ruling Monarch
Key Ratio
"When the Constitution conclusively contemplates a constitutional President it is not permissible nor is it ever intended to invest upon the President a different role of a ruling monarch. Any reference to the President under any rule made under the Constitution must need be to the President as the constitutional head, as envisaged in the Constitution acting with the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers."
When to Use — When opposing party argues that "Central Government" means personal decision by senior authority. Establish: "Central Government" and "President" = decision by competent officer under Rules of Business. Constitutional scheme does not contemplate hands-on decision-making by the highest authority in every case.
Carltona Ltd. v Works Commissioner | Adopted in India via Sanjeevi
UK Court of Appeal 1943 | Adopted: A. Sanjeevi v State of Madras, AIR 1970 SC 1102
Key Ratio — Alter Ego Doctrine
"In the administration of government in this country the functions which are given to ministers... are functions so multifarious that no minister could ever personally attend to them... Constitutionally, the decision of such an official is, of course, the decision of the minister. The minister is responsible."
Indian Law Version — R v Skinner
"It is not strictly a matter of delegation: it is that the official acts as the Minister himself, and the officials decision is the Minister's decision."
When to Use — When Department argues that a particular officer validly exercised power under Rules of Business. Also useful to confirm: civil servant under Rules of Business = alter ego of Minister = Government itself acting. No separate delegation needed, no infirmity in lower officer's order.
King Emperor v Sibnath Bannerji
Privy Council | AIR 1945 PC 156 | Mind Application — Condition Precedent to "Satisfaction"
Key Ratio — Mechanical Action Voids Order
Authority had signed the detention order in a routine manner, had not applied its mind, and mechanically acted on the recommendation of the police, and had not considered the materials before it so as to satisfy itself, independent of the police recommendation. The condition precedent to the making of such an order — the satisfaction of the authority — had not been fulfilled, and hence the order was quashed.
When to Use — Against GST demand orders where proper officer has mechanically accepted the audit report or predecessor's proposal without independent application of mind. Also against SCNs where the SCN and the order are verbatim copies — proving no fresh mind applied. Condition precedent (proper officer's satisfaction) not fulfilled → order void.
State of Haryana v P.C. Wadhwa | Fonseca (P) Ltd. v L.C. Gupta
SC | AIR 1987 SC 1201 | SC | AIR 1973 SC 563 | Rules of Business Cannot Override Statute
Key Ratio — Rules of Business vs Statute
"The Rules of Business that have been framed under Article 166 cannot override the provisions of the Act or any statutory rule." [Wadhwa]

An order made by a Deputy Secretary had no power under the Business Rules for eviction — the order was "wholly illegal, ineffective and void" and was accordingly quashed. [Fonseca]
When to Use — When Department argues that an order by a lower officer is valid under Rules of Business even though the statute requires a higher officer. Counter: Rules of Business cannot confer jurisdiction beyond what the statute permits. An order by wrong officer = wholly illegal = void = not merely an irregularity.
Bachittar Singh v State of Punjab | Mondal Case
SC | AIR 1963 SC 395 | SC | (2001) 8 SCC 443 | Notings ≠ Order | Uncommunicated Order = No Legal Existence
Key Ratio — File Noting is Not an Order
"Merely writing something in the file does not amount to a binding order." Before something amounts to a formal order, two things are necessary: (1) expressed in the name of the Governor as required by Article 166(1), and (2) communicated. Unless this is done, the State Government could not be regarded as being bound by what was stated in the file. [Bachittar Singh]

"An uncommunicated order has no valid existence in the eye of law." [Mondal]
When to Use — In GST proceedings: Challenge an order that was uploaded on portal but not formally communicated. Also useful where Department relies on internal notings / approval notes as if they are orders. Key argument: limitation period for appeal does not begin from an uncommunicated order; rights of taxpayer unaffected till proper formal order is made and communicated.
Rukmani Bai v State of Madhya Pradesh
Supreme Court | AIR 1975 SC 991 | Officer Under Rules of Business — Not a Delegate but the Government
Key Ratio
An officer authorised to pass an order under the Rules of Business does not act as a delegate of the Government concerned. He acts in exercise of the power of the State Government itself. His order is, therefore, regarded as the order of the Government itself. Therefore, no infirmity can attach to such an order on the ground that the necessary power was not delegated to him.
When to Use — To establish that a GST adjudicating officer's order is not defective merely because no specific "delegation" was shown — under Rules of Business / Notification, the officer IS the proper officer and his order is the Government's order. Also useful to counter challenge that "power was not delegated" to a particular officer.
J.P. Bansal v State of Rajasthan
Supreme Court | AIR 2003 SC 1405 | Cabinet Decision Must Be Formalized — Mere Decision Is Not Enforceable
Key Ratio
"The constitution requires that action must be taken by the authority concerned in the name of the Governor. It is not till this formality is observed that the action can be regarded as that of the State. Constitutionally speaking the Council of Ministers are advisors and as the Head of the State, the Governor is to act with the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers. Therefore, till the advice is accepted by the Governor, views of the council of Ministers do not get crystallised into action of the State."
When to Use — In situations where Department claims that a Circular / Board instruction / policy decision is binding even though no formal authenticated order in the name of the authority concerned was issued. Press conferences, instructions, and policy statements do not become orders till reduced to formal authenticated order and communicated.
Kunwar Pal Singh Rathi v State of Uttar Pradesh | Tirlochan Dev Sharma v State of Punjab
Allahabad HC | AIR 2002 All 27 | SC | AIR 2001 SC 2524 | Bureaucracy Must Exercise Independent Judgment
Key Ratio — Bureaucrat Cannot be a Puppet
"No bureaucrat is expected to carry out an illegal order of the Minister, Chief Minister. No bureaucrat is bound to obey an order, which is in violation of a law or regulation. A cabinet decision cannot be construed to mean that the public officials enjoy immunity to act in any manner they like in derogation of the rules and regulations." [Kunwar Pal Singh]

Bureaucrats must not "mortgage their own discretion, volition and decision-making authority and be prepared to give way or being pushed back or pressed ahead at the behest of politicians for carrying out commands having no sanctity in law." [Tirlochan]
When to Use — When GST officer passes order mechanically following departmental instructions / Board Circular without applying independent judicial mind. Proper officer is required to act as a quasi-judicial authority — not as a conduit for revenue targets. This trilogy establishes the duty of independent mind application by adjudicating officers.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter XXII — Sub-Delegation of Non-Legislative Powers

⚖️ Chapter XXII — Sub-Delegation of Non-Legislative Powers

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative route
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
22.1 Sub-Delegation
22.2 Delegatus Non-Potest Delegare
22.2.3 Position in India
22.3 Express Permission to Sub-Delegate
22.4 Electricity Board — Implied Power
22.5 Fundamental Rights & Sub-delegation
22.6 Delegating Power through Definition Clauses
22.7 Power to Sub-Delegate by Implication
22.8 Sub-Delegation or Assistance
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
22.1 — Sub-Delegation: Concept & Necessity
⚖️ Sub-Delegation of Non-Legislative Powers
Chapter XXII | Adjudicative & Non-Adjudicative Functions
📝 Sub-delegation means passing on power that you received from someone else to someone below you. This chapter is only about non-legislative powers (adjudication, administrative decisions) — not about making rules or regulations.
DEFINITION
Sub-delegation = Transfer or transmission of authority / power from a superior to a subordinate
Legislature → A (delegate) → B (sub-delegate)
📝 The Legislature gives power to 'A'. 'A' then gives (sub-delegates) that power to 'B'. B then acts. If B tries to pass it to C, that would be sub-sub-delegation — usually not permitted.
Why Sub-Delegation Becomes Necessary — Key Reasons
Superior authority needs to concentrate on major policy/administration matters — not routine details
Volume of cases too large for one official to handle — causes undue delay if no sub-delegation
Administrative efficiency — without sub-delegation, the system may be seriously jeopardised
WHEN VALIDLY SUB-DELEGATED
Sub-delegate can validly exercise the power without committing any illegality
Route 1
Government functions under Rules of Business
(does NOT involve sub-delegation)
Route 2
Government resorts to sub-delegation
(permitted ONLY if relevant statute authorises it)
⚠️ ISSUES RAISED
(i) How does sub-delegation take place?
(ii) What rules govern sub-delegation?
(iii) How far can delegating authority control the sub-delegate?
(iv) Must delegating authority lay down policy for sub-delegate?
22.2 — Delegatus Non-Potest Delegare | The Maxim & Its Justification
🔒 Delegatus Non-Potest Delegare
"A delegate cannot further delegate the power"
📝 In Latin: "delegatus non potest delegare" = a delegate cannot sub-delegate. If 'A' gives power to 'B', 'B' must personally exercise it. 'B' cannot hand it to 'C'. This is the basic rule of non-delegation.
Justification / Rationale for the Maxim
When A delegates to B → A relies on B's personal judgment and wisdom
This is a relationship of personal trust
B cannot transfer the trust reposed by A to C
A may not even know C → no trust reposed in C
Legislature conferring discretionary power = Legislature's confidence in that specific authority
Authority cannot bypass this trust by sub-delegating
NORMAL RULE
There CANNOT be sub-delegation of power. Power must be exercised by the person on whom it was conferred.
📝 The Supreme Court says: when law appoints an authority to exercise power, it fully considers the fitness of that person. Wide powers given to the executive to interfere with personal liberty must be exercised by the proper authority of due status.
Illustrative Cases — Non-Delegation Principle Applied
Case Power Involved Sub-delegation Attempted Court's Ruling
Vine v National Dock Labour Board
(1957) AC 488 | UK		 Power to terminate appointment of dock labourer vested in Board Board delegated to a disciplinary committee INVALID — function quasi-judicial, too important to delegate. Board's constitution (equal representatives) designed for balance — if delegated, body may not have same balance.
Barnard v National Dock Labour Board
(1953) 1 All ER 1113		 Power to suspend dock worker vested in Dock Labour Board Suspension order passed by Port Manager to whom Board delegated INVALID — Board had no power to delegate; should have decided itself.
Ganapati Singhji v Ajmer
AIR 1955 SC 188		 Power to issue rules conferred on Chief Commissioner Chief Commissioner delegated to District Magistrate ULTRA VIRES — rule-making power cannot be sub-delegated.
Mela Ram v State of Rajasthan
AIR 1984 Raj 116		 Power to certify govt 'satisfaction' for land acquisition — only Secretary or authorised officer Delegated to Collector without power to do so INVALID — impugned notification quashed. In absence of power to delegate, Government cannot delegate.
Mohan Lal v U.I. Trust, Jodhpur
AIR 1978 Raj 201		 Trust functions — law allowed delegation to President/Committees only Trust delegated to its Secretary INVALID — demolition order by Secretary quashed. No legislative permission to delegate to Secretary.
Kumar Keshridas
AIR 1984 MP 158		 Divisional Engineer empowered to disconnect telephone "if he is satisfied" DE relied on Commercial Officer's satisfaction instead of forming his own INVALID — personal satisfaction of DE cannot be delegated to another.
Sitaram v Corporation of Calcutta
AIR 1956 Cal 78		 Contract-making power vested in Mayor / Deputy Mayor Corporation attempted to delegate to others INVALID — only Mayor/Deputy Mayor can enter contracts on behalf of Corporation.
SMC Students' Parents Association v UOI
AIR 2002 SC 302		 Power to fix college fees vested in UGC alone UGC attempted to delegate fee-fixing to another committee/body/person INVALID — Parliament has not authorised UGC to delegate this power to any committee or person.
Medical Association v State of Maharashtra
(2002) 1 SCC 589		 State Government alone can issue essentiality certificate for medical college State Government attempted to delegate to another authority INVALID — would be repugnant to the Central Act.
Marathwada University v SBR Chavan
AIR 1989 SC 1582		 Power to appoint officers vested in Executive Council; power to appoint includes power to dismiss Vice-Chancellor dismissed deputy registrar — action later ratified by Executive Council VOID AB INITIO — ratification of an unauthorized action cannot cure the defect. Statutory body cannot travel beyond power conferred.
22.2.3 — Position in India | Supreme Court Pronouncements
🇮🇳 Position in India — Non-Delegation Fully Recognised
Supreme Court's Settled Principles on Non-Delegation
"It is a settled principle that when the Act prescribes a particular body to exercise a power, it must be exercised only by that body; it cannot be exercised by others unless it is delegated. The law must also provide for such delegation."
📝 If a law says "the Collector shall decide" — only the Collector can decide. No one else can step in unless the same law also says who else may decide.
"'Delegatus non potest delegare' is a well-known maxim which means in absence of any power a delegate cannot sub-delegate its power to another person."
— Supreme Court (Pramod K. Pankaj v State of Bihar, (2004) 3 SCC 723)
22.2.14 IG of Registration — Nuance: Ancillary Tasks vs Essential Functions
PERMITTED
Appellate authority deputing a subordinate to collect information / cause inspection / make a report = ordinary mode of exercising power
(Not sub-delegation of essential functions)
💡 IG of Registration sends Deputy IG to inspect property and make a report. IG then considers the report and takes the decision himself → VALID. The inspection is ancillary.
NOT PERMITTED
Delegating essential function = considering all inputs and arriving at an informed decision
This burden cannot be transferred to a subordinate
GENERAL PRINCIPLE
Sub-delegation may take place ONLY when the parent law permits it — either expressly or by implication. If the parent Act does not permit sub-delegation, it will be invalid.
22.3 — Express Permission to Sub-Delegate
✅ Express Permission — When Sub-Delegation is Valid
📝 The maxim delegatus non potest delegare is merely a rule of construction — not an absolute bar. If the statute expressly says "you can delegate further," then sub-delegation is perfectly valid. Many Indian statutes routinely contain such provisions.
PRINCIPLE
Sub-delegation of power CAN take place when the law expressly provides for the same.
The maxim is merely a rule of construction — sub-delegation can be sustained if permitted by express statutory provision.
💡 Example: Section 27 of the Sick Industrial Companies Act, 1985 — "The Board may, by general or special order, delegate, subject to such conditions and limitations... to any Member or Secretary or other officer or employee of the Board... such powers and duties under this Act as it may deem necessary."
CasePower & ProvisionSub-delegation Upheld?Reason
Om Prakash v Council of Architecture
AIR 1983 Del 223		 Council of Architecture (50 members) set up advisory committee to decide registration appeals u/s 26(3) YES — Valid Section 10(1) expressly permits constitution of committees. Even in quasi-judicial matters, functions can be distributed. Committee consisted of eminent experts.
Central Talkies v Dwarka Pd.
AIR 1961 SC 606		 District Magistrate conferred his powers on ADM under UP Eviction Act — Section 10(2) CrPC expressly permits this YES — Valid Statutory provision (s.10(2)) expressly permitted the DM to confer powers on ADM.
Sita Ram — Essential Commodities Act
AIR 1984 All 286		 Section 5 of EC Act — Central Govt delegated power; control orders permitted further sub-delegation to State officers YES — Valid Section 5 of EC Act expressly sanctions broad sub-delegation. Power of confiscation delegated to Assistant Collectors held valid.
Ajaib Singh v Gurbachan Singh
AIR 1965 SC 1619		 Section 40, Defence of India Act — State could delegate detention power but not below rank of DM PARTIALLY INVALID Statutory provision restrictively interpreted — power sub-delegatable only up to DM level. ADM passing detention order = NOT valid.
Prakash Roadlines v UOI
AIR 1989 SC 1962		 Commissioner under Delhi Municipal Act had power to impose penalty; s.491 permitted delegation to municipal officer YES — Valid Section 491 expressly permitted Commissioner to delegate powers to municipal officer — including penalty power.
Prem Chhabra — subjective satisfaction
1989 Lab IC 1075		 Power under s.10, Industrial Disputes Act exercisable in State Govt's "subjective satisfaction" — s.39 permits delegation YES — Valid Even where power is exercisable at "satisfaction" of govt, it can still be delegated if express provision exists (s.39).
Gunvantray — quasi-judicial powers
AIR 1978 Guj 130		 Gujarat Agriculture Act s.4(2) — delegation to Joint Director "subject to control of Director" NOT for quasi-judicial Delegation only of those powers which can be exercised "subject to control of Director". Quasi-judicial powers CANNOT be subject to the control of another — so s.4(2) excluded quasi-judicial powers from delegation.
Naraindas — statutory procedure must be followed
AIR 1974 SC 1232		 Board had power to delegate to Chairman "through duly made regulations" INVALID Sub-delegation effectuated by simple resolution of general meeting — not by duly made regulations as required. Procedure prescribed by statute must be strictly followed.
V.G. Shipping v Board of Port Trustees
AIR 1986 Bom 394		 Board could delegate under s.21, Major Port Trusts Act — only WITH approval of Central Govt INVALID Central Govt approval is a pre-condition. Without it, delegation is void. Also — quasi-judicial power (penalty) cannot be delegated under a "general power of delegation" clause alone.
Dalmia — approval need not be formal document
AIR 1975 SC 1017		 Chairman distributing board business with prior Central Govt approval YES — Valid Approval by Central Govt need not be in a formal document. Proof of the fact of giving approval is sufficient.
22.4 — Delegation by Liberal / Implied Reading of Statute
📖 Liberal / Implied Reading — Sub-Delegation Without Express Provision
📝 Sometimes a statute does not explicitly say "you can sub-delegate" — but courts may still allow it if: (a) the nature of the function demands it, or (b) a residuary / general provision covers the situation. The leading case is UP State Electricity Board.
22.4 UP State Electricity Board v Abdul Sakoor Hashmi | AIR 1981 SC 1708
REGULATION
Electricity Board framed regulation laying down — appointing authorities, disciplinary authorities, authorities for appeals against disciplinary action across various posts
HIGH COURT
Struck down regulation — no express power of delegation in the Act
SC REVERSED
Section 79(k) — residuary provision — authorised Board to make regulations for "any other matter arising out of Board's functions under this Act for which it is necessary or expedient to make regulations"
→ Power to regulate disciplinary action falls within s.79(k)
PRAGMATIC LOGIC
Electricity Board employing several thousand employees — impossible for the Board itself to take disciplinary action against each employee. Practical need justified the regulation.
💡 GST parallel: A Commissioner (Adjudication) framing Standard Operating Procedures allocating cases to Additional Commissioners / Deputy Commissioners by way of delegation. If the parent act / rules contain a residuary regulation-making power, such allocation may be supported even without an express sub-delegation clause.
22.4.1 Barium Chemicals — Sub-delegation to Chairman of Board
FACTS
Companies Act allowed Govt to delegate power to Company Law Board. Board made rule allowing Chairman to vest investigation power in himself (section 237 investigation). Challenged as sub-delegation without authority.
MAJORITY (3:2)
Sarkar CJ + Mudholkar J — Chairman acts in the name of the Board; merely allocation of procedure, not sub-delegation. Even if sub-delegation, power to make delegation can be inferred.
Bachawat J — Sub-delegation involved but Govt had ample power to frame rules authorising it.
DISSENT (Shelat J + Hidayatullah J)
This was sub-delegation — not procedure. Powers delegated to Board must be exercised by Board, not by its components. A part cannot be treated as equal to the whole.
📝 The Barium case liberal approach has NOT been consistently followed by SC in other situations. The dissenting view of Shelat J is theoretically more sound — sub-delegation is a question of power, not procedure.
KEY PRINCIPLE EMERGING
Whether a sub-delegation provision will be interpreted liberally or restrictively depends upon the nature of the power involved.

Drastic powers (preventive detention, requisition of property) → restrictive interpretation.
Routine administrative powers → liberal interpretation may be applied.
22.5 — Fundamental Rights & Sub-Delegation
⚖️ Sub-Delegation + Fundamental Rights — Special Scrutiny
📝 Where a Fundamental Right (especially Article 19 — right to trade, Article 21 — personal liberty) is involved, courts apply stricter scrutiny to sub-delegation. The officer who exercises the power must be of sufficient status and capable of exercising it fairly.
Article 19 — Right to Trade / Occupation
DANGER
Statutory provision permitting delegation of power without any limit (to any person) may be held invalid as unreasonable restriction under Article 19(1)(g)
💡 Satchidananda v West Bengal — West Bengal Soft Coke Distribution Order allowed Director to delegate powers to any person — held unconstitutional as unreasonable restriction on Article 19(1)(g).
Krishna Bus Service v State of Haryana (AIR 1985 SC 1651)
Powers of inspection/seizure of motor vehicles under s.129 conferred on General Manager, Haryana Roadways
→ He was a business rival of private operators
→ Conflict between duty and interest
→ HELD: Unreasonable restriction on Art 19(1)(g) — appointment void
📝 Administration must be rooted in confidence. Confidence is destroyed when the officer appears biased. A business rival cannot fairly exercise regulatory powers over his competitors.
Article 21 — Preventive Detention (Drastic Power)
John Martin v State of WB (AIR 1975 SC 775)
Power of preventive detention conferred on District Magistrate — challenged as violation of Art 19/21
UPHELD
DM is head of district administration, of sufficiently high status. Further, DM's order must be approved by State Govt within 12 days — inbuilt safeguards → not unreasonable restriction.
A.K. Roy v UOI (AIR 1982 SC 710)
Challenge to State Govt sub-delegating preventive detention power to DM/Commissioner of Police
UPHELD
Power circumscribed by multiple restrictions — period limits, mandatory reporting, approval required within 12 days. In-built safeguards prevent excessive power.
Ajaib Singh (Preventive Detention)
Power cannot be sub-delegated below DM level even if statute permits delegation to "any subordinate authority"
GOVERNING PRINCIPLE
Even when sub-delegation is expressly permissible, the Government must delegate the power to a person who can exercise it properly and without bias in the interest of the general public — especially when a Fundamental Right is involved.
22.6 — Delegating Power through Definition Clauses
📚 Sub-Delegation via Statutory Definition — A Technique
📝 One way to achieve sub-delegation without calling it so: define the authority itself broadly to include subordinates. E.g., define "Registrar" to include "Deputy Registrar" — then the statute doesn't need a separate sub-delegation clause; the subordinate officer IS the authority by definition.
Hutchappa
Mysore Land Acquisition Act — "Deputy Commissioner" defined to include "Assistant Commissioner"
→ No separate empowerment needed. AC can directly perform DC's functions.
Sastriar
Madras Co-operative Societies Act — "Registrar" defined to include any officer on whom powers of Registrar conferred
→ Deputy Registrar on whom powers conferred becomes "Registrar" for all purposes. Even where Act says "Registrar himself" — conferred power officer qualifies.
📝 This formula — defining the authority to include officers on whom powers are conferred — is now commonly adopted in Indian statutes. Very similar to how CGST Act defines "Commissioner" and related authorities.
Mohan — Definition Overrides Maxim
Railway Service (Conduct) Rules — "Government" defined as "General Manager" for non-gazetted staff
→ GM authorising Controller of Stores to call for property statement was NOT sub-delegation — it was the "Government" (as defined) exercising its own power.
CAUTION
Defining 'authority' to include subordinates creates awkward hierarchies — if both are the "same" authority, how can one appeal from one to the other? Better legislative technique is to expressly confer sub-delegation power rather than using definition-clause fiction.
💡 GST parallel: CGST Act Section 2(24) defines "Commissioner" broadly. Section 5 confers powers on proper officers. Section 5(3) allows Commissioner to issue orders specifying conditions under which officers may exercise powers. The definition clause technique and express delegation work together in the GST framework.
22.7 — Power to Sub-Delegate by Implication
🔍 Sub-Delegation by Implication — When Statute is Silent
📝 Sometimes the statute doesn't expressly say "you can delegate further" — but courts imply this power from the nature of the function, the composition of the body, or the practical necessities of the situation. The leading scenarios involve multi-member bodies (High Courts, Boards, Commissions).
Multi-Member Bodies — How They Can Delegate to Committees
NARAINDAS PRINCIPLE
When power is given by statute to a corporate/multi-member body and no provision is made as to how it shall be exercised → the body can by a resolution passed at a general meeting devise its own mode of exercising such power — including authorising one or more members to exercise it on behalf of the body.
📝 But if the parent statute itself provides for delegation of power — then the statutory procedure must be followed. You cannot bypass the prescribed procedure by just passing a board resolution.
Batuk Deo (1978)
High Court set up Administrative Committee to exercise disciplinary control over subordinate judiciary u/Art 235.
SC upheld — nature of control over subordinate courts makes committee essential. Practical needs of situation justify delegation by implication.
📝 SC used the reasoning that committee judges are "alter ego" of the full court — each judge is an integral limb. Commentators note this is artificial but justified by practical necessity.
Tej Pal Singh (1986)
Government passed compulsory retirement order merely on recommendation of administrative judge alone — Administrative Committee of HC ratified later.
SC QUASHED — original recommendation (by single judge) was null and void. Ex post facto ratification by committee cannot validate a void act.
📝 The deviation was an incurable defect — not a mere irregularity. Ratification cannot save what was void from the beginning.
Bihar PSC v Manoj Pandey (1997)
Public Service Commission rules permitted committee of Chairman + 2 members to appoint examiners.
SC upheld — not feasible for every member to associate with every decision. Committees permissible; appointment of examiners by committee suffered no legal infirmity.
THEORETICAL NOTE
The delegatus doctrine is a rule of construction and nothing more — not an absolute bar. If practical needs of the situation demand delegation — it must be permitted even in the absence of express authorisation, provided it does not offend any principle of law or clear statutory prohibition.
22.8 — Sub-Delegation vs. Assistance — The Critical Distinction
🔀 Sub-Delegation vs. Assistance — Two Fundamentally Different Concepts
📝 'Delegation' = you hand over the power and the other person now decides. 'Assistance' = you get help from someone to gather facts/information, but YOU still make the final decision. If you mechanically rubber-stamp the assistant's recommendation without applying your own mind — it becomes unlawful delegation, not assistance.
✓ ASSISTANCE — PERMITTED
• Authority retains general control over subordinate
• Control is of a substantial degree
• Delegate undertakes preparatory work / initial decision
• Authority retains power to approve or disapprove
• Decision ultimately regarded as authority's own
• Routine cases handled by subordinate; new/important cases reserved by authority
📝 Think of it as: the assistant does the groundwork (collects evidence, inspects site, makes a report) and brings it to you. You study the report and decide yourself. That's assistance — no delegation.
✗ DISGUISED DELEGATION — INVALID
• Authority mechanically exercises discretionary power on the suggestion of assistant
• Does NOT apply its own mind
• Effective decision-making power has passed from authority to assistant
• Authority discharges only an empty formality
📝 Jeffs v New Zealand Dairy Production: The decision-maker must apply his mind to the evidence. If he doesn't, the decision-making power effectively flows to the hearing officer — and that is not permissible.
TEST — P.K. Roy (AIR 1968 SC 850)
"If the administrative authority named in the statute has and retains in its hands general control over the activities of the person to whom it has entrusted in part the exercise of its statutory power and the control exercised by the administrative authority is of a substantial degree, there is in the eye of law no 'delegation' at all..."
Key Cases Illustrating the Assistance Doctrine
P.K. Roy (Service Integration)
States prepared initial gradation lists; Central Govt supervised, heard representations, passed final orders
→ HELD: Assistance, NOT sub-delegation. Central Govt retained close supervision throughout.
Kapur (Communication of Order)
Central Govt decision communicated by Under Secretary ("directed to say...")
→ HELD: Assistance. US was merely communicating the order, not making one himself.
Pradyat Kumar — Enquiry Officer Procedure
Puisne judge conducted enquiry against Registrar; reported to Chief Justice; CJ issued show-cause notice and dismissed
→ HELD: Assistance — CJ applied his own mind. Common and valid procedure in modern administration.
Reddy (Routes Committee)
Road Transport Corporation appointed committee to gather information on route nationalisation feasibility
→ HELD: Assistance. Corporation gathered information through expert body — no question of delegation.
GULLAPALLI I — CONTRA
SC ruled that "personal hearing" principle is violated when one authority hears and another decides.
Note: This ruling has NOT been consistently followed in practice — modern administration has largely moved away from requiring the same officer to both hear and decide.
⚠️ GST ADJUDICATION RELEVANCE
In GST adjudication — Show Cause Notice issued by one officer, hearing conducted by same / another officer, order passed by designated proper officer. The assistance doctrine applies: if the proper officer (who issues the OIO) applies his own mind to the entire record — it is assistance, not unlawful delegation. If he merely rubber-stamps the field report / audit conclusion without applying own mind → can be challenged as mechanical exercise of power = invalid adjudication order.
💡 Example: CGST Audit team visits and prepares audit report recommending demand. SCN issued and adjudicated by Assistant/Deputy Commissioner. If the AC applies his own mind to the assessee's reply and the audit record — valid. If AC just passes the audit-recommended figure as the final order without examining the reply on merits — challenged as mechanical / non-application of mind = invalid order.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter XXII | Sub-Delegation of Non-Legislative Powers
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, Appellate Authority & GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name and court.
Delegatus Non-Potest Delegare — The Foundational Maxim
Supreme Court of India | Pramod K. Pankaj v State of Bihar | (2004) 3 SCC 723
Key Ratio
"'Delegatus non potest delegare' is a well-known maxim which means in absence of any power a delegate cannot sub-delegate its power to another person."
Settled Principle
"It is a settled principle that when the Act prescribes a particular body to exercise a power, it must be exercised only by that body; it cannot be exercised by others unless it is delegated. The law must also provide for such delegation."
When to Use — When an order under GST (SCN / OIO / Appellate Order) is issued by an officer who was not the proper officer designated / notified by the Commissioner for that function, or where a power specifically vested in the Commissioner is purportedly exercised by a subordinate without lawful delegation. Cite to establish: the foundational rule requires the very officer on whom power is conferred to exercise it.
Marathwada University v SBR Chavan
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1989 SC 1582 | (1989) 3 SCC 132
Key Ratio — Void Ab Initio
"The action of the Vice-Chancellor being unauthorized was void ab initio and so could not be ratified. A statutory body cannot travel beyond the power conferred and any action without power has no legal validity."
When to Use — When Department argues that even if the order was passed by a wrong officer, it was "ratified" or "approved" by a senior officer subsequently. Cite to establish: ratification of a void action cannot cure it. An unauthorized order is void ab initio — it cannot be given retrospective validity by any subsequent approval.
P.K. Roy v UOI — Test for Assistance vs. Sub-Delegation
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1968 SC 850 | 1968 (2) SCR 186
Key Ratio
"If the administrative authority named in the statute has and retains in its hands general control over the activities of the person to whom it has entrusted in part the exercise of its statutory power and the control exercised by the administrative authority is of a substantial degree, there is in the eye of law no 'delegation' at all and the maxim 'delegatus non potest delegare' does not apply... if a statutory authority empowers a delegate to undertake preparatory work and to take an initial decision in matters entrusted to it but retains in its own hands the power to approve or disapprove the decision after it has been taken, the decision will be held to have been validly made if the degree of control maintained by the authority is close enough for the decision to be regarded as the authority's own."
When to Use — When challenging a GST adjudication order on the ground that the adjudicating officer mechanically accepted audit findings without applying own mind. The test: Did the adjudicating officer retain control and apply his own mind? If the OIO merely reproduces the SCN figures / audit report without engaging with the assessee's reply — cite P.K. Roy to argue this is unlawful delegation of essential decision-making to the audit team, not legitimate assistance.
Kumar Keshridas — Personal Satisfaction Cannot be Delegated
Madhya Pradesh High Court | AIR 1984 MP 158
Key Ratio
Where a statute requires action to be taken by an officer "if he is satisfied," the satisfaction of that officer is a personal statutory requirement and cannot be substituted by the satisfaction of another officer. The High Court quashed the disconnection order holding that the satisfaction of the divisional engineer could not be delegated to someone else.
When to Use — When a GST show cause notice or order is issued using the "satisfaction" of an officer, but the actual satisfaction was formed by a different officer or based on a DRI / audit report without the issuing officer forming independent satisfaction. Section 73/74 CGST require the proper officer to have reason to believe — if that belief is borrowed from another officer without independent application of mind → challenge using this ratio: personal satisfaction of the statutory authority cannot be sub-delegated.
V.G. Shipping — General Delegation Clause Cannot Cover Quasi-Judicial Powers
Bombay High Court | AIR 1986 Bom 394
Key Ratio
A "general power of delegation" conferred by statute is not sufficient to enable delegation of quasi-judicial or quasi-criminal powers. Such powers can only be delegated if there is a "specific and express provision in the Act permitting the delegation of the said power or unless such delegation is permissible by necessary implication." If delegation was without authority — the action is a nullity and non est — and the Trust could not even ratify the action of the unauthorized officer as the same was a nullity.
When to Use — When Department relies on a general office order / circular directing subordinate officers to adjudicate matters above their prescribed pecuniary limits, or to exercise powers specifically vested in a senior authority. Cite to argue: a general administrative order cannot empower a subordinate to exercise quasi-judicial powers (adjudication/penalty imposition) which require specific statutory vesting.
Jeffs v New Zealand Dairy Production — Mechanical Acceptance = Unlawful Delegation
New Zealand Privy Council | (1966) 3 All ER 863 | Approved by SC India
Key Principle
The decision-maker must apply his mind to the evidence on record before deciding the matter. He should not act mechanically. If he does so, the decision-making power effectively flows to the hearing officer and the decision-maker discharges only an empty formality — which is not permissible.
When to Use — Most powerful in challenging GST OIOs (Original Orders) where the adjudicating officer has: (a) not engaged with the detailed reply / submissions of the assessee, (b) simply reproduced the SCN paragraphs as the OIO, (c) not given reasons for rejecting specific arguments. Cite Jeffs to argue: an order that does not reflect genuine application of mind by the adjudicating officer to the assessee's reply is an empty formality = unlawful mechanical exercise of power = invalid order.
Krishna Bus Service v State of Haryana — Biased Officer Cannot Exercise Regulatory Power
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1985 SC 1651 | (1985) 3 SCC 711
Key Ratio
Powers affecting Fundamental Rights must be exercised fairly and without bias. They should be reasonable both from the substantive as well as the procedural standpoint. Such powers should be entrusted to a person who could exercise them fairly and without bias. Administration must be rooted in confidence and that confidence is destroyed when people begin to think that the officer concerned is biased. "In both the cases there is a conflict between his duty on the one hand and his interest on the other."
When to Use — When the same officer who initiated an investigation / audit is also the adjudicating officer for the resulting demand — or where the adjudicating officer has publicly taken a position in a departmental conference/circular that is directly adverse to the assessee's position. Cite to challenge the fairness of the adjudication: an officer with a conflict of interest (investigator = adjudicator) cannot be expected to decide fairly — violates principles of natural justice.
Mohan Lal v U.I. Trust — Legislative Permission Mandatory for Delegation
Rajasthan High Court | AIR 1978 Raj 201
Key Ratio
"The delegation of power is permissible with the legislative permission and in the absence of such legislative permission there can be no delegation of the function of the Trust in favour of the officer of the Trust or its Secretary."
When to Use — When Department argues that a CGST Commissioner's administrative / office order delegating adjudication functions to subordinate officers is sufficient legal basis for those officers to exercise the power. Counter: legislative permission (under the parent Act / Rules) is mandatory for sub-delegation. A mere administrative order by the Commissioner cannot confer jurisdiction that the parent statute has not vested in that officer.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
⚖️ Chapter 22 — Sub-Delegation of Non-Legislative Powers

⚖️ Chapter 22 — Sub-Delegation of Non-Legislative Powers

Source: Principles of Administrative Law | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void / Illegal
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Alternative Route
Neutral Reference / Statutory
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
📋 Index of Sections
22.8 Assistance vs Sub-Delegation (Rajiah & Laxmamma)
22.9 Abdication of Functions
22.10 Usurpation of Power / Dictation
22.11 Ministerial Functions
22.12 Intergovernmental Delegation
22.13 Incidents of Sub-Delegation
22.14 Delegate's Order = Principal's Order
22.15 Delegating Authority Retains Power
22.16 Control Over Sub-Delegate
22.17 Issue of Directions to Sub-Delegate
22.18 Sub-Sub-Delegation
22.19 Sub-Delegation under Essential Commodities Act
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
22.8 — Assistance vs Sub-Delegation | Rajiah & Laxmamma
⚖️ Assistance vs Sub-Delegation
When can the decision-maker seek help vs when must he decide alone?
📝 The key question: Can a statutory decision-maker appoint someone to help him gather facts and then decide himself? YES — that is "assistance". But can he let that helper decide? NO — that is illegal sub-delegation.
✓ ASSISTANCE — PERMITTED
Decision-maker may seek help of subordinates/committee to gather facts & assist — without delegating power to them
📝 Like a judge who appoints a commissioner to record evidence — but the judge himself writes the judgment.
CASE: Rajiah
Chief Justice appointed review committee to examine disciplinary matter
→ Committee = Assistance, NOT sub-delegation
→ Chief Justice (not committee) must decide
BUT — FATAL ERROR
Committee's report was neither placed before full court meeting nor circulated to Judges
→ Order of compulsory retirement QUASHED
✗ SUB-DELEGATION — PROHIBITED (without authority)
Decision-maker cannot allow the assistant/committee to effectively decide the matter
📝 If the helper's report is blindly accepted and becomes the order — the authority has abdicated. The statute's trust in the decision-maker is violated.
CASE: Laxmamma
Assistant Commissioner had exclusive power to resume SC/ST lands
→ Delegated execution of orders to Tahsildars
→ HC quashed: Act conferred exclusive powers on Asst. Commissioner
BUT — ASSISTANCE IS STILL OK
Asst. Commissioner CAN take assistance of subordinates to execute orders — just cannot delegate the decision-making
SPIRIT & INTENTION TEST
Court may interpret provision to require personal inquiry by decision-maker — based on:
• Serious consequences to party
• Onus placed on specific officer by statute
• Language of the provision
📝 Even if Pradyat Kumar ruling would normally permit the procedure — if the statute's "spirit and intention" requires personal inquiry, the authority cannot outsource it.
💡 GST: A SCN adjudication order by an officer who merely rubber-stamps a committee's report without applying his own mind = liable to be quashed as violation of natural justice and sub-delegation doctrine.
22.9 — Abdication of Functions
🚫 Abdication of Functions
Donee of power silently surrenders it to subordinate without formal delegation
📝 "Abdication" = The authority who should decide simply steps aside and lets a subordinate decide — without any formal order delegating the power. No one authorised it; it just happened. This is illegal.
CORE RULE
A holder of power must exercise it himself. Letting a subordinate effectively exercise the power — without formal delegation — is abdication, hit by the "no-delegation" principle.
Two Forms of Abdication
FORM 1
Donee lets subordinate exercise power effectively — without any formal delegation order
📝 The donee simply disappears from the decision — never even looks at the case. The subordinate decides everything in practice.
FORM 2
Donee acts as a rubber stamp — mechanically adopts subordinate's recommendation without applying own mind
📝 The authority signs the order but never actually thought about it. The subordinate's report IS the order in substance.
💡 GST: Adjudicating officer who passes demand order verbatim copying the audit's SCN — without independent application of mind — risks the order being quashed as "rubber stamp" abdication.
INQUIRY COMMITTEE — ALLOWED ASSISTANCE
Authority may appoint enquiry committee to enquire into the matter — PROVIDED:
(1) Ultimate decision is reached by the very decision-maker in whom power is vested
(2) Inquiry committee makes a full report with adequate summary of evidence tendered before it
22.9.1 — Manickchandra v State
General Manager, SRTC published nationalisation scheme u/s 68C, Motor Vehicles Act, 1939
Section 68C requires the Corporation to consider scheme & form its opinion
HELD INVALID
Corporation never applied its mind
→ General Manager had no jurisdiction to publish scheme alone
→ Scheme = ultra vires
22.9.2 — Bharat Wools
Power vested in allotment committee — but Chairman converted himself into sole repository, "epitomised" powers of all. Power vests in one authority but authority lets actual decision be taken by another → = Abdication
22.10 — Usurpation of Power / Dictation
🎯 Usurpation of Power / Dictation
Higher authority exercises power legally vested in lower officer
📝 The mirror image of abdication. In abdication, the authority surrenders upward. In usurpation, a higher authority grabs power downward. Both are illegal. The power must be exercised only by the one in whom the statute vests it.
Feature Abdication Usurpation / Dictation
Who acts? Subordinate decides — without authority Superior decides — for the lower officer
Direction of illegality Power flows down informally Power is grabbed upward
Junior officer's role Decides in place of the authority Merely acts as "conduit pipe" — communicates superior's decision
Legal Effect Order invalid Order invalid
22.10.1 — Kutty | Rice Milling Industry (Regulation) Act, 1958
Central Govt delegated power to State Govt & District Collectors in Kerala
(both have concurrent & coordinate jurisdiction)
Collector rejected application for rice mill permit
→ State Govt directed Collector to issue permit
→ Collector complied
HC QUASHED
"If district collector exercises statutory power in obedience to State Govt's direction → impermissible abdication of his responsibility. Any such order = not legal."
PRINCIPLE Neither statutory authority is superior or inferior to the other under the concurrent notification. Collector must exercise his OWN judgment — guided only by statutory provisions, not State Govt's direction.
📝 GST Application: Where CGST and SGST officers both have jurisdiction — one cannot direct the other on how to decide. Adjudication by SGST officer under dictation of a superior/minister is liable to be quashed.
22.11 — Ministerial Functions
🔧 Ministerial Functions — Non-Delegation Doctrine Not Applied
📝 "Ministerial" = A task that involves NO judgment or discretion — pure mechanical execution. Like posting a letter or filing a form. Since there is nothing to "judge," it doesn't matter who does it. The non-delegation rule doesn't apply here.
GENERAL RULE
Non-delegation doctrine generally NOT applied to sub-delegation of ministerial function (no discretion involved)
→ Immaterial who discharges the mechanical/non-discretionary part
Once discretionary element discharged by the authority itself → mechanical part (communicating the decision) can be left to subordinate
Deciding authority need not sign the communication itself
22.11.1 — Mangulal | EXCEPTION
Commissioner sub-delegated to Medical Officer power to "take" proceedings for offences
→ Complaint filed by Licence Inspector with Medical Officer's permission
→ SC: WRONG — "take" ≠ "cause to be taken"
📝 Too literal interpretation — but Court justified it because criminal prosecution (fundamental right u/Art 21) was at stake. Personal freedom demands personal application of mind.
22.11.2 — Adimurthy | CONTRAST
Prescribed officer directed subordinate to lodge complaint with police — prosecution "at the instance" of officer
→ SC: Valid — officer exercised discretion to prosecute; mechanical function of lodging complaint delegable
📝 The decision to prosecute = discretionary (exercised by the prescribed officer). The act of lodging = mechanical (can be delegated).
KEY PRINCIPLE
Non-delegation doctrine arises from the premise that the Legislature has placed its trust in the judgment of the specific authority. Once the discretionary element is discharged, the "trust" theory need not be applied to the mechanical part. When the authority has made the choice between alternatives → no judgment remains → immaterial who executes.
💡 GST: DRC-01A issued by system / junior officer after the adjudicating officer decides to initiate demand — valid ministerial function. But the actual adjudication order (DRC-07) must bear the mind of the adjudicating officer — not a template copy.
22.12 — Intergovernmental Delegation of Administrative Power
🏛️ Intergovernmental Delegation — Constitutional & Statutory Provisions
Article 258(1) — Centre to States
President may entrust (conditionally or unconditionally) to State Govt or its officers, any function in relation to matters within Centre's executive power
(with State consent)
📝 Centre can give its own work to States — with their permission. Centre decides if conditions apply.
LIMITS — Cannot be entrusted u/Art 258(1):
• Power to promulgate Ordinances (Art 123)
• Emergency suspension of Arts 268-279 (Art 354)
• President's Rule — Art 356
• Financial Emergency — Art 360
• Appoint judges — Arts 124, 217
• Backward Classes Commission — Art 340
• Union servants' pleasure — Art 310
📝 These are personal/constitutional functions of the President — cannot be handed to States at all.
LIMITATION — ONLY EXECUTIVE FUNCTIONS
Balance of judicial opinion: only executive functions delegable u/Art 258(1)
Quasi-judicial & delegated legislation — NOT under Art 258(1); need statutory provision
Article 258A — States to Centre
State Govt may entrust (conditionally or unconditionally) to Central Govt or its officers, functions in relation to matters within State's executive power
(with Centre's consent)
📝 States can give their work to Centre. Inserted in 1956 — made possible after practical gaps in Hirakud Dam project (Singh case).
Singh Case
High Court: relationship u/Art 258A between Centre & State is NOT that of principal & agent
22.12.2 — Delegation through Statutes
Art 154(2)(b): Parliament may confer functions on any authority subordinate to Governor
Art 258(2): A Central law may confer power/duties on States, their officers & authorities
Examples of Statutory Delegation
• CST Act — State sales tax authorities collect inter-State tax
• MISA — Preventive detention on District Magistrates
• Mines & Minerals Act — States regulate minor minerals as delegates of Centre
• Essential Commodities Act — Central power delegated to State Govts
Key Cases — Limits of Delegated Power
Rana Case — Art 258(1)
Presidential notification has force of law — kept alive by s.87, States Reorganisation Act
→ Commissioner in Gujarat could exercise Central functions without fresh notification
Mount Corporation
State constituted committee under Deputy Minister to deal with raw material distribution
→ Director's power under Central Act usurped by committee
→ "Scheme of federation would be myth" if State could direct its officers when they discharge Central functions
Bhupendra
Centre delegated prosecution-consent power to District Magistrates
→ State conferred same on Additional District Magistrates
→ SC: State CANNOT further delegate "a power of the Central Government which Central Government has delegated to the District Magistrate"
CORE RATIO — Mount Corporation
"An authority having power under a law must exercise its own individual judgment and not adopt the decision of any other body as his own."
💡 GST: A State GST officer functioning as delegate under CGST Act cannot be directed by his State Government department on how to adjudicate a specific Central GST matter — Mount Corporation ratio applies directly.
22.13 — Incidents of Sub-Delegation
📋 Incidents & Consequences of Valid Sub-Delegation
RULE 1 — Sub-delegate commits no illegality
When power has been validly sub-delegated, the sub-delegate commits no illegality when he exercises that power
Bansidhar
Opinion that land necessary for defence = formable by any officer to whom power was sub-delegated — valid
Sia Ram
Confiscation power delegated by State to Assistant Collector under Essential Commodities Act — held valid
RULE 2 — No enhancement of hierarchical status
Delegation of power does NOT enhance or improve hierarchical status of the sub-delegate
📝 Getting someone else's powers doesn't make you equal to them in rank. You're still their subordinate — just with some extra powers temporarily.
Krishna Kapur
CEE appointed in Railways — DAEE removed him from service
Art 311(1): Cannot be removed by authority SUBORDINATE to appointing authority
→ Argument: CEE delegated appointment power to DAEE → they are equals — REJECTED
→ "DAEE did not cease to be subordinate in rank to CEE merely because latter's power was delegated to him"
RULE 3 — Sub-delegate acts on OWN judgment
Sub-delegate is entitled to act according to his own judgment — must apply his own mind
RULE 4 — Sub-delegate's decision = Delegator's decision
Decision of sub-delegate is regarded as decision of delegating authority itself
→ Becomes BINDING on delegator
→ Principal authority CANNOT review, vary, rescind or ratify — if quasi-judicial — without express statutory provision
📝 When a government official exercises power delegated to him, his order IS the government's order. The statute creates the power; only the statute decides who can review it.
💡 GST: An order passed by the delegated GST officer = order of the Commissioner/Government. The Commissioner cannot informally recall or modify it without statutory revision/appeal mechanism.
22.14 — Delegate's Order = Principal's Order | Roop Chand, Behari Kunj, CATA, Joginder
📜 Delegate's Order = Principal's Order — Key Consequences
📝 This is the most important practical principle: once a sub-delegate passes an order under delegated power, that order is the Government's own order. Therefore the Government cannot appeal from, or review, its own order — unless the statute specifically allows it.
22.13.4 Roop Chand v State of Punjab | AIR 1963 SC 1503
State Govt was appellate authority under East Punjab Holdings (Consolidation) Act, 1948
→ Delegated appellate power to one of its officers
HELD
Order of sub-delegate appellate authority = order of State Govt itself
→ State Govt DISABLED from hearing appeal from its own order
FURTHER
State's general revision power under statute ALSO could not be used
→ Cannot revise sub-delegate's quasi-judicial order (would = revising own order)
→ Administrative order — different; administrator can review own order
⚖️ ROOP CHAND RATIO:
"No doubt the Act enables the Government to delegate its power but such a power when delegated remains the power of the Government, for the Government can only delegate the power given to it by statute and cannot create an independent power in the officer. When the delegate exercises the power, he does so for the Government."
22.13.5 Behari Kunj — SC 1997
Custodian General's delegate passed order → Revision filed before Custodian General → He correctly held revision incompetent — it would mean exercising power of review not flowing from statute
→ SC upheld: "Order of delegate is to be treated, for all intents and purposes, as an order of the principal itself"
22.14 Commissioner of Land Records — SC 1998
Board delegated revision power to Commissioner
→ Commissioner's order = Board's order
→ Board CANNOT review Commissioner's order
→ SC: "When delegate personifies his principal and his orders are orders of his principal, all other powers attributable to that personality will be exercisable by him"
→ Commissioner authorised to revise his own orders
22.14.1 CATA — DISTINGUISHED from Roop Chand
Government conferred Registrar's powers on Deputy Registrar under statute
→ Deputy Registrar acts "under general superintendence of Registrar"
→ NOT strict sub-delegation (senior officer delegating to junior)
→ Registrar CAN exercise revision power u/s 77 over Deputy Registrar's orders
Key distinction: In Roop Chand — Government sub-delegated ITS power. In CATA — Government independently conferred powers on Deputy Registrar, not through Registrar.
22.14.2 Joginder
Order of Chief Settlement Commissioner merged into Finance Commissioner's order (both delegates of Central Govt)
→ One delegate CANNOT review order of another delegate of the same authority — no statutory provision permits this
→ Earlier order had attained finality; if aggrieved → Writ petition in High Court
📝 Remedy for law: Express provision in statute can reserve power in delegating authority to hear appeal from sub-delegate.
💡 GST: Once an Additional Commissioner (to whom Commissioner has delegated adjudication) passes an order, the Commissioner himself cannot recall or review it. The aggrieved party must go to Appellate Authority u/s 107 — not back to the Commissioner.
22.15 — Delegating Authority Retains Power | Godavari, Malik Medical, Bharat Wools
✅ Delegation Does NOT = Denudation of Power
Delegating authority retains concurrent power to exercise delegated function
📝 "Delegation" means giving someone else the authority to also do what you do — NOT permanently giving away your own power. The delegator is NOT stripped of his power. He can still exercise it himself if he wishes, and can revoke the delegation any time.
SETTLED LAW
(1) Delegator is NOT divested of his power
(2) He can exercise the same in any case if he wishes
(3) Powers sub-delegated may be revoked, altered or resumed at any time
(4) Delegation does NOT involve irrevocable transfer or assignment of power
(5) Delegator and sub-delegate have CONCURRENT jurisdiction
REJECTED VIEWS
• Blackpool (1948): Sub-delegation denuded Minister of all power except expressly reserved — No longer accepted
• King Emperor v Sibnath Bannerjee (PC): Sub-delegation involves "divestiture" of responsibility — Not accepted
Godavari (SC 1966)
State Govt's power of detention sub-delegated to District Magistrates
→ Power exercisable by EITHER District Magistrate OR State Govt
→ Both have concurrent jurisdiction
Malik Medical Hall
Director delegated licensing power to District Officer
→ Director STILL entitled to cancel petitioner's licence
→ "Action of delegate must be considered to be an action taken by Director himself"
Bharat Wools
Govt delegated allotment to committee; Minister cancelled allotment after complaint
→ Valid — "where Govt delegates executive function to any officer/authority, it is not denuded of the plenary power to carry on the executive function"
→ Can interfere where decision arbitrary, unreasonable, or contrary to public interest
⚠️ CAVEAT
"So long as the authority delegated had not been exercised by the delegated authority, the power still remains with the original authority" — Madras HC (G. Vasantha Pai)
BUT — decision taken by delegate is normally binding on delegator who is not entitled to rescind or vary the same.
💡 GST: Commissioner who has delegated show-cause notice powers to Additional Commissioner can still personally issue a notice in a large fraud case — concurrent jurisdiction. But once Additional Commissioner decides, Commissioner cannot override the decision without statutory revisional power.
22.16 — Control Over Sub-Delegate
🔒 Control Over Sub-Delegate — Ultra Vires Doctrine
Basic Principle — Sub-delegate Cannot Exceed Power Delegated
Sub-delegate cannot act beyond parameters of power delegated
Any excess = ILLEGAL and VOID
Any action beyond sub-delegated power = ultra vires
If sub-delegation is CONDITIONAL → sub-delegate must function within conditions imposed
Otherwise = ultra vires
Illustrative Cases
22.16.1 Chittoor
Central Govt delegated powers to State Govts under ECA — subject to prior concurrence of Central Govt
→ State issued notification WITHOUT Centre's prior concurrence
→ SC: "A delegate is not entitled to exercise powers in excess or in contravention of delegated powers. If any order issued in excess of powers delegated — illegal and void"
22.16.2 Marathawad University
Executive Council could delegate disciplinary power to VC — only with Chancellor's approval
→ Council delegated WITHOUT Chancellor's approval
→ VC dismissed officer, Council ratified ex post facto
→ SC QUASHED: (1) VC exceeded power; (2) No valid delegation — no Chancellor's consent; (3) Void ab initio action CANNOT be ratified
📝 Void ab initio = void from the very start. No amount of later approval can cure it. The illegality is incurable.
22.16.3 V.G. Shipping
Deputy Conservator imposed fine — no valid delegation from Board
→ Order = void ab initio and non est
→ Board's ratification ALSO invalid — Board ratified without applying its mind to whether penalty should be imposed and how much
(Illustrates: even ratification requires application of mind)
📝 Non est = does not exist. Not just voidable — treated as if it never happened at all.
PRINCIPLE A statutory authority cannot travel beyond the power conferred on it, and any action without power has NO legal validity. An action void ab initio CANNOT be ratified by the principal or delegator.
💡 GST: If the proper officer issues SCN for a period beyond what is covered by the delegation/jurisdiction order — the entire SCN is void ab initio. Subsequent ratification by Commissioner cannot cure it.
22.17 — Issue of Directions by Delegating Authority to Sub-Delegate
📋 Can the Delegating Authority Direct the Sub-Delegate?
📝 Once power is delegated, can the boss still tell the sub-delegate how to decide? General answer: No — not for specific cases. But general policy guidelines may be permissible with difficulties. Sub-delegates are NOT agents of the delegating authority.
✗ CANNOT — Specific Case Directions
Issue of direction to sub-delegate as to how to deal with a SPECIFIC matter = definitely out of order
→ Inconsistent with first principles of Admin Law: no administrator exercising discretionary power is to act under dictation of anyone — not even delegating authority
📝 Once delegated — the sub-delegate is independent for that decision. He must apply HIS mind, not the boss's mind.
Blackpool & Simms
Court ruled: Minister did NOT retain power to issue directions to corporation/official after delegation
→ Corporation/official could not be regarded as agent of Minister
→ Sub-delegate applies own mind & discretion
Note — Adjudicative Bodies
Discussion about directions is NOT relevant to adjudicative bodies
→ Issue of ANY direction to them would infringe natural justice
→ Only administrative/non-adjudicative bodies discussed here
Distinction — Assistant vs Sub-Delegate
Assistant: Acts in name of principal, under close supervision & directions — analogy of agent may apply
Sub-Delegate: Acts in OWN name, broad discretion, own decisions — NOT an agent
📝 Key test: Does the person decide in their own name or in the principal's name? Own name = sub-delegate (no instructions). Principal's name = assistant (instructions OK).
General Guidelines — May be Possible
Issue of general guidelines/statements of policy to multiple sub-delegates may be useful for:
• Consistency across multiple decision-makers
• Coordination when many officers deal with similar cases
• Uniformity of policy
Solutions available:
(a) Statute itself provides for general administrative directions
(b) Delegation order reserves power to issue general directions
(c) Delegating authority reserves final concurrence in specific types of cases
(d) General directions issued BUT sub-delegates retain discretion for specific cases
SPECIAL CASE — Rules of Business
Where power conferred on Central Govt (President) is exercised through Rules of Business by civil servants → Superior authority CAN issue directions to subordinates — because Rules of Business expressly envisage disposal under Minister's authority. This is different from statutory sub-delegation.
💡 GST: A CBIC Circular issuing general instructions to field officers on classification/valuation issues = permissible general guidelines. But a specific instruction to a Commissioner to decide a particular case in a particular way = usurpation — violates the sub-delegate's independent statutory jurisdiction.
22.18 — Sub-Sub-Delegation
🔗 Sub-Sub-Delegation — Delegatus Non Potest Delegare
📝 "Delegatus non potest delegare" = A delegate cannot further delegate. Legislature → A → B (valid sub-delegation). But B → C (sub-sub-delegation) requires specific statutory authority. Each step downward needs fresh authorization.
CORE RULE
Sub-sub-delegation is NOT possible without statutory authority to do so — either expressly or impliedly.
The maxim delegatus non potest delegare applies here as well.
Sub-sub-delegation also NOT possible when there is a statutory prohibition against it.
📝 Effect of multi-level sub-delegation: Each level removed from the source dilutes accountability. Safeguards of the parent Act also get diluted. Public finds it difficult to know the extent of authority of the officer.
Key Cases on Sub-Sub-Delegation
Case Fact Held
Allingham Minister delegated to committee → committee sub-delegated to its executive officer Sub-delegation by committee to officer = INVALID. "On ordinary principle of delegatus non potest delegare, committee cannot delegate their power to some other person or body." Order not binding on plaintiff.
Oberoi Motors Central Govt delegated to Chief Commissioner → Chief Commissioner delegated price-fixing to manufacturers themselves INVALID. "Further delegation cannot be made by a delegate. Delegatus non potest delegare — a delegated power cannot be delegated by a delegate." Also invalid: delegation of de futuro decisions to manufacturers.
Tej Pal Singh Administrative Committee power — single Administrative Judge acted without consulting full committee; committee ratified ex post facto NULL AND VOID. "Deviation is not mere irregularity — it amounts to incurable defect amounting to illegality." Ex post facto approval cannot validate void ab initio order.
Sahni Silk Mills (SC) Standing committee of ESI Corporation delegated levy-of-damages power to Director-General "or any officer authorised by him" Italicised portion INVALID vis-à-vis s.94A. Statute conceives direct delegation by corporation to officers — no scope for delegate to further sub-delegate. s.94A makes no provision for sub-sub-delegation.
Abdul Razak (SC) s.94A: Corporation delegates to DG. Regulations under s.95(1): DG can further delegate with standing committee approval. VALID. Regulations ≠ s.94A. Regulations made under rule-making power can provide for further delegation by DG. Not inconsistent with s.94A. Sphere of operation different.
Roy State Govt delegated prosecution-consent to Food (Health) Authority → Authority further delegated to food inspectors INVALID. s.20 does not envisage further delegation by the person to whom State Govt delegated power. Only State Govt can delegate — not its sub-delegate. Food inspector must obtain written consent of Food Authority — not issue prosecution independently.
Kshettra Samiti State Govt delegated Director's power → Director issued circular asking District Magistrates to submit proposals VALID. Director only asked DMs to submit proposals (ministerial work). Director would process proposals himself and take ultimate decision. No sub-delegation by Director — merely enlisting assistance for ministerial work.
DISTINCTION — Sub-Sub-Delegation vs Ministerial Assistance
Asking subordinates to submit proposals / gather information / communicate decisions = ministerial assistance (NOT sub-delegation)
Allowing subordinate to actually decide the matter = sub-sub-delegation (requires statutory authority)
💡 GST: Section 5(4) CGST Act allows the Board to assign powers to officers. Further delegation by Commissioner to Deputy Commissioner requires specific authority — otherwise any adjudication order by DC = without authority = void ab initio (Sahni Silk Mills ratio).
22.19 — Sub-Delegation under Essential Commodities Act & Merugu
📦 Sub-Delegation under Essential Commodities Act, 1955
Centre → State → Subordinate Officers — How far can the chain go?
CHAIN
s.3(1): Central Govt has power → s.5: Central Govt delegates to State Govts → State Govts = sub-delegates of Centre
Question: Can State Govt further delegate to its subordinate officers?
LEGISLATIVE POWER — Cannot be further delegated by State
State Govt (as sub-delegate of Centre) CANNOT delegate its legislative power to subordinate officers
→ Subordinate officer cannot make legislative order of general application
Punjab High Court — State v Amir Chand
📝 Legislative power affects rights of MANY. Administrative power affects rights of specific identified persons. Courts should not sanction grant of legislative power to subordinate officers without clear & specific authority.
ADMINISTRATIVE POWER — Can be conferred through Legislative Order
State Govt CAN, through a legislative order (having force of law), vest ADMINISTRATIVE powers in subordinate officers to enforce the order
→ Valid — State has necessarily to administer with help of officials; administration cannot be carried on otherwise
Bombay High Court — Thakorelal
📝 The State must run the show through subordinate staff. Giving them administrative powers (act in specific cases) through a legislative order is permitted — it is implied permission. But giving them rule-making power = different matter altogether.
💡 GST: State Govt issuing a GO conferring adjudication power on specific-rank officers for ECA orders = valid administrative delegation through legislative order. But State Govt empowering its officers to make GST exemption notifications = invalid sub-sub-delegation of legislative power.
ALTERNATIVE — Centre Directly to State Officers
u/s 5, Essential Commodities Act — Central Govt may itself sub-delegate administrative power directly to State officials — without using State Govt as conduit
Hans Raj Bhartiys — delegation to Commissioner in State = valid
22.19.1 — Merugu | Sub-Sub-Delegate Cannot Abdicate
General principle: A sub-sub-delegate has to himself discharge the function delegated to him — he CANNOT abdicate this function in favour of someone else.
Merugu Satyanarayana v State of AP — AIR 1982 SC 1543
📝 Even when a chain of delegations is valid — each person at each level must personally exercise the power delegated to them. You cannot be a sub-sub-delegate and then pass it on again informally.
SUMMARY TABLE — ECA Sub-Delegation
Legislative power to subordinate officers — NOT VALID (no clear authority)
Administrative power through legislative order to subordinate officers — VALID (implied permission)
Centre directly delegating to State officers u/s 5 — VALID
Sub-sub-delegate abdicating his function further — NOT VALID
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter 22 | Sub-Delegation of Non-Legislative Powers
These ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, First Appellate Authority, and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the case name.
Roop Chand v State of Punjab
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1963 SC 1503 : 1963 Supp (1) SCR 539
Key Ratio
"No doubt the Act enables the Government to delegate its power but such a power when delegated remains the power of the Government, for the Government can only delegate the power given to it by statute and cannot create an independent power in the officer. When the delegate exercises the power, he does so for the Government."
When to Use — When Department argues that an order passed by a delegated officer can be reviewed/recalled/revised by the delegating authority without express statutory provision. Cite to establish: delegate's order = Government's own order; Government cannot revise its own quasi-judicial order.
Behari Kunj Sahkari Awas Samiti v State of Uttar Pradesh
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1997 SC 3123 : (1997) 7 SCC 37
Key Ratio
"The order of the delegate is to be treated, for all intents and purposes, as an order of the principal itself." A revision to the delegating authority from an order of its own delegate would tantamount to exercising a power of review which does not flow from the statute.
When to Use — When Revenue seeks to re-open or revise an order already decided by their own delegated officer. Cite to show that without a specific statutory revisional provision, the principal authority cannot review its own delegate's order.
Commissioner of Land Records & Settlement, Cuttack — SC 1998
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1998 SC 3067 : (1998) 7 SCC 162
Key Ratio
"When the delegate personifies his principal and his orders are to be treated as orders of his principal, all other powers attributable to the personality which he personifies, will be exercisable by him."
