When to Use — In appeals where the argument is raised that the delegated officer lacks power to exercise certain incidental powers. Cite to establish: the delegate, once personifying the principal, gets all ancillary powers of the principal including power to revise his own earlier orders.
Mount Corporation v Director of Industries
Mysore High Court | AIR 1965 Mys 144
Key Ratio
"The scheme of federation would be a myth if the State Government, by a clever device, could direct and control the actions of its officers when they discharge statutory powers on behalf of the Central Government. An authority having power under a law must exercise its own individual judgment and not adopt the decision of any other body as his own."
When to Use — When SGST officer's adjudication order appears to be influenced by State Revenue directions rather than independent application of mind. Cite to challenge the order as passed under dictation — violating the independence required of a statutory decision-maker.
Marathawad University v S.B.R. Chavan
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1989 SC 1582 : (1989) 3 SCC 132
Key Ratio / Three Grounds for Invalidity
A statutory authority cannot travel beyond the power conferred on it, and any action without power has no legal validity. (1) Where action of sub-delegate exceeds power conferred on him; (2) Where there is no valid delegation (requisite consent/approval not obtained); (3) Where action is unauthorized and void ab initio — it CANNOT be ratified subsequently. "The error committed amounts to an incurable defect amounting to an illegality."
When to Use — When SCN or adjudication order has been issued by an officer without proper delegation, or where an earlier defective delegation was sought to be cured by subsequent approval. Cite to establish: void ab initio = incurable; ratification cannot save it.
District Collector, Chittoor v Chittoor District Groundnut Traders Association
Supreme Court of India | (1989) 2 SCC 58 : AIR 1989 SC 989
Key Ratio
"A delegate is not entitled to exercise powers in excess or in contravention of the delegated powers. If any order is issued or framed in excess of the powers delegated to the authorities, such order would be illegal and void."
When to Use — When the officer issuing SCN/passing adjudication order has acted beyond the parameters of his delegation — e.g., exceeded monetary limits, covered periods outside jurisdiction, or acted without the requisite prior conditions (like concurrence of higher authority) being satisfied.
Sahni Silk Mills v ESI Corporation
Supreme Court of India | (1994) 5 SCC 345 : JT 1994 (5) SC 11
Key Ratio — Sub-Sub-Delegation without Authority = Invalid
Section 94A conceives direct delegation by the corporation to different officers subordinate to the Corporation — but there is no scope for such delegate to further sub-delegate such power. Delegation by the standing committee to the Director-General was valid under section 94A, but the statutory provision did not authorise the standing committee to authorise its delegate (the Director-General) to further delegate the power to any other officer.
When to Use — When an adjudication order or SCN is issued by an officer at the bottom of a multi-level delegation chain, where intermediate delegation was not sanctioned by statute. Cite to establish that sub-sub-delegation requires specific statutory authority — in its absence, the order at the bottom of the chain is without authority.
Kutty — V.P. Mohd. Kutty v T. Kunhikoya Haji
Kerala High Court | AIR 1985 Ker 33
Key Ratio — Usurpation / Dictation
"If the district collector, while exercising his statutory power, does so in obedience to the direction given by the State Government, it would amount to impermissible abdication of his responsibility and power. Any order passed by the district collector in such circumstances would not be legal."
When to Use — Where there is evidence or allegation that the adjudicating officer has acted under superior instructions rather than independent judgment. Cite to challenge the order as passed under dictation — a form of abdication/usurpation making the order not legal.
Oberoi Motors v Union Territory
Punjab & Haryana High Court | AIR 1978 P&H 294
Key Ratio — Delegatus Non Potest Delegare
"It is an established principle of law that further delegation cannot be made by a delegate. The doctrine of delegatus non potest delegare is well known. It means that a delegated power cannot be delegated by a delegate."
When to Use — Simple, clear citation for the basic rule against sub-sub-delegation. Use in any hearing where an officer has issued an order under powers purportedly sub-delegated by another officer who was himself only a delegate — without statutory authority for the second-level delegation.
Godavari S. Parulekar v State of Maharashtra
Supreme Court of India | AIR 1966 SC 1404 : 1966 (3) SCR 34
Key Ratio — Concurrent Jurisdiction of Delegator and Delegate
Delegation does not denude the delegating authority of its own power. The power could be exercised either by the district magistrate or the State Government, as the Government was not denuded of its power by sub-delegating the same to the district magistrates. In such a case, both the principal authority and the sub-delegate have concurrent jurisdiction.
When to Use — When assessee argues that since the Commissioner has delegated power to Additional Commissioner, the Commissioner himself cannot issue a notice or intervene in the matter. Cite to show concurrent jurisdiction — both the delegator and delegate can exercise the power.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
